6
00:00:13,950 --> 00:00:16,940
So, if you have understood then you can skip this lecture
6

7
00:00:16,950 --> 00:00:18,470
no problem.
7

8
00:00:18,470 --> 00:00:23,420
So, the goal was to print a text or a string in double quotes.
8

9
00:00:23,430 --> 00:00:24,160
Right?
9

10
00:00:24,180 --> 00:00:25,320
So, let's try.
10

11
00:00:26,130 --> 00:00:27,350
So, printf.
11

12
00:00:27,360 --> 00:00:33,320
So, when you use printf you should always write your message inside the double quotes.
12

13
00:00:33,630 --> 00:00:35,540
So, you should remember that.
13

14
00:00:35,640 --> 00:00:42,060
And our string was David says and programming is fun.
14

15
00:00:42,060 --> 00:00:42,620
Right?
15

16
00:00:42,630 --> 00:00:44,320
Something like this.
16

17
00:00:44,340 --> 00:00:46,280
So, this is our message.
17

18
00:00:46,320 --> 00:00:46,620
Right?
18

19
00:00:46,620 --> 00:00:50,380
So now let's terminate the statement with semicolon.
19

20
00:00:50,390 --> 00:00:50,760
All right.
20

21
00:00:50,760 --> 00:00:53,760
So now, let's try to run this.Here it is.
21

22
00:00:53,790 --> 00:00:56,990
So, you actually met with some errors. Right?
22

23
00:00:57,000 --> 00:00:58,280
So, let's check the error.
23

24
00:00:58,290 --> 00:01:04,060
Error is expected a round bracket before programming, that is here.
24

25
00:01:04,110 --> 00:01:09,220
So, insert a bracket before programming. So, which is not actually correct
25

26
00:01:09,240 --> 00:01:10,890
that's not what we wanted.
26

27
00:01:10,890 --> 00:01:17,980
So now here what's happening is, we are confusing the compiler by giving too many a double quotes.
27

28
00:01:18,420 --> 00:01:26,130
If you want to print a double quote, so then you have to use the associated escape sequence for that.
28

29
00:01:26,160 --> 00:01:35,070
So, we have to tell the compiler that consider this double quote as a normal character and otherwise, compiler
29

30
00:01:35,070 --> 00:01:45,520
will consider it as a beginning of the printf statement.So, we tell that by using the escape character.
30

31
00:01:45,550 --> 00:01:50,180
So, our escape character is you know that that is backslash.
31

32
00:01:50,210 --> 00:01:50,970
Right?
32

33
00:01:50,970 --> 00:01:56,240
So, now we are to take the help of backslash.So, give the backslash here.
33

34
00:01:56,270 --> 00:02:03,410
So, when you give the backlash here, this letter or character will be considered as a normal printable
34

35
00:02:03,410 --> 00:02:04,730
character.
35

36
00:02:04,730 --> 00:02:09,510
And after that, we want this double quote to be printed.
36

37
00:02:09,560 --> 00:02:10,280
Right?
37

38
00:02:10,310 --> 00:02:14,670
So, give one more backslash prior to that.
38

39
00:02:14,750 --> 00:02:15,720
That's it.
39

40
00:02:15,740 --> 00:02:18,230
Now you have fixed the problem.
40

41
00:02:18,230 --> 00:02:23,770
And after that, let's give \n to go to the next line and here
41

42
00:02:23,800 --> 00:02:24,650
let's print
42

43
00:02:24,650 --> 00:02:25,790
Good day.
43

44
00:02:25,790 --> 00:02:29,490
Let's terminate the statement with semicolon.
44

45
00:02:29,530 --> 00:02:31,690
So, now let's run.
45

46
00:02:31,690 --> 00:02:33,010
And here it is.
46

47
00:02:33,010 --> 00:02:35,750
So, that is one space inserted at the beginning,
47

48
00:02:35,830 --> 00:02:37,470
so let's remove that.
48

49
00:02:37,570 --> 00:02:38,890
Let's run.
49

50
00:02:38,920 --> 00:02:40,000
Here it is.
50

51
00:02:40,000 --> 00:02:47,050
So, from this lecture you should understand that in order to print a double quote you have to use this
51

52
00:02:47,170 --> 00:02:51,670
escape sequence that is backslash double quote.
52

53
00:02:51,700 --> 00:02:59,500
So now, if you want single quote, then you can use backslash single quote, no problem you can do that.
53

54
00:02:59,530 --> 00:03:01,110
So, let's run.
54

55
00:03:01,270 --> 00:03:01,620
Yes.
55

56
00:03:01,630 --> 00:03:07,290
You can see that it has printed with single quote or you can omit this.
56

57
00:03:07,360 --> 00:03:08,910
No problem with that.
57

58
00:03:08,920 --> 00:03:13,310
You can omit meet the backslash and you can do this also.
58

59
00:03:13,450 --> 00:03:14,170
Let's run.
59

60
00:03:14,200 --> 00:03:15,880
So, both are one and the same.  
60

61
00:03:15,880 --> 00:03:17,880
and it should work fine.
61

62
00:03:17,890 --> 00:03:18,150
All right.
62

63

So, before ending this video let me give you one assignment.

So, remember that the output should exactly look something like this. And you should maintain all the

message. So, you can either use a single printf function or multiple prinft functions.

no problem with that.

Hey, welcome back to the lecture.So,

2
00:00:01,440 --> 00:00:08,290
now let's solve the previous assignment. So, now let me copy and paste the text, so let me paste here.

3
00:00:08,310 --> 00:00:16,560
Now, let me first use printf for each and every line. Let me write printf and open the bracket here,
3

4
00:00:17,160 --> 00:00:22,020
and let me close the bracket and give the semicolon.
4

5
00:00:22,020 --> 00:00:24,800
Let me complete this step for each and every line.
5

6
00:00:25,770 --> 00:00:28,050
Let me close here.
6

7
00:00:28,050 --> 00:00:29,160
That's fine.
7

8
00:00:29,160 --> 00:00:33,830
As you know in printf so you have to write your message in double quotes. Right?
8

9
00:00:33,920 --> 00:00:37,940
Let's open a double quote here and close a double quote here.
9

10
00:00:37,950 --> 00:00:45,280
Let's do that for each and every printf. So, here it is inserting two double quotes.
10

11
00:00:45,290 --> 00:00:47,140
So, let me remove one. Here
11

12
00:00:47,170 --> 00:00:50,730
so opening double quote and here it's a closing double quote.
12

13
00:00:50,740 --> 00:00:55,110
Now, let's resolve the issues associated with this printf. In the first line
13

14
00:00:55,150 --> 00:00:57,200
so we are printing double quote,
14

15
00:00:57,250 --> 00:01:01,760
so we have to use an escape character here,
15

16
00:01:01,810 --> 00:01:03,720
so that is backslash
16

17
00:01:03,720 --> 00:01:10,170
and here as well backslash and let's use \n to go to the new line.
17

18
00:01:10,300 --> 00:01:13,030
And after that, so here there is no problem,
18

19
00:01:13,060 --> 00:01:17,840
so here there is a double quote, let's use \n here as well.
19

20
00:01:18,560 --> 00:01:19,570
And after that,
20

21
00:01:19,700 --> 00:01:23,810
so here, you can see here that this is \M
21

22
00:01:23,810 --> 00:01:31,450
so this is considered as \M. So, \M is not a valid escape sequence, Right?
22

23
00:01:31,470 --> 00:01:34,070
So, it's not a valid escape sequence.
23

24
00:01:34,140 --> 00:01:42,930
So here what we want is, we want printf to considered this \M as normal characters.
24

25
00:01:42,930 --> 00:01:48,000
So, we don't want to consider \M as escape sequence.
25

26
00:01:48,030 --> 00:01:48,690
Right?
26

27
00:01:48,690 --> 00:01:51,740
Anyway it is not a valid escape sequence also.
27

28
00:01:51,750 --> 00:01:59,120
So, what we should do here is, we have to use one more backslash here. So, let's use a backslash here.
28

29
00:01:59,140 --> 00:02:01,260
So now, when you use this backslash,
29

30
00:02:01,260 --> 00:02:06,190
so this backslash is actually the escape character.
30

31
00:02:06,340 --> 00:02:11,020
So now, this backslash is considered as a normal character.
31

32
00:02:11,530 --> 00:02:19,640
So now it is no longer an escape character. So, in order to print a backslash you have to use this escape
32

33
00:02:19,640 --> 00:02:23,960
sequence. Let's use that here as well.
33

34
00:02:24,160 --> 00:02:29,950
And now at the end let's use \n and this string is fine, no problem.
34

35
00:02:30,010 --> 00:02:34,150
So, let's use \n here. And here we are printing backslash,
35

36
00:02:34,150 --> 00:02:42,130
so that's why, we have to use the escape character that is backslash for each and every backslash which
36

37
00:02:42,130 --> 00:02:46,490
we want to print and at the end \n.
37

38
00:02:46,720 --> 00:02:52,160
This is a triple quoted string and here there are multiple double quotes,
38

39
00:02:52,270 --> 00:03:01,530
so we have to use backslash for every double quote here. And after that at the end \n.
39

40
00:03:01,540 --> 00:03:03,690
So, now this resolves all the issues.
40

41
00:03:04,240 --> 00:03:10,960
So now let's run this program. So, here it is. So, we actually got the output.

42
00:03:11,140 --> 00:03:13,120
So, this is a correct output,

so we got exactly the way we wanted.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,290
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,290 --> 00:00:08,130
So, in this lecture let's learn about Commenting. So, generally comments are used to provide the description
2

3
00:00:08,190 --> 00:00:11,060
or documentation about the code you have written.
3

4
00:00:11,580 --> 00:00:15,440
So, through commenting you can mention important note,
4

5
00:00:15,450 --> 00:00:23,040
briefly explain the behavior of the code, and other useful details. Comments help the developer understand
5

6
00:00:23,040 --> 00:00:25,430
the logic/algorithm of the code
6

7
00:00:25,440 --> 00:00:29,660
if he or she revisits the code after a long time.
7

8
00:00:29,750 --> 00:00:33,770
So, commenting your code is really important.
8

9
00:00:33,800 --> 00:00:40,310
So now, you can mention the comments anywhere you want in the program and your comments will be ignored
9

10
00:00:40,340 --> 00:00:41,920
by the compiler.
10

11
00:00:42,050 --> 00:00:49,100
So, comments do not affect program or they do not consume any program memory space.
11

12
00:00:49,100 --> 00:00:52,820
So, they are not part of the final executable generated.
12

13
00:00:52,850 --> 00:00:56,060
So, it is there just for the documentation purpose.
13

14
00:00:56,120 --> 00:01:00,670
There are two types of commenting in 'C' programming language. So, one  
14

15
00:01:00,710 --> 00:01:07,190
is single line commenting and another one is multi line commenting. Single line comments can be provided
15

16
00:01:07,190 --> 00:01:10,620
by using  a two forward slashes.
16

17
00:01:10,820 --> 00:01:17,150
So, your comment should come after two forward slashes (//). In multi line commenting
17

18
00:01:17,150 --> 00:01:24,460
you have to write a comment between combination of a forward slash(/) and asterisk(*).
18

19
00:01:24,520 --> 00:01:31,730
Alright. So, this is an example for multi line commenting. Here you can see that, so the comment should
19

20
00:01:31,730 --> 00:01:37,060
start from forward slash  followed by an asterisk. And after that
20

21
00:01:37,130 --> 00:01:38,930
this is your comment,
21

22
00:01:39,200 --> 00:01:44,890
so whatever you want to write you can write here. So, you can see that it is a multi line commenting. So,
22

23
00:01:44,900 --> 00:01:52,160
multiple lines of comments are involved and then you end the commenting by using asterisk followed
23

24
00:01:52,160 --> 00:01:53,850
by the forward slash.
24

25
00:01:54,020 --> 00:01:58,340
So, that's what we call as multi line commenting. And in the single line commenting,
25

26
00:01:58,340 --> 00:02:04,610
so here we can see that all these are single line commenting where a first comment should begin with two
26

27
00:02:04,640 --> 00:02:06,040
forward slashes.
27

28
00:02:06,120 --> 00:02:09,980
Alright. So, now let's give some comments to our program, so here.
28

29
00:02:10,010 --> 00:02:14,100
So, first let's try the multiple line commenting.
29

30
00:02:14,150 --> 00:02:21,110
So, in order to start multiple line commenting so you can begin with forward slash followed by an asterisk
30

31
00:02:21,410 --> 00:02:23,720
and after that you can write whatever you want
31

32
00:02:23,720 --> 00:02:32,510
let's say, this is my first program. And after that you end the commenting by using asterisk followed
32

33
00:02:32,510 --> 00:02:34,680
by the forward slash.
33

34
00:02:34,760 --> 00:02:37,470
So, this is a comment. Here,
34

35
00:02:37,520 --> 00:02:44,190
if you want to add one more line, then you can give enter and you can add one more comment.
35

36
00:02:44,480 --> 00:02:51,200
So, this is comment line 1, and after that you can add one more line
36

37
00:02:51,200 --> 00:03:02,340
this is comment line 2, like that. So, you can see that all these lines are in between these markers.
37

38
00:03:02,340 --> 00:03:09,740
So, this is what we call as multi line commenting. So, we can convert all these things into single line
38

39
00:03:09,740 --> 00:03:11,490
commenting, so for a single line commenting
39

40
00:03:11,510 --> 00:03:17,540
you have to use double forward slashes for each line, like this.
40

41
00:03:17,600 --> 00:03:22,320
So, this is actually the single line commenting. So, remember that this is not allowed.
41

42
00:03:22,580 --> 00:03:24,080
So, you cannot do like this.
42

43
00:03:24,080 --> 00:03:25,320
This is not allowed.
43

44
00:03:25,340 --> 00:03:33,300
So, this is used only for single line commenting like that. So, that's about a multi line commenting and single
44

45
00:03:33,300 --> 00:03:35,300
line commenting. In the next lecture
45

46
00:03:35,300 --> 00:03:39,020
let's explore about 'C' data types and variables. I will see you in the next lecture. 


==================================================================================

section 4---data types and variables

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,290
Hey, welcome back to the lectures. So,
1

2
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:05,970
from this lecture onwards let's explore about 'C' data types and variables.
2

3
00:00:05,970 --> 00:00:08,320
Let's proceed.
3

4
00:00:08,440 --> 00:00:16,890
So, what are data types? So, data type is used for declaring the type of a variable.
4

5
00:00:17,140 --> 00:00:26,130
So, in 'C' programming, data types determine the type and size of data associated with variables.
5

6
00:00:26,330 --> 00:00:33,500
So, before storing any value in a variable, first programmer should decide its type.
6

7
00:00:33,760 --> 00:00:40,930
Let's explore what are the various data types are available in 'C' programming language. So, everything depends
7

8
00:00:40,930 --> 00:00:44,060
upon representing real world data.
8

9
00:00:44,590 --> 00:00:50,100
So, in day today's life we encounter different types of data. Right? So, data
9

10
00:00:50,100 --> 00:00:58,280
as numbers, data as characters, data as strings. So, strings are nothing but collection of characters.
10

11
00:00:58,300 --> 00:01:03,950
For example, your name is a type of data which is called as a string data.
11

12
00:01:04,510 --> 00:01:07,450
So, a data can be in the form of numbers.
12

13
00:01:07,570 --> 00:01:14,770
A number can be again integer or it can be a real number. So, let's see some examples.
13

14
00:01:14,810 --> 00:01:20,750
Let's say, Roy's age is  45 years, 6 months and 200 days.
14

15
00:01:20,750 --> 00:01:23,380
So here, what you are seeing is numbers, right?
15

16
00:01:23,390 --> 00:01:28,990
So here, data is nothing but number. Like 44, 6, and 200 are numbers
16

17
00:01:29,150 --> 00:01:34,030
and those numbers we have to use in our program in order to manipulate the data.
17

18
00:01:34,130 --> 00:01:35,320
Right?
18

19
00:01:35,390 --> 00:01:43,100
For example, the temperature of the city X is 27.2 degrees. Here, 27.2
19

20
00:01:43,100 --> 00:01:48,130
is a real number and the X is a character.
20

21
00:01:48,350 --> 00:01:54,730
Right? And let's say, I got an A grade in school assignment.
21

22
00:01:54,870 --> 00:01:57,080
So, here the data is grade.
22

23
00:01:57,090 --> 00:01:58,430
That is A.
23

24
00:01:58,490 --> 00:02:00,700
So, which is a character.
24

25
00:02:00,780 --> 00:02:06,330
So, all these are different types of data which are available in real world.
25

26
00:02:06,390 --> 00:02:14,100
And we'll see how to represent them in the program. So, based on the real world data we have two significant
26

27
00:02:14,100 --> 00:02:16,020
data types in 'C'.
27

28
00:02:16,260 --> 00:02:17,100
So, what are those?
28

29
00:02:17,100 --> 00:02:20,090
Let's explore 'C' data types.
29

30
00:02:20,130 --> 00:02:25,250
One is integer data types and another one is float data types.
30

31
00:02:25,370 --> 00:02:29,860
So, integer data types are used to represent whole numbers.
31

32
00:02:29,940 --> 00:02:37,710
For example 10, 20, 30, etc.. So, float data types are used to represent real numbers. For example, temperature
32

33
00:02:37,710 --> 00:02:40,740
value which can be 24.5
33

34
00:02:40,800 --> 00:02:46,440
So, if a programmer wants to give more attention to the variation of temperature, then he has to consider
34

35
00:02:46,500 --> 00:02:49,560
a temperature value as a real number.
35

36
00:02:49,560 --> 00:02:53,210
So, we'll discuss about float data types later.So,
36

37
00:02:53,220 --> 00:03:02,250
first let's discuss about integer data types.So, integer data are represented by different integer
37

38
00:03:02,500 --> 00:03:04,280
data types.
38

39
00:03:04,300 --> 00:03:09,080
So now, first let's discuss integer data types for signed data.
39

40
00:03:09,430 --> 00:03:15,640
The real world data can be sometimes it may be signed or sometimes it may be unsigned.
40

41
00:03:15,640 --> 00:03:17,260
Let's see some examples.
41

42
00:03:17,260 --> 00:03:23,210
Let's take, for example temperature value. A temperature can be negative sometimes
42

43
00:03:23,260 --> 00:03:25,830
or it can be positive sometimes.
43

44
00:03:25,870 --> 00:03:33,700
So, that's why, a temperature value is considered as a signed data. Because, that data can be positive or
44

45
00:03:33,700 --> 00:03:34,390
negative.
45

46
00:03:34,390 --> 00:03:34,630
Right?
46

47
00:03:35,680 --> 00:03:44,170
For example, consider distance between point A to Point B. So, a distance value cannot be negative.
47

48
00:03:44,290 --> 00:03:44,700
Right?
48

49
00:03:45,280 --> 00:03:47,080
So, it will always be positive.
49

50
00:03:47,710 --> 00:03:51,600
So, that type of data is called as unsigned data.
50

51
00:03:51,710 --> 00:04:02,210
All right. So, integer data types for signed data are char, short int, int, long int, and long long int. So, all these
51

52
00:04:02,210 --> 00:04:10,400
are different types of integer data types available in 'C'. So, remember that instead of calling short int
52

53
00:04:10,430 --> 00:04:13,250
so you can also call it as just short.
53

54
00:04:13,460 --> 00:04:19,920
So, if you just call it as short, then it is assumed that you are referring to short int.
54

55
00:04:19,970 --> 00:04:23,210
So, you need not to write or mention long int.
55

56
00:04:23,270 --> 00:04:30,440
So, you can just mention it as long. A long is always assumed as long int and  same way
56

57
00:04:30,620 --> 00:04:33,180
you need not to mention long long int.
57

58
00:04:33,240 --> 00:04:39,510
So, you can just mention long long. And integer data types for unsigned data.
58

59
00:04:39,540 --> 00:04:46,840
So, if your data is unsigned, then you can use these integer data types. Everything is same,
59

60
00:04:46,860 --> 00:04:52,870
so what you have to do is, you have to use the keyword unsigned before the data type name.
60

61
00:04:53,040 --> 00:05:00,140
So, like unsigned char, unsigned short, unsigned int, etc. So, you just have to use the word unsigned.
61

62
00:05:00,210 --> 00:05:08,250
So, now in the next lecture let's see storage sizes of different data types and value ranges.
62

63
00:05:08,250 --> 00:05:09,390
I'll see you in the next lecture.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,200
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,200 --> 00:00:09,400
So, in this lecture let's explore about a 'C' integer data types, their storage sizes and value ranges.
2

3
00:00:09,420 --> 00:00:20,640
So now remember that, signed char data type consumes one byte of memory and it's range is -128 to 127.
3

4
00:00:20,670 --> 00:00:30,120
So, strictly speaking a variable of type signed char uses 1 byte of memory and it can hold the value
4

5
00:00:30,330 --> 00:00:34,590
in between -128 to 127.
5

6
00:00:34,790 --> 00:00:40,890
So, a variable of type unsigned char also consumes 1 byte of memory.
6

7
00:00:41,130 --> 00:00:44,750
So, and it's range is 0 to 255.
7

8
00:00:44,760 --> 00:00:53,130
That means, that variable can store the value between 0 to 255. And the size of the short int type variable
8

9
00:00:53,160 --> 00:00:54,620
is 2 bytes,
9

10
00:00:54,620 --> 00:00:59,180
and this is a range, and unsigned short is also 2 bytes.
10

11
00:00:59,370 --> 00:01:09,360
So, int consumes 4 bytes actually. And unsigned int also 4 bytes, long consumes 8 bytes, and long long
11

12
00:01:09,360 --> 00:01:11,950
consumes 8 bytes.
12

13
00:01:12,100 --> 00:01:19,860
So, what is the meaning of that memory size table?  For example, let's consider GCC compiler.
13

14
00:01:20,140 --> 00:01:29,500
So, the compiler will generate the code to allocate 64 bits for each long long variable.
14

15
00:01:29,620 --> 00:01:39,830
So now let's move forward. 'C' standard doesn't talk about these memory sizes. So, these memory sizes are fixed
15

16
00:01:39,950 --> 00:01:48,440
by the compiler designer. For example, on some compilers a variable of type int may consume 2 bytes and
16

17
00:01:48,440 --> 00:01:51,970
on some compilers the size may be 4 bytes.
17

18
00:01:52,040 --> 00:02:00,170
Now the same is true for long. Some compilers consider size of the long as 4 bytes, and some compilers
18

19
00:02:00,170 --> 00:02:01,990
consider it as 8 bytes.
19

20
00:02:02,000 --> 00:02:10,050
This all depends on the compiler. So, everything depends upon the way compiler is designed.
20

21
00:02:10,340 --> 00:02:18,830
For example, there is one compiler called XC8. So, XC8 is a cross compiler which is used to generate
21

22
00:02:18,920 --> 00:02:27,710
code for PIC 8 bit microcontrollers. And I got this table from the reference manual of that compiler.
22

23
00:02:28,810 --> 00:02:35,740
So, in order to know the memory size of different data types you should consult a  
23

24
00:02:35,770 --> 00:02:36,730
reference manual  
24

25
00:02:36,730 --> 00:02:43,290
of the compiler.So, for every compiler the designer will give the reference   manual. And here you can see  
25

26
00:02:43,290 --> 00:02:46,230
that, the size of the int is actually 
26

27
00:02:46,230 --> 00:02:54,970
two bytes. Right?16 bits. And size of the long is you can see that it is 32 bits or 4 bytes. And
27

28
00:02:55,060 --> 00:03:04,020
this compiler also includes some non-standard a data types such as short long and other things. So,
28

29
00:03:04,030 --> 00:03:04,790
short long is actually 
29

30
00:03:04,790 --> 00:03:11,320
24 bits, so this is not actually from the 'C' standard, it maybe customization added by the compiler 
30

31
00:03:11,350 --> 00:03:14,390
designers of   XC8, which is designed by microchip.
31

32
00:03:14,470 --> 00:03:22,120
So now,  armcc is another cross compiler developed by arm for 32 bit ARM based 
32

33
00:03:22,120 --> 00:03:27,980
microcontrollers. And this is actually not an open source compiler and this is from the documentation 
33

34
00:03:28,000 --> 00:03:32,970
of armcc and here we can see that char is again 1 byte, you know
34

35
00:03:32,980 --> 00:03:33,860
short is 2 bytes
35

36
00:03:33,880 --> 00:03:40,300
that's correct and int is actually 4 bytes here, and long is 4 bytes, and long long is 8 bytes.
36

37
00:03:40,540 --> 00:03:47,530
So, why I'm showing all these tables is because, just don't take a memory sizes for granted 
37

38
00:03:47,710 --> 00:03:48,310
for different  
38

39
00:03:48,310 --> 00:03:55,640
data types.You have to refer to the reference manual of the compiler. So, there are some points I would
39

40
00:03:55,640 --> 00:04:01,950
like to mention.So remember that, 'C' standard does not fix the storage sizes of different data 
40

41
00:04:02,120 --> 00:04:05,920
types.It only talks about minimum and maximum 
41

42
00:04:06,320 --> 00:04:15,350
values.For example 'C' standard says, the minimum storage size of a long type variable is 32 bits, 
42

43
00:04:15,350 --> 00:04:16,550
and the max is 
43

44
00:04:16,760 --> 00:04:23,780
64 bits. So, it talks in terms of max and min. So, exact size of the long type variable 
44

45
00:04:23,780 --> 00:04:32,320
depends on the compiler design. Some compilers fix 32 bits storage size for long type variables and 
45

46
00:04:32,320 --> 00:04:33,650
some compilers fix  
46

47
00:04:33,670 --> 00:04:40,900
64 bits. Same is true for int data type, some compilers fix 16 bits storage size for int type 
47

48
00:04:41,080 --> 00:04:43,780
variables  and some compilers fix 
48

49
00:04:44,080 --> 00:04:46,770
32 bits. So, this all depends on the 
49

50
00:04:47,380 --> 00:04:54,880
compilers.So, these data types will always be fixed size irrespective of the compilers. Short is 
50

51
00:04:55,000 --> 00:05:02,320
always  2 bytes, char is always 1 byte, and long long is always 8 bytes. So, that is guaranteed. Whether 
51

52
00:05:02,320 --> 00:05:06,100
you are using cross compiler for embedded systems or 
52

53
00:05:06,100 --> 00:05:11,810
non cross compiler it doesn't matter. Short is always 2 bytes and the char is always
53

54
00:05:11,830 --> 00:05:18,220
1 byte.So, in the next lecture let's understand integer data type one by one and let's start with 
54

55
00:05:18,220 --> 00:05:19,230
char. And I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,450 --> 00:00:04,560
In this lecture, let's discuss about one of the integer data type that is char.
1

2
00:00:05,190 --> 00:00:13,110
So now remember that, this is an integer data type to store a single character value or ASCII code value
2

3
00:00:13,320 --> 00:00:22,600
or 1 byte of signed integer value (+ve or -ve value). So, a  char data type variable consumes
3

4
00:00:22,600 --> 00:00:30,040
1 byte of memory, so that you already know. And char happens to be the smallest integer data type of  1
4

5
00:00:30,040 --> 00:00:38,530
byte in 'C'. So, there is no other special meaning for the char data type, and it is just another integer
5

6
00:00:38,530 --> 00:00:41,710
data type. So, the name is used as char, 
6

7
00:00:41,730 --> 00:00:49,390
that's because the char data types are majorly used to store the characters, that is  ASCII codes of the
7

8
00:00:49,390 --> 00:00:50,240
characters.
8

9
00:00:50,290 --> 00:00:53,720
So, that's why, the name is given as char.
9

10
00:00:53,740 --> 00:00:59,560
Apart from that there is no special meaning for that and it happens to be the smallest integer data type
10

11
00:00:59,680 --> 00:01:01,970
of one byte.
11

12
00:01:02,010 --> 00:01:11,700
So, what is the range of  char data type?  So, char range is -128 to 127. A char data type will be used to store
12

13
00:01:11,700 --> 00:01:14,040
1 byte of signed data.
13

14
00:01:14,040 --> 00:01:20,100
For example, a temperature value if it is negative some times or if it is positive sometimes, then it
14

15
00:01:20,100 --> 00:01:27,870
is called as a signed data and you can use char data type to store the temperature value.
15

16
00:01:28,230 --> 00:01:33,240
So now, unsigned  char range is from 0 to 255.
16

17
00:01:33,330 --> 00:01:38,040
Unsigned char data type will be used to store 1 byte of unsigned data.
17

18
00:01:38,040 --> 00:01:45,180
For example, if the distance between A and B is 100 kilometers, then we can use a variable of type
18

19
00:01:45,210 --> 00:01:48,180
unsigned char to store the unsigned data.
19

20
00:01:48,570 --> 00:01:52,740
Let's move forward. So, let's see an example.
20

21
00:01:52,740 --> 00:01:58,650
So, let's say, I want to store the current temperature data of city 'X' in my program.
21

22
00:01:59,100 --> 00:02:08,150
So, city X's today's temperature is let's say 25 degrees Celsius. So, I'm sure that X's temperature never goes
22

23
00:02:08,150 --> 00:02:11,950
below 0 degree and never goes above 40 degrees.
23

24
00:02:12,170 --> 00:02:21,500
So, that means city X's temperature will always be a positive value. So, that is temperature of X, let's say
24

25
00:02:21,500 --> 00:02:26,750
40. So, the temperature value will always be a positive value.
25

26
00:02:26,820 --> 00:02:27,400
Right?
26

27
00:02:27,420 --> 00:02:30,330
and it will be less than 40 let's say.
27

28
00:02:30,480 --> 00:02:39,040
So, here we can use unsigned char data type to represent temperature value. So, something like this. So,
28

29
00:02:39,040 --> 00:02:41,750
here this is called as a variable
29

30
00:02:41,860 --> 00:02:47,380
and this is a data type, and the value 25 is stored in the variable.
30

31
00:02:47,910 --> 00:02:50,380
So here, cityXTemperature.
31

32
00:02:50,980 --> 00:02:58,180
So, this is a variable of type unsigned char to hold the integer value 25.
32

33
00:02:58,300 --> 00:03:00,150
So, here we are talking about integers. 
33

34
00:03:00,190 --> 00:03:02,690
You cannot store 25.5.
34

35
00:03:02,720 --> 00:03:03,060
Why?
35

36
00:03:03,070 --> 00:03:04,410
Because, that's a real number.
36

37
00:03:05,180 --> 00:03:08,350
So, for real numbers we have separate data types.
37

38
00:03:08,350 --> 00:03:10,650
So, those are float data types
38

39
00:03:10,690 --> 00:03:12,800
and about that will see later.
39

40
00:03:12,820 --> 00:03:17,050
So, all discussions are actually of integer values.
40

41
00:03:17,110 --> 00:03:19,630
So, these are integer data  types.
41

42
00:03:19,630 --> 00:03:27,520
So now, that leads to our next discussion that is variable definition. So, variable definition always includes
42

43
00:03:27,610 --> 00:03:31,840
data type name followed by variable name.
43

44
00:03:31,850 --> 00:03:37,140
So, this is actually an example of variable definition.
44

45
00:03:37,150 --> 00:03:44,860
So, here we can see that first data type is written. This is a data type which is given by your 'C' standard.
45

46
00:03:44,860 --> 00:03:47,840
So, this is a standard keyword unsigned char.
46

47
00:03:47,860 --> 00:03:50,050
So, you cannot use something else.
47

48
00:03:50,080 --> 00:03:54,860
It has to be unsigned char or char, and this is a variable name.
48

49
00:03:54,860 --> 00:04:00,680
So, this you can use whatever you want. But there are some ruleses to write the variable name, so that will
49

50
00:04:00,680 --> 00:04:06,380
see later. So, a variable definition always includes two part.
50

51
00:04:06,400 --> 00:04:13,920
One is data type and second one is variable name, and variable name should be followed by data
51

52
00:04:13,950 --> 00:04:14,980
type.
52

53
00:04:15,110 --> 00:04:19,960
So before using a variable you should always define it using appropriate data type.
53

54
00:04:20,010 --> 00:04:25,160
You can select the data type according to your program logic and need.
54

55
00:04:25,170 --> 00:04:27,450
So here, the first line is variable definition
55

56
00:04:27,450 --> 00:04:35,060
and the second line is variable initialization, where we are storing value 25 to the variable. And we will
56

57
00:04:35,070 --> 00:04:39,120
discuss more on variable and its memory consumption etc later.
57

58
00:04:39,210 --> 00:04:41,370
And let's move forward.
58

59
00:04:41,370 --> 00:04:41,630
All right.
59

60
00:04:41,640 --> 00:04:43,320
Now, let's see another example.
60

61
00:04:43,350 --> 00:04:47,740
So, let's say storing sun's temperature value in a program.
61

62
00:04:47,940 --> 00:04:53,200
So, sun's surface temperature is 5,505 degree Celsius.
62

63
00:04:53,340 --> 00:04:55,560
So, we can consider it as unsigned data.
63

64
00:04:55,740 --> 00:04:59,610
So, if we assume sun's temperature never goes negative.
64

65
00:05:00,450 --> 00:05:07,350
So, 5505 is greater than 255. So, we cannot use unsigned char. Because, it's range is
65

66
00:05:07,350 --> 00:05:09,420
from 0 to 255.
66

67
00:05:09,450 --> 00:05:10,210
Right?
67

68
00:05:10,230 --> 00:05:16,990
So, in this case we can safely use the unsigned short int data type like below.
68

69
00:05:17,040 --> 00:05:22,200
So, unsigned  short so that's the data type and sunTemperature
69

70
00:05:22,200 --> 00:05:26,400
that's a variable name to hold the value sun's temperature.
70

71
00:05:26,460 --> 00:05:29,100
That is 5505.
71

72
00:05:29,100 --> 00:05:32,490
So, that's about variable definition.
72

73
00:05:32,490 --> 00:05:34,890
And in the next lecture, let's see some examples
73

74
00:05:34,890 --> 00:05:38,940
and after that we'll discuss other integer data types and I'll see you in the next lecture.

9
00:00:33,080 --> 00:00:38,390

9

10
00:00:38,410 --> 00:00:44,750
8 bits the most significant bit will be used to represent the sign of the

11
00:00:45,070 --> 00:00:49,130
data.If it is 0, then the data is considered as 
11

12
00:00:49,330 --> 00:00:56,470

13

00:01:06,050 --> 00:01:17,120
format if data is negative. Signed data means a dedicated 1 bit that is this bit is used to

00:01:17,810 --> 00:01:19,520

-25.

00:01:32,440 --> 00:01:38,540
magnitude.So, sign is negative and magnitude is 25. Since the data

24
00:01:38,540 --> 00:01:41,820
is negative, magnitude will be stored in 2's 
24

25
00:01:41,930 --> 00:01:43,550
complement form.So, you have to
25

26

remember that.So, out of 8 bits the 
26

00:01:46,670 --> 00:01:49,070
seventh bit,that is the most significant

bit will be 1 in this case. And the remaining 7 bits are used to store the magnitude that is 25 in

2's complement

30

form.Let's see how we can do that.

First of all, so the data

111.

So, now

75

00:04:28,760 --> 00:04:29,990

sign is positive,so that's why, the most significant bit will be

0.And since the data is positive you need not to convert the magnitude into 2's 
79

80

complement form. So, you just convert that into the binary format 
80

81

here.So, this is actually the binary format 
81

82
00:04:49,130 --> 00:04:53,930
of 25 and if I convert that into hex, so this will be 9 and

83

25.So, I hope you

forward. So now, based on that let's discuss the char data type

88
00:05:09,770 --> 00:05:14,960
range. So now, let's discuss when the sign is positive. When the
88

89

sign is positive,the seventh bit will be zero. 
89

90
00:05:18,020 --> 00:05:21,260

91
00:05:21,260 --> 00:05:21,590
all

92

zeroes.Right? So, that's why, magnitude

93

will be 0.That is a least possible magnitude. And the

94

94

95

96

Right?So, that's why, the highest positive value would be 127. When the sign is negative, seventh 
96

97

bit will be 1 and 1, the least value possible is -128. So, -128 means all magnitude bits are filled with 
97

98

98

99

possible is that is highest negative value possible is

100

100

101
00:06:07,670 --> 00:06:13,850
1.So, this is actually the 2's complement format for 
101

102
00:06:13,850 --> 00:06:19,700
-1. So, now we have here 2 ranges for positive values and negative

103
00:06:19,850 --> 00:06:24,540
values. Right? For positive values it can go from 0 to

104

105

105

106

of char data type is -128 to 
106

107

representation so no particular

115
00:07:14,310 --> 00:07:17,490

So, now in this lecture let's study about integer data type: short int and unsigned short int. So, variable of
2

3

type short int is used to store 2 bytes of signed data. Variable of type unsigned short int is used
3

4

to store 2 bytes of unsigned data. So, you can just mention short or unsigned short. So, int will
4

5

be always assumed. Short type variable always consumes 2 bytes of memory irrespective of compilers.
5

6
00:00:34,140 --> 00:00:42,400
So you should remember that. So, you go to any compiler  size of short, int will always be 2 bytes.
6

7
00:00:42,410 --> 00:00:45,810
So now, let's do the range calculation of short int.
7

8
00:00:45,870 --> 00:00:53,170
Let's take for example, represent the data -25 in 2 byte signed data representation.
8

9
00:00:53,510 --> 00:00:55,880
So, here the data is negative.
9

10
00:00:55,940 --> 00:00:56,940
Right?
10

11
00:00:57,050 --> 00:01:04,470
So, now first let's take the binary at presentation, that is pure binary representation of 25
11

12
00:01:04,820 --> 00:01:07,600
in 2 byte a data representation.
12

13
00:01:07,640 --> 00:01:11,480
So, that will be this one. And after that take the 1's complement
13

14
00:01:11,490 --> 00:01:14,160
so this is a 1's complement format of 25,
14

15
00:01:14,160 --> 00:01:21,410
where you have to convert all ones into zeros and zeros into ones. And after that let's take 2's complement
15

16
00:01:21,410 --> 00:01:22,140
of that.
16

17
00:01:22,190 --> 00:01:26,240
So, for that you have to add one to 1's complement format,
17

18
00:01:26,360 --> 00:01:28,390
you get 2's complement format.
18

19
00:01:28,790 --> 00:01:34,750
So, this is the representation for -25.
19

20
00:01:35,470 --> 00:01:43,630
So, where you can see that, the 15th bit that is the last bit most significant bit will be 1 to indicate
20

21
00:01:43,780 --> 00:01:51,370
the data is negative and the rest of the bits will be used to store the 2's complement format of
21

22
00:01:51,490 --> 00:01:59,330
the magnitude that is 25. So, the hex value will be 0xFFE7.
22

23
00:02:00,060 --> 00:02:02,880
So, that is actually the 2 byte signed
23

24
00:02:02,910 --> 00:02:05,980
data representation of -25.

25

Let's move forward. So, now represent the data +25 in 2 byte signed data representation.
25

26
00:02:13,950 --> 00:02:18,880
So, that will be 0x0019 in hex.
26

27

So, I hope you can understand this.

28

Let's see the range for short
28

29

it will be from -32,768 to 32,767. And for unsigned short
29

30

the range will be 0 to 65535. Because, for unsigned data representation in 2 bytes you
30

31

have all the 16 bits to represent the magnitude.

32

So, there is no sign bit
32

33
00:02:45,360 --> 00:02:48,860
So, that's why, all 16 bits will be used to store the magnitude.
33

34

So, least value will be 0 and the highest value will be sixty 65535
34

35

that is nothing but 0xFFFF in hex.

1

integer data,

just like short int. But the size of the int is greater than short

or it may be equal to short. So, an int type variable consumes 2 bytes of memory or 4 bytes of
4

5
00:00:22,230 --> 00:00:22,670
memory.
5

00:00:22,860 --> 00:00:25,230

7

8
00:00:32,190 --> 00:00:33,150
hardware.
8

9
00:00:33,150 --> 00:00:38,350

or 8 bytes of memory. So, that is very important.

15
00:01:11,560 --> 00:01:11,980
hardware.
15

16
00:01:12,850 --> 00:01:15,700
So, it is typically 4 or 8 bytes.

00:00:59,560 --> 00:01:08,890
If I use one keyword called 'extern', so 'extern' is a standard keyword this is a reserved keyword of 'C' standard.

16
00:01:08,890 --> 00:01:16,210
So now, if I try to compile this program you can see that there is an error, the error is undefined

reference made to this variable.

So, this is called as variable declaration.

So here why it is called as variable declaration, because when compiler sees this so it will not allocate
19

20

any storage for this variable.

21
00:01:33,010 --> 00:01:41,200
So here, you are telling the compiler by this keyword that, this variable is defined somewhere in other
21

file of the project. That is outside of this main.c file.

23
00:01:47,110 --> 00:01:51,340
So, that is the meaning of using extern keyword.

24
00:01:51,340 --> 00:01:54,880
So, that's why, compiler will not allocate any storage for this.

25
00:01:54,880 --> 00:01:58,470
So, it will assume that it is defined somewhere else in the program.So,
25

26
00:01:58,670 --> 00:02:06,580
but when it tries to generate instructions to put this value into the memory location in the computer
26

27
00:02:06,580 --> 00:02:14,590
memory, so it will have problem. Because, you have not defined that variable.

28

And so that's why, finally the compiler says that undefined reference to this variable.

00:02:20,510 --> 00:02:26,590
So, basically compiler is saying that it couldn't able to find the reference to that variable.

You said that it is extern, but compiler couldn't able to find that.

00:02:31,890 --> 00:02:36,230
So, that's a difference between variable definition and declaration, so this is a declaration

00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:08,790
So, in this lecture let's understand about variable scopes. So, variables have scopes. A variable scope
1

2
00:00:08,970 --> 00:00:16,590
refers to the accessibility of a variable in a given program or function.
2

3
00:00:16,590 --> 00:00:22,280
For example, a variable may only be available within a specific function,
3

4
00:00:22,350 --> 00:00:26,070
or it may be available to the entire 'C' program.
4

5
00:00:26,620 --> 00:00:28,610
So, depending upon the scope
5

6
00:00:28,740 --> 00:00:30,900
we have 2 types of variables.
6

7
00:00:30,930 --> 00:00:33,360
One is local scope variables.
7

8
00:00:33,360 --> 00:00:35,490
So, in short it is also called as
8

9
00:00:35,550 --> 00:00:42,100
local variables. And global scope variables which are also call it as global variables.
9

10
00:00:42,120 --> 00:00:46,090
And let's understand more about variable scopes with some examples.

Alright. Now, first let's understand about local scope variables.

So, here I have a file main.c and I have a function main and it is actually an empty main function,

and let's create a variable inside the main function.

So now let me create a variable. So here, this is a variable myScore which is actually called as a local
14

15

variable. Because, this variable exists only in this function. So, its scope is limited to only this function.
15

16
00:01:19,110 --> 00:01:22,910
So, that's why, this variable is also called as local scope variable.

17

So now, let me create another function.

So now let me give the function name as let's say my function1. After writing the function name

So, here this is start of the function body and this is end of the function body.

That's great.

So, this is called as a function call.

00:02:13,370 --> 00:02:16,630
So, this is a function call.

29
00:02:17,410 --> 00:02:20,400
So, this is a function definition.

So, don't worry about that.

myFun1. And now what I'm going to do is, I'm going to assign some value to the my score here.

And again I'm going to print here, value of the variable myScore

and let me give 002 in order to trace the execution.

Let me try to compile and here it is.

00:03:58,820 --> 00:04:02,890
So, you actually met with an error here that is saying undeclared.

So, here you can see that the error is at the line thirty first.

52

That is right here.

So, here the compiler is saying that so this is undeclared or undefined. Because, you are trying to change
53

54

this variable which is actually local to this function.
54

55
00:04:19,650 --> 00:04:27,370
So, its scope is just limited to these brackets. So, you cannot access them from some other function.
55

56
00:04:27,510 --> 00:04:31,590
So, that's why, these variables are called as local variables.

57
00:04:31,680 --> 00:04:37,400
You can access them anywhere you want inside this function body, but not outside of that.
57

58
00:04:37,410 --> 00:04:44,820
So, what we can do is, in order to solve this issue if you really want to access this variable from some
58

59

60

that means move this variable from local scope to the global scope.

61

62

64

Remember that, so when the variable names are same between local space and global space.
88

89
00:06:58,550 --> 00:07:02,510
The first preference is always given to the local space.
89

90
00:07:02,540 --> 00:07:09,020
So, that's why, here valueA is referring to this variable definition, so not this.
90

91
00:07:09,080 --> 00:07:16,160
So, that's why, valueA will be 67, because the first preference is given to the local space, if the variable
91

92
00:07:16,160 --> 00:07:17,650
names are same.
92

93
00:07:17,660 --> 00:07:20,790
So now, this is absolutely illegal.
93

94
00:07:20,930 --> 00:07:24,500
So, you cannot define another variable of same name.
94

95
00:07:24,500 --> 00:07:26,590
So, this is actually legal.
95

96
00:07:26,720 --> 00:07:30,750
So, it throws error saying conflicting types for valueA.
96

97
00:07:31,460 --> 00:07:33,140
Let me remove this.
97

98
00:07:33,170 --> 00:07:36,150
So now, let me just modify this code.
98

99
00:07:36,260 --> 00:07:38,040
Let me put brackets here.
99

100
00:07:38,090 --> 00:07:41,960
So, I will open a curly bracket here and I will close it here.
100

101
00:07:42,470 --> 00:07:48,150
So, I just created a small execution body by using curly brackets.
101

102
00:07:48,560 --> 00:07:52,680
So now, what do you think the output will be?
102

103
00:07:52,690 --> 00:07:55,550
So now, the output will be 4, not
103

104
00:07:55,560 --> 00:07:56,320
67.
104

105
00:07:56,710 --> 00:08:03,790
Let's see how. First let's run the code, so here we can see that valueA is equal to 4. So now, what's
105

106
00:08:03,800 --> 00:08:09,470
happening here? Let's analyze. Here, we can see that this is actually a local variable which is bound
106

107
00:08:09,470 --> 00:08:11,940
to the scope of this area.
107

108
00:08:11,990 --> 00:08:14,450
So, this is a small execution body here.
108

109
00:08:14,630 --> 00:08:19,460
So, the execution body starts at this bracket and it ends  over here.
109

110
00:08:20,060 --> 00:08:23,720
So, that's why, the scope of this variable is bound to this area.
110

111
00:08:23,750 --> 00:08:28,110
So, the moment execution control goes out of this scope,
111

112
00:08:28,160 --> 00:08:30,740
so this variable is no longer valid.
112

113
00:08:31,130 --> 00:08:31,800
So, that's why,
113

114
00:08:31,970 --> 00:08:36,120
so the moment a control comes to this printf statement
114

115
00:08:36,140 --> 00:08:37,480
so this is no longer there.
115

116
00:08:37,740 --> 00:08:40,650
So, this variable is already died.
116

117
00:08:40,820 --> 00:08:44,800
So, that's why, there is only one variable left. That is global1.
117

118
00:08:44,930 --> 00:08:45,230
Right?
118

119
00:08:45,560 --> 00:08:48,850
So, that's why, printf is printing the global variable value.
119

120
00:08:49,070 --> 00:08:55,850
So, always remember that when the execution control goes out of the scope of a local variable, the local
120

121
00:08:55,850 --> 00:08:56,950
variable dies
121

122
00:08:56,990 --> 00:09:00,800
that means a variable loses its existence.

so let's understand this statement.

00:01:35,680 --> 00:01:37,700
operator. So, equal to sign.

2
00:00:00,420 --> 00:00:04,320
So, now in the previous lecture we discussed about variable scopes.
2

3
00:00:04,320 --> 00:00:04,810
Let's see
3

4
00:00:04,810 --> 00:00:06,610
Summary of global variables.
4

5
00:00:07,050 --> 00:00:12,630
So in terms of scope, remember that, global variables are visible to all the functions of a program.
5

6
00:00:12,630 --> 00:00:18,630
They are everywhere. Even you can access global variables from another file of the project. After that
6

7
00:00:18,660 --> 00:00:26,490
default value. All uninitialized global variables will have 0 as default value. And life time.
7

8
00:00:26,580 --> 00:00:34,470
So, the lifetime of these variables are till the end of the execution of the program. Summary of local
8

9
00:00:34,470 --> 00:00:41,490
variables. So, scope would be within the body of the function.Local variables lose existence once the
9

10
00:00:41,490 --> 00:00:48,300
execution control comes out of the function body. Default value is unpredictable and that what we call
10

11
00:00:48,300 --> 00:00:56,530
as garbage value. And lifetime, till the end of the execution of a function in which a variable is defined.
11

12
00:00:56,550 --> 00:01:01,570
So, now in the next lecture let's discuss about 'C' storage classes.
12

13
00:01:01,680 --> 00:01:03,270
I'll see you in the next lecture.

section 5---Address of variables

2
00:00:01,950 --> 00:00:07,020
So, now in this lecture let's understand about address of variables.
2

3
00:00:07,020 --> 00:00:12,870
So, let's write a program to print the address of variables.
3

4
00:00:12,870 --> 00:00:16,980
Let's say we have a variable called myData.
4

5
00:00:17,020 --> 00:00:21,470
So now, if you write &myData
5

6
00:00:21,880 --> 00:00:30,330
so this actually gives you memory location address of myData or memory location address of the variable.
6

7
00:00:30,850 --> 00:00:37,150
So, all you need to do is you have to use ampersand(&) sign in front of the variable name.
7

8
00:00:37,690 --> 00:00:42,610
So, that actually gives you the memory location address of the variable.
8

9
00:00:42,740 --> 00:00:46,930
So now, let's print the addresses of all these variables.
9

10
00:00:46,930 --> 00:00:54,670
So, in order to print the address of a variable there is one a unique format specifier, that is 
10

11
00:00:54,670 --> 00:01:03,060
%p. So, you can use %p format specifier in order to print the memory location address.
11

12
00:01:03,070 --> 00:01:09,470
So now, let's use printf to print the address of variable a1.
12

13
00:01:09,580 --> 00:01:12,640
So, let's say address of variable
13

14
00:01:12,730 --> 00:01:18,700
a1 is equal to you can use the format specifier %p here.
14

15
00:01:18,700 --> 00:01:25,000
So, %p helps you to print the address of the variable in hexadecimal format.
15

16
00:01:25,030 --> 00:01:27,200
After that, you can give \n here
16

17
00:01:27,220 --> 00:01:34,720
and after that, in order to print the address you have to use the argument here &a1.
17

18
00:01:34,900 --> 00:01:37,000
Let's see what happens.
18

19
00:01:39,420 --> 00:01:40,130
Right?
19

20
00:01:40,200 --> 00:01:40,950
So, here it is.
20

21
00:01:40,950 --> 00:01:46,590
So, this is the address of the variable that is a1.
21

22
00:01:46,620 --> 00:01:48,690
So, the value 65
22

23
00:01:48,720 --> 00:01:53,760
that is the ASCII code for a is actually stored at the location
23

24
00:01:53,760 --> 00:01:54,960
this one.
24

25
00:01:55,070 --> 00:01:56,420
So, this is a memory address.
25

26
00:01:57,410 --> 00:02:05,420
So, basically what we can conclude from this experiment is that, so there is actually a memory location
26

27
00:02:05,510 --> 00:02:15,620
of 1 byte whose address is this one and the address size is 8 bytes. So because, that code is actually
27

28
00:02:15,620 --> 00:02:24,620
running on 64 bit machine. For 64 bit machine the size of the memory location address is 8 bytes.
28

29
00:02:24,710 --> 00:02:26,140
So, this is a address.
29

30
00:02:26,180 --> 00:02:30,980
So, we also call it as a pointer in 'C', because that points to the data.
30

31
00:02:31,010 --> 00:02:31,920
Right?
31

32
00:02:32,060 --> 00:02:40,780
And the data is stored at that memory location and the data happens to be ASCII code of 'A', which is  
32

33

0x41.

34
00:02:42,570 --> 00:02:44,900
Let's go back to the project.

35
00:02:45,080 --> 00:02:49,940
So now, let's print the addresses of other variables.

36

So now, I'm going to print the addresses of all the variables.

00:02:57,330 --> 00:02:58,760

00:02:58,770 --> 00:03:04,480
So, here you can see that these are actually consegative a memory location addresses.

Right?

41

So, 7c79 a b c d e.

42

Right?
42

43
00:03:10,860 --> 00:03:17,290
So, that means your data is actually stored in the consagetive memory location addresses.
