0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:00,660
All right.
1

2
00:00:00,750 --> 00:00:06,090
So, when you use &a1 it actually gives the pointer.
2

3
00:00:06,720 --> 00:00:11,340
So now, you can even store this value into a variable.
3

4
00:00:11,340 --> 00:00:18,750
For example, let's remove all these code for a time being you can even do this. So, you can even create
4

5
00:00:18,750 --> 00:00:22,840
a variable called unsigned long int.
5

6
00:00:22,950 --> 00:00:26,940
Let's call it as addressOfa1. Sorry
6

7
00:00:26,970 --> 00:00:36,680
int. So, I can even use this variable in order to store the address of variable a1.
7

8
00:00:36,760 --> 00:00:43,700
So, all a need to do is &a1. So now, if I do this
8

9
00:00:43,790 --> 00:00:46,770
let's see whether compiler is happy with this or not.
9

10
00:00:47,360 --> 00:00:55,690
So now, let's try to build the project. Here we can see that there are some warnings of unused variables.
10

11
00:00:55,960 --> 00:00:58,510
So, let's do one thing.So, let's comment it out
11

12
00:00:58,630 --> 00:01:04,380
all these variables. So, I commented all other variables
12

13
00:01:04,380 --> 00:01:08,370
let me just build the project, and there is one important warning
13

14
00:01:08,400 --> 00:01:09,900
let's read that.
14

15
00:01:09,900 --> 00:01:20,670
So, the warning says that initialization of 'long unsigned int' from 'char *' makes integer from pointer
15

16
00:01:20,880 --> 00:01:28,370
without a cast.  So basically, what's happening here is that, you should remember that this is not a number.
16

17
00:01:29,420 --> 00:01:32,150
The compiler sees it as a pointer.
17

18
00:01:32,780 --> 00:01:42,810
So, there is a difference between a number and a pointer in 'C'. So, you are trying to assign this entity
18

19
00:01:42,990 --> 00:01:44,520
to a variable.
19

20
00:01:44,520 --> 00:01:45,290
Right?
20

21
00:01:45,330 --> 00:01:54,750
So, this actually represent a variable. So, here the data type of this one is unsigned long int.
21

22
00:01:54,810 --> 00:02:01,380
So, we used unsigned long int because, unsigned long is 8 bytes. So, address size is 8 bytes.
22

23
00:02:01,380 --> 00:02:07,570
So, that's why, I have chosen unsigned long int. So, that's about left hand side.
23

24
00:02:07,670 --> 00:02:09,620
So now let's take a look into the right hand side.
24

25
00:02:10,220 --> 00:02:21,170
So, this is actually a pointer data.So, pointer data are represented by pointer data type in 'C'. So, the
25

26
00:02:21,170 --> 00:02:30,200
type here at the LHS is unsigned long int and the type at the RHS is char*. So, will understand
26

27
00:02:30,200 --> 00:02:34,890
more about pointer data type when we explore more about pointers.
27

28
00:02:34,910 --> 00:02:37,240
So, we have not yet explored that.
28

29
00:02:37,370 --> 00:02:40,660
So, just I'm explaining the meaning of the warning.
29

30
00:02:40,870 --> 00:02:47,450
So, the warning is because you are trying to take an entity of different data type and you are trying
30

31
00:02:47,450 --> 00:02:51,960
to assign that to a variable of entirely different data type.
31

32
00:02:52,130 --> 00:02:55,770
So, that's why, there is a data type mismatch.
32

33
00:02:55,850 --> 00:02:59,270
So, this is actually not just a number
33

34
00:02:59,270 --> 00:03:02,730
this is a pointer, which belongs to pointer data type.
34

35
00:03:03,020 --> 00:03:06,390
And the pointer data type happens to be char*
35

36
00:03:06,470 --> 00:03:07,440
in this case.
36

37
00:03:07,440 --> 00:03:12,030
So, when we cover a pointers so you will understand more about that.
37

38
00:03:12,050 --> 00:03:18,080
So, here we are trying to assign a pointer data type into a variable.Hence
38

39
00:03:18,080 --> 00:03:20,050
there is a data type mismatch
39

40
00:03:20,340 --> 00:03:22,230
warning. So, to solve this issue,
40

41
00:03:22,250 --> 00:03:28,130
convert pointer data type into variable data type using typecasting.
41

42
00:03:28,820 --> 00:03:31,880
So, about type casting we understand later
42

43
00:03:31,880 --> 00:03:38,210
and basically if you want to solve this issue, convert pointer data type into variable data type using
43

44
00:03:38,210 --> 00:03:39,050
typecasting.
44

45
00:03:39,080 --> 00:03:42,570
So, here what you are seeing is typecasting.
45

46
00:03:42,620 --> 00:03:46,400
So, this is what we call as type casting.
46

47
00:03:46,400 --> 00:03:52,610
So, we are converting this entity into unsigned long int type.
47

48
00:03:52,610 --> 00:03:56,010
So now, this whole thing is now a number.
48

49
00:03:56,100 --> 00:03:56,990
It is just a number.
49

50
00:03:57,110 --> 00:03:58,750
So now there is no pointer.
50

51
00:03:58,790 --> 00:04:06,180
So, we converted pointer into a number of unsigned long int. Let's see how compiler responsed to that.
51

52
00:04:06,180 --> 00:04:06,490
All right.
52

53
00:04:06,510 --> 00:04:16,510
So, let's do the typecasting, so just to write the parentheses and give this data type name, and after
53

54
00:04:16,510 --> 00:04:23,520
that let's print this value just like an integer.So now, let's
54

55
00:04:23,560 --> 00:04:25,960
copy this and let me print here
55

56
00:04:25,960 --> 00:04:29,380
just like an integer for that we use %d.
56

57
00:04:29,410 --> 00:04:29,740
Right?
57

58
00:04:30,100 --> 00:04:36,430
But remember that %d for integer, but this is long integer so %ld.
58

59
00:04:36,610 --> 00:04:41,500
Remember that, you need not to use d here. Because, this is unsigned data,
59

60
00:04:41,530 --> 00:04:49,550
so addresses are always unsigned. So, that's why, instead of giving d which is for signed, you can mention
60

61
00:04:49,550 --> 00:04:53,260
u long unsigned integer.
61

62
00:04:53,410 --> 00:04:57,970
And now let's run this code.
62

63
00:04:57,970 --> 00:04:58,430
All right.
63

64
00:04:58,450 --> 00:05:01,800
So, now you can see that the warning is changed to something else.
64

65
00:05:01,810 --> 00:05:08,230
So now, it is saying cast from pointer to integer of different size.
65

66
00:05:09,070 --> 00:05:19,080
So, this a warning is coming because on my machine the unsigned long int is actually a 4 bytes.
66

67
00:05:19,150 --> 00:05:21,360
Basically, it is compiler dependent
67

68
00:05:21,370 --> 00:05:25,990
as I said previously, so everything is compiler dependent here.
68

69
00:05:26,040 --> 00:05:34,150
So, actually the size of the data type int is 4 bytes and size of the long data type is also 4
69

70
00:05:34,150 --> 00:05:36,100
bytes for this compiler.
70

71
00:05:37,360 --> 00:05:41,130
So, that's a reason compiler is saying that here.
71

72
00:05:41,140 --> 00:05:52,360
So, this is actually a pointer data of 8 bytes and you are trying to truncate that by using long
72

73
00:05:52,360 --> 00:05:53,300
int.
73

74
00:05:53,560 --> 00:06:00,770
So, what have to do here is, we have to use long long int. So, long long size is actually 8 bytes. Isn't it? Now it
74

75
00:06:00,810 --> 00:06:02,320
is fine.
75

76
00:06:02,350 --> 00:06:05,400
And in order to print long long data
76

77
00:06:05,560 --> 00:06:12,640
so you have to use I64 format specifier. Just use I64.
77

78
00:06:12,790 --> 00:06:22,870
So, basically you can also use lld or llu. So, lld is for long long integer, llu
78

79
00:06:22,870 --> 00:06:27,350
is for long long unsigned integer.For MinGW
79

80
00:06:27,400 --> 00:06:29,020
you have to use here
80

81
00:06:29,080 --> 00:06:30,930
I64
81

82
00:06:31,160 --> 00:06:32,440
d or u.
82

83
00:06:33,100 --> 00:06:36,080
So, since we are trying to print unsigned data
83

84
00:06:36,110 --> 00:06:39,270
let's use u. I64u.
84

85
00:06:40,070 --> 00:06:43,970
So now let's try to compile.
85

86
00:06:44,100 --> 00:06:54,410
So, now there are no warnings at all. So, now let's try to print this, so this happens to be in integer format.
86

87
00:06:54,530 --> 00:06:59,120
So, let's convert that into hex format, so you can give X here instead of u.
87

88
00:07:02,990 --> 00:07:03,720
Now let's run.
88

89
00:07:07,130 --> 00:07:12,650
So, this is a hex format.So, it is printing only 3 bytes because a rest of the bytes are actually 0.
89

90
00:07:13,370 --> 00:07:20,780
So, the takeaway from this lecture is you can store the pointer as a number in your variable. But for
90

91
00:07:20,780 --> 00:07:23,910
that you have to use a some casting.
91

92
00:07:23,940 --> 00:07:32,030
So, here we are using long long int because, the pointer is of a 8 bytes in this machine. That
92

93
00:07:32,030 --> 00:07:37,940
may not be true if you go to the other machine. For example, if we use ARM based 32 bit microcontrollers
93

94
00:07:38,030 --> 00:07:40,420
then the address size will be 4 bytes.
94

95
00:07:40,640 --> 00:07:43,010
It all depends on the architecture actually.
95

96
00:07:43,010 --> 00:07:48,940
The pointer size is actually architecture driven. For 32 bit architecture
96

97
00:07:49,040 --> 00:07:54,170
the pointer size will be 4 bytes, for 16 bit architecture
97

98
00:07:54,200 --> 00:08:02,490
it will be 2 bytes like that. And here at this part it may confuse some people a like if you run the
98

99
00:08:02,490 --> 00:08:09,140
same code on Ubuntu let's say or Linux, then it may throw errors here.
99

100
00:08:09,210 --> 00:08:13,330
So, in that case you have to change this to llx and in the next lecture
100

101
00:08:13,360 --> 00:08:16,170
let's study about functions in 'C'.


==================================================================================

section 6---storage classes

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:07,380
So, in this lecture let's study about storage classes in 'C'. The storage classes in 'C' language decides
1

2
00:00:07,740 --> 00:00:15,960
scope of a variable, it decides visibility of a variable or a function, and lifetime of a variable.
2

3
00:00:16,530 --> 00:00:21,790
Scope, visibility, and lifetime are features of a variable.
3

4
00:00:21,810 --> 00:00:27,220
These features can be modified by using storage class specifiers.
4

5
00:00:27,300 --> 00:00:32,610
Let's see some of the storage class specifiers which are available in 'C'.
5

6
00:00:32,790 --> 00:00:35,060
There are 2 widely used storage
6

7
00:00:35,130 --> 00:00:37,010
class specifiers. One
7

8
00:00:37,020 --> 00:00:39,720
is static and another one is extern.
8

9
00:00:40,440 --> 00:00:44,920
So now, let's first understand about the static storage class specifier.
9

10
00:00:45,570 --> 00:00:48,850
So, will understand this by using an example.
10

11
00:00:48,920 --> 00:00:49,300
All right.
11

12
00:00:49,320 --> 00:00:52,450
So here I have a function called main.
12

13
00:00:52,550 --> 00:00:59,550
So, now let me create one more function called my function, which doesn't return anything and it doesn't
13

14
00:00:59,550 --> 00:01:00,820
take any arguments.
14

15
00:01:00,900 --> 00:01:02,340
All are void.
15

16
00:01:02,470 --> 00:01:10,630
And now let's give the prototype of this function, so let me end this statement with semicolon.
16

17
00:01:11,250 --> 00:01:19,630
So, now the goal of this program is to count how many times myFun1 is called from the main function.
17

18
00:01:19,800 --> 00:01:22,830
So, I'm going to call this function from main
18

19
00:01:22,830 --> 00:01:24,330
couple of times.
19

20
00:01:24,330 --> 00:01:25,580
Let's call here,
20

21
00:01:25,830 --> 00:01:34,620
and after that let's call one more time, and I'm calling myFun1 4 times. And this function has
21

22
00:01:34,620 --> 00:01:40,660
to print how many times this function gets called. For that, in order to count a
22

23
00:01:40,740 --> 00:01:45,940
let me use one variable so let me call it as int count.
23

24
00:01:45,940 --> 00:01:54,300
So, now let me initialize this to 0 and let me increment the count by 1, count + 1.
24

25
00:01:54,310 --> 00:01:59,570
So, whenever this function is called count will be in incremented by 1.
25

26
00:01:59,650 --> 00:02:08,380
And after that, I'm going to print that count value. Printf this function executed %d times.
26

27
00:02:08,960 --> 00:02:09,370
So now
27

28
00:02:09,400 --> 00:02:10,030
after that
28

29
00:02:10,030 --> 00:02:15,130
let me give the argument that is this variable name count.
29

30
00:02:15,130 --> 00:02:21,690
So, goal of this application is just to print how many times this function gets exe cuted.
30

31
00:02:21,700 --> 00:02:23,890
So, now let me run the program. 
31

32
00:02:25,180 --> 00:02:28,600
And here you can see that, so what's happening here.
32

33
00:02:28,600 --> 00:02:33,610
So, everywhere it is saying that this function executed one time, one time, one time.
33

34
00:02:33,610 --> 00:02:34,090
Right?
34

35
00:02:34,600 --> 00:02:38,580
But our goal is to count how many times this function gets executed.
35

36
00:02:38,590 --> 00:02:39,600
Right?
36

37
00:02:39,610 --> 00:02:44,250
So, first analyze what's happening in this program. Let's start with main.
37

38
00:02:44,260 --> 00:02:46,240
So, execution starts with main. Right?
38

39
00:02:46,810 --> 00:02:48,500
So, inside the main body
39

40
00:02:48,550 --> 00:02:54,190
so we have first there is a call to the myFun1. So, control comes here.
40

41
00:02:54,190 --> 00:02:55,050
Right?
41

42
00:02:55,120 --> 00:03:00,820
And when it comes here a local variable is created and initialize to 0.
42

43
00:03:01,450 --> 00:03:08,330
And after that, 0 is incremented by 1 and then the value is stored back into the variable count.
43

44
00:03:08,370 --> 00:03:08,890
Right?
44

45
00:03:08,950 --> 00:03:10,000
The county is 1.
45

46
00:03:10,000 --> 00:03:10,390
Right?
46

47
00:03:10,900 --> 00:03:14,130
And printf prints the value that is 1.
47

48
00:03:14,170 --> 00:03:15,760
That's great.
48

49
00:03:15,760 --> 00:03:22,190
And after that this function returns. The moment the execution control goes out of this function,
49

50
00:03:22,210 --> 00:03:30,570
what happens is, this local variable dies. The memory location associated with that variable is removed
50

51
00:03:30,760 --> 00:03:32,140
the value is lost.
51

52
00:03:32,140 --> 00:03:39,070
So, this variable loses its existence. And then function returns to main again
52

53
00:03:39,070 --> 00:03:42,660
and after that there is one more call to the same function.
53

54
00:03:42,790 --> 00:03:46,090
And the control again comes to the main function,
54

55
00:03:46,180 --> 00:03:48,280
again the variable is created,
55

56
00:03:48,460 --> 00:03:50,870
and it is initialized to 0,
56

57
00:03:50,950 --> 00:03:54,600
and again that 0 is incremented by 1,
57

58
00:03:54,640 --> 00:03:57,070
and again it prints 1 here.
58

59
00:03:57,130 --> 00:03:58,750
So, it happens like that.
59

60
00:03:58,780 --> 00:04:01,840
So, but our goal is to count. Right?
60

61
00:04:02,080 --> 00:04:05,850
We don't want count variable to die.
61

62
00:04:06,190 --> 00:04:14,130
We want this variable to hold its last value, right? across multiple function calls.
62

63
00:04:14,290 --> 00:04:16,260
So, how to achieve that?
63

64
00:04:16,300 --> 00:04:24,430
So, this problem can be easily solved by removing this variable from local space and moving that variable
64

65
00:04:24,430 --> 00:04:26,230
to global space.
65

66
00:04:26,230 --> 00:04:33,220
So now, the count variable is moved to global space so global variables will not die until you terminate
66

67
00:04:33,220 --> 00:04:34,340
the application.
67

68
00:04:34,390 --> 00:04:41,680
So, there lifetime is until you terminate the application the global variable will be there and there
68

69
00:04:41,690 --> 00:04:44,840
scope is visible to all the functions.
69

70
00:04:44,930 --> 00:04:47,530
Let's try once again.
70

71
00:04:47,530 --> 00:04:49,210
So now, the problem is solved.
71

72
00:04:49,240 --> 00:04:50,200
Right?
72

73
00:04:50,260 --> 00:04:55,570
The function executed one time, two time, three times, four times. So, it is now correctly printing.
73

74
00:04:55,570 --> 00:04:59,750
So, this is what we expected. But, there is a small problem.
74

75
00:04:59,770 --> 00:05:03,750
The problem is now you made this variable public.
75

76
00:05:04,000 --> 00:05:08,260
So, now this variable can be modified by any function in the program.
76

77
00:05:08,290 --> 00:05:08,900
Right?
77

78
00:05:08,950 --> 00:05:15,550
So, even this variable can be modified by some other file of the project.
78

79
00:05:15,550 --> 00:05:20,380
So, this is completely public to all the functions of your project.
79

80
00:05:20,380 --> 00:05:22,450
That is not good.
80

81
00:05:22,450 --> 00:05:31,790
So, now our requirement for this program is, we want a global variable that is private to a function.
81

82
00:05:31,930 --> 00:05:40,540
So, we want private variable that does not lose its existence even if the execution control goes out
82

83
00:05:40,540 --> 00:05:43,160
of the scope of that variable.
83

84
00:05:43,210 --> 00:05:49,660
So, in order to achieve this requirement, that is we want a global variable but it should be private to
84

85
00:05:49,660 --> 00:05:50,320
a function.
85

86
00:05:50,650 --> 00:05:59,320
So, this can be achieved in 'C' by using a storage class specifier called static. Let's see how to use that.
86

87
00:06:00,390 --> 00:06:02,630
Let's go back to the function. Now again,
87

88
00:06:02,690 --> 00:06:08,370
let's remove this variable definition from here and let's again move that into the local space.
88

89
00:06:08,370 --> 00:06:10,300
So, use the static keyword here.
89

90
00:06:10,470 --> 00:06:12,420
Static int count.
90

91
00:06:12,420 --> 00:06:19,370
So now, count is a global variable but it is private to the myFun1.
91

92
00:06:19,380 --> 00:06:21,790
So, now let's run this program.
92

93
00:06:21,840 --> 00:06:28,840
Let's see how it goes. And here you can see that, we got the expected result.
93

94
00:06:29,000 --> 00:06:35,840
But, in this case this count variable is accessible only from myFun1, not from other functions.
94

95
00:06:36,920 --> 00:06:43,700
And also remember that, this variable will not die even if the execution control goes out of this function.
95

96
00:06:44,120 --> 00:06:46,580
The scope of this variable is now global,
96

97
00:06:46,820 --> 00:06:50,260
but visibility is only for this function.
97

98
00:06:50,420 --> 00:06:54,890
So, will understand more about static storage class specifier in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,790
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,800 --> 00:00:11,640
So now, let's see some more use cases of static storage class specifier. So, a 'C' project can be a collection
2

3
00:00:11,700 --> 00:00:13,750
of multiple source files.
3

4
00:00:13,860 --> 00:00:14,840
Right?
4

5
00:00:14,860 --> 00:00:22,020
A project may be having many source files such as let's say file1.c, file2.c, file3.c
5

6
00:00:22,020 --> 00:00:23,490
etc..
6

7
00:00:23,580 --> 00:00:30,480
So, now some files may be written by you and some files may be written by someone else, who is part of
7

8
00:00:30,480 --> 00:00:31,920
the project.
8

9
00:00:32,010 --> 00:00:39,450
In this case, a static storage class specifier can be used to manage the visibility of the variables across
9

10
00:00:39,510 --> 00:00:41,810
various files effectively.
10

11
00:00:41,820 --> 00:00:43,270
Let's see how.
11

12
00:00:43,310 --> 00:00:47,140
Alright. So, now let me demonstrate another usage of static.
12

13
00:00:47,250 --> 00:00:50,210
So, when a project contains multiple files.
13

14
00:00:50,930 --> 00:00:52,300
So, here I have a project.
14

15
00:00:52,320 --> 00:00:58,070
The project name is static_1 and it has one file that is main.c.
15

16
00:00:58,200 --> 00:00:59,460
Let's create another file.
16

17
00:01:02,230 --> 00:01:07,260
New file, and let me give the file name as let's say file1.c.
17

18
00:01:07,870 --> 00:01:14,790
So, in file1.c,  I'm going to create one function a void function, let me create a function here. 
18

19
00:01:16,210 --> 00:01:25,420
Void let's say file1_myFun1. So, an empty function.
19

20
00:01:25,430 --> 00:01:32,210
Let's go to the main.c. And here, what I'm going to do is, I'm going to create one global variable
20

21
00:01:32,440 --> 00:01:35,520
let's say int Private Data.
21

22
00:01:36,170 --> 00:01:41,210
So, now let's say this data is private to the main.c.
22

23
00:01:42,140 --> 00:01:45,380
So now, let me initialize this data here.
23

24
00:01:45,770 --> 00:01:51,170
Let me initialize that to let's say 100 and after that let me print that data.
24

25
00:01:51,170 --> 00:01:58,760
And after that, what I'm going to do is, I'm going to call this function from main. So, remember what I
25

26
00:01:58,760 --> 00:02:06,500
said was this data which is type of int is private to the main.c.
26

27
00:02:06,650 --> 00:02:13,550
So, that means main.c expects that this data should not be modified outside the scope of this file.
27

28
00:02:14,220 --> 00:02:19,040
So but, in file1.c  we can modify this data.
28

29
00:02:19,190 --> 00:02:19,550
Right?
29

30
00:02:19,580 --> 00:02:23,410
So, what I can do is, I can simply change its value.
30

31
00:02:23,480 --> 00:02:25,970
So, let me change it to 900.
31

32
00:02:26,000 --> 00:02:30,510
That's possible, because this is global to the whole project.
32

33
00:02:30,590 --> 00:02:38,110
That means its visibility is to all the files of the project. Let's try to compile, let's see what happens.
33

34
00:02:38,150 --> 00:02:41,040
Here, we may get a couple of errors. So, let's see what are the errors. 
34

35
00:02:41,040 --> 00:02:49,410
First of all, first compiler tried to compile main.c and it met with an error here. Implicit declaration
35

36
00:02:49,410 --> 00:02:50,560
of function.
36

37
00:02:50,560 --> 00:02:56,880
So, basically compiler is saying that, it couldn't able to find the declaration our prototype of this
37

38
00:02:57,090 --> 00:03:00,810
function which is defined somewhere else in the project.
38

39
00:03:00,810 --> 00:03:01,140
Right?
39

40
00:03:01,140 --> 00:03:06,680
So, that's why, it's our duty to give the function prototype of this function.
40

41
00:03:07,020 --> 00:03:14,050
Let's copy this name and let's copy this. And go to the main.c, go to the global section here.
41

42
00:03:14,460 --> 00:03:18,900
And just to make the compiler happy, so let's give the prototype.
42

43
00:03:18,900 --> 00:03:20,210
This is a prototype.
43

44
00:03:20,260 --> 00:03:28,060
Let's try to compile, let's see what happens. So, main.c compiled fine no problem with the main.c.
44

45
00:03:28,070 --> 00:03:34,950
Now the problem is in file1.c, where the compiler is complaining that a this is undeclared.
45

46
00:03:35,030 --> 00:03:41,060
So, that's why, you have to give the declaration now, not definition remember, because it is already defined
46

47
00:03:41,060 --> 00:03:41,690
here.
47

48
00:03:41,690 --> 00:03:41,960
Right?
48

49
00:03:41,990 --> 00:03:44,540
So, you have to declare that it is there somewhere.
49

50
00:03:44,720 --> 00:03:47,120
We do that by using extern. Extern
50

51
00:03:51,360 --> 00:03:52,200
int PrivateData.
51

52
00:03:52,230 --> 00:03:56,630
So, this is a declaration. Before modifying that variable,
52

53
00:03:56,760 --> 00:04:01,650
so we have to declare the compiler, that it is there somewhere else in the project.
53

54
00:04:01,770 --> 00:04:09,940
After that, let me print this printf statement once again after this line. Let me try to compile. Let's
54

55
00:04:09,940 --> 00:04:11,290
see what happens.
55

56
00:04:12,010 --> 00:04:12,340
Right?
56

57
00:04:12,370 --> 00:04:18,460
So, here it is. So, first the value was 100 and after that you called this function which is there
57

58
00:04:18,460 --> 00:04:25,840
in file1.c and file1.c can simply extern that variable and it can modify it.
58

59
00:04:26,050 --> 00:04:26,410
Right?
59

60
00:04:26,410 --> 00:04:29,920
So, that's why, when you do this printf it printed 900.
60

61
00:04:29,920 --> 00:04:37,720
That means the value is modified. But, main.c wanted no one to touch that variable outside the scope
61

62
00:04:37,720 --> 00:04:38,780
of this file.
62

63
00:04:38,890 --> 00:04:46,990
So, that means, if main.c wants to keep this variable private to this file, then it has to use the
63

64
00:04:46,990 --> 00:04:48,010
static keyword here.
64

65
00:04:48,980 --> 00:04:57,980
So now, this is accessible only inside the scope of this file, that is main.c and it cannot be accessed
65

66
00:04:58,130 --> 00:05:01,070
from outside the scope of this file.
66

67
00:05:01,930 --> 00:05:04,940
Let's try to compile. Let's see what happens. Here
67

68
00:05:04,940 --> 00:05:13,210
you can see that, compiler is not letting file1.c to modify this value, it is throwing error.
68

69
00:05:13,340 --> 00:05:15,570
The project compilation is not successful.
69

70
00:05:15,650 --> 00:05:16,270
Right?
70

71
00:05:16,340 --> 00:05:22,770
So, it is saying that undefined reference to this data. In real project implementation, let's say main.c
71

72
00:05:22,770 --> 00:05:24,530
maybe implemented by you.
72

73
00:05:24,740 --> 00:05:28,340
And file1.c maybe implemented by some other developer.
73

74
00:05:28,490 --> 00:05:33,820
And if you want to put such restrictions, then you can use the static keyword.
74

75
00:05:34,640 --> 00:05:42,910
So, that means this is kind of imposing privacy to your global variables. So, that it should not be
75

76
00:05:42,910 --> 00:05:52,340
modified randomly across multiple project files. So, that's one of the important use case of static keyword.
76

77
00:05:52,380 --> 00:05:57,710
So here, if you want to compile this project successfully, then you should first remove this. Because,
77

78
00:05:57,710 --> 00:06:00,910
static variables cannot be externed.
78

79
00:06:01,100 --> 00:06:07,040
So, that's why, you have to remove this and you have to give up this. Otherwise, project will not compile
79

80
00:06:07,040 --> 00:06:07,860
successfully.
80

81
00:06:08,730 --> 00:06:12,030
So, static keyword can be used
81

82
00:06:12,030 --> 00:06:15,450
with functions and we'll see that in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,460
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,500 --> 00:00:05,880
So, now in this lecture let's see how static keyword can be used with functions.
2

3
00:00:05,970 --> 00:00:10,500
So, now let's say we have 2 files. A main.c and file1.c.
3

4
00:00:10,590 --> 00:00:15,600
And now let's say, I create one function in main.c.
4

5
00:00:15,990 --> 00:00:22,870
Now the function name may be let's say, void change_ system_clock.
5

6
00:00:22,890 --> 00:00:27,330
So, now let's say this function takes the argument system_clock from other functions.
6

7
00:00:29,030 --> 00:00:32,370
So, don't worry you'll learn more about functions later.
7

8
00:00:32,390 --> 00:00:39,300
So, what this function does is, it actually takes system_clock value from other functions in this variable,
8

9
00:00:39,710 --> 00:00:43,450
and then it uses this variable to change the system clock.
9

10
00:00:43,670 --> 00:00:46,780
Let's just print system_clock here.
10

11
00:00:46,910 --> 00:00:51,590
So now let me just print a message system clock
11

12
00:00:51,630 --> 00:00:56,280
let's say change to. Let's print this value.
12

13
00:00:56,290 --> 00:01:01,720
So now, let's say for main.c, this is very critical and very important function
13

14
00:01:01,750 --> 00:01:06,410
and this function should not be called from other files.
14

15
00:01:06,520 --> 00:01:13,800
The developer who wrote this function wants only functions of main.c should call this function.
15

16
00:01:13,870 --> 00:01:23,080
So, functions outside the file main.c should not call this function. But, if you leave this function
16

17
00:01:23,080 --> 00:01:24,070
like this,
17

18
00:01:24,190 --> 00:01:32,320
so any function of the project can call this function and it can change the system clock and thus causing
18

19
00:01:32,440 --> 00:01:34,570
problem to the application.
19

20
00:01:34,570 --> 00:01:43,270
For example, in this case we have file1.c and the function of the file1.c can call this
20

21
00:01:43,270 --> 00:01:44,050
function.
21

22
00:01:44,230 --> 00:01:47,080
So, it can just call this function no issues at all.
22

23
00:01:47,230 --> 00:01:49,010
So, it can call with let's say 0.
23

24
00:01:49,180 --> 00:01:52,470
Let's say, it wants to preese the system clock.
24

25
00:01:52,500 --> 00:01:54,270
So, this is perfectly fine. So,
25

26
00:01:54,360 --> 00:02:01,840
all you need to do is just give the prototype of this function, write over here and just call that function.
26

27
00:02:01,980 --> 00:02:09,030
You can even use the extern keyword here, because you are actually calling a function which is implemented
27

28
00:02:09,090 --> 00:02:11,380
outside the scope of this file.
28

29
00:02:11,400 --> 00:02:11,870
Right?
29

30
00:02:11,880 --> 00:02:15,650
So, you can even use the extern with this function prototype.
30

31
00:02:16,180 --> 00:02:18,280
So but, extern is usually assumed here.
31

32
00:02:18,300 --> 00:02:20,910
So, you need not to use the keyword extern actually.
32

33
00:02:20,910 --> 00:02:21,780
So, it is assumed.
33

34
00:02:22,530 --> 00:02:27,810
So now this code works perfectly fine, so now if I run the code what happens here.
34

35
00:02:27,810 --> 00:02:30,290
Here you can see that, there are no errors.
35

36
00:02:30,300 --> 00:02:31,400
Right?
36

37
00:02:31,470 --> 00:02:38,640
So, file1.c simply called that function and it changed the system clock which main.c doesn't
37

38
00:02:38,760 --> 00:02:39,630
want to happen.
38

39
00:02:39,690 --> 00:02:40,650
Right?
39

40
00:02:40,710 --> 00:02:48,390
So, if main.c wants to protect calling this function outside the scope of this file, then you have
40

41
00:02:48,390 --> 00:02:50,890
to use static keyword here.
41

42
00:02:50,910 --> 00:03:00,000
So, this is the static function or it's also called as a private function of the file main.c. So, only
42

43
00:03:00,000 --> 00:03:05,760
functions of the main.c can call this function now. So, no function outside the scope of this file
43

44
00:03:06,060 --> 00:03:08,090
can call this function.
44

45
00:03:08,100 --> 00:03:08,720
All right.
45

46
00:03:08,790 --> 00:03:12,200
So, let's try to compile, let's see what happens.
46

47
00:03:12,480 --> 00:03:14,180
The compilation is not successful.
47

48
00:03:14,190 --> 00:03:14,610
Right?
48

49
00:03:14,610 --> 00:03:19,990
So, the compiler is saying that undefined reference made to this function.
49

50
00:03:20,070 --> 00:03:25,230
So, the compiler is not letting file1.c to compile successfully.
50

51
00:03:25,230 --> 00:03:32,280
So, that's an alert for the developer of file1.c to understand that he should not call change
51

52
00:03:32,280 --> 00:03:42,430
system clock function, because it's a private function. That's how, privacy is imposed in 'C'. So, that's
52

53
00:03:42,440 --> 00:03:45,270
about usage of static. In the next lecture,
53

54
00:03:45,320 --> 00:03:49,340
let's explore about another storage class specifier
54

55
00:03:49,400 --> 00:03:50,630
that is extern,
55

56
00:03:50,960 --> 00:03:52,250
and I will see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 4

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:02,460
So, let's understand about 'Extern' keywords.So,
1

2
00:00:02,520 --> 00:00:04,100
we actually already used
2

3
00:00:04,100 --> 00:00:07,330
this extern keyword in our previous example. Right?
3

4
00:00:07,410 --> 00:00:13,470
But let's understand some points about extern keyword. So, 'extern' storage class specifier
4

5
00:00:13,470 --> 00:00:20,570
is used to access the global variable, which is defined outside the scope of a file. So, 'extern' storage
5

6
00:00:20,580 --> 00:00:24,080
class specifier can also be used during the function call.
6

7
00:00:24,170 --> 00:00:31,280
So, when the function is defined outside the scope of the file. Here, in this case actually a mentioning extern 
7

8
00:00:31,460 --> 00:00:33,380
keyword is not compulsory.
8

9
00:00:33,470 --> 00:00:34,460
It is optional.
9

10
00:00:34,610 --> 00:00:37,460
As we saw in the previous example.
10

11
00:00:37,580 --> 00:00:43,400
So, extern is assumed, when you try to call a function which is outside the scope of that file.
11

12
00:00:43,530 --> 00:00:45,470
And now let's move forward.
12

13
00:00:45,470 --> 00:00:49,220
The keyword 'extern' is relevant only when your
13

14
00:00:49,220 --> 00:00:57,020
project consists of multiple files, and you need to access a variable defined in one file from another
14

15
00:00:57,020 --> 00:00:57,380
file.
15

16
00:00:58,280 --> 00:01:01,830
So, it makes sense only when you have multiple files.
16

17
00:01:01,910 --> 00:01:08,120
Otherwise, you need not to use 'extern'. So, 'extern' keyword is used to extend the visibility of
17

18
00:01:08,120 --> 00:01:08,930
a function
18

19
00:01:08,960 --> 00:01:17,390
or variable.So, that's about 'extern' and in the next lecture we will see some more concepts of 'C', and
19

20
00:01:17,390 --> 00:01:18,410
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 5

0
1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:01,230
Hey, welcome back to the lecture. 
1

2
00:00:01,230 --> 00:00:08,130
So, in this lecture let's explore about ASCII codes. The American National Standards Institute, which developed
2

3
00:00:08,440 --> 00:00:16,500
ANSI C, also developed the ASCII codes. So, ASCII stands for "American Standard Code for Information
3

4
00:00:16,500 --> 00:00:26,550
Interchange". So, by using ASCII standard, that is ASCII standard codes you can encode 128 different characters,
4

5
00:00:26,640 --> 00:00:29,440
which you encounter in your day to day life.
5

6
00:00:29,490 --> 00:00:36,870
That's why, to encode any ASCII character you just need 7 bits. So because, there are 128 characters
6

7
00:00:36,930 --> 00:00:41,280
and one unique code will be used to represent a character.
7

8
00:00:41,280 --> 00:00:48,100
So, in order to know the ASCII codes for the characters you have to referred to the ASCII table.
8

9
00:00:48,210 --> 00:00:52,170
So, this is a ASCII table which I got from internet.
9

10
00:00:52,170 --> 00:00:54,290
So now here you can see that,
10

11
00:00:54,290 --> 00:00:58,320
so these are you know uppercase alphabets. Right?
11

12
00:00:58,320 --> 00:01:04,200
And these are the respective ASCII codes for them in decimal.
12

13
00:01:04,920 --> 00:01:08,940
So, these are actually the lower case letters.
13

14
00:01:08,940 --> 00:01:09,300
Right?
14

15
00:01:09,300 --> 00:01:13,740
And these are the respective ASCII codes.
15

16
00:01:13,830 --> 00:01:21,990
So, not only for English alphabets, you can also get ASCII codes for special characters like space, double
16

17
00:01:21,990 --> 00:01:31,860
quotes, #, &, etc.. You also get ASCII codes to represent numbers from 0 to 9 and also you
17

18
00:01:31,860 --> 00:01:38,040
get ASCII codes to represent some special characters such as Carriage return,
18

19
00:01:38,040 --> 00:01:40,200
and this is for Backspace,
19

20
00:01:40,200 --> 00:01:42,660
this is for Horizontal tab,
20

21
00:01:42,660 --> 00:01:43,350
so like that.
21

22
00:01:43,800 --> 00:01:49,900
So, these are the codes for the keys which you find on your computer keyboard.
22

23
00:01:49,990 --> 00:01:56,630
So, let's take an example. Let's say, I want to represent these characters inside the computer memory.
23

24
00:01:56,640 --> 00:02:02,070
So, I want to store the word Apple in computer memory. For the machine
24

25
00:02:02,070 --> 00:02:03,560
everything is number. Right?
25

26
00:02:03,570 --> 00:02:08,490
So, machine doesn't recognize it like 'A' or, 'p' or, 'l' or, whatever.
26

27
00:02:08,910 --> 00:02:11,870
So, for the machine everything is number.
27

28
00:02:11,910 --> 00:02:18,240
So, basically what we are going to do is, we are going to store the respective ASCII codes of those characters
28

29
00:02:18,360 --> 00:02:20,610
inside the computer memory, right?
29

30
00:02:20,640 --> 00:02:25,800
So, that's how we process the characters, strings, alphabets, etc..
30

31
00:02:25,790 --> 00:02:26,060
All right. 
31

32
00:02:26,070 --> 00:02:33,310
So, now let's do one exercise in order to print the string Apple. First let's represent the word Apple in
32

33
00:02:33,330 --> 00:02:34,090
our program.
33

34
00:02:36,900 --> 00:02:39,690
So, first of all we know the ASCII codes.
34

35
00:02:39,800 --> 00:02:44,930
So, now let's use a variables to store those ASCII codes.
35

36
00:02:44,930 --> 00:02:52,730
Let me create a variable char a1. So, a1 means let's say alphabet 1 is equal to let me store the ASCII code,
36

37
00:02:53,670 --> 00:02:56,320
ASCII code of a, that is 65.
37

38
00:02:57,160 --> 00:02:59,280
a2 (alphabet 2) ASCII code of
38

39
00:02:59,300 --> 00:03:00,860
p,112.
39

40
00:03:02,750 --> 00:03:05,350
So, a3 is 112,
40

41
00:03:05,780 --> 00:03:19,720
char a4 is equal to 'l' is 108, char a6 is equal to 58, a7 is equal to 41.
41

42
00:03:20,480 --> 00:03:23,270
So, I think it's very hard. Right?
42

43
00:03:23,270 --> 00:03:28,460
So, this procedure is very hard that, we should refer to the ASCII table and we have to pick the ASCII
43

44
00:03:28,460 --> 00:03:29,070
code.
44

45
00:03:29,360 --> 00:03:33,590
So, instead of doing this there is one shortcut method.
45

46
00:03:33,620 --> 00:03:35,150
So, this is very chaotic. Right?
46

47
00:03:35,180 --> 00:03:40,290
So, you have to refer to the ASCII table, get the code, and include that in the code,
47

48
00:03:40,340 --> 00:03:46,650
it is actually a very tedious process.There is one simple method. That is instead of writing 65
48

49
00:03:47,210 --> 00:03:51,060
just write A and give single quotes.
49

50
00:03:51,230 --> 00:03:59,600
So, write your alphabet inside or character, inside the single quotes. So, compiler will replace this
50

51
00:03:59,600 --> 00:04:00,780
by 65.
51

52
00:04:01,520 --> 00:04:02,720
So, that is easy. Right?
52

53
00:04:02,910 --> 00:04:06,580
Let's do that, instead of writing all these codes.
53

54
00:04:06,620 --> 00:04:12,940
This is very much easy, right?  So,  a1 holds not 'A', it holds 65.
54

55
00:04:12,940 --> 00:04:15,050
The code of 'A'
55

56
00:04:15,100 --> 00:04:18,010
so that will be done by the compiler at the background.
56

57
00:04:18,340 --> 00:04:29,130
So, you need not worry about that. Let's print, printf the string is, we want to print like characters
57

58
00:04:29,220 --> 00:04:30,960
not like numbers. Right?
58

59
00:04:30,990 --> 00:04:35,910
So, that's why, we have to use %c as the format specifier.
59

60
00:04:36,480 --> 00:04:40,450
So, that's why, we have to use %c, not %d.
60

61
00:04:41,370 --> 00:04:46,010
%c\n and let's print a1.
61

62
00:04:46,720 --> 00:04:47,950
Let's see how it goes.
62

63
00:04:47,950 --> 00:04:49,800
Let me just run this.
63

64
00:04:49,970 --> 00:04:58,120
So, it printed 'A' .%d then it prints integer, it print 65. Right?
64

65
00:04:58,120 --> 00:05:00,850
This is a value which is stored in a1, right?
65

66
00:05:00,850 --> 00:05:02,240
So, that's what printed here.
66

67
00:05:02,320 --> 00:05:09,130
But if we use %c, it prints the format will be changed to ASCII. If I want to print all these
67

68
00:05:09,130 --> 00:05:09,600
variables, 
68

69
00:05:09,620 --> 00:05:11,100
so have to repeat this. Right?
69

70
00:05:11,270 --> 00:05:15,530
So, %c %c %c %c
70

71
00:05:15,630 --> 00:05:16,370
so like this.
71

72
00:05:17,080 --> 00:05:19,950
So, there are 7 format specifiers.
72

73
00:05:20,130 --> 00:05:33,650
Let me write a2, a3, a4, a5, a6,  a7. Let's run. So, it printed Apple. So, you can even
73

74
00:05:33,680 --> 00:05:38,330
remove this space here, now let's run.
74

75
00:05:41,320 --> 00:05:49,570
So, that's how you actually store the characters in your program. All these codes can be replaced by one
75

76
00:05:49,570 --> 00:05:59,500
line by using arrays, like for example, I can do this.  A char a[ ] = Apple. I can do this . For
76

77
00:05:59,500 --> 00:06:07,860
this we have to explore arrays. So, this we will see when we cover the sections arrays and strings.
77

78
00:06:09,360 --> 00:06:15,960
So, that's how you store the characters, and that's how you print the characters, and I hope you get the
78

79
00:06:15,960 --> 00:06:22,590
idea about ASCII and ASCII codes, and about the format specifier, %c. And if you have any
79

80
00:06:22,590 --> 00:06:25,950
questions, please don't hesitate to ask and I'll see you in the next lecture.


==================================================================================

section 7--functions

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,150 --> 00:00:01,410
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,440 --> 00:00:07,440
So, from this lecture onwards let's learn about Functions in 'C'. Let's learn how to write functions,
2

3
00:00:07,620 --> 00:00:13,200
what is a function prototype, and how to call function, and will understand various other things related
3

4
00:00:13,200 --> 00:00:14,380
to functions.
4

5
00:00:14,490 --> 00:00:20,220
So, in 'C',  you write executable statements inside a function. For example,
5

6
00:00:20,220 --> 00:00:20,740
so here,
6

7
00:00:20,760 --> 00:00:22,340
let's say in this program,
7

8
00:00:22,350 --> 00:00:26,940
so I cannot cut this code and I can't put this code here.
8

9
00:00:27,990 --> 00:00:31,040
This is actually an executable 'C' statement. Right?
9

10
00:00:31,050 --> 00:00:33,600
So, we cannot keep this outside the function.
10

11
00:00:33,720 --> 00:00:39,820
So, this is illegal in 'C'. So, it has to be inside a function.
11

12
00:00:39,980 --> 00:00:46,340
So, all the codes that is executable statements must be inside the body of the function.
12

13
00:00:46,490 --> 00:00:51,160
Of course, you can do a variable definition outside the scope of a function,
13

14
00:00:51,170 --> 00:00:51,950
no problem.
14

15
00:00:51,950 --> 00:00:55,770
So, even you can initialize that variable.
15

16
00:00:55,810 --> 00:01:00,010
So, this is nothing but variable initialization. So, that you can do outside the scope of a function,
16

17
00:01:00,450 --> 00:01:04,540
but the executable statements has to be inside the function.
17

18
00:01:07,570 --> 00:01:14,820
So, in 'C', a 'C' function is nothing but collection of statements to perform a specific task.
18

19
00:01:15,070 --> 00:01:22,000
So, every 'C' program, at least, has one function called "main". So, using functions bring modularity to
19

20
00:01:22,000 --> 00:01:22,790
your code.
20

21
00:01:22,810 --> 00:01:31,340
It is easy to debug, modify, and increases the maintainability of the code. Using 'C' function also minimizes
21

22
00:01:31,400 --> 00:01:37,250
code size and minimizes code redundancy. Functions provide abstraction.
22

23
00:01:37,390 --> 00:01:41,550
For example, printf is a function given by standard library code.
23

24
00:01:41,620 --> 00:01:46,870
So, you need not to worry about how it is implemented, and it serves your purpose. Right?
24

25
00:01:46,870 --> 00:01:54,610
So, in your program, if you want to perform the same set of task again and again, then create a function
25

26
00:01:54,640 --> 00:02:00,500
to do that task and call it every time you need to perform that task.
26

27
00:02:00,550 --> 00:02:07,630
So, the main aim of using function is to reduce the code redundancy in the program, and it brings more
27

28
00:02:07,690 --> 00:02:14,420
modularity to your program and also decreases the final executable size of your program.
28

29
00:02:14,440 --> 00:02:21,430
So, this is a general form of a function definition in 'C'.
So, when you write a function you first have 
29

30
00:02:21,430 --> 00:02:28,150
to give a name for the function, a proper name. So, you can follow the same rules what you used to write
30

31
00:02:28,150 --> 00:02:28,990
a variable name.
31

32
00:02:32,570 --> 00:02:38,360
And the function always comes with parenthesis. And inside the parenthesis
32

33
00:02:38,360 --> 00:02:40,940
you have to mention the parameter list.
33

34
00:02:40,940 --> 00:02:47,990
So, these are the input parameters or input arguments to the function, and also you may mention the return
34

35
00:02:48,050 --> 00:02:54,030
data type that is the data type of the data which the function returns.
35

36
00:02:54,030 --> 00:02:57,860
So, a function may return a value or it may not return a value.
36

37
00:02:58,070 --> 00:03:00,980
So, will see more about this as you make a progress.
37

38
00:03:01,520 --> 00:03:08,030
So, this is called as a function prototype. So, a function prototype says it's return type, functions
38

39
00:03:08,060 --> 00:03:10,620
name, and it's parameter list.
39

40
00:03:10,880 --> 00:03:17,090
And after that, you have to use these curly brackets which actually gives the body of the function, and
40

41
00:03:17,090 --> 00:03:22,520
inside the body of the function you have to write your executable 'C' statements.
41

42
00:03:22,760 --> 00:03:24,930
Let's see one example.
42

43
00:03:24,960 --> 00:03:25,340
All right.
43

44
00:03:25,350 --> 00:03:29,040
So, I'm ready with my new project let's say function.c.
44

45
00:03:29,340 --> 00:03:36,250
So, let's first write the main function. So, main is also a function like any other function which has
45

46
00:03:36,400 --> 00:03:41,230
a function body, return type, the parameter list. So,
46

47
00:03:41,240 --> 00:03:47,020
but there are two ways you can define a main function in your program according to the standard.
47

48
00:03:47,030 --> 00:03:53,890
So, let's explore them. So, you can give main function definition in two ways.
48

49
00:03:53,890 --> 00:03:58,790
One is this one. This is a main function definition with no input arguments.
49

50
00:03:58,900 --> 00:04:01,700
Here we can see that there are no input arguments.
50

51
00:04:01,840 --> 00:04:07,660
So, when there are no input arguments you can mention it as void, or you can just leave like this with
51

52
00:04:07,810 --> 00:04:09,280
just a parenthesis.
52

53
00:04:09,310 --> 00:04:10,600
No problem.
53

54
00:04:10,600 --> 00:04:15,580
So, this is another main function definition with 2 input arguments.
54

55
00:04:15,610 --> 00:04:19,050
So, now these arguments also called as command line arguments.
55

56
00:04:19,210 --> 00:04:25,060
So, we don't use this definition of main for embedded systems because most of the time, there are no command
56

57
00:04:25,060 --> 00:04:27,940
line arguments in embedded system programming.
57

58
00:04:27,940 --> 00:04:31,600
So, that's why, we usually don't use a this main function definition.
58

59
00:04:31,600 --> 00:04:38,540
So, usually use this. Let's write that. So, and when you write the main function always make sure that
59

60
00:04:38,630 --> 00:04:40,190
the return type is int.
60

61
00:04:40,370 --> 00:04:47,020
That is this function has to return an integer data, by using return statement.
61

62
00:04:47,030 --> 00:04:47,930
Let's write
62

63
00:04:47,960 --> 00:04:53,330
return 0. Let's explore some more points about 'main'.
63

64
00:04:53,690 --> 00:04:57,110
So, 'main' takes only 0 or 2 arguments.
64

65
00:04:57,390 --> 00:05:02,220
Main() function returns the status of the program to the parent process.
65

66
00:05:02,250 --> 00:05:06,420
So, in this case, the parent process is actually the operating system.
66

67
00:05:06,420 --> 00:05:06,720
Right?
67

68
00:05:06,720 --> 00:05:11,970
So because, we are actually writing application for our windows PC or our machine.
68

69
00:05:11,970 --> 00:05:16,580
So here there is a operating system, and operating system launches the application.
69

70
00:05:16,650 --> 00:05:21,730
So, when the application ends, that application returns a code back to the OS
70

71
00:05:21,840 --> 00:05:26,790
and by that OS comes to know whether that process is terminated properly or not.
71

72
00:05:27,150 --> 00:05:32,280
So, main() function returns the status of the program to the parent process, showing the success or
72

73
00:05:32,340 --> 00:05:38,740
failure of the program. So, 0 means SUCCESS and non zero means ERROR. All right.
73

74
00:05:38,750 --> 00:05:42,140
So, now let's write a function to add 2 numbers.
74

75
00:05:42,170 --> 00:05:44,500
So, now first let me give a function name.
75

76
00:05:44,660 --> 00:05:49,080
So, let's call the function name as function_add_numbers.
76

77
00:05:49,190 --> 00:05:55,730
And after that, let's give the parenthesis and let's write here the argument list or parameter list.
77

78
00:05:55,730 --> 00:06:00,110
So, let's say, this function takes 3 input arguments.
78

79
00:06:00,110 --> 00:06:08,930
So, let me call it as int a, int b, and int c. These are the three input parameters of the function add
79

80
00:06:08,930 --> 00:06:14,300
numbers. And let me decide that this function doesn't return anything.
80

81
00:06:14,300 --> 00:06:15,720
So, no return value.
81

82
00:06:15,980 --> 00:06:20,110
So, that's why, in that case you have to mention the keyword void here.
82

83
00:06:20,200 --> 00:06:24,630
So, void is the return type, so that means the function doesn't return anything.
83

84
00:06:24,800 --> 00:06:31,620
And after that, let's give the function body. So, you have to open 2 curly brackets.
84

85
00:06:31,670 --> 00:06:35,070
So, this is called as function definition.
85

86
00:06:35,420 --> 00:06:40,780
And after that, inside the function you have to write the code to add 2 numbers or add 3 numbers.
86

87
00:06:40,910 --> 00:06:47,710
Let me create one more variable sum. So, sum is equal to a+b+c.
87

88
00:06:47,990 --> 00:06:52,260
So, remember that, this variable and these variables are same.
88

89
00:06:52,310 --> 00:06:55,730
So, all these are local scope variables.
89

90
00:06:55,730 --> 00:07:02,680
So, it is just that these are mentioned to receive the input values for the function.
90

91
00:07:02,690 --> 00:07:09,160
So, these are also called as formal parameters or you can just call them as local scope variables.
91

92
00:07:09,170 --> 00:07:09,770
No problem.
92

93
00:07:10,340 --> 00:07:16,310
And after that, let's print the sum. Sum is equal to %d
93

94
00:07:16,340 --> 00:07:20,090
\n, and let me print the sum.
94

95
00:07:20,090 --> 00:07:20,460
Right?
95

96
00:07:21,230 --> 00:07:27,800
So, now whenever you want to add 3 numbers just call this function, you can call it from main or
96

97
00:07:27,800 --> 00:07:30,320
you can call it from some other function,
97

98
00:07:30,470 --> 00:07:34,820
so that means, this function is dedicated to add three numbers.
98

99
00:07:35,000 --> 00:07:37,330
Let's see how to call this function.
99

100
00:07:37,400 --> 00:07:40,270
So, let's say, I want to call this function from main.
100

101
00:07:40,340 --> 00:07:46,580
So, I go to the main and I write the function name, and then I open the parenthesis,
101

102
00:07:46,670 --> 00:07:49,200
and after that you have to give semicolon.
102

103
00:07:49,220 --> 00:07:51,530
So, this is function calling,
103

104
00:07:51,530 --> 00:07:55,860
and here you have to pass the arguments to the function. So,
104

105
00:07:55,910 --> 00:08:00,250
this function takes three input values of type int.
105

106
00:08:00,290 --> 00:08:02,420
So, you have to do according to that.
106

107
00:08:02,450 --> 00:08:07,370
So, you have to pass three integer values, when you call this function.
107

108
00:08:08,150 --> 00:08:13,290
So, let's use three integer values. Let's say 12, 13, 14.
108

109
00:08:13,460 --> 00:08:20,960
I can call this function one more time, so I can mention -20, +20,14.
109

110
00:08:21,590 --> 00:08:25,530
And after that you can even pass variables as arguments.
110

111
00:08:25,970 --> 00:08:34,470
Let's say you have int valueA = 90, and let's say valueB = 70.
111

112
00:08:34,520 --> 00:08:42,610
You can even do something like this.So, you can pass valueA, valueB, and third argument
112

113
00:08:42,620 --> 00:08:44,520
you can pass anything you want.
113

114
00:08:44,540 --> 00:08:45,920
So, let's say 90.
114

115
00:08:46,010 --> 00:08:47,950
So, this is also a legal statement.
115

116
00:08:47,980 --> 00:08:55,270
So, it can be combination of a number and variables. And after that, so now let's see ho w it goes.
116

117
00:08:55,280 --> 00:08:59,650
Let me try to compile. So, I try to compile this
117

118
00:08:59,770 --> 00:09:01,650
and there are various warnings, and
118

119
00:09:01,660 --> 00:09:04,450
now let's try to understand one by one.
119

120
00:09:04,450 --> 00:09:08,200
The first warning is implicit declaration of function
120

121
00:09:08,570 --> 00:09:10,720
'function_add_numbers'.
121

122
00:09:10,720 --> 00:09:18,490
So, that means, for the compiler before calling a function you have to declare that, in the same way what
122

123
00:09:18,490 --> 00:09:22,030
you did for variables.So, before using a variable
123

124
00:09:22,030 --> 00:09:27,640
you have to define a variable or at least you have to declare a variable that it is exists somewhere.
124

125
00:09:27,670 --> 00:09:28,530
right?
125

126
00:09:28,570 --> 00:09:33,850
So, same rule you have to follow for functions as well. Before using that function,
126

127
00:09:33,850 --> 00:09:41,380
you have to declare to the compiler that it exists somewhere, and we do that by using something called
127

128
00:09:41,470 --> 00:09:46,820
function declaration or it is also called as function prototyping.
128

129
00:09:47,380 --> 00:09:51,040
Let's explore about function prototyping or function declaration.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,480 --> 00:00:03,440
So, now let's explore about function prototype.
1

2
00:00:03,590 --> 00:00:08,110
So, in 'C' functions first have to be declared before they are used.
2

3
00:00:08,340 --> 00:00:16,170
Prototype lets the compiler to know about the return data type, argument list and their data type and
3

4
00:00:16,170 --> 00:00:20,370
order of arguments being passed to the function.
4

5
00:00:20,370 --> 00:00:22,340
So, if you just observe the error here.
5

6
00:00:22,350 --> 00:00:27,190
So, what it is saying? It is saying that, implicit declaration of function.
6

7
00:00:27,210 --> 00:00:28,530
What does that mean?
7

8
00:00:28,530 --> 00:00:30,260
So, basically compiler is saying that,
8

9
00:00:30,570 --> 00:00:39,560
so here I encountered an usage of user defined function, but it's prototype is not found.
9

10
00:00:39,600 --> 00:00:43,740
So, that's why, compiler is assuming the prototype.
10

11
00:00:43,740 --> 00:00:45,600
So, it is assuming something else,
11

12
00:00:45,600 --> 00:00:52,290
and after that compiler precedes. After that, it comes to the real the function definition which is implemented
12

13
00:00:52,410 --> 00:00:54,640
write here. And here,
13

14
00:00:54,640 --> 00:01:02,590
compiler comes to know about the actual prototype, and that's why, the second warning conflicting types.
14

15
00:01:02,700 --> 00:01:07,540
There is a conflict between what it has assumed here and what it has found here.
15

16
00:01:09,000 --> 00:01:14,310
So, this you can leave. When after that it is saying again implicit declaration of function printf, because we
16

17
00:01:14,310 --> 00:01:18,390
have used printf here, but we didn't tell the prototype of that.
17

18
00:01:18,690 --> 00:01:23,630
And we do that by using #include, right? Because, printf is not written by us.
18

19
00:01:23,640 --> 00:01:23,900
Right?
19

20
00:01:23,910 --> 00:01:26,360
So, it is written by standard library code,
20

21
00:01:26,370 --> 00:01:30,390
so  that's why, we have to give standard library header file in order include the prototype.
21

22
00:01:31,020 --> 00:01:38,730
And after that, we have to mention the prototype of this function, before you call that function.
22

23
00:01:38,730 --> 00:01:45,090
So, that's why, what you have to do is, you have to just copy this line, and go to the global scope and just
23

24
00:01:45,090 --> 00:01:48,390
paste that line, and give the semicolon. Right?
24

25
00:01:48,390 --> 00:01:52,440
So, now before calling the function compiler will encounter the prototype.
25

26
00:01:55,700 --> 00:02:00,720
So, you need not to mention int a, int b, int c, you can even mention just int, int, int.
26

27
00:02:00,810 --> 00:02:01,820
So, that is also fine.
27

28
00:02:02,120 --> 00:02:10,200
But, what we usually do is, so we just copy this and paste here and give the semicolon. And now let's try to
28

29
00:02:10,200 --> 00:02:12,280
compile. No warnings.
29

30
00:02:12,660 --> 00:02:19,170
And after that, since it is a void function you need not to mention any return value here in this function.
30

31
00:02:19,340 --> 00:02:21,300
When this function is called,
31

32
00:02:21,300 --> 00:02:24,570
so the value 12 will be copied into  
32

33
00:02:24,600 --> 00:02:27,270
int a,13 will be copied into B,
33

34
00:02:27,510 --> 00:02:33,780
14 will be copied into c, like that. So, and the number of arguments here should match number of arguments what
34

35
00:02:33,780 --> 00:02:34,500
you pass here.
35

36
00:02:34,800 --> 00:02:36,540
So, otherwise it will be a problem.
36

37
00:02:36,540 --> 00:02:41,940
For example, if I just pass two arguments here, then compiler will not let you to proceed
37

38
00:02:41,970 --> 00:02:43,720
so that's an error.
38

39
00:02:43,720 --> 00:02:46,990
It is saying that too few arguments to function. Right?
39

40
00:02:47,040 --> 00:02:52,590
So, let me give number here, and let me try to compile, and let's execute.
40

41
00:02:59,110 --> 00:03:04,720
So, I hope you understood about writing a simple function definition, and in the next lecture let's see
41

42
00:03:04,720 --> 00:03:07,110
how to return a value from a function,
42

43
00:03:07,120 --> 00:03:08,260
and I will see you in the next lecture.

Hey, welcome back to the lecture.Now

2
00:00:01,230 --> 00:00:05,730
let's modify this function to return a value to the caller.
2

3
00:00:05,730 --> 00:00:08,310
So, in this case main is the caller.
3

4
00:00:08,340 --> 00:00:09,590
This is the callee.
4

5
00:00:09,990 --> 00:00:12,930
So, I'm going to remove this printf from here,
5

6
00:00:12,930 --> 00:00:17,470
so let me cut that code, and let me remove all this codes.
6

7
00:00:17,490 --> 00:00:20,570
Let me just keep only one function call.
7

8
00:00:20,880 --> 00:00:27,040
And here, let me use this function to just compute the sum and return the sum back to the caller.
8

9
00:00:27,060 --> 00:00:28,360
I'm not going to print that here.
9

10
00:00:29,010 --> 00:00:34,910
So, I'm just computing the sum, and then I'm returning that sum back to the caller.
10

11
00:00:34,980 --> 00:00:42,520
So, when the program execution control comes over here, that is the value which is stored in the sum will 
11

12
00:00:42,520 --> 00:00:46,750
be return back to the caller, and the control goes back to the caller.
12

13
00:00:47,470 --> 00:00:52,800
So, here you are returning a data or value of integer type.
13

14
00:00:52,840 --> 00:00:53,110
Right?
14

15
00:00:53,140 --> 00:00:56,750
So, that's why, the return data type has to be int now.
15

16
00:00:56,800 --> 00:00:58,420
So, just mention int over here.
16

17
00:00:58,420 --> 00:00:59,830
Very simple. Right?
17

18
00:00:59,830 --> 00:01:02,640
So, after that, the value will be returned back to the caller.
18

19
00:01:02,640 --> 00:01:03,330
Right?
19

20
00:01:03,340 --> 00:01:09,190
So, that's why, you have to catch that return value here, a value which is returned from the function.
20

21
00:01:10,090 --> 00:01:17,180
So, for that, let me create a variable let's say int return value.
21

22
00:01:17,270 --> 00:01:19,610
Let me catch that here,
22

23
00:01:19,610 --> 00:01:22,310
retvalue = function_add_numbers.
23

24
00:01:22,340 --> 00:01:28,310
So, when this function is called, a sum will be computed, and sum will be returned, and that return value
24

25
00:01:28,310 --> 00:01:29,940
will be stored in this variable.
25

26
00:01:30,680 --> 00:01:39,870
And after that, let me print the return value here. Sum is equal to %d\n, return value.
26

27
00:01:39,870 --> 00:01:41,970
We also have to change the prototype.
27

28
00:01:41,970 --> 00:01:43,820
It is now int. Right?

29
00:01:43,830 --> 00:01:47,820
Let's try to compile and the build is fine.

30
00:01:48,120 --> 00:01:48,360
Right?

31
00:01:48,360 --> 00:01:54,450
So, you get the same output. So, that's about returning a value.

32
00:01:55,100 --> 00:02:01,650
So here, the value which is returned from this function is copied into the int variable of main function.

00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,350

2
00:00:01,350 --> 00:00:04,500
So, in this lecture let's do one exercise.
2

3
00:00:04,500 --> 00:00:11,430
So, write a program to do mathematical operations such as addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division
3

4
00:00:11,490 --> 00:00:18,990
of integers. So, you have to follow these 2 steps. One,do all the mathematical operations in a separate
4

5
00:00:19,050 --> 00:00:28,010
functions in a file called math.c, and call the functions from the main.c to test them.
5

6
00:00:28,080 --> 00:00:31,700
So, basically your project should contain two source files.
6

7
00:00:31,740 --> 00:00:32,860
One is  
7

8
00:00:32,880 --> 00:00:36,680
math.c, let's say it is written by you, and inside the math.c
8

9
00:00:36,690 --> 00:00:43,650
you have to write functions to do the mathematical operations. And let's say there is one more source
9

10
00:00:43,650 --> 00:00:45,180
file called main.c.
10

11
00:00:45,300 --> 00:00:51,180
Let's say, it is written by someone else, and main.c uses functions of  
11

12
00:00:51,180 --> 00:00:55,530
math.c. So, here you can use the concept of header file.
12

13
00:00:55,980 --> 00:01:03,060
So, you write all the function definitions in the math.c and you write all the function prototypes
13

14
00:01:03,150 --> 00:01:09,330
in a file called math.h, and then share that math.h with the main.c.
14

15
00:01:09,330 --> 00:01:17,250
So, the main.c will come to know what are all the functions supported by a math.c by looking at
15

16
00:01:17,250 --> 00:01:18,060
its prototype.
16

17
00:01:18,690 --> 00:01:25,440
So, I just created a project called math, and after that, let's add two source files. So, first let's create
17

18
00:01:25,440 --> 00:01:34,280
one source file and I would call this as main.c. And let me add another source file
18

19
00:01:34,340 --> 00:01:36,860
let's call it as math.c.
19

20
00:01:36,860 --> 00:01:39,190
Let me finish.
20

21
00:01:39,190 --> 00:01:42,640
So, this is excluded from the project. So, I don't know why.
21

22
00:01:42,680 --> 00:01:43,270
Don't worry.

23

Apply, Apply and Close.

math.h.

61

62

guards manually.

63
00:04:31,120 --> 00:04:38,980

63

64
00:04:38,980 --> 00:04:42,820
be any value, so it can be set of any characters.

65

00:09:09,660 --> 00:09:10,850
so let's see what happens.

149

this is a hex equivalent, and you can see that
149

so this is actually of 8 bytes. Right?

151
00:09:28,910 --> 00:09:30,450
Long long int type.
151

152
00:09:31,160 --> 00:09:35,390
So, now let's see whether our code prints the correct answer or not.
152

153
00:09:35,410 --> 00:09:37,520
So, this must be the correct answer.

154
00:09:37,520 --> 00:09:39,170
Let's see what happens here.
154

155
00:09:39,200 --> 00:09:42,440
So, by the way we are printing 64 bit value, right?
155

156
00:09:42,470 --> 00:09:46,050
That is 8 bytes value, because this actually returns
156

157
00:09:46,190 --> 00:09:47,440
Oh! sorry, sorry for that.
157

158
00:09:47,440 --> 00:09:54,560
So, I have to use multiplication here. So, this function actually returns long long int, right?
158

159
00:09:54,600 --> 00:10:03,450
So, that's why, I have to use the format specifier long long int. So, that is I64X. And now let's see how
159

160
00:10:03,450 --> 00:10:04,450
it goes.
160

161
00:10:04,470 --> 00:10:06,030
Let me run.
161

162
00:10:06,030 --> 00:10:10,140
So, now you can see that the multiplication result is wrong.
162

163
00:10:10,140 --> 00:10:10,500
Right?
163

164
00:10:10,500 --> 00:10:18,480
So, it just printed the last 4 bytes. And the upper 4 bytes are actually discarded.
164

165
00:10:18,480 --> 00:10:21,330
So, that means there is an information loss.
165

166
00:10:21,330 --> 00:10:22,350
Right?
166

167
00:10:22,380 --> 00:10:27,380
The reason for that is, if you just take a look into this function,
167

168
00:10:27,480 --> 00:10:30,270
so here we do integer multiplication.
168

169
00:10:30,300 --> 00:10:31,320
Right?
169

170
00:10:31,350 --> 00:10:34,470
So, integer multiplied with integer, results in integer.
170

171
00:10:34,470 --> 00:10:37,070
So, now in order to solve this problem,
171

172
00:10:37,080 --> 00:10:42,220
so what have to do is, you have to consider one among these number as long long  int.
172

173
00:10:42,240 --> 00:10:46,830
That means, you have to typecast one of the variable. About typecasting
173

174
00:10:46,830 --> 00:10:47,750
we'll see later.
174

175
00:10:47,790 --> 00:10:51,220
But, let's use the typecasting in order to solve this issue here.
175

176
00:10:51,390 --> 00:10:54,780
So, let's change the type of this number to
176

177
00:10:54,810 --> 00:10:58,720
long long int.So, that we can do by casting.
177

178
00:10:58,740 --> 00:11:04,650
So, in order to casting, in front of the variable name, open the parenthesis and mention the data type
178

179
00:11:04,650 --> 00:11:06,860
name to which you want to cast.
179

180
00:11:06,870 --> 00:11:08,460
So, let's do that.
180

181
00:11:08,460 --> 00:11:12,480
So, here I am casting. This is a casting to this variable.
181

182
00:11:12,510 --> 00:11:19,950
Now I'm changing the type of this variable to long long int. So now, a long long int variable multiplied
182

183
00:11:19,950 --> 00:11:25,050
with int results in long long int. So, now it should be good
183

184
00:11:25,140 --> 00:11:26,950
and more on a typecasting
184

185
00:11:26,970 --> 00:11:29,770
we'll explore in the later lectures.
185

186
00:11:29,850 --> 00:11:36,360
So, there are 2 types of casting actually or type casting. One is explicit casting and another one
186

187
00:11:36,360 --> 00:11:37,930
is implicit casting.
187

188
00:11:38,040 --> 00:11:43,520
And this is what we call as explicit casting, that is you are manually doing that.
188

189
00:11:43,560 --> 00:11:44,880
So, after that,
189

190
00:11:44,910 --> 00:11:51,640
now here also we have to cast, because integer divided by integer results in integer.
190

191
00:11:51,810 --> 00:11:56,150
So, here also you have to consider this number as float.
191

192
00:11:56,200 --> 00:12:00,510
So, one of the number as float. Let's try once again.
192

193
00:12:00,750 --> 00:12:03,700
So, now let me just printf
193

194
00:12:03,750 --> 00:12:12,210
let's also test the division, and the format specifier is %f  for float, and let's divide
194

195
00:12:12,330 --> 00:12:15,280
100 by 8.
195

196
00:12:15,290 --> 00:12:17,270
So, now let's run the program,
196

197
00:12:17,510 --> 00:12:21,870
and here you can see that we actually get the correct answer. Right?
197

198
00:12:21,930 --> 00:12:26,600
Now the answer is correct. That's about this program
198

199

and I would suggest you to reproduce this application at your desk. In the next lecture,
