━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 5

0
1
00:00:00,070 --> 00:00:01,650
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,650 --> 00:00:05,010
So, in this lecture let's learn about typecasting.
2

3
00:00:05,040 --> 00:00:13,440
So, what is typecasting? So, typecasting is a way of converting a variable or data from one data type
3

4
00:00:13,440 --> 00:00:14,520
to another data type.
4

5
00:00:15,270 --> 00:00:22,950
And the data will be truncated when the higher data type is converted into lower data type.
5

6
00:00:22,960 --> 00:00:24,210
So, now let's move forward.
6

7
00:00:24,240 --> 00:00:26,720
So, there are 2 types of casting.
7

8
00:00:26,730 --> 00:00:28,880
One is implicit casting,
8

9
00:00:28,890 --> 00:00:31,660
and another one is explicit casting.
9

10
00:00:31,710 --> 00:00:38,400
So, implicit casting is also called as assumed casting. So because, the compiler does this. 
10

11
00:00:38,400 --> 00:00:46,020
So, compiler will automatically cast some values, or some data, or some variables automatically according
11

12
00:00:46,020 --> 00:00:51,620
to compilers default rules. And another one is explicit casting.
12

13
00:00:52,170 --> 00:00:58,700
So, if you want to override what compiler does or what compiler assumes, you can use explicit casting.
13

14
00:00:58,910 --> 00:01:06,200
So, the explicit casting is as it name indicates it is telling the casting explicitly through your code.So,
14

15
00:01:06,240 --> 00:01:09,050
that's why, it is done by the programmer.
15

16
00:01:09,060 --> 00:01:12,190
Let's understand these 2 types of casting
16

17
00:01:12,240 --> 00:01:17,670
using an example. So here, I have a code snippet and here,
17

18
00:01:17,700 --> 00:01:21,060
so let's understand the first 'C' statement.
18

19
00:01:21,060 --> 00:01:24,920
So, this is actually a variable definition and initialization. Right?
19

20
00:01:25,770 --> 00:01:33,750
So, here the data type of this variable is unsigned char. So, that is actually at the left hand side. And
20

21
00:01:33,750 --> 00:01:37,110
at the right hand side of the assignment operator here.
21

22
00:01:37,560 --> 00:01:40,190
So, there are two constants, right?
22

23
00:01:40,260 --> 00:01:42,960
So, what is the memory size of this number?
23

24
00:01:43,080 --> 00:01:44,520
That is not written here,
24

25
00:01:44,520 --> 00:01:44,880
right?
25

26
00:01:44,880 --> 00:01:46,850
You have just written a number. Right?
26

27
00:01:46,860 --> 00:01:49,930
So, this is actually a one byte data, right?
27

28
00:01:49,930 --> 00:01:57,140
So, which is written in hex format 0x87 is a 1 byte data. That is true for you,
28

29
00:01:57,150 --> 00:02:00,020
so but for the compiler it is 4 byte data.
29

30
00:02:00,530 --> 00:02:04,230
So, this will be treated as a 4 byte data,
30

31
00:02:04,250 --> 00:02:09,500
that is the default data type used for a constant by the compiler.
31

32
00:02:09,560 --> 00:02:16,490
So, and this is a 2 byte data for you, but for the compiler it is again a 4 byte data.
32

33
00:02:16,490 --> 00:02:24,810
So, if you don't mention any data type for this constant, then it is considered as an int type.
33

34
00:02:24,950 --> 00:02:26,520
That's a default type.
34

35
00:02:26,600 --> 00:02:31,910
So, if I try to compile this code. Here it is.
35

36
00:02:31,920 --> 00:02:39,330
There is a warning from the compiler saying, unsigned conversion from int to this is an int, the value
36

37
00:02:39,420 --> 00:02:46,270
is of int type, and you are trying to take that int type and trying to put that into a variable of char
37

38
00:02:46,310 --> 00:02:48,600
type, right? or unsigned char type.
38

39
00:02:48,720 --> 00:02:54,820
So, you are trying to fit 4 byte data into a one byte variable. Right?
39

40
00:02:54,900 --> 00:03:01,470
And that's why, there is an warning saying, the unsigned conversion from 'int' to 'unsigned char' changes the
40

41
00:03:01,470 --> 00:03:03,950
value from this one to this one.
41

42
00:03:03,990 --> 00:03:06,930
So, there is actually an information loss.
42

43
00:03:06,960 --> 00:03:15,330
So, what compiler does is, it actually truncates this int to 1 byte data, and then it stores that data into
43

44
00:03:15,330 --> 00:03:21,930
this variable. So, compiler is warning you that, it actually did the implicit casting and in that process
44

45
00:03:22,080 --> 00:03:25,960
it finds that there is actually an information loss.
45

46
00:03:26,550 --> 00:03:32,870
So, what if I convert this into 01 and let's say 0089.
46

47
00:03:33,060 --> 00:03:36,180
Let's see what happens to this statement.
47

48
00:03:36,630 --> 00:03:39,240
Here we can see that there is no warning. Here
48

49
00:03:39,240 --> 00:03:46,120
also compiler is trying to put 4 byte of data into 1 byte variable.
49

50
00:03:46,130 --> 00:03:47,030
Right?
50

51
00:03:47,050 --> 00:03:49,020
So, but there is no information loss here.
51

52
00:03:49,680 --> 00:03:53,440
So, result will be 0x8A,  right?
52

53
00:03:53,490 --> 00:03:57,910
in this case. So, 8A can be stored into data.
53

54
00:03:57,930 --> 00:03:58,280
Right?
54

55
00:03:58,950 --> 00:04:00,990
So, hence there is no information loss.
55

56
00:04:00,990 --> 00:04:07,980
So, that's why, compiler is not warning you in this case. So, compiler actually sees this statement like
56

57
00:04:07,980 --> 00:04:09,270
this.
57

58
00:04:09,270 --> 00:04:17,820
And after that, it truncates or casts this 4 byte data into 1 byte data, and then it stores in this variable.
58

59
00:04:18,120 --> 00:04:23,400
So, that actually involves implicit casting.
59

60
00:04:23,460 --> 00:04:23,790
All right.
60

61
00:04:23,800 --> 00:04:29,200
So, now if you want to avoid this warning or if you want to resolve this warning, then you have to use
61

62
00:04:29,230 --> 00:04:31,000
explicit casting here.
62

63
00:04:31,000 --> 00:04:36,760
That means you have to tell the compiler explicitly that, just store 1 byte of the result into the destination
63

64
00:04:36,850 --> 00:04:37,590
variable.
64

65
00:04:37,810 --> 00:04:39,990
For that, you can use explicit casting here.
65

66
00:04:40,210 --> 00:04:47,860
So, what I would do is, I'll put these 2 numbers and the + operator in this parenthesis. And after that,
66

67
00:04:48,070 --> 00:04:57,470
what I do is, I actually cast the result to unsigned char. So, this yields one result. Right?
67

68
00:04:57,500 --> 00:05:01,680
So, that is of type int and I am casting that to
68

69
00:05:01,710 --> 00:05:08,730
char explicitly by using this explicit casting. This is called as explicit casting.
69

70
00:05:08,930 --> 00:05:15,130
So, it will not generate any warnings here. Because, the programmer is aware of what is doing.
70

71
00:05:15,170 --> 00:05:16,400
Right?
71

72
00:05:16,400 --> 00:05:18,140
So, there is no warning for that.
72

73
00:05:18,140 --> 00:05:21,160
This is what we call as explicit casting.
73

74
00:05:21,180 --> 00:05:21,540
All right.
74

75
00:05:21,550 --> 00:05:22,970
Now, let's see what's happening here.
75

76
00:05:23,060 --> 00:05:26,390
So, you have a variable called result of float type.
76

77
00:05:26,390 --> 00:05:27,390
Right?
77

78
00:05:27,410 --> 00:05:33,710
So, you have declared that variable as float, because you want to store a real number.
78

79
00:05:33,710 --> 00:05:34,720
Right?
79

80
00:05:34,730 --> 00:05:36,350
See what's happening here.
80

81
00:05:36,350 --> 00:05:38,710
So, when the result is printed.
81

82
00:05:39,050 --> 00:05:43,640
So, 80 divided by 3 should result in fraction part, right?
82

83
00:05:43,700 --> 00:05:46,490
So, that fraction part is omitted.
83

84
00:05:46,790 --> 00:05:47,660
Right?
84

85
00:05:47,660 --> 00:05:49,730
So, that means there is an information loss.
85

86
00:05:49,730 --> 00:05:50,420
Correct?
86

87
00:05:50,420 --> 00:05:52,690
So, the fraction part is lost.
87

88
00:05:52,700 --> 00:05:54,100
So, what's happening here.
88

89
00:05:54,110 --> 00:05:56,870
So, first of all compiler sees this as an integer.
89

90
00:05:56,900 --> 00:05:57,560
Right?
90

91
00:05:57,620 --> 00:06:02,350
So, integer divided by another integer, that will give you another integer.
91

92
00:06:02,390 --> 00:06:02,690
Right?
92

93
00:06:02,690 --> 00:06:05,240
So, integer divided by integer remains integer.
93

94
00:06:05,300 --> 00:06:07,760
So, that's why, the fraction part is lost.
94

95
00:06:08,090 --> 00:06:10,670
After that you are storing the result,
95

96
00:06:10,790 --> 00:06:15,770
that's why, only integer part is getting displayed.
96

97
00:06:15,990 --> 00:06:22,030
Now, in order to solve this you have to tell the compiler to consider this number as a float or this
97

98
00:06:22,030 --> 00:06:24,490
number as a float, not as an int.
98

99
00:06:24,520 --> 00:06:27,720
And here you have to do explicit casting.
99

100
00:06:27,730 --> 00:06:30,840
So, let me do the explicit casting to the numerator.
100

101
00:06:30,880 --> 00:06:34,490
Let me call it as float. Here,
101

102
00:06:34,490 --> 00:06:41,580
I have converted the data type of this number from into float. Compiler will consider this as float. So,
102

103
00:06:41,610 --> 00:06:44,340
a float divided by another float.
103

104
00:06:44,400 --> 00:06:52,110
So here, numerator is float. So, hence compiler treats denominator also as float implicitly.
104

105
00:06:52,110 --> 00:06:55,340
So, it does implicit casting. So here,
105

106
00:06:55,350 --> 00:06:59,820
basically what's happening is float divided by float, which results in a float.
106

107
00:06:59,820 --> 00:07:01,860
And now you're going to get the correct answer,
107

108
00:07:05,490 --> 00:07:10,570
or you can move this casting to denominator no problem, can even do this.
108

109
00:07:10,590 --> 00:07:12,200
So, here 3 is casted.
109

110
00:07:15,130 --> 00:07:17,520
So, which also gives the same result.
110

111
00:07:17,560 --> 00:07:21,940
So, I hope you understood about implicit casting and explicit casting.
111

112
00:07:21,940 --> 00:07:27,660
And remember that, warnings related to casting is very dangerous. Because, most of the time you will lose
112

113
00:07:27,670 --> 00:07:28,330
information.
113

114
00:07:28,330 --> 00:07:28,690
Right?
114

115
00:07:28,690 --> 00:07:33,850
So, that's why, those warnings should be treated with utmost care,
115

116
00:07:33,850 --> 00:07:39,490
and as I said in the beginning of this course warnings are always dangerous and you should always try
116

117
00:07:39,490 --> 00:07:41,520
to resolve those warnings.
117

118
00:07:41,530 --> 00:07:45,190
So, with that note I would like to end this lecture and I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 6

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:00,430
All right.
1

2
00:00:00,450 --> 00:00:03,100
So, now let's continue our discussion about casting.
2

3
00:00:03,180 --> 00:00:08,310
So here, in this code, I would like to include some other data, some different data.
3

4
00:00:08,410 --> 00:00:09,150
So, this time a
4

5
00:00:09,240 --> 00:00:11,200
let me use 6 byte data.
5

6
00:00:11,370 --> 00:00:13,650
So, let me write the 6 byte data,
6

7
00:00:13,740 --> 00:00:15,520
so I'll write it in hex.
7

8
00:00:15,540 --> 00:00:20,300
Let's say, 1FFFFFFFA0.
8

9
00:00:20,310 --> 00:00:21,790
So, this is a 5 byte data.
9

10
00:00:21,870 --> 00:00:22,660
Right?
10

11
00:00:22,800 --> 00:00:24,270
Let's say B0.
11

12
00:00:24,540 --> 00:00:26,860
So, this is actually a 6  byte data.
12

13
00:00:27,540 --> 00:00:30,960
So, now let me add a 2 byte data to this.
13

14
00:00:30,960 --> 00:00:37,360
Let's say, 1245. So, this is a 6 byte data and  this is a 2 byte data.
14

15
00:00:37,360 --> 00:00:38,200
Right?
15

16
00:00:38,260 --> 00:00:41,890
So, 6 byte data cannot be represented by int data type.
16

17
00:00:41,890 --> 00:00:42,350
Right?
17

18
00:00:42,370 --> 00:00:50,510
Compiler will consider this number as long long int, not as int. And compiler
18

19
00:00:50,530 --> 00:00:55,680
treats this number also as a long long int data.
19

20
00:00:55,720 --> 00:01:03,680
So, that means there is a implicit casting on this number, a to upgrade it's data type from into long long
20

21
00:01:03,700 --> 00:01:11,830
int. This is actually addition of a long long int with another long long int. Let's see what happens when
21

22
00:01:11,830 --> 00:01:18,580
you try to compile this code, and there is a warning saying you are trying to store a long long int
22

23
00:01:18,580 --> 00:01:25,670
data to a variable whose type is char, and it is warning you about the data loss.
23

24
00:01:25,700 --> 00:01:31,300
Alright. So, with that note I would like to end the discussion on a  typecasting and we are going to encounter
24

25
00:01:31,330 --> 00:01:38,370
typecasting again when we cover pointers. And it is very important when dealing with pointers.


==================================================================================


section 8---microcontroller and hello world


video 1

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,130
Hey, welcome back to the course.
1

2
00:00:01,140 --> 00:00:07,020
So, in this lecture, let's write a 'Hello World' program for our embedded board which is based on ARM Cortex
2

3
00:00:07,050 --> 00:00:08,130
M4 processor.
3

4
00:00:09,290 --> 00:00:17,620
So, now in our case we'll start a new STM32 project. So, to create a new STM32 project, go to file and
4

5
00:00:17,620 --> 00:00:28,500
then go to new, and then click on STM32 project. So, after that just select this option, and click next.
5

6
00:00:28,500 --> 00:00:31,020
So, now you have to give a name for your project.
6

7
00:00:31,050 --> 00:00:34,920
So, I'm going to give a 001HelloWorld.
7

8
00:00:34,920 --> 00:00:42,900
And after that, the language is 'C', and here targeted project type select empty, and after that click
8

9
00:00:42,900 --> 00:00:44,070
finish.
9

10
00:00:44,070 --> 00:00:44,360
All right.
10

11
00:00:44,390 --> 00:00:47,400
So, we just created an empty Hello World project,
11

12
00:00:47,640 --> 00:00:50,610
so it has added a couple of folders here.
12

13
00:00:50,610 --> 00:00:58,530
So, the Inc folder or include folder which is so where we keep all our header files of our project, and
13

14
00:00:58,590 --> 00:01:03,990
the source folder so where we keep all our source codes that is .c files.
14

15
00:01:03,990 --> 00:01:08,460
And here we can see that, the IDE is already added three files
15

16
00:01:08,480 --> 00:01:10,710
main.c, syscalls.c,
16

17
00:01:10,710 --> 00:01:12,390
sysmem.c.
17

18
00:01:12,390 --> 00:01:17,650
And after that, important thing here is the startup code for the microcontroller.
18

19
00:01:17,670 --> 00:01:23,760
So, before working with any microcontroller, it doesn't matter whether it is from ST, or whether
19

20
00:01:23,760 --> 00:01:28,350
it is from TI, or prescale or whatever it is.
20

21
00:01:28,380 --> 00:01:34,420
So, you should have a startup code for that microcontroller in your project.
21

22
00:01:34,470 --> 00:01:36,930
So, that is very very important 
22

23
00:01:36,930 --> 00:01:42,900
and so for this lecture we are not going to explore the startup code, because that would be too early
23

24
00:01:42,900 --> 00:01:43,620
to explore.
24

25
00:01:43,950 --> 00:01:50,610
But, in later sections I have a plan to introduce writing startup file from scratch.
25

26
00:01:50,880 --> 00:01:56,260
So, we'll see that later when we understand more about the microcontroller and the Embedded 'C' concept.
26

27
00:01:57,010 --> 00:02:02,800
So, the main thing here what you should observe is, it has added main source file where we are going to
27

28
00:02:02,800 --> 00:02:05,560
write our code for the microcontroller.
28

29
00:02:05,560 --> 00:02:05,920
All right.
29

30
00:02:05,950 --> 00:02:10,210
Now, let's write a program to print the message Hello world.
30

31
00:02:10,210 --> 00:02:14,510
So, for that, let's use the standard library function printf.
31

32
00:02:14,650 --> 00:02:15,420
Let me write
32

33
00:02:15,420 --> 00:02:24,830
printf("Hello World"). And since it is a standard library function, I have to include stdio.h 
33

34
00:02:24,830 --> 00:02:25,980
Alright.
34

35
00:02:26,040 --> 00:02:28,880
So, now you may be wondering how would this print work.
35

36
00:02:28,920 --> 00:02:35,060
Because, we don't have any display, or standard output device, which is connected to our embedded board.
36

37
00:02:35,060 --> 00:02:36,050
Right?
37

38
00:02:36,150 --> 00:02:42,410
So, we don't have any monitor, or we don't have an LCD, which are connected to our embedded board.
38

39
00:02:42,930 --> 00:02:46,250
So, how are we going to see the output then? For that,
39

40
00:02:46,250 --> 00:02:51,850
there is one solution, and that solution comes from the ARM Cortex Processor itself.
40

41
00:02:51,900 --> 00:02:54,440
Let's explore that. All right.
41

42
00:02:54,460 --> 00:03:05,330
Now, let's discuss about using printf outputs on ARM Cortex M3/M4/M7 based MCUs. This discussion
42

43
00:03:05,450 --> 00:03:14,160
is only applicable to microcontrollers which are based on ARM Cortex M3 plus processors, M3 or M4 or
43

44
00:03:14,180 --> 00:03:15,880
M7 or higher.
44

45
00:03:15,890 --> 00:03:22,880
So, this discussion will not be applicable to ARM Cortex M0 or M0 plus processors.
45

46
00:03:23,510 --> 00:03:31,040
So, in that case, you will not be able to use the ARM Cortex processors trace functionality. So, which
46

47
00:03:31,040 --> 00:03:34,940
we are going to discuss in a moment. In ARM Cortex
47

48
00:03:34,970 --> 00:03:45,200
Processor, we can make printf work by using the SWO pin of the SWD interface. So, SWO stands for
48

49
00:03:45,260 --> 00:03:47,600
Serial Wire Output.
49

50
00:03:47,790 --> 00:03:49,690
Let's explore more on this.
50

51
00:03:49,730 --> 00:03:51,980
First of all this is your board. Right?
51

52
00:03:52,010 --> 00:04:02,000
So, you have a board, which is STM32F4 discovery board or Nucleo board. And your board has a microcontroller.
52

53
00:04:02,130 --> 00:04:02,990
Right?
53

54
00:04:03,030 --> 00:04:10,650
So, let's say the microcontroller is STM32F407VG. So, this is a microcontroller which is produced
54

55
00:04:10,680 --> 00:04:19,560
by ST microelectronics. That microcontroller has a processor inside, right? which is ARM Cortex M4
55

56
00:04:19,560 --> 00:04:20,260
processor.
56

57
00:04:20,280 --> 00:04:28,710
Right? Now our board also has one more circuitry, so which is at the front end of the board and that we
57

58
00:04:28,710 --> 00:04:30,550
call as ST link
58

59
00:04:30,660 --> 00:04:38,140
V2 or V1 debug circuitry. So, that is a ST link onboard debug circuitry.
59

60
00:04:38,520 --> 00:04:44,850
So, by using that debug circuitry you are PC communicates with the board. Through that debug circuitry
60

61
00:04:44,880 --> 00:04:51,240
you actually write your program to the internal flash of the microcontroller, you read various memory
61

62
00:04:51,240 --> 00:04:58,290
locations of the microcontroller, you make processor run, you make processor stop, so all those debug related
62

63
00:04:58,290 --> 00:05:05,970
activity you do by taking help of this debug circuitry, which is present on the board. Debug circuitry
63

64
00:05:05,970 --> 00:05:13,620
will talk to your PC over a USB connection. So, there is a pin called SWO pin, which is coming all
64

65
00:05:13,620 --> 00:05:20,630
the way from ARM Cortex M processor and it is connected to the debug circuitry. So, the printf
65

66
00:05:20,760 --> 00:05:23,800
actually works over this SWO pin.
66

67
00:05:24,120 --> 00:05:25,930
Let's explore further.
67

68
00:05:25,980 --> 00:05:27,630
So, for that I'm going to zoom
68

69
00:05:27,630 --> 00:05:33,990
this ARM Cortex M4 processor, let's considered only ARM Cortex M4 processor.
69

70
00:05:33,990 --> 00:05:42,720
So, inside the ARM Cortex M4 processor, there is a unit or a peripheral called  ITM unit. ITM stands
70

71
00:05:42,720 --> 00:05:47,110
for Instrumentation Trace Macrocell Unit.
71

72
00:05:47,190 --> 00:05:49,510
So, this is inside the processor.
72

73
00:05:50,010 --> 00:05:57,870
So, the ITM is an optional application driven trace source that supports printf style debugging
73

74
00:05:57,900 --> 00:06:06,240
to trace operating system and application events, and it can also be used to generate diagnostic system
74

75
00:06:06,240 --> 00:06:07,200
information.
75

76
00:06:07,230 --> 00:06:13,050
So, this unit is only available in ARM Cortex M3 or above processors.
76

77
00:06:13,080 --> 00:06:21,390
So, it is not available in ARM Cortex M0 processor. And to debug the processor, so debug means if you want
77

78
00:06:21,390 --> 00:06:28,830
to read the memory location, if you want to read the processor related register, if you want to make the
78

79
00:06:28,830 --> 00:06:32,520
processor halt, or if you want it to run.
79

80
00:06:32,580 --> 00:06:38,070
So, if you want to do all these activities then we do that using the debug interface.
80

81
00:06:38,310 --> 00:06:47,700
The debug interface what we are using here is SWD. So, SWD stands for Serial Wire Debug, which is
81

82
00:06:47,700 --> 00:06:57,030
a two-wire protocol for accessing the ARM debug interface. SWD works over SWD connector, and that
82

83
00:06:57,290 --> 00:07:07,180
SWD connector has three pins. In which two pins are used for debug and one pin is used for trace. So, trace
83

84
00:07:07,200 --> 00:07:11,640
means in order to get the trace related information from the processor.
84

85
00:07:11,640 --> 00:07:16,080
Let's explore some more points about SWD debug interface.
85

86
00:07:16,080 --> 00:07:23,690
Now, the Serial Wire Debug it is a two-wire protocol for accessing the ARM debug interface.
86

87
00:07:24,040 --> 00:07:32,740
So, it is part of the ARM Debug Interface Specification v5 and it is an alternative to JTAG. The physical
87

88
00:07:32,740 --> 00:07:41,740
layer of SWD consists of only two lines. One is called SWDIO which is a bidirectional data line, which
88

89
00:07:41,740 --> 00:07:49,730
carries debug related data, and SWCLK, a clock which is driven by the host.
89

90
00:07:49,840 --> 00:07:54,870
So, in our board the host is actually the  ST link circuitry.
90

91
00:07:55,000 --> 00:08:01,170
So, SWDIO which is a data line, which actually carries the debug related commands.
91

92
00:08:01,390 --> 00:08:09,360
So, like for example, when you insert a breakpoint in your IDE, so that information will be sent over
92

93
00:08:09,400 --> 00:08:11,550
SWDIO data line to the processor.
93

94
00:08:12,070 --> 00:08:18,000
So, if you want to stop the processor from the IDE, then that information is actually carried over the 
94

95
00:08:18,020 --> 00:08:22,720
SWDIO line with the help of SWCLK to the processor.
95

96
00:08:22,720 --> 00:08:29,590
So, in order to talk to the processor you can use these two pins of the SWD interface. SWDIO and
96

97
00:08:29,590 --> 00:08:30,690
SWCLK.
97

98
00:08:30,790 --> 00:08:38,650
So, both are managed by the ST link circuitry which is present on your boards. So, by using SWD interface
98

99
00:08:38,770 --> 00:08:42,030
you should be able to program MCUs internal flash,
99

100
00:08:42,070 --> 00:08:51,610
you can access memory regions, add breakpoints, stop or run the CPU. So,other good thing about SWD is you can
100

101
00:08:51,610 --> 00:08:57,450
use the serial wire viewer for your printf statements for debugging.
101

102
00:08:57,550 --> 00:09:03,370
So, as I said, SWD comes with only two pins which are used for debug, but there is one optional pin that
102

103
00:09:03,370 --> 00:09:09,410
is what we call as SWO which we can use for printf functionality.
103

104
00:09:09,970 --> 00:09:14,850
So now, there is also another debug interface which is called as  
104

105
00:09:14,850 --> 00:09:23,350
JTAG. The difference between JTAG and SWD is, JTAG actually needs more pins than SWD. JTAG was
105

106
00:09:23,350 --> 00:09:31,570
the traditional mechanism for debug connections for ARM7 or ARM9 family, but with Cortex-M family, ARM
106

107
00:09:31,570 --> 00:09:40,270
introduced the Serial Wire Debug interface. SWD is designed to reduce the pin count required for debug
107

108
00:09:40,360 --> 00:09:44,170
from the 4 used by JTAG down to 2.
108

109
00:09:44,230 --> 00:09:52,180
So, in addition, SWD interface provides one more pin called SWO which is used for Single Wire
109

110
00:09:52,210 --> 00:09:57,040
Viewing, which is a low cost tracing technology. Lets move forward.
110

111
00:09:57,070 --> 00:09:59,500
So, if I zoom this ITM unit further.
111

112
00:09:59,530 --> 00:10:05,950
So, what you see is a FIFO, or you can call it as a buffer, or a register.
112

113
00:10:05,960 --> 00:10:09,920
It's a hardware buffer, which is there inside the ITM unit.
113

114
00:10:09,970 --> 00:10:18,220
So, now all you need to do is write the printf data into this FIFO. So, that FIFO is actually connected
114

115
00:10:18,220 --> 00:10:24,730
to the SWO pin, which is coming out of the processor and it is coming all the way to your debug circuitry
115

116
00:10:24,730 --> 00:10:26,700
which is present on the board. Right?
116

117
00:10:26,830 --> 00:10:34,210
So, the moment your printf writes into this FIFO, that messages will actually come over the SWO pin,
117

118
00:10:34,330 --> 00:10:35,590
and you then capture it.
118

119
00:10:35,590 --> 00:10:36,100
That's it.
119

120
00:10:36,550 --> 00:10:43,720
So, in the IDE there is a provision to capture this SWO pin. So, remember that not all IDE support
120

121
00:10:43,780 --> 00:10:51,240
this feature of capturing SWO pin. So, but fortunately STM32Cube IDE and true studio,
121

122
00:10:51,250 --> 00:10:58,140
so those IDE's actually support these functionalities. So, SWO pin is connected to ST link circuitry
122

123
00:10:58,150 --> 00:11:02,440
of the board and can be captured using our debug software.
123

124
00:11:02,800 --> 00:11:09,910
So, let's see that in a moment, how to do that? But, this is a idea behind how printf works in ARM Cortex
124

125
00:11:09,970 --> 00:11:11,120
MX processor.
125

126
00:11:11,200 --> 00:11:17,440
So, there is a ITM unit and it has a FIFO and your printf  somehow should write into that FIFO,
126

127
00:11:17,560 --> 00:11:23,290
and that FIFO is connected to the SWO pin and through that you actually get that message back to the
127

128
00:11:23,360 --> 00:11:23,980
IDE.
128

129
00:11:24,070 --> 00:11:24,370
Alright.
129

130
00:11:24,370 --> 00:11:29,980
So, now in our your application so you have to do some settings or you have to add some code, in order
130

131
00:11:29,980 --> 00:11:34,850
to make your printf  writes into that ITMs FIFO.
131

132
00:11:34,960 --> 00:11:42,250
So, for that you have to add a small code snippet, so that code you can get in our repository. So, you just
132

133
00:11:42,250 --> 00:11:49,410
go to the git repository and here just copy this code. So, once you copy this code,
133

134
00:11:49,430 --> 00:11:56,210
so what you have to do is, you have to open the project and go to syscalls.c.
134

135
00:11:56,270 --> 00:11:57,910
So, in the syscalls.c,
135

136
00:11:58,610 --> 00:12:03,010
so after this  #include just paste that code.
136

137
00:12:03,040 --> 00:12:03,510
All right.
137

138
00:12:03,650 --> 00:12:07,040
So, and after that just save this file.
138

139
00:12:07,040 --> 00:12:13,220
So, this is actually the implementation of printf like feature using ARM Cortex ITM functionality,
139

140
00:12:13,280 --> 00:12:17,440
and this function will not work for ARM M0/M0+.
140

141
00:12:17,530 --> 00:12:23,450
So, if you are using Cortex M0, then you can use semihosting feature of openOCD, so which I covered
141

142
00:12:23,450 --> 00:12:31,030
in a separate video. And this is a code what we added, and which actually writes into that FIFO.
142

143
00:12:31,040 --> 00:12:41,900
So, now let's go to the syscalls.c and here you should identify a function called write. Here is
143

144
00:12:41,900 --> 00:12:43,630
a function called write.
144

145
00:12:43,640 --> 00:12:51,300
So, what you have to do is, you have to comment out this line, so instead of that call this function 
145

146
00:12:51,300 --> 00:12:52,560
ITM_SendChar.
146

147
00:12:52,650 --> 00:12:58,930
So, just call that function here and give this argument . Right?
147

148
00:12:58,990 --> 00:13:04,740
So, instead of calling this you are just calling our ITM message sending function.
148

149
00:13:04,780 --> 00:13:06,220
So, that's all you have to do.
149

150
00:13:06,250 --> 00:13:09,610
And after that, you are ready to test this application.
150

151
00:13:09,610 --> 00:13:09,880
All right.
151

152
00:13:09,880 --> 00:13:16,360
So, what's happening here is that, so when you call a printf function which is actually a standard library
152

153
00:13:16,360 --> 00:13:17,400
function.
153

154
00:13:17,500 --> 00:13:21,300
So, the printf function is actually implemented in 'C' standard library.
154

155
00:13:21,310 --> 00:13:22,020
Right?
155

156
00:13:22,030 --> 00:13:27,230
So, the printf implementation  then calls lower level function called
156

157
00:13:27,250 --> 00:13:28,590
write.
157

158
00:13:28,600 --> 00:13:32,280
So, that write is actually implemented in syscalls.c.
158

159
00:13:32,890 --> 00:13:36,360
And in that write, we actually call our API
159

160
00:13:36,400 --> 00:13:37,230
ITM_SendChar
160

161
00:13:37,240 --> 00:13:41,010
in order to populate the ITMs FIFO.
161

162
00:13:41,020 --> 00:13:45,340
And that's how we actually divert the printf to the ITM unit
162

163
00:13:45,580 --> 00:13:49,040
and then we get the trace through the SWO pin.
163

164
00:13:49,100 --> 00:13:53,500
Suppose, if you have LCD, then you can call LCD_SendChar here.
164

165
00:13:53,890 --> 00:13:57,340
So, if you have UART, then you can call UART_SendChar here.
165

166
00:13:58,240 --> 00:13:58,750
So now,
166

167
00:13:58,750 --> 00:14:03,130
after that, in order to test this application you have to first compile it, isn't it?
167

168
00:14:03,130 --> 00:14:05,530
So, that what we call as cross compilation.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:00,360
All right.
1

2
00:00:00,380 --> 00:00:07,200
So, now in order to compile this program, so all you need to do is just select the project, and then right
2

3
00:00:07,200 --> 00:00:14,370
click, and then click on build project, or you can also select the project and click on this build icon
3

4
00:00:14,370 --> 00:00:14,840
here.
4

5
00:00:14,850 --> 00:00:22,420
Let's compile the code. So, here you can see that under console you should be seeing these logs.
5

6
00:00:23,040 --> 00:00:28,470
So, here you can see that this is a compiler invoked by this IDE. And by the way, you need not to worry 
6

7
00:00:28,470 --> 00:00:30,130
about installing cross compiler here.
7

8
00:00:30,390 --> 00:00:35,800
So because, all the build tools, cross compilation tools, everything is installed by the IDE.
8

9
00:00:36,060 --> 00:00:39,340
Everything comes with the IDE, so you need not to install anything extra.
9

10
00:00:39,930 --> 00:00:44,700
So, if you want to upgrade this compiler to the newer version, then you may have to change the compiler
10

11
00:00:44,700 --> 00:00:46,880
settings and that will see later.
11

12
00:00:46,960 --> 00:00:48,200
So, but other than that,
12

13
00:00:48,240 --> 00:00:51,080
so I don't think you need to touch any compiler settings
13

14
00:00:51,090 --> 00:00:51,880
as of now.
14

15
00:00:51,930 --> 00:00:59,340
So, this is a cross compiler that is  arm-none-eabi-gcc, which is used to compile this source code for the
15

16
00:00:59,340 --> 00:01:01,020
target architecture.
16

17
00:01:01,020 --> 00:01:01,280
All right.
17

18
00:01:01,290 --> 00:01:08,820
So, what is Cross compilation? So, Cross compilation means a you actually compile a program with the intention
18

19
00:01:08,820 --> 00:01:12,540
of producing code for some other architecture.
19

20
00:01:12,540 --> 00:01:20,220
For example, in this case we are using the cross compiler arm-none-eabi-gcc.
20

21
00:01:20,250 --> 00:01:21,750
So, this is a cross compiler.
21

22
00:01:21,810 --> 00:01:22,550
OK.
22

23
00:01:22,590 --> 00:01:32,320
And this cross compiler runs on the host machine, but it produces executable for different machine. For
23

24
00:01:32,320 --> 00:01:32,860
example
24

25
00:01:32,860 --> 00:01:36,400
in this case, the target machine is different a
25

26
00:01:36,400 --> 00:01:43,930
it is based on ARM architecture. So, that's why, compiler is called as a cross compiler when it runs on
26

27
00:01:43,930 --> 00:01:51,760
one architecture, but it produces the code for different architecture. So, that compiler is called as cross
27

28
00:01:51,760 --> 00:01:52,640
compiler
28

29
00:01:52,750 --> 00:01:56,380
and the process is called as cross compilation.
29

30
00:01:56,440 --> 00:02:02,670
So here, the cross compiler actually produces different types of executables
30

31
00:02:02,720 --> 00:02:03,290
OK
31

32
00:02:03,460 --> 00:02:04,450
for the target.
32

33
00:02:04,730 --> 00:02:12,370
So, these are all the different executable types like .elf, .bin, .hex.
33

34
00:02:12,390 --> 00:02:12,810
OK.
34

35
00:02:13,060 --> 00:02:20,100
So, .elf is a type of executable which stands for executable and linkable format.
35

36
00:02:20,170 --> 00:02:23,780
It's a type of executable, which is used for debugging.
36

37
00:02:23,800 --> 00:02:24,270
OK.
37

38
00:02:25,150 --> 00:02:34,000
So, .bin and .hex are type of executables, which are actually pure binary executables which are used
38

39
00:02:34,000 --> 00:02:35,200
for productions.
39

40
00:02:35,290 --> 00:02:43,750
OK? .elf is used during debugging. Suppose, if your giving an executable of your project to your customer,
40

41
00:02:44,520 --> 00:02:51,850
then you should be giving .bin or .hex, not .elf remember. Because, elf has all the information
41

42
00:02:51,850 --> 00:02:59,350
all the debug information and anyone can use elf analyzers to you know to read
42

43
00:02:59,350 --> 00:03:04,840
what are the contents of the elf and they will come to know about the code implementation everything.
43

44
00:03:04,910 --> 00:03:12,160
OK? So because, there are tools available to analyze .elf files and they can disassemble the
44

45
00:03:12,160 --> 00:03:16,220
code and they can get the information about the project.
45

46
00:03:16,250 --> 00:03:17,150
OK.
46

47
00:03:17,380 --> 00:03:22,750
In this course, we will be using .elf format, because in this course we will be debugging our code
47

48
00:03:23,080 --> 00:03:24,190
on the target. Isn't it?
48

49
00:03:24,490 --> 00:03:29,400
So, debugging is not possible if you use .bin or .hex.
49

50
00:03:29,440 --> 00:03:36,010
As I said, those are pure binary files and those are used during production.
50

51
00:03:36,040 --> 00:03:36,570
Okay.
51

52
00:03:36,940 --> 00:03:38,590
And what is Native compilation?
52

53
00:03:38,600 --> 00:03:44,770
So, Native compiler means the compiler runs on a host machine and produces executable which also runs
53

54
00:03:44,770 --> 00:03:45,610
on the same machine.
54

55
00:03:45,670 --> 00:03:46,110
Okay.
55

56
00:03:46,360 --> 00:03:48,960
So, this process is called as native compilation.
56

57
00:03:49,030 --> 00:03:49,240
alright.
57

58
00:03:49,240 --> 00:03:49,900
So for,
58

59
00:03:49,900 --> 00:03:57,130
whatever the code that we wrote for our PC that is actually native compilation.
59

60
00:03:57,170 --> 00:03:58,600
OK. Let's move forward.
60

61
00:03:58,600 --> 00:04:01,590
So, here we actually got .elf of
61

62
00:04:01,590 --> 00:04:03,190
Hello World project.
62

63
00:04:03,280 --> 00:04:07,110
After that, we have to now load this project into our target.
63

64
00:04:07,540 --> 00:04:10,910
So now, for that you have to first connect the target to your machine.
64

65
00:04:10,930 --> 00:04:13,790
I'm going to connect the target to my machine,
65

66
00:04:14,170 --> 00:04:21,290
and after that just right click here, and go to debug as, and click on debug configurations.
66

67
00:04:21,450 --> 00:04:22,100
So here,
67

68
00:04:22,150 --> 00:04:25,810
just double click on this STM32MCU debugging,
68

69
00:04:26,050 --> 00:04:35,630
so it creates a debug configuration here, and so the name you can keep this as it is, no problem.
69

70
00:04:35,660 --> 00:04:42,530
So, it actually tries to load this file, that is .elf which is created during the compilation
70

71
00:04:42,650 --> 00:04:43,720
which is write here.
71

72
00:04:44,150 --> 00:04:47,870
And so don't change anything here, go to debugger,
72

73
00:04:47,870 --> 00:04:52,700
and here you should be selecting ST-LINK GDB server.
73

74
00:04:52,700 --> 00:04:56,590
So, it also has option for openOCD, but we will be using ST-LINK.
74

75
00:04:56,780 --> 00:04:57,490
So, here
75

76
00:04:57,500 --> 00:05:04,960
and after that interface is a SWD, and after that here is a option for Serial Wire Viewer.
76

77
00:05:05,090 --> 00:05:06,760
So, just enable that.
77

78
00:05:06,980 --> 00:05:10,920
And after that, core clock is 16 megahertz, so you can keep that.
78

79
00:05:10,970 --> 00:05:18,290
And after that, SWO clock which is default to 2000 kilohertz and you can keep that, no problem.
79

80
00:05:18,290 --> 00:05:21,890
And after that you need not to do anything here, in the Startup also
80

81
00:05:21,950 --> 00:05:25,210
you need not to do anything. Just click on Apply,
81

82
00:05:25,340 --> 00:05:34,380
and after that you can click close. And after that right click on this project, and you can again go to
82

83
00:05:34,380 --> 00:05:42,180
the debug as and select this one, STM32MCU application. The IDE will load the project into
83

84
00:05:42,180 --> 00:05:46,770
the target hardware and it will switch into the debug perspective.
84

85
00:05:46,800 --> 00:05:51,390
The IDE is asking you to change the perspective into debug perspective.
85

86
00:05:51,750 --> 00:06:01,670
So, for that, you can click switch. So, here you can see that the code is loaded onto the target and the
86

87
00:06:01,670 --> 00:06:06,340
execution is stopped at the very first instruction of the main function.
87

88
00:06:06,350 --> 00:06:12,180
This is what IDE is usually do. So, they will halt the execution at the first instruction of the main.
88

89
00:06:12,830 --> 00:06:14,650
The processor is halted.
89

90
00:06:14,650 --> 00:06:17,940
So because, the IDE is stopped here. Fine.
90

91
00:06:17,960 --> 00:06:19,540
So now, how to see this printf?
91

92
00:06:19,640 --> 00:06:26,930
So, for that, what you have to do is, go to window, and then go to show view, and then go to SWV( that is serial
92

93
00:06:26,930 --> 00:06:35,870
wire viewer), and then go to SWV ITM Data Console. So, just click on that.
93

94
00:06:35,960 --> 00:06:44,810
So, it will appear at the bottom here. And here click on this configure trace and select the port 0, so
94

95
00:06:44,810 --> 00:06:46,220
just select this port
95

96
00:06:46,220 --> 00:06:48,330
and after that click on
96

97
00:06:48,380 --> 00:06:56,200
OK. Let's run the code. So, to run the code at one go, you have to click this resume button.
97

98
00:06:56,200 --> 00:06:57,740
Let's run the code.
98

99
00:06:57,740 --> 00:07:01,590
I'm going to click this. So,
99

100
00:07:01,590 --> 00:07:04,110
actually you didn't see that message here,
100

101
00:07:04,120 --> 00:07:07,550
that's because you have to start the trace.
101

102
00:07:07,570 --> 00:07:09,940
So now, let's reset the board.
102

103
00:07:09,940 --> 00:07:12,510
You can reset the board from IDE itself
103

104
00:07:12,520 --> 00:07:17,020
you need not to keep pressing the reset button of the board, so you can do that from here.
104

105
00:07:17,050 --> 00:07:20,250
Reset the chip and restart debug session.
105

106
00:07:20,260 --> 00:07:21,590
Let me reset.
106

107
00:07:21,640 --> 00:07:25,510
So, now let's click on this start trace button here.
107

108
00:07:25,510 --> 00:07:30,710
This console is ready to accept data on the SWO pin.
108

109
00:07:30,790 --> 00:07:33,350
Let's hit run.
109

110
00:07:33,360 --> 00:07:34,770
Here it is.
110

111
00:07:34,770 --> 00:07:38,160
We got the message Hello world. So, what's happening here? So,
111

112
00:07:38,210 --> 00:07:41,100
Printf is going to standard library, from standard library
112

113
00:07:41,100 --> 00:07:45,890
it is coming to the write function, and in the write function it is going to the ITM FIFO,
113

114
00:07:46,110 --> 00:07:47,600
and from that ITM FIFO
114

115
00:07:47,640 --> 00:07:52,450
it is coming over SWO pin all the way back to the IDE.
115

116
00:07:52,500 --> 00:08:00,390
And for any reason if you are not able to use SWV that is serial wire viewer, then you can use semi
116

117
00:08:00,390 --> 00:08:05,280
hosting using openOCD debugger, that I covered in the next lecture.
117

118
00:08:05,340 --> 00:08:05,980
So,
118

119
00:08:06,150 --> 00:08:07,430
So,  what is the next lecture?
119

120
00:08:07,440 --> 00:08:11,220
only if you can't use this feature. I'll see you in the next lecture.

to the debugging session of the IDE, and you may also encounter it.

4
00:00:14,880 --> 00:00:18,470
So, if you encounter it, then a you can troubleshoot it.

00:00:18,480 --> 00:00:18,730
Okay.

00:00:18,780 --> 00:00:20,540
So, that I will discuss now. So, now

let's say I have a simple project here.

8
00:00:22,690 --> 00:00:24,480
So, let's say a "hello

9
00:00:24,480 --> 00:00:24,920
world".

And let me debug this project first.

11
00:00:28,020 --> 00:00:30,120
OK? Now let me load and debug.
11

12
00:00:30,120 --> 00:00:32,850
So, I would be selecting a Debug As.
12

13
00:00:32,850 --> 00:00:33,290
Right?
13

14
00:00:33,300 --> 00:00:36,680
So, Debug As,  STM32 application. 
14

15
00:00:36,730 --> 00:00:38,970
Now let's do that. Here,
15

16
00:00:38,970 --> 00:00:44,490
you can see that, the IDE is going into the debug perspective.
16

17
00:00:44,500 --> 00:00:44,920
Right?
17

18
00:00:45,150 --> 00:00:46,410
Let's click switch.
18

19
00:00:49,140 --> 00:00:54,240
Right? So, everything is as expected and here you can see that a this is a Debug perspective.

20
00:00:54,340 --> 00:01:02,190
OK? So, let me just enable this tracing and let me test this. So, you can see that there are some unknown
20

21
00:01:02,190 --> 00:01:02,750
characters.
21

22
00:01:02,760 --> 00:01:10,590
So, what you can do is, you can just click on remove all collected data and you can reset the chip and
22

you can try once again.

00:01:13,680 --> 00:01:15,570
So, let's do that, OK.

00:01:15,750 --> 00:01:22,950
Now it works. Now let's say you want to go back to your C and C++ perspective, that is your editing

00:01:22,950 --> 00:01:23,700
perspective.

27
00:01:24,510 --> 00:01:29,910
So, what you should be doing is, you should be terminating this debug session, isn't it?
27

28
00:01:30,600 --> 00:01:32,370
No, there are two termination options.

00:01:32,370 --> 00:01:36,200
One is terminate and another one is a terminate and relaunch.
29

30
00:01:36,250 --> 00:01:42,330
OK? So, if you click this, the debug session will be terminated, but it will again relaunch.
30

31
00:01:42,540 --> 00:01:42,890
All right.
31

32
00:01:43,080 --> 00:01:45,050
Now, let me click this terminate.
32

33
00:01:45,690 --> 00:01:52,770
And when I click that, you can see that, the IDE automatically went back to the C++ perspective.
33

34
00:01:53,040 --> 00:01:54,180
OK? Where you can 
34

35
00:01:55,550 --> 00:01:59,660
again edit your code, and you can build your code, and you can again debug. 
35

36
00:01:59,910 --> 00:02:02,490
So, let's again try to debug.
36

37
00:02:02,720 --> 00:02:06,320
OK? Now let me again try to debug the code.
37

38
00:02:07,220 --> 00:02:10,670
And here you can see that, it again goes into the 
38

39
00:02:11,780 --> 00:02:13,360
debug perspective, right?
39

40
00:02:13,940 --> 00:02:14,870
That's correct.
40

41
00:02:14,910 --> 00:02:21,920
OK? So, this should be the normal behavior. But the problem comes when there is one more terminate button
41

42
00:02:21,920 --> 00:02:22,250
here.
42

43
00:02:22,790 --> 00:02:28,590
Now the problem comes when you use this button to terminate the debug session.
43

44
00:02:28,850 --> 00:02:34,130
So, now let's use that button, OK? to terminate the debug session.
44

45
00:02:35,190 --> 00:02:42,000
I'm clicking that, and here you can see that the debug session is terminated and the perspective is changed
45

46
00:02:42,000 --> 00:02:44,280
back to the C and C++ perspective.
46

47
00:02:45,540 --> 00:02:47,910
Now try to again debug the code.
47

48
00:02:55,030 --> 00:02:56,240
And here is a problem.
48

49
00:02:56,950 --> 00:03:01,730
OK. Now it says that error in final launch sequence.
49

50
00:03:02,380 --> 00:03:07,990
Now, this error is observed in newer versions of STM32CubeIDE, OK? In the older versions
50

51
00:03:08,020 --> 00:03:10,050
I didn't find these problems.
51

52
00:03:10,330 --> 00:03:13,460
I don't know whether it is a known bug or it may not be a bug.
52

53
00:03:14,050 --> 00:03:17,530
So, now the workaround for this issue is very simple.
53

54
00:03:17,530 --> 00:03:22,120
Just click OK, and go to the debug perspective manually,
54

55
00:03:23,080 --> 00:03:23,410
Right 
55

56
00:03:23,410 --> 00:03:25,510
Click on this option,
56

57
00:03:26,570 --> 00:03:27,710
Terminate and remove.
57

58
00:03:29,020 --> 00:03:37,060
OK? And then go back to the C and C++ perspective, after that again try to debug the code, and this
58

59
00:03:37,060 --> 00:03:38,130
time it should work.
59

60
00:03:38,950 --> 00:03:44,410
I don't know whether you will face this issue or not, but if you face this issue, then just use this
60

61
00:03:44,410 --> 00:03:47,560
workaround, OK? or don't use this terminate button, 
61

62
00:03:47,560 --> 00:03:49,780
always use this terminate button.
62

63
00:03:49,780 --> 00:03:50,510
Great.
63

64
00:03:50,530 --> 00:03:52,300
So, I will see you in the next lecture.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 4

0
1
00:00:00,450 --> 00:00:00,800
All right.
1

2
00:00:00,810 --> 00:00:08,660
So, in the previous video we used ARM Cortex M4 processors, ITM feature to get the prints over SWO
2

3
00:00:08,720 --> 00:00:18,320
line of the debugger. Right? And this won't work if your using Cortex M0 based STM32 microcontroller.
3

4
00:00:18,650 --> 00:00:23,200
So, we can try openOCD based semi-hosting technique.
4

5
00:00:24,270 --> 00:00:31,230
So, that I'm going to explain in this video. By the way openOCD is a debugger which helps you to program
5

6
00:00:31,260 --> 00:00:33,750
and debug your code on the board.
6

7
00:00:33,830 --> 00:00:38,470
OK. It stands for open on chip debugger.
7

8
00:00:38,880 --> 00:00:45,840
So, to get the print using openOCD debugger, you have to do couple of settings in the project.
8

9
00:00:45,840 --> 00:00:46,100
All right.
9

10
00:00:46,110 --> 00:00:53,070
Now, let's go to our IDE and I have created a new project here
10

11
00:00:53,070 --> 00:00:55,500
HelloWorld_semihosting.
11

12
00:00:56,420 --> 00:01:00,570
So, now first what have to do is, you have to a build this project.
12

13
00:01:00,570 --> 00:01:02,320
OK.So, let's build this.
13

14
00:01:02,330 --> 00:01:02,640
All right.
14

15
00:01:02,640 --> 00:01:05,240
So, now the binary is generated here
15

16
00:01:05,280 --> 00:01:06,990
the .elf file
16

17
00:01:07,710 --> 00:01:15,230
OK. Then what you have to do is, right click on this project and go to debug configurations.
17

18
00:01:15,530 --> 00:01:17,490
OK here.
18

19
00:01:17,520 --> 00:01:18,860
All right.
19

20
00:01:18,860 --> 00:01:23,430
And then go to STM32 MCU Debugging, just expand that.
20

21
00:01:24,270 --> 00:01:25,030
Okay.
21

22
00:01:25,230 --> 00:01:28,970
Here, click on new launch configuration.
22

23
00:01:32,250 --> 00:01:39,230
So, now we are going to create a debug configuration for our .elf file.
23

24
00:01:39,320 --> 00:01:46,610
So, now here in the debugger you have to change the debugger now. The debug probe or debug software you
24

25
00:01:46,610 --> 00:01:47,740
have to change.
25

26
00:01:47,750 --> 00:01:50,880
So, earlier we used ST-LNK. Right?
26

27
00:01:50,960 --> 00:01:55,070
So, that is actually the default, one for the STM32Cube IDE.
27

28
00:01:55,460 --> 00:01:57,990
Now, you have to change this to openOCD.
28

29
00:01:58,490 --> 00:02:00,830
So, that is the first change you have to do.
29

30
00:02:00,830 --> 00:02:01,920
Change the debugger.
30

31
00:02:02,150 --> 00:02:03,660
OK.
31

32
00:02:03,830 --> 00:02:07,410
And after that go to Startup, and in the Startup tab
32

33
00:02:07,940 --> 00:02:15,410
so you have to mention the semi-hosting run command. OK. And command is this one monitor arm semi hosting
33

34
00:02:15,490 --> 00:02:15,940
enable.
34

35
00:02:16,520 --> 00:02:17,720
So, just copy this
35

36
00:02:18,120 --> 00:02:18,500
OK,
36

37
00:02:18,530 --> 00:02:22,550
I have given this command in the resource section, so just download that.
37

38
00:02:22,580 --> 00:02:23,440
OK.
38

39
00:02:23,630 --> 00:02:26,060
Go to the IDE and paste here.
39

40
00:02:26,120 --> 00:02:26,920
All right.
40

41
00:02:26,960 --> 00:02:30,600
And click on Apply, and then click on close.
41

42
00:02:31,490 --> 00:02:31,820
All right.
42

43
00:02:32,330 --> 00:02:41,930
And after that, right click on the project and go to properties, go to C++ build, and go to settings, go
43

44
00:02:41,930 --> 00:02:47,840
to tool settings here, go to linker, and go to miscellaneous,
44

45
00:02:47,960 --> 00:02:54,430
and here you have to mention these linker arguments.
45

46
00:02:54,500 --> 00:02:55,770
OK?
46

47
00:02:55,900 --> 00:02:56,210
All right.
47

48
00:02:56,210 --> 00:03:05,390
Now let's copy these linker arguments, and here just click on + here, and just paste that flags, click
48

49
00:03:05,420 --> 00:03:15,220
OK, and click apply here, and apply and close. And now go to your Src, and go to main.c,
49

50
00:03:16,040 --> 00:03:18,320
and now here let's write some code.
50

51
00:03:18,410 --> 00:03:22,510
OK. So, let me copy the same code from the earlier project.
51

52
00:03:22,850 --> 00:03:23,890
Let me copy this.
52

53
00:03:24,170 --> 00:03:24,560
All right.
53

54
00:03:24,620 --> 00:03:29,600
Now let me paste here. And here before using any printf's
54

55
00:03:29,620 --> 00:03:30,240
OK,
55

56
00:03:30,410 --> 00:03:34,790
so you have to add this function call in the main function.
56

57
00:03:34,920 --> 00:03:35,680
OK?
57

58
00:03:36,020 --> 00:03:40,190
So, just copy this statement and before using any prints
58

59
00:03:40,370 --> 00:03:45,590
so just call this function, and also you should add it's prototype.
59

60
00:03:45,680 --> 00:03:47,720
So, here is a prototype of that.
60

61
00:03:47,720 --> 00:03:50,000
So, add the prototype here.
61

62
00:03:51,380 --> 00:03:59,020
And after that, one more important thing you have to do, that is you have to exclude this syscalls.c
62

63
00:03:59,080 --> 00:04:01,550
from the build.
63

64
00:04:01,550 --> 00:04:01,880
All right.
64

65
00:04:02,180 --> 00:04:14,310
So, for that just right click on syscalls.c file, go to properties, and go to C/C++ build,
65

66
00:04:14,450 --> 00:04:17,450
and check this exclude resource from build.
66

67
00:04:17,530 --> 00:04:18,520
OK.
67

68
00:04:18,800 --> 00:04:21,890
Apply, Apply and close. That's it.
68

69
00:04:22,250 --> 00:04:22,580
OK.So,
69

70
00:04:22,640 --> 00:04:25,520
now this file is excluded from the build.
70

71
00:04:25,760 --> 00:04:28,640
And after that just build the project.
71

72
00:04:28,640 --> 00:04:31,130
So, your project should build successfully.
72

73
00:04:31,130 --> 00:04:37,470
Here we can see that the project successfully. And then connect your hardware to the PC,
73

74
00:04:38,300 --> 00:04:43,920
and after that just debug the code as STM32 C application.
74

75
00:04:45,110 --> 00:04:47,010
OK.
75

76
00:04:48,330 --> 00:04:48,650
All right.
76

77
00:04:48,650 --> 00:04:50,280
Now, click on switch.
77

78
00:04:50,510 --> 00:04:55,670
And here you can see that, a it went to a debug perspective,
78

79
00:04:55,670 --> 00:05:02,780
and this is a log which is produced by openOCD debugger.
79

80
00:05:02,780 --> 00:05:05,770
And here you can see that semihosting is enabled.So,
80

81
00:05:06,560 --> 00:05:10,530
now you can get the prints right here on the console.
81

82
00:05:11,410 --> 00:05:13,670
So, let's just step over.
82

83
00:05:14,810 --> 00:05:15,390
OK?
83

84
00:05:15,440 --> 00:05:22,620
And now let's step over one more time and here it is, you just got the print. Great!
84

85
00:05:22,620 --> 00:05:32,010
So, remember that, while using prints using printf in semihosting, this \n is really important.
85

86
00:05:32,090 --> 00:05:32,360
OK.
86

87
00:05:32,370 --> 00:05:34,270
You should not miss that.
87

88
00:05:34,440 --> 00:05:42,570
The string should end with \n, when you are using printf under semihosting with openOCD debugger.
88

89
00:05:44,310 --> 00:05:49,050
So, you can use this feature whenever you want to debug your project using a prints.
89

90
00:05:49,140 --> 00:05:57,070
OK? So, that's about getting prints using openOCD and semihosting on STM32Cube IDE
90

91
00:05:57,390 --> 00:05:59,040
and I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 5

0
1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,200
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,200 --> 00:00:04,640
So, now let's do one more exercise on our Embedded Target.
2

3
00:00:04,710 --> 00:00:11,400
So, now by using sizeof operator, find out storage sizes of the below data types for your cross compiler.
3

4
00:00:11,400 --> 00:00:15,660
Similar thing we actually did in the earlier part of the course, where we used to sizeof operator on
4

5
00:00:15,660 --> 00:00:21,220
our machine to print out the data size values for the various standard data types which are available
5

6
00:00:21,270 --> 00:00:22,040
in 'C'.
6

7
00:00:22,050 --> 00:00:27,300
Same thing you have to do, but in this case you have to do for Embedded Target, and after that you can use
7

8
00:00:27,300 --> 00:00:30,450
the printf statement in order to print the result
8

9
00:00:30,540 --> 00:00:34,310
something similar to this. And you can create a new project,
9

10
00:00:34,410 --> 00:00:40,470
no problem. But, whenever you create a new project you would have observed here, the IDE actually creates a
10

11
00:00:40,470 --> 00:00:45,610
main function and it adds this statement at the end of the main function.
11

12
00:00:45,630 --> 00:00:48,610
So, this is called 'C's loop statement.
12

13
00:00:48,720 --> 00:00:52,550
So, about loop statements we are going to explore in later part of the course,
13

14
00:00:52,740 --> 00:00:59,610
but, please note that this is a for loop, and this is a implementation of infinite loop. When the execution
14

15
00:00:59,610 --> 00:01:00,840
control reaches here,
15

16
00:01:00,840 --> 00:01:02,510
processor actually hangs here,
16

17
00:01:02,610 --> 00:01:10,290
executing this infinite loop. And  why it is required? We will explore that in the next lecture. So, just
17

18
00:01:10,290 --> 00:01:14,460
keep this for loop at the end of the main function.
18

19
00:01:14,460 --> 00:01:19,700
Go ahead and create a new project for the sizeof experiment and I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 6

0
1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,400
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,410 --> 00:00:04,690
So, I just created a new project here 002Sizeof.
2

3
00:00:04,790 --> 00:00:09,060
I am going to use printf here, again I have to edit the syscalls.c.
3

4
00:00:09,150 --> 00:00:13,400
So, what I suggest you to do is, just go to this syscalls.c of previous  project,
4

5
00:00:13,440 --> 00:00:20,840
just copy everything here and then go to your new project and Ctrl+A select everything,
5

6
00:00:20,880 --> 00:00:25,110
delete everything, and paste whatever you copied from the previous project.
6

7
00:00:25,560 --> 00:00:27,360
So, that will be the quickest method to do.
7

8
00:00:28,260 --> 00:00:33,360
So, remember that, this is required only if you want to use printf functionality. 
8

9
00:00:33,360 --> 00:00:35,900
Let me implement the sizeof code here.
9

10
00:00:35,910 --> 00:00:43,140
So, here what I'm going to do is, I'm going to first use the   stdio.h.
10

11
00:00:43,140 --> 00:00:45,130
Let me include that file first.
11

12
00:00:45,750 --> 00:00:50,630
And after that, let me use printf to print the sizeof a char data type.
12

13
00:00:51,120 --> 00:00:59,340
So, size of char data type is %u, because size is always unsigned, so I can use %u
13

14
00:00:59,490 --> 00:01:03,750
\n and size of char.
14

15
00:01:04,080 --> 00:01:05,850
So, this seems to be good.
15

16
00:01:05,880 --> 00:01:12,720
And let me try to compile this. So, the compilation is successful.
16

17
00:01:12,780 --> 00:01:14,740
No issues with that.
17

18
00:01:14,790 --> 00:01:16,820
So, some people maybe having doubts,
18

19
00:01:16,830 --> 00:01:19,280
so why didn't I use %d.
19

20
00:01:19,290 --> 00:01:23,130
That's because, d is for signed integer. Right?
20

21
00:01:23,880 --> 00:01:26,070
I know size cannot be negative, right?
21

22
00:01:26,100 --> 00:01:27,660
So, that's why, I used u.
22

23
00:01:27,750 --> 00:01:28,700
That is for unsigned.
23

24
00:01:28,730 --> 00:01:30,010
So, even if you use d,
24

25
00:01:30,040 --> 00:01:31,070
that's not an issue.
25

26
00:01:31,070 --> 00:01:32,880
So, that is also correct.
26

27
00:01:33,000 --> 00:01:40,850
After that, let me do for sizeof let's say short, sizeof long,
27

28
00:01:40,890 --> 00:01:45,380
and let me again do it for sizeof long long,
28

29
00:01:45,750 --> 00:01:57,800
so let me just try sizeof int here, and after that sizeof double. So, let me just change this to short, int,
29

30
00:01:58,460 --> 00:02:09,910
long, long long, double. Let me try to compile this. So, the compilation is successful.
30

31
00:02:10,350 --> 00:02:10,690
All right. So, 
31

32
00:02:10,700 --> 00:02:16,750
after that, now let's create the debug configuration, so go to Debug as and click on Debug Configuration,
32

33
00:02:16,750 --> 00:02:21,840
and so there is already one configuration which is for our previous project, and here
33

34
00:02:21,850 --> 00:02:28,110
now select this and double click. And it creates another one here for our current project.
34

35
00:02:28,180 --> 00:02:30,370
And here again you have to go to the debugger,
35

36
00:02:30,400 --> 00:02:36,200
make sure that ST-LINK is selected, and also the SWV is enabled.
36

37
00:02:36,220 --> 00:02:41,470
So because, we are using printf, so click apply and start debugging.
37

38
00:02:41,470 --> 00:02:46,570
So, now it went to the debug mode and every time it is actually asking this option,
38

39
00:02:46,570 --> 00:02:49,070
so, for me I would so just select this
39

40
00:02:49,090 --> 00:02:57,070
remember my decision, and I will just click switch here, and now let's go to the data console of ITM
40

41
00:02:57,250 --> 00:03:00,940
and make sure that 0 is being monitored here,
41

42
00:03:00,970 --> 00:03:02,700
that is port 0, or
42

43
00:03:02,710 --> 00:03:11,340
this is also called as channel 0 of ITM. Actually, ITM has 32 channels and our printf works over channel
43

44
00:03:11,340 --> 00:03:11,960
Zero.
44

45
00:03:11,980 --> 00:03:17,280
So, that's why, we are monitoring the channel 0. So, channel 1 can be used for some other purpose.
45

46
00:03:17,290 --> 00:03:19,210
Let's say, if you use RTOS or something.
46

47
00:03:19,240 --> 00:03:25,120
So, if you want to get the diagnostic information of some other events then you can use channel 1,
47

48
00:03:25,120 --> 00:03:31,020
channel 2, channel 3 like that. So, but by default channel 0 will be used.
48

49
00:03:31,050 --> 00:03:38,400
So, that's why, we are monitoring the channel 0 here. And let's click OK, and after that let's start the
49

50
00:03:38,400 --> 00:03:41,640
trace, start trace. And after that
50

51
00:03:41,650 --> 00:03:43,460
now let's use this option
51

52
00:03:43,470 --> 00:03:47,610
that is Step Over to execute the 'C' statement one by one.
52

53
00:03:47,610 --> 00:03:50,230
Let's first click this Step Over.
53

54
00:03:50,520 --> 00:03:55,070
So, here we can see that this line is executed and the size is one.
54

55
00:03:55,080 --> 00:03:55,710
That's correct.
55

56
00:03:55,710 --> 00:03:56,310
Right?
56

57
00:03:56,310 --> 00:03:57,510
And next is short.
57

58
00:03:57,900 --> 00:04:04,910
So, short is 2. Right? For this cross compiler the size of an int is 4 bytes.
58

59
00:04:04,910 --> 00:04:11,540
So, after that long. Long is 4, long long 8, double is 8.
59

60
00:04:11,540 --> 00:04:17,450
So, a simple exercise to find out different data size of different standard data types available in
60

61
00:04:17,450 --> 00:04:19,020
'C' programming language,
61

62
00:04:19,070 --> 00:04:21,370
and this time we used the cross compiler.
62

63
00:04:21,380 --> 00:04:21,710
Right?
63

64
00:04:22,370 --> 00:04:29,270
So, and in the next lecture let's explore more about this IDE to browse the memory window, and to understand
64

65
00:04:29,270 --> 00:04:36,540
where exactly the code is stored, and will also analyze some of the disassembly code of our project.
65

66
00:04:36,530 --> 00:04:37,930
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 7

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,290
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,290 --> 00:00:06,070
So, in this lecture let's see some of the compiler settings of STM32Cube IDE.
2

3
00:00:06,240 --> 00:00:13,020
So, now let's go to the IDE, and if you want to modify  or if you want to check the compiler settings,
3

4
00:00:13,210 --> 00:00:19,920
so then you can do that. For that, you have to select the project, and then right click, and go to properties,
4

5
00:00:22,890 --> 00:00:28,980
so after that just expand this C and C++ build option, and here go to settings.
5

6
00:00:28,980 --> 00:00:31,550
So, here you will find a various steps.
6

7
00:00:31,560 --> 00:00:33,470
First one is  Toolchain version,
7

8
00:00:33,630 --> 00:00:39,290
and it is actually set to default, so you can keep the same need not to change anything here.
8

9
00:00:39,300 --> 00:00:48,210
So, here you can see that it is using the cross compiler and the version is this one. If you want to update
9

10
00:00:48,210 --> 00:00:51,820
the version or if you want to download new Toolchain
10

11
00:00:51,930 --> 00:00:58,550
you can do that by downloading the newest toolchain by going to the ARM website.
11

12
00:00:58,590 --> 00:01:06,040
So here, in this Website, the Toolchain can be found in a developer.arm.com.
12

13
00:01:06,040 --> 00:01:12,310
So, you have to go to this url and here all the GNU Arm Embedded Toolchains are hosted.
13

14
00:01:12,340 --> 00:01:20,800
So, this website will give you Toolchain for Arm Cortex A family of processors, also for Cortex R
14

15
00:01:20,800 --> 00:01:22,670
and M family of processors.
15

16
00:01:23,140 --> 00:01:26,820
So, you just have to go here and click downloads.
16

17
00:01:27,090 --> 00:01:29,790
So, and here you can see that this is a latest version
17

18
00:01:29,800 --> 00:01:30,230
available.
18

19
00:01:30,490 --> 00:01:35,980
So, but the STMcube IDE uses, it uses actually an old version, but that's OK.
19

20
00:01:36,250 --> 00:01:43,240
Now after that, go to Tool settings and here you will see the microcontroller settings like the board
20

21
00:01:43,240 --> 00:01:47,300
you selected, the microcontroller you are using, and various other options.
21

22
00:01:47,440 --> 00:01:55,660
And here you can see that, this project actually uses a reduced version of 'C' standard library called
22

23
00:01:55,900 --> 00:01:57,320
nano library.
23

24
00:01:57,490 --> 00:02:05,030
So, there are 2 options either you can select standard C or reduced C. So, this IDE by default uses
24

25
00:02:05,090 --> 00:02:13,330
reduced C standard library, whose memory footprint is very much small compared to the standard C. And
25

26
00:02:13,390 --> 00:02:20,290
after that, you can go and explore MCU post build outputs, so for any reason if you want to generate
26

27
00:02:20,290 --> 00:02:27,010
binary file, or hex file, or any other extension, then you can do so by selecting these options.
27

28
00:02:27,190 --> 00:02:33,610
So, by default, a this project build is not generating any binary or hex file, it is generating only elf
28

29
00:02:33,610 --> 00:02:33,930
file.
29

30
00:02:34,300 --> 00:02:41,200
So, if you want binary then you can check this. And after that, if you go to the MCU GCC Compiler tab
30

31
00:02:41,200 --> 00:02:42,120
here.
31

32
00:02:42,190 --> 00:02:47,620
So, here it actually lots of default compiler flags.
32

33
00:02:48,070 --> 00:02:53,430
So, you cannot edit that, but you can add extra flag by using these options.
33

34
00:02:53,620 --> 00:02:56,370
So, if you go to the General tab here,
34

35
00:02:56,410 --> 00:03:03,630
So, in general you can able to change the 'C' standard, a which is used for your project build.
35

36
00:03:04,120 --> 00:03:13,240
So, by default you can see that it is set to GNU11. So, GNU11 is actually it is an extension to the
36

37
00:03:13,240 --> 00:03:15,500
ISO C11 standard.
37

38
00:03:15,580 --> 00:03:22,710
So, what GNU does is, it actually takes ISO standard and then adds a GNU related extension to that standard,
38

39
00:03:22,780 --> 00:03:25,030
and they call it as GNU11.
39

40
00:03:25,050 --> 00:03:31,120
So, if you want to change that and if you want to use pure ISO standard, then you can do that by selecting
40

41
00:03:31,210 --> 00:03:33,460
the appropriate standard you want.
41

42
00:03:33,460 --> 00:03:34,660
I think that would be fine
42

43
00:03:34,690 --> 00:03:35,940
need not to change that.
43

44
00:03:36,010 --> 00:03:40,630
And after that, in the debugging you need not to change anything, Preprocessor
44

45
00:03:40,640 --> 00:03:43,610
so will see all these things later, Includepaths
45

46
00:03:43,630 --> 00:03:44,590
will see that later.
46

47
00:03:45,220 --> 00:03:49,690
So, optimization of the code during compilation is set to none,
47

48
00:03:49,690 --> 00:03:52,000
so I would suggest you to maintain the same.
48

49
00:03:52,120 --> 00:03:55,780
So, don't change the optimization level. After that, miscellaneous.
49

50
00:03:55,810 --> 00:03:57,430
So, here you need not anything.
50

51
00:03:57,790 --> 00:03:59,710
So, now let's go to the linker flags.
51

52
00:03:59,710 --> 00:04:02,710
So, these are the linker flags by default.
52

53
00:04:02,710 --> 00:04:04,330
So, you cannot edit this,
53

54
00:04:04,330 --> 00:04:07,560
so here you can see that it is provided with the linker script and all.
54

55
00:04:07,600 --> 00:04:12,300
So, these are some of the advanced concepts to understand about the linker script etc.
55

56
00:04:12,310 --> 00:04:17,620
So, we will see that in later part of the course, but for a time being you need not to do anything in
56

57
00:04:17,620 --> 00:04:18,890
the linker section.
57

58
00:04:19,030 --> 00:04:26,290
So, you just leave that as it is. So, that's it about the compiler settings, which is recommended to do.
58

59
00:04:26,400 --> 00:04:29,090
So, even if you don't do these settings, that's fine,
59

60
00:04:29,100 --> 00:04:31,950
the compiler will compile with default settings.
60

61
00:04:31,950 --> 00:04:36,720
After that, what you have to do is, you have to click on Apply, and then Apply and close.
61

62
00:04:36,720 --> 00:04:40,800
So, with that note I would like to end this lecture and I will see you in the next lecture.
62

63
00:04:40,800 --> 00:04:44,700
So, in the next lecture let's learn about the compilation process.
63

64
00:04:44,730 --> 00:04:46,170
I will see you in the next lecture.
