section 9---Build process


video 1

0
1
00:00:00,040 --> 00:00:03,720
So, in this lecture let's understand about the build process.
1

2
00:00:03,780 --> 00:00:08,250
So, when you hit the build option in your IDE is your project will be built. Right?
2

3
00:00:08,250 --> 00:00:12,330
So, the building process actually involves  couple of stages. 
3

4
00:00:12,600 --> 00:00:13,690
Your source file
4

5
00:00:13,740 --> 00:00:14,790
what you have written
5

6
00:00:14,790 --> 00:00:17,430
actually first undergoes Preprocessing.
6

7
00:00:17,790 --> 00:00:25,560
And after that, your source code will be parsed, and then object files will be generated, and linking of
7

8
00:00:25,560 --> 00:00:27,790
all those object files will happen,
8

9
00:00:27,810 --> 00:00:34,120
and finally an executable will be generated for your project, and optionally you can do post-processing
9

10
00:00:34,260 --> 00:00:36,030
of the final executable.
10

11
00:00:36,030 --> 00:00:37,910
Let's understand all these steps.
11

12
00:00:38,040 --> 00:00:42,660
So, broadly the build process can be divided into 2 stages.
12

13
00:00:42,660 --> 00:00:46,690
One is compilation stage and another one is linking stage.
13

14
00:00:47,070 --> 00:00:54,840
So, your source code that is .c files will be first preprocessed using preprocessor engine of the
14

15
00:00:54,840 --> 00:00:58,890
compiler, and the preprocessing stage produces .i
15

16
00:00:58,920 --> 00:01:00,590
for every .c file.
16

17
00:01:01,080 --> 00:01:08,310
So, this is a stage where the #include files will be resolved, and all the 'C' macros will be resolved,
17

18
00:01:08,430 --> 00:01:11,120
and .i file will be created.
18

19
00:01:11,340 --> 00:01:15,630
The next stage of the compilation or the compiler is parsing.
19

20
00:01:16,020 --> 00:01:23,730
So, the parser engine of the compiler will then parse the .i files, in order to check the syntax of
20

21
00:01:23,730 --> 00:01:28,110
the statements you have written in the source file.
21

22
00:01:28,410 --> 00:01:34,590
So, syntaxes will be checked against the standard which you are selected and if there is any error,
22

23
00:01:34,620 --> 00:01:38,600
if there is any problem with the code, it will be reported to the programmer
23

24
00:01:38,610 --> 00:01:39,670
at this stage.
24

25
00:01:40,050 --> 00:01:48,150
So, if everything is fine, then the code is then converted into .s file using another engine of the
25

26
00:01:48,150 --> 00:01:50,580
compiler called code generator.
26

27
00:01:50,580 --> 00:01:54,960
So, that means .i later it will be converted into .s. So,
27

28
00:01:55,350 --> 00:02:02,160
here is a place where higher level language such as 'C' is converted into lower level language, such as
28

29
00:02:02,280 --> 00:02:03,510
assembly language.
29

30
00:02:03,510 --> 00:02:10,530
That means, for every 'C' statement you have written the equivalent mnemonics will be created in the .s
30

31
00:02:10,530 --> 00:02:12,810
file by the code generator.
31

32
00:02:12,810 --> 00:02:19,970
And after that, the assembler engine of the compiler converts mnemonics into machine codes.
32

33
00:02:20,250 --> 00:02:24,240
So, at the end of the day machines understand numbers, isn't it?
33

34
00:02:24,270 --> 00:02:26,800
So, machine wants numbers. Right?
34

35
00:02:27,330 --> 00:02:33,410
So, you are higher level language is converted into mnemonics and after that those mnemonics later it'll
35

36
00:02:33,450 --> 00:02:42,170
be converted into machine codes by the assembler. So, assembler actually generates .o files, so which
36

37
00:02:42,170 --> 00:02:45,670
are also called as relocatable object files.
37

38
00:02:45,920 --> 00:02:50,920
So, for every .c file, a .o file will be created by the assembler,
38

39
00:02:51,020 --> 00:02:56,780
so .o file is nothing but collection of machine codes of your .c files.
39

40
00:02:56,960 --> 00:03:03,290
And after that, linking stage of the build will be invoked. In the linking stage, so multiple relocatable
40

41
00:03:03,290 --> 00:03:07,460
object files will be combined into one executable file.
41

42
00:03:07,970 --> 00:03:14,000
So, if you have let's say five .c files, then five .o files will be created.
42

43
00:03:14,000 --> 00:03:18,700
So, each .o file will be having its own machine code. Right?
43

44
00:03:18,740 --> 00:03:25,340
So, the linker will merge all those machine codes into one file and then it creates a final executable
44

45
00:03:25,340 --> 00:03:25,830
file.
45

46
00:03:26,160 --> 00:03:33,830
So, in our case, the format of the final executable file will be .elf, where elf stands for executable
46

47
00:03:33,920 --> 00:03:36,050
and link format.
47

48
00:03:36,050 --> 00:03:43,570
So basically, the GCC based compilers will actually generate executable file of .elf.
48

49
00:03:43,820 --> 00:03:51,230
So, it's a debug + executable file. And after that, you can do postprocessing on that elf file using
49

50
00:03:51,260 --> 00:03:59,570
tools such as object copy and other tools, to produce various other files like .bin, .hex, etc.
50

51
00:04:00,020 --> 00:04:03,040
So, that what we call as Post processing.
51

52
00:04:03,140 --> 00:04:08,530
So, these are the steps involved in producing the executable file from the source file.
52

53
00:04:09,110 --> 00:04:14,940
So, all these things happen at the background when you trigger the build.
53

54
00:04:14,940 --> 00:04:17,130
So, now let's get into our project.
54

55
00:04:17,130 --> 00:04:22,500
So here, I have a project called add and I have a main.c here.
55

56
00:04:22,500 --> 00:04:29,010
So, here it is a very simple program, where I have 2 global variables and I am just adding those global
56

57
00:04:29,010 --> 00:04:29,730
variables,
57

58
00:04:29,790 --> 00:04:34,410
and after that  I'm storing the result into the 3rd global variable that is result,
58

59
00:04:34,440 --> 00:04:36,240
and after that I am printing that.
59

60
00:04:36,240 --> 00:04:38,430
So now, let's compile this.
60

61
00:04:38,430 --> 00:04:45,100
So, let me just clean the project first, and after that let me build the project.
61

62
00:04:45,550 --> 00:04:46,840
So, when you build a project
62

63
00:04:46,860 --> 00:04:53,170
so here you can see that the compiler is invoked. So, that compiler itself takes care of all the stages
63

64
00:04:53,380 --> 00:04:58,100
like it does Preprocessing,  Parsing, Assembling, it does linking,
64

65
00:04:58,180 --> 00:05:04,690
so everything is taken care by this executable.So, that is actually our cross compiler.
65

66
00:05:04,720 --> 00:05:09,250
So, here you can see that first it actually compiles the startup file, so
66

67
00:05:09,280 --> 00:05:11,770
then it compiles the main.c,
67

68
00:05:11,800 --> 00:05:20,650
then it compiles syscalls.c. Once the compilation and linking stage ends the elf file is generated,
68

69
00:05:20,680 --> 00:05:22,030
that is final executable.
69

70
00:05:22,540 --> 00:05:25,470
And after that, on that elf a Postprocessing 
70

71
00:05:25,470 --> 00:05:34,690
tools can be invoked such as object dump, or size, in order to gather more information about the executable
71

72
00:05:34,870 --> 00:05:38,760
which is generated. Like for example, when you invoke this command,
72

73
00:05:38,860 --> 00:05:45,820
size, it actually gives the size of the executable like size of the code section, the size of the data
73

74
00:05:45,820 --> 00:05:48,760
section, the size of the bss section etc..
74

75
00:05:48,790 --> 00:05:55,160
So, if you want to see all these intermediate files such as .i, .s, and .o,
75

76
00:05:55,240 --> 00:06:01,320
then you can go to the build folder of your project. So, where all these files are stored.
76

77
00:06:01,360 --> 00:06:02,230
Let's check that.
77

78
00:06:02,320 --> 00:06:08,080
So, for that, what you have to do is, you have to right click on the project, and then select properties. And
78

79
00:06:08,080 --> 00:06:09,220
go to the resource,
79

80
00:06:09,220 --> 00:06:12,000
and after that click this icon here.
80

81
00:06:12,070 --> 00:06:19,960
So, this will take you to the folder where this project is placed. And the project folder will be opened.
81

82
00:06:20,160 --> 00:06:28,440
So, here let's open this, and go to debug. Here we can see that you actually by default don't find .i
82

83
00:06:28,450 --> 00:06:33,700
and .s files, because those are actually not generated for the user.
83

84
00:06:33,710 --> 00:06:38,710
So, those are generated internally and compiler uses them internally.
84

85
00:06:38,710 --> 00:06:44,740
So, you have to instruct the compiler to create them and store in the file system. So, we can instruct
85

86
00:06:44,740 --> 00:06:45,530
the compiler.
86

87
00:06:45,550 --> 00:06:53,850
So, for that what you have to do is, you have to go to the properties here, and go to the settings. And in
87

88
00:06:53,870 --> 00:06:58,000
the settings, go to the compiler settings, go to the miscellaneous,
88

89
00:06:58,100 --> 00:07:05,490
and here click on +, and type save-temps. And click OK, Apply,
89

90
00:07:06,100 --> 00:07:13,530
Apply and Close, and then rebuild the project. So, when you rebuild the project, so you can see that this 
90

91
00:07:13,530 --> 00:07:19,040
folder is now populated with .i, .s, and various other files.
91

92
00:07:19,050 --> 00:07:19,380
Right?
92

93
00:07:19,410 --> 00:07:27,270
So, for every source file there will be .i, .s, syscalls.i, syscalls.s like that. And you already
93

94
00:07:27,270 --> 00:07:28,740
know what is main.i.
94

95
00:07:28,790 --> 00:07:29,100
Right?
95

96
00:07:29,100 --> 00:07:37,500
So, this is actually Preprocessed source file. And if you open main.s, so this is nothing but mnemonics,
96

97
00:07:38,370 --> 00:07:39,030
Sorry about that.
97

98
00:07:39,060 --> 00:07:40,880
So, let me open once again.
98

99
00:07:40,980 --> 00:07:47,880
So, this is nothing but mnemonics. Right? Mnemonics means, it is actually an instruction which are generated
99

100
00:07:48,030 --> 00:07:49,710
for every 'C' statement
100

101
00:07:49,710 --> 00:07:56,430
what you have written. So, these mnemonics are from ARM Cortex instruction set architecture. If you want
101

102
00:07:56,430 --> 00:08:03,330
to understand about these mnemonics, then you have to refer to the instruction set document of ARM Cortex
102

103
00:08:03,540 --> 00:08:11,330
processor. And after that, .o file you can find in the source folder.
103

104
00:08:11,360 --> 00:08:18,890
So, these are the .o files created for every .c file, so .o file is actually it is a machine code.
104

105
00:08:18,980 --> 00:08:19,310
Right?
105

106
00:08:19,340 --> 00:08:22,990
So, these are the machine code, so you will not be able to read that.
106

107
00:08:23,060 --> 00:08:29,810
So, the summary of build process, so the build process we can broadly classify into 3 stages. Preprocessing,
107

108
00:08:29,990 --> 00:08:32,210
compilation, and linking.
108

109
00:08:32,630 --> 00:08:36,980
So, with that note I would like to end this video and let me know if you have any questions and I'll
109

110
00:08:36,980 --> 00:08:37,910
see you in the next lecture.


==================================================================================

section 10---Analyzing Embedded C code

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:07,080
From this lecture onwards, let's take one  Embedded C 
program, that is a program which we have 
1

2
00:00:07,110 --> 00:00:10,050
written for a microcontroller and let's 
2

3
00:00:10,170 --> 00:00:14,220
analyze that. So, we are going to understand anatomy of the 
3

4
00:00:14,220 --> 00:00:21,450
microcontroller, so identifying code and data parts of the program, so we will understand the code memory  
4

5
00:00:21,450 --> 00:00:24,120
and data memory of the microcontroller.
5

6
00:00:24,120 --> 00:00:31,090
And also we are going to explore the disassembly of the final executable, using disassembly
6

7
00:00:31,110 --> 00:00:38,400
feature of the IDE, and also we are going to analyze the executable which is in the format .elf 
7

8
00:00:38,400 --> 00:00:41,510
using GNU tools (like objdump 
8

9
00:00:41,570 --> 00:00:42,650
and size).
9

10
00:00:42,660 --> 00:00:42,980
Let's start.
10

11
00:00:42,990 --> 00:00:47,130
All right.Now, first let's understand what is microcontroller.
11

12
00:00:47,130 --> 00:00:54,390
So, a microcontroller which is also abbreviated as MCU, which stands for microcontroller 
12

13
00:00:54,390 --> 00:01:03,960
unit  is a small computer system on a single chip. But its resources and capabilities such as memory, speed, external 
13

14
00:01:04,020 --> 00:01:13,170
interfaces are very much limited than of a desktop computer because MCU targets embedded applications. 
14

15
00:01:13,440 --> 00:01:21,330
A typical microcontroller includes a processor, volatile and non-volatile memories, input/output
15

16
00:01:21,420 --> 00:01:27,350
interfaces,peripherals, clock unit, bus interfaces everything on a 
16

17
00:01:27,390 --> 00:01:31,860
single chip.So, that's why, a microcontroller is also called as a system on
17

18
00:01:32,430 --> 00:01:39,330
chip.So, all the required units such as a processor unit, which is also called as CPU. 
18

19
00:01:39,330 --> 00:01:48,780
Peripherals  such as analog peripherals such as ADC, DAC, timing related peripherals such as timers, connectivity peripheral 
19

20
00:01:48,780 --> 00:01:57,270
such as  UART,USB, and different types of memories such as volatile memory, such as SRAM, non-volatile 
20

21
00:01:57,270 --> 00:02:06,780
memory such as flash, ROM, EEPROM, clock unit, bus interface, so everything is implemented or fabricated
21

22
00:02:06,810 --> 00:02:10,280
on a single chip and that what we call as a 
22

23
00:02:10,380 --> 00:02:15,330
microcontroller. All right. Now let's understand anatomy of a typical small 
23

24
00:02:15,390 --> 00:02:19,240
microcontroller. So, now let's see what exactly is inside a microcontroller.
24

25
00:02:19,260 --> 00:02:23,640
This is one of the simplest diagram, the real picture maybe 
25

26
00:02:23,640 --> 00:02:29,850
complicated, if you take a look into a modern microcontroller. So, usually a microcontroller has a 
26

27
00:02:29,850 --> 00:02:35,160
CPU inside. So, that's what we call as a processor or a central processing unit.
27

28
00:02:35,190 --> 00:02:42,930
Now, the central processing unit executes instructions, your program instructions, and those
28

29
00:02:42,930 --> 00:02:45,750
instructions are actually stored in a program 
29

30
00:02:46,230 --> 00:02:54,000
memory. So, usually program memory is a non-volatile memory. So, that means once you store your 
30

31
00:02:54,000 --> 00:02:56,580
program into the program memory that 
31

32
00:02:56,580 --> 00:02:57,960
is permanent. I mean 
32

33
00:02:57,990 --> 00:03:05,160
you can erase it, you can write a different programs. But, even if you take out the power from microcontroller 
33

34
00:03:05,160 --> 00:03:11,270
the program memory will not be corrupted, so your instructions will be there permanently unless you change 
34

35
00:03:11,310 --> 00:03:17,820
it. So, once you give power to the microcontroller,  a CPU actually reads instructions from the 
35

36
00:03:17,940 --> 00:03:19,890
program memory and it 
36

37
00:03:19,890 --> 00:03:25,590
executes. So, that means before that CPU should communicate with the program memory. Right? So, 
37

38
00:03:25,590 --> 00:03:28,050
CPU is a different entity and program memory 
38

39
00:03:28,080 --> 00:03:33,200
is a different entity. Right? CPU communicates with a program memory using bus
39

40
00:03:33,330 --> 00:03:40,440
interfaces.So, there is a bus called address bus. Because, before reading an instruction a CPU must produce the 
40

41
00:03:40,560 --> 00:03:42,590
location or the address of 
41

42
00:03:42,600 --> 00:03:42,920
that
42

43
00:03:42,930 --> 00:03:45,260
instruction,right? which is there in the program
43

44
00:03:45,260 --> 00:03:51,030
memory. So, CPU first produces the address on the address bus, and after that the 
44

45
00:03:51,030 --> 00:03:55,330
instruction will then come into instruction bus, or you can also call it as
45

46
00:03:55,380 --> 00:04:03,560
a data bus,and from there instruction will reach to the CPU and CPU has something called instruction 
46

47
00:04:03,560 --> 00:04:09,470
decoder,  so CPU decodes the instruction and according to that action will be taken.
47

48
00:04:09,510 --> 00:04:18,220
So, how fast CPU executes a instructions depend upon the clock supply to the CPU. So, 
48

49
00:04:18,240 --> 00:04:24,860
all these things will see as we make a progress, by taking industry standard microcontroller such as 
49

50
00:04:24,930 --> 00:04:30,630
STM32. So, this is a simplest diagram which I am showing to you. So, there will be one clocking 
50

51
00:04:30,630 --> 00:04:37,370
unit which will supply clock to the CPU, depending upon the speed of the clock, the speed of code execution will be 
51

52
00:04:37,830 --> 00:04:43,980
controlled. So, apart from non-volatile memory, there is also something called volatile memory 
52

53
00:04:43,980 --> 00:04:44,960
which is called as 
53

54
00:04:45,360 --> 00:04:49,070
data memory. So, basically a program operates on data. 
54

55
00:04:49,770 --> 00:04:55,580
Right? So, the data part or data of the program is actually stored in the 
55

56
00:04:55,800 --> 00:04:59,560
data memory.So, you can also use data memory as a
56

57
00:04:59,580 --> 00:05:05,700
scratch pad.That means, if you want to store some data temporarily then you can use 
57

58
00:05:05,700 --> 00:05:13,260
data on memory. So basically, the data memory is consumed during run-time of the program. Because, there are various 
58

59
00:05:13,380 --> 00:05:18,840
occasions when a program needs to store and delete data 
59

60
00:05:18,840 --> 00:05:23,090
temporarily. So, in that cases program can use data 
60

61
00:05:23,220 --> 00:05:29,070
memory. So, that's why, it makes sense to use volatile memory as a data memory, right? 
61

62
00:05:29,100 --> 00:05:33,150
as a scratch pad. So because, we don't want that data to be stored 
62

63
00:05:33,150 --> 00:05:36,120
permanently. Right? That's why, data memory is actually a 
63

64
00:05:36,240 --> 00:05:43,060
volatile memory. So, when the power is taken off from the microcontroller the contents of the data memory 
64

65
00:05:43,110 --> 00:05:49,340
will be lost.And apart from that, a microcontroller program also should communicate with 
65

66
00:05:49,340 --> 00:05:50,370
the external world.
66

67
00:05:50,520 --> 00:05:57,810
Right? Let's say, you are sending some command from your mobile phone to the microcontroller over Bluetooth. 
67

68
00:05:57,900 --> 00:06:01,440
So, a microcontroller should communicate with a 
68

69
00:06:01,470 --> 00:06:02,320
Bluetooth module. 
69

70
00:06:02,370 --> 00:06:06,790
Right? And that can be enabled by using the IO 
70

71
00:06:06,810 --> 00:06:10,170
interfaces. So, there is something called Serial I/O, Parallel 
71

72
00:06:10,230 --> 00:06:17,040
I/O. For example, if you want to connect a Bluetooth receiver to this microcontroller, then you
72

73
00:06:17,040 --> 00:06:20,700
would be connecting that Bluetooth module through 
73

74
00:06:20,700 --> 00:06:27,630
Serial I/O. So, that's why, I/O interfaces are very much important and almost all microcontrollers a 
74

75
00:06:27,630 --> 00:06:33,580
support this feature. And let's see how a modern microcontroller
75

76
00:06:33,590 --> 00:06:40,460
looks like.Let's take an example of one of the most famous microcontroller which is produced by ST 
76

77
00:06:40,590 --> 00:06:43,610
microelectronics, series of
77

78
00:06:43,610 --> 00:06:47,490
microcontrollers. This is a picture which is given by the ST 
78

79
00:06:47,540 --> 00:06:54,380
documents.And here we can see that, it actually shows all the internal parts of the STM32 
79

80
00:06:54,800 --> 00:07:00,950
microcontroller. Alright. So, if you just take a look into this image, you can identify various parts of this microcontroller,
80

81
00:07:01,340 --> 00:07:06,350
the heart of this microcontroller is a processor which is sitting inside the 
81

82
00:07:06,350 --> 00:07:12,380
microcontroller, and in this microcontroller that is STM32F407 
82

83
00:07:12,440 --> 00:07:18,860
microcontroller. So, then what you see here is an ARM Cortex M4 based central 
83

84
00:07:18,890 --> 00:07:19,820
processing unit. So,that's a
84

85
00:07:20,330 --> 00:07:23,430
processor.So, this processor is actually developed 
85

86
00:07:23,460 --> 00:07:26,060
by ARM.  So, this is not actually developed
86

87
00:07:26,240 --> 00:07:33,930
by ST.So, ARM develops processors and then it licenses to other microcontroller manufacturers. 
87

88
00:07:34,100 --> 00:07:41,300
In this case, it is ST microelectronics and they actually take the software implementation of
88

89
00:07:41,300 --> 00:07:45,280
the processor unit, and they customize according to their 
89

90
00:07:45,410 --> 00:07:47,560
need,  and after that they implement it on the 
90

91
00:07:48,260 --> 00:07:55,820
chip. So, in this case the ARM processor has the capacity to run at maximum of 180 
91

92
00:07:55,820 --> 00:07:57,920
megahertz. So, that's pretty 
92

93
00:07:57,920 --> 00:08:04,160
fast, right? for the Embedded application. Apart from that all these things what you are seeing here, it is 
93

94
00:08:04,220 --> 00:08:05,280
implementation of 
94

95
00:08:05,420 --> 00:08:11,870
ST. So, they have implemented different peripherals around the processor, and all these are actually 
95

96
00:08:11,870 --> 00:08:18,710
implementation of ARM. So, if you just look at the peripheral set, so they have different peripherals to 
96

97
00:08:18,710 --> 00:08:20,650
serve different purpose. 
97

98
00:08:20,650 --> 00:08:23,390
For example, so these are connectivity related 
98

99
00:08:23,600 --> 00:08:29,600
peripherals.So, if a microcontroller wants to communicate with the external world, then connectivity 
99

100
00:08:29,600 --> 00:08:35,390
related peripherals will be used such as SPI (that is Serial Peripheral Interface), 
100

101
00:08:35,390 --> 00:08:46,100
camera interface, CAN, I2C, USB, so on. And for the analog work you have digital to analog converters, ADCs, 
101

102
00:08:46,100 --> 00:08:48,010
and temperature sensor, and various 
102

103
00:08:48,200 --> 00:08:50,920
other things. And so these are control related 
103

104
00:08:51,080 --> 00:08:56,180
peripherals. They actually don't communicate with the external world, but those are actually will help 
104

105
00:08:56,180 --> 00:09:03,110
you to do control related activities like PWM, wave generation, timers, and various other 
105

106
00:09:03,290 --> 00:09:10,810
things. And these are system related peripherals such as RTC, which takes care of a real time clocking.
106

107
00:09:10,810 --> 00:09:12,170
So, it has PLL
107

108
00:09:12,200 --> 00:09:18,510
engine. So, which is used to boost the clock and the microcontroller has inbuilt  
108

109
00:09:18,550 --> 00:09:21,220
oscillators, they are called as Internal
109

110
00:09:21,370 --> 00:09:28,470
RC oscillator.So, even if you don't connect any clock or any crystal to this microcontroller, this 
110

111
00:09:28,480 --> 00:09:35,110
microcontroller can  run by using its default oscillators which are right there inside the 
111

112
00:09:35,110 --> 00:09:40,390
microcontroller. So, you need not to connect any external crystal oscillators to this 
112

113
00:09:40,510 --> 00:09:46,490
microcontroller. So, it has the capacity to make use of internal RC oscillators, so those RC oscillators 
113

114
00:09:46,510 --> 00:09:54,370
actually produce clock to the central processing unit and for various bus interfaces and to the peripherals. 
114

115
00:09:54,370 --> 00:09:56,950
And it also has a low power 
115

116
00:09:56,950 --> 00:10:04,450
control circuitry, it also has internal power regulator, so which is used to produce regulated power supply to the 
116

117
00:10:04,630 --> 00:10:08,560
microcontroller, and also it has watchdog 
117

118
00:10:08,590 --> 00:10:11,650
timers. And let's take a look into the embedded 
118

119
00:10:11,660 --> 00:10:15,580
memory. So, these are the embedded memories which are there inside 
119

120
00:10:15,580 --> 00:10:19,450
the microcontroller. So, it actually uses a flash for the 
120

121
00:10:19,590 --> 00:10:24,990
code memory. So, flash is used for the code memory, and SRAM is used for 
121

122
00:10:25,360 --> 00:10:31,440
data memory. So, SRAM is actually volatile memory and a flash memory is actually non-volatile
122

123
00:10:31,570 --> 00:10:38,200
memory.So, inside the flash memory you are going to store your instructions. And apart from that 
123

124
00:10:38,200 --> 00:10:39,490
it has external 
124

125
00:10:39,490 --> 00:10:47,050
memory interfaces, if you want to connect to more external memories such as a flash memory, or SDRAM memory. And 
125

126
00:10:47,200 --> 00:10:48,170
also it has memory
126

127
00:10:48,180 --> 00:10:54,090
OTP memory that is one time programmable memory, and all these are actually internal
127

128
00:10:54,250 --> 00:11:00,010
memories.And this is how a modern microcontroller looks like for the embedded
128

129
00:11:00,010 --> 00:11:06,730
applications.Let's take an example of PIC Microcontroller. PIC stands for peripheral interface 
129

130
00:11:06,730 --> 00:11:13,420
controller, which is a quite famous microcontroller in the 8 bit application domain, and this microcontroller is 
130

131
00:11:13,420 --> 00:11:16,430
manufactured from the company called a
131

132
00:11:16,690 --> 00:11:24,340
microchip.And if you take a look into it's internal parts, it is almost similar to the previous one. 
132

133
00:11:24,340 --> 00:11:26,070
But here also there is a central 
133

134
00:11:26,170 --> 00:11:29,650
processing unit, but this is not an ARM processor 
134

135
00:11:29,740 --> 00:11:35,460
remember. So, this is actually a proprietary processor design from the microchip 
135

136
00:11:35,500 --> 00:11:37,710
itself. It's their own CPU 
136

137
00:11:37,840 --> 00:11:39,690
architecture, you can call it as PIC 
137

138
00:11:39,760 --> 00:11:40,900
architecture. It's their 
138

139
00:11:41,080 --> 00:11:43,750
own CPU.So, that's why, it has its own 
139

140
00:11:43,780 --> 00:11:50,200
instruction set.So, that's why, the code what you have wrote for STM will not run on microchip, until 
140

141
00:11:50,200 --> 00:11:54,880
you cross compile it again using the compiler which is produced by 
141

142
00:11:54,880 --> 00:11:58,880
microchip.So, apart from that you can see that it also has 
142

143
00:11:58,900 --> 00:11:59,370
internal oscillators.
143

144
00:11:59,380 --> 00:12:03,640
Right? So, in order to produce clock, a default clock to 
144

145
00:12:03,670 --> 00:12:10,690
the CPU and for the peripherals. And it has a various peripherals similar to previous one, and
145

146
00:12:10,750 --> 00:12:20,240
timers,it also has a program memory, data memory, and it also has a onchip EEPROM, and the I/O
146

147
00:12:20,240 --> 00:12:21,680
ports to communicate with the 
147

148
00:12:21,700 --> 00:12:25,000
external world. So, the analog peripherals, timers, 
148

149
00:12:25,180 --> 00:12:26,780
PWM. So, it's quite 
149

150
00:12:26,800 --> 00:12:29,830
similar. Right? Let's take a look into another embedded 
150

151
00:12:29,830 --> 00:12:38,560
board here.So, this is a Tiva Development Board, which is produced by Texas Instruments and it carries a
151

152
00:12:38,560 --> 00:12:39,760
Tiva series of
152

153
00:12:39,820 --> 00:12:46,030
microcontroller. So, this is a microcontroller, and this microcontroller is also based on ARM Cortex M4
153

154
00:12:46,450 --> 00:12:53,890
processor.So, in this case, the maximum achievable speed is 80 MHz, and this microcontroller also 
154

155
00:12:53,890 --> 00:12:54,910
has various 
155

156
00:12:54,930 --> 00:12:58,630
embedded memories, so you can see here it also has 2 kilobytes
156

157
00:12:58,660 --> 00:13:00,950
of EEPROM,these are all the analog 
157

158
00:13:00,970 --> 00:13:09,580
peripherals, it also has various connectivity related peripherals, and these are some of the other peripherals. 
158

159
00:13:09,580 --> 00:13:12,270
So,the peripherals to include and peripherals 
159

160
00:13:12,360 --> 00:13:18,640
to exclude.So, all these are actually driven from the marketing end, and it depends on the application 
160

161
00:13:18,640 --> 00:13:20,880
area which the microcontroller targets.

162
00:13:20,890 --> 00:13:27,370
So, at the end of the day the features of the microcontroller is driven from the

marketing end. So, in the next lecture let's explore about different types of code memories which are

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,070 --> 00:00:01,260
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,290 --> 00:00:05,500
So, in this lecture let's explore about the Code memory of the microcontroller.
2

3
00:00:05,610 --> 00:00:07,260
The purpose of the code
3

4
00:00:07,290 --> 00:00:12,770
or program memory is to store instructions and constant data of your program.
4

5
00:00:13,020 --> 00:00:19,560
So, there are different types of code memories which are implemented inside the microcontroller.
5

6
00:00:19,560 --> 00:00:24,960
So, very simplest and cost efficient program memory is ROM.
6

7
00:00:25,020 --> 00:00:26,910
That is read only memory.
7

8
00:00:26,910 --> 00:00:33,960
There are different types of read only memories like MP ROM which stands for mask programmable read
8

9
00:00:33,960 --> 00:00:39,680
only memory, EPROM that stands for erasable programmable ROM. 
9

10
00:00:39,700 --> 00:00:42,640
This is truly read only memory.
10

11
00:00:42,640 --> 00:00:46,260
That means once you  program MPROM you cannot erase it.
11

12
00:00:46,330 --> 00:00:54,580
So, once you program it then it is final. But, a EEPROM is actually erasable, but the erasing procedure is
12

13
00:00:54,580 --> 00:00:55,740
very cumbersome. 
13

14
00:00:55,750 --> 00:01:03,480
You actually have to take the chip outside of your circuit and that you have to put that under the ultraviolet
14

15
00:01:03,520 --> 00:01:10,930
machine, and then you have to pass the ultraviolet rays, and then you can erase the memory, and then you
15

16
00:01:10,930 --> 00:01:12,510
can program it once again.
16

17
00:01:12,550 --> 00:01:16,600
That's very tedious and a very cumbersome method.
17

18
00:01:16,690 --> 00:01:20,130
So, usage of EEPROM is almost obsolete
18

19
00:01:20,200 --> 00:01:26,590
so you no longer see those kind of memories inside the microcontroller. And after that, there is one more
19

20
00:01:26,590 --> 00:01:33,310
type of read only memory, that is called  EEPROM, which stands for Electrically Erasable Programmable
20

21
00:01:33,310 --> 00:01:33,730
ROM.
21

22
00:01:33,820 --> 00:01:40,170
And we just saw that, it is quite famous and it is being used in some of the modern microcontrollers
22

23
00:01:40,180 --> 00:01:40,660
as well.
23

24
00:01:40,690 --> 00:01:46,720
We just saw in the previous slide that TI's microcontroller actually has onchip 2 kilobytes of
24

25
00:01:46,720 --> 00:01:48,900
EEPROM. Even though it is called as  ROM,
25

26
00:01:48,910 --> 00:01:53,160
it doesn't mean that you are not able to erase it, you can erase it.
26

27
00:01:53,260 --> 00:01:56,450
So, and the erasing technique is also very simple,
27

28
00:01:56,560 --> 00:02:00,130
so it is erased by using a electric voltage.
28

29
00:02:00,280 --> 00:02:04,790
So, that's why, it is called as electrically erasable programmable  ROM.
29

30
00:02:04,990 --> 00:02:10,840
All right. So, apart from that there is another type of code memory that is called as OTP,
30

31
00:02:10,890 --> 00:02:17,150
which stands for one time programmable and also these days a flash memories are very much popular in the
31

32
00:02:17,150 --> 00:02:18,660
microcontroller domain.
32

33
00:02:18,710 --> 00:02:26,210
So, flash is also Electrically Erasable Programmable ROM, but the fabrication technology which is used
33

34
00:02:26,300 --> 00:02:32,780
for flash and EEPROM is completely different. Even though the purpose is same
34

35
00:02:32,780 --> 00:02:37,120
that is both are non-volatile and erasable,
35

36
00:02:37,130 --> 00:02:38,310
writable memories, 
36

37
00:02:38,330 --> 00:02:41,840
there is a difference between cost and speed.
37

38
00:02:41,900 --> 00:02:48,290
So, the flash memory is being dominated as code memory in today's microcontrollers. And apart from that,
38

39
00:02:48,290 --> 00:02:56,240
there is one more non-volatile memory, that is FRAM. FRAM stands for a Ferroelectric Random Access
39

40
00:02:56,240 --> 00:02:56,710
memory.
40

41
00:02:56,870 --> 00:03:02,150
So, most of the STM32 microcontrollers, they don't have this memory as code memory.
41

42
00:03:02,210 --> 00:03:07,400
The reason is, so if you use this as a code memory then the cost of the microcontroller will shoot up.
42

43
00:03:07,430 --> 00:03:09,140
This is costlier than flash.
43

44
00:03:09,380 --> 00:03:15,710
But the important thing about Ferroelectric Random Access memory is its speed, the access speed is very
44

45
00:03:15,710 --> 00:03:18,140
much high compared to the flash memory.
45

46
00:03:18,230 --> 00:03:26,170
So, you find FRAMs as code memory in some of the Texas Instruments MSP microcontrollers. For example,
46

47
00:03:26,180 --> 00:03:32,640
here is a block diagram of the MSP430FR422 microcontroller,
47

48
00:03:32,690 --> 00:03:38,080
and here you can see that, in this microcontroller you don't see a Flash as code memory.
48

49
00:03:38,180 --> 00:03:43,460
So, they have used FRAM here, and it is of around 8 kilobytes.
49

50
00:03:43,460 --> 00:03:47,220
So, take a look into its size, it's just 8 kilobytes.
50

51
00:03:47,240 --> 00:03:52,910
I mean this microcontroller may be targeted for some low power, ultra low power applications maybe.
51

52
00:03:53,190 --> 00:03:53,500
All right.
52

53
00:03:53,510 --> 00:04:00,290
So, these are some of the different types of code memories which are implemented inside the microcontroller.
53

54
00:04:00,290 --> 00:04:01,970
So, I'll see you in the next lecture.

1

All right.
1

2
00:00:00,420 --> 00:00:02,670
So, here we have a program.
2

3
00:00:02,700 --> 00:00:08,990
The project name is 003Add. And it is a very simple program,
3

4
00:00:09,000 --> 00:00:15,960
and here we have a 3 global variables, and 2 global variables are initialized to some data.
4

5
00:00:16,320 --> 00:00:23,400
And after that, I'm just adding those to global variables and I am storing the result into another global
5

6
00:00:23,400 --> 00:00:24,750
variable. Here,
6

7
00:00:24,780 --> 00:00:28,760
if you look at this program, now here we have data. Right?
7

8
00:00:28,830 --> 00:00:30,720
So, these are the data.
8

9
00:00:30,840 --> 00:00:35,470
And after that on the data we are doing an arithmetic operation,

10

and after that we are storing the result back into the variable.

11

Right?

21

29

memory of the microcontroller, which is nothing but flash.

or not.

34

35
00:02:41,420 --> 00:02:42,110
memory

you have to take a look into the memory window. So, to get the memory window, so go to window here, go to

show view,

and here you can see memory and memory browser.

These details you have to get from the reference manual of the microcontroller.

44

All right.

So, open the reference manual of your microcontroller.

So, here the microcontroller I'm using STM32F407, and go to the embedded flash memory
46

47
00:03:28,820 --> 00:03:29,750
section.
47

48
00:03:29,800 --> 00:03:36,470
Let's go there, and you have to browse through this section. And here it is.

49
00:03:36,480 --> 00:03:42,900
So, I get the flash module organization table. If you're using STM32 based microcontrollers,
49

50
00:03:42,930 --> 00:03:48,490
then you should see similar table, but addresses maybe different or maybe same. The number of sections

51

maybe different. But if you are using some other microcontrollers from entirely different vendor like

Texas Instruments, or NXP, or something like that. Then you should be able to get the base address
52

53
00:04:01,320 --> 00:04:08,810
of your program memory. Here you can see that, the embedded non-volatile memory organization.
53

54
00:04:08,820 --> 00:04:15,920
So, this is actually flash memory. So, this is a flash memory of 512 Kilobytes.
54

55
00:04:16,090 --> 00:04:19,140
And apart from that, that is a read only memory.
55

56
00:04:19,260 --> 00:04:26,190
So, that's what we call as system memory. So, user cannot write into this read only memory.
56

57
00:04:26,380 --> 00:04:35,520
So, the ST has already placed a bootloader code into this memory. So, in this table this is our program
57

58
00:04:35,520 --> 00:04:39,750
code area, and the base address is this one.
58

59
00:04:39,750 --> 00:04:43,510
And the final address is this one. From here to here

60
00:04:43,530 --> 00:04:46,710
we can save our program instructions.

61
00:04:46,710 --> 00:04:53,700
So, when you download the code, the code will start accumulating right from this address onwards.

00:04:53,700 --> 00:05:01,110
So, now let's examine so this memory address, and let's see whether the codes are really present a from this

63
00:05:01,110 --> 00:05:03,270
location onwards or not.

64
00:05:03,270 --> 00:05:08,760

65

66

67

memory location onwards.

68
00:05:20,900 --> 00:05:28,420
And this memory location happens to be the memory location of the flash memory, right? in our microcontroller.

69

70

71
00:05:38,090 --> 00:05:45,080

72
00:05:48,070 --> 00:05:54,640

73
00:05:54,640 --> 00:05:56,740
So, here it is showing you word by word.

74
00:05:56,860 --> 00:06:04,120
So, that means 32 bits by 32 bits. For example, this is the contents of the location
74

75
00:06:04,120 --> 00:06:08,850
this one, that is 0x8000000.
75

76
00:06:08,860 --> 00:06:16,470
So, this is the content of the location 0x08000004.
76

77
00:06:16,480 --> 00:06:17,670
So, like that.
77

78
00:06:17,740 --> 00:06:22,080
So, if you want to check byte by byte, so then you have to do some settings here.

79

So, right click on this window and you get columns.

80
00:06:26,860 --> 00:06:29,250
So, set the columns to 1.

Now you can see a word by word. If you want to see byte by byte,
81

00:06:34,480 --> 00:06:38,980
then again select, go to cell size, and click on 1 byte.
82

83
00:06:39,370 --> 00:06:40,000
So, here it is.
83

84
00:06:40,120 --> 00:06:41,890
So, at the memory location
84

85
00:06:41,890 --> 00:06:45,970
this one the content is 0, at the memory location 01
85

86
00:06:45,970 --> 00:06:47,400
the content is 0.
86

87
00:06:47,410 --> 00:06:48,670
So, like that.
87

88
00:06:48,670 --> 00:06:49,000
All right.
88

89
00:06:49,000 --> 00:06:51,120
So, that's about the program memory.

90

Right?

00:06:52,120 --> 00:06:57,800
Let's examine the data memory. The data memory is SRAM, right?

92
00:06:57,880 --> 00:07:04,530
So, before checking the SRAM contents we have to first find out the base address of the SRAM memory,
92

93
00:07:04,540 --> 00:07:08,130
that is the data memory in our microcontroller.
93

94
00:07:08,140 --> 00:07:15,070
So, now for that again we have to refer to the reference manual. In the reference manual should mention
94

95
00:07:15,160 --> 00:07:17,190
the base address of the SRAM.
95

96
00:07:17,410 --> 00:07:21,780
That is actually there in the section memory and bus architecture,
96

97
00:07:21,850 --> 00:07:24,780
I would go to memory organization,
97

98
00:07:24,880 --> 00:07:30,060
so or you have to search by the word SRAM. I'm not sure here.
98

99
00:07:30,070 --> 00:07:39,550
So, now let me search by the word SRAM. So, here you can see that there are 2 SRAMs in this microcontroller,
99

100
00:07:39,550 --> 00:07:41,700
that means there are 2 data memories.
100

101
00:07:41,910 --> 00:07:44,680
Now the primary data memory is SRAM1,
101

102
00:07:44,680 --> 00:07:46,700
so that's a main data memory.
102

103
00:07:46,930 --> 00:07:53,120
So, its size is 112 kilobytes for this microcontroller, and the base address is
103

104
00:07:53,260 --> 00:07:59,810
let me search and you have to refer to the section 2.3.1.
104

105
00:07:59,920 --> 00:08:04,860
So, I think it is there in this section 2.3.1 Embedded SRAM.
105

106
00:08:05,590 --> 00:08:14,280
So, here we can see that SRAM1 and SRAM2 mapped at address this one. So, that means a in the memory map,
106

107
00:08:14,330 --> 00:08:15,440
so about the memory map
107

108
00:08:15,440 --> 00:08:16,760
we are going to discuss later.
108

109
00:08:16,760 --> 00:08:17,870
So, don't worry about that.
109

110
00:08:18,160 --> 00:08:25,910
So, in the memory map of the microcontroller this address belongs to SRAM1, that means from this location
110

111
00:08:25,940 --> 00:08:28,130
onwards SRAM starts.
111

112
00:08:28,190 --> 00:08:31,300
That is SRAM1 starts for this microcontroller.
112

113
00:08:31,340 --> 00:08:34,130
So, many microcontrollers have only one SRAM.
113

114
00:08:34,250 --> 00:08:34,770
That's OK.
114

115
00:08:34,790 --> 00:08:39,950
So, all these are marketing techniques of the microcontroller. Since it is a high end microcontroller it
115

116
00:08:39,950 --> 00:08:46,880
has multiple SRAMs, which are used for different purposes. But the primary data memory is SRAM1
116

117
00:08:46,880 --> 00:08:55,460
and its address starts from this memory location. Let's copy this, and let's go to our memory browser,
117

118
00:08:55,940 --> 00:09:03,230
and here let's paste this address, and hit enter, and here it is.
118

119
00:09:03,230 --> 00:09:10,120
So, this is a data memory and the data that is -4000 is actually stored here.
119

120
00:09:10,250 --> 00:09:19,800
I can show that to you by changing the radix to hex, a decimal signed. So, let's change the format decimal
120

121
00:09:19,810 --> 00:09:20,510
signed.
121

122
00:09:20,560 --> 00:09:25,310
And here you can see that, at this location -4000 is stored.
122

123
00:09:25,370 --> 00:09:30,700
And after that, at this location 004 this 200 is stored.

All right. So, we actually downloaded the executable into program memory of the microcontroller.

2

Right?

3

That is the flash memory.
3

4

So, the data of our program must be in FLASH memory.
4

5
00:00:16,580 --> 00:00:17,030
Right?
5

6
00:00:17,810 --> 00:00:21,230
So, how come data arrived in data memory?
6

7
00:00:21,410 --> 00:00:22,400
That is SRAM.
7

8
00:00:22,790 --> 00:00:24,770
So, that's a very important question, right?
8

9
00:00:24,800 --> 00:00:27,730
So, and this actually bothers most of the beginners.
9

10
00:00:27,740 --> 00:00:30,740
So, how come the data arrived in SRAM?
10

11
00:00:31,250 --> 00:00:33,160
So, we have to investigate this.

12
00:00:33,230 --> 00:00:36,320
So, as I said before, when we created the .elf 
12

13
00:00:36,320 --> 00:00:41,150
file, so these data are also part of that executable file, right?
13

14
00:00:41,510 --> 00:00:46,580
And after that, we download that into the program memory, that happens to be FLASH memory, right?
14

15
00:00:46,790 --> 00:00:51,650
So, that means, data is indeed stored in the FLASH memory. So, we can see that.
15

16

command prompt, and you will see some information and let's analyze that.

All right.

00:01:19,230 --> 00:01:24,310
So, while executing this command, if you see this error, 'command not recognized', then don't worry,

you can follow this method.

So, go to your STM32

CubeIDE installation folder

OK, that is in the C drive, ST, CubeIDE

OK?

And here go inside plugins.

And here just search for gnu

and you see something like this.

-eabi-objdump.exe.

003Add.elf.

So, here it is.

59
00:03:55,450 --> 00:03:57,160
So, you will see something similar.
