60
00:03:57,400 --> 00:04:01,930
So, if you can't able to run that command, just examine a dot list file.
60

61
00:04:03,110 --> 00:04:04,610
First of all, so, what's happening here?
61

62
00:04:04,640 --> 00:04:07,130
So, when I run this command with -h.
62

63
00:04:07,250 --> 00:04:11,510
So, what happened was, let's see, what exactly is a -h.
63

64
00:04:12,470 --> 00:04:20,200
So, -h is actually display the contents of the section headers. So, elf format has a section.
64

65
00:04:20,210 --> 00:04:26,870
It has a section header. And if you provide -h, it will display the contents of the section headers.
65

66
00:04:28,100 --> 00:04:36,650
So, our elf file actually has all these sections. And there is a section called .text, where under
66

67
00:04:36,650 --> 00:04:43,250
this section all the codes are stored. The executable codes or you can say Opcodes s or machine
67

68
00:04:43,250 --> 00:04:50,150
codes are stored under the .text section. And all the constant data are stored under the section
68

69
00:04:50,150 --> 00:04:51,020
rodata.
69

70
00:04:53,550 --> 00:04:57,510
So, actually it needs a some time to understand these sections.
70

71
00:04:58,460 --> 00:05:02,300
So, but for a time being, just take a look into data section.
71

72
00:05:02,490 --> 00:05:03,770
So, .elf
72

73
00:05:03,800 --> 00:05:06,050
has a section called .data.
73

74
00:05:06,320 --> 00:05:12,650
So, this section contains the data of your program, at what address?
74

75
00:05:12,890 --> 00:05:15,140
So, here it is, at this address.
75

76
00:05:15,290 --> 00:05:20,150
So, this address is nothing but LMA: load memory address.
76

77
00:05:20,600 --> 00:05:25,440
The data is actually loaded into this address and just see this address.
77

78
00:05:25,460 --> 00:05:27,870
So, this address starts from 0 
78

79
00:05:27,890 --> 00:05:28,910
0x08
79

80
00:05:29,000 --> 00:05:29,420
Right?
80

81
00:05:29,420 --> 00:05:34,400
So, that is nothing but it falls into the FLASH memory area.
81

82
00:05:34,460 --> 00:05:38,990
So, our FLASH memory actually starts from 0x0800, right?
82

83
00:05:39,290 --> 00:05:45,080
So, that means, the data section is actually loaded into the flash memory at this address.
83

84
00:05:45,470 --> 00:05:48,230
And after that, take a look into this column.
84

85
00:05:48,260 --> 00:05:50,480
So, this is virtual memory address.
85

86
00:05:50,690 --> 00:05:57,380
Now, the word virtual is used because this is actually Linux terminology, so don't give much importance
86

87
00:05:57,380 --> 00:05:57,980
to the virtual.
87

88
00:05:58,040 --> 00:05:59,550
There is nothing virtual here.
88

89
00:06:00,260 --> 00:06:02,300
So, everything is physically implemented.
89

90
00:06:02,300 --> 00:06:06,920
So, all memory addresses are physically implemented, at least for this microcontroller.
90

91
00:06:08,020 --> 00:06:14,480
So, if you take a look into this virtual memory address, it says a RAM address. So, that means, it's
91

92
00:06:14,480 --> 00:06:15,250
the destination.
92

93
00:06:15,850 --> 00:06:21,610
So, someone has to move the data which is stored in the flash memory to the RAM memory.
93

94
00:06:22,150 --> 00:06:23,350
So, someone has to do that.
94

95
00:06:23,350 --> 00:06:24,370
Someone did that.
95

96
00:06:24,580 --> 00:06:24,860
Right?
96

97
00:06:24,880 --> 00:06:25,750
So, who did that?
97

98
00:06:25,780 --> 00:06:28,780
So, whether it is done by the hardware or software.
98

99
00:06:28,810 --> 00:06:32,530
The answer for that is, it is actually done by the software.
99

100
00:06:32,800 --> 00:06:39,700
So, the data which is there in this memory location in the FLASH is actually copied into the destination
100

101
00:06:39,700 --> 00:06:45,270
location in the RAM, at this address and that is actually done by our code.
101

102
00:06:45,280 --> 00:06:48,560
But, we have not written any such code in the program, right?
102

103
00:06:48,580 --> 00:06:50,140
So, our program is very simple.
103

104
00:06:50,140 --> 00:06:51,630
It is just adding two numbers.
104

105
00:06:51,640 --> 00:06:52,780
So, where is that code?
105

106
00:06:53,110 --> 00:06:55,160
So, now let's examine that later.
106

107
00:06:55,180 --> 00:06:58,720
But before that, let's copy this address
107

108
00:06:59,720 --> 00:07:09,050
and let's see in the memory browser, whether it really has our data or not, so let's hit enter and here
108

109
00:07:09,050 --> 00:07:10,190
it is. Indeed
109

110
00:07:10,220 --> 00:07:13,520
these data are present in the FLASH memory.
110

111
00:07:13,820 --> 00:07:21,220
So, from here, someone is copying into the data memory and that copying of data section is taken care
111

112
00:07:21,290 --> 00:07:24,830
by the startup code of your project.
112

113
00:07:25,250 --> 00:07:28,700
So, now the code is right here, in the startup file.
113

114
00:07:29,510 --> 00:07:29,900
All right.
114

115
00:07:29,900 --> 00:07:31,430
So, in the startup file,
115

116
00:07:31,700 --> 00:07:35,630
so there is a routine or a function called 'Reset_Handler'.
116

117
00:07:35,640 --> 00:07:39,950
So, all these a routines are implemented in assembly level language.
117

118
00:07:40,130 --> 00:07:42,200
This startup file is given by the ST.
118

119
00:07:42,470 --> 00:07:44,600
So, whenever the processor,
119

120
00:07:44,810 --> 00:07:48,190
so in this case, arm processor undergoes reset.
120

121
00:07:48,200 --> 00:07:54,980
The first function or routine, which gets called is   'Rset_Handler', which is implemented in the
121

122
00:07:54,980 --> 00:07:55,850
startup code.
122

123
00:07:56,210 --> 00:08:00,050
So, now this handler will get called automatically by the hardware.
123

124
00:08:00,560 --> 00:08:04,220
And this is a very first instruction, which is there the Reset_handlers.
124

125
00:08:04,220 --> 00:08:10,130
So, that means, this is a first instruction which will get executed after resetting the processor.
125

126
00:08:10,670 --> 00:08:16,970
And here you can see that, this is a place where data is copied from FLASH to SRAM.
126

127
00:08:17,660 --> 00:08:22,610
So, here actually these are initialization of source and destination addresses.
127

128
00:08:22,790 --> 00:08:29,780
And after that, here is a place where data is copied from FLASH to the destination location that happens
128

129
00:08:29,780 --> 00:08:31,730
to be the SRAM locations.
129

130
00:08:32,030 --> 00:08:37,430
And after that, there is a 'bss' section initialization, that is zeroing of bss section.
130

131
00:08:37,440 --> 00:08:41,690
So, all those things we will explode in later part of the course, now it is not required.
131

132
00:08:41,960 --> 00:08:48,400
After that, you can see that, this Reset_Handler calls main, so which is implemented in your main.c.
132

133
00:08:48,410 --> 00:08:50,930
So, branch to main.
133

134
00:08:51,740 --> 00:08:57,650
So, before that, it also calls standard library initialization function, that is libc_init.
134

135
00:08:57,800 --> 00:09:03,260
So, because we are using standard library or reduced C library in this project, right?
135

136
00:09:03,560 --> 00:09:09,620
So, that's why, before using those libraries, the initialization function must be called. So, that is called
136

137
00:09:09,620 --> 00:09:10,460
before main.
137

138
00:09:10,880 --> 00:09:16,850
So, if you are not using any standard libraries, then this branch to a libc_init is not required.
138

139
00:09:17,360 --> 00:09:21,110
So, you can keep a breakpoint here to see data copying.
139

140
00:09:21,500 --> 00:09:23,870
So, I would keep a breakpoint here.
140

141
00:09:24,080 --> 00:09:28,970
So, what you can do is, just go to the debug mode 
141

142
00:09:30,620 --> 00:09:37,370
and you can keep a breakpoint here, just double click over here. And after that, hit run.
142

143
00:09:39,450 --> 00:09:40,080
So,
143

144
00:09:41,330 --> 00:09:42,350
let's try once again.
144

145
00:09:44,790 --> 00:09:49,410
So, here it is. The code execution is actually halted at this place.
145

146
00:09:49,770 --> 00:09:51,540
So, let me show that once again.
146

147
00:09:51,540 --> 00:09:55,080
So, first what you do is, so the IDE is in the debug mode,
147

148
00:09:55,170 --> 00:09:57,890
so just remove this breakpoint first.
148

149
00:09:58,480 --> 00:10:01,410
What you do is, first reset the chip.
149

150
00:10:02,530 --> 00:10:04,960
So, now the execution halted at main.
150

151
00:10:05,080 --> 00:10:05,800
That's correct.
151

152
00:10:06,670 --> 00:10:12,310
Go to the startup file, and keep a breakpoint here in the Reset_Handler at this position,
152

153
00:10:12,430 --> 00:10:14,470
and now again reset the chip.
153

154
00:10:14,800 --> 00:10:18,190
So, once you reads reset the chip, the code execution hangs here.
154

155
00:10:19,440 --> 00:10:28,080
Right? Now, check the contents of r0 and r2. Because, those registers contain source and
155

156
00:10:28,080 --> 00:10:29,430
destination addresses.
156

157
00:10:29,530 --> 00:10:36,270
Now to check the processor registers, what you have to do is, go to window, Show view, and go to registers.
157

158
00:10:38,240 --> 00:10:41,750
Here this is a general register, so just open that.
158

159
00:10:41,900 --> 00:10:46,520
And now let's just select this and here you can see that r2.
159

160
00:10:46,580 --> 00:10:47,940
So, let's select r2.
160

161
00:10:47,960 --> 00:10:53,270
And here you can see that, r2 is actually the source address, which is actually a flash address
161

162
00:10:53,270 --> 00:10:55,400
where data is stored, right?
162

163
00:10:56,150 --> 00:11:01,550
So, from r2 a value or data is copied into r4, right?
163

164
00:11:02,090 --> 00:11:05,230
So, you can see that, r4 is -4000.
164

165
00:11:05,240 --> 00:11:09,140
So, that's our data. And then it is stored to r0.
165

166
00:11:09,440 --> 00:11:12,590
So, what is r0? r0 is RAM address.
166

167
00:11:13,250 --> 00:11:17,660
So, like that data copying takes place and that code is added by the startup code.
167

168
00:11:19,890 --> 00:11:24,690
So, with that note I would like to end this video, so take away from all these lectures is that you
168

169
00:11:24,690 --> 00:11:31,320
should be able to identify data and code section of your program and the code actually, 'code' that means,
169

170
00:11:31,320 --> 00:11:33,030
opcodes, machine codes,
170

171
00:11:33,120 --> 00:11:37,770
they actually will get stored in the flash memory, that is our program memory.
171

172
00:11:37,770 --> 00:11:45,320
And the data of your program is copied from FLASH to a data memory, that is SRAM from the startup
172

173
00:11:45,330 --> 00:11:52,290
code. So, that startup code is actually part of your project and that is added automatically by the
173

174
00:11:52,290 --> 00:11:54,420
IDE when you created the project.
174

175
00:11:55,080 --> 00:12:04,350
And in the next lecture, let's understand about the disassembly feature of the IDE to see a various instructions
175

176
00:12:04,350 --> 00:12:08,190
generated for our code, and I will see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 5

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,520
Hey, welcome back to the lectures.
1

2
00:00:01,560 --> 00:00:05,340
Now let's understand about Disassembly feature of the IDE.
2

3
00:00:05,520 --> 00:00:08,230
So, what exactly is disassembling the code?
3

4
00:00:08,260 --> 00:00:16,320
If you check the dictionary meaning, disassemble means translate from machine code into higher level
4

5
00:00:16,320 --> 00:00:17,750
programming language.
5

6
00:00:17,910 --> 00:00:24,030
So, in our case, disassemble means so we have a tool called Object dump.
6

7
00:00:24,150 --> 00:00:31,860
So, we take that tool and we run that on the executable file what we generated, that is .elf file. And
7

8
00:00:31,860 --> 00:00:37,380
we can get back the assembly  instructions generated for the program
8

9
00:00:37,380 --> 00:00:43,680
what we have written. This will be helpful when you want to carry out instruction level debugging.So,
9

10
00:00:43,710 --> 00:00:49,880
that means if you want to see what are the processor instructions generated for the code we have written.
10

11
00:00:50,160 --> 00:00:56,220
And if you want to understand how exactly the control is passing from one part of the program to another
11

12
00:00:56,220 --> 00:00:57,170
part of the program,
12

13
00:00:57,330 --> 00:01:02,730
and if you want to check whether there is any room for optimization at all. You can do the instruction
13

14
00:01:02,730 --> 00:01:07,440
level debugging using a disassembly feature.
14

15
00:01:07,470 --> 00:01:14,560
So, the IDE also gives you disassembly window from which you can observe the instructions generated.
15

16
00:01:14,560 --> 00:01:16,150
So, the IDE also does the same,
16

17
00:01:16,150 --> 00:01:19,720
so it also runs objdump tool internally on the elf file.
17

18
00:01:20,050 --> 00:01:24,190
Let's go to the IDE, and let's see how to get the disassembly feature.
18

19
00:01:24,190 --> 00:01:26,260
Now, once you are in debug mode,
19

20
00:01:26,260 --> 00:01:31,840
so what you have to do is, you have to go to window, show view, and just click on disassembly.
20

21
00:01:31,930 --> 00:01:33,370
And here it is.
21

22
00:01:33,370 --> 00:01:37,470
So, these are the instructions generated for our code.
22

23
00:01:37,570 --> 00:01:44,860
So, all these are ARM instructions, or strictly speaking these are Thumb-2 instructions of ARM instruction
23

24
00:01:44,860 --> 00:01:45,850
set architecture.
24

25
00:01:46,180 --> 00:01:53,200
So, please note that the processor what we're using here is ARM Cortex M4, and the processor architecture
25

26
00:01:53,320 --> 00:01:54,330
is 
26

27
00:01:54,330 --> 00:02:00,160
ARMv7E-M. That's a architecture name for the processor.
27

28
00:02:00,310 --> 00:02:07,810
This architecture uses this ISA, that is instruction set architecture designed by ARM. And the instruction
28

29
00:02:07,810 --> 00:02:13,760
set architecture name is Tumb-2, which is a collection of 16 and 32 bits
29

30
00:02:13,810 --> 00:02:18,130
instructions. So, here you are not seeing this cursor, right? here.
30

31
00:02:18,130 --> 00:02:23,800
So, if you want to get that, what you can to do is, just click on go to current program counter, so which is
31

32
00:02:23,800 --> 00:02:24,570
right here.
32

33
00:02:24,570 --> 00:02:24,840
All right.
33

34
00:02:24,870 --> 00:02:30,880
So, these are the ARM instructions which are generated for our main function.
34

35
00:02:30,880 --> 00:02:33,730
So, if we just check our code, so what's happening here.
35

36
00:02:33,730 --> 00:02:40,900
So, this is actually a case of read, modify, and write. Isn't it?
36

37
00:02:40,900 --> 00:02:44,230
So, if you observe the equivalent assembly code here,
37

38
00:02:44,240 --> 00:02:50,800
so these are the assembly mnemonics, and these are the opcodes, and this is a location where the
38

39
00:02:50,800 --> 00:02:54,710
opcode is placed for this instruction mnemonic.
39

40
00:02:54,910 --> 00:02:56,870
So, if you just observe this,
40

41
00:02:56,920 --> 00:03:00,590
so this is nothing but the collection of load and store  instructions.
41

42
00:03:00,630 --> 00:03:01,150
Right?
42

43
00:03:01,190 --> 00:03:07,650
First the data is read from the memory locations into the processor registers.
43

44
00:03:07,660 --> 00:03:10,240
So, by using load instructions.
44

45
00:03:10,330 --> 00:03:17,450
So, this is where the read takes place, reading from memory location to the processor registers.
45

46
00:03:17,500 --> 00:03:25,120
After that this is the modify operation add. So, two registers are added, and the result is placed in the
46

47
00:03:25,120 --> 00:03:26,680
register r3.
47

48
00:03:26,770 --> 00:03:31,020
And after that this is right back to the memory location.
48

49
00:03:31,030 --> 00:03:39,360
Now the content of the r3 is written back to the memory location. So, if you are not able to see this
49

50
00:03:39,660 --> 00:03:47,580
opcode then what you can do is, you just right click here, and just select this show opcodes. And here you
50

51
00:03:47,580 --> 00:03:53,160
can see that, even though we have written only this much opcode the disassembly is showing lots of
51

52
00:03:53,160 --> 00:03:53,910
code, right?
52

53
00:03:53,910 --> 00:04:00,480
Because, you have called printf here which actually includes codes from the standard library.
53

54
00:04:00,500 --> 00:04:03,090
So, because our project also use a standard library.
54

55
00:04:03,090 --> 00:04:03,340
Right?
55

56
00:04:03,900 --> 00:04:11,550
So, you can also check this a disassembly a using objdump tool on your .elf file. For example, I can
56

57
00:04:11,550 --> 00:04:20,740
run the command arm-none-eabi-objdump. So, just run this command and you can use the argument  
57

58
00:04:20,740 --> 00:04:21,590
-d.
58

59
00:04:21,610 --> 00:04:26,570
So, that is disassemble. Display assembler contents of executable sections.
59

60
00:04:26,830 --> 00:04:32,180
If I run that command -d on my elf, so here it is.
60

61
00:04:32,190 --> 00:04:39,210
So, it disassembles all the code sections and gives you the assembly code generated.
61

62
00:04:42,480 --> 00:04:45,420
So, you can see that there are lots of code, isn't it?
62

63
00:04:45,420 --> 00:04:51,360
So, even though your program looks very simple, since you have used to printf, so all these codes from the
63

64
00:04:51,360 --> 00:04:56,710
standard library are included. So, our main code is right here.
64

65
00:04:56,710 --> 00:05:04,480
So, this is our main code, and our main code starts at this location in the program memory and this happens
65

66
00:05:04,480 --> 00:05:08,180
to be the first instruction of our main function,
66

67
00:05:08,200 --> 00:05:15,070
and you can see that most of the instructions are of 16 bits and since it uses Thumb-2 instruction
67

68
00:05:15,070 --> 00:05:18,190
sets, some instructions also of 32 bits.
68

69
00:05:18,730 --> 00:05:21,850
So, for example this instruction is a 32 bit instruction.
69

70
00:05:22,000 --> 00:05:27,310
So, if you want to explore more about Thumb-2, so please go to the ARM website and explore about the  
70

71
00:05:27,310 --> 00:05:32,470
Thumb-2 instruction set architecture and the discussion is completely out of the scope of this course.
71

72
00:05:33,130 --> 00:05:34,950
So, that's about the disassembly,
72

73
00:05:34,960 --> 00:05:41,020
so use it whenever you want to do instruction level debugging. Let's say, I want to debug what exactly
73

74
00:05:41,020 --> 00:05:43,810
is happening at this line of code.
74

75
00:05:43,810 --> 00:05:47,800
So, what I would do is, I would just put two breakpoints.
75

76
00:05:48,310 --> 00:05:50,530
So, one is right here,
76

77
00:05:50,560 --> 00:05:57,350
so just double click over that blue strip and put one more breakpoint right here.
77

78
00:05:57,880 --> 00:06:04,460
So, here you can see that at the right hand side in the disassembly window also there are two break points
78

79
00:06:04,510 --> 00:06:05,420
got inserted.
79

80
00:06:05,470 --> 00:06:13,510
Right? Now, if I run the code and you can see that all these instructions actually run, isn't it?
80

81
00:06:13,510 --> 00:06:14,700
And I got the result.
81

82
00:06:14,720 --> 00:06:21,520
So, the result is this much. But, if you want to do instruction level debugging of this line, then you have
82

83
00:06:21,520 --> 00:06:24,910
to insert breakpoints in the disassembly window.
83

84
00:06:24,910 --> 00:06:27,700
Let me reset everything.
84

85
00:06:27,800 --> 00:06:32,980
Let's see, I want to see what exactly happens in these two instructions.
85

86
00:06:33,040 --> 00:06:40,100
So, I would put a breakpoint here, and then I would run the code. And now let me check the register r2,
86

87
00:06:40,330 --> 00:06:42,640
because that was the affected register.
87

88
00:06:42,640 --> 00:06:43,050
Right?
88

89
00:06:43,090 --> 00:06:43,950
r2.
89

90
00:06:44,080 --> 00:06:47,160
Let's check the registrar r2.
90

91
00:06:47,390 --> 00:06:49,830
Now, let's go to the registers r2.
91

92
00:06:49,850 --> 00:06:51,290
And here you can see that,
92

93
00:06:51,410 --> 00:06:58,470
so the value from the data memory is read and placed into the internal register r2.
93

94
00:06:58,520 --> 00:07:00,290
So, that is -4000.
94

95
00:07:00,470 --> 00:07:03,770
And if you want to see the address, then this is the address.
95

96
00:07:03,770 --> 00:07:13,180
Now, let's change the format to hex. So, from r3 that is a address, value is read and placed into
96

97
00:07:13,210 --> 00:07:14,070
r2.
97

98
00:07:14,350 --> 00:07:19,310
And after that, what happens? r3 is loaded with another address.
98

99
00:07:19,360 --> 00:07:21,110
Let's see what is that address.
99

100
00:07:21,130 --> 00:07:27,790
So, I'm going to remove this breakpoint and I'm going to place the breakpoint here next line, and I'm
100

101
00:07:27,790 --> 00:07:34,610
going to run, and r3 content is now changed to this value,
101

102
00:07:34,630 --> 00:07:39,310
right? to fetch the next value, right?  After that,
102

103
00:07:39,310 --> 00:07:42,150
now let me put the breakpoint here,
103

104
00:07:42,200 --> 00:07:50,330
so now let me remove these breakpoints, and let's run. And here we can see that, now r3 contains  
104

105
00:07:50,330 --> 00:07:51,740
0xc8,
105

106
00:07:51,740 --> 00:07:56,680
that is nothing but 200. So now, r2 has -4000
106

107
00:07:56,850 --> 00:07:59,390
and r3 has 200,
107

108
00:07:59,640 --> 00:08:06,890
and after that add is executed, right? Now let me keep a breakpoint here, and let's run.
108

109
00:08:06,960 --> 00:08:12,200
You can see that, add is executed and the result is placed in the register r3.
109

110
00:08:12,240 --> 00:08:18,130
The result should be written back to the data memory, right? to the variable result.
110

111
00:08:18,180 --> 00:08:21,920
So, that's why, for that r2 is loaded with a address.
111

112
00:08:22,020 --> 00:08:25,100
You can see that, let me put the breakpoint here
112

113
00:08:25,120 --> 00:08:34,210
and let's run. And here you can see that r2 is loaded with the address.
113

114
00:08:34,240 --> 00:08:44,220
So, this is the address of the variable result. And after that store is executed, that means value is placed
114

115
00:08:44,250 --> 00:08:46,680
into this memory location.
115

116
00:08:46,680 --> 00:08:52,990
Right? So, you can see that in the memory location window.
116

117
00:08:53,440 --> 00:08:56,130
Let's go to the memory browser,
117

118
00:08:59,570 --> 00:09:01,080
so this contains 0,
118

119
00:09:01,160 --> 00:09:02,870
let's execute.
119

120
00:09:02,870 --> 00:09:05,360
And here you can see that the result is placed here.
120

121
00:09:05,450 --> 00:09:12,030
So, this is nothing but -3800. So, that's how you can do instruction level
121

122
00:09:12,120 --> 00:09:13,740
debugging using disassembly
122

123
00:09:13,830 --> 00:09:16,650
and with that note I would like to end this video.
123

124
00:09:16,740 --> 00:09:21,000
So, try this at your desk and see whether you are able to do all these things or not.
124

125
00:09:21,660 --> 00:09:22,770
And I will see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 6

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,740
Hey, there is a quick update here.
1

2
00:00:01,770 --> 00:00:06,990
So, if you want to do instruction level debugging using disassembly window,
2

3
00:00:06,990 --> 00:00:10,490
so there is one option called instruction stepping mode.
3

4
00:00:10,590 --> 00:00:17,250
So, instead of keeping breakpoint at every line, what you can do is, you can enable this instruction stepping
4

5
00:00:17,250 --> 00:00:18,210
mode.
5

6
00:00:18,210 --> 00:00:24,640
So, just click on that to enable instruction stepping mode. So,  you can see that, that is enabled.
6

7
00:00:24,660 --> 00:00:31,490
So now, you can just use this a step into option to execute the instruction one by one.
7

8
00:00:31,530 --> 00:00:36,000
So, it just executes one instruction and stops at the other.
8

9
00:00:36,920 --> 00:00:40,860
So, instead of putting and removing breakpoints you can use this feature.
9

10
00:00:40,860 --> 00:00:47,520
So, when you want to debug your 'C' program, then you can disable this instruction stepping mode and you
10

11
00:00:47,520 --> 00:00:52,270
can use this step into or step over in your 'C' program.
11

12
00:00:52,290 --> 00:00:54,170
So, this is a very helpful option,
12

13
00:00:54,180 --> 00:00:57,350
so I just forgot to use that in the previous lecture.
13

14
00:00:57,450 --> 00:00:58,890
So, you can make use of that.
14

15
00:00:58,910 --> 00:01:00,510
So, I'll see you in the next lecture.


==================================================================================

section 11---Data types to manipulate floating point data


video 1

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,320
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:06,760
So, in this lecture let's understand about Manipulating decimal numbers in 'C'.
2

3
00:00:06,790 --> 00:00:08,210
Let's explore.
3

4
00:00:08,220 --> 00:00:10,260
So, what exactly is a decimal number?
4

5
00:00:10,320 --> 00:00:14,650
So, a decimal number is a number which contains a decimal point.
5

6
00:00:14,700 --> 00:00:19,790
For example, consider this number 125.55, it is the decimal number
6

7
00:00:19,830 --> 00:00:22,520
and it is also called as a real number.
7

8
00:00:22,620 --> 00:00:29,910
So, in computer memory, some of the real numbers like the real numbers which are too small  or too big or a
8

9
00:00:29,910 --> 00:00:32,600
real number having a fractional part.
9

10
00:00:32,610 --> 00:00:38,940
Those are stored according to the representation standardized by the IEEE standard 754.
10

11
00:00:38,940 --> 00:00:40,850
So, IEEE754
11

12
00:00:40,890 --> 00:00:45,210
is also called as floating point representation of a real number.
12

13
00:00:45,330 --> 00:00:49,910
So, which is an approximate representation of real numbers.
13

14
00:00:50,010 --> 00:00:57,020
So, all computer systems and microcontrollers nowadays use this standard to store real numbers in memory.
14

15
00:00:57,540 --> 00:01:03,990
So, if you are working with numbers that have a fractional part or in case you are using integers that
15

16
00:01:03,990 --> 00:01:06,470
don't fit into a long data type,
16

17
00:01:06,530 --> 00:01:10,830
then we can use floating-point representation. So for,
17

18
00:01:10,830 --> 00:01:13,000
we have discussed about integer data types.
18

19
00:01:13,180 --> 00:01:17,390
So, those data are not stored according to this standard remember.
19

20
00:01:17,410 --> 00:01:21,490
So, let's consider a number which is too small in nature.
20

21
00:01:21,490 --> 00:01:23,740
For example, charge of an electron.
21

22
00:01:23,740 --> 00:01:25,850
How do you represent this in your  'C' program?
22

23
00:01:26,020 --> 00:01:28,230
So, you cannot use integer data types.
23

24
00:01:28,300 --> 00:01:32,080
So, you have to use something called floating point data types
24

25
00:01:32,320 --> 00:01:35,890
and those are called as float and double in 'C'.
25

26
00:01:35,950 --> 00:01:38,250
So, we are going to explore that in a moment.
26

27
00:01:38,570 --> 00:01:40,420
Let's consider a very big real number. 
27

28
00:01:40,420 --> 00:01:43,750
Let's say, distance between Earth and Andromeda galaxy.
28

29
00:01:44,140 --> 00:01:45,560
So, it's a very big number.
29

30
00:01:45,580 --> 00:01:50,060
So, you cannot a fit this data by using integer data type like long long.
30

31
00:01:50,130 --> 00:01:51,670
So because, it's a very big number.
31

32
00:01:52,180 --> 00:01:58,000
So, that's why, remember that in 'C' too small or too big numbers or numbers
32

33
00:01:58,000 --> 00:02:05,260
having a fractional part are represented using floating-point representation data types such as float
33

34
00:02:05,320 --> 00:02:06,630
and double.
34

35
00:02:06,640 --> 00:02:13,030
So, in summary, why do we need floating point representation ? To store a too small number in memory, or
35

36
00:02:13,090 --> 00:02:20,070
to store a too big number in memory, or to store a number which has fractional part,
36

37
00:02:20,140 --> 00:02:22,360
or we can also call it as a decimal number.
37

38
00:02:22,990 --> 00:02:29,020
So, in the next lecture let's explore about the IEEE-754 floating-point standard  to store floating
38

39
00:02:29,020 --> 00:02:29,860
point numbers.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:07,200
So, now let's explore about the IEEE-754 floating-point formats, to store a real number in this
1

2
00:00:07,200 --> 00:00:15,570
format. So, the IEEE-754 is a standard for representing and manipulating floating-point quantities,
2

3
00:00:15,700 --> 00:00:20,290
so that are followed by all modern computer systems and microcontrollers.
3

4
00:00:20,310 --> 00:00:21,960
For example, consider this number.
4

5
00:00:21,990 --> 00:00:23,640
So, this is a very big number.
5

6
00:00:23,760 --> 00:00:30,030
So now, how do you save this number in your memory? So, you can't save this by converting this into it's
6

7
00:00:30,110 --> 00:00:34,600
binary equivalent, that would consume lot of computer memory, isn't it?
7

8
00:00:34,620 --> 00:00:41,010
So, that's why, this standard says don't store this number as it is. Instead of that, approximate this number
8

9
00:00:41,130 --> 00:00:48,980
and then only store the required information such as sign, the Exponent, and then the  Mantissa part, so
9

10
00:00:48,990 --> 00:00:51,300
that what we call as significant.
10

11
00:00:51,300 --> 00:00:57,300
So, in this format the number will be approximated and it will be saved in the memory.
11

12
00:00:57,310 --> 00:01:01,170
Now the next question is, how many bits are required to store all these things?
12

13
00:01:01,180 --> 00:01:02,840
There are 2 formats.
13

14
00:01:02,860 --> 00:01:09,130
One is single precision format, which consumes 32 bits to store all this information such as significant,
14

15
00:01:09,220 --> 00:01:10,690
exponent, and the sign.
15

16
00:01:11,140 --> 00:01:17,680
So here, in this format, 23 bits is given to significant, 8 bits are given to exponent, and 1 bit
16

17
00:01:17,680 --> 00:01:18,760
is given to sign.
17

18
00:01:18,760 --> 00:01:22,570
So, what we call as single precision representation.
18

19
00:01:22,870 --> 00:01:29,440
And there is also a one more representation that is called as double precision, which consumes 64 bits.
19

20
00:01:29,440 --> 00:01:33,400
So, double precision does higher level of approximation,
20

21
00:01:33,400 --> 00:01:34,560
that is the result
21

22
00:01:34,570 --> 00:01:41,410
what we get from double precision implementation is more accurate compared to the single position implementation
22

23
00:01:41,440 --> 00:01:43,260
or single precision storage.
23

24
00:01:43,630 --> 00:01:50,380
So here, as you can see 52 bits are given to store the significant and 11 bits are used to store
24

25
00:01:50,380 --> 00:01:53,790
the exponent and 1 bit is used to store the sign.
25

26
00:01:54,200 --> 00:01:58,610
So, but it consumes double the memory of single precision storage.
26

27
00:01:58,630 --> 00:01:59,860
So, consider this number.
27

28
00:02:00,040 --> 00:02:02,310
So, this number has  integer part,
28

29
00:02:02,350 --> 00:02:04,700
decimal point, and the fractional part.
29

30
00:02:04,700 --> 00:02:10,660
Let's see, in 'C' programming how do we make use of this single precision storage and double precision storage.
30

31
00:02:10,690 --> 00:02:13,910
So, for that, you have to use some special data types.
31

32
00:02:13,930 --> 00:02:20,240
So, for example, if we want to store this number in memory you cannot use integer data types such as int,
32

33
00:02:20,350 --> 00:02:27,610
char, long. So, if you use int, char, long, to store this data only integer part will be stored.
33

34
00:02:27,810 --> 00:02:29,790
So, you will lose the fractional part.
34

35
00:02:29,790 --> 00:02:36,450
So, instead of that, use the data types which are used to represent these decimal numbers those are float
35

36
00:02:36,510 --> 00:02:38,270
and double.
36

37
00:02:38,350 --> 00:02:44,120
So, now the float is for 32 bit floating-point representation that is single precision, and double is
37

38
00:02:44,120 --> 00:02:49,430
far 64 bit floating-point representation which is double precision.
38

39
00:02:49,470 --> 00:02:54,510
So now, let's explore about the Format specifier for float and double date types
39

40
00:02:54,500 --> 00:03:03,550
when we do a inputting or outputting these decimal numbers through our 'C' program. Use %lf
40

41
00:03:03,560 --> 00:03:10,950
format specifier to read or write double type variable, use %f format specifier to read or
41

42
00:03:10,950 --> 00:03:11,400
write
42

43
00:03:11,400 --> 00:03:18,710
float type variable, use %e or %le format specifier to read or write real
43

44
00:03:18,750 --> 00:03:21,900
numbers in scientific notation. So,
44

45
00:03:21,960 --> 00:03:28,010
%e for float scientific notation and %le for double scientific notation.
45

46
00:03:28,380 --> 00:03:35,220
So, all constants with a decimal point are considered as double by default by the compiler.
46

47
00:03:35,220 --> 00:03:39,780
Now, in the next lecture let's do some exercises using float and double and I will see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:03,500
So, in this lecture let's experiment with float and double data types.
1

2
00:00:04,010 --> 00:00:09,120
So now, before that let me explain the range of these data types.
2

3
00:00:09,260 --> 00:00:16,020
Range of float. So, float storage size is 4 bytes, and its precision is up to 6 decimal places after
3

4
00:00:16,020 --> 00:00:22,710
the decimal point, and the value range is 1.2 into 10 to the power -38 to
4

5
00:00:22,710 --> 00:00:28,090
3.4 into 10 to the power +38. And the range of double is 2.3 into 10
5

6
00:00:28,080 --> 00:00:35,190
to the power -308 to 1.7 into 10 to the power 308, and the precision is up to
6

7
00:00:35,220 --> 00:00:36,890
15 decimal places,
7

8
00:00:36,900 --> 00:00:42,240
and since it is a double precision storage, it consumes 8 bytes in the memory.
8

9
00:00:42,720 --> 00:00:48,370
So, now in the program, I have a variable called number and it is initialized to this number.
9

10
00:00:48,420 --> 00:00:54,510
And this is actually a real number, which has integer part, decimal point, and the fractional part.
10

11
00:00:54,660 --> 00:00:57,110
And I have used the data type  float here.
11

12
00:00:57,120 --> 00:00:58,890
Let's see how to print this number.
12

13
00:00:59,040 --> 00:01:05,830
If I want to print this, what I would do is, I would just use %f and let me print that number.
13

14
00:01:05,850 --> 00:01:08,970
Let's see, how the output looks like.
14

15
00:01:09,070 --> 00:01:12,100
Let me run the code and this is how the output looks like,
15

16
00:01:12,100 --> 00:01:16,360
and here you can see that it printed these many decimal places,
16

17
00:01:16,360 --> 00:01:23,620
so the %f actually prints 6 decimal places of the number after the decimal point.
17

18
00:01:23,620 --> 00:01:29,080
So, now the result is actually correct with respect to 6 decimal places.
18

19
00:01:29,170 --> 00:01:36,110
And if you want to know what is the status of these decimal spaces you can increase the width of the output
19

20
00:01:36,130 --> 00:01:39,250
by mentioning the width specifier here.
20

21
00:01:39,250 --> 00:01:43,080
So, what I can do is, I can write 0.9.
21

22
00:01:43,130 --> 00:01:50,800
So now, this is like telling print up to 9 decimal places after the decimal point and now let's see.
22

23
00:01:51,190 --> 00:01:56,040
And here you can see that, the result is now with respect to a 9 decimal places,
23

24
00:01:56,050 --> 00:01:59,020
but up to 6 is correct,
24

25
00:01:59,020 --> 00:02:02,120
and after that these three are actually wrong.
25

26
00:02:02,140 --> 00:02:04,000
So, there is a precision loss, isn't it?
26

27
00:02:04,120 --> 00:02:06,760
So, now let's change this data type to double.
27

28
00:02:06,790 --> 00:02:08,290
So, you will get better precision
28

29
00:02:08,290 --> 00:02:10,330
now. You can print up to 15,
29

30
00:02:10,400 --> 00:02:16,050
let's print up to 14 decimal places and I can give 'lf' here.
30

31
00:02:16,210 --> 00:02:19,210
So, let's use lf for the double.
31

32
00:02:19,240 --> 00:02:21,860
Let's run this code,
32

33
00:02:22,030 --> 00:02:26,170
and here you can see that you actually got the better precision.
33

34
00:02:26,170 --> 00:02:28,840
So, the result is actually correct.
34

35
00:02:28,840 --> 00:02:32,640
So, the result is actually identical with this a fractional part.
35

36
00:02:33,070 --> 00:02:34,090
That's correct.
36

37
00:02:34,090 --> 00:02:39,790
So, now what we can do is, we can also print this output in scientific notation. For that what have
37

38
00:02:39,790 --> 00:02:47,320
to do is, you have to use %e here, instead of %f. So, %e will print this result
38

39
00:02:47,440 --> 00:02:52,290
in scientific notation. Let's try this program.
39

40
00:02:52,320 --> 00:02:56,370
And here we can see that you got the result in scientific notation.
40

41
00:02:56,370 --> 00:03:00,630
So, the meaning of this is 10 to the power 1.
41

42
00:03:00,680 --> 00:03:07,070
So, now you can also ask the printf to print with respect to let's say 2 decimal places.
42

43
00:03:07,070 --> 00:03:10,050
So, for that, I would use 2 here, 0.2.
43

44
00:03:10,070 --> 00:03:12,420
Let's see how the output looks like.
44

45
00:03:12,440 --> 00:03:13,980
So, this is the output.
45

46
00:03:14,030 --> 00:03:19,770
You can see that the output is printed up to 2 decimal places after the decimal point.
46

47
00:03:19,790 --> 00:03:23,760
So, here you can see that the 8 actually rounds of to it's a nearest number. So,
47

48
00:03:23,780 --> 00:03:27,610
that's why, it printed 45.79. Isn't it?
48

49
00:03:27,620 --> 00:03:33,970
And look at the scientific notation it is 4.58 into 10 to the power 1.
49

50
00:03:33,980 --> 00:03:37,160
So, that would give you 45.8.
50

51
00:03:40,210 --> 00:03:43,030
So, now let's modify this program to store a
51

52
00:03:43,240 --> 00:03:43,870
let's say
52

53
00:03:43,870 --> 00:03:45,160
charge of an electron.
53

54
00:03:45,580 --> 00:03:47,150
Let me create this variable let's say
54

55
00:03:47,160 --> 00:03:51,820
chargeE, and the charge of an electron is this number,
55

56
00:03:51,820 --> 00:03:54,390
you can also use the minus sign here.
56

57
00:03:54,400 --> 00:04:00,580
You can also store the number in scientific notation into the float or double data type variables.
57

58
00:04:00,730 --> 00:04:03,010
So, but you cannot write like this
58

59
00:04:03,070 --> 00:04:08,220
what you have to do here is, a remove this and type e -19.
59

60
00:04:08,260 --> 00:04:10,930
This is how you store the number with scientific notation.
60

61
00:04:11,050 --> 00:04:14,690
So, this is like 10 to the power -19.
61

62
00:04:14,700 --> 00:04:16,160
So now, let's print this number.
62

63
00:04:16,160 --> 00:04:17,800
Let's see how this looks like.
63

64
00:04:17,820 --> 00:04:24,590
Let me print using %f and let me also print using %e. Sorry,
64

65
00:04:24,630 --> 00:04:26,670
so there is some problem.
65

66
00:04:26,670 --> 00:04:28,200
So, here is a output.
66

67
00:04:28,470 --> 00:04:31,350
First of all in the first output it printed 0,
67

68
00:04:31,350 --> 00:04:35,030
that makes sense because it's a very small number.
68

69
00:04:35,160 --> 00:04:40,050
And look at the scientific notation, it actually printed almost correct

70
00:04:40,050 --> 00:04:45,910
and for the fractional part it printed with respect to a 6 decimal places.
70

71
00:04:45,930 --> 00:04:49,090
So, that's why, this 6 became 7 here.
71

72
00:04:49,230 --> 00:04:50,160
Correct?
72

73
00:04:50,160 --> 00:04:56,670
So, that means you cannot use a float variable here to store the exact value of the charge of an electron.
73

74
00:04:57,060 --> 00:04:59,950
Because, there is a precision loss here. Isn't it?
74

75
00:04:59,970 --> 00:05:06,930
So here, let's say your requirement is to show the data with respect to 8 decimal places after the
75

76
00:05:06,930 --> 00:05:08,310
decimal point.

77
00:05:08,310 --> 00:05:10,750
So, for that you can mention 0.8,

78

but the result will not improve

79
00:05:12,210 --> 00:05:12,990
you can see that.

80
00:05:15,730 --> 00:05:18,790
Sorry, let me give point 8 here and here as well.

81

You can see that the result won't improve.

82
00:05:22,490 --> 00:05:28,520
So, here you can see that 602176 it is showing 60.
82

83
00:05:28,540 --> 00:05:35,780
So, the result will not improve, isn't it?  So, that's why, it makes sense to use a double here, because double
83

84
00:05:35,780 --> 00:05:39,300
gives you precision up to 15 decimal places.
84

85
00:05:39,320 --> 00:05:40,550
Let's use double here,
85

86
00:05:41,790 --> 00:05:49,540
and let's change this to lf and le. The format specifier and we can now see better

87
00:05:49,540 --> 00:05:51,840
precision for this program.

88
00:05:53,170 --> 00:05:57,970
Here you can see that the fractional part is exactly like the input, isn't it?

89
00:05:58,030 --> 00:06:00,070
The reason for that is in double
89

90
00:06:00,070 --> 00:06:05,060
you can obtain the precision up to 15 decimal places after the decimal point.

91
00:06:05,080 --> 00:06:11,380
So now, when you try to print in a %lf format, you can see that it is printing all zeros,
91

92

isn't it?

93

So, because it's a very small number.

So, if you want to get output let's introduce more decimal places,

95

let me give 28 here. And let's see how this goes.

96

So, now you can see that it is printing the fractional part with respect to a 28 decimal places
96

97

after the decimal point.

section 12--Taking input from the user using scanf()

video 1

00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,550

2
00:00:01,560 --> 00:00:09,330
So, in this lecture let's explore about another important 'C' library function Scanf. So, scanf is a standard
2

3
00:00:09,330 --> 00:00:18,050
library function that allows you to read input data from standard in. So, standard in for us is
3

4
00:00:18,120 --> 00:00:25,050
generally the keyboard, if you are working on your personal computer then usually a standard in means
4

5
00:00:25,350 --> 00:00:28,400
the keyboard, right? So, if it is embedded system,
5

6
00:00:28,400 --> 00:00:34,960
then it may not be keyboard. So, it may be a touchscreen or it may be a keypad like that.
6

7
00:00:34,980 --> 00:00:42,720
So, by using scanf library function so you can read both characters and numbers from the keyboard.
7

8
00:00:43,870 --> 00:00:46,130
Let's explore how to use this scanf.
8

9
00:00:46,660 --> 00:00:50,000
So now, take a look into this code snippet here.
9

10
00:00:50,080 --> 00:00:53,440
So, here that is a variable definition age,
10

11
00:00:53,620 --> 00:00:55,330
and in this code
11

12
00:00:55,390 --> 00:00:59,900
so you are actually asking user to enter the age.So,
12

13
00:01:00,010 --> 00:01:04,950
now this is how the user data is read inside the program.
13

14
00:01:05,170 --> 00:01:07,620
Here,observe this part here.
14

15
00:01:07,690 --> 00:01:11,380
So here, whatever the user enters usually the number, right?
15

16
00:01:11,860 --> 00:01:15,610
So, that is actually put into the address of the variable.
16

17
00:01:16,290 --> 00:01:17,750
So, what scanf does is,
17

18
00:01:17,890 --> 00:01:25,330
whatever the value you enter it will be read from the input stream and it will be saved at the address
18

19
00:01:25,330 --> 00:01:26,330
of this variable.
19

20
00:01:26,570 --> 00:01:31,620
So, that's the reason you have to mention the address operator here, so address of age.
20

21
00:01:31,630 --> 00:01:33,880
This is a format specifier.
21

22
00:01:34,090 --> 00:01:41,830
So, in this case, scanf reads an integer(that is a number) which the user enters and scanf puts the
22

23
00:01:41,830 --> 00:01:45,070
read value "At the address of " 'age' variable.
23

24
00:01:45,550 --> 00:01:49,530
So, now let's say I enter the number let's say 8.
24

25
00:01:49,530 --> 00:01:57,670
So, when I enter 8 from my keyboard, the number 8 is stored at the address of age, not it's  
25

26
00:01:57,670 --> 00:01:59,130
ASCII equivalent.So,
26

27
00:01:59,160 --> 00:02:00,360
you have to remember that.
27

28
00:02:00,430 --> 00:02:00,820
Why?
28

29
00:02:00,820 --> 00:02:04,910
Because, I have used the format specifier %d.
29

30
00:02:04,960 --> 00:02:08,670
So, I want a number to be stored in the variable.So,
30

31
00:02:08,900 --> 00:02:16,000
suppose if I use %c here, then it's ASCII equivalent will be stored in this variable. So,
31

32
00:02:16,000 --> 00:02:17,050
that is 56.
32

33
00:02:17,730 --> 00:02:22,170
So, that's how you should differentiate.So, what actually should be stored in the variable,
33

34
00:02:22,180 --> 00:02:29,810
you can decide that by using the format specifier. So here, I want to store a character.
34

35
00:02:29,900 --> 00:02:34,660
So, that means an ASCII value of the character into the variable.
35

36
00:02:34,670 --> 00:02:39,790
So, in this case, I use %c. So, suppose if I enter the letter A,
36

37
00:02:39,860 --> 00:02:47,070
then it's ASCII value will be stored in the variable c. There is one more a library function called
37

38
00:02:47,080 --> 00:02:48,130
getchar().
38

39
00:02:48,160 --> 00:02:51,880
This is also used to read input from the standard in.
39

40
00:02:51,970 --> 00:03:00,030
So, if you just want to read a single character from the keyboard in ASCII format then just use getchar().
40

41
00:03:00,050 --> 00:03:03,970
So, it's use is to read a character from the keyboard.
41

42
00:03:04,360 --> 00:03:12,250
So, getchar() function takes no argument and just returns an int value which happens to be the ASCII
42

43
00:03:12,250 --> 00:03:14,110
value of the key pressed.
43

44
00:03:14,730 --> 00:03:21,290
So for example, in this case, int a = getchar();. So, here what happens is, the program
44

45
00:03:21,290 --> 00:03:28,310
hangs until you press a key from your keyboard followed by pressing the enter key.
45

46
00:03:28,390 --> 00:03:35,110
So, we'll see how to use these functions practically in the next lecture. So, in the next lecture,
46

47
00:03:35,160 --> 00:03:41,960
so we'll be doing one exercise using scanf and other functions. So, write a program which takes 3
47

48
00:03:41,960 --> 00:03:44,210
numbers from the user.
48

49
00:03:44,210 --> 00:03:48,410
And the program should compute so average of those numbers,
49

50
00:03:48,500 --> 00:03:53,870
and the result must be printed. And we'll see how to implement this exercise in the next lecture.

So, here we are creating project for our PC, not for the embedded board. So, C and C++ project, so now

let me give the name average, let me create new source
4

5
00:00:15,450 --> 00:00:18,420
file, let me call main.c. First
5

6

let's create a variable.

Let's create 3 variables.

Let me create int number1,
8

9

number2, and number3.

And after that,
10

11

now let me first ask the user to enter the first number.

So, user may enter real number, right?
12

13
00:00:37,870 --> 00:00:39,160
So, that is also possible.
13

14
00:00:39,190 --> 00:00:46,760
So, that's why, let's take float here instead of int. And now let me also create one more variable called
14

15
00:00:47,060 --> 00:00:50,440
float average, to store the average value.
15

16
00:00:50,480 --> 00:00:57,630
Let me ask the user to enter the first number "Enter the first number
16

17

\n" or
17

18

let me not give \n here.

So, let user enter after this colon. So, I would just give this message "Enter the first number", so
19

20

cursor will not move to the next line rather it will just stick at this position.

21

All right.

Now, let's use the scanf function scanf. 
23

24
00:01:19,700 --> 00:01:26,650
So, now let's first mention the format specifier that is %f, after that let's give a comma,
24

25
00:01:26,720 --> 00:01:34,260
and after that let's give the address of the first variable number1. So, after that
25

26

let's repeat these two statements, for the second number, and for the third number.

27

Let me change this to second.

28

This is number2 and this is number3. Now after that
28

29

let's compute the average. Average is equal to now
29

30

let me keep all these things in a bracket and let's divide the whole thing by 3.

31

Right?
31

32

And after that let's print the average, printf average. So here, just observe that this message will
32

33
00:02:08,610 --> 00:02:10,890
get printed right after this message.
33

34

Right?
34

35
00:02:11,590 --> 00:02:14,050
So, because we didn't use \n here.
35

36
00:02:14,070 --> 00:02:18,330
So, what we can do is, we can use \n at the beginning of the second printf 
36

37
00:02:18,810 --> 00:02:22,240
and here as well. It looks fine and
37

00:02:22,290 --> 00:02:30,570
now let's try to build this project, it built successfully and let's run.So,

39
00:02:33,390 --> 00:02:37,650
now here we can see that when you try to run the application
39

40
00:02:37,650 --> 00:02:44,030
the Average.exe ran, isn't it ? So, but the first message is not getting printed.
40

41
00:02:44,040 --> 00:02:50,200
Now the reason for that is this message is actually not reaching to the console.
41

42
00:02:50,280 --> 00:02:54,930
So, that means this message is actually buffered inside the operating system.
42

43
00:02:55,530 --> 00:02:59,620
So, that is not pushed or flushed to the console.
43

44
00:02:59,730 --> 00:03:04,400
So, problems like this you may encounter a when you use different console.
44

45
00:03:04,710 --> 00:03:08,360
So, here we are using console of an eclipse software.
45

46
00:03:08,370 --> 00:03:09,360
Right?
46

47
00:03:09,360 --> 00:03:14,150
So, there maybe a console buffering. When you execute printf
47

48
00:03:14,220 --> 00:03:19,050
actually what happens is, the content will not be push  directly to the display.
48

49
00:03:19,110 --> 00:03:22,800
It is actually buffered inside the output buffer.
49

50
00:03:22,800 --> 00:03:27,200
So, this is actually a software buffer. When your application returns
50

51
00:03:27,210 --> 00:03:32,290
so the content of the output buffer will be automatically flushed to the display. So,
51

52
00:03:32,640 --> 00:03:37,320
but in our program what's happening is, we are waiting for the user input.
52

53
00:03:37,320 --> 00:03:38,370
Right?
53

54
00:03:38,370 --> 00:03:43,730
So, there is no trigger for the output buffer to flush the contents to the display.
54

55
00:03:43,980 --> 00:03:47,550
In the earlier program we were returning from the function.
55

56
00:03:47,550 --> 00:03:48,310
Right?
56

57
00:03:48,330 --> 00:03:54,060
So, there was a trigger to flush the contents of the output buffer to the display.
57

58
00:03:54,070 --> 00:04:01,840
So now, here we have to give explicit command for the output buffer to flush the contents to the display.
58

59
00:04:01,840 --> 00:04:06,280
Now we do that by using a function called fflush.
59

60
00:04:06,280 --> 00:04:14,320
So, fflush is actually an API which is used to clear the output buffers of the output stream.
60

61
00:04:14,350 --> 00:04:20,260
So, when you use fflush the contents of the output buffer will be flushed to the display.
61

62
00:04:20,260 --> 00:04:22,590
Let's use this fflush.
62

63
00:04:22,590 --> 00:04:29,260
So here, right after this line I'm going to use fflush. fflush(stdout);
63

64
00:04:29,260 --> 00:04:32,410
So, the argument what you have to pass here is stdout,
64

65
00:04:32,410 --> 00:04:35,340
that is standard output, flush to the standard output.
65

66
00:04:36,010 --> 00:04:38,290
Let me close this application.
66

67
00:04:38,290 --> 00:04:43,300
So, here you can see that when I end that application all the contents are automatically flushed.
67

68
00:04:43,420 --> 00:04:43,960
Right?
68

69
00:04:44,020 --> 00:04:47,850
Let's try to build this project and let's see how this behaves.
69

70
00:04:51,410 --> 00:04:51,800
Right?
70

71
00:04:51,830 --> 00:04:55,640
So, here we can see that now we are getting the message, right?

72

Let's do this fflush right after every printf. So now

73

let's see how this behaves.

that's a problem.

All right. Now let's launch this application, just double click and here you can see that this actually
100

101
00:06:23,680 --> 00:06:28,540
looks good, because it is actually showing you the correct cursor position for you to enter the number.

102

Right?

103

Let me enter some number, let's say 45

104

and then press enter, and then let me enter some other number.

105
00:06:38,570 --> 00:06:44,330

We couldn't able to see the result, right?  Now

that's because, so in the program after you print the average a this code exits.

110

111
00:07:01,130 --> 00:07:08,860
Let's implement some logic in the program, so that your program hangs after printing the result. For that
111

112
00:07:08,870 --> 00:07:16,270
what we can do is, so we can make the application wait until user presses any key from the keyboard.
112

113
00:07:16,310 --> 00:07:19,430
So, that means let's use getchar() here.
113

114
00:07:19,430 --> 00:07:21,500
So, let me use the getchar() API.
114

115
00:07:21,500 --> 00:07:27,640
This API waits until user presses any key from the keyboard.
115

116
00:07:27,650 --> 00:07:29,840
So, now let me just call getchar().
116

117
00:07:29,960 --> 00:07:32,360
So, that means get a character from the keyboard.
117

118
00:07:32,420 --> 00:07:36,860
So, until user enters a character this API waits.
118

119
00:07:36,890 --> 00:07:38,790
Let's test this.
119

120
00:07:38,880 --> 00:07:45,230
Let's see whether our application waits for the user input or not before it exits.
120

121
00:07:45,270 --> 00:07:49,420
Let me launch once again. So now, it doesn't wait.
121

122
00:07:49,440 --> 00:07:52,620
So now, we have to understand what exactly going on here.
122

123
00:07:52,620 --> 00:07:54,580
So, it actually didn't wait here.
123

124
00:07:54,690 --> 00:08:00,180
Let's understand the relationship between the keyboard, input buffer, and the program.
124

125
00:08:00,240 --> 00:08:03,200
Now, whatever you enter from the keyboard.
125

126
00:08:03,390 --> 00:08:07,140
So, it actually doesn't come to the program directly.
126

127
00:08:07,140 --> 00:08:10,400
It actually goes to the input buffer.
127

128
00:08:10,470 --> 00:08:16,970
For example, let's say we are actually scanning for the first number. Let's say you enter 10,
128

129
00:08:17,250 --> 00:08:20,100
and after that you hit enter, isn't it?
129

130
00:08:20,100 --> 00:08:27,860
So, once you do that at the keyboard side, what happens is, those two data will come to the input buffer.
130

131
00:08:28,320 --> 00:08:31,410
So, now the input buffer has two different data.
131

132
00:08:31,410 --> 00:08:33,150
One is a number what you enter,
132

133
00:08:33,150 --> 00:08:33,940
that is 10
133

134
00:08:33,960 --> 00:08:36,960
and another one is the new line character,
134

135
00:08:37,050 --> 00:08:39,260
so that is your enter key,
135

136
00:08:39,300 --> 00:08:41,380
so that is also stored in the input buffer.
136

137
00:08:41,880 --> 00:08:46,660
Once it comes to the input buffer scanf actually reads the data.
137

138
00:08:46,740 --> 00:08:50,420
In this case, scanf should read one real number.
138

139
00:08:50,430 --> 00:08:50,820
Right?
139

140
00:08:51,120 --> 00:08:52,790
So, that is what our code,
140

141
00:08:52,800 --> 00:08:53,670
Isn't it?
141

142
00:08:53,670 --> 00:08:56,470
So, we want to read a real number, so
142

143
00:08:56,610 --> 00:09:01,920
that's why, we specified the format specifier %f, Isn't it?
143

144
00:09:01,920 --> 00:09:04,690
So, this data will be read from the scanf.
144

145
00:09:04,950 --> 00:09:11,130
So, we have instructed scanf just to read one data, because there is only one format specifier.
145

146
00:09:11,760 --> 00:09:12,660
Right?
146

147
00:09:12,660 --> 00:09:19,290
So, once scanf reads that this will be the state of the input buffer. So now \n is remaining
147

148
00:09:19,410 --> 00:09:20,250
in the input buffer.
148

149
00:09:20,820 --> 00:09:27,820
So, that is actually an unread data, right? After that you want to execute the second scanf.
149

150
00:09:28,020 --> 00:09:28,800
Right?
150

151
00:09:28,800 --> 00:09:32,820
So, once you make a call to the second scanf, what happens?
151

152
00:09:32,820 --> 00:09:36,870
So, this \n that is actually a special character.
152

153
00:09:36,930 --> 00:09:43,020
So, this new line character will be read and discarded by the scanf because, it's not a valid number
153

154
00:09:43,140 --> 00:09:44,920
but a special character.
154

155
00:09:45,180 --> 00:09:52,590
So, scanf actually waits for the new user input. And after that, let's say user enters a new number followed
155

156
00:09:52,590 --> 00:09:53,460
by the enter key.
156

157
00:09:54,030 --> 00:09:56,760
So, scanf will read this number 45
157

158
00:09:56,760 --> 00:10:02,340
and after that let's say you execute the final scanf for the number 3, let's say this time
158

159
00:10:02,340 --> 00:10:06,240
input buffer contain 65 and a new line character.
159

160
00:10:06,900 --> 00:10:10,500
So now, 65 will be read by the scanf.
160

161
00:10:10,500 --> 00:10:11,580
Right?
161

162
00:10:11,610 --> 00:10:15,370
And after that, this is a final state of the input buffer
162

163
00:10:15,450 --> 00:10:17,810
after 3 scanf's in the program.
163

164
00:10:18,300 --> 00:10:23,470
So, there is a data which is still there in the input buffer, isn't it?
164

165
00:10:23,520 --> 00:10:29,550
After that what we are doing in the program is, we are calculating the average and after that we are
165

166
00:10:29,550 --> 00:10:33,110
printing the average and after that we are using getchar().
166

167
00:10:33,120 --> 00:10:34,290
Right?
167

168
00:10:34,320 --> 00:10:36,060
So, getchar() this time
168

169
00:10:36,060 --> 00:10:41,940
it doesn't wait for the user input. Because, getchar() goes and fetches whatever the data is there in the
169

170
00:10:41,940 --> 00:10:42,570
input buffer.
170

171
00:10:42,600 --> 00:10:44,250
So, that's this behavior.
171

172
00:10:44,250 --> 00:10:44,610
Sorry,
172

173
00:10:44,640 --> 00:10:46,470
So, that is actually \n.
173

174
00:10:46,640 --> 00:10:49,060
So, getchar() fetches \n
174

175
00:10:49,110 --> 00:10:50,540
and it returns.
175

176
00:10:50,550 --> 00:10:52,290
That's the  behavior of the getchar().
176

177
00:10:52,320 --> 00:10:57,810
So, whenever it encounters a new line character the  getchar() returns immediately.
177

178
00:10:57,810 --> 00:10:59,760
So, that is the behavior of that API.
178

179
00:11:00,240 --> 00:11:04,370
So, that's why, getchar() actually fetches \n and it returns.
179

180
00:11:04,500 --> 00:11:10,020
So, that's why, our application didn't wait, so it exited immediately.
180

181
00:11:10,110 --> 00:11:11,160
How to solve this issue?
181

182
00:11:11,160 --> 00:11:14,090
One thing is you can give getchar() twice here.
182

183
00:11:14,850 --> 00:11:18,650
So now, you can solve this problem by giving getchar() twice.
183

184
00:11:18,660 --> 00:11:25,260
So, this is actually not a generic solution, but just to see the effect you can give getchar() twice
184

185
00:11:25,260 --> 00:11:33,290
here and you can give one message to the user saying "press enter key to exit the application". Let's
185

186
00:11:33,300 --> 00:11:36,090
try.
186

187
00:11:36,110 --> 00:11:42,500
So now here you can see that the application is waiting, because of that second getchar(), and if
187

188
00:11:42,500 --> 00:11:46,120
you press enter the getchar() immediately returns.
188

189
00:11:46,130 --> 00:11:50,990
So now, let me press enter key and here we can see that the application exited.
189

190
00:11:51,530 --> 00:11:54,170
So, as I said, this is not a generic solution,
190

191
00:11:54,170 --> 00:11:56,600
the generic solution looks something like this.
191

192
00:11:56,630 --> 00:11:59,610
What I would do is, I would use a while loop here.
192

193
00:11:59,630 --> 00:12:00,140
Don't worry,
193

194
00:12:00,170 --> 00:12:06,770
so I have not yet covered while loop, so you will understand this code after I cover the looping in 'C'.
So, 
194

195
00:12:06,770 --> 00:12:15,960
what I would do is, getchar() is not is equal to let's say \n. Here, I just loop here until I read every
195

196
00:12:15,990 --> 00:12:17,900
data from the input buffer.
196

197
00:12:18,560 --> 00:12:23,620
So, after that, at the end I again wait for the user input.
197

198
00:12:23,660 --> 00:12:25,510
Let me just test this.
198

199
00:12:25,550 --> 00:12:32,190
So, this is a average, so you can cross-check this with your calculator and then press enter key to exit
199

200
00:12:32,190 --> 00:12:33,500
the application.
200

201
00:12:33,500 --> 00:12:36,900
So, try to do this at your desk and let me know if you have any questions,
201

202
00:12:36,920 --> 00:12:44,150
and in the next lecture let's use only one scanf to read all the 3 numbers instead of using three
202

203
00:12:44,150 --> 00:12:45,610
scanf functions.And 
203

204
00:12:45,650 --> 00:12:46,690
I'll see you in the next lecture.

16

123

00:02:41,880 --> 00:02:44,130

41
00:02:44,130 --> 00:02:48,040
remember. It will not take a these 2 numbers.

42
00:02:48,140 --> 00:02:50,320

43
00:02:50,400 --> 00:02:50,840
Right?
43

44
00:02:51,500 --> 00:02:54,960
So, here you can see that there is actually an accuracy issue.
44

45
00:02:54,980 --> 00:02:55,310
Right?
45

46
00:02:55,340 --> 00:02:59,890
So, the result should have been point 2. So now to resolve this issue,
46

47
00:02:59,890 --> 00:03:07,430
so what you can do is, you can convert this to double instead of float. Because, double actually has more
47

48
00:03:07,430 --> 00:03:10,430
precision than float, isn't it?
48

49
00:03:10,430 --> 00:03:11,340
So, instead of float
49

50
00:03:11,360 --> 00:03:13,110
we can use double here.
50

51
00:03:13,280 --> 00:03:22,390
Once you use double, a you have to change this format specifier to lf. So, that's a format specifier
51

52
00:03:22,390 --> 00:03:25,600
for the double.
52

53

So now here let me use lf, let me print only 3 numbers after the decimal point.

54

55
00:03:38,350 --> 00:03:40,620
and here you can see that this is the average.
55

56
00:03:40,840 --> 00:03:43,120
Now actually the result is correct.
56

57

Right?
57

58

So, 241.2.

59

So, try this at your desk and let me know if you have any doubts and I'll see you in the next lecture.
