00:00:00,390 --> 00:00:02,100
So, now let's do one exercise.
1

2
00:00:02,190 --> 00:00:06,210
Find out the number of electrons responsible for producing the given charge.
2

3
00:00:06,210 --> 00:00:09,830
Use scientific notation while inputting and outputting the numbers.
3

4
00:00:09,930 --> 00:00:15,810
So the equation is, Number of electrons = given charge / charge of an Electron.
4

5
00:00:16,620 --> 00:00:18,930
So, now let's do this exercise.
5

6
00:00:18,930 --> 00:00:21,260
First of all let me create some variables.
6

7
00:00:21,330 --> 00:00:26,760
Let me use a data type double to take the charge. Let's say charge.
7

8
00:00:26,820 --> 00:00:29,580
And after that to take the charge of an electron,
8

9
00:00:29,640 --> 00:00:37,730
let me create one more variable chargeE. And after that let me use printf to give the message "Enter
9

10
00:00:37,740 --> 00:00:39,480
the charge"
10

11
00:00:39,480 --> 00:00:42,670
and after that let's scan the number. For that
11

12
00:00:42,680 --> 00:00:48,780
I'm going to use lf, for double you have to use lf, and & of charge.
12

13
00:00:48,780 --> 00:00:51,090
And after that printf,
13

14
00:00:51,300 --> 00:01:02,870
let me ask the user to enter the charge of an electron, and let me take this number in scientific notation.
14

15
00:01:02,870 --> 00:01:13,160
So, for that, I am going to use scanf("%le",&chargeE);. And after that let me calculate
15

16
00:01:13,160 --> 00:01:22,490
the number of electrons, for that let me create one more variable double electrons. So, electrons is equal
16

17
00:01:22,520 --> 00:01:30,250
to the given charge divided by charge of single electron, and this would give you the negative result,
17

18
00:01:30,380 --> 00:01:37,840
and let me multiplied by -1. Fine. And after that let's print the result in scientific notation,
18

19
00:01:37,920 --> 00:01:50,070
Total number of electrons = %le and let's print electrons here. And also let me print
19

20
00:01:50,070 --> 00:01:55,650
in non-scientific notation, for that let me use lf, and let me just hang the application.
20

21
00:01:58,850 --> 00:02:01,090
So, now let's see how this goes.
21

22
00:02:01,110 --> 00:02:07,200
Let me just compile this code, and let me execute the binary as command line application.
22

23
00:02:10,850 --> 00:02:17,780
So now enter the charge let me type 0.005, now enter the charge of an electron.
23

24
00:02:17,840 --> 00:02:25,760
So now, you can input the number in scientific notation, so let me input -1.602e
24

25
00:02:26,060 --> 00:02:27,620
-19.
25

26
00:02:27,740 --> 00:02:33,950
These are the outputs. So, now the first output is in scientific notation, and the second output is in
26

27
00:02:33,950 --> 00:02:41,550
non-scientific notation.So, that's about usage of float and double data types in 'C' and we'll do more
27

28
00:02:41,550 --> 00:02:45,830
examples as we make a progress in this course. So, I'll see you in the next lecture.


==================================================================================

section 13--pointers

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,150 --> 00:00:00,810
Hey, welcome back.
1

2
00:00:00,840 --> 00:00:08,100
So, from this lecture onwards let's explore about Pointers in 'C'. Pointers are one of the most important
2

3
00:00:08,130 --> 00:00:16,560
and essential programming feature which is available in 'C' programming language. Pointers make 'C' programming
3

4
00:00:16,560 --> 00:00:18,790
language more powerful.
4

5
00:00:18,840 --> 00:00:26,520
Also pointers are heavily used in embedded 'C' programming in order to talk to the peripherals, read data from
5

6
00:00:26,520 --> 00:00:33,330
the peripheral, write data to the peripheral, to configure the peripheral, to store data in some RAM location, so
6

7
00:00:33,330 --> 00:00:35,040
everywhere we use pointers.
7

8
00:00:35,490 --> 00:00:40,890
So, from this lecture onwards let's study about pointers step by step.
8

9
00:00:40,950 --> 00:00:42,720
First of all, what is a pointer?
9

10
00:00:42,930 --> 00:00:50,340
So now let's explore that. Consider this a memory organization in a computer memory.
10

11
00:00:50,550 --> 00:00:59,520
So, here these are actually different memory locations of a computer memory and each location can hold
11

12
00:00:59,610 --> 00:01:00,990
1 byte of data.
12

13
00:01:01,030 --> 00:01:11,150
Right? And here, so these are the addresses, and unique address is given to each and every memory location
13

14
00:01:11,300 --> 00:01:12,230
of the computer.
14

15
00:01:13,370 --> 00:01:22,730
So, a memory location address is called as a pointer.So, pointer is nothing but a memory location address.
15

16
00:01:22,740 --> 00:01:24,620
So, this is called as a pointer.
16

17
00:01:24,680 --> 00:01:31,670
The reason for that is it actually points to the data, right? which is stored in the associated memory
17

18
00:01:31,670 --> 00:01:32,150
location.
18

19
00:01:32,150 --> 00:01:34,370
So, this is the data. Right?
19

20
00:01:34,370 --> 00:01:36,890
So, this address points to the data.
20

21
00:01:37,220 --> 00:01:39,410
So, this is called as a pointer.
21

22
00:01:39,410 --> 00:01:43,940
So, by using this pointer you can do various operations.
22

23
00:01:44,330 --> 00:01:46,320
So, what are the operations possible?
23

24
00:01:46,430 --> 00:01:48,650
So, you can write into this pointer.
24

25
00:01:48,740 --> 00:01:49,160
Right?
25

26
00:01:49,160 --> 00:01:53,580
So, that means you can write a new value into this memory location.
26

27
00:01:53,660 --> 00:02:01,490
You can read from this pointer, so that means this value can be read in the program by using this pointer.
27

28
00:02:02,150 --> 00:02:07,760
So, you can increment this pointer and you can go to the next memory location,
28

29
00:02:07,820 --> 00:02:08,180
right?
29

30
00:02:08,180 --> 00:02:15,170
so  or you can decrement this pointer and you can go to the previous memory location. So, all these operations
30

31
00:02:15,200 --> 00:02:17,250
can be done on this pointer.
31

32
00:02:17,990 --> 00:02:24,960
So, hence a memory location address is called as a pointer. And on 64 bit machine,
32

33
00:02:24,980 --> 00:02:31,370
the pointer size the size of this pointer is 8 bytes, that is 64 bit.
33

34
00:02:31,520 --> 00:02:38,390
So, for example, if you consider 32 bit ARM microcontroller the pointer size will be 4 bytes  or
34

35
00:02:38,390 --> 00:02:39,730
32 bit. If
35

36
00:02:39,740 --> 00:02:47,960
you consider PIC18 microcontrollers, then a pointes size will be two bytes. So, pointer is nothing but
36

37
00:02:48,110 --> 00:02:56,410
memory location address. And in the next lecture, let's see how to store a pointer inside the program.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,420 --> 00:00:05,570
So, in the previous lecture we come to know that a memory location address is a pointer.
1

2
00:00:05,640 --> 00:00:07,230
Right? For example this one.
2

3
00:00:07,740 --> 00:00:11,580
Let's see how to store this pointer inside the program.
3

4
00:00:11,580 --> 00:00:11,850
All right.
4

5
00:00:11,850 --> 00:00:16,240
So, before that how do you store a data or a value inside your program.
5

6
00:00:16,320 --> 00:00:19,760
Let's say, I want to store the value of the temperature.
6

7
00:00:19,830 --> 00:00:23,340
So, what I do? I actually create a variable for that, right?
7

8
00:00:23,370 --> 00:00:32,170
So, I actually select a data type. Let's say char, and after that I create a variable let's say temp, and
8

9
00:00:32,170 --> 00:00:34,910
after that I initialize that variable.
9

10
00:00:34,930 --> 00:00:36,070
So, let's say 17.
10

11
00:00:36,970 --> 00:00:43,060
So here, this is a data and we stored that data inside our program by using a variable,
11

12
00:00:43,090 --> 00:00:46,900
so this variable will be used to manipulate this data.
12

13
00:00:46,900 --> 00:00:47,170
Right?
13

14
00:00:47,170 --> 00:00:51,540
So, I can compare the temperature, I can increase the temperature, decrease the temperature,
14

15
00:00:51,550 --> 00:00:53,070
I can print the temperature,
15

16
00:00:53,110 --> 00:00:56,710
so all I do by using this variable. Right?
16

17
00:00:56,830 --> 00:01:01,180
The purpose of variable is to manipulate the data.
17

18
00:01:01,180 --> 00:01:01,980
Right?
18

19
00:01:01,990 --> 00:01:09,020
So, in the same way, if you want to manipulate a pointer you have to create a pointer variable.
19

20
00:01:09,130 --> 00:01:16,050
So, once you create the pointer variable, you can use that variable to manipulate this pointer.
20

21
00:01:16,540 --> 00:01:19,980
So, you can carry out various operations.
21

22
00:01:19,990 --> 00:01:23,210
So, what are the operations possible on this pointer?
22

23
00:01:23,260 --> 00:01:24,990
You can read from that pointer,
23

24
00:01:25,240 --> 00:01:27,060
you can write into that pointer,
24

25
00:01:27,100 --> 00:01:30,910
you can increment that pointer, so you can decrement that pointer.
25

26
00:01:31,060 --> 00:01:37,020
So, these are various operations, so as I mentioned before and all you can do that once you create the
26

27
00:01:37,150 --> 00:01:38,500
pointer variable.
27

28
00:01:38,560 --> 00:01:47,470
So, now let's see how to create a pointer variable and that we call as pointer variable definition.
28

29
00:01:47,530 --> 00:01:48,580
Same as your
29

30
00:01:48,580 --> 00:01:55,750
normal variable definition. Here also you have to first mention the pointer data type and after that you
30

31
00:01:55,750 --> 00:02:02,420
have to give a appropriate name for the pointer variable. So now before that,
31

32
00:02:02,440 --> 00:02:08,380
let's explore what are the pointer data types are available in 'C' programming language.
32

33
00:02:08,380 --> 00:02:13,120
So, these are the pointer data types which are available in 'C'.
33

34
00:02:13,120 --> 00:02:21,160
So, you have to use this asterisk(*) in order to differentiate between a pointer variable definition and
34

35
00:02:21,190 --> 00:02:23,020
a normal variable definition.
35

36
00:02:23,680 --> 00:02:28,990
So, instead of char you have to use char*, instead of int you have to use int*,
36

37
00:02:29,020 --> 00:02:33,960
so like that. You can even use unsigned flavor of that.
37

38
00:02:34,020 --> 00:02:38,240
So, now let's write a code in order to store a pointer inside the program.
38

39
00:02:38,730 --> 00:02:44,610
Let's say, this is a pointer and let's see how to store this pointer in the program.
39

40
00:02:45,060 --> 00:02:46,800
So, this is a number, right?
40

41
00:02:46,800 --> 00:02:52,440
So, from the compiler point of view or from your point of view this is a number.
41

42
00:02:52,440 --> 00:02:52,830
Right?
42

43
00:02:53,550 --> 00:02:55,770
Let's select a suitable data type for this.
43

44
00:02:55,770 --> 00:02:59,450
So, this seems to be a very big number of 8 bytes,
44

45
00:02:59,520 --> 00:03:01,860
so we have to use long long type.
45

46
00:03:01,860 --> 00:03:02,670
Right?
46

47
00:03:02,670 --> 00:03:10,050
So, what I do is, I actually create a variable of type long long int. Let's say someAddress and then
47

48
00:03:10,080 --> 00:03:14,450
I initialize this variable with this number.
48

49
00:03:14,460 --> 00:03:17,510
So, Is this a pointer variable definition?
49

50
00:03:17,520 --> 00:03:18,720
No, it is not.
50

51
00:03:18,720 --> 00:03:22,010
Because, for you or for the compiler
51

52
00:03:22,080 --> 00:03:23,430
this is just a number,
52

53
00:03:23,460 --> 00:03:29,730
this is not a pointer, and you have store that number inside a variable of type long long int.
53

54
00:03:29,970 --> 00:03:32,860
So, there is nothing special about this statement.
54

55
00:03:32,970 --> 00:03:34,190
Right?
55

56
00:03:34,230 --> 00:03:39,980
You may be thinking that this is a pointer, but for the compiler it is absolutely not, for the compiler
56

57
00:03:39,990 --> 00:03:41,730
it is just a number.
57

58
00:03:41,730 --> 00:03:45,080
So now how to tell the compiler that, this is not a number
58

59
00:03:45,090 --> 00:03:52,320
but this is a pointer. So, unless you tell that to the compiler, compiler will not let you to treat that
59

60
00:03:52,320 --> 00:03:59,970
as a pointer and it will not let you to do the pointer operation such as read, write, increment, decrement,
60

61
00:04:00,000 --> 00:04:01,780
and various other things.
61

62
00:04:01,800 --> 00:04:07,600
So now, you have to tell the compiler that this is not just a number, but it is a pointer,
62

63
00:04:07,620 --> 00:04:14,040
it is a memory location address. For that, we have to use pointer variable definition.
63

64
00:04:14,250 --> 00:04:17,190
So, what I do is, so I go to the next line.
64

65
00:04:17,190 --> 00:04:19,980
So, now let me create a pointer variable.
65

66
00:04:19,980 --> 00:04:22,080
So, first you have to select the data type
66

67
00:04:22,080 --> 00:04:22,510
right?
67

68
00:04:22,520 --> 00:04:23,460
pointer data type.
68

69
00:04:24,240 --> 00:04:27,330
So, now let's randomly select one pointer data type.
69

70
00:04:27,330 --> 00:04:29,060
Let me select char*.
70

71
00:04:29,160 --> 00:04:31,660
This is a pointer data type.
71

72
00:04:31,860 --> 00:04:36,540
After that let me create a variable, let's say address1.
72

73
00:04:36,600 --> 00:04:44,710
And after that, I initialize this variable to this number. So now again for the compiler this is just a
73

74
00:04:44,710 --> 00:04:46,960
number, right? of type
74

75
00:04:46,960 --> 00:04:48,860
long long int, right?
75

76
00:04:49,120 --> 00:04:54,050
But the type of this variable is pointer type.
76

77
00:04:54,190 --> 00:04:54,580
Right?
77

78
00:04:54,580 --> 00:04:58,210
So, hence there is a data type mismatch.
78

79
00:04:58,210 --> 00:04:58,910
Right?
79

80
00:04:58,930 --> 00:05:06,850
So, tell the compiler this is not a number, but this is a pointer by using explicit casting. So, let's cast
80

81
00:05:06,850 --> 00:05:09,670
this number to char*.
81

82
00:05:09,670 --> 00:05:16,330
So, now compiler will consider this as a pointer not as a normal value or data,
82

83
00:05:16,330 --> 00:05:22,330
this is a pointer. And you have stored a pointer inside the pointer variable.
83

84
00:05:22,330 --> 00:05:29,440
Now you can use this a pointer variable to manipulate this pointer. So, hence
84

85
00:05:29,470 --> 00:05:32,090
this is not a valid pointer
85

86
00:05:32,110 --> 00:05:33,250
variable definition.
86

87
00:05:33,250 --> 00:05:34,250
So, let me remove that.
87

88
00:05:34,270 --> 00:05:36,990
So, this is a valid pointer
88

89
00:05:37,040 --> 00:05:40,420
variable definition and initialization.
89

90
00:05:40,480 --> 00:05:46,990
So, remember that, if I remove this explicit casting, then what happens is compiler will generate errors.
90

91
00:05:46,990 --> 00:05:52,030
So, you can see that or it can generate warnings. Right?
91

92
00:05:52,080 --> 00:05:52,830
Sorry about that.
92

93
00:05:55,910 --> 00:05:57,430
So, see here there is a warning,
93

94
00:05:57,500 --> 00:05:58,050
right?
94

95
00:05:59,050 --> 00:06:01,690
Initialization of char*.
95

96
00:06:01,960 --> 00:06:05,860
Yes, initialization of a char* from long long int.
96

97
00:06:06,040 --> 00:06:09,130
That means the compiler is considering this as a normal number,
97

98
00:06:09,520 --> 00:06:15,270
so tell the compiler that this is not normal number, but a pointed by using pointer data type.
98

99
00:06:15,610 --> 00:06:21,370
So, by casting with pointer data type which you used at the left hand side.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,300
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,300 --> 00:00:06,330
So, in the previous lecture you just learnt about pointer variable definition and initialization,
2

3
00:00:06,360 --> 00:00:09,560
and here we did something like this, right?
3

4
00:00:09,600 --> 00:00:16,430
So here, this is called as pointer variable of type char*.
4

5
00:00:16,490 --> 00:00:19,770
Let's understand more about this variable definition.
5

6
00:00:19,770 --> 00:00:28,860
So, the moment compiler sees this definition, compiler allocates 8 bytes for this variable to store
6

7
00:00:28,950 --> 00:00:30,090
this pointer.
7

8
00:00:30,460 --> 00:00:30,970
All right.Now
8

9
00:00:31,050 --> 00:00:32,070
let's move forward.
9

10
00:00:32,400 --> 00:00:34,560
So, when the pointer variable is created,
10

11
00:00:34,920 --> 00:00:38,620
so, let's say the pointer variable in our case is address1.
11

12
00:00:38,640 --> 00:00:45,310
So, when it is created, 8 bytes of memory will be reserved for this variable.
12

13
00:00:45,330 --> 00:00:45,850
Why?
13

14
00:00:45,870 --> 00:00:51,900
Because, in 64 bit machine a pointer size is of 8 bytes.
14

15
00:00:51,930 --> 00:00:52,230
Right?
15

16
00:00:52,260 --> 00:00:59,880
So, that's why, irrespective of the pointer variable type compiler will reserve 8 bytes of memory to store
16

17
00:01:00,030 --> 00:01:00,920
a pointer.
17

18
00:01:01,590 --> 00:01:07,720
And here we can see that, the initialized pointer is actually stored inside the variable.
18

19
00:01:07,880 --> 00:01:08,180
Right?
19

20
00:01:08,190 --> 00:01:16,920
So, this is actually the address of this pointer variable, and this is a pointer stored. And that pointer
20

21
00:01:16,980 --> 00:01:21,220
is nothing but one of the memory location of the computer,
21

22
00:01:21,270 --> 00:01:22,110
right?
22

23
00:01:22,110 --> 00:01:28,570
and it may be having some value. So, briefly you can visualize something like this.
23

24
00:01:28,590 --> 00:01:33,950
So, this is a created pointer variable and it is initialized to this pointer,
24

25
00:01:34,290 --> 00:01:43,640
and this is the address of this variable and this may be pointing to some data in the computer memory.
25

26
00:01:44,050 --> 00:01:44,420
Right?
26

27
00:01:45,020 --> 00:01:47,320
So, you can visualize something like this.
27

28
00:01:47,540 --> 00:01:54,500
Let's move forward. So, it doesn't matter which pointer data type you use for the pointer variable.
28

29
00:01:54,500 --> 00:01:59,390
The compiler will always reserve 8 bytes for the pointer variable
29

30
00:01:59,480 --> 00:02:06,190
irrespective of their pointer data type. So, it doesn't matter whether you use char*, int*, or
30

31
00:02:06,200 --> 00:02:08,840
long long int*,  it doesn't matter.
31

32
00:02:08,870 --> 00:02:15,620
So, compiler will always reserve 8 bytes in this case, because our machine architecture is actually
32

33
00:02:15,740 --> 00:02:17,310
64 bit.
33

34
00:02:17,390 --> 00:02:25,460
So, in other words, the pointer data type doesn't control the memory size of the pointer variable.
34

35
00:02:26,150 --> 00:02:30,440
So then, what is the purpose of mentioning the "Pointer data type"?
35

36
00:02:30,440 --> 00:02:32,690
So then, why do we have to mention this?
36

37
00:02:33,410 --> 00:02:36,280
So, in a normal variable definition
37

38
00:02:36,290 --> 00:02:41,860
so when I write char let's say temperature is equal to 30.
38

39
00:02:41,870 --> 00:02:48,650
So here, compiler actually reserves 1 byte, right? for the temp variable, correct? in order to store this
39

40
00:02:48,650 --> 00:02:49,110
value.
40

41
00:02:49,530 --> 00:02:53,500
So, but that is not the case in pointer variable definition.
41

42
00:02:53,600 --> 00:02:59,210
So, irrespective of the pointer data type always 8 bytes will be reserved for the variable.
42

43
00:02:59,230 --> 00:02:59,930
Right?
43

44
00:02:59,960 --> 00:03:02,970
So then, what is the purpose of mentioning the "pointer data type"?
44

45
00:03:02,990 --> 00:03:12,250
Let's see why, the pointer data type decides the behavior of the operations carried out on the pointer
45

46
00:03:12,260 --> 00:03:12,990
variable.
46

47
00:03:13,520 --> 00:03:18,830
So, the operations are read, write, increment, decrement and so on.
47

48
00:03:18,830 --> 00:03:25,940
So basically, this pointer datatype is used to control the behavior of the operations
48

49
00:03:25,970 --> 00:03:33,150
what you perform on this pointer variable. All right. So, here couple of pointer variable
49

50
00:03:33,180 --> 00:03:37,060
definitions by using different pointer datatypes.
50

51
00:03:37,070 --> 00:03:39,150
So now let's consider this statement.
51

52
00:03:39,180 --> 00:03:45,110
So, here a pointer variable is defined using char* pointer datatype.
52

53
00:03:45,510 --> 00:03:52,830
So, the meaning of this variable definition is when the read operation is performed on the address1,
53

54
00:03:53,190 --> 00:03:58,940
then the read operation yields 1 byte of data out of this pointer.
54

55
00:03:59,100 --> 00:04:07,800
So, this actually decides how many bytes of data can be read from this pointer, when read operation is
55

56
00:04:07,800 --> 00:04:08,720
performed.
56

57
00:04:08,730 --> 00:04:12,660
Same thing applies to write operation, increment operation, decrement operation.
57

58
00:04:12,660 --> 00:04:19,290
So, if you consider this definition, so when the read operation on this pointer variable is performed the
58

59
00:04:19,290 --> 00:04:23,740
operation is going to yield 4 bytes of data out of this pointer.
59

60
00:04:24,360 --> 00:04:30,360
So, the read operation on this variable in this case yields 8 bytes of data.
60

61
00:04:30,540 --> 00:04:39,660
So, that's why, in this case this variable is also called as a pointer variable to char type data or pointer 
61

62
00:04:39,660 --> 00:04:41,680
variable to 1 byte data.
62

63
00:04:42,210 --> 00:04:51,360
So, in this case this variable is also called as a pointer variable to int type data or 4 bytes data.
63

64
00:04:51,480 --> 00:05:00,500
And in this case, a this variable is also called as a pointer variable to long long int type data.
64

65
00:05:00,510 --> 00:05:07,260
So, if you're confused, then when we do the programming or when we cover the examples, so it will get cleared,
65

66
00:05:07,290 --> 00:05:09,650
but the takeaway from this slide is
66

67
00:05:09,670 --> 00:05:13,200
so this actually decides the behavior of the operation
67

68
00:05:13,230 --> 00:05:15,630
what you do on the pointer variable.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 4

0
1
00:00:00,360 --> 00:00:08,220
So, now let's understand how to read data from the pointer. So, all you have do is, you have to dereference the
1

2
00:00:08,220 --> 00:00:09,310
pointer.
2

3
00:00:09,360 --> 00:00:12,480
So, this is called dereferencing a pointer variable.
3

4
00:00:12,510 --> 00:00:21,580
So here, you have to use * in front of the pointer variable name. So, if you do this value at this address
4

5
00:00:21,760 --> 00:00:22,750
will be fetched
5

6
00:00:22,900 --> 00:00:25,900
and it will be stored in this variable data.
6

7
00:00:26,320 --> 00:00:33,370
So, in the pointer context this symbol is also called as value at address operator.
7

8
00:00:33,760 --> 00:00:42,010
So, the compiler will fetch 1 byte of data from this pointer and it will store that data into this
8

9
00:00:42,130 --> 00:00:43,240
variable.
9

10
00:00:43,390 --> 00:00:49,030
So, why compiler will fetch 1 byte of  data? Because, its type is char*.
10

11
00:00:49,450 --> 00:00:52,380
So, that's why, only 1 byte will be fetched.
11

12
00:00:52,390 --> 00:00:54,080
So, now let's move forward.
12

13
00:00:54,130 --> 00:00:55,360
Let's take an example.
13

14
00:00:55,600 --> 00:01:02,860
So, this is a variable a pointer variable and it is initialized to this pointer and this pointer that is
14

15
00:01:02,920 --> 00:01:07,000
currently having let's say this value, that is 0x85.
15

16
00:01:07,330 --> 00:01:13,770
So, when you do this, this is actually nothing but you are dereferencing this pointer variable.
16

17
00:01:13,770 --> 00:01:14,050
Right?
17

18
00:01:14,050 --> 00:01:16,940
So, that means you are dereferencing this address.
18

19
00:01:17,080 --> 00:01:25,550
So, that means dereferencing means fetching the value which is present at this pointer, which is 85. So, 85
19

20
00:01:25,660 --> 00:01:27,610
will be stored in this variable.
20

21
00:01:27,650 --> 00:01:29,590
That's what we call as dereferencing.
21

22
00:01:29,680 --> 00:01:36,310
Let's move forward. So, write operation on the pointer. For example, if you want to write data into this
22

23
00:01:36,310 --> 00:01:44,080
pointer, so then all you need to do is, you dereference that pointer and you replace the value with
23

24
00:01:44,140 --> 00:01:45,900
new value.
24

25
00:01:45,970 --> 00:01:49,210
So, this is called dereferencing a pointer to write new data.
25

26
00:01:49,570 --> 00:01:56,850
So here, we are actually writing so new data into the memory location which is addressed by this pointer.
26

27
00:01:57,160 --> 00:01:58,180
So simple, isn't it?
27

28
00:01:58,480 --> 00:02:05,680
So, for example, let's say this memory location currently has this value 0x85 in the memory
28

29
00:02:05,680 --> 00:02:06,640
location.
29

30
00:02:06,640 --> 00:02:14,040
So, once you do this what happens is, it is like you are replacing the content of that memory location.
30

31
00:02:14,230 --> 00:02:14,940
Right?
31

32
00:02:14,950 --> 00:02:16,240
So, it becomes like this.
32

33
00:02:16,360 --> 00:02:19,930
So, you are replacing this value by a new value.
33

34
00:02:19,930 --> 00:02:20,140
right?
34

35
00:02:20,140 --> 00:02:22,860
So, now it becomes 0x89.
35

36
00:02:22,870 --> 00:02:26,790
So, that is what we call as write operation on the pointer.
36

37
00:02:26,830 --> 00:02:30,220
So, all these operations you do by using the pointer variable.
37

38
00:02:30,240 --> 00:02:34,360
So, instead of directly using the address here you are using pointer variable.
38

39
00:02:34,750 --> 00:02:38,250
So, with that note I would like to end this video, before ending this video
39

40
00:02:38,260 --> 00:02:40,140
I would like to give you one exercise.
40

41
00:02:40,410 --> 00:02:45,300
So, this exercise you have to do and I'm going to solve this exercise in the next lecture.
41

42
00:02:45,300 --> 00:02:50,090
The exercise is you have to follow these steps a to do certain things.
42

43
00:02:50,110 --> 00:02:56,060
First one is create a char type variable and initialize it to value 100.
43

44
00:02:56,260 --> 00:02:57,810
That is very simple to do, right?
44

45
00:02:57,820 --> 00:02:59,180
So, you can do that.
45

46
00:02:59,240 --> 00:03:05,230
So, after that the second thing what have to do is, print the address of the above variable, so you can do this
46

47
00:03:05,230 --> 00:03:09,080
by using printf statement with a format specifier %p.
47

48
00:03:09,100 --> 00:03:10,050
Right?
48

49
00:03:10,060 --> 00:03:15,470
And after that, create a pointer variable and store the address of the above variable.
49

50
00:03:15,820 --> 00:03:22,480
So, for this you have to use address operator that is ampersand(&). After that, perform read operation on
50

51
00:03:22,480 --> 00:03:29,860
the pointer variable to fetch 1 byte of data from the pointer. Print the data obtained from the read
51

52
00:03:29,860 --> 00:03:38,230
operation on the pointer variable. Perform write operation on the pointer to store the value 65.
52

53
00:03:38,650 --> 00:03:42,890
Print the value of the variable defined in step 1.
53

54
00:03:42,910 --> 00:03:44,620
So, I want you to do all these steps
54

55
00:03:44,650 --> 00:03:50,560
and by doing these steps, so will learn lot of things about how pointer behaves and I will explain
55

56
00:03:50,560 --> 00:03:52,410
you this exercise in the next lecture.

00:01:11,210 --> 00:01:16,220

25

variable. First let's create a pointer variable for that we have to select a pointer data type.

26

27

Let me select char* as a pointer data type.

28
00:01:34,340 --> 00:01:37,450
So, data type followed by the pointer variable name.

29
00:01:37,580 --> 00:01:40,100
Let's create a pointer variable.
29

30
00:01:40,170 --> 00:01:45,570
So, while writing a pointer variable name, so always start with the letter 'p'.

31
00:01:45,690 --> 00:01:47,490
So, this is just a recommendation.
31

32

All right.
32

33
00:01:47,820 --> 00:01:55,620
So, this is not a compulsory thing that we have to do. So, the lowercase p signifies that this variable
33

34
00:01:55,620 --> 00:01:58,520
is actually a pointer type variable.
34

35

So, it will help you differentiate between a different variables in a program.

36

Let me give the name address is equal to

39

byte of data from the pointer.

41

of data.

00:02:30,980 --> 00:02:33,010
value is equal to

now. Let's print that %d and let me print this. And now let me just compile this and let's see, how

this goes.And

So now, let's see how this goes.

64
00:03:52,120 --> 00:04:00,300
So, here you can see that you actually changed the data which is stored in this variable by using  
64

65
00:04:00,300 --> 00:04:01,210
pointer.

66

Right?
66

67

So, here you can see that first when we printed this  variable
67

it was 100.

Now it is modified to 65,

that's because, you actually dereferenced its address and you wrote a new value here.

71
00:04:14,260 --> 00:04:23,670
So, you replaced 100 by 65 now, by going through the address of that variable. Alright. So, now let's summarize
71

72
00:04:23,670 --> 00:04:26,530
what we just did in this exercise.So, first 
72

73
00:04:26,730 --> 00:04:32,930
what we did was, we actually defined a variable and we initialized the variable to value 100.
73

74
00:04:32,940 --> 00:04:34,560
So, this is a variable name
74

75
00:04:34,740 --> 00:04:36,750
and this is the address of the variable
75

76
00:04:36,780 --> 00:04:41,850
and this is a value stored in the memory location addressed by this address.
76

77
00:04:41,850 --> 00:04:44,030
So, that is 100, right?
77

78
00:04:44,040 --> 00:04:52,750
And after that second thing what we did was, we actually stored the address of this variable into a pointer
78

79
00:04:52,770 --> 00:04:55,530
variable called pAddress.
79

80
00:04:55,530 --> 00:04:58,810
So, that's why, this variable is now holding the value

81
00:04:58,950 --> 00:05:02,580
this one, which happens to be the address of the variable data.
81

82
00:05:02,940 --> 00:05:09,360
And after that what we did was here, we actually dereferenced the pointer variable. So, dereferencing
82

83
00:05:09,420 --> 00:05:10,460
a pointer variable
83

84
00:05:10,470 --> 00:05:16,060
is also means that, dereferencing the address to read the value stored.
84

85
00:05:16,320 --> 00:05:22,130
So, the value stored was 100. So, that why, 100 will be stored in the variable value.
85

86
00:05:22,290 --> 00:05:29,640
After that what we did was, we actually dereferenced the pointer variable in order to write the data
86

87
00:05:29,640 --> 00:05:30,650
65.
87

88
00:05:30,870 --> 00:05:38,160
So, dereferencing a pointer variable is also means that we actually dereferenced the address this one
88

89
00:05:38,400 --> 00:05:46,170
to write the value 65. So, 65 will be stored in the memory location addressed by the address
89

90
00:05:46,350 --> 00:05:46,860
this one.
90

91
00:05:47,490 --> 00:05:53,590
So, that's why, in this memory location 100 is replaced by the value 65.
91

92
00:05:53,610 --> 00:06:00,610
So, that's why, when you printed the value of the data it actually printed 65 instead of 100.
92

93
00:06:00,650 --> 00:06:00,990
All right.
93

94
00:06:01,020 --> 00:06:03,160
So, also please keep this in mind.

95
00:06:03,210 --> 00:06:06,760
So, here "data" is of type "char". Isn't it?
95

96
00:06:06,770 --> 00:06:12,960
So therefore, "&data" that is address of data, this quantity will be of type
96

97
00:06:12,960 --> 00:06:14,830
char* by default.
97

98
00:06:14,940 --> 00:06:24,150
So, if int temp = 90, here "temp" is of type "int" , so therefore, "&temp" will be of type "int*".
98

99
00:06:25,310 --> 00:06:31,990
So, please remember this point. I hope you understood this exercise and in the next lecture we'll discuss
99

more about pointers

and I'll see you the next lecture.

1
00:00:00,210 --> 00:00:01,340
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,390 --> 00:00:08,370
So, in this lecture let's understand Effect of using different pointer data types on pointer operations.
2

3
00:00:08,390 --> 00:00:11,190
We will discuss this by taking an example.
3

4
00:00:11,910 --> 00:00:16,980
So, I am in my IDE and let's write some code. First of all our code
4

5
00:00:16,980 --> 00:00:21,830
now is going to have one global variable of type long long int.
5

6
00:00:22,200 --> 00:00:23,240
Let me type
6

7
00:00:23,250 --> 00:00:29,150
long long int and the variable is let's say g_data.
7

8
00:00:29,490 --> 00:00:30,860
g_data.
8

9
00:00:31,380 --> 00:00:33,390
And I'm going to initialize
9

10
00:00:33,390 --> 00:00:36,810
this variable with 8 bytes long data.
10

11
00:00:36,960 --> 00:00:38,280
I would write in hex.
11

12
00:00:38,310 --> 00:00:40,610
Let's say FFFE,
12

13
00:00:41,070 --> 00:00:45,540
so that is 2 bytes and ABCD
13

14
00:00:45,630 --> 00:00:49,350
1111 and 22345.
14

15
00:00:49,650 --> 00:00:52,950
So, this is 8 bytes long data, right?
15

16
00:00:52,980 --> 00:00:57,560
2468. So now,
16

17
00:00:57,560 --> 00:01:05,570
after that I'm going to create a pointer variable of type "char*". "char*" a pointer variable I will
17

18
00:01:05,570 --> 00:01:13,430
give the name pAddress1. So, remember that some people also write this pointer variable definition
18

19
00:01:13,430 --> 00:01:19,110
like this. So, they actually move this asterisk(*) from here to here.
19

20
00:01:19,190 --> 00:01:23,660
So, please note that both these syntaxes are correct.
20

21
00:01:23,720 --> 00:01:27,130
So, these 2 definitions are identical.
21

22
00:01:27,980 --> 00:01:34,520
So, in most of the books and in most of the time you actually find this one, rather this one.
22

23
00:01:34,580 --> 00:01:38,460
So, that's why, from this lecture onwards I would like to use this syntax.
23

24
00:01:38,480 --> 00:01:44,090
There is nothing wrong in using previous pointer definition syntax, so that one and this one is completely
24

25
00:01:44,090 --> 00:01:49,310
identical.So, but I don't want to confuse you with books and other literature's.
25

26
00:01:49,310 --> 00:01:53,300
So, that's why, I'm going to stick with this convention.
26

27
00:01:53,300 --> 00:01:57,920
After that, I'm going to store the address of this variable.
27

28
00:01:58,310 --> 00:02:00,400
So, let me do that.
28

29
00:02:00,420 --> 00:02:06,010
So now, if you try to compile this you'll see a warning here.
29

30
00:02:06,010 --> 00:02:06,370
Right?
30

31
00:02:06,370 --> 00:02:10,720
So, here it is. So, compiler is saying that, there is a type mismatch.
31

32
00:02:10,720 --> 00:02:11,140
Why?
32

33
00:02:11,170 --> 00:02:13,820
Because, this is of type
33

34
00:02:13,840 --> 00:02:14,800
long long int.
34

35
00:02:14,830 --> 00:02:16,670
So, this is of type
35

36
00:02:16,690 --> 00:02:18,590
long long int*.
36

37
00:02:18,610 --> 00:02:19,170
Right?
37

38
00:02:19,210 --> 00:02:21,130
But this is of type char*.
38

39
00:02:21,460 --> 00:02:24,760
So, LHS and RHS types are not matching.
39

40
00:02:24,910 --> 00:02:25,190
Right?
40

41
00:02:25,350 --> 00:02:26,720
So, that's why, there is a warning.
41

42
00:02:26,770 --> 00:02:28,410
So, in order to suppress the warning,
42

43
00:02:28,510 --> 00:02:33,930
so what you can do is, you can just typecast this pointer with char*.
43

44
00:02:34,040 --> 00:02:35,530
Let's do char* here.
44

45
00:02:35,560 --> 00:02:38,520
This is what we call as explicit casting.
45

46
00:02:38,530 --> 00:02:39,610
So, now this is fine.
46

47
00:02:42,970 --> 00:02:44,380
So, now this is fine, no problem.
47

48
00:02:45,100 --> 00:02:48,600
So now, here this is a pointer of type char*
48

49
00:02:49,210 --> 00:02:53,550
and this is a pointer variable of type char* and both are matching.
49

50
00:02:53,560 --> 00:03:00,300
So, there is no warning. After that, what I'm going to do is, I'm going to print the address and the value.
50

51
00:03:00,430 --> 00:03:05,980
So, what I'm going to do is, just observe this value at address
51

52
00:03:06,010 --> 00:03:07,150
let's print the address
52

53
00:03:07,150 --> 00:03:10,570
%p is %d.
53

54
00:03:10,570 --> 00:03:16,880
So, I'm going to print this pointer variable and the value which is stored at this pointer.
54

55
00:03:17,650 --> 00:03:22,570
So, for that I have to use * pointer variable. Let's see what will be the result.
55

56
00:03:22,840 --> 00:03:24,360
Let's run this code.
56

57
00:03:24,550 --> 00:03:28,460
So, let me print in hex, let's see how it goes.
57

58
00:03:28,470 --> 00:03:32,200
I'm printing in hex. So, value at address.
58

59
00:03:32,220 --> 00:03:34,880
So, this is a address of this variable,
59

60
00:03:35,190 --> 00:03:39,180
right? is 45. So, observe here.
60

61
00:03:39,190 --> 00:03:46,420
So, when you dereferenced this pointer variable it actually printed 1 byte of data, not 2, not
61

62
00:03:46,420 --> 00:03:49,120
3, not 4, not 8.
62

63
00:03:49,120 --> 00:03:51,870
It just printed only 1 byte of data
63

64
00:03:51,940 --> 00:03:56,280
that is because its type is char*.
64

65
00:03:56,530 --> 00:04:00,930
So, it's supposed to fetch only  1 byte of data from that address.
65

66
00:04:00,940 --> 00:04:03,370
That's why, only 1 byte is printed here.
66

67
00:04:03,490 --> 00:04:05,070
That is this one.
67

68
00:04:05,080 --> 00:04:06,650
The first byte is printed.
68

69
00:04:07,060 --> 00:04:13,720
Let's create another pointer variable and I will call it as pAddress2,
69

70
00:04:13,780 --> 00:04:19,210
and this time I'm going to use int*, and again let's do the same thing.
70

71
00:04:24,880 --> 00:04:32,700
So here, address2 is equal to let me change this int*,  and value at address
71

72
00:04:32,710 --> 00:04:37,210
so let's change this to address2 and this is also to address2.
72

73
00:04:37,600 --> 00:04:40,390
So, take a look here what happened.
73

74
00:04:40,390 --> 00:04:43,240
So now, again value at address
74

75
00:04:43,360 --> 00:04:45,180
this one is,
75

76
00:04:45,310 --> 00:04:49,390
so you are dereferencing an int* pointer variable.
76

77
00:04:49,390 --> 00:04:54,740
So, that's the reason this operation is now yielding 4 bytes.
77

78
00:04:54,790 --> 00:05:02,920
So, that means, if you want to read 4 bytes at a time from a memory location, then use int*. If you
78

79
00:05:02,920 --> 00:05:09,110
want to read only 1 byte from the memory location, then use char*,
79

80
00:05:09,280 --> 00:05:17,170
and if you want to read 8 bytes at a time from the memory location, then you use long long int*.
80

81
00:05:17,770 --> 00:05:19,780
So, now let's use long long int*.
81

82
00:05:20,440 --> 00:05:23,580
So, now let me again copy and paste here.
82

83
00:05:23,770 --> 00:05:26,610
So, before that, I would like to make one change here.
83

84
00:05:26,640 --> 00:05:28,280
So, here let's use short.
84

85
00:05:28,300 --> 00:05:29,380
Let's see what happens.
85

86
00:05:29,490 --> 00:05:31,200
short address 3.
86

87
00:05:31,330 --> 00:05:33,340
After that, we can try long long int.
87

88
00:05:33,700 --> 00:05:37,490
So, let me call it as address3, address3,
88

89
00:05:37,510 --> 00:05:38,580
address3,
89

90
00:05:38,700 --> 00:05:43,110
and this is short, short*, short means short int.
90

91
00:05:43,600 --> 00:05:48,450
And after that again I create one more block, let me call it as 4
91

92
00:05:49,020 --> 00:05:56,390
and this will be long long int and or long long, you can just use long long
92

93
00:05:56,390 --> 00:05:57,230
both are same.
93

94
00:05:58,030 --> 00:05:58,950
And here
94

95
00:05:59,050 --> 00:06:06,840
let's print, so this let's change this to long long  *. So now let's print, so here
95

96
00:06:06,850 --> 00:06:12,130
actually this will yield 8 bytes of data, right? from the memory location.
96

97
00:06:12,130 --> 00:06:16,720
So, that's why, I have to change this format specifier to support 8 bytes.
97

98
00:06:16,750 --> 00:06:24,570
So, that is I64X. Let's try.
98

99
00:06:24,630 --> 00:06:25,530
Here it is.
99

100
00:06:25,620 --> 00:06:30,840
The first one printed only 1 byte, second one printed 4 bytes of data,
100

101
00:06:30,930 --> 00:06:35,110
and third one printed 2 bytes of data, because it is short*
101

102
00:06:35,220 --> 00:06:36,430
Right?
102

103
00:06:36,450 --> 00:06:37,810
So, take a look here.
103

104
00:06:37,850 --> 00:06:44,140
So, all these values are getting fetched from this address which happens to be the address of this variable.
104

105
00:06:44,250 --> 00:06:45,920
And when you do
105

106
00:06:46,050 --> 00:06:47,760
long long int*.
106

107
00:06:47,760 --> 00:06:51,150
So, here we can see that 8 bytes are fetched.
107

108
00:06:51,150 --> 00:06:52,110
Right?
108

109
00:06:52,140 --> 00:07:00,000
So, that's how, changing this pointer data type affects the behavior of the operation what you carry
109

110
00:07:00,000 --> 00:07:01,890
out on the pointer variable.
110

111
00:07:02,550 --> 00:07:05,770
So, all these are actually read operations, right?
111

112
00:07:05,880 --> 00:07:09,530
you are reading from the pointer.
112

113
00:07:09,700 --> 00:07:10,020
All right.
113

114
00:07:10,050 --> 00:07:11,890
So here, observe one thing that,
114

115
00:07:11,940 --> 00:07:16,440
so even if you drop this explicit casting the result will be same.
115

116
00:07:16,440 --> 00:07:19,940
But the thing is compiler will issue a warning.
116

117
00:07:19,950 --> 00:07:22,840
So but, this warning actually it is not dangerous,
117

118
00:07:22,950 --> 00:07:25,010
but that doesn't mean that you should ignore that.
118

119
00:07:25,020 --> 00:07:27,230
So, you should resolve that warning.
119

120
00:07:27,230 --> 00:07:30,830
But, so here compiler is just saying that there is a type mismatch,
120

121
00:07:31,050 --> 00:07:32,600
but there is no information loss here.
121

122
00:07:32,610 --> 00:07:32,960
Right?
122

123
00:07:32,970 --> 00:07:39,180
Because see, the size of this entity is 8 bytes, right?
123

124
00:07:39,270 --> 00:07:43,510
and the memory reserved for this variable is also 8 bytes.
124

125
00:07:43,710 --> 00:07:49,950
So, a 8 byte pointer is stored in 8 bytes of memory space.
125

126
00:07:49,950 --> 00:07:51,420
So, that is perfectly fine.
126

127
00:07:51,510 --> 00:07:53,420
So, there is no information loss.
127

128
00:07:53,630 --> 00:07:59,790
So, but it is always good to maintain same data types at the left hand side as well as at the right hand
128

129
00:07:59,790 --> 00:08:00,500
side.
129

130
00:08:00,510 --> 00:08:06,930
So, that's why, it is better to typecast explicitly. Just to make sure that the data types of LHS
130

131
00:08:06,930 --> 00:08:08,150
and RHS are same.
131

132
00:08:08,460 --> 00:08:13,560
So, with that note I would like to end this video here and in the next lecture let's understand more
132

133
00:08:13,560 --> 00:08:15,450
about pointer arithmetic.

video 7
