1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,380
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,380 --> 00:00:09,570
So, in this lecture let's understand Effect of using different pointer data types on pointer operations.
2

3
00:00:09,900 --> 00:00:13,070
Let's start.
3

4
00:00:13,130 --> 00:00:15,380
So  now let's understand this with an example.
4

5
00:00:15,590 --> 00:00:17,510
So here I have a project.
5

6
00:00:17,690 --> 00:00:20,140
The project name is pointer _2
6

7
00:00:20,210 --> 00:00:23,130
and here I have a small code snippet.
7

8
00:00:23,270 --> 00:00:32,150
And here I have a global variable called g_data and its type is long long int, and this is  initialized to
8

9
00:00:32,180 --> 00:00:33,280
this number.
9

10
00:00:33,440 --> 00:00:41,960
And here I have a pointer variable pAddress and its type is char*, and then it is initialized to address
10

11
00:00:42,040 --> 00:00:50,600
of this variable &g_data and I have type casted this to  char*. And after that,
11

12
00:00:50,600 --> 00:00:58,360
here I am printing the value of this pointer variable. I'm just printing the contents of this variable,
12

13
00:00:58,390 --> 00:01:01,100
so that is nothing but an address.
13

14
00:01:01,100 --> 00:01:01,430
Right?
14

15
00:01:01,460 --> 00:01:04,490
So, that's why, I have used %p here.
15

16
00:01:04,700 --> 00:01:09,500
Let's see what is the output of this. Output you cannot guess,
16

17
00:01:09,500 --> 00:01:13,060
because it is going to print the address of this variable, isn't it?
17

18
00:01:13,070 --> 00:01:14,440
Let's see what happens.
18

19
00:01:14,450 --> 00:01:18,700
So, we actually got the output. So, value of this variable
19

20
00:01:18,710 --> 00:01:21,710
is this one that is actually an address.
20

21
00:01:21,740 --> 00:01:22,070
Right?
21

22
00:01:22,070 --> 00:01:23,440
so address of this variable.
22

23
00:01:23,900 --> 00:01:26,430
So now let's print the value.So,
23

24
00:01:26,480 --> 00:01:30,280
let me print both address and value at address.
24

25
00:01:31,680 --> 00:01:34,170
So now let's run this code and let's see what happens. So now,
25

26
00:01:34,200 --> 00:01:36,080
Can you guess the output here?
26

27
00:01:36,120 --> 00:01:39,700
So now here we are  dereferencing this variable, isn't it?
27

28
00:01:39,720 --> 00:01:43,740
So, that means we are dereferencing this address, isn't it?
28

29
00:01:43,740 --> 00:01:50,580
So, that means we are going to fetch one byte of data or we are going to read one byte of data from this
29

30
00:01:50,610 --> 00:01:54,620
address and we are going to print that. Let's verify.
30

31
00:01:54,960 --> 00:01:56,780
So, that looks great.
31

32
00:01:56,910 --> 00:02:04,410
So now, what we do is, we are going to increment this pointer  variable content by 1.
32

33
00:02:04,470 --> 00:02:09,300
So for that, what I'm going to do is, this is is equal to plus 1.
33

34
00:02:09,300 --> 00:02:10,890
I'm going to do plus 1.
34

35
00:02:10,890 --> 00:02:11,250
All right.
35

36
00:02:11,270 --> 00:02:16,510
So, after that, I'm again going to execute these two statements.
36

37
00:02:16,590 --> 00:02:25,030
So now, can you guess the output?  So first we actually had the data, right? g_data a global variable and its
37

38
00:02:25,060 --> 00:02:26,430
address is this one.
38

39
00:02:26,430 --> 00:02:27,320
Right?
39

40
00:02:27,420 --> 00:02:31,470
And in that address we have this data, right?
40

41
00:02:31,860 --> 00:02:34,270
So, this is a data or value.So
41

42
00:02:34,320 --> 00:02:39,320
now first we  initialized pAddress is equal to address of  g_data.
42

43
00:02:39,570 --> 00:02:40,390
Correct?
43

44
00:02:40,410 --> 00:02:43,200
And after that we dereferenced this.
44

45
00:02:43,200 --> 00:02:47,610
So, that's why, we fetched one byte of data from this address.
45

46
00:02:47,640 --> 00:02:49,040
That happens to be 45.
46

47
00:02:49,380 --> 00:02:50,400
Correct?
47

48
00:02:50,400 --> 00:02:54,490
And now we increased the pointer content by 1.
48

49
00:02:54,520 --> 00:02:55,430
Right?
49

50
00:02:55,470 --> 00:02:59,100
The content of this pAddress is this address
50

51
00:02:59,100 --> 00:02:59,370
Right?
51

52
00:02:59,370 --> 00:03:00,080
11
52

53
00:03:00,270 --> 00:03:01,230
Correct?
53

54
00:03:01,230 --> 00:03:08,940
And now if I dereference this pointer or  this pointer variable, so I'm going to get this data, right?
54

55
00:03:09,020 --> 00:03:11,380
0x23.
55

56
00:03:11,610 --> 00:03:15,130
So, if I increment P address by 5,
56

57
00:03:15,210 --> 00:03:18,690
so pAddress value will be this address.
57

58
00:03:18,690 --> 00:03:19,680
Right?
58

59
00:03:19,680 --> 00:03:24,000
So, if I dereference that I will get this value, correct?
59

60
00:03:24,030 --> 00:03:25,860
Let's test this.
60

61
00:03:25,970 --> 00:03:27,380
I'm going to execute this.
61

62
00:03:33,020 --> 00:03:36,440
As expected, pAddress is incremented by 1.
62

63
00:03:36,470 --> 00:03:39,170
So, 10 becomes  11 here.
63

64
00:03:39,440 --> 00:03:42,550
And the value at that address is fetched,
64

65
00:03:42,620 --> 00:03:44,480
that happens to be 23.
65

66
00:03:44,570 --> 00:03:44,810
All right.
66

67
00:03:44,810 --> 00:03:47,330
So, now let me change this to +5.
67

68
00:03:47,960 --> 00:03:49,760
Let's see the output.
68

69
00:03:49,760 --> 00:03:53,280
So now here as expected the address is incremented by 5.
69

70
00:03:53,300 --> 00:03:54,330
Right?
70

71
00:03:54,410 --> 00:03:58,630
So,  1 2 3 4  5
71

72
00:03:58,670 --> 00:03:58,970
Right?
72

73
00:03:59,000 --> 00:04:02,290
So, now that's why the address is 3015 now.
73

74
00:04:02,450 --> 00:04:06,710
And the value one byte of data at that address is AB.
74

75
00:04:06,770 --> 00:04:07,070
Right?
75

76
00:04:12,140 --> 00:04:14,990
So, that's why, AB is getting printed here.
76

77
00:04:15,680 --> 00:04:23,580
So now these ff's are just to indicate that, that is actually a negative number. So now what we're going
77

78
00:04:23,580 --> 00:04:32,840
to do is, let's change the data type of this  pointer. So, I'm going to change this to short * and let
78

79
00:04:32,840 --> 00:04:34,320
me change here as well,
79

80
00:04:34,520 --> 00:04:36,440
and I'm going to do +1 here.
80

81
00:04:36,440 --> 00:04:41,310
Let's see how all these outputs look like and that's it.
81

82
00:04:41,330 --> 00:04:42,940
And now let me run this code.
82

83
00:04:46,490 --> 00:04:48,000
So, here is a interesting thing.
83

84
00:04:48,080 --> 00:04:50,950
First it actually printed this address.
84

85
00:04:50,960 --> 00:04:51,880
that's correct,
85

86
00:04:51,890 --> 00:05:00,340
and now it actually fetched  2 bytes of data, and that memory location then incremented by 2
86

87
00:05:00,440 --> 00:05:02,110
when you did +1.
87

88
00:05:02,120 --> 00:05:02,870
Right?
88

89
00:05:02,870 --> 00:05:07,550
So, when it was char*, the address incremented by 1.
89

90
00:05:07,610 --> 00:05:10,990
But here, it incremented by 2.
90

91
00:05:11,000 --> 00:05:16,500
So, that's why you see 3012 here, and then it fetch the next two bytes
91

92
00:05:16,550 --> 00:05:17,580
that is 11.
92

93
00:05:17,600 --> 00:05:20,490
So, first two three four five got fetched,
93

94
00:05:20,510 --> 00:05:23,870
and after that the address is incremented by two,
94

95
00:05:23,870 --> 00:05:26,000
after that the next two bytes are fetched.
95

96
00:05:26,810 --> 00:05:28,430
So, I hope this is clear to you.
96

97
00:05:28,430 --> 00:05:33,680
So, that's how, this pointer data type affects the pointer arithmetic.
97

98
00:05:34,430 --> 00:05:37,600
So, this is what we call as pointer arithmetic, right?
98

99
00:05:37,610 --> 00:05:41,280
So, you are doing some arithmetic operation on the pointer, right?
99

100
00:05:41,300 --> 00:05:48,590
So, when the pointer variable is  of type short * doing  +1 actually incremented the pointer value
100

101
00:05:48,680 --> 00:05:50,680
by two.
101

102
00:05:50,680 --> 00:05:51,010
All right.
102

103
00:05:51,010 --> 00:05:53,230
So now let me change this to int *.
103

104
00:05:53,770 --> 00:05:55,750
Let's see what happens.
104

105
00:05:55,780 --> 00:06:02,360
So, I am going to run this code now. So, here is a output.
105

106
00:06:02,380 --> 00:06:09,200
First it printed the base address 4030, and when you dereferenced that pointer variable
106

107
00:06:09,250 --> 00:06:13,810
it fetched 4 bytes of data from that memory location.
107

108
00:06:13,810 --> 00:06:18,130
So, that's why, it actually printed the first  4 bytes.
108

109
00:06:18,430 --> 00:06:25,030
And after that, so when you increment the pointer by 1, the value actually increments by 4
109

110
00:06:25,030 --> 00:06:29,050
now. That's why, it is now showing 3014.
110

111
00:06:29,050 --> 00:06:29,410
Right?
111

112
00:06:29,410 --> 00:06:33,670
And from there it again fetched another four bytes.
112

113
00:06:33,670 --> 00:06:39,030
That means so all these bytes. So, the pointer type is now int *.
113

114
00:06:39,070 --> 00:06:42,750
So, first you initialized pAddress to this address.
114

115
00:06:43,090 --> 00:06:44,980
And when you do  +1,
115

116
00:06:44,980 --> 00:06:48,340
so the address will be incremented by 4.
116

117
00:06:48,340 --> 00:06:50,470
That means size of an int.
117

118
00:06:50,680 --> 00:06:53,860
So plus one, two, three, four.
118

119
00:06:53,860 --> 00:06:58,040
So, now this is the value of the pAddress.
119

120
00:06:58,150 --> 00:07:00,760
And from there 4 bytes will be fetched,
120

121
00:07:00,850 --> 00:07:02,980
when you dereference that pointer.
121

122
00:07:03,670 --> 00:07:09,230
So, when the pointer is of type int *, the pointer moves with the offset of 4 bytes.
122

123
00:07:09,250 --> 00:07:09,520
Right?
123

124
00:07:09,520 --> 00:07:10,950
That is size of an int.
124

125
00:07:10,960 --> 00:07:12,130
So, that means  +1.
125

126
00:07:12,130 --> 00:07:16,960
This is +1, +2, +3, +4 like that.
126

127
00:07:17,230 --> 00:07:24,600
And when the pointer variable is of type long long * then the offset will be 8,
127

128
00:07:24,610 --> 00:07:27,280
so this is actually +1,
128

129
00:07:27,520 --> 00:07:29,380
this is +2,
129

130
00:07:29,470 --> 00:07:33,980
this is +3. So, offset is actually 8 bytes.
130

131
00:07:34,090 --> 00:07:35,770
So, hope this is clear to you.
131

132
00:07:35,770 --> 00:07:42,000
So now a pointer arithmetic and pointers are highly used in  embedded system programming.
132

133
00:07:42,000 --> 00:07:47,770
So, pointers are very much important in embedded system programming. Because, you can use pointers to
133

134
00:07:47,770 --> 00:07:51,350
store data into required SRAM locations.
134

135
00:07:51,590 --> 00:07:58,830
So, pointers are heavily used for copying data from  Peripheral register to SRAM memory and vice versa.
135

136
00:07:59,050 --> 00:08:05,780
Because, in microcontroller we actually talk to the  peripheral registers by using its address.
136

137
00:08:05,800 --> 00:08:10,370
So, if you want to put some data into the peripheral or if you want to configure the  peripheral.
137

138
00:08:10,580 --> 00:08:12,570
So, you have to use it's address.
138

139
00:08:12,750 --> 00:08:20,020
So, all these things you'll see when we do exercise on the embedded board. So but, all these pointers concept
139

140
00:08:20,020 --> 00:08:25,840
and pointer arithmetics and the type casting what we just saw in the previous lectures. So, all will
140

141
00:08:25,840 --> 00:08:32,470
be used while doing exercises for the embedded board or while writing code for the microcontroller.
141

142
00:08:32,650 --> 00:08:36,360
Pointers are also heavily used to configure the  peripheral registers.
142

143
00:08:36,460 --> 00:08:42,790
Because peripheral registers are memory-mapped and each register will be given unique address in the
143

144
00:08:42,880 --> 00:08:44,230
MCU memory map.
144

145
00:08:44,770 --> 00:08:51,880
So, about the memory map and all.So,will see later. So, just you have to know this a peripheral registers are
145

146
00:08:51,880 --> 00:08:59,170
actually part of the memory map of the microcontroller. A unique address will be given by the microcontroller
146

147
00:08:59,170 --> 00:09:04,710
vendor to configure all those peripherals and will explore
147

148
00:09:04,720 --> 00:09:06,030
more on this later.
148

149
00:09:06,100 --> 00:09:13,540
And after that pointers  to ISRs are stored in a vector table to handle the interrupts, and pointers are
149

150
00:09:13,540 --> 00:09:20,610
also used to configure the memory-mapped processor specific registers like interrupt configuration registers
150

151
00:09:20,620 --> 00:09:21,010
and all.
151

152
00:09:21,430 --> 00:09:26,080
So, that means that pointers are very much important in  embedded system programming
152

153
00:09:26,080 --> 00:09:32,680
and as we make a progress we will explore more. And in the next lecture I am going to explain you one
153

154
00:09:32,680 --> 00:09:40,450
important topic, that is we are going to understand about the importance of a standard library header
154

155
00:09:40,450 --> 00:09:43,500
file called stdint.h.


==================================================================================


section 14---importance of stdint.h

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,100 --> 00:00:01,320
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:05,200
So, in this lecture let's learn about importance of stdint.h.
2

3
00:00:05,750 --> 00:00:13,560
So, stdint.h is one of the standard library header file. Let's say, you have written a 'C' program
3

4
00:00:13,610 --> 00:00:19,680
keeping in mind certain data types and its associated sizes.
4

5
00:00:19,780 --> 00:00:22,910
Now, let's say tomorrow you change the compiler.
5

6
00:00:23,050 --> 00:00:28,000
So, when you change the compiler the size of the data type may differ.
6

7
00:00:28,030 --> 00:00:28,940
Right?
7

8
00:00:28,990 --> 00:00:36,740
So, the code may compile fine, but it may be buggy, because the new compiler uses different sizes for the
8

9
00:00:36,740 --> 00:00:39,310
data types you have used in the program.
9

10
00:00:39,700 --> 00:00:45,070
So, that means you will face portability issues with your code, right?
10

11
00:00:45,070 --> 00:00:47,840
So, there will not be a problem when you use your compiler,
11

12
00:00:47,920 --> 00:00:50,720
but the same code is compiled using another compiler,
12

13
00:00:50,740 --> 00:00:55,690
there's a possibility that code may become buggy and it will cause problems.
13

14
00:00:55,690 --> 00:01:00,370
So, when you write a 'C' program you should a give attention to all these details. 
14

15
00:01:00,880 --> 00:01:09,730
So, there is a possibility of a portability issue in your 'C' code a due to the selection of data types.
15

16
00:01:09,760 --> 00:01:17,770
So, let's discuss portability issues in 'C' programming code due to size up data types. So, in 'C' programming
16

17
00:01:17,800 --> 00:01:24,190
language the most commonly used data types "int" and "long" cause portability issues.
17

18
00:01:24,190 --> 00:01:31,590
The reason is that the storage size for 'int', 'long' type variable is not defined within the 'C' standard.
18

19
00:01:31,630 --> 00:01:34,480
It all depends on the compiler.
19

20
00:01:34,630 --> 00:01:41,290
So, the compiler designers are the compiler vendors have the choice to define the storage size for the
20

21
00:01:41,290 --> 00:01:47,840
variable depending solely on hardware capabilities of the target platform,
21

22
00:01:47,920 --> 00:01:52,050
with respect to the minimum widths defined by the standard.
22

23
00:01:52,080 --> 00:01:55,990
So, the standard only talks about minimum and maximum values.
23

24
00:01:56,140 --> 00:02:01,430
So, it doesn't talk about fixing a certain amount of size for 'int' or 'long'.
24

25
00:02:02,140 --> 00:02:11,050
So, for example in XC8 compiler, why would XC8 compiler designers have selected 2 byte for an int data
25

26
00:02:11,140 --> 00:02:18,010
type.  The reason maybe according to the compiler designer, keeping the size of the "int" type variable as
26

27
00:02:18,010 --> 00:02:23,860
2 bytes will be the most efficient for data manipulation considering the underlying architecture of
27

28
00:02:23,860 --> 00:02:30,420
the PIC 8-bit microcontrollers. So, that maybe the reason for that and for ARM it may not be true.
28

29
00:02:30,430 --> 00:02:37,060
So, that's why, ARM compiler designers have selected "int" as 4 bytes. Because, that would give more speed
29

30
00:02:37,060 --> 00:02:39,320
and code size benefits.
30

31
00:02:39,340 --> 00:02:43,710
So now, let's take a look into this code snippet. Here,
31

32
00:02:43,720 --> 00:02:48,660
there is a variable called count and it's data type is unsigned int.
32

33
00:02:48,820 --> 00:02:55,860
And here, there is one conditional statement. If count is greater than this value,
33

34
00:02:55,870 --> 00:03:03,380
so this task will be done. So, if you just check this code, so this code will cause portability issue. So,
34

35
00:03:03,380 --> 00:03:04,900
when you change the compiler.
35

36
00:03:05,120 --> 00:03:07,940
So here, let's say I use one compiler
36

37
00:03:07,940 --> 00:03:14,000
I would call it as compiler A, and compiler treats this variable as 4 bytes.
37

38
00:03:14,000 --> 00:03:19,100
So, when this variable is of 4 bytes, then this code will work fine, no problem.
38

39
00:03:19,160 --> 00:03:26,540
And when I use this code under compiler B, and let's say if compiler B treats unsigned int as 2 bytes,
39

40
00:03:26,690 --> 00:03:29,680
then so this task will never be executed.
40

41
00:03:29,720 --> 00:03:30,530
Right?
41

42
00:03:30,710 --> 00:03:35,530
Because, the maximum value for count is 65,535
42

43
00:03:35,660 --> 00:03:41,510
and if you further increment this what happens, so the value actually rolls back to 0.
43

44
00:03:41,510 --> 00:03:41,840
Right?
44

45
00:03:41,840 --> 00:03:43,160
and it starts over again.
45

46
00:03:43,760 --> 00:03:50,360
So, that's why, this code will become buggy on compiler B if it uses 2 bytes for the unsigned int.
46

47
00:03:50,390 --> 00:03:58,340
So, in order to get rid of these kinds of problems, you have to stop using these standard data type names.
47

48
00:03:58,490 --> 00:04:06,290
So, instead use the data type alias names given by the header file stdint.h.
48

49
00:04:06,380 --> 00:04:14,660
So, the standard library header file stdint.h defines fixed-width  integers using alias data types
49

50
00:04:14,770 --> 00:04:23,240
for the standard data types available in 'C'. A fixed-width integer data type is an aliased data type that
50

51
00:04:23,240 --> 00:04:27,770
is based on the exact number of bits required to store the data.
51

52
00:04:28,670 --> 00:04:34,460
So, for example, here this is a alias name for 8
52

53
00:04:34,480 --> 00:04:36,350
bits signed data type.
53

54
00:04:36,890 --> 00:04:42,970
So, what is actually 8 bits signed data type in 'C'? It is signed char, right? or char.
54

55
00:04:43,100 --> 00:04:47,000
So, instead of using char use int8_t.
55

56
00:04:47,090 --> 00:04:51,410
So, this is actually an aliased name for the standard data type.
56

57
00:04:52,070 --> 00:04:57,630
So, instead of using unsigned char use uint8_t.
57

58
00:04:57,860 --> 00:05:00,670
So, where 'u' stands for unsigned.
58

59
00:05:01,010 --> 00:05:08,180
So, all these are aliased data type names for the standard data types managed by the stdint.h. So,
59

60
00:05:08,180 --> 00:05:11,010
how it is managed, so you need not to worry about that.
60

61
00:05:11,510 --> 00:05:14,430
So, that will be resolved by the stdint.h.
61

62
00:05:14,480 --> 00:05:19,580
So, if you want you can open and check the stdint.h file.
62

63
00:05:19,700 --> 00:05:27,290
So, if I use uint32_t instead of unsigned int. Here, doesn't matter under which compiler
63

64
00:05:27,320 --> 00:05:35,840
this code compiles, the compiler will always reserve 32 bits for the variable by using suitable standard
64

65
00:05:35,840 --> 00:05:38,830
data type. So, that is guaranteed.
65

66
00:05:39,020 --> 00:05:43,910
So, that's why, you need not to worry about portability issue. Alright.
66

67
00:05:43,930 --> 00:05:49,550
So, now if you want to locate stdint.h or any standard library header file, so you have to
67

68
00:05:49,610 --> 00:05:57,530
open the toolchain installation folder. For example, in my case I have a Windows 64 bit machine and if
68

69
00:05:57,530 --> 00:06:04,270
I want to compile a 'C' program for my PC, actually I'm using a MinGW 64 bit toolchain.
69

70
00:06:04,400 --> 00:06:12,380
So, that is actually installed in MinGW folder, so if I go there, I will find include,
70

71
00:06:12,560 --> 00:06:21,650
so all these are standard library include files and if I search for stdint.h, so it is right here.
71

72
00:06:21,650 --> 00:06:22,910
So, let me open that.
72

73
00:06:25,680 --> 00:06:27,340
So, here it is.
73

74
00:06:27,390 --> 00:06:34,760
So, this is a stdint.h and these are the aliased names, which you'll be using in your program.
74

75
00:06:34,770 --> 00:06:40,020
So, these are aliased to standard data type names.
75

76
00:06:40,110 --> 00:06:47,510
So now, how it is aliased is compiler dependent, different compilers issue different stdint.h.
76

77
00:06:47,550 --> 00:06:54,600
So, the stdint.h contents will not be same across different compilers. And bin folder usually
77

78
00:06:54,600 --> 00:07:02,650
contains all the binaries which are used during you are code compilation. Let's check the cross compilation
78

79
00:07:02,650 --> 00:07:03,630
toolchain.
79

80
00:07:03,640 --> 00:07:09,120
So, now let me check the cross compilation toolchain for PIC microcontrollers.
80

81
00:07:09,120 --> 00:07:10,540
So, that also I have installed
81

82
00:07:10,600 --> 00:07:12,570
you can just watch here.
82

83
00:07:12,640 --> 00:07:19,020
So, for example, I have a microchips XC8 and XC32 compilers.
83

84
00:07:19,090 --> 00:07:25,060
These are actually used for code generation for PIC8 and for PIC32 microcontrollers,
84

85
00:07:25,060 --> 00:07:28,060
and this is the XC8 compiler.
85

86
00:07:28,150 --> 00:07:32,920
And if you go inside that and if you go inside bin.
86

87
00:07:32,950 --> 00:07:36,100
So, actually this is a compiler XC8.
87

88
00:07:36,220 --> 00:07:40,060
And here you will find a folder called pic
88

89
00:07:40,090 --> 00:07:41,290
and here include
89

90
00:07:41,320 --> 00:07:44,030
and here you will find different standards.So,
90

91
00:07:44,050 --> 00:07:45,750
let's go inside C99
91

92
00:07:45,760 --> 00:07:49,690
and here you will find all the standard library header files,
92

93
00:07:49,780 --> 00:07:50,920
so stdint.h.
93

94
00:07:50,950 --> 00:07:51,550
So, here it is.
94

95
00:07:52,000 --> 00:07:57,580
So, you can see that, the content of this file is different compared to the earlier one which we just
95

96
00:07:57,580 --> 00:07:58,330
saw this one.
96

97
00:07:58,960 --> 00:08:02,730
So, this is for MinGW, this is for XC8,
97

98
00:08:03,130 --> 00:08:03,900
so here
98

99
00:08:04,000 --> 00:08:09,370
the implementation will be different. But you still get the same aliased names like int8,
99

100
00:08:09,430 --> 00:08:13,170
int16, int32, uint8 etc..
100

101
00:08:13,660 --> 00:08:21,100
Let's check the cross toolchain for our STM32 microcontroller which is based on ARM processor, so that
101

102
00:08:21,100 --> 00:08:26,980
is installed here in ST folder, cubeIDE,
102

103
00:08:27,100 --> 00:08:28,720
you can go to plugins.
103

104
00:08:28,780 --> 00:08:38,750
So here, let's open gnu-arm-embedded toolchain here and here go to tools and let's go to arm-none-eabi,
104

105
00:08:38,810 --> 00:08:41,680
and here you can go to include,
105

106
00:08:41,890 --> 00:08:46,360
and here you will find all the standard library header files,
106

107
00:08:46,660 --> 00:08:53,440
and here you can find the stdint.h. So, now the implementation maybe different, but as i said
107

108
00:08:53,560 --> 00:08:56,530
you will still see various alias names.
108

109
00:08:57,190 --> 00:09:00,610
So, I would like to introduce some of the useful stdint.h  alias names
109

110
00:09:00,610 --> 00:09:06,800
apart from what I mentioned before like int8, uint8, int32, uint32 etc..
110

111
00:09:06,850 --> 00:09:09,480
So, if you use uintmax_t.
111

112
00:09:09,580 --> 00:09:14,000
This actually defines the largest fixed-width unsigned integer possible on the system.
112

113
00:09:14,440 --> 00:09:21,420
So, you need not to worry whether it is 16 or 32 or 64 bits, so just use uintmax_t.
113

114
00:09:21,610 --> 00:09:27,760
So, if you use intmax_t this defines the largest fixed-width signed integer possible on the
114

115
00:09:27,760 --> 00:09:28,470
system.
115

116
00:09:28,630 --> 00:09:37,300
And if you use uintptr_t, then this actually defines an unsigned integer type that is wide
116

117
00:09:37,300 --> 00:09:44,360
enough to store the value of a pointer. For example, for our 64 bit machine the size of the pointer is
117

118
00:09:44,390 --> 00:09:46,830
64 bits, right? 8 bytes.
118

119
00:09:46,840 --> 00:09:47,970
Right?
119

120
00:09:47,980 --> 00:09:54,210
So, when you are coding for some unknown architecture like PIC architecture or ARM architecture, so you need not
120

121
00:09:54,210 --> 00:10:00,020
to worry what is the size of the pointer, so you just use uintptr_t.
121

122
00:10:00,280 --> 00:10:06,370
So, the stdint.h will take care of mapping that to appropriate pointer data type.
122

123
00:10:06,510 --> 00:10:13,990
So, if you are unsure about the size of the pointer, then just use uintptr_t, which defines
123

124
00:10:14,110 --> 00:10:19,540
unsigned integer type that is wide enough to store the value of a pointer.
124

125
00:10:20,110 --> 00:10:25,750
So, if you are confused, don't worry when we start using all these things in the program then it will
125

126
00:10:25,960 --> 00:10:28,900
become obvious and you will start appreciating that.
126

127
00:10:29,380 --> 00:10:33,880
So, with that note I would like to end the discussion on stdint.h,
127

128
00:10:33,880 --> 00:10:41,230
So, from this lecture onwards we will be using stdint.h header file and its various alias names,
128

129
00:10:41,710 --> 00:10:46,740
and in the next lecture I will cover operators in 'C'. I'll see you in the next lecture.

==================================================================================


section 15---operators

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:07,020
From this lecture onwards let's understand about operators in 'C'. So, there are many operators in
1

2
00:00:07,020 --> 00:00:07,630
'C'.
2

3
00:00:07,710 --> 00:00:15,300
So, what is an operator? An operator is a symbol that actually tells the compiler to perform a certain
3

4
00:00:15,300 --> 00:00:19,800
mathematical or logical manipulation on the operands.
4

5
00:00:20,130 --> 00:00:28,020
So, in 'C' we have so many operators. For example increment and decrement operator, arithmetic operators,
5

6
00:00:28,110 --> 00:00:36,870
relational operators, logical operators, bitwise operators, assignment operators, and conditional operators.
6

7
00:00:37,100 --> 00:00:41,240
Alright. So, some operators require only one operand.
7

8
00:00:41,550 --> 00:00:43,710
So, those are called as unary operators.
8

9
00:00:43,740 --> 00:00:51,180
So, some operators require 2 operands, those are called as binary operators and there is one more
9

10
00:00:51,180 --> 00:00:56,790
operator called conditional operator, that actually require 3 operands.
10

11
00:00:56,820 --> 00:01:00,610
So, that's why, it is called as ternary operator.
11

12
00:01:00,740 --> 00:01:02,820
All right. Now let's move forward.
12

13
00:01:02,820 --> 00:01:07,280
So, now first let's explore about the very famous arithmetic operators.
13

14
00:01:07,470 --> 00:01:14,760
So, here you can see that these are actually the arithmetic operators. The '%' sign that is called as
14

15
00:01:14,940 --> 00:01:17,720
arithmetic modulus operator.
15

16
00:01:17,850 --> 00:01:24,510
I know you are familiar with these operators of arithmetic operators, but this may be new to you.
16

17
00:01:24,510 --> 00:01:29,640
So, the modulus operation actually produces the remainder from division.
17

18
00:01:29,640 --> 00:01:33,780
For example, let's consider 14 modulus 4.
18

19
00:01:34,140 --> 00:01:36,180
So, this evaluates to 2.
19

20
00:01:36,610 --> 00:01:43,590
So because, when you divide 14 by 4 that actually yields a reminder of 2.
20

21
00:01:43,620 --> 00:01:46,140
So, that's how the modulus operator behaves.
21

22
00:01:46,140 --> 00:01:46,430
All right.
22

23
00:01:46,460 --> 00:01:51,390
So here, I have a variable and that variable is initialize to this expression.
23

24
00:01:51,400 --> 00:01:57,250
So now, what is the value which is stored in the variable value ? Is it 20 or 14?
24

25
00:01:57,300 --> 00:02:02,760
So, now if you want to understand how exactly this expression is evaluated, then you have to think from
25

26
00:02:02,760 --> 00:02:09,300
the perspective of the compiler. So, you should understand how compiler thinks about this expression in
26

27
00:02:09,300 --> 00:02:16,470
order to evaluate that. For that, something called operator precedence rule is defined by the standard
27

28
00:02:16,470 --> 00:02:17,470
for the compilers.
28

29
00:02:17,760 --> 00:02:21,910
So, all the compilers should follow this precedence rule.
29

30
00:02:21,960 --> 00:02:25,090
So, that's what we call as operator precedence rule.
30

31
00:02:25,290 --> 00:02:31,680
So, all the compilers which a compile the 'C' program should adhere to this precedence rule. Operator
31

32
00:02:31,680 --> 00:02:36,290
precedence rules determine which mathematical operation takes place first.
32

33
00:02:36,390 --> 00:02:39,870
That is takes precedence over others.
33

34
00:02:39,870 --> 00:02:47,730
So, remember that, parentheses may be used to force an expression to higher precedence. All right. Now let's
34

35
00:02:47,730 --> 00:02:53,080
move forward, and let's take a look into the precedence of operators.
35

36
00:02:53,100 --> 00:03:00,390
So, now this table actually tells you the precedence of operators, so lower that number higher the precedent.
36

37
00:03:00,720 --> 00:03:06,250
So, this image actually took from cppreference.com and don't try to memorize this table
37

38
00:03:06,330 --> 00:03:07,810
that is not required.
38

39
00:03:08,010 --> 00:03:15,990
So, use parenthesis if you want to promote the precedence of any operator of your choice in an expression. 
39

40
00:03:16,600 --> 00:03:19,150
Let's see that how in the next lecture.
40

41
00:03:19,140 --> 00:03:23,880
Let's take an example, let's consider the + operator.
41

42
00:03:23,880 --> 00:03:26,440
So, this is actually an addition operator.
42

43
00:03:26,490 --> 00:03:27,120
Right?
43

44
00:03:27,160 --> 00:03:29,510
It's precedence is 4.
44

45
00:03:29,730 --> 00:03:31,570
And if you take division,
45

46
00:03:31,720 --> 00:03:36,920
so this is actually the division operator and it's precedence is 3.
46

47
00:03:36,930 --> 00:03:45,780
So, that means in an expression of addition and division, division has the higher precedence over addition.
47

48
00:03:46,320 --> 00:03:49,560
So, just remember that lower the value higher the precedence.
48

49
00:03:49,710 --> 00:03:54,330
You can see that the multiplication and division both have the same precedence
49

50
00:03:54,330 --> 00:04:00,570
value. After that, there is something called associativity so which actually defines how exactly the expression
50

51
00:04:00,570 --> 00:04:05,930
should be evaluated, either from left to right or right to left. And associativity is used
51

52
00:04:05,940 --> 00:04:10,050
so only when we encounter 2 operators of same precedence.
52

53
00:04:10,200 --> 00:04:12,810
So, will explore about associativity later.
53

54
00:04:12,810 --> 00:04:14,230
Let's move forward. So, now
54

55
00:04:14,250 --> 00:04:15,450
let's see some examples.
55

56
00:04:15,820 --> 00:04:17,870
Let's consider this expression
56

57
00:04:17,880 --> 00:04:19,500
2 plus 3 multiplied by 4.
57

58
00:04:20,070 --> 00:04:23,480
So, this actually evaluates to 14, not to 20.
58

59
00:04:24,000 --> 00:04:26,220
Let's understand that how.
59

60
00:04:26,250 --> 00:04:30,810
Now, let's take that expression first 2+3*4.
60

61
00:04:30,810 --> 00:04:37,230
So now here, multiplication operator has higher precedence level than the addition operator.
61

62
00:04:37,230 --> 00:04:38,110
Right?
62

63
00:04:38,130 --> 00:04:41,490
So, that's why, this is evaluated first.
63

64
00:04:41,580 --> 00:04:46,650
So, that's why, you get 12 here and 2+12 will be done later.
64

65
00:04:46,650 --> 00:04:48,530
So, that's why, the result would be 14.
65

66
00:04:48,540 --> 00:04:54,560
There are two operators plus and multiplication, and multiplication operator has higher precedence.
66

67
00:04:54,840 --> 00:04:57,730
So, that's why, it's operands are considered first.
67

68
00:04:57,810 --> 00:04:58,620
Very simple, right?
68

69
00:04:58,710 --> 00:05:01,900
Now let's proceed to next expression.
69

70
00:05:01,920 --> 00:05:08,790
So, this is a expression 2 + 3 and this expression is inside the parentheses and multiplied 
70

71
00:05:08,790 --> 00:05:09,650
by 4.
71

72
00:05:09,720 --> 00:05:14,590
If you just check this table parentheses has higher precedence.
72

73
00:05:14,760 --> 00:05:15,300
Right?
73

74
00:05:15,300 --> 00:05:16,780
It's precedence is 1.
74

75
00:05:16,800 --> 00:05:17,900
Right?
75

76
00:05:17,910 --> 00:05:23,150
So, than addition or multiplication, so parentheses has higher precedence.
76

77
00:05:23,160 --> 00:05:24,210
Right?
77

78
00:05:24,240 --> 00:05:28,340
So, that's why, parentheses will be evaluated first.
78

79
00:05:28,350 --> 00:05:32,560
So, what our there inside the parentheses will be valued at first?
79

80
00:05:32,580 --> 00:05:40,110
So, that's why, if you want to promote the precedence of addition over this operator's precedence, then
80

81
00:05:40,110 --> 00:05:41,120
use parentheses.
81

82
00:05:41,130 --> 00:05:45,570
So, here you are promoting the precedence of the addition operator.
82

83
00:05:45,570 --> 00:05:46,480
Right?
83

84
00:05:46,500 --> 00:05:49,170
So, that's why, 2 + 3 will be evaluated first.
84

85
00:05:49,350 --> 00:05:50,250
So, that is 5
85

86
00:05:50,280 --> 00:05:53,050
and after that this is carried out 20.
86

87
00:05:53,430 --> 00:05:54,360
Let's move forward.
87

88
00:05:54,450 --> 00:05:55,980
Let's consider this expression.
88

89
00:05:56,010 --> 00:05:57,900
So, what do you think 2 or 50?
89

90
00:05:57,900 --> 00:05:59,700
Let's see that.
90

91
00:05:59,700 --> 00:06:02,220
So, first of all there are 2 operators, right?
91

92
00:06:02,310 --> 00:06:04,000
Multiplication and division.
92

93
00:06:04,470 --> 00:06:10,920
So, both multiplication and division they have the same precedence level.
93

94
00:06:10,930 --> 00:06:11,420
Right?
94

95
00:06:11,430 --> 00:06:12,180
That is 3.
95

96
00:06:12,180 --> 00:06:14,780
So, that means there is a conflict, right?
96

97
00:06:14,820 --> 00:06:18,740
If you want to solve this conflict you have to use associativity.
97

98
00:06:19,080 --> 00:06:25,830
So, associativity comes into picture only when there is a conflict between 2 operators of having same
98

99
00:06:25,830 --> 00:06:27,480
precedence level.
99

100
00:06:27,480 --> 00:06:31,920
So, here the multiplication and division have the same precedence level,
100

101
00:06:31,950 --> 00:06:34,550
so you have to consider the associativity now.
101

102
00:06:34,660 --> 00:06:38,060
So, the associativity is from left to right.
102

103
00:06:38,070 --> 00:06:45,330
So, that's why, in order to evaluate this expression compiler starts from left, and when it starts from
103

104
00:06:45,330 --> 00:06:49,230
left it first encounters multiplication operation.
104

105
00:06:49,470 --> 00:06:54,020
So, that's why, it actually considers this operands first.
105

106
00:06:54,120 --> 00:06:55,580
That's why, you got 20.
106

107
00:06:55,860 --> 00:07:00,100
And after that again there are 2 operators of same precedence level,
107

108
00:07:00,150 --> 00:07:04,760
so again left to right compiler encounters this first.
108

109
00:07:04,800 --> 00:07:10,560
So, that's why, these operands are considered. So, the result will be 10
109

110
00:07:10,620 --> 00:07:16,800
and after that 10 * 5, which is 50. So, associativity is used to evaluate the expression
110

111
00:07:16,830 --> 00:07:24,060
when there are two or more operators of the same precedence is present in an expression. Let's move forward.
111

112
00:07:25,220 --> 00:07:27,890
Alright. So, now let's consider this expression.
112

113
00:07:27,890 --> 00:07:33,290
So, this expression looks little complicated, but you can apply the precedence rules and you can solve
113

114
00:07:33,290 --> 00:07:34,340
this equation.
114

115
00:07:34,340 --> 00:07:40,760
But remember that never ever think of remembering precedence level of different operators.
115

116
00:07:40,760 --> 00:07:42,730
So, that is absolutely not required.
116

117
00:07:42,920 --> 00:07:46,160
So, don't try to memorize the operators precedence level.
117

118
00:07:46,160 --> 00:07:52,790
So, in an expression if you want to promote the precedence of an operator of your choice, then don't hesitate
118

119
00:07:52,790 --> 00:07:54,400
to use parentheses.
119

120
00:07:54,470 --> 00:07:58,660
So, use parentheses generously in your expression.
120

121
00:07:58,670 --> 00:08:02,450
Let's see, how we can evaluate this expression using precedence rule.
121

122
00:08:02,510 --> 00:08:07,490
First of all we have a couple of operators here, so + and -.
122

123
00:08:07,490 --> 00:08:12,170
So, this is for division and this is a less than symbol, right?
123

124
00:08:12,200 --> 00:08:19,820
And if you take a look into the precedence chart, so division has higher precedence, right? than addition
124

125
00:08:19,820 --> 00:08:21,100
and subtraction.
125

126
00:08:21,410 --> 00:08:27,740
And after that, here we can see that the less than and greater than symbols they have the lower precedence.
126

127
00:08:27,740 --> 00:08:28,640
Right?
127

128
00:08:28,640 --> 00:08:31,460
So, that's why, division is done first here.
128

129
00:08:31,460 --> 00:08:33,660
So, that's why, these operands are considered.
129

130
00:08:33,710 --> 00:08:40,250
So, that actually gives you this expression, right? Here we have now plus, minus.
130

131
00:08:40,250 --> 00:08:40,950
Right?
131

132
00:08:40,970 --> 00:08:43,100
So, there is a conflict.
132

133
00:08:43,100 --> 00:08:43,730
Right?
133

134
00:08:43,730 --> 00:08:45,290
So, whenever there is a conflict,
134

135
00:08:45,380 --> 00:08:51,910
so the conflict is whether to do 3 - 2 first or 2 + 3 
first.  
135

136
00:08:51,950 --> 00:08:52,660
Right?
136

137
00:08:52,700 --> 00:08:55,950
So, that's a conflict. In order to solve this conflict
137

138
00:08:55,970 --> 00:09:01,840
check the associativity of addition and subtraction, that is left right.
138

139
00:09:01,840 --> 00:09:05,980
So, that's why, compiler evaluates this expression from left to right.
139

140
00:09:05,990 --> 00:09:08,690
So, that's why, it encounters addition first.
140

141
00:09:08,690 --> 00:09:10,920
So, that's why, addition is done first.
141

142
00:09:10,970 --> 00:09:16,020
And again there is a conflict here, between this operation and this operation.
142

143
00:09:16,070 --> 00:09:16,810
Right?
143

144
00:09:16,850 --> 00:09:18,590
Again use left to right
144

145
00:09:18,590 --> 00:09:19,670
associativity,
145

146
00:09:19,730 --> 00:09:24,240
so compiler actually considers these operands first.
146

147
00:09:24,290 --> 00:09:26,340
So, that's why, you get 13 here.
147

148
00:09:26,360 --> 00:09:30,290
So now, in this expression addition has the highest precedence.
148

149
00:09:30,300 --> 00:09:32,510
So, 3 + 1 will be done,
149

150
00:09:32,510 --> 00:09:33,240
that is 4.
150

151
00:09:33,650 --> 00:09:41,730
After that, this is the final expression that is 13 < 4, which is actually a relational statement.
151

152
00:09:41,750 --> 00:09:45,110
So, 13 - 4 evaluates to 0.
152

153
00:09:45,140 --> 00:09:46,550
That is false.
153

154
00:09:46,610 --> 00:09:48,700
So hence, the result is 0 here.
154

155
00:09:48,800 --> 00:09:51,500
So, about relational operations we will see later.
155

156
00:09:51,590 --> 00:09:53,090
Let's move forward.
156

157
00:09:53,090 --> 00:09:56,810
Remember that all arithmetic operators are binary operators
157

158
00:09:56,840 --> 00:10:00,920
that is they need at least 2 operands to operate.
158

159
00:10:00,920 --> 00:10:05,600
So, in the next lecture let's understand about unary operators in 'C'.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,120 --> 00:00:01,780
Hey, welcome back to the lecture
1

2
00:00:01,800 --> 00:00:05,490
and in this lecture let's explore about unary operators in 'C'.
2

3
00:00:05,520 --> 00:00:09,010
So, there are couple of unary operators in 'C' and in this lecture
3

4
00:00:09,010 --> 00:00:11,880
let's discuss about unary increment operator.
4

5
00:00:12,120 --> 00:00:17,570
So, this is a symbol for unary increment operator and unary decrement operator.
5

6
00:00:17,580 --> 00:00:23,250
So, this is a symbol for unary decrement operator. These operators need only one operand.
6

7
00:00:23,400 --> 00:00:27,630
So, that's why, these operators are called as unary operators.
7

8
00:00:27,630 --> 00:00:29,170
Consider this expression.
8

9
00:00:29,190 --> 00:00:39,510
So here, 'x' is a variable and this is actually an expression of incrementing the value of x by 1.
9

10
00:00:39,510 --> 00:00:49,120
So now, this is an expression of decrementing the value of x by 1 using increment and decrement operators.
10

11
00:00:49,140 --> 00:00:54,000
So suppose, if I write something like this x = x + 1
11

12
00:00:54,000 --> 00:00:56,310
so this is also increment operation, right? 
12

13
00:00:56,310 --> 00:01:02,420
So, but here I used addition operator, that is actually arithmetic operator, right?
13

14
00:01:02,460 --> 00:01:08,940
So now, the equivalent of this expression using unary operator would be x++.
14

15
00:01:09,070 --> 00:01:09,570
Right?
15

16
00:01:09,600 --> 00:01:10,590
So, both are same.
16

17
00:01:10,590 --> 00:01:14,620
So, in both cases x value will be incremented by 1.
17

18
00:01:14,630 --> 00:01:20,690
So, but this is done using arithmetic operator and this is done using unary operator.
18

19
00:01:21,030 --> 00:01:22,440
Let's move forward.
19

20
00:01:22,440 --> 00:01:30,570
So, first let's discuss about the unary increment operator ++. So, ++ is a unary operator and it can
20

21
00:01:30,570 --> 00:01:33,910
appear on either side of an expression.
21

22
00:01:34,110 --> 00:01:36,130
Consider this expression here.
22

23
00:01:36,150 --> 00:01:43,590
So, here there is a variable x, and if you do x++, then x is incremented by 1 and you can also
23

24
00:01:43,590 --> 00:01:44,930
do ++x.
24

25
00:01:45,210 --> 00:01:47,820
So now, this is also same as x++.
25

26
00:01:47,820 --> 00:01:50,030
So, here also x is incremented by 1.
26

27
00:01:50,040 --> 00:01:57,030
So, the operator you can use either side of an expression. You have to remember that the increment operator
27

28
00:01:57,150 --> 00:02:02,090
adds 1 to the value of the operand and updates the operand.
28

29
00:02:02,490 --> 00:02:05,370
Let's move forward to understand some examples.
29

30
00:02:05,400 --> 00:02:07,920
So, here there are 2 variables x and y.
30

31
00:02:08,220 --> 00:02:10,620
So now, here x is initialized to 5
31

32
00:02:10,620 --> 00:02:19,080
and after that just observe this expression, so y = ++x. After this expression y's value
32

33
00:02:19,080 --> 00:02:21,930
will be 6 and x value will be 6.
33

34
00:02:21,930 --> 00:02:23,370
This is a final result.
34

35
00:02:23,370 --> 00:02:29,100
Let's analyze how. Here, first this expression will be evaluated in this case.
35

36
00:02:29,100 --> 00:02:34,190
So, if you consider this expression this is what we call as pre-incrementing.
36

37
00:02:34,230 --> 00:02:38,580
So, in this case value of the x will be incremented first.
37

38
00:02:38,580 --> 00:02:44,390
So, 5 becomes 6. And after that, that new value will be assigned to y.
38

39
00:02:44,520 --> 00:02:46,470
So, that's why, 6 will be assigned to y.
39

40
00:02:46,980 --> 00:02:53,660
So, first the value of x will be incremented by 1, and then value of x will be assigned to y.
40

41
00:02:54,090 --> 00:02:57,420
So, that's why, y = 6, x = 6.
41

42
00:02:57,420 --> 00:02:58,830
Consider this case.
42

43
00:02:58,830 --> 00:03:02,830
So here, n = 5 and m = n++.
43

44
00:03:03,330 --> 00:03:06,760
So, this is what we call as post-incrementing.
44

45
00:03:07,020 --> 00:03:14,740
So here, first value of x will be assigned to m, and then value of x will be incremented by 1.
45

46
00:03:14,760 --> 00:03:21,510
So, that's why, after this expression the result will be 
m = 5 and x = 6.
46

47
00:03:21,540 --> 00:03:24,000
So, there are 2 types of incrementing.
47

48
00:03:24,030 --> 00:03:25,540
One is pre-incrementing
48

49
00:03:25,560 --> 00:03:29,130
and another one is post- incrementing.
49

50
00:03:29,190 --> 00:03:34,230
So, now let's understand about pre-decrementing and post-decrementing.
50

51
00:03:34,920 --> 00:03:37,920
So, here consider x = 5.
51

52
00:03:38,070 --> 00:03:41,910
Here what happens is, this is pre-decrementing.
52

53
00:03:42,000 --> 00:03:45,750
So, that's why, first value of x will be decremented by 1.
53

54
00:03:46,020 --> 00:03:47,720
So, x becomes 4.
54

55
00:03:47,820 --> 00:03:52,260
After that, the new value of x will be assigned to y.
55

56
00:03:53,010 --> 00:03:53,670
So, that's why,
56

57
00:03:53,670 --> 00:03:56,070
y =4,  x = 4.
57

58
00:03:56,070 --> 00:03:58,690
Consider this case,
58

59
00:03:58,830 --> 00:04:02,040
this is what we call as post-decrementing.
59

60
00:04:02,070 --> 00:04:10,560
So, in this expression first the value of n will be assigned to m, so m will be 5 and after that n
60

61
00:04:10,560 --> 00:04:12,850
will be documented by 1.
61

62
00:04:12,910 --> 00:04:17,990
So, that's why, result will be m = 5 and n= 4.
62

63
00:04:18,090 --> 00:04:19,560
Let's move forward.
63

64
00:04:19,560 --> 00:04:24,740
So, in the next lecture let's understand unary operators with pointer variables.I'll
64

65
00:04:24,790 --> 00:04:25,950
see you in the next lecture.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:05,260
So, now let's understand about unary operators with pointer variables. Here
1

2
00:00:05,270 --> 00:00:08,230
there is a pointer variable definition
2

3
00:00:08,430 --> 00:00:11,840
and this is initialized to this value,
3

4
00:00:12,090 --> 00:00:19,500
and the pointer variable type is  uint32_t*. Let's say, if I want to increment this pointer
4

5
00:00:19,560 --> 00:00:20,320
by 1.
5

6
00:00:20,340 --> 00:00:24,120
So, I just do this, right?  pAddress = pAddress +1.
6

7
00:00:24,210 --> 00:00:27,550
So, this is an arithmetic add operation, right?
7

8
00:00:28,530 --> 00:00:35,360
So, if I try to increment this address by 1 it actually gets incremented by 4.
8

9
00:00:35,370 --> 00:00:35,800
Right?
9

10
00:00:35,820 --> 00:00:40,040
Because, its type is uint32_t*, right?
10

11
00:00:40,080 --> 00:00:44,970
So, that's why, the final result will be 0xFFFF0004
11

12
00:00:45,000 --> 00:00:51,780
in this case. Now, instead of doing this you can also use unary increment operator on the pointer variable.
12

13
00:00:52,290 --> 00:00:57,180
So, if you do this, then this also yields the same result.
13

14
00:00:57,210 --> 00:01:02,880
So, the final value of the pAddress will be the same value like this one.
14

15
00:01:02,880 --> 00:01:06,540
So, it will not be 0xFFFF001.
15

16
00:01:07,080 --> 00:01:13,830
So, when you do increment operation or decrement operation on the pointer variable the increment or decrement
16

17
00:01:13,830 --> 00:01:16,860
depends upon the pointer type.
17

18
00:01:16,860 --> 00:01:18,240
Right?
18

19
00:01:18,320 --> 00:01:20,060
There are 2 takeaways from this lecture.
19

20
00:01:20,060 --> 00:01:28,350
One is you can use pointer variable as an operand with unary decrement or unary increment operator.
20

21
00:01:28,490 --> 00:01:36,440
And when you do that the arithmetic, that is addition or subtraction depends upon the pointer variable
21

22
00:01:36,500 --> 00:01:42,580
type. And in the next lecture let's discuss about relational operators in 'C'.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 4

0
1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,320
Hey, welcome back to the course.
1

2
00:00:01,350 --> 00:00:10,380
So, let's explore relational operators in 'C'. So, relational operators do some kind of evaluation on the
2

3
00:00:10,380 --> 00:00:14,990
operands and then return value 1 or 0. 1 means
3

4
00:00:14,990 --> 00:00:22,320
true that evaluation is true, and  0 means the evaluation is false. Relation operators are binary operators
4

5
00:00:22,350 --> 00:00:30,760
because they require two operands to operate. The relational operators are evaluated left to right.
5

6
00:00:30,900 --> 00:00:38,400
These are the relational operators which are available in 'C' programming language, so use it for the evaluation.
6

7
00:00:38,520 --> 00:00:41,750
First, relational operator is equal to
7

8
00:00:41,790 --> 00:00:45,270
please note the == sign here.
8

9
00:00:45,370 --> 00:00:51,690
So, most of the beginners make mistake by mentioning single = sign. So, single = sign is
9

10
00:00:51,780 --> 00:00:56,550
assignment operator, not equal to operator. So, for equal to operator
10

11
00:00:56,550 --> 00:00:58,750
you have to use double equal to sign.
11

12
00:00:58,800 --> 00:01:01,820
Let's say you have 2 variables A and B.
12

13
00:01:02,100 --> 00:01:09,120
And if you want to check whether A == B, then you have to use the equal to operate.
13

14
00:01:09,780 --> 00:01:12,990
So, a == b returns 1.
14

15
00:01:12,990 --> 00:01:18,450
So, this evaluation returns 1, if  a and b are the same.
15

16
00:01:18,450 --> 00:01:20,520
So, that's very simple to understand, right?
16

17
00:01:20,520 --> 00:01:29,790
This is another relational operator for greater than evaluation. Now, a>b this expression
17

18
00:01:30,120 --> 00:01:31,310
returns 1,
18

19
00:01:31,500 --> 00:01:35,010
if a is larger than b like that.
19

20
00:01:35,400 --> 00:01:41,880
So, this is for less than , this is for greater than or equal to, less than or equal to, and this is for not
20

21
00:01:41,880 --> 00:01:42,710
equal to.
21

22
00:01:42,900 --> 00:01:49,660
So, suppose if you do A!= B, so this expression returns 1,
22

23
00:01:49,770 --> 00:01:56,550
if A is really not equal to B. So, if A == B, then this expression will give you 0.
23

24
00:01:57,000 --> 00:02:03,770
So, that's why, relation operators are evaluation operators. So, they will either turn into 1 or 0.
24

25
00:02:04,050 --> 00:02:06,290
So, True and False in 'C'.
25

26
00:02:06,780 --> 00:02:14,100
So, in 'C' zero is interpreted as false and anything non-zero is interpreted as true
26

27
00:02:14,100 --> 00:02:14,900
remember that.
27

28
00:02:15,200 --> 00:02:18,870
So, in 'C' zero is false and anything non-zero is true.
28

29
00:02:18,930 --> 00:02:22,440
So, -1 is true, -9 is true,
29

30
00:02:22,440 --> 00:02:31,410
so non-zero means true in 'C'. The expression using relational operators evaluate to a value of either true
30

31
00:02:31,620 --> 00:02:37,790
or false. Relational expressions are often used within if and while statements.
31

32
00:02:37,800 --> 00:02:39,390
So, we'll see that when we use them.
32

33
00:02:39,900 --> 00:02:41,430
Let's see some examples.
33

34
00:02:41,460 --> 00:02:44,310
Let's say you have 2 variables A and B.
34

35
00:02:44,700 --> 00:02:47,530
So, let's take a look into this statement.
35

36
00:02:47,550 --> 00:02:49,940
So, this is a valid 'C' statement.
36

37
00:02:50,000 --> 00:02:52,830
So here remember that, this is the assignment operator,
37

38
00:02:53,100 --> 00:02:54,780
so this is the equal to operator.
38

39
00:02:55,500 --> 00:03:02,160
So here, just take a look into this expression A == B, so that means you are checking whether
39

40
00:03:02,250 --> 00:03:04,970
A is really equal to B or not.
40

41
00:03:05,370 --> 00:03:11,190
If A and B are the same value, then this expression evaluates to be true(1)
41

42
00:03:11,250 --> 00:03:18,540
otherwise false(0). Since, A is not equal to B in reality, this expression evaluated to be false(0).
42

43
00:03:18,540 --> 00:03:21,480
So, c = 0 in this case.
43

44
00:03:21,630 --> 00:03:23,700
So, zero will be stored in 'C'.
44

45
00:03:23,700 --> 00:03:26,070
So here, take a look into this expression.So,
45

46
00:03:26,100 --> 00:03:30,910
this expression will yield you 1, because A is really less than B.
46

47
00:03:30,960 --> 00:03:31,310
Isn't it?
47

48
00:03:31,350 --> 00:03:34,170
So, c = 1 in this case.
48

49
00:03:34,170 --> 00:03:37,580
So, what happens here? and what happens here?
49

50
00:03:37,680 --> 00:03:41,580
So, A!= B which is true, isn't it?
50

51
00:03:41,580 --> 00:03:43,500
So, that's why, c will be 1
51

52
00:03:43,530 --> 00:03:44,310
in this case.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 5

0
1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,350
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,410 --> 00:00:05,610
So, in this lecture let's explore about logical operators in 'C'.
2

3
00:00:05,850 --> 00:00:13,740
So, there are 3 logical operators. So, logical-AND (&&), logical - OR (||), and logical -NOT (!). So, the logical operators
3

4
00:00:13,740 --> 00:00:18,370
perform logical- AND (&&), logical-OR (||), and logical-NOT (!) operations.
4

5
00:00:18,390 --> 00:00:23,740
So, these are binary operators except NOT (!), because it's a unary operator.
5

6
00:00:24,360 --> 00:00:28,430
So, first let's explore about logical- AND operator.
6

7
00:00:28,440 --> 00:00:31,560
So, this is the symbol or this is a operator
7

8
00:00:31,560 --> 00:00:36,210
in order to perform logical-AND operation on the operands.
8

9
00:00:36,320 --> 00:00:40,800
So, the logical-AND operator produces the value 1
9

10
00:00:40,830 --> 00:00:45,300
if both operands have nonzero values.
10

11
00:00:45,300 --> 00:00:50,120
So, if either operand is equal to 0, the result is 0.
11

12
00:00:50,130 --> 00:00:55,110
So, if the first operand of a logical-AND operation is equal to 0,
12

13
00:00:55,110 --> 00:00:58,460
the second operand is not evaluated.
13

14
00:00:58,560 --> 00:01:05,190
For example, consider this expression C = (A && B)
14

15
00:01:05,230 --> 00:01:07,620
So, these are the values of A and B.
15

16
00:01:07,620 --> 00:01:11,310
So here,  A is nonzero and B is nonzero.
16

17
00:01:11,310 --> 00:01:11,580
Right?
17

18
00:01:11,580 --> 00:01:13,860
So, nonzero means true, right? in 'C'.
18

19
00:01:13,980 --> 00:01:15,480
So, this is true,
19

20
00:01:15,480 --> 00:01:17,270
this operand is also true.
20

21
00:01:17,290 --> 00:01:22,870
So, according to the truth table of the logical-AND this expression will be evaluated. So, what's a truth
21

22
00:01:22,880 --> 00:01:23,380
table?
22

23
00:01:23,400 --> 00:01:29,460
So, I hope you know the truth table of AND,  OR, and NOT. So, if both
23

24
00:01:29,480 --> 00:01:30,860
operands are true,
24

25
00:01:30,930 --> 00:01:32,820
the result of the AND operation will be true.
25

26
00:01:32,820 --> 00:01:33,820
Right?
26

27
00:01:33,870 --> 00:01:36,530
So, that's why, this whole expression will be true.
27

28
00:01:36,660 --> 00:01:39,070
So, that's why, C = 1.
28

29
00:01:39,090 --> 00:01:42,470
Now, let's say if A is 0 instead of -10.
29

30
00:01:42,500 --> 00:01:44,020
Now what happens to this expression.
30

31
00:01:44,020 --> 00:01:46,600
So, A is 0 means A is false, isn't it?
31

32
00:01:46,710 --> 00:01:47,210
So, false.
32

33
00:01:47,240 --> 00:01:53,400
So, if one of the operand is false here, then this operand is not at all checked. So, because the result
33

34
00:01:53,400 --> 00:01:54,360
is anyway false.
34

35
00:01:54,360 --> 00:01:54,940
Right?
35

36
00:01:54,960 --> 00:01:57,410
So, that's why, it will be straight away false.
36

37
00:01:57,450 --> 00:01:59,220
So, C will be 0.
37

38
00:01:59,220 --> 00:02:01,080
So, now if B is 0,
38

39
00:02:01,080 --> 00:02:03,360
then what happens. So, A is true.
39

40
00:02:03,360 --> 00:02:07,200
So, if this is true, then only this operand will be checked.
40

41
00:02:07,320 --> 00:02:08,610
In this case it is 0,
41

42
00:02:08,610 --> 00:02:10,240
that means false.
42

43
00:02:10,320 --> 00:02:14,520
So, that's why, the result of the expression will be false like that.
43

44
00:02:14,520 --> 00:02:21,570
So, remember that, when you are using logical operators so you have to talk in terms of logical states like
44

45
00:02:21,570 --> 00:02:25,690
true and false of the variables or of the expression.
45

46
00:02:25,770 --> 00:02:30,320
You should not consider the real value of the variables or the expressions.
46

47
00:02:31,020 --> 00:02:36,930
So, you should always talk in terms of logical states either true or false.
47

48
00:02:36,930 --> 00:02:39,960
For example, let's say  C = A + B
48

49
00:02:39,990 --> 00:02:44,410
So, what is the value of C? C will be 10, right? in this case.
49

50
00:02:44,460 --> 00:02:55,860
So, what about this? C is equal to let's say 1 &&(A + B). So, this will be 1 &&(A + B)
50

51
00:02:55,860 --> 00:02:58,060
So, A + B is 10.
51

52
00:02:58,110 --> 00:02:58,770
Right?
52

53
00:03:00,310 --> 00:03:02,840
So, 1 logical-AND of 10.
53

54
00:03:02,860 --> 00:03:03,200
Right?
54

55
00:03:03,370 --> 00:03:05,140
So, both are non-zero values.
55

56
00:03:05,170 --> 00:03:06,900
So, that's why, c = 1
56

57
00:03:06,910 --> 00:03:08,050
in this case.
57

58
00:03:08,050 --> 00:03:15,820
So, that's why, when you use logical operators the result will be either true or false, that is 1 or 0.
58

59
00:03:15,850 --> 00:03:24,340
Let's move forward and let's explore logical-OR operator.So, logical-OR operator performs an inclusive-OR
59

60
00:03:24,460 --> 00:03:26,950
operation on its operands.
60

61
00:03:27,040 --> 00:03:29,080
The result is 0 if both
61

62
00:03:29,080 --> 00:03:32,760
operands have 0 values according to the truth table.
62

63
00:03:32,770 --> 00:03:33,600
Right?
63

64
00:03:33,610 --> 00:03:36,640
So, if either operand has a nonzero value,
64

65
00:03:36,700 --> 00:03:42,960
the result is 1. If the first operand of a logical-OR operation has a nonzero value,
65

66
00:03:42,970 --> 00:03:45,450
the second operand is not evaluated.
66

67
00:03:46,060 --> 00:03:49,120
So now, what about logical-NOT operator?
67

68
00:03:49,150 --> 00:03:52,580
So, it actually reverses the state of the operand.
68

69
00:03:52,620 --> 00:03:59,380
So, if a condition or expression is true, then logical NOT operator will make it false.
69

70
00:03:59,380 --> 00:04:08,420
Let's say, you have something like this. C = !(A == B). So,this is actually
70

71
00:04:08,420 --> 00:04:11,900
the combination of relational operator and logical operator.
71

72
00:04:11,930 --> 00:04:12,380
Right?
72

73
00:04:12,930 --> 00:04:17,260
So, now let's say A = 1 and B = -5.
73

74
00:04:17,270 --> 00:04:18,510
So, what happens here?
74

75
00:04:18,620 --> 00:04:21,340
So, first the parentheses will be evaluated.
75

76
00:04:21,380 --> 00:04:25,360
So, !(A == B), right?
76

77
00:04:25,370 --> 00:04:26,870
So, this is a relational operator.
77

78
00:04:26,990 --> 00:04:28,490
So, which is false.
78

79
00:04:28,580 --> 00:04:29,110
Right?
79

80
00:04:29,120 --> 00:04:30,780
So, that's why, it will be 0.
80

81
00:04:30,800 --> 00:04:31,570
Right?
81

82
00:04:31,580 --> 00:04:33,580
So, !(0)
82

83
00:04:33,650 --> 00:04:35,250
that is not of
83

84
00:04:35,330 --> 00:04:36,430
False.
84

85
00:04:36,440 --> 00:04:39,890
So, not of false is true.
85

86
00:04:39,890 --> 00:04:40,400
Right?
86

87
00:04:40,400 --> 00:04:43,020
So, 1 will be stored here, c  = 1.
87

88
00:04:43,640 --> 00:04:45,550
So, now let's consider this example.
88

89
00:04:45,560 --> 00:04:46,600
x =10.
89

90
00:04:46,700 --> 00:04:50,140
So, state of the x is true this expression.
90

91
00:04:50,240 --> 00:04:53,660
So, not of true, which is false.
91

92
00:04:53,660 --> 00:04:54,620
Right?
92

93
00:04:54,680 --> 00:04:58,590
So, 0 will be stored in x, x = 0.
93

94
00:04:58,610 --> 00:05:00,880
So, what is the value of A
94

95
00:05:00,890 --> 00:05:02,120
in the below expression?
95

96
00:05:09,710 --> 00:05:13,160
So, just think for a few seconds.
96

97
00:05:13,200 --> 00:05:16,650
So, first of all parentheses will be valued at first here.
97

98
00:05:16,770 --> 00:05:17,180
Right?
98

99
00:05:17,190 --> 00:05:19,670
So, from left to right.
99

100
00:05:19,800 --> 00:05:22,890
So, x is greater than 5 (x>5), which is true
100

101
00:05:22,890 --> 00:05:24,960
remember that, this is a relational operator.
101

102
00:05:25,380 --> 00:05:32,290
So, x > 5, 1 && (y < 5).
102

103
00:05:32,790 --> 00:05:33,150
Right?
103

104
00:05:33,150 --> 00:05:38,640
So, that will be 0. So, and the result will be false.
104

105
00:05:38,650 --> 00:05:39,370
Right?
105

106
00:05:39,370 --> 00:05:41,950
So, that's why, A will be 0.
106

107
00:05:42,070 --> 00:05:44,630
So, !(0).
107

108
00:05:44,740 --> 00:05:46,550
So, A will be 1
108

109
00:05:46,570 --> 00:05:47,970
in this case.
109

110
00:05:47,970 --> 00:05:56,440
So, I hope you understood about logical operators AND,OR, and NOT. And in the next lecture let's understand
110

111
00:05:56,440 --> 00:05:58,570
about the decision making in 'C'.


==================================================================================


section 16---Decision Making

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,050 --> 00:00:01,350
Hey, welcome back 
1

2
00:00:01,560 --> 00:00:06,650
to the lecture. So, from this lecture onwards let's understand about decision making
2

3
00:00:06,660 --> 00:00:12,480
in 'C'.So, when you are going to write some program, it will always be necessary that 
3

4
00:00:12,480 --> 00:00:18,120
you will have to put some code which has to be executed only when a specific condition is 
4

5
00:00:18,120 --> 00:00:25,710
satisfied. That means, your program has to take decision based on internal/external events or conditions.
5

6
00:00:25,710 --> 00:00:32,370
For example, if the user presses a key, then execute one set of statements or if the 
6

7
00:00:32,370 --> 00:00:36,140
user doesn't press any key, then execute another set of statements.
7

8
00:00:36,180 --> 00:00:43,560
So, this we can achieve by using decision making statements of 'C'. So, in embedded 
8

9
00:00:43,560 --> 00:00:46,680
programming, let's take an example of water level indication and control
9

10
00:00:47,130 --> 00:00:53,580
program.So, if the sensor detects water level rising above the threshold, then the program 
10

11
00:00:53,580 --> 00:00:54,640
executes the code to turn 
11

12
00:00:54,750 --> 00:01:02,400
off the motor, otherwise program doesn't turn off the motor. In 'C' there are 5 different ways to take 
12

13
00:01:02,400 --> 00:01:05,040
decisions by making use of below decision taking statements.
13

14
00:01:05,520 --> 00:01:13,290
So, those are if statement, if-else statement, if-else-if ladder, conditional operators, switch/case
14

15
00:01:13,470 --> 00:01:17,670
statement.So, we'll understand each one of these statements 
15

16
00:01:17,670 --> 00:01:23,700
one by one.  So but for this lecture let's understand the if statement which is very very simple
16

17
00:01:23,730 --> 00:01:27,460
and this is how the syntax of the if statement looks 
17

18
00:01:27,720 --> 00:01:34,500
like.  So, if you want to use if statement in your code, then you have to use the
18

19
00:01:34,530 --> 00:01:43,230
standard keyword if along with the parentheses. So, if is a standard keyword and inside the parentheses
19

20
00:01:43,290 --> 00:01:45,000
you have to write your 
20

21
00:01:45,000 --> 00:01:46,830
expression. Expression 
21

22
00:01:46,830 --> 00:01:50,480
can be anything it can be made up of relational operators, or 
22

23
00:01:50,520 --> 00:01:53,580
it can be made up of arithmetic operators, or 
23

24
00:01:53,580 --> 00:01:54,570
bitwise operators it can be 
24

25
00:01:54,960 --> 00:01:58,000
anything. First let's understand single statement 
25

26
00:01:58,080 --> 00:02:06,660
execution. If the evaluation of this expression is true that is nonzero, then the statement which
26

27
00:02:06,660 --> 00:02:09,670
follows right after this if statement will be 
27

28
00:02:09,840 --> 00:02:17,580
executed. If the evaluation of this expression is false that is 0, then a statement which 
28

29
00:02:17,580 --> 00:02:19,990
follows right after this if statement will 
29

30
00:02:20,040 --> 00:02:21,180
be skipped. It will not be executed.
30

31
00:02:22,790 --> 00:02:30,010
So, the execution of this statement depends upon the evaluation of this 
31

32
00:02:30,020 --> 00:02:31,300
expressions. Very simple.
32

33
00:02:31,420 --> 00:02:34,760
Here, this is a case of a single 
33

34
00:02:34,760 --> 00:02:37,100
statement execution, this is the case of multiple 
34

35
00:02:37,100 --> 00:02:44,390
statement execution.So, if this expression is true and if you want to execute multiple statements,
35

36
00:02:44,390 --> 00:02:46,670
then remember that you have to use these curly 
36

37
00:02:46,670 --> 00:02:50,380
braces. So, this actually makes the body of the if 
37

38
00:02:50,450 --> 00:02:55,840
statement.So, if this expression is true, then all these statements will be executed 
38

39
00:02:56,060 --> 00:03:03,410
one by one and if this expression is false, then none of these statements will be executed and
39

40
00:03:03,410 --> 00:03:06,140
the execution of this whole body will be
40

41
00:03:06,140 --> 00:03:12,470
skipped.So, this is a flowchart of the if statement. So, first the expression will be evaluated 
41

42
00:03:12,530 --> 00:03:20,510
if the expression result is true, then the statement or statements will be executed. If it is false, 
42

43
00:03:20,510 --> 00:03:27,080
then the statements will not be executed and the execution control continues with the rest of the code of the 
43

44
00:03:27,950 --> 00:03:29,690
program. So, now 
44

45
00:03:29,690 --> 00:03:34,800
let's see an example. So here, there is a variable myData, which is initialize to
45

46
00:03:34,910 --> 00:03:38,770
a value 20.So, here we can see that, this is a case of single 
46

47
00:03:38,810 --> 00:03:45,350
statement execution. So, there is only one statement which is coming right after the if statement. 
47

48
00:03:45,350 --> 00:03:46,010
And take a look
48

49
00:03:46,010 --> 00:03:50,120
into the expression,so this is a expression which is used for the if 
49

50
00:03:50,120 --> 00:03:52,710
statement. So, this actually uses a relational
50

51
00:03:53,180 --> 00:04:00,410
operator.So now here if myData is greater than 40, if this evaluation is true, then only 
51

52
00:04:00,410 --> 00:04:01,680
this statement will be 
52

53
00:04:01,730 --> 00:04:08,180
executed. So, in this case myData is 20. So, 20 > 40, which is 
53

54
00:04:08,210 --> 00:04:11,730
actually false.So, that's why, this statement will not be 
54

55
00:04:11,900 --> 00:04:18,620
executed.So, the control actually reaches this point and this statement will be executed, because 
55

56
00:04:18,650 --> 00:04:22,140
this statement is outside the if block. So, it is 
56

57
00:04:22,160 --> 00:04:28,820
always executed. It doesn't matter whether this expression evaluation is true or false, this is outside the 
57

58
00:04:28,820 --> 00:04:30,440
if block, so it is always
58

59
00:04:30,440 --> 00:04:35,750
executed.So here, what happens is, when 20 is compared with 40 the result is actually 
59

60
00:04:36,200 --> 00:04:38,870
zero.So, that's why, it will become 
60

61
00:04:38,870 --> 00:04:47,320
something like this. if(0). So, the expression result is false. So, that's why, this statement will not be 
61

62
00:04:47,330 --> 00:04:49,930
executed. You can even put curly 
62

63
00:04:49,970 --> 00:04:51,640
braces here.No problem with 
63

64
00:04:51,680 --> 00:04:55,450
that.So, even for the single statement execution 
64

65
00:04:55,460 --> 00:04:56,350
you are free to put curly 
65

66
00:04:56,930 --> 00:05:01,360
braces.So but, since it is a single statement we don't usually put 
66

67
00:05:01,370 --> 00:05:03,910
a braces.So now, let's take a look into 
67

68
00:05:03,920 --> 00:05:07,550
this program.So here, our goal is to execute 2
68

69
00:05:07,550 --> 00:05:10,460
statements.If the condition or expression is 
69

70
00:05:10,460 --> 00:05:15,350
true.So, that's why, you have to use curly braces around the if 
70

71
00:05:15,350 --> 00:05:22,190
statement.So, if you are trying to execute 2 or more statements if the condition is true, so 
71

72
00:05:22,190 --> 00:05:23,770
then you have to use the
72

73
00:05:23,810 --> 00:05:30,220
curly braces,you have to remember that. So here, myData = 60, so 60>40 
73

74
00:05:30,240 --> 00:05:32,010
which is true.So, it 
74

75
00:05:32,010 --> 00:05:33,910
looks something like this.if(1).
75

76
00:05:33,930 --> 00:05:37,140
So hence, these 2 statements will be
76

77
00:05:37,200 --> 00:05:44,160
executed.And after that control comes out of the body of the if statement, and then it will execute 
77

78
00:05:44,190 --> 00:05:46,320
this statement and after that this statement will be executed.
78

79
00:05:47,040 --> 00:05:53,870
So, here these statements are called as conditional statements, because its execution depends upon 
79

80
00:05:53,880 --> 00:05:55,020
evaluation of
80

81
00:05:55,020 --> 00:05:56,010
an expression.
81

82
00:05:56,130 --> 00:06:03,240
Let's continue.So, beginners mistakes, so observe that there is no semicolon for the if statement. 
82

83
00:06:03,240 --> 00:06:05,380
So, you can see that I have not given any 
83

84
00:06:05,430 --> 00:06:10,700
semicolon here.So, if you give a semicolon then the meaning of the execution of statement a 
84

85
00:06:10,860 --> 00:06:16,720
completely changes. And please note that the compiler will not issue any syntax error 
85

86
00:06:16,740 --> 00:06:22,620
for this.Now, I will tell you, what exactly is the meaning of giving semicolon, but logically it is 
86

87
00:06:22,890 --> 00:06:28,210
incorrect here and so compiler maybe issuing some warning but it will not generate 
87

88
00:06:28,260 --> 00:06:31,180
any error. Let's say, I give semicolon 
88

89
00:06:31,340 --> 00:06:34,710
here. Let's analyze how exactly the program 
89

90
00:06:34,710 --> 00:06:38,270
behaves.So, now this code is equivalent to this code.
90

91
00:06:39,300 --> 00:06:42,510
So, here observe that, I have given a
91

92
00:06:42,510 --> 00:06:49,410
semicolon.So now, this is  a equivalent code, I have just used some curly braces in order to explain
92

93
00:06:50,070 --> 00:06:51,620
properly.This 
93

94
00:06:51,660 --> 00:07:01,240
statement you can  visualize something like this. So, if myData is greater than 40, then execute a semicolon. 
94

95
00:07:01,240 --> 00:07:10,660
So, in 'C', a semicolon (;) by itself or an empty block( {}),  empty block means this is empty block a pair of 
95

96
00:07:10,900 --> 00:07:11,760
curly braces is an empty
96

97
00:07:12,520 --> 00:07:13,950
block.So, 
97

98
00:07:13,990 --> 00:07:15,720
which is actually, no operation.
98

99
00:07:16,390 --> 00:07:21,130
So, whenever this condition is true, this 
99

100
00:07:21,130 --> 00:07:22,660
semicolon will be executed. That means
100

101
00:07:22,960 --> 00:07:27,010
NOP.And these statements are now outside the
101

102
00:07:27,010 --> 00:07:30,940
if body.So, that's why, the meaning of your logic completely 
102

103
00:07:31,450 --> 00:07:34,210
changes here. So, now these are not conditional 
103

104
00:07:34,210 --> 00:07:40,290
statements.So, it doesn't matter whether this expression is true or false these statements will always be
104

105
00:07:40,310 --> 00:07:45,020
executed.So, that's why, giving a semicolon completely changes the logic of your 
105

106
00:07:45,100 --> 00:07:47,570
program.So, that's why, you have to be careful 
106

107
00:07:47,650 --> 00:07:52,780
with a semicolon. So, don't give a semicolon with if statement unless you are very sure about what you are trying 
107

108
00:07:53,830 --> 00:07:57,350
to do.So, never give semicolon with a if
108

109
00:07:57,430 --> 00:08:02,240
statement.So, the relation operator such as is equal to greater 
109

110
00:08:02,240 --> 00:08:06,480
than, lesser than,so these relation operators and the logical operators are 
110

111
00:08:06,520 --> 00:08:12,070
frequently used, I mean of course the bitwise operations are frequently used as the basis of conditions 
111

112
00:08:12,100 --> 00:08:13,060
or expression
112

113
00:08:13,060 --> 00:08:16,680
in if and if/else conditional statements to direct the 
113

114
00:08:16,810 --> 00:08:22,630
program flow. So, most of the time this expression is made up of relation operators, or logical 
114

115
00:08:22,630 --> 00:08:27,490
operators,  or bitwise operators. And we'll see as we make a progress when we take up 
115

116
00:08:27,490 --> 00:08:29,290
examples. So, now take a look at
116

117
00:08:29,320 --> 00:08:35,500
this example. So here, there is a program and the expression here is nothing but a
117

118
00:08:35,500 --> 00:08:38,800
variable itself.So, this is also a valid
118

119
00:08:38,800 --> 00:08:42,160
expression.So here, first this variable is
119

120
00:08:42,220 --> 00:08:46,780
initialized to 0.So, now this condition now becomes if(0), 
120

121
00:08:46,780 --> 00:08:53,950
which is false.So, whenever the button is pressed, the interrupt service routine associated with a button is executed 
121

122
00:08:53,950 --> 00:08:57,390
and inside the ISR code the variable is set
122

123
00:08:57,400 --> 00:09:04,960
to 1.So, when this variable is set to 1 and when this if statement is again executed, then it 
123

124
00:09:05,080 --> 00:09:05,540
becomes if
124

125
00:09:05,590 --> 00:09:07,000
of 1,and
125

126
00:09:07,000 --> 00:09:09,580
that is true. And at that time these statements
126

127
00:09:09,580 --> 00:09:15,310
will be executed.So, the takeaway from this slide is you can even use a variable as an expression.

So, this will automatically removes the decimal part. So, only integer part will be stored in the variable
15

16
00:01:04,560 --> 00:01:05,280
n1.
16

17
00:01:05,340 --> 00:01:08,850
So similarly, I can do this, n2 = num2.
17

18
00:01:09,450 --> 00:01:19,690
So, we are storing only integer part of the real number. And after that, let's use n1 and n2 for the comparisons.
18

19
00:01:20,010 --> 00:01:24,030
So, let me change all num1 to n1 and num2 to n2.
19

20
00:01:30,610 --> 00:01:34,930
So, give a warning here for the user, if we enters a real number.
20

21
00:01:34,960 --> 00:01:36,850
So, let's give a warning.
21

22
00:01:36,850 --> 00:01:43,050
So, what I want to do is, if n1, if it is not is equal to num1
22

23
00:01:43,120 --> 00:01:45,310
so if n1 is not is equal to num1,
23

24
00:01:45,340 --> 00:01:49,710
so that means that user has entered a real number.
24

25
00:01:49,900 --> 00:01:58,650
So, that's why, let's print a warning here "warning! comparing only integer part".
25

26
00:01:58,770 --> 00:02:00,730
So, this is actually a relational operator.
26

27
00:02:01,140 --> 00:02:04,880
So, if( n1 != num1)
27

28
00:02:04,890 --> 00:02:07,650
So, that means num1 is a real number.
28

29
00:02:07,650 --> 00:02:09,750
So, it has decimal part, correct?
29

30
00:02:09,750 --> 00:02:12,660
So, now let's do the same for num 2.
30

31
00:02:12,660 --> 00:02:21,540
So now let me keep this inside the parentheses and let's use the logical-OR operator, or if or n2 is
31

32
00:02:21,540 --> 00:02:24,530
not is equal to num2.
32

33
00:02:24,540 --> 00:02:25,530
So, this makes sense.
33

34
00:02:26,460 --> 00:02:33,230
So, if(n1 != num1)  ||  (n2 != num2)
34

35
00:02:33,270 --> 00:02:41,760
So, if one among these expressions is true, then that means that user has entered a real number and this
35

36
00:02:41,760 --> 00:02:43,140
warning will be printed.
36

37
00:02:43,170 --> 00:02:43,660
All right.
37

38

Let's try to compile this code and let's see how it works.

39

Let me execute the application.
39

40

So, enter the first number.
40

41

so 45 is bigger.
41

42
00:02:56,490 --> 00:02:57,460
That's correct.

44

Let me enter 4.5
44

45

you can see that your application is not breaking, it is behaving properly.
45

46

So now, enter the second number I would enter 0, and here you can see that it is now giving us a warning.
46

47

Warning! comparing only integer part
47

48
00:03:16,860 --> 00:03:19,400
that means only 4 and 0 is compared.

50
00:03:27,100 --> 00:03:31,220

and numbers are equal.

52
00:03:32,650 --> 00:03:33,750
That's correct.

Let's try once again. 4.0, 4.0,

54
00:03:37,480 --> 00:03:46,310

55
00:03:46,450 --> 00:03:47,080
