39
00:02:24,420 --> 00:02:28,260
so here is a picture of the microcontroller, which is used.
39

40
00:02:28,630 --> 00:02:35,180
So, this  U4A is a part number of the microcontroller, the microcontroller is STM32F4
40

41
00:02:35,230 --> 00:02:43,300
07VGT6. And it has many pins, but on this board all these pins are exposed.
41

42
00:02:43,470 --> 00:02:52,290
So, in STM32Fx based MCUs, each port has 16 pins where you can connect external peripherals such
42

43
00:02:52,290 --> 00:03:01,650
as LED, Displays, buttons, Bluetooth transceiver, external memory such as EEPROM,  Joystick, keypad, etc. So,
43

44
00:03:01,650 --> 00:03:07,800
if you want to do those external connections, then you can take help of these ports which are exposed
44

45
00:03:07,890 --> 00:03:08,660
on the board.
45

46
00:03:09,150 --> 00:03:15,840
So, if you take a look into this picture all these pins can be segregated into different ports like Port A,
46

47
00:03:15,930 --> 00:03:17,990
Port E, Port D,
47

48
00:03:18,030 --> 00:03:23,400
and each port is a collection of 16 pins. For example,
48

49
00:03:23,400 --> 00:03:34,200
So, all these pins actually belong to Port A. So, the first pin is called as PA0, that is Port A PIN number
49

50
00:03:34,200 --> 00:03:34,610
0,
50

51
00:03:34,650 --> 00:03:36,780
and the last pin here is PA15.
51

52
00:03:37,410 --> 00:03:41,730
And this is a PIN number of the microcontroller. So,
52

53
00:03:41,760 --> 00:03:46,320
that means microcontrollers 23 pin is actually PA0,
53

54
00:03:46,380 --> 00:03:50,670
that is the 0th pin of the Port A, it's like that.
54

55
00:03:50,880 --> 00:03:56,550
This microcontroller has many ports like it has Port F,  Port G , Port H, etc.
55

56
00:03:56,730 --> 00:04:00,520
but only these ports are exposed on the board.
56

57
00:04:01,110 --> 00:04:02,810
So, let's move forward.
57

58
00:04:02,850 --> 00:04:12,840
So, now let's consider a this LED, that is LD4. This LED is connected to PD12
58

59
00:04:13,080 --> 00:04:14,190
exactly like this.
59

60
00:04:14,550 --> 00:04:15,950
So, this is a microcontroller,
60

61
00:04:15,960 --> 00:04:23,200
so this is Port D, and at the 12th pin this LED is connected.
61

62
00:04:23,270 --> 00:04:31,940
So, now the question is, how do you access 12th pin of Port D? So, Port D is also called as GPIO Port D. So,
62

63
00:04:32,030 --> 00:04:34,400
GPIO stands for general purpose IO.
63

64
00:04:34,430 --> 00:04:41,180
So, these are called general purpose IO's because, it can be used for any purpose. Like you can use that
64

65
00:04:41,180 --> 00:04:48,260
to connect external peripherals, or you can use that pins to do some other protocol communication like
65

66
00:04:48,410 --> 00:04:54,340
Ethernet communication, or UART communication, I2C communication, etc.
66

67
00:04:54,470 --> 00:04:58,400
So, that's why, these pins are called as general purpose IO's.
67

68
00:04:58,430 --> 00:05:04,300
So, now you have to think about controlling this pin, if you control this pin you can control the LED.
68

69
00:05:04,310 --> 00:05:07,610
So now the question is, how do you control this pin from the software?
69

70
00:05:08,210 --> 00:05:15,380
So, now your goal is to control the I/O pin PD12's state either HIGH or LOW through the software to make
70

71
00:05:15,440 --> 00:05:17,290
LED turn ON or turn OFF,
71

72
00:05:17,600 --> 00:05:19,840
and we are going to learn that. So, now for this
72

73
00:05:19,840 --> 00:05:25,550
you have to understand how these pins are controlled from the microcontroller and we understand that
73

74
00:05:25,550 --> 00:05:26,300
in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,620
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,620 --> 00:00:07,380
Let's continue our discussion about how we can control these microcontroller pins or I\Os  by using
2

3
00:00:07,380 --> 00:00:10,540
the software. So, to control these pins
3

4
00:00:10,560 --> 00:00:15,300
there is a peripheral called  GPIOD inside the microcontroller.
4

5
00:00:15,420 --> 00:00:20,760
So, GPIOD is a peripheral which is used to control pins of the Port D.
5

6
00:00:21,480 --> 00:00:30,080
So, this peripheral has its own set of registers which are used to control pin's mode, state, and other functionalities.
6

7
00:00:30,210 --> 00:00:36,570
So, the software has to write into that register, so there by you can control the modes of the pins, you
7

8
00:00:36,570 --> 00:00:42,890
can control what data you can transmit over the pins, or and you can write data to the port,
8

9
00:00:42,900 --> 00:00:49,200
you can read data from the port, so various activities you do through the registers.
9

10
00:00:49,200 --> 00:00:52,290
Now the question is how to access these registers.
10

11
00:00:52,380 --> 00:00:56,340
You can access these registers using memory addresses,
11

12
00:00:56,400 --> 00:00:59,670
that means each register has its own memory address.
12

13
00:00:59,670 --> 00:01:06,630
So, by using that memory address you can access these registers and you can control the pins. Hence
13

14
00:01:06,660 --> 00:01:16,030
you can also say that the peripheral registers of let's say GPIOD peripheral are memory mapped. So,
14

15
00:01:16,030 --> 00:01:17,580
that leads to our next discussion
15

16
00:01:17,590 --> 00:01:20,560
that is, what exactly is memory mapped I/O?
16

17
00:01:20,590 --> 00:01:27,970
So, the memory mapped I/O means the IO pins are controlled using peripheral registers which are
17

18
00:01:27,970 --> 00:01:32,410
mapped on to processor addressable memory locations.
18

19
00:01:32,650 --> 00:01:37,710
That leads to our next discussion, what are processor addressable memory locations?
19

20
00:01:37,840 --> 00:01:42,340
So, we'll understand what are processor addressable memory locations in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:06,690
So, now in this lecture let's understand about what are processor addressable memory locations.
1

2
00:00:06,690 --> 00:00:08,910
So, now let's consider this figure.
2

3
00:00:08,910 --> 00:00:09,540
Now here,
3

4
00:00:09,540 --> 00:00:15,000
there is a system bus which connects the processor with the peripherals and with the memory.
4

5
00:00:15,150 --> 00:00:18,900
So, in ARM Cortex Mx based microcontroller
5

6
00:00:18,900 --> 00:00:25,440
this is a central bus which connects peripherals, processor, and the memories.
6

7
00:00:25,440 --> 00:00:33,090
And this bus is also called as system bus, which is based on AHB specification designed by ARM and AHB
7

8
00:00:33,090 --> 00:00:35,550
stands for Advanced High performance Bus,
8

9
00:00:35,580 --> 00:00:38,340
so that's actually a specification.
9

10
00:00:38,340 --> 00:00:41,240
So, this bus comprises of two channels.
10

11
00:00:41,310 --> 00:00:45,270
So, 32 bit address channel and 32 bit data channel.
11

12
00:00:45,360 --> 00:00:49,990
That means that a this bus has 32 bit address bus.
12

13
00:00:50,100 --> 00:00:58,140
So, that means you can put two to the power 32 a different addresses on this bus, in order to target different
13

14
00:00:58,170 --> 00:01:00,770
peripherals and different memories.
14

15
00:01:00,810 --> 00:01:08,340
For example, let's say you want to transmit data from data memory to the GPIOD peripheral and you
15

16
00:01:08,340 --> 00:01:15,000
have to send that data out to the external world over the ports of the GPIOD peripheral.
16

17
00:01:15,030 --> 00:01:20,140
So, if this is your objective, then what you have to do? You have to send the data over this system
17

18
00:01:20,160 --> 00:01:25,780
bus, and you have to send that data to the one of the register of this peripheral.
18

19
00:01:25,860 --> 00:01:32,010
And if you place the data into that register of this peripheral the data will be transmitted to the IO pins
19

20
00:01:32,070 --> 00:01:33,000
to the external world.
20

21
00:01:33,090 --> 00:01:34,580
So, that's how it works.
21

22
00:01:34,590 --> 00:01:41,700
So now, that means that you have to find out which address you have to place on this address bus to target
22

23
00:01:41,760 --> 00:01:45,330
this particular peripheral. So, that you have to find out.
23

24
00:01:45,330 --> 00:01:52,050
So, now we know that details by going through the memory map of the processor. So, you cannot put any random
24

25
00:01:52,080 --> 00:01:59,410
address on the address bus to target a peripheral. For example, in this case if you want to target ADC peripheral,
25

26
00:01:59,490 --> 00:02:07,950
let's say you want to read the data from the ADC into the memory. So, you have to place appropriate address
26

27
00:02:08,040 --> 00:02:16,960
on the address bus, to read data from the data register of the ADC peripherals. This system bus
27

28
00:02:16,980 --> 00:02:19,210
as I said comprises of a
28

29
00:02:19,380 --> 00:02:22,600
an address channel whose width is 32 bit.
29

30
00:02:22,710 --> 00:02:28,670
So, that means you can put 2 to the power 32 different memory locations.
30

31
00:02:28,770 --> 00:02:38,250
That is 4 gig. 4 gig different memory location values onto the address bus in order to target different
31

32
00:02:38,250 --> 00:02:40,100
peripherals and different memories.
32

33
00:02:40,140 --> 00:02:47,030
So, you have to remember that. The processor can produce a 4 gig of different memory locations on the
33

34
00:02:47,030 --> 00:02:51,840
address bus to target different memories and to target different peripherals.
34

35
00:02:52,590 --> 00:02:56,100
So now, let's conclude processor addressable memory location.
35

36
00:02:56,250 --> 00:03:03,890
Since the address bus width is 32 bits, processor can put  address ranging from 0x0000
36

37
00:03:03,980 --> 00:03:09,530
0000 to 0xFFFF_FFFF on the address bus.
37

38
00:03:09,650 --> 00:03:11,590
So, that means 4 gig,
38

39
00:03:11,600 --> 00:03:16,430
so these many different addresses can be put on the address bus.
39

40
00:03:16,490 --> 00:03:22,280
So, now let's understand how these different addresses are arranged to address, different memories, and
40

41
00:03:22,280 --> 00:03:25,280
different peripherals of the microcontroller.
41

42
00:03:25,280 --> 00:03:32,510
When the processor puts these memory locations on the address bus, the address bus will be targeting
42

43
00:03:32,750 --> 00:03:35,310
to the code memory of the microcontroller.
43

44
00:03:35,390 --> 00:03:41,210
So, when the processor puts the processor means the address generation unit of the processor, so there
44

45
00:03:41,210 --> 00:03:48,000
is a address generation unit inside the processor, that will get activated when an instruction is decoded.
45

46
00:03:48,140 --> 00:03:51,200
So, I'm not going into the details of processor architecture.
46

47
00:03:51,200 --> 00:03:56,570
So, when I say processor that means the address generation unit of the processor. When the processor
47

48
00:03:56,600 --> 00:04:01,070
puts an address which belongs to this region on the address bus,
48

49
00:04:01,100 --> 00:04:06,070
so then the address bus will be talking to the data in memory of the microcontroller.
49

50
00:04:06,530 --> 00:04:13,740
So similarly, when the processor puts an address on the address bus which falls in this region, then the
50

51
00:04:13,750 --> 00:04:18,880
address bus will be talking to the peripherals of the microcontroller like that.
51

52
00:04:18,890 --> 00:04:22,580
So, this arrangement is called as Memory map of the processor.
52

53
00:04:22,820 --> 00:04:23,850
So, this is fixed.
53

54
00:04:23,960 --> 00:04:30,770
This is fixed by the ARM Cortex Mx architecture and the microcontroller designers who use ARM Cortex
54

55
00:04:30,860 --> 00:04:34,220
Mx processor in their design should follow this memory map.
55

56
00:04:34,910 --> 00:04:41,660
So, this diagram is also mentioned in the ARM Cortex Mx technical reference manual, so you can explore
56

57
00:04:41,690 --> 00:04:42,680
from the document.
57

58
00:04:43,370 --> 00:04:49,870
So, to conclude this memory map of the processor, so just remember that program memory, data memory,
58

59
00:04:49,880 --> 00:04:57,810
registers of various peripherals are organized within the same linear 4 gigabyte of address space.
59

60
00:04:58,040 --> 00:05:02,800
So, our GPIOD is a peripheral, so GPIOD is a peripheral.
60

61
00:05:02,990 --> 00:05:09,390
So, the addresses of GPIOD registers must fall within this address.
61

62
00:05:09,500 --> 00:05:15,650
So, in a later lectures we'll find out what is a exact address of the GPIOD registers.
62

63
00:05:16,010 --> 00:05:20,920
So, when you have this address at your hand, then your job becomes very easy.
63

64
00:05:21,140 --> 00:05:28,190
So, you already know the pointers. So, treat this as a pointer and you can start writing and reading data
64

65
00:05:28,220 --> 00:05:29,300
from this pointer.
65

66
00:05:29,360 --> 00:05:31,140
So, that's how you control the peripheral.
66

67
00:05:31,610 --> 00:05:39,380
So, now let's do one thing, let's explore the real memory map of the STM32 microcontroller and I
67

68
00:05:39,380 --> 00:05:44,840
will take up this in the next lecture and if you have any doubts it will get clarified maybe in the next
68

69
00:05:44,840 --> 00:05:45,200
lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 4

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:05,430
So, in this case I'm considering the microcontroller STM32F407 which is there on my discovery
1

2
00:00:05,430 --> 00:00:11,220
board, and memory map of the microcontroller you can refer from the data sheet or reference manual of
2

3
00:00:11,220 --> 00:00:12,030
the microcontroller.
3

4
00:00:12,030 --> 00:00:15,750
So, most of the time the memory map will be mentioned in the reference manual.
4

5
00:00:16,230 --> 00:00:22,350
So here I have opened the reference manual, and here if you go to this section memory and bus architecture
5

6
00:00:22,410 --> 00:00:24,110
and here you can get the memory map.
6

7
00:00:24,520 --> 00:00:31,730
So, this is a memory map. So, before programming the peripheral registers of different peripherals you
7

8
00:00:31,730 --> 00:00:33,490
must refer to this table.
8

9
00:00:33,530 --> 00:00:38,770
Otherwise, you'll not be having any idea what are the addresses of the peripheral registers.
9

10
00:00:38,780 --> 00:00:41,970
So, let's take an example of our GPIOD peripheral.
10

11
00:00:42,080 --> 00:00:49,430
We know that in order to control the LED we have to configure the GPIOD peripheral registers.
11

12
00:00:49,430 --> 00:00:54,770
So now, if you browse through this document you will see GPIOD peripheral which is right here.
12

13
00:00:54,800 --> 00:01:03,140
So, this number says that here, so the first register of the GPIOD peripheral is at the address  
13

14
00:01:03,140 --> 00:01:06,170
0x4002 0C00.
14

15
00:01:06,230 --> 00:01:11,320
That's a address of the first register of the GPIOD peripheral.
15

16
00:01:11,320 --> 00:01:12,480
So, what is that register?
16

17
00:01:12,500 --> 00:01:13,690
will explore later.
17

18
00:01:13,940 --> 00:01:19,440
So, you can also say that, this is the base address of GPIOD peripheral registers.
18

19
00:01:19,490 --> 00:01:24,640
So, for example, if you want to know what is the base address of the ADC registers,
19

20
00:01:24,770 --> 00:01:28,020
so then you can explore from this table.
20

21
00:01:28,040 --> 00:01:29,050
So, here it is.
21

22
00:01:29,060 --> 00:01:35,220
So, all ADC1, ADC2, ADC3 registers are placed between these addresses.
22

23
00:01:35,360 --> 00:01:42,320
So, this microcontroller has 3 ADC's. And these are for timers, where it is mentioned like time TIM11,  
23

24
00:01:42,320 --> 00:01:44,650
TIM10, TIM9 those are timers.
24

25
00:01:44,960 --> 00:01:51,650
So, these are UART's. So, it has register space for
25

26
00:01:51,640 --> 00:02:00,790
CAN, I2C, UART like that. If the processor puts this address on the address bus, then the 
26

27
00:02:00,790 --> 00:02:07,600
address bus will be talking to the CAN peripheral registers. So, the Ethernet MAC registers are placed between
27

28
00:02:07,810 --> 00:02:14,230
a these addresses. So, the registers used to control and configure the Ethernet MAC peripheral and
28

29
00:02:14,320 --> 00:02:21,460
registers used to read data from and write data into Ethernet MAC peripheral are mapped in this address
29

30
00:02:21,460 --> 00:02:22,530
range.
30

31
00:02:22,840 --> 00:02:31,720
So, now this is a memory map of lpc48xx microcontroller which is designed by NXP.
31

32
00:02:31,810 --> 00:02:35,790
So, this microcontroller is also based on ARM Cortex Mx processor.
32

33
00:02:35,890 --> 00:02:42,520
So, here also you can see that, there is a memory map table in the reference manual of lpc48xx user
33

34
00:02:42,520 --> 00:02:52,240
manual. And here you can see that, this memory map says that 00 to 0x1FFFFFFF is for on chip
34

35
00:02:52,300 --> 00:02:53,590
non-volatile memory,
35

36
00:02:53,620 --> 00:02:59,800
that's actually a flash memory. And the RAM, that is the data memory starts from this address, the Boot
36

37
00:02:59,800 --> 00:03:09,700
ROM starts from this address, and AHB peripherals start from this address, and APB peripherals start from this address
37

38
00:03:09,700 --> 00:03:10,480
onwards.
38

39
00:03:10,660 --> 00:03:13,210
So, it doesn't matter which microcontroller you use.
39

40
00:03:13,240 --> 00:03:18,310
So, just open up your user manual and you should be able to locate the memory map, without the memory
40

41
00:03:18,310 --> 00:03:18,640
map
41

42
00:03:18,640 --> 00:03:20,730
you cannot program the peripherals.
42

43
00:03:20,980 --> 00:03:22,360
Let's move forward.
43

44
00:03:22,360 --> 00:03:27,130
So, what is the address range of GPIOD peripheral registers? So, we just found out this is a address
44

45
00:03:27,130 --> 00:03:29,040
range of GPIOD peripherals
45

46
00:03:29,050 --> 00:03:34,090
and if you are using different MCU, then please refer to your device reference manual to obtain the
46

47
00:03:34,090 --> 00:03:35,420
correct address range.
47

48
00:03:35,440 --> 00:03:38,580
So, this is for STM32F407 microcontroller.
48

49
00:03:38,620 --> 00:03:43,510
So, if you are using some other microcontroller, it  doesn't matter whether it is from same vendor or different
49

50
00:03:43,510 --> 00:03:44,020
vendor
50

51
00:03:44,050 --> 00:03:47,280
I would highly recommend you to cross-check these numbers.
51

52
00:03:47,320 --> 00:03:48,150
It may be different.
52

53
00:03:48,180 --> 00:03:52,320
So, you have to check in the reference manual. So, don't code blindly.
53

54
00:03:52,360 --> 00:03:55,520
So, in the next lecture let's understand about peripheral registers.
54

55
00:03:55,570 --> 00:03:56,530
I will see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 5

0
1
00:00:00,150 --> 00:00:06,820
So, in this lecture let's understand about peripheral registers. So, all peripheral registers in STM32
1

2
00:00:06,820 --> 00:00:14,350
microcontroller are of 32 bits wide. So, different peripherals have different number of peripheral registers.
2

3
00:00:14,370 --> 00:00:22,560
For example, a GPIO peripheral may have 10 registers, ADC may have 15, I2C may have 20, so that depends
3

4
00:00:22,560 --> 00:00:28,540
upon the complexity of the peripheral. Simple peripheral may have less number of peripheral registers.
4

5
00:00:28,680 --> 00:00:35,670
So, a complex peripheral like Timer, or Ethernet MAC, CAN may have more number of registers. So, you should
5

6
00:00:35,670 --> 00:00:40,830
never assume about the address of the peripheral registers. Always you should refer to the device reference
6

7
00:00:40,830 --> 00:00:41,560
manual.So,
7

8
00:00:41,800 --> 00:00:50,340
these are the peripheral registers of GPIOD peripheral. So, GPIOA also has the same set of registers,
8

9
00:00:50,370 --> 00:00:57,560
GPIOB also, GPIOC also, but I'm considering GPIOD because we'll be working on GPIOD peripheral.
9

10
00:00:57,840 --> 00:01:06,390
So, it has up to 10 registers, each register is of 32 bits. So, the GPIO mode register is used to control
10

11
00:01:06,390 --> 00:01:13,140
the mode of an IO pin, whether you want to drive that IO pin as an input or output.
11

12
00:01:13,140 --> 00:01:19,770
For example, if you are driving an LED, then you probably configuring the mode of an IO pin as
12

13
00:01:19,770 --> 00:01:24,180
output, if you are using an IO pin to connect a button,
13

14
00:01:24,180 --> 00:01:30,660
in that case you probably want to configure the IO pin as input. So, all those configurations you
14

15
00:01:30,660 --> 00:01:34,860
do using the mode register of the GPIO port.
15

16
00:01:34,860 --> 00:01:38,440
So, now after that there is something call output type register.
16

17
00:01:38,460 --> 00:01:45,810
So, which decides what should be the output type of an IO pin, if it is in output mode. And also there
17

18
00:01:45,810 --> 00:01:52,800
is one important register to activate or deactivate the internal pull-up resistors for the IO pins.
18

19
00:01:53,670 --> 00:02:01,350
And if you want to read data from the IO pins, then you probably would be using a input data register.
19

20
00:02:01,440 --> 00:02:09,840
So, this is used to read data from the IO pins. So, the output data register is used to write data into
20

21
00:02:09,840 --> 00:02:11,400
the IO pins.
21

22
00:02:11,400 --> 00:02:18,970
So, in order to turn on or turn off an LED you probably using output data register. Because, through
22

23
00:02:18,990 --> 00:02:26,490
the output data register we actually write data into the IO pins. And there are other registers so we
23

24
00:02:26,490 --> 00:02:28,920
will not be using for this exercise,
24

25
00:02:28,920 --> 00:02:32,100
so that's why, need not to explore all registers.
25

26
00:02:32,100 --> 00:02:39,420
So, for this exercise I think you should be using mode register and output data register.
26

27
00:02:39,480 --> 00:02:43,740
This is to configure the mode and this is to write data.
27

28
00:02:43,860 --> 00:02:49,860
For example, if you want to turn on the LED, then you have to write 1 into the output data register.
28

29
00:02:49,890 --> 00:02:51,570
So, if you want to turn off the LED,
29

30
00:02:51,570 --> 00:02:53,340
then you have to write 0.
30

31
00:02:53,490 --> 00:02:55,210
So, we'll see that when we do programming.
31

32
00:02:55,380 --> 00:02:56,510
Let's move forward.
32

33
00:02:56,520 --> 00:03:00,320
So, these are the register sets of the GPIOD peripheral.
33

34
00:03:00,720 --> 00:03:05,470
So, all these registers everything is implemented inside the microcontroller.
34

35
00:03:05,490 --> 00:03:07,500
Let's see how they are arranged.
35

36
00:03:07,560 --> 00:03:14,880
For example, the first register you find in the address range of GPIOD peripheral registers is
36

37
00:03:14,970 --> 00:03:16,270
GPIO mode register.
37

38
00:03:16,290 --> 00:03:23,910
So, that's a very first register you find at the address 0x4002_0C00.
38

39
00:03:23,910 --> 00:03:25,550
So, that's a first register.
39

40
00:03:25,590 --> 00:03:29,070
As I said each register is of 32 bits.
40

41
00:03:29,070 --> 00:03:38,220
So, that's why, this register consumes these many memory locations. So, 0C00 to 0C03
41

42
00:03:38,250 --> 00:03:41,110
so that is actually 32 bit width.
42

43
00:03:41,350 --> 00:03:49,400
So, the GPIOD port output type register is a next register, so which starts from this address onwards.
43

44
00:03:49,410 --> 00:03:52,360
So, like that this peripheral has 10 registers
44

45
00:03:52,410 --> 00:03:58,290
and so these are the addresses of the individual registers.
45

46
00:03:58,350 --> 00:04:00,520
So, this is the address of mode register,
46

47
00:04:00,690 --> 00:04:03,960
so this is the address of output type register, like that.
47

48
00:04:03,990 --> 00:04:08,340
So, if you just add +4 to this you get the address of the next register.
48

49
00:04:08,520 --> 00:04:12,570
So, if you add +4 to this you get the address of the next register.
49

50
00:04:15,570 --> 00:04:17,050
Let's move forward.
50

51
00:04:17,050 --> 00:04:20,700
So now, how do you control the output state of the GPIO pin?
51

52
00:04:20,740 --> 00:04:26,420
So, you must be using GPIO output data register, because through the output data register we send data
52

53
00:04:26,420 --> 00:04:28,960
to the IO pins. For example,
53

54
00:04:28,960 --> 00:04:31,860
so you can easily calculate the address of this register,
54

55
00:04:31,900 --> 00:04:33,930
so  you already know how to calculate that.
55

56
00:04:34,000 --> 00:04:40,780
So, the 0th bit position of this register controls the 0th pin of that port.
56

57
00:04:40,780 --> 00:04:44,890
So, that is this bit field controls PD0.
57

58
00:04:45,010 --> 00:04:52,300
So, if you make this bit field as high that is 1, if you write 1 here, then that makes the state of the
58

59
00:04:52,300 --> 00:04:57,190
PD0 as high, that means PD0 will be pull to +VCC.
59

60
00:04:57,360 --> 00:05:05,450
So, that is actually 3.3 volt. So, if you make this bit field 0, then PD0 will be pull
60

61
00:05:05,450 --> 00:05:08,510
to ground, it is like driven to ground 0 volt.
61

62
00:05:08,900 --> 00:05:09,980
So, like that.
62

63
00:05:09,980 --> 00:05:14,380
So, now tell me how do you control the PD12 where our LED is connected.
63

64
00:05:14,870 --> 00:05:21,080
So, you should be touching the 12th bit of this register, if you want to turn on the LED make the 12th bit
64

65
00:05:21,260 --> 00:05:22,790
position as 1,
65

66
00:05:23,110 --> 00:05:28,220
so if you want to turn off the LED make the 12th bit position of this register as 0.
66

67
00:05:28,390 --> 00:05:33,830
That's all you have to do. So, in the next lecture let's understand the procedure to turn on the LED and
67

68
00:05:33,830 --> 00:05:34,970
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 6

0
1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,620
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,620 --> 00:00:07,050
So, in this lecture onwards let's understand procedure to turn on the LED. To write a code to turn
2

3
00:00:07,050 --> 00:00:08,830
on the LED is not that easy. So,
3

4
00:00:09,040 --> 00:00:14,820
that's what makes the embedded system programming so unique that you must know so many things such as memory
4

5
00:00:14,820 --> 00:00:19,690
map, peripheral registers, register addresses, hardware connections, etc..
5

6
00:00:19,890 --> 00:00:26,100
So, that's why, please be patient so until I cover all the required things to do this exercise.
6

7
00:00:26,100 --> 00:00:28,410
So, now this is a procedure to turn on the LED.
7

8
00:00:28,440 --> 00:00:34,560
So, first of all you have to identify the GPIO port, that's a peripheral which is used to connect the LED.
8

9
00:00:35,070 --> 00:00:37,050
So and we have identified that.
9

10
00:00:37,080 --> 00:00:38,800
So, that is GPIOD.
10

11
00:00:38,820 --> 00:00:45,300
And after that, identify the GPIO  pin where the LED is connected and we also identified that pin
11

12
00:00:45,300 --> 00:00:54,930
number 12 of the Port D, isn't it? The third procedure is activate the GPIOD peripheral. By
12

13
00:00:54,930 --> 00:00:57,220
default in the microcontroller
13

14
00:00:57,240 --> 00:00:59,430
most of the peripherals are dead,
14

15
00:00:59,430 --> 00:01:06,750
you have to activate them. So, activating a peripheral is nothing but enabling the clock for them.
15

16
00:01:06,750 --> 00:01:14,010
So, until you enable the clock for a peripheral, the peripheral is dead and it is neither functions nor it
16

17
00:01:14,010 --> 00:01:19,980
takes any configuration values set by you. You cannot configure the peripheral until you activate the
17

18
00:01:19,980 --> 00:01:20,470
clock.
18

19
00:01:21,210 --> 00:01:26,430
So, once you activate the clock for a peripheral, the peripheral is ready to take your configuration and
19

20
00:01:26,430 --> 00:01:29,140
control-related commands or arguments,
20

21
00:01:29,190 --> 00:01:36,420
what we call as configuration values. For some microcontrollers, the peripheral maybe ON by default, and
21

22
00:01:36,420 --> 00:01:38,170
you need not to do any activation.
22

23
00:01:38,270 --> 00:01:43,830
So, this actually depends upon what kind of microcontroller you are using, and who is the vendor.
23

24
00:01:43,830 --> 00:01:47,420
So, different vendor adopt different design goals.
24

25
00:01:47,490 --> 00:01:54,030
So, that's why, in almost all the microcontrollers designed by ST microelectronics, the peripherals are dead
25

26
00:01:54,060 --> 00:01:54,870
by default,
26

27
00:01:54,930 --> 00:01:56,520
you should enable the clock first.
27

28
00:01:56,520 --> 00:01:56,850
All right.
28

29
00:01:56,850 --> 00:02:04,860
So, the next step is configure the GPIO pin mode as output. Since you are driving an LED (to ON or OFF),
29

30
00:02:04,920 --> 00:02:11,800
the operation mode of the GPIO pin has to be configured as OUTPUT. Write to the GPIO pin.
30

31
00:02:11,820 --> 00:02:19,590
So, you have to write 1 to make the GPIO pin state HIGH, and you have to write 0 to make the  
31

32
00:02:19,590 --> 00:02:20,810
GPIO pin state LOW.
32

33
00:02:20,850 --> 00:02:22,030
That is 0 Volt.
33

34
00:02:23,120 --> 00:02:25,710
So, this is a summary of the procedure,
34

35
00:02:25,730 --> 00:02:32,090
so first one is identify the GPIO port used to connect LED, identify the GPIO pin number, activate
35

36
00:02:32,120 --> 00:02:39,260
the GPIOD peripheral that is enabling the clock, configure the mode as output, and write to the GPIO
36

37
00:02:39,260 --> 00:02:39,650
pin.
37

38
00:02:40,190 --> 00:02:46,910
So, the next question is obviously how to enable the peripheral clock? and will understand this
38

39
00:02:46,910 --> 00:02:47,710
in the next lecture.

6
00:00:23,340 --> 00:00:26,780

7

7

8

address range in the memory map of 
8

9
00:00:36,180 --> 00:00:44,720
the microcontroller. So, in STM32 microcontroller, there is an engine called RCC, where RCC stands for reset and 
9

10
00:00:44,730 --> 00:00:52,140
clock control. This RCC engine takes care of controlling clocks for various part of the microcontroller
10

11

such as processor,different peripherals, different buses, 
11

12
00:00:56,370 --> 00:01:04,440
memories, etc.. So, the RCC engine has its own set of addresses to control the clock and all those
12

13
00:01:04,500 --> 00:01:06,690
registers are mapped at this 
13

14
00:01:06,690 --> 00:01:11,700
address range.So, now you have to go to the RCC section 
14

15
00:01:11,700 --> 00:01:16,880
and you have to explore what are the different registers are available to activate the
15

16
00:01:16,980 --> 00:01:24,060
clock.So, in our case all you have to do is select a appropriate register in the RCC
16

17
00:01:24,060 --> 00:01:25,830
block to enable the clock
17

18
00:01:26,040 --> 00:01:29,880
for our peripheral. So, our peripheral of interest is 
18

GPIOD peripheral.

00:01:31,290 --> 00:01:32,850
So, let's do that. Let's go to the

22

clock control and I am interested in the RCC registers. So, RCC has

23
00:01:47,840 --> 00:01:53,090
various registers you can see here. RCC clock control register, PLL

24

configuration register, so if you just browse through this section you will get something like peripheral clock 
24

25
00:02:00,170 --> 00:02:02,840
enable registers which is right here,  peripheral clock 
25

26
00:02:03,510 --> 00:02:10,970
enable register. So, AHB1 peripheral clock register is used to enable the clocks for those peripherals 
26

27
00:02:11,030 --> 00:02:13,160
which are hanging on the 
27

28
00:02:13,160 --> 00:02:20,000
AHB1 bus. So, in our case the GPIOD peripheral is actually hanging on the
28

29
00:02:20,000 --> 00:02:22,730
AHB1 bus.So, if you are thinking how come I

30

know this information, so this information you can get from the data sheet of the microcontroller. Let me

of the

microcontroller. All right.So, I'm just referring to the data sheet of this a

34

microcontroller and when I browse through this document, so I here find the internal architecture of this

microcontroller. So, if you can

this is a processor and this is a peripheral

38
00:02:49,130 --> 00:02:50,960
GPIOD. And look how they 
38

39
00:02:51,800 --> 00:02:53,030
communicate.

40
00:02:53,060 --> 00:03:00,290
So, here is a thing, this GPIOD peripheral is actually connected to the AHB1 bus and then that is
40

41
00:03:00,350 --> 00:03:01,570
further connected

42
00:03:01,570 --> 00:03:09,110
to the processor via this bus-matrix. So, that's why, I can say that GPIOD is actually 
42

43
00:03:09,110 --> 00:03:09,950
connected to the 
43

44
00:03:10,160 --> 00:03:14,140
AHB1 bus.So, take a look into this architecture diagram so you'll
44

45
00:03:14,270 --> 00:03:21,890
understand more.For example, if I want to understand where exactly is ADC is connected, then ADC is
45

46
00:03:21,920 --> 00:03:29,120
not directly connected to the AHB bus. It is actually connected to the APB bus. APB is a slower bus compared to the 
46

47
00:03:29,120 --> 00:03:34,880
AHB bus and there is a bridge and through that bridge it is connected to the.AHB bus.

48
00:03:34,940 --> 00:03:37,600
So, this is a simplified 
48

49
00:03:37,670 --> 00:03:44,840
diagram actually.So, here there is a AHB bus and the data exchange between peripheral and memory happens over this bus.
49

50
00:03:48,220 --> 00:03:52,380
Let's go back to our RCC Register's 
50

51
00:03:52,420 --> 00:03:59,740
discussion. So, in the RCC registers we should be looking at RCC AHB1 peripheral clock register.
51

52
00:03:59,740 --> 00:04:07,140
Because, this register controls clocks for all the peripherals which are hanging on the AHB1 bus.
52

53
00:04:07,150 --> 00:04:09,430
So now, what is the 
53

54
00:04:09,550 --> 00:04:16,330
address of this register? So, let's calculate that. Here you can see that, this documentation says that offset is 
54

55
00:04:16,360 --> 00:04:23,630
0x30. So, that means you have to add this offset to the base address of the RCC peripheral
55

56
00:04:23,650 --> 00:04:27,480
registers.So, what is the base address of the RCC 
56

57
00:04:27,490 --> 00:04:31,390
peripheral registers? So, this is the base address of the RCC peripheral 
57

58
00:04:31,840 --> 00:04:36,250
registers.So, this information again you get from the memory map of the 
58

59
00:04:36,250 --> 00:04:37,840
microcontroller. 
59

60
00:04:37,840 --> 00:04:41,290
So, let's open the memory map and here you find RCC, 
60

61
00:04:41,290 --> 00:04:42,360
right here. So, 
61

62
00:04:42,820 --> 00:04:49,060
this is a base address. So, you have to add this offset to that base address, to get the address of
62

63
00:04:49,780 --> 00:04:55,600
this register.And here you have to activate or set the 
63

64
00:04:55,810 --> 00:05:00,850
3rd bit position. You can see that, this is a bit position to turn on the clock for 
64

65
00:05:01,150 --> 00:05:03,690
GPIOD peripheral. So, now let's explore the 
65

66
00:05:03,820 --> 00:05:04,320
3rd bit 
66

67
00:05:04,330 --> 00:05:09,250
position.Here it is. It is written here GPIOD enable, IO port D 
67

68
00:05:09,730 --> 00:05:12,100
clock enable. So, this bit is set and
68

69
00:05:12,340 --> 00:05:20,320
cleared by the software.So, if you make this bit as 1, the IO port D clock will be enabled. So similarly,  if you set 
69

70
00:05:20,320 --> 00:05:23,200
this bit field, then GPIO port A 
70

71
00:05:23,290 --> 00:05:28,890
clock will be enabled.It's like that. And also you have to pay attention to the reset value of 
71

72
00:05:29,020 --> 00:05:33,760
this register.So, that's about how you enable the clock for a 
72

73
00:05:33,760 --> 00:05:39,970
particular peripheral. So, first you have to identify which register you have to use in the RCC block to 
73

74
00:05:39,970 --> 00:05:43,710
control the clock and then you have to program the appropriate

75

bit fields. So, with that note I would like to end this lecture and in the next lecture let's

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

0
1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,200
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,200 --> 00:00:07,150
So, in the previous lecture you understood about how to enable the peripheral clock of GPIOD peripheral.
2

3
00:00:07,380 --> 00:00:16,020
So, we do that by using the RCC register and the RCC register in our case is AHB1ENR. Let's calculate
3

4
00:00:16,050 --> 00:00:18,310
the address of the AHB1ENR.
4

5
00:00:18,330 --> 00:00:20,920
So, that happens to be 0 cross
5

6
00:00:21,170 --> 00:00:26,610
so first we have to take the base address and then we have to add this offset.
6

7
00:00:26,610 --> 00:00:31,040
So, what is a base address?
7

8
00:00:31,070 --> 00:00:32,870
So, this is the base address.
8

9
00:00:32,900 --> 00:00:34,600
So, let's copy this.
9

10
00:00:34,700 --> 00:00:38,680
Let's document it, so +30, 0x30
10

11
00:00:38,770 --> 00:00:42,840
that's a offset. We will get this number.
11

12
00:00:43,180 --> 00:00:45,600
Address of the GPIO mode register,
12

13
00:00:45,640 --> 00:00:48,790
so this we need in order to control the mode.
13

14
00:00:48,850 --> 00:00:52,540
So, now by the way what is the base address of the GPIOD peripheral register?
14

15
00:00:52,540 --> 00:00:54,490
So, first let's document that,
15

16
00:00:54,580 --> 00:00:57,160
so GPIOD is actually this one.
16

17
00:00:57,160 --> 00:00:58,170
So, let's copy that.
17

18
00:00:59,420 --> 00:01:04,880
So, this is a base address, for that you have to add the offset if you want to get the offset you have
18

19
00:01:04,880 --> 00:01:11,720
to go to the GPIO section. And here go to registers, and the first register is mode register. Here, you have
19

20
00:01:11,720 --> 00:01:17,950
to configure the mode. You can see that by reset this register is 0 for Port D.
20

21
00:01:18,140 --> 00:01:25,280
So, it is non-zero only for port A and port B, but for port D this registers reset value is 0.
21

22
00:01:26,000 --> 00:01:33,340
And here in this register you can see that 32 bits are divided into 16 sections.
22

23
00:01:33,410 --> 00:01:34,990
So, this is a first section,
23

24
00:01:35,030 --> 00:01:42,050
this is a second section, and this is the 15th section. So, every 2 bits are used to configure
24

25
00:01:42,080 --> 00:01:52,010
the mode for 1 pin of the port, each port has 16 pins. So, the mode for all those pins are controlled
25

26
00:01:52,100 --> 00:01:53,660
using this register.
26

27
00:01:53,900 --> 00:01:59,760
So, in this register every two bits is given to control the mode for a single pin.
27

28
00:01:59,960 --> 00:02:06,590
So, why two bits are dedicated? Because, there are 4 different modes.So, input, general purpose output,
28

29
00:02:06,740 --> 00:02:15,570
alternate function, and analog mode. For this exercise we will be keeping the pin in output mode. So, that's
29

30
00:02:15,570 --> 00:02:21,440
why, what you have to do is, you have to go to the appropriate section here. So, for pin-12
30

31
00:02:21,480 --> 00:02:26,250
we should be looking at these bit fields 24 and 25.
31

32
00:02:30,520 --> 00:02:34,630
And here you have to program the value 0 1.
32

33
00:02:34,810 --> 00:02:41,940
So, if you do that the pin number 12 of the GPIOD peripheral will be in output mode.
33

34
00:02:42,160 --> 00:02:43,670
Let's calculate the address.
34

35
00:02:43,720 --> 00:02:45,600
So, address offset is 0.
35

36
00:02:45,640 --> 00:02:52,060
So, we get this address. Next calculate the address of the GPIOD output data register,
36

37
00:02:52,060 --> 00:02:55,200
this is used to write to the IO pins.
37

38
00:02:55,380 --> 00:02:59,030
Let's again explore about other GPIO registers.
38

39
00:02:59,060 --> 00:02:59,920
So, here it is.
39

40
00:03:00,520 --> 00:03:03,050
So, GPIO output data register.
40

41
00:03:03,100 --> 00:03:05,560
So, this is also a 32 bit register,
41

42
00:03:05,560 --> 00:03:09,880
but here from 16 to 31 bits are not used they are reserved,
42

43
00:03:10,120 --> 00:03:13,260
so only 16 bits are implemented.
43

44
00:03:13,330 --> 00:03:18,490
So, these bit fields are used to control the different pins of a port.
44

45
00:03:18,520 --> 00:03:23,960
So, in this case, the bit 0 controls the 0th pin of the port.
45

46
00:03:24,010 --> 00:03:26,260
So, this bit field controls the first pin,
46

47
00:03:26,410 --> 00:03:30,290
so this is for the second pin, all the way to 15th pin.
47

48
00:03:30,400 --> 00:03:36,190
So, here we will be programming one into the bit field 12 in order to make LED high
48

49
00:03:36,250 --> 00:03:37,890
and this is the offset.
49

50
00:03:37,990 --> 00:03:40,000
So, let's calculate the address.
50

51
00:03:40,000 --> 00:03:45,890
So, now let's go back to our documentation and let's copy this and paste here.
51

52
00:03:45,940 --> 00:03:47,740
So, the address is 14.
52

53
00:03:47,770 --> 00:03:51,080
So, now you have everything you have the addresses.
53

54
00:03:51,100 --> 00:03:58,180
So, what I want you to do is, create pointer variables to store these addresses and then activate the
54

55
00:03:58,180 --> 00:03:59,940
appropriate bit fields.
55

56
00:04:00,320 --> 00:04:06,120
So, here to enable the clock, here to select the mode, and here to make LED high
56

57
00:04:06,220 --> 00:04:10,870
and when you execute the program you should observe that your LED getting turned on.
57

58
00:04:11,380 --> 00:04:16,690
So, I would leave this as an exercise to you, and try to complete the exercise
58

59
00:04:16,690 --> 00:04:18,790
and I will implement this in the next lecture.

ctrlreg.

ENR. Next step is configure the mode of the IO pin as output.

114

00:14:28,490 --> 00:14:29,510
We are going to set it.
214

215
00:14:30,110 --> 00:14:30,860
That's correct.
215

216
00:14:30,860 --> 00:14:33,170
So, 24th and 25th, 01.
216

217
00:14:33,170 --> 00:14:35,530
So, the pin is now in the output mode.
217

218
00:14:35,540 --> 00:14:40,300
Now the last step is output Reg, and output Reg is here ODR,
218

219
00:14:40,490 --> 00:14:43,320
and the 12th bit we want to  observe this bit.
219

220
00:14:43,610 --> 00:14:48,200
And if you make that 1, LED should turn on and let's see.
220

221
00:14:48,230 --> 00:14:48,990
Yes,
221

222
00:14:49,000 --> 00:14:50,360
LED is indeed turn on.
222

223
00:14:51,010 --> 00:14:56,900
So, congratulations! you just programmed everything from scratch in order to turn on an LED.
223

224
00:14:56,900 --> 00:15:02,690
So, here you didn't use any inbuilt functions or anything like that. So, you just coded by taking the
224

225
00:15:02,690 --> 00:15:06,440
pointers of the peripheral registers.
225

226
00:15:06,460 --> 00:15:12,490
Now, you can apply this trick to any microcontroller you have if the registers are memory mapped, then
226

227
00:15:12,520 --> 00:15:18,690
you can follow this method and with the help of pointers you can touch and configure any peripheral registers.
227

228
00:15:18,700 --> 00:15:21,310
So, with that note I would like to end this video,
228

229
00:15:21,350 --> 00:15:22,810
hope you like this video
229

230
00:15:22,810 --> 00:15:28,630
and if you are facing issues, so you can check various things, check whether you are really enabled
230

231
00:15:28,630 --> 00:15:29,410
clock or not,
231

232
00:15:29,440 --> 00:15:31,880
whether you are mask values are correct or not,
232

233
00:15:31,930 --> 00:15:38,800
and various other things. And in the next lecture, let's see how to find out these mask values using
233

234
00:15:38,800 --> 00:15:44,620
some shortcuts. Because, you just saw that finding out this mask value is a tedious process.
234

235
00:15:44,650 --> 00:15:50,500
We have to go to every bit field, and then you have to calculate what is a mask value, and then we have
235

236
00:15:50,500 --> 00:15:53,220
to program, which is actually a very tedious process.
236

237
00:15:53,470 --> 00:15:56,420
So, we'll see whether there is any shortcut or not.
237

238
00:15:56,470 --> 00:16:02,830
So, to understand the shortcut, first we have to understand the bitwise shift operators. So, in the next
238

239
00:16:02,830 --> 00:16:05,740
lecture I will cover the bitwise shift operators.


==================================================================================


section 19--Bitwise Shift Operations

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:02,480
Hey, welcome back to the course and in this lecture
1

2
00:00:02,490 --> 00:00:06,340
let's explore about the remaining bitwise operators in 'C'.
2

3
00:00:06,390 --> 00:00:11,930
So, that is bitwise right shift operator and bitwise left shift operator. First
3

4
00:00:11,940 --> 00:00:17,790
let's get started with bitwise right shift operator. So, this is operator symbol for bitwise right
4

5
00:00:17,790 --> 00:00:21,130
shift operator. This operator takes two operands
5

6
00:00:21,150 --> 00:00:22,700
this is how you write?
6

7
00:00:22,710 --> 00:00:23,810
So, this is a syntax.
7

8
00:00:23,830 --> 00:00:31,200
So, operand1 >> operand2. So, bits of the first operand will be right shifted
8

9
00:00:31,290 --> 00:00:34,380
by the amount decided by the second operand.
9

10
00:00:34,410 --> 00:00:37,150
So, that's how the shift operator works.
10

11
00:00:37,230 --> 00:00:38,160
Let's see an example.
11

12
00:00:38,490 --> 00:00:45,930
Let's say you have a variable a, so which is initialized with a value let's say 111. And you have to
12

13
00:00:45,930 --> 00:00:50,750
find out the value of 'a' which is right shifted by 4.
13

14
00:00:50,760 --> 00:00:56,370
So, this is the amount, this is the operand 2, and this is the value, which needs to be right shifted. Let's
14

15
00:00:56,370 --> 00:00:57,170
analyze this.
15

16
00:00:57,180 --> 00:01:05,700
So, I write 'a' in the binary format, so this is a binary format of 111, so which is encoded in 8 bits.
16

17
00:01:05,760 --> 00:01:08,890
So, here 'a' must be right shifted by four times.
17

18
00:01:09,750 --> 00:01:11,190
Let's see how it is done.
18

19
00:01:11,250 --> 00:01:13,600
First of all this is the original number 111.
19

20
00:01:14,130 --> 00:01:18,100
First let's see what happens when you right shift by 1.
20

21
00:01:18,150 --> 00:01:22,080
So, let's start with the least significant bit position.
21

22
00:01:22,140 --> 00:01:29,700
So, this will be right shifted. Right shifted means in this direction to your right. So, one is shifted
22

23
00:01:29,700 --> 00:01:30,210
out.
23

24
00:01:30,300 --> 00:01:33,190
So, that means this bit position is lost.
24

25
00:01:33,210 --> 00:01:40,050
And after that, this bit position is shifted to this bit position, this bit position is shifted to its right,
25

26
00:01:40,260 --> 00:01:43,290
like that. Bits will get shifted to that right.
26

27
00:01:43,950 --> 00:01:45,240
So, that's why, here
27

28
00:01:45,240 --> 00:01:47,370
this MSB will be shifted here.
28

29
00:01:47,400 --> 00:01:55,360
So, that means this bit position is now empty and empty bit position will be filled with zeros. So, 0 will be
29

30
00:01:55,360 --> 00:01:57,670
shifted into the empty position, like that.
30

31
00:01:57,670 --> 00:02:02,520
So, this is a result after you shift the original number by 1,
31

32
00:02:02,710 --> 00:02:05,780
and after that again you shift these bits by 1.
32

33
00:02:05,830 --> 00:02:07,360
So, you get this number.
33

34
00:02:07,360 --> 00:02:12,010
So, this is a result after you shift the original number by two.
34

35
00:02:12,100 --> 00:02:16,440
And here we can see that you actually lost these two bits here.
35

36
00:02:16,570 --> 00:02:17,140
Correct?
36

37
00:02:17,140 --> 00:02:23,110
So, like that this is a result after you right shift the original value by 4
37

38
00:02:23,200 --> 00:02:27,940
and here you can see that these 4 bits are actually lost in the process.
38

39
00:02:27,970 --> 00:02:35,860
So, 110 they actually occupied the least significant bit positions, and the remaining bit positions are actually
39

40
00:02:35,860 --> 00:02:37,300
filled with zeros.
40

41
00:02:37,900 --> 00:02:43,420
So, if you want to a quickly find out what happens when you shift a number by 4, then you need not
41

42
00:02:43,420 --> 00:02:44,700
to analyze like this.
42

43
00:02:44,710 --> 00:02:46,210
So, there is a shortcut.
43

44
00:02:46,330 --> 00:02:47,500
First of all take the original
44

45
00:02:47,500 --> 00:02:48,190
number
45

46
00:02:48,190 --> 00:02:51,130
and you want to right shift by 4, isn't it?
46

47
00:02:51,130 --> 00:02:53,260
So, that's why, create a block of 4 bits.
47

48
00:02:53,530 --> 00:02:59,410
So, this is a block1 and this is a block2. So, block1 is collection of 4 bits and block2
48

49
00:02:59,410 --> 00:03:03,650
is collection of 4 bits. And then shift this block out,
49

50
00:03:03,690 --> 00:03:04,970
so this is lost.
50

51
00:03:05,140 --> 00:03:12,730
So, shift this block in, so that actually like this. So, block 2 comes here and for the remaining bits
51

52
00:03:12,880 --> 00:03:14,550
you just write zeros.
52

53
00:03:14,620 --> 00:03:16,910
You can also analyze like this.
53

54
00:03:16,960 --> 00:03:20,690
So, for example, let's say you want to shift 'a' by three.
54

55
00:03:20,710 --> 00:03:23,340
So, create blocks of three bits.
55

56
00:03:23,350 --> 00:03:25,610
So, this is block1, block2,
56

57
00:03:25,720 --> 00:03:27,350
and this is block3,
57

58
00:03:27,380 --> 00:03:29,590
and in the block3 you can keep only 2 bits.
58

59
00:03:30,190 --> 00:03:31,470
So, this is lost
59

60
00:03:31,540 --> 00:03:37,660
and this comes here, and this comes here. And since 3 bits are lost,
60

61
00:03:37,660 --> 00:03:40,210
so there will be three empty places,
61

62
00:03:40,210 --> 00:03:41,690
so make them zeros.
62

63
00:03:41,710 --> 00:03:42,640
That's it.
63

64
00:03:42,640 --> 00:03:47,140
So, this is a result you get after you right shift 'a' by three.
64

65
00:03:47,140 --> 00:03:49,450
So, that's how right shift happens.
65

66
00:03:49,450 --> 00:03:52,570
So, in the next lecture let's discuss about the left shift operator.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:03,380
So, in the previous lecture you understood about the right shift operator,
1

2
00:00:03,390 --> 00:00:05,850
now let's understand the left shift operator.
2

3
00:00:05,970 --> 00:00:11,580
So, this is a symbol you use for left shift operator, and this is similar to right shift
3

4
00:00:11,580 --> 00:00:17,670
the only thing is operand, the bits of the first operand will be left shifted by the amount decided
4

5
00:00:17,670 --> 00:00:19,020
by the second operand.
5

6
00:00:19,020 --> 00:00:20,220
This is a syntax.
6

7
00:00:20,280 --> 00:00:21,210
Let's see an example.
7

8
00:00:21,240 --> 00:00:25,840
So, let's say this is the number which is represented in binary format.
8

9
00:00:25,890 --> 00:00:29,510
So, now let's understand what happens when you left shifted by 1.
9

10
00:00:29,520 --> 00:00:30,890
Let's start with a MSB.
10

11
00:00:30,900 --> 00:00:34,200
So, this is actually left shifted by 1.
11

12
00:00:34,290 --> 00:00:35,720
So, that is lost.
12

13
00:00:35,730 --> 00:00:37,140
So, this is moved out.
13

14
00:00:37,140 --> 00:00:39,870
So, this bit position will be left shifted,
14

15
00:00:39,870 --> 00:00:46,320
this will be shifted to its left like that. And this becomes an empty space where zero will be shifted
15

16
00:00:46,320 --> 00:00:46,470
in.
16

17
00:00:46,590 --> 00:00:51,860
So, that's why, this is a result after you left shift this number by 1.
17

18
00:00:52,020 --> 00:00:54,580
So, you should observe that 0 occupied the LSB.
18

19
00:00:54,930 --> 00:00:57,790
So, this is a result after you left shift by 2,
19

20
00:00:57,810 --> 00:01:05,380
this is a result after you left shift by 3, and after you left shift by 4, you get this result.
20

21
00:01:05,400 --> 00:01:09,710
So, here you can see that the first 4 a least significant bits
21

22
00:01:09,720 --> 00:01:18,270
this is a lower nibble, which is actually to the higher nibble side, and these four higher bits are lost
22

23
00:01:18,660 --> 00:01:21,600
and 4 zeros actually occupied the lower number.
23

24
00:01:22,500 --> 00:01:28,650
And also you should note that  original value was 111, now it is increased. In the previous case, that
24

25
00:01:28,650 --> 00:01:30,450
is in the case of right shift operation
25

26
00:01:30,450 --> 00:01:31,780
the value was decreased.
26

27
00:01:32,130 --> 00:01:34,060
So, the result was 6 in that case.So,
27

28
00:01:34,230 --> 00:01:36,880
here the final value is increased.
28

29
00:01:36,900 --> 00:01:39,210
So, that's a difference between left shift and right
29

30
00:01:39,220 --> 00:01:42,000
shift. When you do left shift on a number
30

31
00:01:42,000 --> 00:01:44,550
so the weight of the number will increase.
31

32
00:01:44,850 --> 00:01:48,010
For example, here 4 is left shifted by 0.
32

33
00:01:48,030 --> 00:01:49,120
So, no effect.
33

34
00:01:49,140 --> 00:01:51,990
So, 4 is left shifted by 1,
34

35
00:01:51,990 --> 00:01:54,090
the final value will be 8.
35

36
00:01:54,090 --> 00:01:56,100
So, actually the value doubled.
36

37
00:01:56,550 --> 00:02:01,100
So, again if you left shift by 1, the value again doubles here, like that.
37

38
00:02:01,110 --> 00:02:04,370
So, when you do left shift the value increases.
38

39
00:02:04,500 --> 00:02:11,420
So, that's because the weightages of the bit positions will increase if you left shift a number.
39

40
00:02:11,430 --> 00:02:17,440
So, now contrary to that when you right shift  a number the value actually decreases.
40

41
00:02:17,580 --> 00:02:23,610
Here you can see that 128 when it is right shifted by 2 it becomes 32.
41

42
00:02:23,610 --> 00:02:26,780
So, it's like 128 divided by 2 to the power 2.
42

43
00:02:26,790 --> 00:02:31,200
So, here it is 128 divided by 2 to the power 3, like that.
43

44
00:02:31,200 --> 00:02:34,150
So, here it is 128 divided by 2 to the power 5.
44

45
00:02:34,660 --> 00:02:38,610
So, whereas here it is 4 into 2 to the power 5.
45

46
00:02:38,610 --> 00:02:45,000
So, that's because, when you do the left shift operation the weightages of the bit positions increase, and
46

47
00:02:45,000 --> 00:02:49,880
when you do right shift operation the weightages of the bit positions decrease.
47

48
00:02:49,890 --> 00:02:53,790
So, a value will be multiplied by 2 for each left shift.
48

49
00:02:53,820 --> 00:03:00,140
Value will be divided by 2 for each right shift. So, all these things doesn't matter actually when we
49

50
00:03:00,140 --> 00:03:01,130
do programming,
50

51
00:03:01,160 --> 00:03:06,230
So, that's why, we have to understand what exactly  is a applicability of bitwise shift operations in
51

52
00:03:06,230 --> 00:03:07,170
embedded programming.
52

53
00:03:07,640 --> 00:03:12,740
So, all those things are theory actually, but we need to understand what are the real applicability of
53

54
00:03:12,820 --> 00:03:17,480
bitwise shift operation in embedded programming code, and we'll understand that in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:07,090
So, now in this lecture, let's understand applicability of the bitwise shift operations in embedded programming.
1

2
00:00:07,260 --> 00:00:15,150
So, bitwise shift operators are very much useful in bit masking of data along with other bitwise operators.
2

3
00:00:15,490 --> 00:00:19,750
So, predominantly used while setting or clearing of bits.
3

4
00:00:19,770 --> 00:00:24,660
Lets consider this problem statement: Set 4th bit of the given data.
4

5
00:00:24,660 --> 00:00:26,390
How do you do this?
5

6
00:00:26,520 --> 00:00:30,180
So, now to do this, that is set the 4th bit of a given data.
6

7
00:00:30,270 --> 00:00:32,000
So, now there are 2 methods.
7

8
00:00:32,010 --> 00:00:35,610
This is a first method what we used in the previous lectures.
8

9
00:00:35,610 --> 00:00:40,310
So, let's say this is the given data and the given data is 0x08.
9

10
00:00:40,380 --> 00:00:42,330
In order to set the 4th bit,
10

11
00:00:42,360 --> 00:00:46,380
so this is a fourth bit. So, you have to find out a mask value,
11

12
00:00:46,410 --> 00:00:48,520
so this happens to be the mask value here
12

13
00:00:48,570 --> 00:00:50,330
and you have to do bitwise
13

14
00:00:50,390 --> 00:00:51,090
OR.
14

15
00:00:51,150 --> 00:00:54,560
And the mask value here happens to be 0x10.
15

16
00:00:54,990 --> 00:00:59,160
So, Data = Data | 0x10.
16

17
00:00:59,160 --> 00:01:01,270
So, if you do that you get the result.
17

18
00:01:01,290 --> 00:01:05,510
So, in the output you can see that the 4th bit is indeed set.
18

19
00:01:05,520 --> 00:01:09,810
Now the problem with this method is you have to create a mask value.
19

20
00:01:09,810 --> 00:01:15,720
So now, for this exercise creating a mask value is very much easier, because here the width of the data
20

21
00:01:15,750 --> 00:01:17,580
is just 8 bits.
21

22
00:01:17,580 --> 00:01:23,730
So, in the previous example when we were coding for our led_on exercise, you just saw that creating a
22

23
00:01:23,730 --> 00:01:29,370
mask value for 32 bits data or 64 bits data is a tedious process.
23

24
00:01:29,520 --> 00:01:31,920
So, that's why, there is one shortcut method
24

25
00:01:31,920 --> 00:01:36,750
you can follow. So, the shortcut method involves the shift operators.
25

26
00:01:36,810 --> 00:01:43,260
So, this is a method 2. So, which I would recommend you to use instead of this method.Here, what you do
26

27
00:01:43,260 --> 00:01:45,760
is, you take the number 1
27

28
00:01:45,840 --> 00:01:49,260
this is 1, and you take the binary value of that.
28

29
00:01:49,260 --> 00:01:56,400
So, this is a binary value of the number 1. And then you left shift this one by the amount given in
29

30
00:01:56,400 --> 00:01:58,470
the problem. In the problem,
30

31
00:01:58,470 --> 00:02:00,620
they have told set the 4th bit.
31

32
00:02:00,690 --> 00:02:05,150
So, that's why, shift the number1 four times to the left.
32

33
00:02:05,160 --> 00:02:05,900
So, what happens?
33

34
00:02:05,910 --> 00:02:10,900
So, this is shifting by one, shifting by two, shifting by three, and shifting by four.
34

35
00:02:10,920 --> 00:02:13,800
So, one appears here. Just check the mask value,
35

36
00:02:13,800 --> 00:02:15,380
so both are same.
36

37
00:02:15,480 --> 00:02:24,110
So, that's how you quickly find out the mask value. So, data is equal to data bitwise OR of  1
37

38
00:02:24,200 --> 00:02:30,060
shifted by 4, this entity will finally it will get resolved into this value.
38

39
00:02:30,680 --> 00:02:36,110
So, that's why, you need not to calculate the mask value, you just use the left shift operator in order
39

40
00:02:36,110 --> 00:02:40,240
to shift the value 1 to the left.
40

41
00:02:40,240 --> 00:02:46,090
So, now let's explore a clearing using shift operators. So, clear 4th bit of given data.
41

42
00:02:46,210 --> 00:02:51,420
So, let's say this is a given data that is 0 x18. Here, in the traditional method
42

43
00:02:51,430 --> 00:02:55,470
if you want to clear the 4th bit, then you have to first create the mask value,
43

44
00:02:55,480 --> 00:02:59,060
so in the mask value the bit position corresponding to the 4th
44

45
00:02:59,100 --> 00:03:02,590
bit we keep 0 and rest all we keep as 1,
45

46
00:03:02,590 --> 00:03:03,970
so you have to calculate it.
46

47
00:03:03,970 --> 00:03:08,400
So, the mask value happens to be 0xEF and you just do  bitwise
47

48
00:03:08,410 --> 00:03:13,590
AND.So, that will give you this output where the fourth bit is cleared.
48

49
00:03:14,290 --> 00:03:17,110
So, now instead of doing this you can do this.
49

50
00:03:17,110 --> 00:03:19,750
So, what we can do is, take the number1
50

51
00:03:19,810 --> 00:03:26,110
so let's shift 4 times, because we want to clear 4th bit. And after that what you do is, you negate this.
51

52
00:03:26,380 --> 00:03:35,060
When you negate what happens? zeroes becomes ones. So, 11101111. So, which is exactly this value.
52

53
00:03:35,920 --> 00:03:37,020
So, here is a code.
53

54
00:03:37,060 --> 00:03:41,910
Data = Data & ~(1 << 4)
54

55
00:03:41,920 --> 00:03:44,320
but you have to negate this.
55

56
00:03:44,470 --> 00:03:46,810
So, you can even keep this in the brackets.
56

57
00:03:46,840 --> 00:03:47,740
This would be good.
57

58
00:03:47,740 --> 00:03:50,720
So, this turns out to be exactly this value.
58

59
00:03:50,740 --> 00:03:56,440
So, there is no question of creating this mask by calculating the bit positions.
59

60
00:03:56,440 --> 00:03:59,510
So, now you can appreciate this method.
60

61
00:03:59,550 --> 00:04:00,920
So, this is very easy
61

62
00:04:00,970 --> 00:04:04,720
and I would recommend you to use this method in programming.
62

63
00:04:04,720 --> 00:04:06,600
So, now by using these techniques,
63

64
00:04:06,610 --> 00:04:14,460
so now let's reattend our led_on exercise. Instead of using mask values like these we can use shift
64

65
00:04:14,460 --> 00:04:17,510
operators and we will fix our code in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 4

0
1
00:00:00,100 --> 00:00:01,480
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,480 --> 00:00:06,800
So, now let's modify these codes by using bitwise shift operators.
2

3
00:00:06,990 --> 00:00:08,700
First let's check this.
3

4
00:00:08,700 --> 00:00:13,570
So here, we are actually enabling the clock for the GPIOD peripheral in the AHB1
4

5
00:00:13,590 --> 00:00:14,780
ENR register.
5

6
00:00:15,000 --> 00:00:19,870
So, on the AHB1ENR we actually modifying the 3rd bit position.
6

7
00:00:20,130 --> 00:00:24,460
What we can do here is, so this can be rewritten like this.
7

8
00:00:24,480 --> 00:00:28,730
So, here we have to SET the 3rd position.
8

9
00:00:30,170 --> 00:00:36,800
So, what I would do is, 1 << 3. That's it. So,
9

10
00:00:36,840 --> 00:00:37,560
second step.
10

11
00:00:37,860 --> 00:00:42,370
So here, we have to clear the 24th and 25th bit position.
11

12
00:00:42,420 --> 00:00:48,170
So, what we can do is, we can do something like this 1 << 24
12

13
00:00:48,600 --> 00:00:51,540
and you have to negate this.
13

14
00:00:51,540 --> 00:00:53,070
So, negating is very important,
14

15
00:00:53,100 --> 00:00:56,160
otherwise you will 0 out all other bit positions.
15

16
00:00:56,370 --> 00:00:58,710
So, negation is very important during clearing.
16

17
00:00:59,250 --> 00:01:03,720
And for the 25th you can do again, like this 25th.
17

18
00:01:03,750 --> 00:01:06,720
But instead of using 2 lines you can do it in 1 line.
18

19
00:01:06,780 --> 00:01:11,010
So, what you can do is, so instead of using 1 here you can use 3.
19

20
00:01:11,370 --> 00:01:14,430
So, the binary value of three is 11.
20

21
00:01:14,460 --> 00:01:22,620
So, it's like pushing 11 to the 24th and 25th bit positions. So, in the mode register
21

22
00:01:22,630 --> 00:01:25,540
so you have to clear 24th and 25th.
22

23
00:01:25,990 --> 00:01:31,870
So, what you do is, you take the number 3 and left shift this by 24.
23

24
00:01:31,960 --> 00:01:32,800
When you do that
24

25
00:01:32,800 --> 00:01:35,770
so, what happens? 3 will come and settle here.
25

26
00:01:35,770 --> 00:01:41,050
So, what is 3?  3 means in binary it is 11 and then you are negating this.
26

27
00:01:41,860 --> 00:01:43,690
So, it will become 00
27

28
00:01:44,080 --> 00:01:49,170
and all others become 11.So, that's why, you can use 3 here.
28

29
00:01:49,250 --> 00:01:50,790
Next make 24th
29

30
00:01:50,820 --> 00:01:52,280
bit position as 1,
30

31
00:01:52,400 --> 00:01:54,670
so again you know what we have to do here
31

32
00:01:54,680 --> 00:01:56,410
1 << 24.
32

33
00:01:56,990 --> 00:01:59,340
And here you are setting 12th bit position.
33

34
00:01:59,540 --> 00:02:02,960
So, this can be replaced by 1 << 12.
34

35
00:02:03,410 --> 00:02:07,580
So, after modification just recheck the code whether it is working or not,
35

36
00:02:07,580 --> 00:02:11,730
let me compile this code, so let me just check this code.
36

37
00:02:11,990 --> 00:02:14,410
So yes, the LED is indeed turning on.
37

38
00:02:14,570 --> 00:02:18,470
So, that's about the applicability of bitwise shift operators
38

39
00:02:18,710 --> 00:02:23,390
and we also need to understand one more thing that is bit extraction.
39

40
00:02:23,390 --> 00:02:26,720
And I will cover the Bit extraction in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 5

0
1
00:00:00,050 --> 00:00:01,510
Hey, welcome back to the course,
1

2
00:00:01,560 --> 00:00:05,360
and in this lecture, lets understand about bit extraction.
2

3
00:00:05,370 --> 00:00:07,730
Lets consider this problem statement.
3

4
00:00:07,730 --> 00:00:10,930
Extract bit positions from 9 to 14th.
4

5
00:00:11,100 --> 00:00:16,650
So, which is denoted something like this, in a given data and save it in to another variable. 
5

6
00:00:16,640 --> 00:00:19,060
Lets understand how we can do this.
6

7
00:00:19,230 --> 00:00:21,030
So, let's consider a data.
7

8
00:00:21,030 --> 00:00:24,160
So, here the data is 16 bit value,
8

9
00:00:24,210 --> 00:00:29,180
the data is 0xB410 which is written in binary something like this.
9

10
00:00:29,340 --> 00:00:36,210
And here we have to extract only this portion from bit position 9 to 14.
10

11
00:00:36,210 --> 00:00:40,180
This can be done using the method described here. First,
11

12
00:00:40,210 --> 00:00:47,190
what you have to do is, shift the identified portion to right hand side until it touches the least significant
12

13
00:00:47,190 --> 00:00:47,550
bit.
13

14
00:00:47,940 --> 00:00:54,570
So, that means you just move this portion to the extreme right until it touches the LSB.
14

15
00:00:54,690 --> 00:01:01,770
And after that, mask the value to extract only 6 bits and then save it in to another variable.
15

16
00:01:01,830 --> 00:01:05,160
So, let's see how we can do the first step, that is shifting.
16

17
00:01:05,910 --> 00:01:10,500
So, this is a original data and you have to shift this portion to the right.
17

18
00:01:10,500 --> 00:01:13,100
So, you have to shift this by the amount
18

19
00:01:13,100 --> 00:01:18,900
9. If you do that, then the 9th bit position will come and touch the 0th bit position.
19

20
00:01:19,170 --> 00:01:23,910
So, shifting by 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9.
20

21
00:01:23,940 --> 00:01:25,770
So, you have to shift by 9.
21

22
00:01:25,770 --> 00:01:28,470
So, if you shift by 9 you get this. 
22

23
00:01:28,470 --> 00:01:33,750
So, here we can see that these 6 bits actually came to extreme right, isn't it?
23

24
00:01:34,560 --> 00:01:38,210
So, now here only this is the useful portion,
24

25
00:01:38,250 --> 00:01:42,060
so this is what we want to keep and this we have to discard.
25

26
00:01:42,270 --> 00:01:48,750
So, that's why, you how to zero out this portion and only keep this portion, for that you have to mask.
26

27
00:01:48,780 --> 00:01:49,620
isn't it?
27

28
00:01:49,620 --> 00:01:51,450
So, now that is the second step.
28

29
00:01:51,630 --> 00:01:57,650
Mask the new value to extract only 6 bits from 0 to 5 and then save into another variable.
29

30
00:01:57,810 --> 00:02:04,830
And here, since we don't want this portion we have to mask that portion with zeros, and we only want this
30

31
00:02:04,830 --> 00:02:05,370
portion
31

32
00:02:05,370 --> 00:02:08,790
so we have to mask that portion with once. Isn't it?
32

33
00:02:08,820 --> 00:02:12,840
So, that's why, I select the mask value 003F.
33

34
00:02:13,020 --> 00:02:15,350
So, this is nothing but 3F. Isn't it?
34

35
00:02:15,360 --> 00:02:16,470
This is 3,
35

36
00:02:16,470 --> 00:02:19,670
this is F, and then you have to do bitwise
36

37
00:02:19,710 --> 00:02:25,500
AND. So, this portion will be zeroed out and we only keep this portion.
37

38
00:02:25,500 --> 00:02:27,480
How do you do this by writing a code?
38

39
00:02:29,580 --> 00:02:31,030
Let's say you have the data.
39

40
00:02:31,140 --> 00:02:37,280
So, let's say the data is 0xB410, so and you have to save the output in the variable output.
40

41
00:02:37,500 --> 00:02:41,150
So, output is equal to Data
41

42
00:02:42,250 --> 00:02:50,860
right shifted by 9 and then you have to & with 0x003F. So then, you have to save the final
42

43
00:02:50,860 --> 00:02:52,690
value into the variable output
43

44
00:02:52,780 --> 00:02:58,140
and this is of type uint8_t, so you can also typecast uint8_t .
44

45
00:02:58,330 --> 00:03:00,210
So, this is how you should be doing.
45

46
00:03:00,280 --> 00:03:01,300
So, that's about bit
46

47
00:03:01,300 --> 00:03:02,110
extraction
47

48
00:03:02,110 --> 00:03:06,640
and with that note I would like to end this video and let me know if you have any issues and I'll see you
48

49
00:03:06,640 --> 00:03:07,390
in the next lecture.

section 20---looping

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:04,190
From this lecture onwards let's study about looping in 'C'.
1

2
00:00:04,230 --> 00:00:08,040
So, first let's understand why exactly we need looping.
2

3
00:00:08,040 --> 00:00:11,630
So, take a look into this program which is at the left hand side,
3

4
00:00:11,700 --> 00:00:15,700
so this is a program to print numbers from 1 to 10.
4

5
00:00:15,750 --> 00:00:20,770
And I have used a couple of print statements with different arguments to achieve that goal.
5

6
00:00:21,540 --> 00:00:27,450
So, of course you can write a program like this. But there is a smarter way which saves your code space
6

7
00:00:27,480 --> 00:00:34,110
and avoides a code redundancies. So, I can modify the same code using loops statements of 'C'.
7

8
00:00:34,560 --> 00:00:36,650
So, this looks lot better than the previous one.
8

9
00:00:37,230 --> 00:00:40,620
So, looping helps you to execute certain set of statements
9

10
00:00:40,650 --> 00:00:44,530
again and again until some condition is met.
10

11
00:00:44,670 --> 00:00:50,700
So, usage of loops statements are very much common in programming and use it generously whenever you
11

12
00:00:50,850 --> 00:00:52,850
need it in the programming.
12

13
00:00:52,920 --> 00:00:58,020
So, in 'C' there are 3 types of loops statements while loop, for loop,
13

14
00:00:58,020 --> 00:00:58,910
and do while loop.
14

15
00:00:59,550 --> 00:01:05,650
So, here while, for, and do all these are reserved keywords of 'C' standard.
15

16
00:01:05,900 --> 00:01:09,190
So, in the next lecture let's understand the while loop.
16

17
00:01:09,240 --> 00:01:10,590
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:00,720
Hey, welcome back.
1

2
00:00:00,750 --> 00:00:06,720
So, in this lecture let's study about while loop, let's learn about the syntax of the while loop and  later
2

3
00:00:06,750 --> 00:00:09,120
we can use that in our exercises.
3

4
00:00:09,120 --> 00:00:13,890
As I said looping is a way of executing certain set of statements
4

5
00:00:13,890 --> 00:00:21,430
again and again until certain condition is met, that is accomplished using a while loop here.
5

6
00:00:21,450 --> 00:00:23,450
So, this is a syntax of the while loop.
6

7
00:00:23,460 --> 00:00:27,870
So here, these are actually set of statements.
7

8
00:00:28,080 --> 00:00:33,510
So, we want to execute these statements again and again until certain condition is met.
8

9
00:00:33,540 --> 00:00:40,890
So, in order to use while loop you have to first start with the 'while' keyword and you have to create the parentheses.
9

10
00:00:41,300 --> 00:00:47,370
Write the parentheses of the while loop and inside the parentheses write the expression.
10

11
00:00:47,370 --> 00:00:52,740
And after that if you have multiple statements, then you have to open the body of the while loop.
11

12
00:00:52,890 --> 00:00:55,510
So, this is actually start of the loop body
12

13
00:00:55,710 --> 00:00:58,530
and this is the end of the loop body.
13

14
00:00:58,530 --> 00:01:02,750
So, inside that you have to write multiple statements.  First,
14

15
00:01:02,820 --> 00:01:08,940
what happens is, when this while loop is executed, first expression will be evaluated.
15

16
00:01:09,060 --> 00:01:16,200
That is the first thing happens. If this expression evaluation result is true, then these statements will
16

17
00:01:16,200 --> 00:01:17,240
be executed.
17

18
00:01:17,640 --> 00:01:19,880
And these statements will be executed
18

19
00:01:19,890 --> 00:01:23,740
again and again until this expression becomes false.
19

20
00:01:23,760 --> 00:01:32,270
So, once the expression becomes false, the loop breaks automatically. The program actually moves out
20

21
00:01:32,270 --> 00:01:34,880
of this while loop and continues with the rest of the code.
21

22
00:01:35,540 --> 00:01:41,510
So, this body will be executed only if this expression is true.
22

23
00:01:41,570 --> 00:01:43,450
So, that's how looping works.
23

24
00:01:43,460 --> 00:01:49,340
So, if you have only one statement which needs to be executed again and again, then you need not to create
24

25
00:01:49,370 --> 00:01:50,390
this a body.
25

26
00:01:50,660 --> 00:01:53,210
So, even if you create the body then no problem,
26

27
00:01:53,300 --> 00:01:54,930
you can do that.
27

28
00:01:55,010 --> 00:02:03,450
So, while loop means repeat execution of code inside the loop body until expression evaluates to false.
28

29
00:02:03,470 --> 00:02:11,420
So, let's look at the flowchart of while loop. So, in while loop what happens is, first expression is evaluated.
29

30
00:02:11,750 --> 00:02:17,340
If it is true, then only the statements inside the loop body will be executed.
30

31
00:02:17,510 --> 00:02:21,890
And again control loops back to check the expression once again.
31

32
00:02:22,190 --> 00:02:24,010
So, like that it will be looping.
32

33
00:02:24,050 --> 00:02:31,010
So, if the expression evaluation result is false, then the loop breaks and program continues with the
33

34
00:02:31,010 --> 00:02:33,010
code outside the loop body.
34

35
00:02:33,050 --> 00:02:34,570
So, that's how while loop works.
35

36
00:02:34,760 --> 00:02:41,870
So, you have to remember that in while loop expression is always evaluated first. Let's try to use the
36

37
00:02:41,870 --> 00:02:48,020
while loop in our program. Write a program which prints from 1 to 10, or 1 to 100, whatever it may
37

38
00:02:48,030 --> 00:02:49,410
be a using 'while' loop,
38

39
00:02:49,490 --> 00:02:52,600
and so, I will teach you how to do this in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,880
So, I just created a new project,
1

2
00:00:01,950 --> 00:00:04,860
the project name is WhileLoop1to10.
2

3
00:00:04,880 --> 00:00:10,500
So, now in this lecture, let's write a program which prints numbers from 1 to 10 using loop
3

4
00:00:10,500 --> 00:00:12,640
and in this case we are using while loop.
4

5
00:00:13,020 --> 00:00:13,880
Let's begin.
5

6
00:00:13,890 --> 00:00:16,290
So, first let me create a variable.
6

7
00:00:16,290 --> 00:00:24,090
Let's say, I do uint8_t let's create a variable num. So, let's use the while loop. So,
7

8
00:00:24,090 --> 00:00:30,770
let's print the number first printf, I will print the number %d\n, num.
8

9
00:00:30,780 --> 00:00:33,270
So, we want from 1 to 10, isn't it?
9

10
00:00:33,270 --> 00:00:35,500
So, that's why, I will initialize the num to 1.
10

11
00:00:35,700 --> 00:00:36,450
That looks good.
11

12
00:00:36,870 --> 00:00:39,290
So, this code actually prints 1.
12

13
00:00:39,390 --> 00:00:43,780
After that, I want to increment that number, because I want to print 2.
13

14
00:00:43,830 --> 00:00:51,000
So, what I do is, I do num++. And again the controls should go back and it should print once again.
14

15
00:00:51,090 --> 00:00:53,840
So, that's why, I'm going to use while loop. So, while
15

16
00:00:53,910 --> 00:00:54,950
so, what's a condition?
16

17
00:00:54,960 --> 00:00:56,580
So, let's think about the condition later,
17

18
00:00:56,580 --> 00:00:58,620
let's create the body of the while loop.
18

19
00:00:58,620 --> 00:01:00,420
So, give a bracket here
19

20
00:01:00,420 --> 00:01:03,660
curly bracket and close the curly bracket here.
20

21
00:01:03,720 --> 00:01:10,440
So, this code is not properly indented, so you can give the indentation if you want. Fine.
21

22
00:01:10,610 --> 00:01:17,140
So now, what should be the condition here, until number is less than or equal to 10.
22

23
00:01:17,150 --> 00:01:20,360
This is the condition or expression. Correct?
23

24
00:01:20,480 --> 00:01:22,070
So, let's analyze this code.
24

25
00:01:22,140 --> 00:01:23,720
First num will be 1.
25

26
00:01:23,720 --> 00:01:27,020
So, 1 <= 10, which is true.
26

27
00:01:27,020 --> 00:01:33,650
So, this will be printed and number will be incremented and again control comes back to check the expression
27

28
00:01:33,740 --> 00:01:34,470
of the while.
28

29
00:01:34,880 --> 00:01:37,150
So, again 2 < 10.
29

30
00:01:37,160 --> 00:01:38,050
again it is printed.
30

31
00:01:38,080 --> 00:01:42,650
So, like that. When it becomes 11, so again it will be checked
31

32
00:01:42,650 --> 00:01:46,000
11<= 10 is false,
32

33
00:01:46,010 --> 00:01:53,090
and at that case this body breaks and control comes here. So, it's a simple program.
33

34
00:01:53,120 --> 00:01:58,010
So, instead of doing this here, you can also remove this and you can do this right here
34

35
00:01:58,010 --> 00:01:59,630
num++.
35

36
00:01:59,630 --> 00:02:01,640
So here, a num will be printed first
36

37
00:02:01,670 --> 00:02:08,270
that is 1 will be printed first and then num will be incremented to 2. Let's check the output.
37

38
00:02:08,270 --> 00:02:08,900
So, here it is.
38

39
00:02:08,900 --> 00:02:11,810
So, our program indeed printed 1 to 10.
