video 4

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,920
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,940 --> 00:00:05,110
Let's discuss about while loop and a semicolon.
2

3
00:00:05,250 --> 00:00:10,050
So, if you just take a look into our earlier code. So, we didn't use any semicolon with a while loop.
3

4
00:00:10,350 --> 00:00:12,550
So, here there was no semicolon.
4

5
00:00:12,570 --> 00:00:16,410
You should not give any semicolon when you are working with loops.
5

6
00:00:16,410 --> 00:00:18,600
What happens if I give a semicolon here?
6

7
00:00:18,930 --> 00:00:21,170
So, usually beginners do this mistake,
7

8
00:00:21,180 --> 00:00:22,230
they give a semicolon.
8

9
00:00:22,920 --> 00:00:27,580
If you give a semicolon, then it is not a compilation or syntax error
9

10
00:00:27,630 --> 00:00:29,280
is a compilation will go well.
10

11
00:00:29,460 --> 00:00:36,000
The compiler may warn you, so by saying something like this 'while' clause does not guard something, but
11

12
00:00:36,000 --> 00:00:38,460
it doesn't talk about the semicolon.
12

13
00:00:38,460 --> 00:00:41,010
So,  but what happens if you give semicolon?
13

14
00:00:41,040 --> 00:00:46,080
So, giving semicolon completely changes the logic of your program.
14

15
00:00:46,080 --> 00:00:51,830
So, for example, if I give a semicolon here this code is actually equivalent to this code.
15

16
00:00:51,840 --> 00:00:56,070
So, this statement I have rewritten something like this.
16

17
00:00:56,100 --> 00:00:57,150
So, what happens here?
17

18
00:00:57,180 --> 00:01:01,020
So, you are actually executing the NOP again and again.
18

19
00:01:01,050 --> 00:01:03,720
So, here the i is never incremented.
19

20
00:01:03,780 --> 00:01:07,380
So, that's why, this actually becomes an in finite loop
20

21
00:01:07,380 --> 00:01:14,140
so the control never reaches to this point. So, here you can clearly see that giving a semicolon for the
21

22
00:01:14,140 --> 00:01:21,130
while loop actually creates sometimes an infinite loop and it completely destroys your logic.
22

23
00:01:21,190 --> 00:01:23,410
So, that's why, be careful with the semicolon.
23

24
00:01:23,470 --> 00:01:29,100
You can give semicolon sometimes with the while loop, but you should be aware of what you are trying to do.
24

25
00:01:29,140 --> 00:01:31,130
So, take a look into this program.
25

26
00:01:31,240 --> 00:01:35,450
So here, the program completely hangs over this point.
26

27
00:01:35,470 --> 00:01:42,550
So here, we have given a semicolon with while loop. The purpose of this code is to hang the program permanently.
27

28
00:01:42,940 --> 00:01:44,680
So, code hangs forever here.
28

29
00:01:44,680 --> 00:01:46,690
So, main never returns.
29

30
00:01:46,690 --> 00:01:52,750
So, this is actually an infinite loop, because the condition or expression is always true, and you have
30

31
00:01:52,750 --> 00:01:54,160
given a semicolon.
31

32
00:01:54,220 --> 00:01:58,450
So, this is used sometimes in the microcontroller programming using 'C'.
32

33
00:01:58,450 --> 00:02:02,350
So, sometimes in the microcontroller code we use this statement,
33

34
00:02:02,350 --> 00:02:07,310
that is while loop with semicolon to hang the code at the main function.
34

35
00:02:07,360 --> 00:02:12,550
And now let's discuss about forever loop. A special form of the while loop is the forever loop.
35

36
00:02:12,610 --> 00:02:14,790
So, this is a loop that never ends.
36

37
00:02:15,010 --> 00:02:21,130
For example, here this loop is called us forever loop or super loop. So, here the condition is always
37

38
00:02:21,130 --> 00:02:21,770
true.
38

39
00:02:21,850 --> 00:02:26,390
So, it executes these a set of statements forever.
39

40
00:02:26,500 --> 00:02:31,950
So, it is common to see this forever loop in an embedded application in the main program.
40

41
00:02:32,050 --> 00:02:38,740
Unlike a PC program, an embedded program may just run forever as long as it is powered up.
41

42
00:02:39,220 --> 00:02:44,490
So, we see a super loop or a forever loop in Embedded 'C' programming.
42

43
00:02:44,530 --> 00:02:48,620
So, now in the next lecture let's do one exercise using a while loop.
43

44
00:02:48,760 --> 00:02:54,870
So, write a program to print all even numbers between 0 to 100 (including the boundary numbers).
44

45
00:02:55,150 --> 00:03:02,410
So, also count and print how many even numbers you find. So, use 'while' loop,  or 'for' loop, or 'do while' loop,
45

46
00:03:02,680 --> 00:03:06,380
so anyway, so we didn't cover for loop and do while loop yet
46

47
00:03:06,460 --> 00:03:08,290
so you can use only while loop.
47

48
00:03:08,680 --> 00:03:12,610
So, first it should ask for starting and ending numbers.
48

49
00:03:13,070 --> 00:03:14,770
So, starting and ending numbers,
49

50
00:03:14,800 --> 00:03:22,300
so let me type -2 90, and then it has to print all the even numbers which come between -2
50

51
00:03:22,300 --> 00:03:23,620
to 90.
51

52
00:03:23,620 --> 00:03:30,920
So, there are 47 even numbers. So, I hope you can do that and I'll see you in the next lecture.

if ending number is less than starting number that is not allowed.

video 6

0
1
00:00:00,330 --> 00:00:02,100
Let's continue our discussion of looping. 
1

2
00:00:02,110 --> 00:00:05,450
So, in this lecture let's explore about do while loop.
2

3
00:00:05,550 --> 00:00:08,420
So, this is the syntax of the do while loop.
3

4
00:00:08,580 --> 00:00:15,360
So, remember that the difference between a while loop and a do while loop is, so in do while loop statements
4

5
00:00:15,360 --> 00:00:19,930
are always executed first and then expression will be checked.
5

6
00:00:20,550 --> 00:00:29,070
So, do while loop says, repeat the execution of code inside the 'do' body until expression evaluates to false(0).
6

7
00:00:29,070 --> 00:00:32,160
So, this is a syntax of the do while loop
7

8
00:00:32,160 --> 00:00:36,210
and please take a note that, there is a semicolon at the end
8

9
00:00:36,210 --> 00:00:38,630
and this semicolon is compulsory.
9

10
00:00:38,800 --> 00:00:42,170
So, not giving semicolon is actually  a syntax mistake,
10

11
00:00:42,360 --> 00:00:44,330
so you'll face a compilation error.
11

12
00:00:44,640 --> 00:00:46,530
So, this semicolon is compulsory.
12

13
00:00:46,530 --> 00:00:47,370
So, what happens here?
13

14
00:00:47,400 --> 00:00:49,280
So, it is very easy to understand.
14

15
00:00:49,290 --> 00:00:51,610
So, first this statement will be executed,
15

16
00:00:51,690 --> 00:00:55,860
which is there inside the 'do' body and then this expression will be evaluated.
16

17
00:00:55,890 --> 00:01:00,420
So, if this expression evaluation result is 0, that is false
17

18
00:01:00,420 --> 00:01:01,800
then loop ends.
18

19
00:01:02,280 --> 00:01:08,580
So, that means a control goes out of this body and continues with rest of the code of the program.
19

20
00:01:08,580 --> 00:01:14,850
Suppose, if this expression evaluation is true, then control loops back once again to the 'do' body and
20

21
00:01:14,850 --> 00:01:16,680
it executes the statements
21

22
00:01:16,740 --> 00:01:22,740
once again.So, like that.So, statements will be executed again and again until this expression becomes
22

23
00:01:22,740 --> 00:01:23,750
false.
23

24
00:01:23,760 --> 00:01:29,640
So, here is a flowchart. So, first statements will be executed, then expression will be evaluated. If
24

25
00:01:29,640 --> 00:01:33,930
it is false, then program continues with the code outside loop body.
25

26
00:01:33,930 --> 00:01:41,140
So, these are the points you should always remember statements are executed first, expression is evaluated
26

27
00:01:41,140 --> 00:01:42,340
next.
27

28
00:01:42,340 --> 00:01:45,190
So, that's about the do while loop and what
28

29
00:01:45,220 --> 00:01:49,540
you should note here is that, statements are executed at least once.
29

30
00:01:49,540 --> 00:01:54,250
That's a difference between a while loop and a do while loop. In do while loop statements are executed at
30

31
00:01:54,250 --> 00:01:55,370
least once.
31

32
00:01:55,420 --> 00:01:58,380
So, now do I while loop is rarely used while looping,
32

33
00:01:58,530 --> 00:02:02,620
so because, we can always replace do while loop with while loop or for loop. 
33

34
00:02:02,800 --> 00:02:04,290
But, sometimes it is used
34

35
00:02:04,300 --> 00:02:08,560
it is not like you know it is never used, but it is always encouraged to use
35

36
00:02:08,560 --> 00:02:09,190
do while loop,
36

37
00:02:09,190 --> 00:02:12,020
if it is necessary. Actually we do use a
37

38
00:02:12,070 --> 00:02:13,670
do while loop a lot, a
38

39
00:02:13,810 --> 00:02:18,600
while defining the multiline macros in the embedded system programming.
39

40
00:02:18,700 --> 00:02:21,700
So, we'll discuss more on that when we cover a
40

41
00:02:21,700 --> 00:02:26,810
'C' macros and pre-processor directives of the 'C' programming language.
41

42
00:02:26,950 --> 00:02:33,990
In that case, I will show you how you can write a function like macros and multiline macros using do
42

43
00:02:33,990 --> 00:02:34,580
while loop.
43

44
00:02:34,990 --> 00:02:37,120
So, in embedded system programming,  you see
44

45
00:02:37,120 --> 00:02:42,250
do while loop most of the time in header files, and we'll discuss that when we reach there.
45

46
00:02:42,430 --> 00:02:47,200
And again please take a note that, there is a semicolon here and it is compulsory.
46

47
00:02:47,860 --> 00:02:50,240
So, with that note I would like to end this
47

48
00:02:50,260 --> 00:02:50,920
do while loop,
48

49
00:02:50,920 --> 00:02:53,170
so I'm not going to waste too much time on this.
49

50
00:02:53,260 --> 00:02:59,560
Anyway, we are going to explore this again when we explore a 'C' macros. And in the next lecture, I will
50

51
00:02:59,560 --> 00:03:03,150
explain you very important loop that is for loop.
51

52
00:03:03,250 --> 00:03:04,340
I'll see you in the next lecture.

0
1
00:00:00,040 --> 00:00:01,320
Hey, welcome back to the lecture. 
1

2
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:08,460
So, in this lecture, let's explore one of the famous and most widely used looping statement in 'C', that
2

3
00:00:08,460 --> 00:00:09,480
is for loop.
3

4
00:00:09,780 --> 00:00:11,440
This is a syntax of the for loop.
4

5
00:00:11,550 --> 00:00:14,500
So, this is actually the 'for' keyword.
5

6
00:00:14,520 --> 00:00:16,390
This is a 'C' reserved keyword.
6

7
00:00:17,100 --> 00:00:23,640
And once you write the for loop you have to create a parentheses, and inside the parentheses
7

8
00:00:23,670 --> 00:00:30,540
so you are going to write three expressions, all three expressions are not compulsory, but you can write
8

9
00:00:30,660 --> 00:00:35,130
3 expressions and those expressions are separated by semicolon.
9

10
00:00:35,430 --> 00:00:40,110
So, two semicolons create three blocks for you inside the parentheses.
10

11
00:00:40,110 --> 00:00:45,820
So, these two semicolons are compulsory, but whether to leave that block empty or filled
11

12
00:00:45,870 --> 00:00:51,380
that depends upon your logic. So, that is not compulsory, but these semicolons are compulsory.
12

13
00:00:51,390 --> 00:00:52,520
Let's see how this works.
13

14
00:00:52,650 --> 00:00:56,220
By the way this is a body where you write your statements.
14

15
00:00:56,290 --> 00:01:06,020
So now, when it is executed so first what happens is, this block1 is executed only once. So, you should
15

16
00:01:06,020 --> 00:01:16,310
remember this. So, block 1 is executed only once. So, block 2 is used for, I mean the expression inside the
16

17
00:01:16,310 --> 00:01:21,880
block 2 is used for whether to continue with the looping or to break the looping,
17

18
00:01:21,890 --> 00:01:27,340
that means the expression in the block 2 is evaluated as true or false.
18

19
00:01:27,440 --> 00:01:34,780
So, this block decides whether to break the loop or continue with the loop. So, block 3 doesn't decide
19

20
00:01:34,780 --> 00:01:35,440
anything.
20

21
00:01:35,620 --> 00:01:42,790
So, you can put any expression here. So, in block 3 you can put any expression.
21

22
00:01:46,740 --> 00:01:50,070
So, those are the significance of these blocks.
22

23
00:01:50,070 --> 00:01:55,800
So, now the question is when the expression of block 1 executes, when the expression of block2 executes,
23

24
00:01:55,890 --> 00:01:59,300
and when does the expression of block 3 executes.
24

25
00:01:59,310 --> 00:02:01,850
So, these are executed in specific order.
25

26
00:02:01,860 --> 00:02:09,070
So, now let's understand that. So, when a for loop is started for the first time block 1 is executed for
26

27
00:02:09,070 --> 00:02:10,590
one time, that's it.
27

28
00:02:10,690 --> 00:02:11,610
And after that,
28

29
00:02:11,620 --> 00:02:20,170
block 2 is evaluated, this is evaluated. If the evaluation result is true, then only body of the loop is
29

30
00:02:20,170 --> 00:02:21,400
executed.
30

31
00:02:21,400 --> 00:02:24,840
Suppose, if the evaluation is false, loop breaks.
31

32
00:02:25,270 --> 00:02:27,170
So, first this is executed
32

33
00:02:27,220 --> 00:02:29,680
and after that this is evaluated.
33

34
00:02:29,680 --> 00:02:31,390
Let's say the evaluation is true,
34

35
00:02:31,390 --> 00:02:37,070
so loop executes one time and after that block 3 is executed.
35

36
00:02:37,090 --> 00:02:40,540
That means, expression in the block 3 is executed.
36

37
00:02:40,540 --> 00:02:44,430
And after that again block 2 is compared. If it is true,
37

38
00:02:44,440 --> 00:02:46,120
again loop is executed,
38

39
00:02:46,120 --> 00:02:49,450
and again block 3 is executed, like that.
39

40
00:02:49,450 --> 00:02:52,930
It may be a little confusing, but there is a flowchart to understand this better.
40

41
00:02:52,930 --> 00:02:54,070
So, here it is.
41

42
00:02:54,220 --> 00:02:57,290
This is the flowchart of for loop. Execute block 1
42

43
00:02:57,340 --> 00:03:01,240
only one time, check the result of block-2. If it is false,
43

44
00:03:01,240 --> 00:03:02,170
loop is done,
44

45
00:03:02,170 --> 00:03:03,690
that is loop breaks.
45

46
00:03:03,700 --> 00:03:10,800
Suppose, if the block 2 is true, then execute the body and then execute block 3.
46

47
00:03:11,050 --> 00:03:15,640
And again come back check result of block 2. If it is true, again
47

48
00:03:15,640 --> 00:03:18,090
execute the body, after executing the body
48

49
00:03:18,100 --> 00:03:22,250
you should remember that execution of block 3 happens.
49

50
00:03:22,360 --> 00:03:29,920
So now, this feature of the for loop we can use for iterations, iterations over a variable, or iterations
50

51
00:03:29,950 --> 00:03:31,270
over an array,
51

52
00:03:31,270 --> 00:03:34,040
so iteration over pointer array etc..
52

53
00:03:34,240 --> 00:03:40,450
So, we usually use block 1 for initialization, because that is executed only once.
53

54
00:03:40,450 --> 00:03:44,880
After that the evaluation expression we keep in block 2.
54

55
00:03:45,220 --> 00:03:47,910
Like for example,  i <10
55

56
00:03:47,950 --> 00:03:55,900
that's an evaluation. So, that evaluation expression we keep in block 2. And in block 3 we usually increment
56

57
00:03:56,020 --> 00:03:56,800
the iterator.
57

58
00:03:56,950 --> 00:04:02,280
So here, in this case 'i' is a iterator and will see more on that later, when we do
58

59
00:04:02,280 --> 00:04:03,480
example.
59

60
00:04:03,520 --> 00:04:10,120
So, we use a block 3 to increment or decrement the iterator. And in the next lecture, let's repeat our
60

61
00:04:10,120 --> 00:04:14,350
exercise that is printing of even  numbers using for loop and I will see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 8

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:06,220
So, we actually wrote an a program for printing even numbers using while loop.
1

2
00:00:06,540 --> 00:00:09,100
So, this was our code.
2

3
00:00:09,120 --> 00:00:13,200
So, now let's implement the same logic using for loop.
3

4
00:00:13,290 --> 00:00:21,750
So, what I'm going to do is, I'm going to just copy this program and I actually created a new project
4

5
00:00:22,020 --> 00:00:30,360
called for loop even number and let's paste here. And I'm going to a keep this section same
5

6
00:00:30,360 --> 00:00:33,560
this is where we receive the number and this is a small check,
6

7
00:00:33,570 --> 00:00:36,260
and so I'm going to remove this
7

8
00:00:36,260 --> 00:00:38,810
while loop and I'm going to use for loop.
8

9
00:00:39,600 --> 00:00:42,530
Let's see how to use for loop here.
9

10
00:00:42,570 --> 00:00:45,420
So, first you have to use a 'for' keyword,
10

11
00:00:45,420 --> 00:00:48,660
open the parentheses and create the body.
11

12
00:00:48,660 --> 00:00:53,880
So, in the parentheses you have to create 3 blocks by using two semicolons.
12

13
00:00:54,180 --> 00:01:01,050
And after that, you know that the first block is for some initialization or you can put any expression
13

14
00:01:01,050 --> 00:01:06,480
you want here, that will be executed only one time and the second block is for evaluation.
14

15
00:01:06,510 --> 00:01:11,430
So, that's why, I would use this for evaluation and our evaluation is we want to check whether
15

16
00:01:11,430 --> 00:01:20,130
start_num <= end_num. So, I would keep that evaluation here. And after that, I would
16

17
00:01:20,130 --> 00:01:27,020
just use the same logic that is if( start_num % 2).
17

18
00:01:27,060 --> 00:01:34,650
So, if it is 0, so then I would print %d\t
18

19
00:01:34,710 --> 00:01:36,710
start_num. Logic is same.
19

20
00:01:36,720 --> 00:01:40,200
And after that I'm going to increment even++ here.
20

21
00:01:40,260 --> 00:01:41,480
That's it.
21

22
00:01:41,520 --> 00:01:52,620
And here you have to increment the start_num. So, that you can place here in this block start_num++.
22

23
00:01:52,630 --> 00:01:54,160
Because,
23

24
00:01:54,220 --> 00:01:57,400
so this is executed after the body is executed.
24

25
00:01:57,670 --> 00:02:04,710
So, you can also keep start_ num++ here also, in that case you can leave the space empty.
25

26
00:02:04,710 --> 00:02:05,840
No problem with that.
26

27
00:02:05,860 --> 00:02:06,660
You can do that.
27

28
00:02:06,660 --> 00:02:12,960
Otherwise, you can also put this code here. And after that,
28

29
00:02:13,080 --> 00:02:13,290
Yeah,
29

30
00:02:13,320 --> 00:02:19,320
so this also gives a same result. And you can see that this place is empty, so you can put anything
30

31
00:02:19,320 --> 00:02:24,910
you want there. For example, I can even put this printf here. So, don't give the semicolon
31

32
00:02:25,230 --> 00:02:31,630
and by giving comma, I can give even this statement, you can do that.
32

33
00:02:31,690 --> 00:02:36,130
So, all these are legal activities. Because, this is executed only one time
33

34
00:02:36,130 --> 00:02:37,710
that is for the very first time.
34

35
00:02:37,900 --> 00:02:39,730
Let's see how this executes.
35

36
00:02:39,730 --> 00:02:43,910
Let me compile this code, so the compilation is fine.
36

37
00:02:43,910 --> 00:02:45,310
Sorry, about that.
37

38
00:02:45,380 --> 00:02:48,230
So, I actually messed up with the windows,
38

39
00:02:48,230 --> 00:02:51,780
if you ever messed up with the windows then don't worry at all.
39

40
00:02:52,040 --> 00:02:55,790
Just go to Windows, Perspective, Reset perspective.
40

41
00:03:01,100 --> 00:03:04,280
So, let me again explain this for loop, so how it executes? First
41

42
00:03:04,280 --> 00:03:06,980
what happens is, this is executed only one time.
42

43
00:03:07,010 --> 00:03:09,900
So, our even variable is initialized to 0,
43

44
00:03:09,950 --> 00:03:17,160
and after that start_num is compared with this number, if it is true then this is executed
44

45
00:03:17,180 --> 00:03:20,040
and after that this is executed,
45

46
00:03:20,060 --> 00:03:23,920
and after that again this is compared again body executes
46

47
00:03:23,930 --> 00:03:25,580
and again this is executed,
47

48
00:03:25,580 --> 00:03:26,700
and so on.
48

49
00:03:26,780 --> 00:03:30,340
So, the loop breaks when this check fails.
49

50
00:03:30,380 --> 00:03:37,150
So, that's about the for loop and we use for loop in the place of while loop when we do iterations
50

51
00:03:37,160 --> 00:03:43,900
as I said before like iterations over array, or when we do iterations over a variables, etc.
51

52
00:03:43,940 --> 00:03:49,230
So, when we cover arrays, so we'll explore more about how to do that iterations using for loop.
52

53
00:03:49,700 --> 00:03:53,260
So, with that note I would like to end this video and I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 9

0
1
00:00:00,150 --> 00:00:01,330
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,330 --> 00:00:07,620
So, I would like to give one exercise like you have to print a number pyramid. So, you should print something
2

3
00:00:07,620 --> 00:00:08,230
like this.
3

4
00:00:08,250 --> 00:00:11,850
So, this pattern you had to print at the output.
4

5
00:00:11,850 --> 00:00:16,680
So, basically what you have to ask the user is to enter the height of the pyramid
5

6
00:00:16,740 --> 00:00:19,830
and after that you have to print in this pattern.
6

7
00:00:20,460 --> 00:00:24,570
So, you have to do this exercise by using for loops.
7

8
00:00:24,570 --> 00:00:27,630
First let me tell you the logic how you should implement this.
8

9
00:00:27,690 --> 00:00:30,320
So, you have to use a nested for loop here.
9

10
00:00:30,330 --> 00:00:33,330
So, this is actually an outer for loop
10

11
00:00:33,450 --> 00:00:34,910
and this is a inner for loop.
11

12
00:00:35,370 --> 00:00:43,970
So, use the outer for loop to track the height and use the inner for loop to print the rows.
12

13
00:00:43,980 --> 00:00:47,850
So, actually this output is displayed by row by row.
13

14
00:00:47,850 --> 00:00:51,950
This row by row printing is taken care by the inner for loop
14

15
00:00:51,990 --> 00:00:56,190
and how long you should proceed that is decided by the outer for loop.
15

16
00:00:56,220 --> 00:00:58,270
So, let me give you some more hints.
16

17
00:00:58,290 --> 00:01:00,150
So, consider two variables
17

18
00:01:00,150 --> 00:01:00,930
i and j.
18

19
00:01:01,290 --> 00:01:09,360
So, use the variable 'i' to track the height and use the variable 'j' to decide how many numbers you should
19

20
00:01:09,360 --> 00:01:11,220
print in a given row.
20

21
00:01:11,370 --> 00:01:16,010
For example, if you consider this line here currently the i is 6.
21

22
00:01:16,350 --> 00:01:18,560
So, you should print j,
22

23
00:01:18,630 --> 00:01:23,590
starting from i up to 0, but not 0. So, like that
23

24
00:01:23,590 --> 00:01:26,440
you have to decide and I hope you can implement this,
24

25
00:01:26,560 --> 00:01:31,210
I have given you enough hints and I will implement this in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 10

0
1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,290
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,290 --> 00:00:06,320
So, now let's implement the number pyramid program using nested for loops.
2

3
00:00:06,330 --> 00:00:10,080
So, here I have a variable to receive the height.
3

4
00:00:10,080 --> 00:00:14,250
So now, I'm going to use for loop first to track the height.
4

5
00:00:14,400 --> 00:00:16,890
So, for i is equal to zero.
5

6
00:00:16,890 --> 00:00:20,040
I'm going to create a variable to track the height.
6

7
00:00:20,220 --> 00:00:26,030
So, and that variable I will call as 'i', so before using any variable I have to define.
7

8
00:00:26,040 --> 00:00:28,470
So, I can define right here.
8

9
00:00:28,470 --> 00:00:31,890
Let's say, uint32_t i = 0;
9

10
00:00:31,890 --> 00:00:34,560
I can do that here, or instead of doing here
10

11
00:00:34,560 --> 00:00:38,520
I can also do it inside the for loop itself.
11

12
00:00:38,530 --> 00:00:40,870
You can do that in the initialization section
12

13
00:00:40,870 --> 00:00:43,710
that is the block1, you can do this
13

14
00:00:43,720 --> 00:00:45,850
no problem with that.
14

15
00:00:45,850 --> 00:00:47,730
So, this is a for loop.
15

16
00:00:48,050 --> 00:00:51,190
So, I want to start from i = 1.
16

17
00:00:51,340 --> 00:00:54,100
So, let me start from i = 1.
17

18
00:00:54,220 --> 00:00:55,850
So, i =1,
18

19
00:00:55,900 --> 00:01:04,190
so i <= height, so I'm going to do i++ here. That seems good.
19

20
00:01:04,190 --> 00:01:06,330
This actually tracks the height.
20

21
00:01:06,440 --> 00:01:11,070
So, inside that, I'm going to use another for loop
21

22
00:01:11,190 --> 00:01:13,900
and let me create one more variable here,
22

23
00:01:13,950 --> 00:01:24,990
let's say 'j'. So, this loop actually prints these rows. Here  j = i, because I should start from
23

24
00:01:25,080 --> 00:01:27,420
i. So, here i and 
24

25
00:01:27,430 --> 00:01:28,450
j are equal.
25

26
00:01:28,450 --> 00:01:30,590
j  > 0
26

27
00:01:30,610 --> 00:01:32,220
you should not go beyond 0.
27

28
00:01:32,380 --> 00:01:36,180
For example, if it is 5, then you should go up to 1,
28

29
00:01:36,190 --> 00:01:44,170
you should not touch 0. So,  j > 0 ensures that j is always greater than 0.
29

30
00:01:44,210 --> 00:01:46,700
And here, I am going to print
30

31
00:01:46,710 --> 00:01:48,470
value of j.
31

32
00:01:49,220 --> 00:01:51,990
So, let me give two spaces here.
32

33
00:01:52,790 --> 00:01:58,940
So, after that what you have to do is, you have to keep decrementing the j, j--.
33

34
00:01:59,090 --> 00:02:01,680
So, let's take an example. Let's say i=1.
34

35
00:02:01,700 --> 00:02:03,030
Let's analyze this.
35

36
00:02:03,170 --> 00:02:04,730
Let's say height = 10,
36

37
00:02:04,760 --> 00:02:06,420
user has entered 10.
37

38
00:02:06,440 --> 00:02:09,280
So, now let's start with i = 1. First
38

39
00:02:09,290 --> 00:02:13,280
this is executed i = 1 and i < 10,
39

40
00:02:13,310 --> 00:02:15,220
that is 1 is less than 10.
40

41
00:02:15,230 --> 00:02:16,260
That's true.
41

42
00:02:16,280 --> 00:02:20,180
So, the control comes here. And here j = i,
42

43
00:02:20,240 --> 00:02:22,230
that means  j =1.
43

44
00:02:22,250 --> 00:02:24,110
So, 1 is greater than 0.
44

45
00:02:24,110 --> 00:02:25,100
That's true.
45

46
00:02:25,100 --> 00:02:27,020
So, 1 is printed,
46

47
00:02:27,020 --> 00:02:29,580
and after that j is decremented.
47

48
00:02:29,660 --> 00:02:30,870
So,  j become 0.
48

49
00:02:31,250 --> 00:02:35,820
So, again this is compared 0 is greater than 0, which is false.
49

50
00:02:35,840 --> 00:02:40,070
So, this loop breaks. And we again go to this loop
50

51
00:02:40,160 --> 00:02:43,490
and here i is incremented i++.
51

52
00:02:43,520 --> 00:02:45,630
So, like that you have to analyze this.
52

53
00:02:46,190 --> 00:02:48,770
So, this completes printing of 1 row, isn't it?
53

54
00:02:49,340 --> 00:02:55,070
So, to go to the next row you have to print \n here.
54

55
00:02:55,160 --> 00:02:57,290
That looks good.
55

56
00:02:57,290 --> 00:03:01,460
So, for the beginners it maybe little hard to grasp, but I mean the nested loops,
56

57
00:03:01,520 --> 00:03:04,390
but I suggest you to work it out on the paper.
57

58
00:03:04,400 --> 00:03:09,440
Take a pen and paper and write these statements and just try to work it out on the paper.
58

59
00:03:09,800 --> 00:03:11,860
So, that will give you more clarity
59

60
00:03:11,870 --> 00:03:17,200
and by coding more exercises so you will get to know the syntaxes better.
60

61
00:03:17,270 --> 00:03:23,490
So, enter height of pyramid let's say 2, that's great.
61

62
00:03:23,590 --> 00:03:26,050
Let's try for some bigger number,
62

63
00:03:26,080 --> 00:03:28,240
what happens if I type -2.
63

64
00:03:28,510 --> 00:03:30,850
Oh! it goes to the infinite loop.
64

65
00:03:30,940 --> 00:03:33,190
So, this condition you have to a take care.
65

66
00:03:33,430 --> 00:03:35,290
So, negative numbers are not allowed,
66

67
00:03:35,290 --> 00:03:39,940
I hope you can handle that and let me try with 8.
67

68
00:03:39,960 --> 00:03:41,610
So, that works good.
68

69
00:03:41,610 --> 00:03:44,730
So, try to reproduce this and I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 11


0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,410
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,410 --> 00:00:05,470
So, in the previous lecture you implemented  led_on exercise.
2

3
00:00:05,850 --> 00:00:08,110
So, now let's implement another exercise.
3

4
00:00:08,120 --> 00:00:09,690
The exercise is very simple.
4

5
00:00:09,690 --> 00:00:16,680
Modify the LED on program into a LED toggle program by creating a software delay between LED
5

6
00:00:16,710 --> 00:00:22,770
on and LED off. The LED should continuously toggle with certain time delay forever.
6

7
00:00:22,770 --> 00:00:25,700
So now, how to introduce time delay in a program?
7

8
00:00:26,010 --> 00:00:32,970
So, either you can use software delay or hardware delay. The software delay means introducing a time delay
8

9
00:00:32,970 --> 00:00:39,550
using loops statements and in the hardware delay we you introduce the delay using hardware peripherals
9

10
00:00:39,570 --> 00:00:40,890
such as timers.
10

11
00:00:41,250 --> 00:00:47,490
So, for this exercise we will be using software delay. So, to introduce software delay, you should make the
11

12
00:00:47,490 --> 00:00:51,220
processor busy in a software loop doing nothing.
12

13
00:00:51,270 --> 00:00:57,960
So, this method is not an accurate method to obtain the desired time delay. So, the accurate method is
13

14
00:00:57,960 --> 00:01:04,950
actually using hardware peripherals such as timers. But we don't want accuracy in this application, so we
14

15
00:01:04,950 --> 00:01:12,930
just want certain amount of delay between LED on and LED off a human observable delay . So, precision
15

16
00:01:12,990 --> 00:01:15,040
is not required in this application.
16

17
00:01:15,060 --> 00:01:21,540
So, just a random delay which can be perceived by human eyes, in order to distinguish between LED on
17

18
00:01:21,540 --> 00:01:27,320
and LED off. So, the processor will keep wasting the execution cycles until a certain delay is met.
18

19
00:01:28,050 --> 00:01:33,810
I want you to implement this exercise,a create a new project and try to modify the LED on program into
19

20
00:01:33,810 --> 00:01:41,430
a LED toggle program. And for the loop implementation you can either use a while loop or for loop. And
20

21
00:01:41,760 --> 00:01:44,740
try to attempt this exercise and I will see you in the next lecture.

I have created a new project 005led_toggle, and I have an empty main.c file and what I'm going
2

to do now is, I'm going to led_on program and I'm just copying this and less paste here.

4

So, we have to convert this program into LED toggle program.
4

5
00:00:24,250 --> 00:00:28,090
So, here is a place where we are turning on the LED.

6
00:00:28,090 --> 00:00:34,570
So, after that we have to introduce small human observable delay and we are going to create the delay
6

7
00:00:34,600 --> 00:00:40,730
by using loop statement. So, you can either use a while loop or for loop.
7

8
00:00:40,900 --> 00:00:42,340
It doesn't matter actually.
8

9
00:00:42,340 --> 00:00:45,280
So, I'm going to use a for loop to create a delay.
9

10
00:00:45,700 --> 00:00:50,410
Basically, we have to trap the processor for certain amount of delay inside a loop.
10

11
00:00:50,680 --> 00:00:52,600
So, that's all we need to do.
11

12
00:00:52,600 --> 00:00:59,490
Let me use for loop, for let me create 3 blocks here and let's create the body. So, inside the body
12

13
00:00:59,500 --> 00:01:03,250
there is nothing to execute. So, I just mention semicolon.
13

14
00:01:03,280 --> 00:01:05,050
So, how to create the delay?
14

15
00:01:05,080 --> 00:01:11,230
So, now let's create one variable and let's keep incrementing that variable, until it increases to certain
15

16
00:01:11,230 --> 00:01:12,190
number.
16

17
00:01:12,190 --> 00:01:18,010
For that, let's create a local variable. So, a local variable you can create outside the loop or you can
17

18
00:01:18,040 --> 00:01:22,070
also create here in the very first block of the for loop.
18

19
00:01:22,150 --> 00:01:26,270
So, let me create a variable let's say uint32_t i
19

20
00:01:26,280 --> 00:01:28,190
and lets initialize this to 0.
20

21
00:01:28,210 --> 00:01:33,790
And after that let's give the condition, that is conditional expression i is less than let's assume
21

22
00:01:33,850 --> 00:01:35,340
1 number, let's say 10K.
22

23
00:01:35,680 --> 00:01:39,390
So, i < 10000 and i++.
23

24
00:01:39,430 --> 00:01:43,450
So, this loop executes for 10K times.
24

25
00:01:43,450 --> 00:01:47,660
So, when i becomes 10K this loop breaks.
25

26
00:01:47,710 --> 00:01:52,690
So, that's how this loop is going to introduce some small amount of delay, because the processor will
26

27
00:01:52,690 --> 00:01:56,770
be looped inside this for loop for 10K times.
27

28
00:01:57,130 --> 00:02:04,270
But we are not sure whether 10K times of looping will result in human observable delay or not,
28

29
00:02:04,270 --> 00:02:05,470
so that we are not sure.
29

30
00:02:05,560 --> 00:02:08,990
So, we have to fix that by using trial and error method.
30

31
00:02:09,130 --> 00:02:11,380
So, instead of creating an empty body here,
31

32
00:02:11,380 --> 00:02:13,100
so what we can do is,
32

33
00:02:13,120 --> 00:02:18,340
so this code is also identical to this code. So, I can keep the semicolon here as well.
33

34
00:02:18,400 --> 00:02:20,020
So, both are identical codes.
34

35
00:02:20,710 --> 00:02:25,420
So, let's use this syntax that would be better, because code looks compact.
35

36
00:02:25,870 --> 00:02:30,150
So, now after the delay we have to turn off the LED.
36

37
00:02:30,400 --> 00:02:37,150
For that, we have to reset the 12th bit of GPIO port D output data register.
37

38
00:02:37,210 --> 00:02:39,740
So, let's reset the 12th bit,
38

39
00:02:39,850 --> 00:02:43,550
so let's use PortDOutReg.
39

40
00:02:43,600 --> 00:02:49,330
So, how do you reset? &= ~(1 << 12)
40

41
00:02:49,960 --> 00:02:52,660
and after this again we have to introduce delay.
41

42
00:02:52,810 --> 00:02:54,310
Let's give the delay here.
42

43
00:02:54,340 --> 00:02:58,210
So, this will toggle the LED, but the LED should toggle forever.
43

44
00:02:58,210 --> 00:03:04,240
So, that's why, we have to keep all these instructions inside an infinite loop.
44

45
00:03:04,270 --> 00:03:05,140
So, that's why,
45

46
00:03:05,140 --> 00:03:07,180
let's create one infinite loop.
46

47
00:03:07,470 --> 00:03:13,930
So, let me use while loop for that, while(1) will create an infinite loop or you can also use for loop.
47

48
00:03:14,230 --> 00:03:16,570
So, if you want to use for loop, you have to write like this.
48

49
00:03:17,020 --> 00:03:22,070
So, this creates a infinite loop using for loop but I would stick with the while loop.
49

50
00:03:22,120 --> 00:03:25,820
We have to keep all these 4 statements.
50

51
00:03:25,840 --> 00:03:32,210
So, now let's indent the code and let's remove this while loop, because this is a super loop. So, the LED
51

52
00:03:32,220 --> 00:03:33,880
will toggle forever.
52

53
00:03:33,880 --> 00:03:41,090
So, you just created LED toggling application and now let's compile, let's debug the program.
53

54
00:03:41,250 --> 00:03:43,980
So, let's start stepping over,
54

55
00:03:44,100 --> 00:03:45,810
let me open the disassembly here.
55

56
00:03:49,800 --> 00:03:50,820
The LED is on
56

57
00:03:50,850 --> 00:03:52,890
and after that let's execute.
57

58
00:03:52,890 --> 00:03:54,490
So, it is going to take some time,
58

59
00:03:54,510 --> 00:03:56,780
let's see whether it comes to this line or not.
59

60
00:03:56,790 --> 00:03:58,970
So, the debugger is not showing that,
60

61
00:03:58,980 --> 00:04:00,990
let me pause this.So,
61

62
00:04:01,050 --> 00:04:02,510
what we can do is here,
62

63
00:04:02,520 --> 00:04:04,140
so let's create a breakpoint here,
63

64
00:04:04,320 --> 00:04:11,940
so just double click over this blue strip here in front of the line number and let's hit run or resume.
64

65
00:04:12,720 --> 00:04:14,540
And the delay is over,
65

66
00:04:14,700 --> 00:04:20,430
and after that let's step over again and you can see that the LED is off.
66

67
00:04:20,790 --> 00:04:26,480
So, let's remove all the break points. So, you can remove all the break points from breakpoints tab.
67

68
00:04:26,490 --> 00:04:32,400
So, if you don't find this breakpoint step, then what you can do is, you can go to show view and you can
68

69
00:04:32,490 --> 00:04:37,310
click on breakpoints, and if you want to remove all the break points you can click this.
69

70
00:04:37,620 --> 00:04:39,840
Let's run that code.
70

71
00:04:39,840 --> 00:04:46,140
And here we can see that the LED is actually toggling, but we are not able to observe that, because this
71

72
00:04:46,140 --> 00:04:47,400
delay is too small
72

73
00:04:47,400 --> 00:04:49,440
so let's fix this.
73

74
00:04:49,590 --> 00:04:56,260
So, to fix this, so let's increase this count by 10 times, and let's try once again.
74

75
00:04:56,270 --> 00:04:59,370
So, this is not a correct method, but that's OK.
75

76
00:04:59,400 --> 00:05:00,900
Let's learn by hard way
76

77
00:05:00,900 --> 00:05:04,300
so there are better methods to do this, so that will see later.
77

78
00:05:04,560 --> 00:05:05,730
Let's try once again.
78

79
00:05:06,000 --> 00:05:13,470
So, now let's hit resume and you can see that, now we are able to see LED toggling. The toggling is a little
79

80
00:05:13,470 --> 00:05:14,220
fast,
80

81
00:05:14,220 --> 00:05:15,240
so what we can do is,
81

82
00:05:15,960 --> 00:05:20,480
so let's increase this to 200K or 300K.
82

83
00:05:21,030 --> 00:05:28,990
And now let's hit resume, and you can see that we are able to see the LED toggling clearly.
83

84
00:05:28,990 --> 00:05:37,600
So, if you just check the disassembly of this program. So, let's go to the delay statement here for loop
84

85
00:05:37,610 --> 00:05:40,930
statement and let's execute that.
85

86
00:05:44,030 --> 00:05:47,290
If you observe the disassembly of the delay,
86

87
00:05:47,390 --> 00:05:51,120
so let's step over until we reach for loop.
87

88
00:05:51,180 --> 00:05:52,640
Here it is.
88

89
00:05:52,640 --> 00:05:55,100
So, this is a implementation of for loop
89

90
00:05:55,310 --> 00:05:58,230
and it actually takes couple of clock cycles.
90

91
00:05:58,250 --> 00:06:05,420
So, if you calculate how many clock cycles it takes to execute these instructions, then you can calculate
91

92
00:06:05,540 --> 00:06:08,470
how much delay is actually caused by this for loop.
92

93
00:06:09,260 --> 00:06:15,260
So, that discussion we will do later. But in one loop the processor actually goes through all these instruction.
93

94
00:06:15,350 --> 00:06:18,500
So, it surely consumes couple of clock cycles.
94

95
00:06:18,570 --> 00:06:26,120
That's about your LED toggling application, and in the next lecture let's attempt one more exercise
95

96
00:06:26,480 --> 00:06:29,330
where we are going to read from the pin.
96

97
00:06:29,330 --> 00:06:31,460
So, this is actually writing to a pin.
97

98
00:06:31,520 --> 00:06:34,910
So, in the next lecture let's read from the pin and I'll see you in the next lecture.

section 21---Type qualifier 'const'

video 1


0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,110
Welcome back to the course.
1

2
00:00:01,140 --> 00:00:05,780
So, from this lecture onwards we are going to learn about type qualifiers in 'C'.
2

3
00:00:05,790 --> 00:00:07,700
All right. Let's get started.
3

4
00:00:07,740 --> 00:00:12,920
So, there are two important type qualifiers in 'C' const and volatile.
4

5
00:00:13,050 --> 00:00:15,480
So, these are very much important qualifiers
5

6
00:00:15,480 --> 00:00:16,970
you should understand to write
6

7
00:00:16,970 --> 00:00:21,080
proper and bug free code for your embedded device.
7

8
00:00:21,210 --> 00:00:28,410
It is also true that, students usually get confused about understanding the proper usage of these qualifiers.
8

9
00:00:28,770 --> 00:00:34,350
Because, many text books they mention very little about these qualifier usages
9

10
00:00:34,350 --> 00:00:39,650
and students usually don't have clear idea why they should use them in the program.
10

11
00:00:40,020 --> 00:00:46,620
And understanding about these qualifiers also helps you during interviews to answer questions about
11

12
00:00:46,680 --> 00:00:52,110
these qualifiers and complex syntaxes using these qualifiers.
12

13
00:00:52,110 --> 00:00:59,040
So, I promise most of your doubts about these qualifiers will come to an end today with clear examples
13

14
00:00:59,070 --> 00:01:00,390
and demonstrations.
14

15
00:01:00,420 --> 00:01:06,840
I will show you the importance of these qualifiers especially of volatile qualifier in the code.
15

16
00:01:06,840 --> 00:01:14,040
OK. So, when you understand how things work, then you can write a very much clean code involving these
16

17
00:01:14,040 --> 00:01:16,950
qualifiers to make your code bug free.
17

18
00:01:16,950 --> 00:01:17,970
Now, let's get started.
18

19
00:01:17,970 --> 00:01:22,770
First let's understand what exactly is a meaning of the word qualifier.
19

20
00:01:22,770 --> 00:01:29,940
If you look at the English meaning of this word, a word or phrase, especially an adjective, used to
20

21
00:01:30,120 --> 00:01:34,430
attribute a quality to another word. In programming
21

22
00:01:34,440 --> 00:01:35,980
also the same case.
22

23
00:01:36,030 --> 00:01:42,680
So, we use these qualifiers that is 'const' and 'volatile' to attribute a feature to a variable.
23

24
00:01:42,780 --> 00:01:47,790
First let's get started with 'const' type qualifier, so then  will move on to volatile.
24

25
00:01:48,000 --> 00:01:56,960
So, 'const' is a type qualifier in 'C' used to enforce read-only feature on variables. So, this actually
25

26
00:01:56,960 --> 00:02:02,380
enforces a read only feature or read only attribute onto the variable.
26

27
00:02:02,390 --> 00:02:05,070
So, now consider this variable definition.
27

28
00:02:05,240 --> 00:02:12,740
So, 'data1' is a variable of type uint8 and that is initialized to 10. And in the program you can
28

29
00:02:12,830 --> 00:02:15,760
modify the value of that variable, that is allowed.
29

30
00:02:15,770 --> 00:02:18,940
So, 'data1' value can be modified throughout the program.
30

31
00:02:18,950 --> 00:02:20,240
No issues with that.
31

32
00:02:20,270 --> 00:02:28,760
So, but when you use const qualifier with a variable definition, so then the feature of this variable
32

33
00:02:28,790 --> 00:02:37,640
changes, it becomes read only. Here 'data2' is called a constant variable or read only variable. And
33

34
00:02:37,640 --> 00:02:44,120
in the program if you try to change the value of that variable, then this will trigger a compilation
34

35
00:02:44,150 --> 00:02:44,510
error.
35

36
00:02:44,510 --> 00:02:46,160
So, this is not allowed.
36

37
00:02:46,190 --> 00:02:50,120
So, you have to initialize that variable when you define it.
37

38
00:02:50,120 --> 00:02:54,410
After that you are not allowed to change the value of that  variable.
38

39
00:02:54,480 --> 00:02:57,480
Because, now it is read only variable.
39

40
00:02:57,510 --> 00:03:00,870
Let's analyze this statement. Here
40

41
00:03:00,920 --> 00:03:05,670
uint8_t you know that what it is, it is actually the data type.
41

42
00:03:05,690 --> 00:03:09,680
So, we also call it as type specifier of a variable.
42

43
00:03:09,740 --> 00:03:10,370
So, now here
43

44
00:03:10,370 --> 00:03:14,120
this is called type qualifier of a variable.
44

45
00:03:14,120 --> 00:03:18,860
And this is a variable and in this case it is read only variable.
45

46
00:03:18,860 --> 00:03:22,250
And here, the value of the data1 is 10
46

47
00:03:22,310 --> 00:03:26,910
and it is fixed at 10. The value of the data1 can't be modified.
47

48
00:03:26,950 --> 00:03:28,280
Let's move forward.
48

49
00:03:28,280 --> 00:03:34,970
So, let's understand the syntax how you should use this 'const' keyword in the variable definition.
49

50
00:03:34,970 --> 00:03:38,280
There are two possible ways you can either write it like this,
50

51
00:03:38,300 --> 00:03:45,320
that means you first start with a 'const' that is a type qualifier, followed by a type specifier, followed
51

52
00:03:45,320 --> 00:03:46,790
by a variable name.
52

53
00:03:46,820 --> 00:03:48,170
You can do like this
53

54
00:03:48,170 --> 00:03:50,740
no problem, or you can also do like this.
54

55
00:03:50,780 --> 00:03:57,170
Start with the type specifier, followed by the type qualifier that is 'const', and then variable name.
55

56
00:03:57,350 --> 00:04:03,290
So, 'const' keyword can appear before type specifier or after type specifier.
56

57
00:04:03,590 --> 00:04:08,620
So, both are allowed. So, that's why, both statements are identical,
57

58
00:04:08,620 --> 00:04:14,740
no issues with that. But, I usually prefer this method, that is first you mentioned the data type that
58

59
00:04:14,740 --> 00:04:21,660
is the type specifier, and then the type qualifier. Because it helps you to understand the complex statements
59

60
00:04:21,700 --> 00:04:28,750
better. Here, if you want to understand this statement or if you want to convert this statement into English,
60

61
00:04:28,840 --> 00:04:33,220
then what you should do is, you should first identify the variable name.
61

62
00:04:33,400 --> 00:04:37,750
So, this is a variable name and then move to your left hand side.
62

63
00:04:37,750 --> 00:04:39,790
So, this is how you should read it.
63

64
00:04:39,910 --> 00:04:47,770
Data1 is a constant variable of type uint8. So, that's how you should read it.
64

65
00:04:47,800 --> 00:04:50,160
So, it helps you to read the statement better,
65

66
00:04:50,170 --> 00:04:52,750
that's why, always start with the type specifier.
66

67
00:04:52,930 --> 00:04:54,970
I hope you remember this
67

68
00:04:54,970 --> 00:05:02,230
and now let's move on to the next slide, and let's understand about constants of a variable.
68

69
00:05:02,230 --> 00:05:09,790
So, by using the 'const' keyword, you are just making a promise to the compiler that you won't try to
69

70
00:05:09,790 --> 00:05:13,900
modify the content of the variable using its name.
70

71
00:05:14,080 --> 00:05:20,710
If you try to modify the variable by its name, the compiler stops you by throwing an error.
71

72
00:05:20,740 --> 00:05:26,080
So, that's actually compile time error. So, you can still modify the content of the variable by using its
72

73
00:05:26,080 --> 00:05:33,410
address. So, 'const' variable  means that you cannot change the value by its name, but you can change the
73

74
00:05:33,410 --> 00:05:36,340
value of that variable by its address.
74

75
00:05:36,350 --> 00:05:38,580
Let's write a small code to understand this.
75

76
00:05:39,570 --> 00:05:44,460
So, now let's write a small program to understand constants of a variable.
76

77
00:05:44,460 --> 00:05:50,580
So, now let me create a variable of type uint8_t data = 10;
77

78
00:05:51,240 --> 00:05:54,390
and let me print the value of that variable.
78

79
00:05:54,570 --> 00:06:03,390
So, if I try to change the value of data is equal to 50, then there is no issues with that. Compiler compiles it
79

80
00:06:03,420 --> 00:06:04,600
no problem.
80

81
00:06:04,740 --> 00:06:10,140
Let me make this read only. So, what I do is, I introduce a 'const' keyword here.
81

82
00:06:10,230 --> 00:06:16,860
When I try to compile this, you can see that there is an error. The error says that, assignment of read
82

83
00:06:16,860 --> 00:06:17,940
only variable.
83

84
00:06:17,940 --> 00:06:22,950
So, now this has converted into read only variable, so that's why, compiler is saying that this is not
84

85
00:06:22,950 --> 00:06:23,370
allowed.
85

86
00:06:23,610 --> 00:06:25,800
But, still I can change the value of this data.
86

87
00:06:25,830 --> 00:06:27,560
So, this is how I do it?
87

88
00:06:27,570 --> 00:06:34,080
So, what I do is, I simply create a pointer ptr and I initialize the pointer variable with the address
88

89
00:06:34,200 --> 00:06:35,050
of the variable.
89

90
00:06:35,130 --> 00:06:43,160
I can do this. Then, I write whatever value I like into the address pointed by ptr.
90

91
00:06:43,290 --> 00:06:47,260
So, that's why, *ptr is equal to I do 50.
91

92
00:06:47,340 --> 00:06:50,870
So, let's print here and here.
92

93
00:06:51,330 --> 00:06:53,100
Let's execute, what happens?
93

94
00:06:53,100 --> 00:06:54,990
Let's try to build a project.
94

95
00:06:55,170 --> 00:06:56,340
So, there is an warning.
95

96
00:06:56,340 --> 00:07:03,190
Now the warning is because, ptr is a pointer variable of type uint8 *.
96

97
00:07:03,210 --> 00:07:13,810
So, this is a pointer entity of type  uint8_t const *.
97

98
00:07:13,810 --> 00:07:15,340
So, that's why, there is a mismatch.
98

99
00:07:15,340 --> 00:07:23,590
So, what we can do is, you can just type cast this uint8_t const *. Let's compile. Yes, the compilation
99

100
00:07:23,590 --> 00:07:24,330
is fine.
100

101
00:07:24,580 --> 00:07:27,720
If I execute this, you can see that the value is changed.
101

102
00:07:27,790 --> 00:07:32,570
So, that's why, you should remember that 'const' doesn't mean that the value never changes,
102

103
00:07:32,620 --> 00:07:40,060
its only programming safety feature to ensure that the programmer shouldn't try to modify the value. Because,
103

104
00:07:40,060 --> 00:07:46,400
compiler alerts you, if anyone tries to change the value of that variable by its name.
104

105
00:07:46,450 --> 00:07:50,450
So, that's why, 'const' is used as a programming safety feature.
105

106
00:07:50,560 --> 00:07:56,830
Don't worry, we'll see applicability and use cases of a const variable as we make progress. In the next
106

107
00:07:56,830 --> 00:07:57,160
lecture,
107

108
00:07:57,160 --> 00:08:01,150
lets understand about placement of 'const' variables in memory.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,230
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,230 --> 00:00:07,230
So, in this lecture lets understand about placement of 'const' variables in memory. So, all local
2

3
00:00:07,230 --> 00:00:11,580
const variables are just like non-const variables
3

4
00:00:11,580 --> 00:00:16,110
as far as memory placement is concerned. They are placed in RAM.
4

5
00:00:16,140 --> 00:00:24,240
The only speciality of a constant variable is, it is read only. All global const variables are stored
5

6
00:00:24,240 --> 00:00:25,120
in ROM
6

7
00:00:25,170 --> 00:00:32,130
that is read only memory or FLASH. So, this also further depends on linker script rules and the hardware
7

8
00:00:32,160 --> 00:00:38,880
on which the code runs. In STM32 target hardware that is in our embedded hardware,
8

9
00:00:38,880 --> 00:00:43,130
all global const variables live in FLASH memory.
9

10
00:00:43,170 --> 00:00:51,480
So, when you try to modify the const variable using its address, operation has no effect. Because flash
10

11
00:00:51,480 --> 00:01:00,850
memory of the microcontroller is write-protected. So, by default, what happens is, all global const variables
11

12
00:01:00,940 --> 00:01:04,660
will be stored in read only memory or in the FLASH.
12

13
00:01:04,870 --> 00:01:10,170
So, that is FLASH is nothing but code space and code space is usually write-protected.
13

14
00:01:10,210 --> 00:01:16,960
You cannot just write into different flash addresses using pointers in your code. Because that has no
14

15
00:01:16,960 --> 00:01:22,570
effect. Because the code space or the flash memory is right protected by default.
15

16
00:01:23,050 --> 00:01:29,380
Let's go back to our program and let's remove this statement from here and let's put that in the global
16

17
00:01:29,380 --> 00:01:30,400
section.
17

18
00:01:30,640 --> 00:01:34,880
So, here data is a read only variable in global scope.
18

19
00:01:34,900 --> 00:01:36,630
It's a global read only now.
19

20
00:01:36,730 --> 00:01:38,130
Let's see what happens.
20

21
00:01:39,690 --> 00:01:41,810
So, now let me execute this program
21

22
00:01:41,850 --> 00:01:45,420
and here it is the program crashes.
22

23
00:01:45,420 --> 00:01:46,680
So, why is that?
23

24
00:01:46,680 --> 00:01:54,630
Because, the compiler has placed that read only variable in the read only section or in the read only
24

25
00:01:54,630 --> 00:01:55,230
memory.
25

26
00:01:55,290 --> 00:01:57,790
So, the operating system expects that
26

27
00:01:57,830 --> 00:02:02,450
so no one should change the address where that variable value is placed.
27

28
00:02:02,490 --> 00:02:03,650
That is protected.
28

29
00:02:03,720 --> 00:02:10,680
But what you did was, you used a pointer way and you try to modify that particular address which is
29

30
00:02:10,800 --> 00:02:11,760
write-protected.
30

31
00:02:11,880 --> 00:02:17,640
So, that's why, the operating system detected that activity and the application crashed.
31

32
00:02:17,640 --> 00:02:23,970
If you attempt this on your target hardware that is on your embedded target like STM32
32

33
00:02:23,970 --> 00:02:32,160
microcontroller, then your application will not crash but the operation what you just did has no effect.
33

34
00:02:32,160 --> 00:02:39,510
So, that's why, always remember that modifying the value of the global const variable using its address
34

35
00:02:39,660 --> 00:02:41,660
has undefined behavior.
35

36
00:02:41,700 --> 00:02:43,560
You should never try to do that.
36

37
00:02:43,560 --> 00:02:49,670
So, that's why, use a const as a safety feature to guard your variable.
37

38
00:02:49,710 --> 00:02:55,590
So, when someone tries to change the value of this variable deep inside the program, then the compiler
38

39
00:02:55,620 --> 00:03:02,010
alerts him. A throwing an error saying you are trying to change the value of the read only variable.
39

40
00:03:02,280 --> 00:03:09,240
So, that's why, you should use the const qualifier as a guard to your data. In the next lecture
40

41
00:03:09,260 --> 00:03:13,430
we'll understand about const pointer and different case study.
41

42
00:03:13,430 --> 00:03:14,490
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,380
Hey, welcome back to the course.
1

2
00:00:01,380 --> 00:00:07,230
So, in this lecture let's understand about const pointer and will go through different case study involving
2

3
00:00:07,230 --> 00:00:08,760
const with pointer.
3

4
00:00:09,180 --> 00:00:11,610
So, the first case constant data.
4

5
00:00:11,640 --> 00:00:14,990
So, this actually we encountered in the previous lecture.
5

6
00:00:15,000 --> 00:00:17,610
So, this is a case of constant data.
6

7
00:00:18,030 --> 00:00:19,860
So, what are the use cases of this?
7

8
00:00:19,950 --> 00:00:26,040
You can use this case to define mathematical constants in the program. Like to define the value of pi
8

9
00:00:26,100 --> 00:00:30,630
or any mathematical constants,  which you use in your application.
9

10
00:00:30,630 --> 00:00:40,580
The second case is using const keyword with pointers. The case 2 which I'm going to explain is modifiable
10

11
00:00:40,580 --> 00:00:43,130
pointer and constant data.
11

12
00:00:43,460 --> 00:00:48,980
So here, just observe this statement without const keyword.
12

13
00:00:49,010 --> 00:00:52,690
So, this is nothing but the pointer variable definition.
13

14
00:00:52,700 --> 00:01:00,280
So, this is a pointer variable and it is initialize to this address. Just introduce const here.
14

15
00:01:00,280 --> 00:01:05,080
This is the case of modifiable pointer and constant data.
15

16
00:01:05,080 --> 00:01:13,980
That means, here the pointer pData is modifiable but the data pointed by the pData cannot be modifiable,
16

17
00:01:13,980 --> 00:01:20,670
that is read only. So, that means here the data is read only not a pointer remember.
17

18
00:01:20,760 --> 00:01:27,410
So, we can say that pData is a pointer pointing to read-only data.
18

19
00:01:27,570 --> 00:01:30,350
So, now let's understand how to read this statement.
19

20
00:01:30,360 --> 00:01:35,020
So first, identify the variable, this is a variable and move to the left.
20

21
00:01:35,100 --> 00:01:43,260
So, pData is a pointer pointing to constant data of type uint8_t. So, that's how you should
21

22
00:01:43,260 --> 00:01:45,890
read it. So, this is allowed.
22

23
00:01:45,900 --> 00:01:49,710
That means you can change the value of the pointer variable.
23

24
00:01:49,800 --> 00:01:53,630
That means you can assign different addresses to this pointer variable.
24

25
00:01:53,880 --> 00:01:55,260
But this is not allowed.
25

26
00:01:55,260 --> 00:02:01,700
So, that means you cannot be able to modify the data pointed by the pointer.
26

27
00:02:01,840 --> 00:02:07,750
So, now what is a use case of this case, that is modifiable pointer and constant data. Here,
27

28
00:02:07,750 --> 00:02:09,490
I just want to give one example.
28

29
00:02:09,490 --> 00:02:13,090
Consider this function copy_src_to_dst.
29

30
00:02:13,120 --> 00:02:20,530
So, this function actually copies data from source pointer to destination pointer. So, here when you take
30

31
00:02:20,530 --> 00:02:26,690
a look into this function implementation, so whatever the data which is pointed by the source that is
31

32
00:02:26,690 --> 00:02:31,780
source pointer needs to be copied into the destination. And then destination pointer should be increased
32

33
00:02:31,810 --> 00:02:37,300
to point to the next location and source pointer needs to be incremented to point to the next location,
33

34
00:02:37,540 --> 00:02:40,640
until all length bytes are copied. Here,
34

35
00:02:40,690 --> 00:02:47,050
let's say, the guy who is implementing this function if he ever tries to do something like this src
35

36
00:02:47,080 --> 00:02:52,160
is equal to some other value. The compiler will not alert him
36

37
00:02:52,170 --> 00:02:54,830
in this case. The compiler will be quiet. 
37

38
00:02:55,020 --> 00:03:00,540
But, what is a goal of this function? The goal of this function is to copy whatever there in the source
38

39
00:03:00,570 --> 00:03:07,540
to destination, the data which is pointed by the source pointer should not be modified. But if there is
39

40
00:03:07,570 --> 00:03:11,510
any such activity then it will not be reported to the programmer.
40

41
00:03:11,590 --> 00:03:14,350
So, that's why, this method is prone to mistake.
41

42
00:03:14,350 --> 00:03:18,600
So, that's why, you can change the prototype of this function to this one.
42

43
00:03:19,180 --> 00:03:22,800
So, this is actually the defensive implementation.
43

44
00:03:22,900 --> 00:03:26,680
What you did was, you just mentioned const here, const data.
44

45
00:03:27,040 --> 00:03:34,180
So, that's why, if you just try to do this let's say, then the compiler triggers an error saying that, hey
45

46
00:03:34,330 --> 00:03:39,670
the data which is pointed by the source should be constant but you are trying to change it,
46

47
00:03:39,700 --> 00:03:41,770
so that's a mistake.
47

48
00:03:41,770 --> 00:03:49,370
So, a programmer will be alerted here. So, a programmer when sees this prototype, then there is a possibility
48

49
00:03:49,370 --> 00:03:53,780
that the programmer may change the data which is pointed by the source.
49

50
00:03:53,800 --> 00:03:59,500
So because, there is no guard here for the source. He may think that it's OK to change the data pointed
50

51
00:03:59,510 --> 00:04:05,360
by the source. But when the programmer sees this prototype then he clearly understands that the data
51

52
00:04:05,370 --> 00:04:07,760
pointed by the source should not be modified.
52

53
00:04:07,760 --> 00:04:10,860
That's why, I would always go with this implementation.
53

54
00:04:10,880 --> 00:04:14,990
I mean the code implementation is same, but the prototype implementation is different here.
54

55
00:04:15,140 --> 00:04:18,680
So, that's the only difference compared to this implementation.
55

56
00:04:18,680 --> 00:04:24,840
So, you can see the data guarding or pointer guarding in all your professional code implementations.
56

57
00:04:24,920 --> 00:04:31,220
For example, consider the prototype of open system call in Linux. Now you can see that, this is a case
57

58
00:04:31,220 --> 00:04:35,470
of modifiable pointer and constant data.
58

59
00:04:35,480 --> 00:04:38,370
So, this is a prototype of write system call.
59

60
00:04:38,570 --> 00:04:39,790
So, what write
60

61
00:04:39,790 --> 00:04:49,150
system call does? So, writes up to count bytes from buffer to file referred by the file descriptor. So, this
61

62
00:04:49,150 --> 00:04:50,780
buffer is given by the user,
62

63
00:04:50,850 --> 00:04:51,710
that's a user data.
63

64
00:04:51,790 --> 00:04:53,720
So, user data has to be constant.
64

65
00:04:53,770 --> 00:04:55,660
The implementation should not modify that.
65

66
00:04:55,660 --> 00:05:02,920
So, that's why, it is guarded with const and I hope you understood the use case scenarios and use const
66

67
00:05:02,980 --> 00:05:06,190
generously wherever you find the chance to use it.
67

68
00:05:06,730 --> 00:05:10,110
And in the next lecture, let's understand
68

69
00:05:10,110 --> 00:05:14,710
Case 3. Case 3 is modifiable data and constant pointer.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 4

0
1
00:00:00,060 --> 00:00:00,810
Hey, welcome back.
1

2
00:00:00,810 --> 00:00:07,890
So, now let's continue our discussion with const. The case 3 is modifiable data and constant pointer.
2

3
00:00:07,890 --> 00:00:15,030
Just observe the position of the keyword const now it is positioned inside the asterisk(*).
3

4
00:00:15,060 --> 00:00:19,290
So, this is a case of constant pointer and modifiable data.
4

5
00:00:19,830 --> 00:00:28,670
So, how do you read this?  pData is a constant pointer pointing to a data of type uint8_t.
5

6
00:00:28,890 --> 00:00:35,970
So, here the pointer pData is read-only but the data pointed by the pData can be modifiable. So, we can
6

7
00:00:35,970 --> 00:00:40,530
say that pData is a read only pointer pointing to modifiable data.
7

8
00:00:40,980 --> 00:00:42,570
So, this is not allowed.
8

9
00:00:42,660 --> 00:00:48,720
So, once the value of pData is fixed you cannot assign the pointer variable with new address.
9

10
00:00:48,720 --> 00:00:51,370
But this is allowed.
10

11
00:00:51,380 --> 00:00:53,530
So, what is the use case of this?
11

12
00:00:53,570 --> 00:00:58,220
The use case is to improve the readability and guard the pointer variables.
12

13
00:00:58,250 --> 00:01:00,020
For example, consider this function.
13

14
00:01:00,080 --> 00:01:06,320
So, this function updates the details of age and salary into the pointer provided by the caller.
14

15
00:01:06,440 --> 00:01:13,650
When this function is called, caller actually provides the address here, in order to update the user's
15

16
00:01:13,670 --> 00:01:15,260
age and salary.
16

17
00:01:15,290 --> 00:01:21,880
So, this function should populate the user's age and salary into the corresponding addresses.
17

18
00:01:22,250 --> 00:01:28,460
So, this function should not modify this address, so otherwise it will be a chaos.
18

19
00:01:28,460 --> 00:01:32,570
So, that's why, this pointer is guarded by const here.
19

20
00:01:32,900 --> 00:01:37,290
So because, this function receives addresses from some other function.
20

21
00:01:37,370 --> 00:01:43,010
So, that's why, the addresses are guarded. So, now let's move forward.
21

22
00:01:43,030 --> 00:01:46,450
The next case is constant data and constant pointer.
22

23
00:01:46,840 --> 00:01:49,500
So, this is how you write that.
23

24
00:01:49,510 --> 00:01:52,540
So, constant pointer, constant data.
24

25
00:01:52,540 --> 00:01:53,920
So, how do you read this?
25

26
00:01:54,000 --> 00:02:00,960
pData is a const pointer pointing to const data of type uint8.
26

27
00:02:00,970 --> 00:02:08,560
So, here the pointer pData is read-only and the data pointed by the pData is also read-only. So, we can
27

28
00:02:08,560 --> 00:02:12,750
say that pData is a read only pointer pointing to read-only data.
28

29
00:02:13,450 --> 00:02:14,950
So, what is the use case of this?
29

30
00:02:14,980 --> 00:02:20,710
So, this is usually used whenever you want to read some data from the status register of the
30

31
00:02:20,710 --> 00:02:24,070
hardware. The status register should not be modified actually.
31

32
00:02:24,070 --> 00:02:27,910
So, if you modify the status register, then it will cause system to fail.
32

33
00:02:27,910 --> 00:02:35,410
So, that's why, you can guard the pointer of the status register using double const here. A guard for data
33

34
00:02:35,440 --> 00:02:37,170
as well as guard for pointer.
34

35
00:02:37,180 --> 00:02:43,780
So, this function, for example it reads and returns the content of the status register provided a pointed
35

36
00:02:43,780 --> 00:02:45,130
by the pStatusReg.
36

37
00:02:45,850 --> 00:02:46,910
So, accidental write
37

38
00:02:46,920 --> 00:02:50,140
to status register may cause unpredictable consequences.
38

39
00:02:50,170 --> 00:02:56,320
So, that is actually guarded by this part and the address of the status register should not be modified
39

40
00:02:56,350 --> 00:02:57,850
inside the implementation.
40

41
00:02:57,850 --> 00:03:02,400
So, that's why, tha t is regarded by this part.
41

42
00:03:02,410 --> 00:03:11,020
So, now let's see the summary of a const type qualifier. So, it adds some safety while you write code.
42

43
00:03:11,020 --> 00:03:17,030
The compiler warns you when you trying to change the value of the const variable. Since a constant
43

44
00:03:17,030 --> 00:03:21,140
variable doesn't change, it has only one state throughout the program.
44

45
00:03:21,190 --> 00:03:24,250
So, you need not track its various states.
45

46
00:03:24,460 --> 00:03:33,340
So, usage of const improves the readability of your program. So, use it generously to enforce pointer access
46

47
00:03:33,340 --> 00:03:37,410
restrictions while writing functions and function prototypes.
47

48
00:03:37,630 --> 00:03:44,190
So, whenever you are in charge of writing function prototypes, then you should use const variable generously
48

49
00:03:44,210 --> 00:03:45,380
remember that.
49

50
00:03:45,640 --> 00:03:50,070
So, it may also help compiler to generate optimized code.
50

51
00:03:50,740 --> 00:03:53,280
So, now we actually finished discussion on the const,
51

52
00:03:53,380 --> 00:03:59,610
but now let's use it in our embedded code. For that, let's take our earlier exercise where we implemented
52

53
00:03:59,620 --> 00:04:05,040
LED_on exercise and let's see whether there is any provision to use const keyword.
53

54
00:04:05,170 --> 00:04:06,940
And I'll see you in the next lecture, 
54

55
00:04:06,940 --> 00:04:09,220
and after that I will cover volatile.


==================================================================================

section 22---pin-read

video 1


0
1
00:00:00,070 --> 00:00:01,410
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,410 --> 00:00:03,840
So, now let's do one more exercise.
2

3
00:00:03,840 --> 00:00:09,060
The exercise is write a program which reads the status of the pin PA0.
3

4
00:00:09,180 --> 00:00:16,430
If the status of PA0 is LOW, then turn off the on board LED and if the status of the PA0 is HIGH,
4

5
00:00:16,470 --> 00:00:22,980
then turn on the LED. So, change the status of PA0 pin manually by connecting between GND
5

6
00:00:22,980 --> 00:00:25,140
and  VDD points of the board.
6

7
00:00:25,140 --> 00:00:28,430
So, the GND point of the board is actually 0v
7

8
00:00:28,800 --> 00:00:32,460
and the VDD point of the board gives you 3.3v.
8

9
00:00:32,580 --> 00:00:35,710
So, when the PA0 is connected to VDD point,
9

10
00:00:35,730 --> 00:00:41,130
so when you read the PA0 pin then you should read HIGH or digitally it is 1.
10

11
00:00:41,610 --> 00:00:47,270
So, when you observe a PA0 HIGH, then you should turn on the LED, otherwise you turn off the
11

12
00:00:47,260 --> 00:00:47,900
LED.
12

13
00:00:48,150 --> 00:00:54,500
And as I said you have to change the status of the PA0 manually by taking one jumper wire.
13

14
00:00:54,660 --> 00:01:02,520
So, just take one connector wire and connect PA0, so PA0 is right here, PA0 to GND point.
14

15
00:01:02,520 --> 00:01:10,480
So, if you do this then the LED should turn off,  and if you connect PA0 to VDD point, then the
15

16
00:01:10,480 --> 00:01:15,310
program should turn on the LED.So, try to implement this exercise.
16

17
00:01:15,310 --> 00:01:17,140
So, now I want you to do this exercise,
17

18
00:01:17,170 --> 00:01:22,580
but before doing this exercise let me clear some points and let me give you some hints.
18

19
00:01:22,660 --> 00:01:25,950
First of all you have to check whether PA0 is a free
19

20
00:01:25,960 --> 00:01:27,260
IO or not.
20

21
00:01:27,400 --> 00:01:33,400
For example, on the discovery board PA0 is a free IO you can use that, but if you are using some other
21

22
00:01:33,400 --> 00:01:36,130
board then PA0 may not be available on the board.
22

23
00:01:36,130 --> 00:01:42,130
So, you have to explore the user manual of the board to understand the status of PA0, whether it is
23

24
00:01:42,130 --> 00:01:43,000
free or
24

25
00:01:43,090 --> 00:01:49,150
it is used by some other functionality of the board circuit. And after that on the discovery board, so on
25

26
00:01:49,150 --> 00:01:50,410
board LED is PD12,
26

27
00:01:50,440 --> 00:01:55,600
so if your on board LED is connected to some other pin and then you have to handle that. First,
27

28
00:01:55,690 --> 00:01:58,510
let's go to the user manual of the board.
28

29
00:01:58,570 --> 00:02:03,550
So, now I am in the user manual of the board STM32F4 discovery
29

30
00:02:03,550 --> 00:02:08,630
and here I am in the section 6.11 Extension connectors.
30

31
00:02:08,680 --> 00:02:12,780
And here I am just checking this table and the status of the PA0
31

32
00:02:12,790 --> 00:02:17,140
you can see in the column free I/O it is mentioned as  PA0,
32

33
00:02:17,140 --> 00:02:23,140
that means it is available as a free I/O pin. So, you can use that for your I/O functionality, that is
33

34
00:02:23,140 --> 00:02:25,390
general purpose I/O functionality.
34

35
00:02:25,480 --> 00:02:31,380
So, if this place is blank for a pin like this. Suppose, let's consider PA4,
35

36
00:02:31,450 --> 00:02:37,210
so here it is mentioned that PA4 is not actually available as a free I/O.
36

37
00:02:37,300 --> 00:02:39,450
So, here it is blank.
37

38
00:02:39,460 --> 00:02:46,390
So, in that case you cannot use a this pin for general purpose I/O functionality.
38

39
00:02:46,390 --> 00:02:48,220
So, we can definitely use PA0.
39

40
00:02:48,310 --> 00:02:49,690
So, no issues with that.
40

41
00:02:49,750 --> 00:02:56,170
So. now let me give you some hints, the hint number one is PA0 should be in input mode. Because you are
41

42
00:02:56,170 --> 00:02:57,980
reading from that pin, isn't it?
42

43
00:02:58,000 --> 00:03:01,560
And the second hint is to read from pin PA0,
43

44
00:03:01,570 --> 00:03:09,280
your code has to read PORT-A's input data register. So, try to code by using these hints and I'll see
44

45
00:03:09,280 --> 00:03:11,020
you in the next lecture with the solution.
