video 2

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:07,620
So, I just created a new project  006pin_read and let's implement exercise.
1

2
00:00:07,740 --> 00:00:15,240
So, first of all what I do is, I just go to my led_on program and I will just copy this code, because 
2

3
00:00:15,320 --> 00:00:18,180
anyway we are going to turn on and turn off the LED.
3

4
00:00:18,180 --> 00:00:23,000
So, I go to my new project main.c and I paste here.
4

5
00:00:23,040 --> 00:00:28,360
So, first of all we are using Port A in this case. Before using Port A
5

6
00:00:28,440 --> 00:00:30,870
we have to first turn on the clock for that.
6

7
00:00:31,260 --> 00:00:38,520
So, because we did the same thing for Port D. Because Port D is handled by GPIO D peripheral. In the
7

8
00:00:38,520 --> 00:00:42,940
same way Port A is handled by GPIOA peripheral.
8

9
00:00:43,110 --> 00:00:46,860
So, that's why, we have to first turn on the clock for that.
9

10
00:00:46,860 --> 00:00:51,040
So here, what we did was, enable the clock for GPIOD peripheral,
10

11
00:00:51,120 --> 00:00:55,630
so now you have to enable the clock for GPIOA peripheral as well.
11

12
00:00:55,860 --> 00:01:04,590
So, let's change this comment GPIOA peripheral. So, enable the clock for GPIOD and GPIOA peripherals in AHB1ENR.
12

13
00:01:04,920 --> 00:01:11,530
This register will be same because all these GPIO peripherals are hanging on AHB1 bus.
13

14
00:01:11,580 --> 00:01:15,690
So, that's why, register will be same but bit fields may be different.
14

15
00:01:15,690 --> 00:01:22,480
Let me remove this comment. So, for GPIOD you actually enabled the bit position 3.
15

16
00:01:22,710 --> 00:01:25,530
Let's explore what we should do for GPIOA
16

17
00:01:25,590 --> 00:01:26,310
peripheral.
17

18
00:01:26,850 --> 00:01:34,590
Let's go to the reference manual of the microcontroller to understand about the AHB1ENR register,
18

19
00:01:34,950 --> 00:01:46,040
and I'm going to reset and clock control and here I'm going to AHB1ENR. So, the bit position 3 was for
19

20
00:01:46,040 --> 00:01:50,360
GPIOD, the bit position 0 is for GPIOA.
20

21
00:01:50,420 --> 00:01:59,660
Let's make a change to our code. What I do is, I just copy this and I paste here, and here I use 0.
21

22
00:01:59,700 --> 00:02:02,780
This is for GPIOA and this is for GPIOD.
22

23
00:02:05,560 --> 00:02:09,180
So, these codes are for Port D pin number 12.
23

24
00:02:09,310 --> 00:02:10,680
So, that is for our LED. So,
24

25
00:02:10,810 --> 00:02:12,760
now let's change the comment here.
25

26
00:02:12,790 --> 00:02:14,680
So, now let me change these numbers.
26

27
00:02:16,210 --> 00:02:21,860
This code is for configuring PD12 as output.
27

28
00:02:22,490 --> 00:02:31,620
So now, let's implement the code to configuring PA0 as input.
28

29
00:02:31,640 --> 00:02:32,110
Let's write
29

30
00:02:32,110 --> 00:02:39,230
the code to configure PA0 as input mode, because we are reading from PA0. For this
30

31
00:02:39,270 --> 00:02:44,660
we have to explore GPIOA mode register.
31

32
00:02:45,200 --> 00:02:51,010
So, now let's create a variable to store the address of the GPIOA mode register.
32

33
00:02:51,020 --> 00:02:54,870
Remember that, this is a address of GPIOD mode register.
33

34
00:02:54,920 --> 00:03:00,200
We have to create another pointer to store the address of GPIOA mode register.
34

35
00:03:00,290 --> 00:03:06,680
That's why, let's create one variable and let me change this name to A. 
35

36
00:03:06,680 --> 00:03:10,010
PortAModeReg and lets change this address.
36

37
00:03:10,010 --> 00:03:10,760
What is the address?
37

38
00:03:10,760 --> 00:03:15,700
Now we have to calculate the address of mode register of GPIO Port A.
38

39
00:03:15,770 --> 00:03:17,210
So, how do you calculate this?
39

40
00:03:17,210 --> 00:03:22,580
So, for that again we have to refer to the reference manual of the microcontroller.
40

41
00:03:22,580 --> 00:03:30,290
So, here we have to first go and explore the Memory map, isn't it? to understand the base address of the
41

42
00:03:30,290 --> 00:03:33,530
GPIOA peripheral registers.
42

43
00:03:33,560 --> 00:03:41,300
So, let's go to the Memory and bus architecture, Memory map and let's see where exactly GPIOA is placed.
43

44
00:03:41,300 --> 00:03:42,020
So, here it is.
44

45
00:03:42,140 --> 00:03:45,400
This is a base address of GPIOA registers.
45

46
00:03:45,620 --> 00:03:52,840
Let's copy this. And what is the offset of the mode register? So, mode register is always at offset
46

47
00:03:52,840 --> 00:03:53,400
00.
47

48
00:03:53,840 --> 00:03:57,350
So, let me show you once again. So, go to the GPIO section,
48

49
00:03:57,400 --> 00:03:58,480
go to the mode register.
49

50
00:03:58,720 --> 00:04:03,690
So, for every GPIO peripheral, the mode register is at the offset 0
50

51
00:04:03,850 --> 00:04:06,040
but from different base addresses.
51

52
00:04:06,200 --> 00:04:09,780
So, that's why, it is 0. So, we can keep like this.
52

53
00:04:09,790 --> 00:04:17,280
So, this is a mode register and this is the place for configuring mode for pin number 0.
53

54
00:04:17,380 --> 00:04:24,010
So, you have to keep these two bits cleared in order to set the pin mode to input.
54

55
00:04:24,060 --> 00:04:25,790
Let's use *
55

56
00:04:28,410 --> 00:04:36,720
pPortAModeReg &=  negation of 0x3 or 3 shifted by 0.
56

57
00:04:36,720 --> 00:04:46,020
That actually clears the first two positions of the Port A mode register. So, after that you have to read
57

58
00:04:46,020 --> 00:04:50,930
the pin status of the pin PA0.
58

59
00:04:50,940 --> 00:04:55,980
So, now how do you read? For this you have to refer to the GPIOA
59

60
00:04:56,160 --> 00:04:57,000
INPUT
60

61
00:04:57,820 --> 00:05:01,060
DATA REGISTER.
61

62
00:05:01,120 --> 00:05:03,940
So, now let's explore the input data register.
62

63
00:05:04,270 --> 00:05:10,070
Let's go to the GPIO section in the reference manual of the microcontroller
63

64
00:05:10,070 --> 00:05:13,500
and in the GPIO section you get input data register.
64

65
00:05:13,510 --> 00:05:14,260
Here it is.
65

66
00:05:14,260 --> 00:05:18,140
And here you can see that this is a read only register,
66

67
00:05:18,190 --> 00:05:20,200
you cannot write into this register.
67

68
00:05:20,230 --> 00:05:24,940
Let's say whenever you connect the pin PA0 to high voltage that is VDD,
68

69
00:05:24,940 --> 00:05:29,350
so then the corresponding bit position in this register will be set.
69

70
00:05:29,530 --> 00:05:36,010
So, in this case the bit position 0 will be set, because we are dealing with pin PA0.  So, if it is PA1
70

71
00:05:36,020 --> 00:05:37,450
then this bit will be set,
71

72
00:05:37,510 --> 00:05:43,360
if the pin is connected to +VDD and if you connect the pin to GND, then the corresponding position
72

73
00:05:43,360 --> 00:05:44,770
will be reset.
73

74
00:05:44,800 --> 00:05:46,210
So, let's do that.
74

75
00:05:46,210 --> 00:05:51,620
So, for that first we have to find out the address of the Port A input data register.
75

76
00:05:51,790 --> 00:05:53,740
So, let's create that.
76

77
00:05:53,740 --> 00:06:00,940
Let me create one more variable and let me call this as GPIOA InReg.
77

78
00:06:01,080 --> 00:06:04,350
Let me call InReg, GPIOA InReg.
78

79
00:06:04,470 --> 00:06:06,620
And what is the address of this? Base address
79

80
00:06:06,630 --> 00:06:10,410
plus the offset. So, what is a offset? 0x10.
80

81
00:06:10,410 --> 00:06:13,350
So, that's why, let me change this to 10.
81

82
00:06:13,350 --> 00:06:15,520
And from here you have to read it.
82

83
00:06:15,540 --> 00:06:21,960
So, what I'm going to do is, I'm going to create a variable uint8_t status or pinStatus
83

84
00:06:23,480 --> 00:06:25,480
is equal to I have to read this.
84

85
00:06:25,640 --> 00:06:29,170
Sorry, so we have to read this.
85

86
00:06:29,260 --> 00:06:37,330
So, when you do this you actually get 32 bits of data, but what we want is just one bit the status of
86

87
00:06:37,450 --> 00:06:38,800
bit position 0.
87

88
00:06:38,830 --> 00:06:46,690
So, what we can do is, we can do a masking in order to 0 out all other bits except the bit position
88

89
00:06:46,690 --> 00:06:47,280
0.
89

90
00:06:47,290 --> 00:06:51,370
So, that's why, what I would do is, & with 0x1.
90

91
00:06:51,400 --> 00:06:55,510
So, this will 0 out all the bits except the bit position 0.
91

92
00:06:55,570 --> 00:07:03,820
I think we can do this. And after that let me just typecast this to uint8_t, after that
92

93
00:07:04,000 --> 00:07:10,240
let's implement decision making. If pin status is non-zero, then turn on the LED
93

94
00:07:14,760 --> 00:07:15,830
else turn off the LED.
94

95
00:07:16,200 --> 00:07:22,290
So, we have the code to turn on the LED, let's cut this code, let's remove this, and let's paste here.
95

96
00:07:22,910 --> 00:07:32,040
And to turn off the LED we have this code &= ~( 1 << 12 ). That's it and
96

97
00:07:32,130 --> 00:07:33,690
this should work forever.
97

98
00:07:33,720 --> 00:07:36,110
So, that's why, let's keep that in a super loop.
98

99
00:07:36,270 --> 00:07:38,960
So, this should happen again and again,
99

100
00:07:38,980 --> 00:07:41,630
you have to read the pin status and take the decision.
100

101
00:07:41,670 --> 00:07:43,470
This should happen forever.
101

102
00:07:43,470 --> 00:07:47,350
So, let's implement the while loop here, infinite while loop.
102

103
00:07:47,820 --> 00:07:52,640
So, let's put this inside the infinite while loop.
103

104
00:07:52,650 --> 00:07:55,390
I hope you also implemented something like this.
104

105
00:07:55,560 --> 00:07:58,060
So, here there is missing semicolon
105

106
00:07:58,110 --> 00:08:02,820
and now let's compile this code and let's see whether it works or not.
106

107
00:08:02,820 --> 00:08:09,180
So, now let's start executing this by stepping over the code and so first
107

108
00:08:09,240 --> 00:08:13,000
what I do is, I connect the PA0 to VDD.
108

109
00:08:13,020 --> 00:08:19,170
I have already connected that in my board. So, the decision taking code should reach here sorry, it should
109

110
00:08:19,170 --> 00:08:19,790
reach here.
110

111
00:08:19,950 --> 00:08:20,980
It should turn on the LED
111

112
00:08:21,000 --> 00:08:29,840
now. Let's see. So, here we can see that the variable field it's already showing that a pin status is 1.
112

113
00:08:29,870 --> 00:08:32,810
So, that means PA0 is connected to VDD.
113

114
00:08:33,320 --> 00:08:40,880
So, if you want to get this variables, then you can go to show view and go to variables here it is.
114

115
00:08:40,880 --> 00:08:44,960
You can open that. Let me just execute this.
115

116
00:08:44,960 --> 00:08:46,830
Yes, the LED is indeed turned on
116

117
00:08:46,910 --> 00:08:52,700
and again we are checking the pinStatus. This time I'm going to remove the connection and I'm going
117

118
00:08:52,700 --> 00:08:55,990
to put the connection to GND.
118

119
00:08:56,060 --> 00:09:01,130
Let's try. And here we can see that it actually turned off my LED.
119

120
00:09:01,520 --> 00:09:03,240
Let's run this at once
120

121
00:09:03,290 --> 00:09:09,230
and now the code is running on the board and you can keep changing between VDD and GND and you can
121

122
00:09:09,230 --> 00:09:12,800
see that your LED toggles on and off.
122

123
00:09:12,800 --> 00:09:20,500
That's about reading a pin status from your code using the input data register of the GPIO peripheral.
123

124
00:09:20,660 --> 00:09:28,100
And we need all this knowledge when we build complex exercises like when we work on keypads, LCDs,
124

125
00:09:28,110 --> 00:09:30,650
LEDs, 7 segment LEDs, etc.
125

126
00:09:30,710 --> 00:09:36,530
So, with that note I would like to end this video and try to reproduce this at your desk and let me know
126

127
00:09:36,530 --> 00:09:38,810
if you face any issues, I'll see you in the next lecture.


==================================================================================

section 23---optimization

video 1


0
1
00:00:00,120 --> 00:00:01,470
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,470 --> 00:00:04,970
So, in this lecture lets understand about Optimization.
2

3
00:00:05,240 --> 00:00:10,280
In order to understand and appreciate the use of  volatile type qualifier,
3

4
00:00:10,330 --> 00:00:15,630
we have to first understand what happens when compiler does optimization on your code.
4

5
00:00:16,020 --> 00:00:17,250
Let's get started.
5

6
00:00:17,430 --> 00:00:25,440
So, optimization is nothing but it's a series of actions taken by the compiler on your programs code
6

7
00:00:25,440 --> 00:00:32,760
generation process. To reduce a number of instructions of your program, a to reduce the memory access time,
7

8
00:00:32,940 --> 00:00:35,650
and also to reduce the power consumption.
8

9
00:00:35,670 --> 00:00:42,300
So, if memory access time and a number of instructions come down then you may get better power consumption.
9

10
00:00:43,660 --> 00:00:48,590
So, by default, the compiler doesn't invoke any optimization on your program.
10

11
00:00:48,730 --> 00:00:52,990
So, all the exercise what we did so for is with no optimization.
11

12
00:00:52,990 --> 00:00:56,080
You can enable optimization using compiler flags.
12

13
00:00:56,080 --> 00:01:00,220
Let's explore GCC compiler flags to enable optimization.
13

14
00:01:00,220 --> 00:01:05,920
So, these are the compiler flags O0, O1, O2, O3.
14

15
00:01:05,920 --> 00:01:12,280
So, these are the different optimization flags which you can supply to the compiler in order to optimize
15

16
00:01:12,280 --> 00:01:17,520
your code. So, by default, compiler uses no optimization.
16

17
00:01:17,550 --> 00:01:21,030
So, that means the optimization level is zero.
17

18
00:01:21,120 --> 00:01:22,790
So, this is no optimization.
18

19
00:01:22,860 --> 00:01:25,230
So, this is not recommended for productions
19

20
00:01:25,320 --> 00:01:32,250
if you have limited code and  RAM space, because almost no optimization is carried out on your code. And
20

21
00:01:32,370 --> 00:01:39,030
since the compiler doesn't invoke any optimization algorithms this actually has a fastest compilation
21

22
00:01:39,080 --> 00:01:39,860
time.
22

23
00:01:39,930 --> 00:01:43,430
So, this is debugging friendly and used during development.
23

24
00:01:44,130 --> 00:01:50,010
So, this is debugging friendly because, when you are trying to debug your code there will be one to one
24

25
00:01:50,010 --> 00:01:53,520
map between the instructions generated and the source code
25

26
00:01:53,520 --> 00:02:00,870
what you have written. And please note that, a code which works with O0 optimization may not work with
26

27
00:02:00,990 --> 00:02:08,490
O0+ optimization levels, that is O1, O2, O3. Code needs to be verified again.
27

28
00:02:08,580 --> 00:02:16,240
So, if you use O1 optimization level, then it is kind of introducing moderate optimization to decrease
28

29
00:02:16,240 --> 00:02:18,990
memory access time and code space.
29

30
00:02:18,990 --> 00:02:22,380
So, after that comes O2 optimization level.
30

31
00:02:22,380 --> 00:02:28,800
So, this actually carries out full optimization and it results in slow compilation time, because now the
31

32
00:02:28,800 --> 00:02:35,400
compiler has to invoke various optimization algorithms on your code. So, it results in slow compilation
32

33
00:02:35,400 --> 00:02:40,250
time and it is not debugging friendly. So, you cannot be able to debug your code properly.
33

34
00:02:40,290 --> 00:02:46,350
If you enable a O2 or O3 optimization level, because there will not be one to one mapping between
34

35
00:02:46,350 --> 00:02:50,010
the instructions generated and the source code what you have written.
35

36
00:02:50,100 --> 00:02:57,060
So, the next optimization level is O3. So, O3 means full optimization of O2 + some more aggressive
36

37
00:02:57,150 --> 00:02:59,550
optimization steps will be taken by the compiler.
37

38
00:02:59,880 --> 00:03:05,160
So, this also results in slowest compilation time and it may cause bugs in the program,
38

39
00:03:05,190 --> 00:03:10,230
so if you enable O3 optimization level, then your application may not behave properly,
39

40
00:03:10,260 --> 00:03:15,580
so you have to revisit your code and you may have to rewrite your application or something.
40

41
00:03:15,660 --> 00:03:20,160
So, you should be careful with O3 optimization level.
41

42
00:03:20,340 --> 00:03:26,440
And remember that, you need to work with different optimization levels to find out what works for you.So,
42

43
00:03:26,480 --> 00:03:31,980
there is no standard guideline that says you should use this optimization for this application.
43

44
00:03:31,980 --> 00:03:39,180
So, there is no such guidelines. And for all code exercises what we do in this course, we will consider  
44

45
00:03:39,180 --> 00:03:41,070
O0 optimization level.
45

46
00:03:41,100 --> 00:03:47,560
These are the different optimization levels which you can activate from your IDE, so you can pass all
46

47
00:03:47,580 --> 00:03:50,230
these optimization flags from the IDE itself.
47

48
00:03:50,250 --> 00:03:56,460
So, we'll explore that in the next lecture, and we'll compare our code when we trigger a different optimization
48

49
00:03:56,460 --> 00:03:58,440
levels and I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,120 --> 00:00:01,230
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,230 --> 00:00:05,490
So, now let's experiment on different optimization levels.
2

3
00:00:05,490 --> 00:00:08,090
We have this pin read application.
3

4
00:00:08,090 --> 00:00:10,190
Let's use different optimization levels
4

5
00:00:10,230 --> 00:00:15,200
on this program. First of all let me tell you where to configure the optimization level.
5

6
00:00:15,300 --> 00:00:19,740
So, you have to right click on the project and go to the properties,
6

7
00:00:22,670 --> 00:00:25,940
and after that just expand this C and C++ build,
7

8
00:00:25,970 --> 00:00:30,320
go to settings, and go to MCU GCC compiler.
8

9
00:00:30,320 --> 00:00:37,010
And here, you see optimization tab. Click that, and you can see that the optimization level by default
9

10
00:00:37,040 --> 00:00:38,060
is O0.
10

11
00:00:38,240 --> 00:00:39,160
So, that is none.
11

12
00:00:39,170 --> 00:00:42,210
So, no optimization will be invoked on our program.
12

13
00:00:42,350 --> 00:00:46,600
So, we know that our code works fine with this optimization level.
13

14
00:00:46,660 --> 00:00:49,720
Let's change this optimization to O1.
14

15
00:00:49,760 --> 00:00:55,380
Let's see what happens. So, after that just click apply, and apply and close.
15

16
00:00:55,400 --> 00:01:01,060
So, what we can do is, first let's compile with O0  and lets note the code size.
16

17
00:01:01,130 --> 00:01:09,970
So, let me first go to O0, and let me just clean this and let's build the project,
17

18
00:01:09,970 --> 00:01:13,580
and let me just take a note of this size.
18

19
00:01:13,780 --> 00:01:16,160
So, this is with O0.
19

20
00:01:16,360 --> 00:01:22,510
Here we can see that the size of the text section that is code section is a 820 bytes
20

21
00:01:22,510 --> 00:01:25,130
and this is a data section size .
21

22
00:01:25,130 --> 00:01:34,880
Let's change the optimization flag to O1 and let's build the project, and here it is.
22

23
00:01:34,930 --> 00:01:40,870
This is for O1. And here you can see that the text section is reduced.
23

24
00:01:40,950 --> 00:01:46,400
So, that means we indeed saved around 80 bytes of a code space.
24

25
00:01:46,530 --> 00:01:49,540
So, for this application it may not be much
25

26
00:01:49,590 --> 00:01:51,170
this is a very small application.
26

27
00:01:51,240 --> 00:01:55,930
So, that's why, it is not that much improvement, but for bigger project
27

28
00:01:55,950 --> 00:01:56,940
it may not be trivial.
28

29
00:01:57,360 --> 00:02:05,240
Let's change the optimization flag to O2 or I will try O3 and this is for O3.
29

30
00:02:05,280 --> 00:02:11,550
So, here you can see that when you check this number it is not a great reduction compared to O1 just
30

31
00:02:11,590 --> 00:02:18,380
28 bytes less. But that's OK, because that's all what compiler can able to do in this application.
31

32
00:02:18,540 --> 00:02:22,710
So, achieving the reduction in code space is not a big deal.
32

33
00:02:22,770 --> 00:02:25,080
But thing is your application should work fine.
33

34
00:02:25,260 --> 00:02:27,840
So, what is a point of reducing a code size,
34

35
00:02:27,840 --> 00:02:30,150
but seeing your application doesn't work.
35

36
00:02:30,570 --> 00:02:35,490
So, at the end of the day application should work the way you designed your application.
36

37
00:02:35,490 --> 00:02:40,290
So, we know that our application works fine in O0 optimization level.
37

38
00:02:40,290 --> 00:02:47,070
So, you have to recheck whether your application indeed works the way it is designed in O1 optimization
38

39
00:02:47,070 --> 00:02:47,340
level.
39

40
00:02:47,900 --> 00:02:49,450
So, now let's check that.
40

41
00:02:49,450 --> 00:03:01,400
So, let me just change this optimization level back to O1 and let's check this.
41

42
00:03:01,450 --> 00:03:07,660
Let me run the application and let me just check by changing the value of PA0 pin,
42

43
00:03:07,810 --> 00:03:08,830
Yes, it works.
43

44
00:03:08,830 --> 00:03:14,320
So, that means I can keep this optimization level for this application. So, which will definitely save
44

45
00:03:14,320 --> 00:03:16,300
some bytes in the code space.
45

46
00:03:16,300 --> 00:03:18,120
How about O3?
46

47
00:03:18,250 --> 00:03:21,140
So, let's try O3 or O2.
47

48
00:03:21,160 --> 00:03:26,040
Let's try O2. Let's see whether it works or not
48

49
00:03:26,040 --> 00:03:27,600
with O2,
49

50
00:03:27,600 --> 00:03:35,430
so, let me run the code and interestingly the code doesn't work in O2, code breaks.
50

51
00:03:35,430 --> 00:03:36,930
You can try this at your desk
51

52
00:03:36,960 --> 00:03:38,310
the code doesn't work.
52

53
00:03:38,310 --> 00:03:42,900
So, if code doesn't work in O2, then the code also doesn't work in O3.
53

54
00:03:42,900 --> 00:03:46,990
So, we will analyze this why the code is not working in
54

55
00:03:46,990 --> 00:03:48,420
O2 in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,650
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,650 --> 00:00:08,010
So, in the previous lecture we just found out that when we activated the O2 optimization on our code,
2

3
00:00:08,190 --> 00:00:10,020
our code actually stopped working.
3

4
00:00:10,050 --> 00:00:12,410
We have to investigate why it is not working.
4

5
00:00:12,450 --> 00:00:19,800
So because, this investigation is very much required in order to understand how volatile works, and this
5

6
00:00:19,800 --> 00:00:26,310
will help us in understanding the volatile type qualifier in the next lecture.First let's investigate
6

7
00:00:26,390 --> 00:00:29,530
the disassembly code of this program.
7

8
00:00:32,640 --> 00:00:41,150
First let me activate the O0 optimization, and let me try to analyze the disassembly. So, here is a
8

9
00:00:41,150 --> 00:00:46,970
disassembly and the code execution currently stopped, and all these are actually assignments.
9

10
00:00:46,970 --> 00:00:51,120
So, we are assigning some value to variables and this is a configuration.
10

11
00:00:51,230 --> 00:00:54,560
So, the main logic of our code actually lies here, isn't it?
11

12
00:00:55,250 --> 00:01:01,970
So, if the pin status is 1, then LED will be turned on and if the pin status is 0, LED will be
12

13
00:01:01,970 --> 00:01:02,610
turned off.
13

14
00:01:02,630 --> 00:01:08,090
So, that means processors should read this memory location again and again and it has to update this
14

15
00:01:08,090 --> 00:01:08,600
variable.
15

16
00:01:08,750 --> 00:01:15,710
So, for every loop processor actually reads this memory location and then it updates this variable, isn't
16

17
00:01:15,700 --> 00:01:15,890
it?
17

18
00:01:15,890 --> 00:01:17,630
and based on that decision will be taken.
18

19
00:01:18,110 --> 00:01:20,760
So, let's analyze what's happening here.
19

20
00:01:20,770 --> 00:01:23,330
We will investigate the disassembly of this line.
20

21
00:01:23,690 --> 00:01:31,950
So, let's put a breakpoint here and let's run. So, here is a code, so just check what's happening here.
21

22
00:01:32,110 --> 00:01:39,190
So, what's happening? First the data will be loaded into r3 register from this memory location.
22

23
00:01:39,190 --> 00:01:41,770
That is 40020010.
23

24
00:01:41,980 --> 00:01:44,800
So, you can even see that in the register tab.
24

25
00:01:44,800 --> 00:01:52,750
So, let's open the registers tab, and let's activate this instruction stepping mode, and let's just do
25

26
00:01:52,750 --> 00:02:02,020
step over. Here you can see that r3, let's change the format, so r3 is loaded with the input
26

27
00:02:02,050 --> 00:02:07,840
port A input data registers address and from there value will be read and it will be populated in 
27

28
00:02:07,840 --> 00:02:08,690
r3.
28

29
00:02:08,710 --> 00:02:14,740
So, now let's do step over and here we can see that, this is a current value of the r3
29

30
00:02:14,770 --> 00:02:22,590
and after that you do some post-processing on that, and after that you can find that r3 is 1. So, now  
30

31
00:02:22,600 --> 00:02:23,820
r3 is 1,  isn't it?
31

32
00:02:23,830 --> 00:02:28,690
So, after that what happens the value of r3 will be stored into this variable.
32

33
00:02:28,960 --> 00:02:30,120
So, that's what happening here.
33

34
00:02:30,140 --> 00:02:32,280
So, that's why, strb is executed. 
34

35
00:02:32,350 --> 00:02:37,540
So, this is load from input data register and stored to the variable.
35

36
00:02:38,050 --> 00:02:44,080
So, that means a code is behaving as expected. For each iteration of this loop value will be read from
36

37
00:02:44,080 --> 00:02:46,960
this memory location and it will be populated.
37

38
00:02:46,960 --> 00:02:48,390
So, you can see that.
38

39
00:02:48,400 --> 00:02:57,070
So, let me just deactivate this instruction mode. And here you can see that, for each iteration the fresh
39

40
00:02:57,070 --> 00:03:02,420
value will be read from the input data register and r3 will be updated.So,
40

41
00:03:02,470 --> 00:03:04,060
that is very important.
41

42
00:03:04,090 --> 00:03:10,000
That means for every iteration processor actually obtains the fresh value from that memory location
42

43
00:03:10,330 --> 00:03:15,680
and it populates the register r3, and r3 value will be saved into this variable.
43

44
00:03:15,700 --> 00:03:17,460
So, it happens for every loop.
44

45
00:03:17,590 --> 00:03:18,820
So, that is very important,
45

46
00:03:18,820 --> 00:03:20,470
it should happen for every loop.
46

47
00:03:20,470 --> 00:03:20,800
Why?
47

48
00:03:20,800 --> 00:03:24,610
Because, the user can change the value of the pin at any time.
48

49
00:03:24,610 --> 00:03:31,570
So, that's why, every time the processor should read the fresh value from the port A input data register
49

50
00:03:31,720 --> 00:03:37,180
and the processor should update the corresponding variable, that is pin status
50

51
00:03:37,180 --> 00:03:45,370
in this case. So,that's why, you can see that for every iteration this load and this store will get executed.
51

52
00:03:45,690 --> 00:03:47,550
Let's analyze the same thing
52

53
00:03:47,550 --> 00:03:50,370
when we enable the O2 optimization.
53

54
00:03:53,710 --> 00:03:59,450
I'm going to activate the O2 optimization. So, let's debug this code
54

55
00:03:59,630 --> 00:04:06,440
and please note that debugging in O2 level is not that easy, because there will not be one to one mapping
55

56
00:04:06,440 --> 00:04:10,410
between the disassembly and the source code what you have written.
56

57
00:04:10,460 --> 00:04:17,540
For example, let's say if I try to execute step over you can see that the execution it looks like the
57

58
00:04:17,540 --> 00:04:21,080
execution is taking place in random fashion, isn't it?
58

59
00:04:21,370 --> 00:04:28,320
So, some time it goes inside the while loop and sometimes it comes out of the while loop like that.
59

60
00:04:28,540 --> 00:04:34,930
You can see that,  we are trapped in the while loop and here we can see that it is not actually executing
60

61
00:04:34,990 --> 00:04:36,660
this line, isn't it?
61

62
00:04:36,670 --> 00:04:40,810
So, this code actually depends upon the old value of the pin status.
62

63
00:04:40,870 --> 00:04:43,270
So, that's the reason why our code is not working.
63

64
00:04:43,810 --> 00:04:50,740
So, the pin status is updated maybe only one time in the code and after that the processor is keep reusing
64

65
00:04:50,740 --> 00:04:59,560
that without reading the fresh value from the Port A input data register. So, you can also check in the disassembly
65

66
00:04:59,560 --> 00:05:03,720
code here at the right hand side. So, here it is,
66

67
00:05:03,740 --> 00:05:07,820
it is comparing whether pin status is zero or not. For that,
67

68
00:05:07,820 --> 00:05:14,220
it is using the instruction cbz. cbz stands for compare and branch on zero.
68

69
00:05:14,660 --> 00:05:21,220
So, if r0 is really 0, the code will jump to this location. But r0 is not getting updated,
69

70
00:05:21,230 --> 00:05:22,350
so that's a problem.
70

71
00:05:22,490 --> 00:05:24,260
So, r0 is always 0.
71

72
00:05:24,260 --> 00:05:26,430
You can see here. See,
72

73
00:05:26,460 --> 00:05:29,460
so here r3 is modified, here r0 is compared,
73

74
00:05:29,570 --> 00:05:32,300
so, where do you think r0 is getting updated?
74

75
00:05:32,300 --> 00:05:39,070
It is not getting updated. So, processor is not visiting the required address and it is gathering the
75

76
00:05:39,080 --> 00:05:46,170
fresh value from the address, that is from this address. So, that's how the compiler optimized the code
76

77
00:05:46,170 --> 00:05:46,710
for you.
77

78
00:05:46,740 --> 00:05:52,290
It actually avoided processor visiting that memory location again and again and acquiring the new
78

79
00:05:52,290 --> 00:05:59,070
value, that means it actually reduced the number of memory hits. When you reduce the memory hits your code
79

80
00:05:59,190 --> 00:06:01,180
becomes faster, isn't it?
80

81
00:06:01,230 --> 00:06:07,400
So, the compiler try to make your code more faster by decreasing the amount of memory hits.
81

82
00:06:07,980 --> 00:06:12,990
So, that may be good idea of the compiler but that actually breaks your application.
82

83
00:06:12,990 --> 00:06:18,890
So, that means we have to now tell the compiler that don't do any optimization on this particular address.
83

84
00:06:19,140 --> 00:06:22,090
How to tell that? So, welcome to the word of volatile.
84

85
00:06:22,140 --> 00:06:29,700
We can tell the compiler not to do any optimization on this address by using the volatile type qualifier.
85

86
00:06:30,300 --> 00:06:35,820
So, we'll understand in the next lecture maybe how to fix this problem using volatile type qualifier. 
86

87
00:06:35,910 --> 00:06:36,020
But
87

88
00:06:36,030 --> 00:06:40,110
before that we have to understand what exactly volatile does.
88

89
00:06:40,230 --> 00:06:42,830
So, will explore about the volatile in the next lecture.


==================================================================================


section 24---'volatile' type qualifier

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:02,590
So, now let's understand next
1

2
00:00:02,580 --> 00:00:04,590
type qualifier that is 'volatile'.
2

3
00:00:05,010 --> 00:00:08,100
So, volatile is a type qualifier in 'C'
3

4
00:00:08,100 --> 00:00:14,840
used with variables to instruct the compiler not to invoke any optimization on the variable operation.
4

5
00:00:14,850 --> 00:00:18,860
What is a variable operation? So, variable operation is either read or write.
5

6
00:00:19,050 --> 00:00:26,700
So, it tells the compiler that the value of the variable may change at any time with our without the programmer's
6

7
00:00:26,700 --> 00:00:27,740
consent.
7

8
00:00:27,780 --> 00:00:35,700
So, the compiler turns off optimizing the read-write operations on variables which are declared using
8

9
00:00:35,790 --> 00:00:36,450
volatile
9

10
00:00:36,480 --> 00:00:43,710
keyword. So, volatile is very much helpful in embedded systems code in order to reduce the bug.
10

11
00:00:43,710 --> 00:00:47,280
So, now let's see some example of volatile effect.
11

12
00:00:47,280 --> 00:00:49,710
So, for that let's create one project.
12

13
00:00:49,710 --> 00:00:53,910
So, I'm going to my IDE and let me create a project.
13

14
00:00:54,390 --> 00:01:02,670
So, this project I'm creating for my target STM32 project, what I'm going to do now is, I'm going to create
14

15
00:01:02,670 --> 00:01:10,230
one variable, let's say uint8_t data1. And I'm going to initialize this data1 to
15

16
00:01:10,240 --> 00:01:16,980
50 let's say, and after that I'm going to create one more variable let's say data2. And what I do
16

17
00:01:16,980 --> 00:01:19,610
is, data2 = data1.
17

18
00:01:19,620 --> 00:01:24,890
So, here our goal is to understand the volatile keyword effect.
18

19
00:01:25,080 --> 00:01:30,900
I copy data1 into data2, the same code once again let's say  
data2 = data1.
19

20
00:01:31,080 --> 00:01:36,150
So, here you can see that this is actually a redundant 'C' statement,
20

21
00:01:36,150 --> 00:01:40,230
this is not required here. Because, the data is already copied into data2.
21

22
00:01:40,260 --> 00:01:43,460
So, executing one more time doesn't make any sense.
22

23
00:01:43,470 --> 00:01:49,440
So, what would you do if you are a compiler? So, you would generate instructions only for this 'C' statement.
23

24
00:01:49,560 --> 00:01:53,070
So, you would be ignoring this 'C' statement.
24

25
00:01:53,130 --> 00:01:55,080
So, that's one type of optimization.
25

26
00:01:55,140 --> 00:01:58,980
So, you just optimized to decrease the number of instructions.
26

27
00:01:59,190 --> 00:02:00,980
But let's see what compiler does.
27

28
00:02:00,990 --> 00:02:11,080
So, what I'm going to do now is, I'm just going to debug this. So, the code actually is in O0 optimization
28

29
00:02:11,080 --> 00:02:17,590
level and let's see whether the compiler really generates instructions or not.
29

30
00:02:17,620 --> 00:02:19,080
Let me check the disassembly
30

31
00:02:19,120 --> 00:02:21,730
and here it is. So, for
31

32
00:02:21,730 --> 00:02:22,660
data1.
32

33
00:02:22,780 --> 00:02:25,520
So, we are actually putting 50 into data1.
33

34
00:02:25,600 --> 00:02:28,190
So, yes there is the store instruction.
34

35
00:02:28,270 --> 00:02:30,670
50 is stored into this variable.
35

36
00:02:31,000 --> 00:02:31,810
That's correct.
36

37
00:02:31,870 --> 00:02:34,910
And copying data from data1 to data2.
37

38
00:02:34,960 --> 00:02:35,550
Yes, first
38

39
00:02:35,600 --> 00:02:43,100
there is a load instruction, that is the value is loaded from the memory location to r3 register
39

40
00:02:43,180 --> 00:02:49,400
and after that there is a store instruction, the value is stored into the variable data2.
40

41
00:02:49,810 --> 00:02:52,370
And again it generated these two instructions.
41

42
00:02:52,390 --> 00:02:55,350
So, these are actually redundant instructions.
42

43
00:02:55,450 --> 00:03:01,000
These instructions are not actually required but still compiler generated them, because we are using
43

44
00:03:01,060 --> 00:03:10,890
O0 optimization level. Let's use  O1 optimization level and let's see how the disassembly looks. So,
44

45
00:03:10,890 --> 00:03:12,510
let's explore the disassembly
45

46
00:03:12,510 --> 00:03:15,090
and here it is. In the main function 
46

47
00:03:15,090 --> 00:03:20,220
the compiler removed all the instructions. So, it just kept this statement.
47

48
00:03:20,220 --> 00:03:23,730
So, code is not generated for all these 'C' statements.
48

49
00:03:23,760 --> 00:03:25,070
So, why is that?
49

50
00:03:25,140 --> 00:03:27,740
For that you have to again think in the compiler way.
50

51
00:03:27,840 --> 00:03:31,420
So, of course this is a redundant 'C' statement.
51

52
00:03:31,500 --> 00:03:35,390
So, if you remove that, then this much is left. Again here
52

53
00:03:35,400 --> 00:03:38,480
all these are redundant 'C' statements.
53

54
00:03:38,490 --> 00:03:38,960
Why?
54

55
00:03:38,970 --> 00:03:45,100
Because, so you have created variables but you have not used those variables in the program,
55

56
00:03:45,480 --> 00:03:48,080
so all these are unused variables.
56

57
00:03:48,210 --> 00:03:50,090
So, why do you create variables in the program?
57

58
00:03:50,100 --> 00:03:56,550
You create variables in the program to use it in your program, to do some operation, to pass some argument
58

59
00:03:56,580 --> 00:03:58,630
or to receive some argument.
59

60
00:03:58,650 --> 00:04:00,770
So, here you are not doing anything like that.
60

61
00:04:00,840 --> 00:04:07,200
So, you have just created the variables, you just initialize them, and you just left it. All these are actually
61

62
00:04:07,320 --> 00:04:08,330
unused variables.
62

63
00:04:08,340 --> 00:04:13,110
So, that's why, the compiler actually didn't generate any instructions for them.
63

64
00:04:13,140 --> 00:04:20,320
That's why, you see only one instruction in the main that is for this for loop statement. So, now the question
64

65
00:04:20,320 --> 00:04:28,030
is, being in optimization level 1, how to tell the compiler to generate instructions for these 'C' statements?
65

66
00:04:28,030 --> 00:04:30,590
We do that by using volatile keyword.
66

67
00:04:30,700 --> 00:04:37,930
So, tell the compiler that don't do any optimizations on these variable operations. So, you can tell that
67

68
00:04:37,930 --> 00:04:39,750
using the volatile keyword.
68

69
00:04:40,000 --> 00:04:41,740
So, just make them volatile.
69

70
00:04:42,100 --> 00:04:46,930
So, again I am actually mentioning the volatile keyword after the type specifier.
70

71
00:04:47,110 --> 00:04:55,360
So, now here data1 is a volatile data of uint8 and data 2 is also a volatile data of
71

72
00:04:55,540 --> 00:04:57,150
uint8.
72

73
00:04:57,310 --> 00:04:59,880
Let's compile and observe the disassembly.
73

74
00:04:59,890 --> 00:05:02,640
Now the optimization level is still O1
74

75
00:05:03,040 --> 00:05:05,310
and I'm going to debug this code.
75

76
00:05:05,380 --> 00:05:11,350
So, here it is. Compiler actually didn't optimize the operations on these two variables.
76

77
00:05:11,410 --> 00:05:18,670
So, now you can see the instructions are generated for data1 and data2 operations.
77

78
00:05:18,730 --> 00:05:22,150
So, what is the value of data2? It is 50, data1 is 50.
78

79
00:05:22,210 --> 00:05:30,880
That's correct. So, that's why, by using volatile we enforced the compiler not to invoke any optimization
79

80
00:05:30,970 --> 00:05:36,250
on these variables, even when the compiler is working on higher optimization levels.
80

81
00:05:36,280 --> 00:05:42,340
Let's again examine this code in O3 optimization level and let's see how this goes.
81

82
00:05:42,340 --> 00:05:44,230
Let me just debug this code.
82

83
00:05:44,320 --> 00:05:46,980
So, here it is  O3 optimization.
83

84
00:05:46,990 --> 00:05:50,980
So, even though O3 is a very aggressive optimization,
84

85
00:05:51,040 --> 00:05:56,050
so look at the code generated for these operations.
85

86
00:05:56,050 --> 00:05:57,590
They are not optimized.
86

87
00:05:57,640 --> 00:06:00,520
So, the takeaway from this lecture is very very simple
87

88
00:06:00,550 --> 00:06:08,130
by using volatile keyword. So, you can enforce the compiler not to do any optimization on a variables
88

89
00:06:08,290 --> 00:06:14,140
operation. And you will understand and analyze more on this feature in the coming lectures.
89

90
00:06:14,140 --> 00:06:15,280
So, I will see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:04,500
So, when to use  'Volatile' qualifier? So, a variable must be declared
1

2
00:00:04,500 --> 00:00:11,030
using a volatile qualifier when there is a possibility of unexpected changes in the variable value.
2

3
00:00:11,040 --> 00:00:14,140
So, this scenario we saw in our pin read example.
3

4
00:00:14,160 --> 00:00:18,860
So, for the variable pin status there was the possibility of unexpected change.
4

5
00:00:18,870 --> 00:00:21,690
So, who does the unexpected change?  The user.
5

6
00:00:21,690 --> 00:00:26,350
The user may change the state of the pin PA0 at any time.
6

7
00:00:26,460 --> 00:00:31,040
So, that's why, that is a case of unexpected change, so there we should use a volatile.
7

8
00:00:31,090 --> 00:00:33,490
So, we'll use that in the next video.
8

9
00:00:33,630 --> 00:00:39,990
So, the unexpected changes in the variable value may happen from within the code or from outside the
9

10
00:00:39,990 --> 00:00:41,400
code or from the hardware.
10

11
00:00:41,850 --> 00:00:46,590
So, use volatile when your code is dealing with below scenarios.
11

12
00:00:46,590 --> 00:00:52,800
So, first one is when you are working with memory mapped peripheral registers of the microcontrollers.
12

13
00:00:53,250 --> 00:00:55,290
When you are using multiple task
13

14
00:00:55,350 --> 00:01:02,430
accessing global variables in an RTOS multi threaded application. When a global variable is used
14

15
00:01:02,520 --> 00:01:08,280
to share data between the main code and an ISR code. In all these scenarios
15

16
00:01:08,280 --> 00:01:15,450
use volatile generously. So, for other cases volatile is not required. Specially for these three reasons
16

17
00:01:15,540 --> 00:01:17,230
you should use volatile.
17

18
00:01:17,340 --> 00:01:21,620
Now, our pin read example actually falls under this case.
18

19
00:01:21,720 --> 00:01:27,200
We were actually accessing the memory mapped peripheral register. So, we'll see that how to fix that.
19

20
00:01:27,200 --> 00:01:30,840
Lets understand syntax of using a volatile, lets understand
20

21
00:01:30,840 --> 00:01:34,290
couple of cases. The case 1 is  volatile data.
21

22
00:01:34,290 --> 00:01:40,980
For example, if you have a variable lets say my_data, then you can convert this into volatile data using
22

23
00:01:40,980 --> 00:01:41,460
volatile
23

24
00:01:41,470 --> 00:01:48,670
keyword, so you can use this method. First mention the type specifier and then mention the
24

25
00:01:48,810 --> 00:01:52,230
volatile keyword and then write the variable name.
25

26
00:01:52,260 --> 00:01:55,820
So, this is the preferred method or you can also use this.
26

27
00:01:55,830 --> 00:01:56,730
No problem.
27

28
00:01:56,730 --> 00:01:58,080
Both are identical,
28

29
00:01:58,080 --> 00:01:59,770
and now how do you read this?
29

30
00:01:59,790 --> 00:02:03,480
So, my_data is a volatile variable of type
30

31
00:02:03,510 --> 00:02:11,640
uint8. So, now the case 2 is non-volatile pointer to volatile data.
31

32
00:02:11,850 --> 00:02:16,650
So, this is the case we use most frequently in embedded system programming,
32

33
00:02:16,650 --> 00:02:22,460
when we deal with memory mapped a peripheral registers. This is similar to const.
33

34
00:02:22,980 --> 00:02:24,980
So, here this is for the data
34

35
00:02:25,080 --> 00:02:26,300
and this is a pointer.
35

36
00:02:26,880 --> 00:02:36,840
So, here pStatusReg is a non-volatile pointer, pointing to volatile data of type unsigned integer_8.
36

37
00:02:36,960 --> 00:02:38,180
So, what is a use case of this?
37

38
00:02:38,180 --> 00:02:42,810
So, this is a perfect case of accessing memory-mapped registers.
38

39
00:02:43,320 --> 00:02:50,160
So, use this syntax generously whenever you are accessing memory mapped registers in your microcontroller
39

40
00:02:50,160 --> 00:02:51,030
code.
40

41
00:02:51,030 --> 00:02:58,680
So, by using this syntax basically we are telling the compiler that the data pointed by this pointer
41

42
00:02:58,680 --> 00:03:00,300
is volatile in nature.
42

43
00:03:00,300 --> 00:03:08,460
That means the data may change unexpectedly. So, that's why, not to do any optimization on data read or
43

44
00:03:08,460 --> 00:03:11,800
data write operations using this pointer.
44

45
00:03:13,420 --> 00:03:17,410
And the Case 3 and Case 4 are rarely used.
45

46
00:03:17,410 --> 00:03:21,300
So, the Case 3 is volatile pointer to non-volatile data.
46

47
00:03:21,460 --> 00:03:28,270
So, the syntax is this one, and the Case 4 is volatile pointer to volatile data that looks something
47

48
00:03:28,270 --> 00:03:29,060
like this.
48

49
00:03:29,080 --> 00:03:31,700
And as I said these are rarely used,
49

50
00:03:31,720 --> 00:03:34,980
so we will not be discussing about these syntaxes.
50

51
00:03:35,140 --> 00:03:35,630
So,
51

52
00:03:35,650 --> 00:03:41,830
and remember that, the keywords 'const' and 'volatile' can be applied to any declaration, including those
52

53
00:03:41,830 --> 00:03:44,860
of structures, unions, enumerated types
53

54
00:03:44,870 --> 00:03:53,110
or typedef names. The exercise right after this video for you is go back to the pin read code and try
54

55
00:03:53,110 --> 00:03:57,790
to resolve the issue which we encountered in O2 optimization.
55

56
00:03:57,790 --> 00:04:03,630
So, now you have to use volatile that in order to make it work under O2 optimization
56

57
00:04:03,640 --> 00:04:04,220
level.
57

58
00:04:04,270 --> 00:04:05,470
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,350
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,350 --> 00:00:04,940
So, now you have a good idea about volatile keyword
2

3
00:00:04,980 --> 00:00:09,390
and now let's try to use that in order to fix the issue with our code.
3

4
00:00:09,750 --> 00:00:16,260
So, that is with our pin_read example code and we know that, this application actually breaks when
4

5
00:00:16,260 --> 00:00:19,680
we activate the O2 optimization level.
5

6
00:00:19,800 --> 00:00:26,550
So, when we were debugging this through disassembly we came to know that, the compiler has generated instructions
6

7
00:00:26,670 --> 00:00:34,710
where this code is executed only once. So, processor is not reading this memory address again and again
7

8
00:00:34,830 --> 00:00:38,550
for every iteration in order to fetch the new value.
8

9
00:00:38,550 --> 00:00:46,380
So, that's because a compiler has done an optimization on this address. The compiler has assumed that
9

10
00:00:46,470 --> 00:00:51,450
the data at this memory location will never change. So,
10

11
00:00:51,460 --> 00:00:58,860
but now we have to tell the compiler that the data at this address can change unexpectedly.
11

12
00:00:58,960 --> 00:01:03,040
So, that's why, lets change the declaration of this pointer.
12

13
00:01:03,370 --> 00:01:11,760
So, here we have to mention volatile. So now, compiler will not optimize any read and write
13

14
00:01:11,760 --> 00:01:17,070
operations performed on this pointer. So, that would solve this issue,
14

15
00:01:17,070 --> 00:01:24,320
but I said one thing while explaining the theory that, whenever you are accessing memory mapped peripheral
15

16
00:01:24,390 --> 00:01:27,240
registers use volatile generously.
16

17
00:01:27,330 --> 00:01:30,530
So, that's why, let's use volatile everywhere.
17

18
00:01:30,570 --> 00:01:39,940
So, for all these pointers let's use volatile, voaltile for the data not for the pointer, like this. So,
18

19
00:01:39,940 --> 00:01:44,620
all these are actually pointing to memory mapped peripheral registers.
19

20
00:01:44,890 --> 00:01:45,630
So, here as well.
20

21
00:01:46,450 --> 00:01:49,330
And this should fix the bug and
21

22
00:01:49,330 --> 00:01:56,790
now let's try. So, it compiled successfully and now let's check the optimization level first.
22

23
00:01:56,810 --> 00:01:57,790
This is O2.
23

24
00:01:57,790 --> 00:02:02,390
That's correct.
24

25
00:02:02,390 --> 00:02:07,020
Now let's just debug this code.
25

26
00:02:07,050 --> 00:02:14,370
So, now here you see, it is now going to read the fresh value from the Port A input data register.
26

27
00:02:14,370 --> 00:02:15,120
This should work
27

28
00:02:15,120 --> 00:02:16,140
now.
28

29
00:02:16,320 --> 00:02:21,450
So, now let's try with optimization level
29

30
00:02:21,600 --> 00:02:26,220
let's say 3. Yes, it works.
30

31
00:02:26,230 --> 00:02:29,110
You can see that the pin status is changing.
31

32
00:02:29,110 --> 00:02:31,990
So, every time it is reading from this address.
32

33
00:02:32,020 --> 00:02:37,990
So, that's how you just fixed a bug in the program using volatile, and in the next lecture we'll see
33

34
00:02:38,020 --> 00:02:39,680
how to use volatile with
34

35
00:02:39,700 --> 00:02:41,590
the interrupt service routine.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 4

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:00,930
Hey, welcome back.
1

2
00:00:01,020 --> 00:00:09,420
So, we just explored about how to use the volatile qualifier. So, we explored the case of using  
2

3
00:00:09,420 --> 00:00:13,980
'volatile' keyword with memory mapped peripheral register access.
3

4
00:00:13,980 --> 00:00:16,700
In this lecture let's explore this case.
4

5
00:00:16,710 --> 00:00:22,580
That is when a global variable is used to share data between the main code and an ISR code.
5

6
00:00:22,950 --> 00:00:26,790
So, now let's see how we can use volatile with this scenario.
6

7
00:00:26,790 --> 00:00:34,800
Here I have a project 008button_ISR, and this project is available in our git repository, so you
7

8
00:00:34,800 --> 00:00:36,390
can download from there.
8

9
00:00:36,420 --> 00:00:38,730
So, let me just explain this source code.
9

10
00:00:38,740 --> 00:00:40,020
It's very simple.
10

11
00:00:40,020 --> 00:00:42,470
There are 2 main functions in this code.
11

12
00:00:42,540 --> 00:00:48,390
So, let me just maximize this. So, the first function is a main function
12

13
00:00:48,390 --> 00:00:50,870
and the second function is the ISR.
13

14
00:00:51,000 --> 00:00:57,960
So, this is the button ISR. This button ISR will get executed whenever the onboard button of the board
14

15
00:00:57,990 --> 00:00:59,200
is pressed.
15

16
00:00:59,250 --> 00:01:02,400
So, what we do in this ISR is very simple,
16

17
00:01:02,400 --> 00:01:05,050
we actually set the global flag.
17

18
00:01:05,370 --> 00:01:08,340
So, g_button_pressed is a global flag
18

19
00:01:08,340 --> 00:01:10,670
here it is and
19

20
00:01:10,690 --> 00:01:13,570
that will be set and the ISR exits.
20

21
00:01:13,630 --> 00:01:15,930
So, that's the only thing ISR does.
21

22
00:01:16,000 --> 00:01:17,560
So, here is a main function,
22

23
00:01:17,560 --> 00:01:24,400
so what main function does is, it is actually trapped inside the infinite loop and for every iteration
23

24
00:01:24,580 --> 00:01:27,550
the main code checks this global flag.
24

25
00:01:27,640 --> 00:01:32,480
And if this global flag is really set that is by the ISR,
25

26
00:01:32,530 --> 00:01:38,890
if it is really set, then it prints how many times the button is pressed so for. What it does is, it actually
26

27
00:01:38,890 --> 00:01:41,620
just uses one count variable
27

28
00:01:41,620 --> 00:01:48,400
it just increments that count and then it just prints that count value to indicate these many times
28

29
00:01:48,400 --> 00:01:53,310
the button is pressed so for. After that it resets this global flag.
29

30
00:01:53,650 --> 00:01:58,000
So, it is like invalidates the flag. And again the flag will be set by the ISR,
30

31
00:01:58,000 --> 00:02:02,510
and again this if condition executes, and then printf will printed, like that.
31

32
00:02:02,530 --> 00:02:07,070
So, here is a small delay, so this delay is for button debouncing.
32

33
00:02:07,240 --> 00:02:11,130
So, this introduce a some delay until button debouncing gets over.
33

34
00:02:11,860 --> 00:02:17,170
So, these are the memory mapped peripheral registers being configured and accessed
34

35
00:02:17,170 --> 00:02:24,690
in this project. So, if you want to reproduce this code on your board, then you should be using 
35

36
00:02:24,690 --> 00:02:26,260
STM32F4 discovery board.
36

37
00:02:26,430 --> 00:02:31,920
So, if you have some other board then you have to modify this code. Because different boards have different
37

38
00:02:32,280 --> 00:02:38,130
pins where the button is connected and also the interrupt configuration changes. So, you cannot simply
38

39
00:02:38,160 --> 00:02:40,880
download it on some other board and you can make it work.
39

40
00:02:41,130 --> 00:02:44,400
So, if you have a discovery board then you can try this code.
40

41
00:02:44,430 --> 00:02:52,770
Otherwise, please identify the places where you can use volatile in this project to make this application
41

42
00:02:52,860 --> 00:02:59,670
behave as intended. Because, this project works only with the O0 optimization level,
42

43
00:02:59,730 --> 00:03:04,340
so the moment you change the optimization level this application breaks.
43

44
00:03:04,500 --> 00:03:06,400
Let me show you how to run this code.
44

45
00:03:06,420 --> 00:03:16,260
So, first let's make sure that the optimization is none, and after that let's build the project,
45

46
00:03:16,400 --> 00:03:24,690
and now let's debug. And after that, go to SWV ITM Data Console
46

47
00:03:24,690 --> 00:03:27,250
and make sure that this is checked,
47

48
00:03:27,250 --> 00:03:29,700
and let's start the tracing,
48

49
00:03:29,700 --> 00:03:30,570
and after that
49

50
00:03:30,570 --> 00:03:31,460
just run the code.
50

51
00:03:32,600 --> 00:03:35,000
The code is running and press the button
51

52
00:03:35,030 --> 00:03:39,560
and here we can see that, it is printing the button is pressed 4 time,
52

53
00:03:39,560 --> 00:03:41,650
5 time, 6 time, like that.
53

54
00:03:41,660 --> 00:03:44,100
So, this is working properly.
54

55
00:03:44,120 --> 00:03:46,550
Let's change the optimization level.
55

56
00:03:46,550 --> 00:03:48,530
Let me terminate.
56

57
00:03:48,710 --> 00:03:51,510
Let's change the optimization level to O3.
57

58
00:03:51,530 --> 00:03:53,940
Let's start the trace first.
58

59
00:03:53,960 --> 00:03:57,740
Let's start the tracing and then hit run.
59

60
00:03:57,750 --> 00:04:00,630
So, now let me press the button once,
60

61
00:04:00,930 --> 00:04:01,630
here it is.
61

62
00:04:01,710 --> 00:04:07,410
It printed 18 times. So, I just pressed the button once but it printed 18 times.
62

63
00:04:07,650 --> 00:04:10,200
So, that means there is something problem with the code.
63

64
00:04:10,650 --> 00:04:13,260
Let me press the button once again, before that
64

65
00:04:13,260 --> 00:04:15,480
let me just clear this.
65

66
00:04:15,520 --> 00:04:17,160
Let me press one more time.
66

67
00:04:17,200 --> 00:04:19,570
Here we can see that it just printed 4 times.
67

68
00:04:19,840 --> 00:04:22,270
So, the application is not behaving properly,
68

69
00:04:22,300 --> 00:04:23,670
so you have to fix this.
69

70
00:04:24,130 --> 00:04:29,330
So, identify the places where and all you can use volatile to fix this issue.
70

71
00:04:29,350 --> 00:04:30,430
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 5

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:00,750
Hey, welcome back 
1

2
00:00:00,780 --> 00:00:05,790
and in the previous lecture I actually give you a small assignment to identify the places where we can
2

3
00:00:05,820 --> 00:00:07,310
keep a volatile
3

4
00:00:07,350 --> 00:00:13,710
in this application, and we know that this application breaks in O3 optimization level and now
4

5
00:00:13,710 --> 00:00:14,900
let's get started.
5

6
00:00:14,970 --> 00:00:16,450
Let's analyze this code.
6

7
00:00:16,470 --> 00:00:22,350
First of all as a rule of thumb all these are memory mapped register addresses.
7

8
00:00:22,590 --> 00:00:25,330
So, that's why, use volatile generously.
8

9
00:00:25,380 --> 00:00:35,920
So, let's use volatile here for the data pointed by these addresses. So, I have to use a volatile here.
9

10
00:00:36,070 --> 00:00:36,860
Fine.
10

11
00:00:36,880 --> 00:00:40,960
So, that's a first change you should be doing. So, never assume any function,
11

12
00:00:40,960 --> 00:00:42,940
go inside and check the code.
12

13
00:00:42,940 --> 00:00:44,650
Let's see what button_init is doing,
13

14
00:00:44,650 --> 00:00:49,030
it is just modifying these memory mapped registers.
14

15
00:00:49,030 --> 00:00:50,050
No problem here.
15

16
00:00:50,080 --> 00:00:54,280
We actually made them all volatile, so it is taken care already
16

17
00:00:54,280 --> 00:00:55,270
here. Next,
17

18
00:00:55,360 --> 00:01:02,260
while(1) there is nothing to do here and this is again modifying a register and after that there is
18

19
00:01:02,260 --> 00:01:04,750
a global flag button pressed.
19

20
00:01:04,840 --> 00:01:09,250
So, that's why, this global flag or global variable should be made volatile
20

21
00:01:09,280 --> 00:01:14,920
according to our rule of thumb. Because, it has a potential to undergo unexpected changes by the ISR,
21

22
00:01:14,920 --> 00:01:20,170
or by the multiple task, etc. in an multiple threaded application. 
22

23
00:01:20,200 --> 00:01:24,250
So, that's why, as a rule of thumb we'll make it volatile. By the way,
23

24
00:01:24,270 --> 00:01:29,520
this is also a global variable but this is not shared between a task and an ISR.
24

25
00:01:29,910 --> 00:01:32,700
So, that's why, need not to make this volatile.
25

26
00:01:32,730 --> 00:01:35,390
So, after that actually we have a for loop.
26

27
00:01:35,640 --> 00:01:42,570
So, this for loop is being used to introduce a small delay for the button debouncing. But the compiler
27

28
00:01:42,570 --> 00:01:44,340
will definitely going to remove this.
28

29
00:01:44,340 --> 00:01:44,790
Why?
29

30
00:01:44,790 --> 00:01:48,960
Because, compiler thinks that this will slow down the application.
30

31
00:01:48,960 --> 00:01:54,090
The compiler is absolutely right, because compiler doesn't know what is our requirement. Our requirement
31

32
00:01:54,210 --> 00:01:58,970
is to compensate for the button debouncing by introducing a small delay.
32

33
00:01:59,310 --> 00:02:04,110
So, for us this delay is really required, but compiler doesn't think in that direction.
33

34
00:02:04,110 --> 00:02:08,540
So, what compiler thinks is, the i is simply incremented to some level
34

35
00:02:08,550 --> 00:02:12,380
and afterwards i used not at all used to anywhere in the code.
35

36
00:02:12,390 --> 00:02:16,600
So, compiler thinks that the operations on the i is redundant.
36

37
00:02:16,740 --> 00:02:21,370
So, that's why, compiler will consider this loop as redundant.
37

38
00:02:21,370 --> 00:02:28,860
And it also tries to optimize it, because optimizing this will make application faster. But making application
38

39
00:02:28,860 --> 00:02:30,850
faster will break our application.
39

40
00:02:30,900 --> 00:02:36,020
So, that's why, we have to tell the compiler not to do any optimization on i.
40

41
00:02:36,210 --> 00:02:40,650
So, that's why, we have to make i also volatile here.
41

42
00:02:40,830 --> 00:02:42,870
So, after that this is a function call,
42

43
00:02:43,110 --> 00:02:48,690
so no optimization is required here, anyway it will get called and then we are invalidating this flag,
43

44
00:02:48,690 --> 00:02:54,440
so operations on this flag will not be optimized, because it is already volatile.
44

45
00:02:54,570 --> 00:02:55,310
That's it.
45

46
00:02:55,350 --> 00:02:58,530
That's how you fix the code by using volatile keyword.
46

47
00:02:58,530 --> 00:03:05,010
So, first you have to remember all these 3 cases where you should use volatile generously, and after
47

48
00:03:05,010 --> 00:03:11,880
that you should also remember this case where if you have empty loop, the loop variable has to be made
48

49
00:03:11,970 --> 00:03:14,810
volatile in order to skip the optimization.
49

50
00:03:14,940 --> 00:03:21,120
And I hope you understood about the use of volatile and in the next lecture let's discuss about  
50

51
00:03:21,120 --> 00:03:24,000
usage of 'const' and 'volatile' together.
51

52
00:03:24,000 --> 00:03:25,320
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 6

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:06,230
So, in the previous couple of videos you understood about the 'const' and 'volatile' type qualifiers and
1

2
00:00:06,240 --> 00:00:07,670
its usage.
2

3
00:00:07,710 --> 00:00:15,350
So, now let's discuss about usage of 'const' and 'volatile' together. So, you can also use both 'const' and
3

4
00:00:15,430 --> 00:00:18,140
'volatile'  qualifiers in a variable declaration
4

5
00:00:18,150 --> 00:00:19,020
as per your goal.
5

6
00:00:19,230 --> 00:00:23,210
So, some people think that you know const is a opposite of volatile.
6

7
00:00:23,310 --> 00:00:27,450
There is nothing like that. So, const and volatile are entirely different.
7

8
00:00:27,450 --> 00:00:33,360
Don't try to compare them. So, if you think const is opposite of volatile, then it is wrong.
8

9
00:00:33,360 --> 00:00:35,160
So, you should not assume like that.
9

10
00:00:35,370 --> 00:00:38,260
So, both are entirely different type qualifiers.
10

11
00:00:38,280 --> 00:00:40,310
For example, consider this statement.
11

12
00:00:40,310 --> 00:00:40,790
So here,
12

13
00:00:40,800 --> 00:00:41,910
how do you read this?
13

14
00:00:41,970 --> 00:00:47,720
pReg is a const pointer pointing to volatile data of type uint8.
14

15
00:00:47,760 --> 00:00:55,350
So, here const is used with pointer just to guard this pointer from unexpected changes from the programmer.
15

16
00:00:55,590 --> 00:01:02,310
So, this is just a case of defensive programming here. So, where we are guarding pReg is using const and we are
16

17
00:01:02,310 --> 00:01:08,270
telling the compiler that, the data present at this address may undergo unexpected changes,
17

18
00:01:08,340 --> 00:01:12,870
so not to optimize any read and write operations on this pointer
18

19
00:01:12,870 --> 00:01:14,610
by using this volatile.
19

20
00:01:14,700 --> 00:01:20,070
So, that is this is a case of constant pointer and volatile data.
20

21
00:01:20,070 --> 00:01:22,340
So, now consider this case here,
21

22
00:01:22,350 --> 00:01:28,400
so how do you read this? pReg is a const pointer pointing to volatile const data
22

23
00:01:28,720 --> 00:01:30,800
of type uint8.
23

24
00:01:30,810 --> 00:01:31,590
So here,
24

25
00:01:31,590 --> 00:01:33,720
this may be confusing volatile const.
25

26
00:01:33,810 --> 00:01:40,080
So, the meaning of this is the data pointed by this address is volatile,
26

27
00:01:40,140 --> 00:01:43,520
that is it is subjected to unexpected changes,
27

28
00:01:43,590 --> 00:01:49,020
but the programmer must not change the data present at this address.So,
28

29
00:01:49,050 --> 00:01:50,790
that's a meaning of const volatile.
29

30
00:01:51,000 --> 00:01:56,730
So, const here is for the programmer not to change the value present at this address. 
30

31
00:01:56,880 --> 00:02:05,430
And this is for the compiler not to optimize the read and write operation on this address. Let's understand
31

32
00:02:05,440 --> 00:02:13,230
the case of reading from read-only buffer or address which is prone to unexpected change.
32

33
00:02:13,230 --> 00:02:20,550
So, if you are reading from a read-only buffer or address like in the case of a input data register. So,
33

34
00:02:20,550 --> 00:02:23,100
input data register is a read-only register,
34

35
00:02:23,160 --> 00:02:24,480
you should only read from that,
35

36
00:02:24,540 --> 00:02:25,950
you should not modify that.
36

37
00:02:26,220 --> 00:02:30,100
So, in that case you can use this complex syntax.
37

38
00:02:30,210 --> 00:02:32,250
So, in that case you can use this.
38

39
00:02:32,460 --> 00:02:38,190
So, in this statement the data at this address location should not be modified by the programmer, because
39

40
00:02:38,190 --> 00:02:45,090
it is const. But the data at this address may get changed by the data coming from the external world. The
40

41
00:02:45,090 --> 00:02:47,520
data may come into the input data register
41

42
00:02:47,520 --> 00:02:54,600
let's say from an external world like from the protocols, or from the network, or from user pressing
42

43
00:02:54,600 --> 00:02:56,080
the button, etc..
43

44
00:02:56,130 --> 00:03:01,720
So, the address can still undergo unexpected change, but the programmer should not modify it.
44

45
00:03:01,740 --> 00:03:04,800
So, in that cases you can use const volatile.
45

46
00:03:05,100 --> 00:03:10,320
So, I hope you understood about a using 'const' and 'volatile' together.
46

47
00:03:10,320 --> 00:03:15,950
So, now let's go back to our pin_read example, and let's see whether we can able to use const volatile
47

48
00:03:15,950 --> 00:03:16,970
together here.
48

49
00:03:17,040 --> 00:03:22,680
It is not actually required, but still if you want to guard these pointers then what you can do is, you
49

50
00:03:22,680 --> 00:03:24,920
can convert this into const pointers here.
50

51
00:03:28,290 --> 00:03:35,070
So, you can convert all these into const pointers and after that this is actually a Port A input data
51

52
00:03:35,070 --> 00:03:39,350
register, and for the input data register programmer should not write anything.
52

53
00:03:39,510 --> 00:03:42,700
So, that's why, you can even make this as const volatile.
53

54
00:03:43,120 --> 00:03:45,460
So, that's a only change I would like to make
54

55
00:03:45,540 --> 00:03:47,520
and that's it.
55

56
00:03:47,520 --> 00:03:54,860
So, that's how you can use const and volatile together. But you cannot make these a statements as const.
56

57
00:03:54,930 --> 00:03:55,920
So, this is wrong.
57

58
00:03:55,950 --> 00:04:02,880
If you do this what happens? Compiler will throw some errors. You are trying to change the read only location,
58

59
00:04:03,030 --> 00:04:04,360
so you cannot do this.
59

60
00:04:04,380 --> 00:04:09,240
So, use const volatile only for read-only memory addresses.
60

61
00:04:09,240 --> 00:04:15,480
So, with that note I would like to end these lectures on const and volatile and I will see you in the
61

62
00:04:15,480 --> 00:04:16,140
next lecture.


==================================================================================


section 25--Structures and Bit fields

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,120 --> 00:00:01,540
Hey, welcome back to the course
1

2
00:00:01,620 --> 00:00:06,250
and from this lecture onwards let's understand about structures in 'C'.
2

3
00:00:06,390 --> 00:00:10,630
So, what is a structure? So, structure is a data structure
3

4
00:00:10,660 --> 00:00:20,120
used to create user-defined data types in 'C'. So, structures allow us to combine data of different types.
4

5
00:00:20,160 --> 00:00:26,430
So, let's understand about how to create a structure. A structure is nothing but a record,
5

6
00:00:26,430 --> 00:00:31,810
it's a record where we a club data of different data types.
6

7
00:00:31,830 --> 00:00:33,810
So, this is how you create a structure.
7

8
00:00:33,810 --> 00:00:35,220
So, this is a syntax.
8

9
00:00:35,220 --> 00:00:38,730
So, first you start with the keyword struct.
9

10
00:00:38,730 --> 00:00:42,790
This is a standard reserved keyword in 'C' programming, struct.
10

11
00:00:42,840 --> 00:00:46,580
So, start with a keyword 'struct' and then you have to write the tag name.
11

12
00:00:46,650 --> 00:00:51,990
You have to give one name for your record or you have to give a name for your structure.
12

13
00:00:52,110 --> 00:00:56,420
And then this is the body and the body must be terminated with a semicolon,
13

14
00:00:56,430 --> 00:00:57,750
you should keep this in mind.
14

15
00:00:58,290 --> 00:01:03,810
And after that inside the body you can mention n number of member elements.
15

16
00:01:03,840 --> 00:01:08,670
So, this is a syntax to create a structure in 'C' and we'll explore more.
16

17
00:01:08,670 --> 00:01:09,660
Let's see one example.
17

18
00:01:09,870 --> 00:01:12,720
So, here is a structure called CarModel,
18

19
00:01:13,320 --> 00:01:16,030
and these are the member elements of this structure.
19

20
00:01:16,140 --> 00:01:22,200
So, the member elements usually describe the record. Like for example, in the structure CarModel,
20

21
00:01:22,440 --> 00:01:29,130
first member element describes the car number, car price, car maximum speed, car rate.
21

22
00:01:29,130 --> 00:01:35,030
So, you can see that all of these are different data of different data types.
22

23
00:01:35,070 --> 00:01:42,270
So, by using a structure you can combine all those data of different data types in a single record.
23

24
00:01:42,270 --> 00:01:47,100
So, you should remember that structure definition doesn't consume any memory.
24

25
00:01:47,100 --> 00:01:49,860
So, this is also called as structure definition.
25

26
00:01:49,860 --> 00:01:55,260
So, this doesn't consume any memory. It is just like a description or a record.
26

27
00:01:55,980 --> 00:01:58,800
Let's see how to create variables of a structure.
27

28
00:01:58,920 --> 00:02:02,190
So, you know that how to create a variable. To create a variable
28

29
00:02:02,190 --> 00:02:06,200
you have to first mention the data type followed by the variable name.
29

30
00:02:06,300 --> 00:02:08,370
So, same thing you have to use here.
30

31
00:02:08,380 --> 00:02:09,560
So, here it is.
31

32
00:02:09,560 --> 00:02:13,170
So, this is the creation of structure variables.
32

33
00:02:13,170 --> 00:02:15,900
First you have to mention the data type.
33

34
00:02:15,930 --> 00:02:20,830
So, in this case this is a data type struct CarModel.
34

35
00:02:20,950 --> 00:02:22,860
So, that's a user defined data type
35

36
00:02:23,620 --> 00:02:27,120
and then you have to mention various variables. Here,
36

37
00:02:27,160 --> 00:02:29,250
we have created 3 variables.
37

38
00:02:29,320 --> 00:02:34,480
So, memory will be consumed when you create structure variables. For example,
38

39
00:02:34,630 --> 00:02:41,020
so here you can calculate how much memory does a CarBMW variable will consume.
39

40
00:02:41,260 --> 00:02:46,180
So, if you want to calculate this, then you have to calculate the total memory consumed by the member
40

41
00:02:46,180 --> 00:02:47,890
elements of that structure.
41

42
00:02:48,040 --> 00:02:49,030
So, if you calculate this.
42

43
00:02:49,030 --> 00:02:56,110
So, this consumes 4, this consumes 4 bytes, this consumes 2 bytes, 4 bytes, so totally it looks
43

44
00:02:56,110 --> 00:02:59,780
like a single variable consumes 14 bytes of memory.
44

45
00:02:59,830 --> 00:03:01,150
So, it's like that.
45

46
00:03:01,180 --> 00:03:05,100
So here, how do you describe the data type of this variable?
46

47
00:03:05,110 --> 00:03:07,560
So, now here what is the data type of 
47

48
00:03:07,570 --> 00:03:09,840
CarBMW? The data type of  
48

49
00:03:09,850 --> 00:03:13,070
CarBMW variable is "struct CarModel".
49

50
00:03:13,270 --> 00:03:17,950
So, you have to mention something like that. struct CarModel is the data type name.
50

51
00:03:18,190 --> 00:03:20,610
So, that's a user defined data type.
51

52
00:03:20,650 --> 00:03:24,910
Lets understand how to initialize the member elements of the structure.
52

53
00:03:24,910 --> 00:03:27,900
Now, we will understand this by going through one example.
53

54

So, write a program to create a carModel structure discussed and create two variables of type carModel.

55
00:03:34,870 --> 00:03:41,380
So, initialize the variables with the below given data and then print them. So,we'll do this exercise in the

1

2
00:00:02,340 --> 00:00:09,130

3
00:00:09,130 --> 00:00:12,480
So, this is a CarModel structure you have to create.

4
00:00:12,480 --> 00:00:15,290
Let's see how to create this in our program.
4

5
00:00:15,360 --> 00:00:19,290
I have just created a new project structInit.
5

6
00:00:19,290 --> 00:00:21,090
So, this is my project name
6

7
00:00:21,300 --> 00:00:23,680
and let's create the structure.
7

8
00:00:24,330 --> 00:00:28,200
So, usually structure definition is created outside the function.
8

9
00:00:28,500 --> 00:00:30,850
So, you can even create inside the function,
9

10
00:00:30,870 --> 00:00:32,940
no problem with that.
10

11
00:00:32,970 --> 00:00:36,780
So, but it is usually done outside the program or in the header file.
11

12
00:00:37,470 --> 00:00:41,230
It's better if you create the structure definition in the header file.
12

13
00:00:42,210 --> 00:00:45,470
Let's first create here itself in the main.c.
13

14
00:00:45,570 --> 00:00:52,460
So, let's write the 'struct' keyword and let's give the tag name CarModel,
14

15
00:00:52,470 --> 00:01:01,810
and after that open the curly brackets. So, you can open here itself or you can do something like this.
15

16
00:01:02,000 --> 00:01:04,520
And now let's create the member elements.
16

17
00:01:04,830 --> 00:01:10,340
So, member elements are car number, car price, car maximum speed, and car weight.

18
00:01:10,470 --> 00:01:13,550
I just created the member elements of this structure.

19
00:01:14,130 --> 00:01:18,150
So, after that let's create 2 variables.
19

20
00:01:18,150 --> 00:01:20,770
So, that's what our example says. Again
20

21
00:01:20,880 --> 00:01:24,990
you can create either local variables or global variables.
21

22
00:01:24,990 --> 00:01:27,300
So, for this we can create local variables.
22

23
00:01:28,200 --> 00:01:30,230
So, now how to create a variable?
23

24
00:01:30,240 --> 00:01:32,410
First you have to mention the data type.
24

25
00:01:32,520 --> 00:01:43,190
So, this is a data type. And after that let me type carBMW and carFord. So, these are the two variables.
25

26
00:01:44,120 --> 00:01:48,130
We have to initialize the variables by using these data.

27
00:01:48,950 --> 00:01:55,570
So, these data you have to use for variable1 and these data you have to use for variable2.

28
00:01:55,640 --> 00:01:59,410
So, now there are two ways to initialize these variables.

29
00:01:59,520 --> 00:02:01,950
So, one is you initialize here itself
29

30
00:02:02,030 --> 00:02:07,020
when you define the variables, that we can do something like this.

31
00:02:07,070 --> 00:02:13,180
So, now let's start with only one variable to make  explanation symbol. So, here itself you can 
31

32
00:02:13,180 --> 00:02:20,670
initialize this variable using assignment operator is equal to you have to open the curly bracket, and then
32

33
00:02:20,700 --> 00:02:26,970
you have to start mentioning the values for these member elements. So, let's say I mentioned 0,

00:02:27,000 --> 00:02:30,980
0,0,0.

35
00:02:30,990 --> 00:02:38,220
So, now you just initialized all these member elements to 0, or whatever the data given in the exercise.

36
00:02:39,230 --> 00:02:41,570
So, let's use the data given in the exercise.
36

37
00:02:41,570 --> 00:02:46,370
Remember that, there is a one to one mapping between the order in which you have used the values and the
37

38
00:02:46,370 --> 00:02:49,950
order in which the member elements appear.
38

39
00:02:49,970 --> 00:02:54,800
So, for example, 2021 will be stored in car number,
39

40
00:02:54,800 --> 00:02:57,890
this will be stored in car price, like that.

41
00:02:57,890 --> 00:03:08,740
So, order is important here. Let's create the second variable, let's call carFord and let's use
41

42
00:03:09,010 --> 00:03:11,680
the values given in the exercise.
42

43
00:03:11,830 --> 00:03:22,060
So, we just created two variables of the structure and we initialize them. Let's try to compile this. So,
43

44
00:03:22,060 --> 00:03:22,910
it compiles well.
44

45
00:03:22,930 --> 00:03:25,540
So, there is a warning, because these 2 variables are unused. 
45

46
00:03:25,570 --> 00:03:26,880
That's fine.

47
00:03:27,010 --> 00:03:35,000
So, we just tried this method. This is also called as C89 method.
47

48
00:03:35,000 --> 00:03:43,370
So, this method was introduced in C89 and here the order is important. After that C99 standard
48

49
00:03:43,760 --> 00:03:52,370
introduced this method. Here the order is not important, but you have to use designated initializers using
49

50
00:03:52,370 --> 00:03:58,890
dot operator. Let's try to use C99 method to initialize  the structure variable.
50

51
00:03:59,090 --> 00:04:00,650
Very simple.

52
00:04:00,650 --> 00:04:02,740
Let's go to the project.

53
00:04:02,810 --> 00:04:07,670
So, what I can do here is, let me create one more variable here.

54
00:04:08,000 --> 00:04:12,190
Let's call it a struct carModel
54

55
00:04:12,260 --> 00:04:20,900
carHonda is equal to so let's open and close the curly bracket. So here, I can initialize this member element
55

56
00:04:20,900 --> 00:04:22,890
first. To do that
56

57
00:04:22,890 --> 00:04:29,910
what you have to do is, you have to use a dot operator .(dot) then you have to mention this member element
57

58
00:04:29,910 --> 00:04:38,040
name is equal to  let's say 90.90. This is also valid.
58

59
00:04:38,110 --> 00:04:39,610
So here, order is not important
59

60
00:04:39,640 --> 00:04:45,830
you just use dot operator and use the member element which you want to initialize.

61

You can give comma, and then give the dot operator, and then press control space eclipse will
61

62

give some suggestion about the member elements, then you can select whatever you want.

63
00:04:57,700 --> 00:05:05,840
carPrice = 15000. Let's print each and every member elements of this  structure. For
63

64
00:05:05,970 --> 00:05:07,130
that we have to understand
64

65
00:05:07,130 --> 00:05:09,340
accessing structure members.
65

66
00:05:09,690 --> 00:05:12,390
So, when a structure variable is created,
66

67
00:05:12,420 --> 00:05:16,660
use a .( dot) operator to access the member elements.
67

68
00:05:16,650 --> 00:05:18,590
Let's use the dot operator. 
68

69

Let's go back to the code and let's use a printf. printf

70

let's say details of a car

71

BMW is as follows.
71

72

First let's print carNumber = %u\n.

73
00:05:40,550 --> 00:05:45,390
So, now how to access the member element carNumber.
73

74
00:05:45,420 --> 00:05:48,550
So, for that first you have to use the structure variable,
74

75
00:05:49,930 --> 00:05:51,900
and then give the dot operator,
75

76
00:05:53,290 --> 00:05:58,310
and after that select member element carNumber. That's it.

77
00:05:58,480 --> 00:06:03,150
That's how you access the member element of a structure variable.
77

78
00:06:03,170 --> 00:06:06,800
Let me quickly finish this for other member elements.
78

79
00:06:06,800 --> 00:06:09,420
So, I just completed all this printf to print
79

80
00:06:09,440 --> 00:06:11,960
different member elements.
80

81
00:06:11,960 --> 00:06:12,880
And after that,
81

82
00:06:12,890 --> 00:06:15,500
now let's print the details of this variable.
