83
00:06:16,160 --> 00:06:23,140
So, I just used printf to print the details of all the member elements of these structure variables.
83

84
00:06:23,780 --> 00:06:28,860
Let's say you want to change the value of member elements of some structure variables.
84

85
00:06:29,420 --> 00:06:33,250
Let's say you want to  overwrite these initialized values.
85

86
00:06:33,350 --> 00:06:38,620
You can do that by again assigning new values to the member elements of the structure variable.
86

87
00:06:38,630 --> 00:06:45,650
Let's say, if I want to change the value of a carNumber of carFord, I can do that.
87

88
00:06:45,680 --> 00:06:53,340
So, just mention the member element and after that just mention the structure variable,
88

89
00:06:53,420 --> 00:07:03,350
then select the member element let's say carNumber, and then you can assign some different value.
89

90
00:07:03,400 --> 00:07:09,820
So, this is called assigning a new value to the member element of a structure variable. The takeaway from
90

91
00:07:09,820 --> 00:07:14,800
this lecture is you should understand how to create a structure record.
91

92
00:07:14,800 --> 00:07:17,570
This is also called a structure definition.
92

93
00:07:17,620 --> 00:07:24,920
So, usually we create this in the header to file. But it's OK, if you create in .c file also.
93

94
00:07:24,940 --> 00:07:28,270
But make sure that it is present outside the function.
94

95
00:07:28,510 --> 00:07:32,770
You can even keep it inside the function, but that is not a correct method,
95

96
00:07:32,920 --> 00:07:38,120
I can even keep this here itself but that doesn't look good. Because it's a record.
96

97
00:07:38,980 --> 00:07:40,380
Let's keep that outside.
97

98
00:07:41,170 --> 00:07:44,630
And after that you create the variable something like this,
98

99
00:07:44,690 --> 00:07:45,010
where
99

100
00:07:45,010 --> 00:07:48,460
you first mention the data type. So, data type
100

101
00:07:48,460 --> 00:07:54,970
in this case is user defined data type, that is struct carModel. And then create as many variables
101

102
00:07:55,090 --> 00:07:59,290
as you want, either globally or locally.
102

103
00:07:59,290 --> 00:08:02,010
That depends on your application.
103

104
00:08:02,040 --> 00:08:06,250
So, this is how you optionally initialize the structure variable
104

105
00:08:06,540 --> 00:08:08,280
when you define them.
105

106
00:08:08,280 --> 00:08:12,330
This is actually optional, but it helps when you use constant structure.
106

107
00:08:12,330 --> 00:08:16,290
For example, I can also use const here. If you use const
107

108
00:08:16,290 --> 00:08:21,800
for example, let's say I use const here. So, you already studied about const.
108

109
00:08:22,690 --> 00:08:27,660
So, in that case this will help you to initialize at the first place.
109

110
00:08:27,730 --> 00:08:30,700
So, if you use const here, what happens?
110

111
00:08:30,700 --> 00:08:36,580
So, each and every member element of this structure variable will become constant variables.
111

112
00:08:37,390 --> 00:08:41,570
So, now you are trying to modify one of the member element here.
112

113
00:08:41,800 --> 00:08:43,830
So, this will give you an error,
113

114
00:08:43,900 --> 00:08:49,100
you can check that. So, let's try to compile this and there is an error.
114

115
00:08:49,330 --> 00:08:56,480
This is read only. And if you want to access the member elements of a structure variable, then use dot
115

116
00:08:56,520 --> 00:08:58,740
operator. With that note
116

117
00:08:58,740 --> 00:09:01,140
I would like to end this lecture.
117

118
00:09:01,140 --> 00:09:06,740
So, in the next lecture let's understand aligned/un-aligned data access and structure
118

119
00:09:06,740 --> 00:09:09,090
padding. I'll see you in the next lecture.

Hey, welcome back to the lecture.

2
00:00:02,250 --> 00:00:08,010
So, in this lecture and in the coming lectures you'll understand about aligned and unaligned data access
2

3
00:00:08,010 --> 00:00:09,340
and structure padding,
3

4
00:00:09,390 --> 00:00:16,350
but before that we have to do one small experiment. Lets use size of operator in order to find out the
4

5
00:00:16,350 --> 00:00:19,650
total memory consumed by our structure variables
5

6
00:00:19,650 --> 00:00:20,280
in this case.
6

7
00:00:21,180 --> 00:00:23,290
So, now let's use the same project
7

8
00:00:23,340 --> 00:00:30,120
and I'm going to delete all these things. I'm going to delete this as well,
8

9
00:00:30,200 --> 00:00:33,210
let me keep only one variable.
9

10
00:00:33,330 --> 00:00:35,490
So now, I want to find out
10

11
00:00:35,490 --> 00:00:38,950
memory consumed by this variable.
11

12
00:00:39,210 --> 00:00:48,640
For that, I can use size of operator. Let's print that. Printf sizeof of let's say struct carModel
12

13
00:00:48,940 --> 00:00:52,040
is %I64d.
13

14
00:00:52,240 --> 00:01:00,110
Let's use u. And I can use sizeof, you can mention this variable itself here.
14

15
00:01:00,420 --> 00:01:05,360
You can do that or you can also mention this data type.
15

16
00:01:05,490 --> 00:01:13,330
You can also do this. So, first let's calculate theoretically, how much memory it would consume.
16

17
00:01:13,500 --> 00:01:22,360
As I said it would consume around 14 bytes. Let's verify that. I just compared this.
17

18
00:01:22,360 --> 00:01:28,310
So, now let's execute and it says that it consumes 16 bytes.
18

19
00:01:28,670 --> 00:01:31,380
So, it is showing two bytes more.
19

20
00:01:31,620 --> 00:01:38,030
So, that's because of structure padding. Most of the time you programmer need not to worry about structure
20

21
00:01:38,060 --> 00:01:43,100
padding. But, if you are really curious what's happening behind the scene,
21

22
00:01:43,100 --> 00:01:50,570
so we should dig into the details of how exactly the compiler generates instructions to store our member
22

23
00:01:50,570 --> 00:01:53,170
elements in the memory.
23

24
00:01:53,210 --> 00:01:57,360
What if I have only one member element in this structure. 
24

25
00:01:58,160 --> 00:02:04,980
Let's say uint8_t carSpeed, only one member element.
25

26
00:02:04,980 --> 00:02:12,230
So, I would remove all these things, let's check. It consumes one byte.
26

27
00:02:12,230 --> 00:02:24,240
What if I have let's say uint or if I have uint32 here. Lets say carPrice. So, your guess maybe
27

28
00:02:24,300 --> 00:02:28,890
it may consume 4  + 1, 5 or 6.
28

29
00:02:28,890 --> 00:02:33,630
So, let's see how much it consumes. It consumes 8 bytes.
29

30
00:02:33,630 --> 00:02:37,050
So, let's investigate why it happens like that.
30

31
00:02:37,050 --> 00:02:42,900
The reason for that is data alignment and we'll discuss about this in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 4

0
1
00:00:00,120 --> 00:00:07,270
In this lecture and in the subsequent lectures, let's learn about aligned and unaligned data access.
1

2
00:00:07,500 --> 00:00:15,390
So, please note that, for efficiency, the compiler generates instructions to store variables on their natural
2

3
00:00:15,390 --> 00:00:15,780
size
3

4
00:00:15,780 --> 00:00:18,340
boundary addresses in the memory.
4

5
00:00:18,340 --> 00:00:26,230
So, this is also true for structures. So, member elements of a structure are located on their natural
5

6
00:00:26,230 --> 00:00:32,190
size boundary. Let's explore about natural size boundaries of different data types.
6

7
00:00:32,200 --> 00:00:34,360
Let's first consider the char data type.
7

8
00:00:35,230 --> 00:00:39,040
So, what is the natural size of a char data type?
8

9
00:00:39,040 --> 00:00:41,140
It's one byte.Char type
9

10
00:00:41,210 --> 00:00:50,320
variables can be placed at any addresses ending with 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, etc. So, let's consider these are the
10

11
00:00:50,320 --> 00:00:51,700
addresses.
11

12
00:00:51,700 --> 00:00:55,290
What is the natural size of a short type variable?
12

13
00:00:55,290 --> 00:01:05,770
It's two bytes. Short type variables will be stored in the memory at addresses which end with 0, 2, 4, 8,
13

14
00:01:06,520 --> 00:01:17,550
etc. And in the same way int type variables will be stored at addresses which end with 0, 4, 8, C, etc. So,
14

15
00:01:17,550 --> 00:01:21,900
compiler does this automatically when you generate the code.
15

16
00:01:21,900 --> 00:01:28,830
Basically, when you compile the code, compiler takes care of generating instructions which will arrange
16

17
00:01:28,890 --> 00:01:35,700
these variables in the memory in align fashion. So, when you compile the code, the compiler generates
17

18
00:01:35,700 --> 00:01:43,950
the instructions to store the variables in memory according to the variables natural size boundary, and
18

19
00:01:43,950 --> 00:01:47,910
that what we call as align data storage.
19

20
00:01:47,910 --> 00:01:55,380
So, in the next lecture let's write a simple program to investigate the data alignment of variables of
20

21
00:01:55,380 --> 00:01:58,850
different data types, and I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Hey, welcome back to the lecture.

2
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:05,410
So, here I have just created a program data alignment.
2

3
00:00:06,270 --> 00:00:10,680
So, this application I'm going to write for my host machine,
3

4
00:00:11,100 --> 00:00:15,070
and after that we can also reproduce this same program for our target.
4

5
00:00:16,050 --> 00:00:20,170
So here, I have different variables of different data types 
5

6
00:00:20,580 --> 00:00:22,330
char, int, and short.
6

7
00:00:22,770 --> 00:00:30,370
What I'm going to do is, I'm going to create one structure let me call this structure as let's say
7

8
00:00:30,700 --> 00:00:33,010
packet or whatever it is.
8

9
00:00:33,160 --> 00:00:42,620
Let me call this structure as DataSet. And after that, I'll just keep all these variables as member
9

10
00:00:42,620 --> 00:00:50,240
elements of this structure. So, you cannot initialize a structure like this.
10

11
00:00:50,260 --> 00:00:51,220
So, this is wrong.
11

12
00:00:52,120 --> 00:01:01,630
So, we have to remove this. What I do is, i will just copy this, I may need later, I will just remove this.
12

13
00:01:05,100 --> 00:01:07,560
So, we just created a record.
13

14
00:01:07,650 --> 00:01:09,840
A definitions should not be initialize like that.
14

15
00:01:12,970 --> 00:01:23,490
And after that I'm going to create one variable of this DataSet. So, struct DataSet let me call it data.
15

16
00:01:24,920 --> 00:01:32,490
And after that data of data1, I'm going to initialize like this data of data1 is equal to  
16

17
00:01:32,490 --> 00:01:33,750
0x11,
17

18
00:01:34,000 --> 00:01:43,080
and data of data2 is equal to this much, and data of data3 is equal to 0x22,
18

19
00:01:43,530 --> 00:01:48,580
and data of data4 is equal to 0xABCD
19

20
00:01:48,750 --> 00:01:56,220
I can do that. After that, I'm just going to build this, the build is successful OK, unused variable
20

21
00:01:56,260 --> 00:02:03,070
that's fine. After that, what I'm going to do is, I'm going to create one pointer variable of type  
21

22
00:02:03,070 --> 00:02:13,070
uint8 *. So, uint8_t *ptr. So, this is a pointer. And I'm going to initialize
22

23
00:02:13,080 --> 00:02:20,970
that pointer 'ptr' with the address of this structure variable. How do you get the address?
23

24
00:02:20,980 --> 00:02:30,580
you have to use & address operator. Address of data.The data type of this entity is struct data
24

25
00:02:30,700 --> 00:02:37,800
set * remember that. Because, this is a variable of type struct DataSet.
25

26
00:02:38,790 --> 00:02:40,270
So, this is uint8 *.
26

27
00:02:41,180 --> 00:02:47,970
So, there will be an issue. So, that's why you have to typecast this uint8_t *.
27

28
00:02:49,220 --> 00:02:56,410
So, after that I'm going to print the content which is pointed by this pointer. For that, what I am going
28

29
00:02:56,410 --> 00:03:05,490
to do is, first I'm going to take the total size of the struct. So, total size is equal to sizeof struct
29

30
00:03:05,530 --> 00:03:10,450
DataSet. So, let me take this as uint32_t.
30

31
00:03:11,740 --> 00:03:20,050
So, here I just store total size of the struct. And after that, I use a for loop to print the content of
31

32
00:03:20,050 --> 00:03:29,340
this pointer. Here it is. So, I have just print statement, so I just cut this, and I just keep here.
32

33
00:03:30,150 --> 00:03:38,490
I'm going to print in this format memory address and content. So, I'm going to use printf("%p").
33

34
00:03:38,860 --> 00:03:45,590
First I'm going to print the address, and after that I'm going to print the data \n
34

35
00:03:45,820 --> 00:03:49,450
and let me print in caps, 
35

36
00:03:49,540 --> 00:03:57,830
after that, I'm going to print the address first ptr and the content *ptr. And after that, I'm going
36

37
00:03:57,830 --> 00:04:01,790
to increment the ptr, ptr++.
37

38
00:04:01,790 --> 00:04:04,060
So, what exactly I'm doing here?
38

39
00:04:04,310 --> 00:04:07,610
So, I'm actually realizing this structure.
39

40
00:04:08,510 --> 00:04:15,920
So, I want to show you how exactly the member elements of this structure is stored in a memory by printing
40

41
00:04:15,920 --> 00:04:18,100
them byte by byte.
41

42
00:04:18,140 --> 00:04:24,100
So, that's why, I have equated the address of this structure into a char type pointer.
42

43
00:04:25,040 --> 00:04:31,160
So, we have to run this loop for these many times, total size times.
43

44
00:04:31,160 --> 00:04:38,030
So, that's why, let me take one iterator uint32_t i = 0, i is less than
44

45
00:04:38,090 --> 00:04:47,110
total size, and i++. After that, let me use getchar() here in order to hang the application.
45

46
00:04:47,590 --> 00:04:54,300
So, after that, here let's also print the total memory consumed by this struct variable.
46

47
00:04:54,580 --> 00:04:56,130
Let me use the sizeof operator.
47

48
00:04:56,620 --> 00:05:02,530
Lets compile this program and let's run. So, here is a output.
48

49
00:05:03,570 --> 00:05:05,450
Let's understand this output.
49

50
00:05:06,300 --> 00:05:11,220
By the way, the total memory consumed by this struct variable is 12 bytes. So,
50

51
00:05:11,280 --> 00:05:17,850
but if you count it manually this should be 4 + 1= 5, 6, 8.
51

52
00:05:18,150 --> 00:05:25,410
But, the actual memory consumed by the struct variable is 12, the extra 4 bytes are actually consumed
52

53
00:05:25,440 --> 00:05:26,950
by the padding,
53

54
00:05:27,160 --> 00:05:29,670
that's what you are seeing in this output.
54

55
00:05:29,670 --> 00:05:33,560
Let's analyze this output. The address what you are seeing here,
55

56
00:05:33,570 --> 00:05:38,200
the first address is nothing but the base address of the struct variable.
56

57
00:05:39,140 --> 00:05:42,050
And from there we actually printed  12
57

58
00:05:42,140 --> 00:05:49,260
consagative memory location contents. In the first memory location the first memory element is stored,
58

59
00:05:49,290 --> 00:05:53,720
that is 11. So, that is of type char.
59

60
00:05:53,770 --> 00:06:01,130
The interesting thing here is in the next memory location the next member element is not stored. Look
60

61
00:06:01,130 --> 00:06:03,440
at where the next member element is stored.
61

62
00:06:04,250 --> 00:06:10,340
So, the second member element is actually stored at this address which ends with 8.
62

63
00:06:10,370 --> 00:06:11,520
Why?
63

64
00:06:11,630 --> 00:06:18,930
Because, compiler stored that variable according to its natural size boundary.
64

65
00:06:19,010 --> 00:06:27,870
So, what is us natural size boundary of an int? 0, 4, 8, C etc. So, that's why, compilers stored the int
65

66
00:06:27,990 --> 00:06:36,980
member element from this address onwards. And what about these three memory locations? those are wasted.
66

67
00:06:37,290 --> 00:06:40,740
So, that's why, they are written with zeros.
67

68
00:06:40,740 --> 00:06:45,840
That's a 0 padding. So, you just lost three bytes of memory.
68

69
00:06:46,310 --> 00:06:48,960
And after that, the next member element is of type
69

70
00:06:48,960 --> 00:06:57,960
char. So, char be stored at any places, because its natural size boundary is 1. So, it can be stored
70

71
00:06:57,960 --> 00:07:00,720
at any memory locations.
71

72
00:07:00,780 --> 00:07:08,040
So, now the next member element is short and short is stored according to its natural size boundary,
72

73
00:07:08,850 --> 00:07:13,420
and its next natural size boundary falls in 0E.
73

74
00:07:13,680 --> 00:07:19,240
So, that's why, compilers stored that variable here and there is a zero padding here.
74

75
00:07:19,290 --> 00:07:28,740
So, in this structure storage you just lost 4 bytes of memory, because of aligned data storage and these
75

76
00:07:28,740 --> 00:07:30,490
are called as padding.
76

77
00:07:31,170 --> 00:07:39,060
So, that's why, the total memory consumed by this struct variable is not 8 bytes, it is 12 bytes.
77

78
00:07:39,120 --> 00:07:43,210
So, now that's about the aligned data storage and structure
78

79
00:07:43,220 --> 00:07:48,170
padding. One question maybe bothering you why compiler does like this.
79

80
00:07:48,420 --> 00:07:54,840
We will address this again when we execute the same code in our target hardware, and there I will show
80

81
00:07:54,840 --> 00:08:01,080
you why aligned data storage is better than unaligned data storage.
81

82
00:08:01,080 --> 00:08:08,220
Please note that, when the data is stored in aligned fashion it becomes lot easier for the processor
82

83
00:08:08,250 --> 00:08:15,090
to do read and write transactions with the memory. Because, there will be less instructions dealing with
83

84
00:08:15,090 --> 00:08:23,280
the memory and there will be less bus transactions with a memory. So, processors execution performance
84

85
00:08:23,280 --> 00:08:28,340
will be improved if the data is stored in aligned fashion in the memory.
85

86
00:08:29,220 --> 00:08:36,600
If the data is stored in unaligned fashion, more instructions have to be added to your executable in order
86

87
00:08:36,600 --> 00:08:43,920
to deal with the memory, in order to fetch the value from the memory, to decode it, extract it, to do
87

88
00:08:43,920 --> 00:08:44,720
all these things
88

89
00:08:44,730 --> 00:08:52,740
lot of instructions will be generated . Unaligned data access actually increases your code size. So, you
89

90
00:08:52,740 --> 00:08:57,690
can experiment this on the target, we'll do that in the next lecture.
90

91
00:08:57,900 --> 00:09:04,470
So, the only negative side effect of aligned data storage is due to padding you will lose some memory
91

92
00:09:04,470 --> 00:09:09,160
locations. But, if you are memory is really precious
92

93
00:09:09,180 --> 00:09:16,290
if you can't afford to lose your memory locations, then you can go with unaligned data storage.
93

94
00:09:16,410 --> 00:09:24,470
We can make this as unaligned data storage by using packed structure. So, there is a GCC attribute called
94

95
00:09:24,570 --> 00:09:25,840
packed.
95

96
00:09:26,190 --> 00:09:33,750
By using that we can convert this structure into packet structure which results in unaligned data storage.
96

97
00:09:33,770 --> 00:09:36,260
We will try this in the next lecture maybe.
97

98
00:09:36,720 --> 00:09:42,620
So, I hope you understood about the aligned data storage and structure padding,  
98

99
00:09:42,720 --> 00:09:43,710
and I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 6

0
1
00:00:00,120 --> 00:00:06,830
So, in the previous video you calculated the structure size including padding using a program.
1

2
00:00:07,680 --> 00:00:10,850
So, now let's learn how to calculate this manually.
2

3
00:00:10,950 --> 00:00:13,450
For that, let's consider the same structure,
3

4
00:00:14,350 --> 00:00:19,390
and here there is a diagram in order to analyze the memory consumption.
4

5
00:00:19,390 --> 00:00:24,600
Let's consider the width of this memory as a 4 bytes.
5

6
00:00:24,670 --> 00:00:33,780
So, first what happens is, let's start from 0. First member element is of one byte. So, let's divide this.
6

7
00:00:33,810 --> 00:00:44,200
This is one byte. So, that's why, data1 will be stored here. So, next member element is int, int is of
7

8
00:00:44,200 --> 00:00:52,210
4 bytes, but there is room for only 3 bytes. So, that's why, int will occupy here.
8

9
00:00:52,250 --> 00:00:58,870
This is the place for data2. So, these are padded with zeros.
9

10
00:01:04,360 --> 00:01:10,370
And after that next is char, it will occupy this position  data
10

11
00:01:10,390 --> 00:01:10,660
3.
11

12
00:01:13,510 --> 00:01:15,130
And next is short.
12

13
00:01:15,580 --> 00:01:24,240
You cannot fit short here. So, short will be move to the next aligned memory locations, short is stored here
13

14
00:01:24,250 --> 00:01:24,970
data4
14

15
00:01:28,450 --> 00:01:34,530
and this will be padded with zero. So, the previous output you can understand something like this and you
15

16
00:01:34,530 --> 00:01:39,330
can theoretically calculate the size of the struct including padding.
16

17
00:01:39,570 --> 00:01:44,580
So, 4 + 4 + 4, which is 12 bytes.
17

18
00:01:44,700 --> 00:01:48,350
So, what happens if you do no padding? To do no padding
18

19
00:01:48,360 --> 00:01:56,400
you have to attach this compiler attribute called packed. If you do that, padding will not be inserted.
19

20
00:01:57,390 --> 00:02:07,040
So, what happens is, first is char, data1 will be here. Next member element will be force to use
20

21
00:02:07,160 --> 00:02:16,490
these locations, the data2 will occupy here, the first byte of the data2. After that
21

22
00:02:16,490 --> 00:02:24,440
this is data2 second byte, data2 third byte, here
22

23
00:02:24,440 --> 00:02:27,920
data2 fourth byte.
23

24
00:02:28,010 --> 00:02:33,920
So, here we can see that you know there is a split of data2, which is actually not good if you consider
24

25
00:02:33,920 --> 00:02:38,430
the performance. And after that this is a place for data3,
25

26
00:02:38,570 --> 00:02:41,320
so one byte is sufficient, no problem data
26

27
00:02:41,360 --> 00:02:41,980
3.
27

28
00:02:42,370 --> 00:02:43,310
And next
28

29
00:02:43,340 --> 00:02:48,300
these are for data4. So, 4 + 4.
29

30
00:02:48,330 --> 00:02:55,320
So, totally you consume 8 bytes. So, you can see that in a packet structure everything is packed, so no
30

31
00:02:55,320 --> 00:02:57,020
room for padding.
31

32
00:02:57,060 --> 00:03:03,220
So, now in the next lecture let's do assembly code analysis of packed and non packed structure.
32

33
00:03:03,340 --> 00:03:04,430
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


video 7

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:06,900
Let's do one exercise, where we are going to do assembly code analysis of packed and non-packed structure
1

2
00:00:06,910 --> 00:00:07,570
access.
2

3
00:00:07,680 --> 00:00:13,530
For that, I have just created one project here 009packed_Vsnonpacked. Here
3

4
00:00:13,530 --> 00:00:15,300
I just created one structure,
4

5
00:00:15,300 --> 00:00:18,400
this is obviously a non-packed structure.
5

6
00:00:18,510 --> 00:00:21,510
And after that I just created a global variable of that,
6

7
00:00:21,600 --> 00:00:26,580
and after that I just initialized each and every member elements of that variable,
7

8
00:00:26,580 --> 00:00:28,060
then I'm just printing that.
8

9
00:00:28,140 --> 00:00:38,490
So, this project I wrote for my target hardware. Let's build and debug this code, lets analyze the disassembly
9

10
00:00:38,490 --> 00:00:39,480
of this code.
10

11
00:00:39,480 --> 00:00:43,120
So, here is a disassembly of these initializations.
11

12
00:00:43,350 --> 00:00:44,710
So, let's start from here.
12

13
00:00:44,760 --> 00:00:53,070
You can see that I am copying data 0xAA into this variable, these are the instructions generated.
13

14
00:00:53,820 --> 00:00:59,330
You can see that there is a instructions store b, that means store a byte into memory. Here
14

15
00:00:59,340 --> 00:01:05,370
the compiler actually used strb instruction, because we are dealing with a byte.
15

16
00:01:05,460 --> 00:01:09,750
So, in the same way check the instruction for this one.
16

17
00:01:09,750 --> 00:01:14,310
So, here we are putting a word value into a variable.
17

18
00:01:14,430 --> 00:01:17,830
So, that's why, you can see that it is using str instruction.
18

19
00:01:17,880 --> 00:01:23,430
So, depending upon the data type the compiler actually uses different instructions, which are supported
19

20
00:01:23,430 --> 00:01:28,550
by the ARM instruction set architecture. For byte it uses strb, for word
20

21
00:01:28,560 --> 00:01:38,220
it uses str, and again for the byte it uses strb, and for the short you can see that it uses strh.
21

22
00:01:38,280 --> 00:01:40,160
That means store a half word.
22

23
00:01:40,200 --> 00:01:47,690
'h' stands for half word. So, and you should also note that, for the 'STR' instruction the address must be word-aligned
23

24
00:01:47,850 --> 00:01:51,780
and for the 'STRH' instruction the address must be
24

25
00:01:51,810 --> 00:01:55,470
that means the address what you give as an operand must be half word aligned.
25

26
00:01:55,530 --> 00:01:57,480
Otherwise, the instruction will not work.
26

27
00:01:57,510 --> 00:02:04,600
So, that's a restriction on these instruction. STR works only with the word-aligned addresses, STRH works
27

28
00:02:04,620 --> 00:02:06,900
only with the halfword-aligned addresses.
28

29
00:02:07,080 --> 00:02:14,760
So, that's a reason why all your data gets stored in the memory in an aligned fashion. So, to store a value
29

30
00:02:14,880 --> 00:02:18,750
into this variable it just uses 3 instructions, that's it. The job is done.
30

31
00:02:19,170 --> 00:02:25,150
So, now let's analyze the same disassembly when we use unaligned packed structure. For that, what you have
31

32
00:02:25,150 --> 00:02:30,300
to do is, you have to put this attribute, there is a __attribute__ ((packed))
32

33
00:02:30,360 --> 00:02:31,860
in double parentheses.
33

34
00:02:31,920 --> 00:02:38,100
So, this you have to use along with the structure definition. This structure becomes packed structure which
34

35
00:02:38,160 --> 00:02:40,810
leads to unaligned data storage.
35

36
00:02:40,810 --> 00:02:43,650
Let's recompile the code and let's see the disassembly
36

37
00:02:46,560 --> 00:02:49,780
Here is a disassembly of packed structure access.
37

38
00:02:49,840 --> 00:02:55,580
So, this looks different from the previous one. So, let's start with this line.
38

39
00:02:55,710 --> 00:03:03,330
We are actually putting 0xAA into memory, where this variable is located. So, you can see that this
39

40
00:03:03,330 --> 00:03:09,720
is actually aligned transaction, because it's a byte of data. So, strb is used, that's fine. But take a look
40

41
00:03:09,750 --> 00:03:14,730
into the codes generated for this statement.
41

42
00:03:14,730 --> 00:03:20,520
Now you can see that earlier there were only 3 instructions, but here you can see that now we have
42

43
00:03:20,580 --> 00:03:24,550
lot of instructions. Why is that? So,
43

44
00:03:24,620 --> 00:03:32,660
this clearly shows that accessing unaligned data increases your code size, the compiler has added lots
44

45
00:03:32,660 --> 00:03:33,840
of instructions here.
45

46
00:03:33,860 --> 00:03:34,640
Why is that?
46

47
00:03:34,640 --> 00:03:41,670
The reason is to store a word into memory only STR instruction is sufficient.
47

48
00:03:41,690 --> 00:03:49,730
But, here STR cannot be used. Because, as I said previously STR works with only word-aligned addresses.
48

49
00:03:50,820 --> 00:03:57,480
But here you are trying to store a word value in its non-natural boundary address.
49

50
00:03:57,480 --> 00:04:00,030
That's a reason STR can't be used here.
50

51
00:04:00,090 --> 00:04:04,040
So, the transaction will be divided into number of strb.
51

52
00:04:04,140 --> 00:04:11,780
So, that's the reason why you see 4 strb instructions here. So, in the earlier case the processor was
52

53
00:04:11,780 --> 00:04:15,140
talking to the memory only one time using STR.
53

54
00:04:15,140 --> 00:04:22,760
Now the processor is talking to the memory 4 times, there you lose lots of block cycles.
54

55
00:04:22,760 --> 00:04:25,180
So, that's how the performance degrades
55

56
00:04:25,220 --> 00:04:27,640
if you use unaligned data access.
56

57
00:04:27,710 --> 00:04:35,940
So, now you see here, STR is divided into 4 strb and also for the data manipulation lots of orn
57

58
00:04:35,960 --> 00:04:42,820
instructions have been used. So, that surely decrease the performance of your application.
58

59
00:04:42,920 --> 00:04:49,550
So, adding of all these instructions will surely increase the executable code size of your application.
59

60
00:04:49,550 --> 00:04:51,190
After that, take a look into this statement
60

61
00:04:51,200 --> 00:04:52,650
again this is aligned access.
61

62
00:04:52,730 --> 00:04:56,500
So, that's why, you see only 3 instructions here, strb is used.
62

63
00:04:56,510 --> 00:04:57,070
That's fine.
63

64
00:04:57,070 --> 00:04:59,660
And now check this one. Again
64

65
00:04:59,720 --> 00:05:00,920
this is unaligned access.
65

66
00:05:00,920 --> 00:05:07,360
So, here you are trying to put the short value into its non-natural boundary address.
66

67
00:05:07,370 --> 00:05:08,000
So, that's why,
67

68
00:05:08,000 --> 00:05:10,670
again two strb's are used here.
68

69
00:05:10,670 --> 00:05:16,380
So, this would have done easily by 1 strh as I shown in the earlier part of this video.
69

70
00:05:16,400 --> 00:05:22,820
So, that's a proof that you know using unaligned access degrades performance and increases code size of
70

71
00:05:22,820 --> 00:05:24,200
the final executable.
71

72
00:05:24,500 --> 00:05:29,240
So, that's why, you should be careful a before you pack your structure. So, you can compare the code
72

73
00:05:29,240 --> 00:05:34,970
size here, for unpacked it takes 5112 and for the packed you see increase in this number.
73

74
00:05:35,030 --> 00:05:38,360
So, now in the next lecture I will cover typedef with structure.
74

75
00:05:38,390 --> 00:05:39,410
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 8

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,710
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,710 --> 00:00:07,890
So, in this lecture let's understand about using typedef keyword with structure.
2

3
00:00:08,870 --> 00:00:18,580
Now, typedef keyword in 'C' is used to give an alias name to primitive and user defined data types.
3

4
00:00:19,130 --> 00:00:24,000
For example, consider this structure definition.
4

5
00:00:24,040 --> 00:00:24,690
OK.
5

6
00:00:24,920 --> 00:00:33,960
So here, you have use the 'struct' keyword and you actually identify this structure with the name, that
6

7
00:00:33,980 --> 00:00:37,600
what we call as tag name CarModel.
7

8
00:00:37,850 --> 00:00:45,320
And when you want to create variables of this structure definition, then what you have to
8

9
00:00:45,320 --> 00:00:49,000
do is, you have to mention this struct CarModel, right?
9

10
00:00:49,010 --> 00:00:50,590
So, this is a data type.
10

11
00:00:50,710 --> 00:00:54,490
Now instead of doing this OK, you can also do this.
11

12
00:00:54,860 --> 00:00:55,230
OK.
12

13
00:00:55,260 --> 00:00:59,270
Instead of writing this long name struct CarModel,
13

14
00:00:59,270 --> 00:01:01,710
you can just finish it of in one word.
14

15
00:01:01,960 --> 00:01:02,170
OK.
15

16
00:01:02,180 --> 00:01:10,970
So, this is what we call as typedef name or an alias name. For that, you have to create the structure in
16

17
00:01:10,970 --> 00:01:12,080
this fashion.
17

18
00:01:12,080 --> 00:01:17,140
First you have to use two standard reserved keywords, typedef struct.
18

19
00:01:17,190 --> 00:01:17,500
OK.
19

20
00:01:17,510 --> 00:01:18,680
There is a space in between.
20

21
00:01:18,790 --> 00:01:19,080
OK.
21

22
00:01:19,850 --> 00:01:23,720
So, typedef struct and don't mention any tag name,
22

23
00:01:23,750 --> 00:01:24,560
this is optional.
23

24
00:01:24,650 --> 00:01:32,660
Okay. So, don't mention any tag name here. But, give an alias name to this structure.
24

25
00:01:33,470 --> 00:01:33,900
Okay.
25

26
00:01:33,920 --> 00:01:36,530
Like this.
26

27
00:01:36,550 --> 00:01:38,540
So, this is a typedef name
27

28
00:01:38,540 --> 00:01:38,850
Ok
28

29
00:01:38,860 --> 00:01:43,180
or an alias name for this structure.
29

30
00:01:43,180 --> 00:01:51,700
And after that, by just using that typedef name you can you know create the variables. So, by using type
30

31
00:01:51,700 --> 00:01:53,580
that you can give an alias name.
31

32
00:01:53,590 --> 00:01:54,480
Okay.
32

33
00:01:54,550 --> 00:02:00,470
Now this is also used with primitive data type like integer, char, unsigned int, like that.
33

34
00:02:00,500 --> 00:02:02,440
So, we have already used that, right?
34

35
00:02:02,830 --> 00:02:06,190
So, when we include the file stdint.h,
35

36
00:02:06,280 --> 00:02:14,080
So, stdint.h  has various typedef definitions for different primitive data types. And this
36

37
00:02:14,170 --> 00:02:20,110
\t is just used to distinguish between and enum and a typedef.
37

38
00:02:20,110 --> 00:02:20,740
Okay?
38

39
00:02:20,950 --> 00:02:23,950
For example, for enum will use _e.
39

40
00:02:24,790 --> 00:02:28,780
So, if you don't mention this then what happens? If you just mentioned this
40

41
00:02:28,780 --> 00:02:30,450
it actually confuses the programmer.
41

42
00:02:30,460 --> 00:02:31,460
They don't understand.
42

43
00:02:31,480 --> 00:02:36,910
So, whether it is an enum definition or whether it is a typedef definition.
43

44
00:02:37,660 --> 00:02:42,010
So, to resolve that confusion we just use _t here.
44

45
00:02:42,010 --> 00:02:47,480
So, there is no a you know strict requirement that you should use _t.
45

46
00:02:47,530 --> 00:02:50,830
So, this is just done for readability purpose.
46

47
00:02:51,420 --> 00:02:51,850
All right.
47

48
00:02:51,850 --> 00:02:57,280
So, now let's convert this structure in our earlier program into typedef structure.
48

49
00:02:57,320 --> 00:02:58,150
OK.
49

50
00:02:58,390 --> 00:03:00,490
So, what have to do is, first
50

51
00:03:00,520 --> 00:03:02,530
you have to remove this a tag name
51

52
00:03:02,600 --> 00:03:03,320
OK?
52

53
00:03:03,460 --> 00:03:07,190
and then give the typedef keyword.
53

54
00:03:07,220 --> 00:03:08,290
All right.
54

55
00:03:08,300 --> 00:03:12,540
And after that mention the alias name or typedef name.
55

56
00:03:12,700 --> 00:03:13,070
OK.
56

57
00:03:13,150 --> 00:03:20,850
So, now let me write data set and for the identification purposes that it is a typedef name I use  
57

58
00:03:20,850 --> 00:03:21,190
_t.
58

59
00:03:21,640 --> 00:03:25,800
So, some people use all caps here for the typedef name.
59

60
00:03:26,700 --> 00:03:27,010
All right.
60

61
00:03:27,070 --> 00:03:31,250
So, you can follow that method or you can use pascal case OK.
61

62
00:03:31,280 --> 00:03:35,060
That is the first letter you can keep capital data set.
62

63
00:03:35,050 --> 00:03:36,340
So, this is also fine.
63

64
00:03:36,430 --> 00:03:37,290
OK.
64

65
00:03:37,390 --> 00:03:40,860
So, after that you need not to now write like this.
65

66
00:03:40,930 --> 00:03:41,160
OK.
66

67
00:03:41,170 --> 00:03:49,440
You can remove this and you can just mention the typedef name. So, that's how you can use a typedef struct.
67

68
00:03:49,480 --> 00:03:50,510
Okay.
68

69
00:03:50,610 --> 00:03:57,730
So, if you open the microcontroller device header file you will see lots of typedef structure. 
69

70
00:03:57,820 --> 00:04:01,910
So, this a idea behind that and let's move forward.
70

71
00:04:01,910 --> 00:04:02,390
OK.
71

72
00:04:02,480 --> 00:04:06,770
And now let's move forward, now let's explore some more points about the structure.
72

73
00:04:06,800 --> 00:04:12,710
So, remember that a struc ture type cannot contain itself as a member.
73

74
00:04:12,850 --> 00:04:13,610
OK?
74

75
00:04:13,910 --> 00:04:16,180
So, that means this is not allowed.
75

76
00:04:16,220 --> 00:04:18,880
This will actually throw compilation error.
76

77
00:04:19,130 --> 00:04:23,160
So, a structure type cannot contain itself as a member.
77

78
00:04:23,300 --> 00:04:29,950
And this is interesting. Structure types can contain pointers to their own type.
78

79
00:04:29,990 --> 00:04:36,740
This is allowed OK. So, such self-referential structures are used in implementing linked list and binary
79

80
00:04:36,740 --> 00:04:38,770
trees. For example,
80

81
00:04:38,840 --> 00:04:39,980
this is allowed.
81

82
00:04:39,980 --> 00:04:40,230
OK.
82

83
00:04:40,280 --> 00:04:42,620
So, this will not give any compilation error.
83

84
00:04:43,490 --> 00:04:49,370
So, you have to note that structure definition can contain pointers to there own type.
84

85
00:04:49,370 --> 00:04:54,830
So, now let's explore about the nested structure. Nested structure is nothing but structure inside
85

86
00:04:54,830 --> 00:04:55,430
a structure.
86

87
00:04:55,460 --> 00:04:56,620
So, this is also allowed.
87

88
00:04:56,750 --> 00:05:02,390
Here we can see that, this is a main structure and these are the member elements. In which one member
88

89
00:05:02,390 --> 00:05:04,580
element is of type struct.
89

90
00:05:04,580 --> 00:05:12,710
So, here this is a member element moreData. So, this moreData is not actually a tag name, so this
90

91
00:05:12,710 --> 00:05:17,570
moreData is neither a tagname nor a typedef name
91

92
00:05:17,570 --> 00:05:24,650
remember. So, this is a variable. So, you can also create a variable like this without giving any tag name,
92

93
00:05:24,670 --> 00:05:27,750
you can mention the variable name here itself
93

94
00:05:27,760 --> 00:05:36,190
and this method we usually use when we use structure inside a structure.So, that's why, here there
94

95
00:05:36,190 --> 00:05:44,350
are 5 member elements for this structure and 4 are of primitive data types and one is of user defined
95

96
00:05:44,350 --> 00:05:49,400
data type. So, in the next lecture I will cover structures and pointers.
96

97
00:05:49,570 --> 00:05:54,910
So, we are going to learn creating pointer variables of a structure and also will explore reading and
97

98
00:05:54,910 --> 00:06:02,190
writing data with the member elements using structure pointers. And I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 9

0
1
00:00:00,330 --> 00:00:04,680
So, from this lecture onwards let's understand structures and pointers.
1

2
00:00:05,280 --> 00:00:10,930
So, in this section we are going to learn creating pointer variables of a structure
2

3
00:00:11,100 --> 00:00:18,420
and also we're going to learn reading and writing data with member elements using structure pointers.
3

4
00:00:18,420 --> 00:00:20,550
Before that let's understand some basics.
4

5
00:00:21,120 --> 00:00:29,160
So, this output we actually got in the earlier videos. So, where I showed you how  a structure variable is
5

6
00:00:29,160 --> 00:00:30,990
stored in the memory.
6

7
00:00:31,050 --> 00:00:33,600
Let's just recap this.
7

8
00:00:33,660 --> 00:00:35,900
So, this is a structured definition
8

9
00:00:35,940 --> 00:00:39,900
and here we created a variable or data.
9

10
00:00:39,900 --> 00:00:42,750
So, as I said this doesn't consume any memory.
10

11
00:00:42,990 --> 00:00:44,890
So, this is just a record.
11

12
00:00:44,910 --> 00:00:51,700
So, this is the structure variable, this consumes the memory. And this output shows how exactly the
12

13
00:00:51,700 --> 00:00:56,080
member elements of this structured variable is stored in the memory.
13

14
00:00:56,140 --> 00:00:59,560
So, they are in contiguous memory locations.
14

15
00:00:59,680 --> 00:01:04,920
So, here is the first address happens to be the base address of the structure variable.
15

16
00:01:05,230 --> 00:01:12,010
And it is also true that it happens to be the base address of the first member element.
16

17
00:01:12,700 --> 00:01:18,430
So, here we can see that in the first member element we actually stored the value 11, same you can
17

18
00:01:18,430 --> 00:01:19,420
see here.
18

19
00:01:19,510 --> 00:01:27,400
So, that proves that the base address of a structure variable is nothing but the address of the first
19

20
00:01:27,520 --> 00:01:28,930
member element.
20

21
00:01:29,560 --> 00:01:34,960
Let's say you have been given with the base address of a structure variable and asked to change the
21

22
00:01:35,230 --> 00:01:37,120
member element values
22

23
00:01:37,120 --> 00:01:38,500
What would you do?
23

24
00:01:38,500 --> 00:01:43,290
So, you have to change the member element values using the base address of the structure variable.
24

25
00:01:44,000 --> 00:01:45,280
So, how do you do it?
25

26
00:01:45,310 --> 00:01:52,490
Let's try this. Because, this is very important to understand the structure pointers. Let's try by writing
26

27
00:01:52,550 --> 00:01:53,470
some piece of code.
27

28
00:01:54,230 --> 00:01:58,040
So, what I said was, you have been given with the address of the structure variable.
28

29
00:01:58,770 --> 00:02:00,920
Let's consider &data.
29

30
00:02:01,550 --> 00:02:06,700
So now, where do you store this address? Let's create one pointer variable.
30

31
00:02:06,710 --> 00:02:11,610
When I say create a pointer variable, so what is a type of that pointer variable?
31

32
00:02:11,810 --> 00:02:18,630
Let's consider the basic primitive pointer type, that is uint8_t.
32

33
00:02:18,860 --> 00:02:25,150
So, let's create a pointer let's say ptr = &data.
33

34
00:02:25,160 --> 00:02:33,650
This is not a valid syntax so we have to typecast here. Lets modify the first element.
34

35
00:02:33,970 --> 00:02:36,000
So, because I got the address here.
35

36
00:02:36,000 --> 00:02:40,350
So, this is a base address of the structure variable and which happens to be the address of the first
36

37
00:02:40,350 --> 00:02:40,920
member element.
37

38
00:02:41,730 --> 00:02:48,420
So, I can easily do this. *ptr is equal to let's say I put some other value 55, which actually
38

39
00:02:48,420 --> 00:02:50,510
modifies the data1.
39

40
00:02:50,550 --> 00:02:52,240
So, this may work.
40

41
00:02:52,620 --> 00:02:56,760
How about modifying second element?
41

42
00:02:56,760 --> 00:03:03,300
This becomes tedious. Because, you don't know how many times you have to increment this to point to the
42

43
00:03:03,300 --> 00:03:05,700
next member element.
43

44
00:03:05,740 --> 00:03:11,810
So because, in between the padding bits are there, so it becomes tedious to guess.
44

45
00:03:12,000 --> 00:03:18,720
So, even if you do that, most of the time you will end up with programming wrong values into wrong member
45

46
00:03:18,720 --> 00:03:20,180
elements. Here
46

47
00:03:20,220 --> 00:03:23,930
the easiest method is you do something like this.
47

48
00:03:24,060 --> 00:03:28,330
So, instead of creating a pointer variable of uint8
48

49
00:03:28,350 --> 00:03:34,590
or uint16 etc. So, create a pointer variable of that struct.
49

50
00:03:34,590 --> 00:03:44,460
So here, pData is a pointer variable of type struct DataSet *. And after that you store the address
50

51
00:03:44,550 --> 00:03:48,880
of this structure into the pointer variable.
51

52
00:03:48,930 --> 00:03:57,250
This is a pointer. So, pData now holds the address of structure variable data.
52

53
00:03:57,360 --> 00:04:05,210
After that, you can change the value of first member element data1 like this. You use that pointer
53

54
00:04:05,220 --> 00:04:13,660
variable and dereference that, and select the first member element and then assign the value.
54

55
00:04:13,710 --> 00:04:22,420
So, this what we call as dereferencing operator when a structure pointer is involved. So, you have
55

56
00:04:22,420 --> 00:04:27,510
to use this dereferencing operator only when structure pointer is involved
56

57
00:04:27,580 --> 00:04:35,150
remember. You use a dot operator when a non-pointer type variable is involved.
57

58
00:04:35,230 --> 00:04:39,040
So, now this code is equivalent to this code.
58

59
00:04:39,310 --> 00:04:46,030
The meaning of this syntax is nothing but you are actually dereferencing the address of the first member
59

60
00:04:46,030 --> 00:04:51,850
element and storing the value. But you need not to calculate the address of the first member element,
60

61
00:04:51,880 --> 00:04:54,160
you just use this syntax.
61

62
00:04:54,280 --> 00:05:00,880
So, that's why, you have to remember these two points. Use dot (.) operator when you use structure variable
62

63
00:05:00,970 --> 00:05:05,050
of non pointer type. Use arrow (->) operator
63

64
00:05:05,050 --> 00:05:07,960
this is also called as dereferencing operator when  structure
64

65
00:05:07,960 --> 00:05:15,750
pointers are involved. So, use arrow (->) operator when you use structure variable of pointer type.
65

66
00:05:15,750 --> 00:05:20,750
Let's go back to our code, and let's remove all these things,
66

67
00:05:20,980 --> 00:05:26,400
first let's create one structure variable of pointer type.
67

68
00:05:26,650 --> 00:05:28,480
Let me call it as pData.
68

69
00:05:29,290 --> 00:05:39,970
And after that, you can easily take this pointer and you can assign the address of this structure variable.
69

70
00:05:40,180 --> 00:05:44,700
So, I'm just using pointer to change the values of the member element.
70

71
00:05:45,040 --> 00:05:51,490
And after that you can use this pointer variable and use the arrow mark,
71

72
00:05:51,490 --> 00:05:58,250
and here we can see that the eclipse automatically shows the member elements, select the data1 and store
72

73
00:05:58,300 --> 00:06:00,590
some different value 0x55.
73

74
00:06:00,590 --> 00:06:10,460
Let's print the value before and after. Let's say, printf data1 is equal to %d, data
74

75
00:06:10,490 --> 00:06:14,580
of data1 this is before.
75

76
00:06:14,630 --> 00:06:24,170
So, now we modified this data1 through pointer. Let's see the effect. Lets write after.
76

77
00:06:24,620 --> 00:06:27,950
So, now it should print different values.
77

78
00:06:27,950 --> 00:06:35,640
Let's use 'X' here, let's print this in hex. Let me execute the program and here we can see that the first
78

79
00:06:35,640 --> 00:06:38,700
member element data1 is indeed changed.
79

80
00:06:39,030 --> 00:06:46,890
So, some of you may be having a question like you know why did I change the member element values of
80

81
00:06:46,890 --> 00:06:53,600
this structure variable using pointers? The reason is most of the time you'll be sending the address
81

82
00:06:53,600 --> 00:06:59,360
of the structure variable to some other function, and that function then has to receive your address
82

83
00:06:59,820 --> 00:07:04,560
and it has to manipulate the member elements. Let's see how to do that.
83

84
00:07:04,620 --> 00:07:06,570
That is passing
84

85
00:07:06,720 --> 00:07:11,320
the address of the structure variable to some other function.
85

86
00:07:11,400 --> 00:07:13,900
For that, let's create one function.
86

87
00:07:13,980 --> 00:07:20,910
Let me call it as a displayMemberElements, and let's for a temporarily let's call it as void,
87

88
00:07:20,990 --> 00:07:27,680
let me delete all these things. Let's say you are going to send the address of this struct variable to
88

89
00:07:27,680 --> 00:07:31,970
this function. So, this function has to receive it.
89

90
00:07:32,020 --> 00:07:39,170
So, here you need to create a structure pointer. Because, main function is going to send the address.
90

91
00:07:39,190 --> 00:07:45,810
Let's create a struct variable to receive the pointer, struct DataSet
91

92
00:07:45,820 --> 00:07:49,010
* pdata.
92

93
00:07:49,270 --> 00:07:50,860
Let's give the prototype of
93

94
00:07:50,860 --> 00:07:53,490
this, right here.
94

95
00:07:53,510 --> 00:07:57,710
So now, you can call this function from main and data.
95

96
00:07:57,740 --> 00:08:04,200
Now you can print different member elements of this structure, in this function using this address.
96

97
00:08:04,340 --> 00:08:05,090
How do you do that?
97

98
00:08:05,720 --> 00:08:13,570
Let's say, data1 =  %X\n, pdata
98

99
00:08:13,570 --> 00:08:17,700
now you have to use the arrow operator not dot operator
99

100
00:08:17,700 --> 00:08:22,620
remember. We are dealing with the structure pointer now and select the data1.
100

101
00:08:23,040 --> 00:08:31,400
So, by doing this you can access each and every member elements. Let's compile this.
101

102
00:08:32,010 --> 00:08:35,810
So, there is an issue. OK.So, there is an error.
102

103
00:08:35,820 --> 00:08:38,950
Conflicting types for this one.
103

104
00:08:39,180 --> 00:08:45,790
Problem is with the place where this prototype is mentioned. Remember that you are using user defined
104

105
00:08:45,790 --> 00:08:51,370
data type. So, the compiler sees the user defined data type here, but it doesn't know the definition of
105

106
00:08:51,370 --> 00:08:52,390
that.
106

107
00:08:52,430 --> 00:08:53,880
The definition comes next.
107

108
00:08:54,840 --> 00:08:55,710
This is wrong.
108

109
00:08:55,720 --> 00:08:58,840
That's the reason why we keep this in header file.
109

110
00:08:58,950 --> 00:09:01,630
So, in this case it has to come first.
110

111
00:09:01,680 --> 00:09:08,250
The compiler understands your user defined data type and then it can interpret this properly.
111

112
00:09:08,250 --> 00:09:11,430
So, now you can resolve this issue.
112

113
00:09:11,430 --> 00:09:12,570
No problem with the code.
113

114
00:09:13,560 --> 00:09:14,750
Now let's execute this
114

115
00:09:14,770 --> 00:09:21,100
and here we can see that, you are able to print the contents of the structure variable using pointers.
115

116
00:09:22,000 --> 00:09:30,580
So here, a the passing of this structure pointer to another function is called as pass by reference,
116

117
00:09:31,390 --> 00:09:35,360
because you are sending the address of the structure.
117

118
00:09:35,410 --> 00:09:40,230
You can also pass this structure variable by value.
118

119
00:09:40,310 --> 00:09:44,240
You can also do that, and that case is very rarely used.
119

120
00:09:45,780 --> 00:09:53,790
So, the more widely used method is to send the address of the structure variable, rather than structure
120

121
00:09:53,790 --> 00:09:55,130
itself.
121

122
00:09:55,200 --> 00:09:56,790
You can even do that,
122

123
00:09:56,790 --> 00:10:04,780
you can send the structure itself. So, that what we call as pass by value. For that, let's change this
123

124
00:10:04,780 --> 00:10:06,130
code,
124

125
00:10:06,130 --> 00:10:07,990
so let me show you that.
125

126
00:10:08,080 --> 00:10:12,360
Let's say you want to send this structure variable itself.So,
126

127
00:10:12,550 --> 00:10:19,150
in that case let's create a structure variable here. Let's change this prototype.
127

128
00:10:19,150 --> 00:10:26,200
So, here what happens is, the member elements of this structure variable will be copied into the member
128

129
00:10:26,200 --> 00:10:28,520
elements of this structure variable.
129

130
00:10:29,020 --> 00:10:38,100
Then here we have to use dot (.) operator remember, we cannot use arrow (->) operator here . We have to use dot operator.
130

131
00:10:39,370 --> 00:10:43,000
So, with that note I would like to end this video and I will see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 10

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:00,660
Welcome back.
1

2
00:00:00,660 --> 00:00:06,350
So, now let's do one exercise. The exercise is very simple.
2

3
00:00:06,560 --> 00:00:14,310
Write a program to decode a given 32 bit packet information and print the values of different fields.
3

4
00:00:15,220 --> 00:00:21,510
So, you'll be given with the 32 bit packet and you have to decode the packet according to this packet format.
4

5
00:00:22,270 --> 00:00:31,900
So, the packet has a multiple fields here, like CRC field whose size is 2 bits and status, status is 1
5

6
00:00:31,900 --> 00:00:32,160
bit,
6

7
00:00:32,170 --> 00:00:41,200
Payload is 12 bit, a battery status is 3 bits, sensor field is 3 bits, like that . A packet information
7

8
00:00:41,200 --> 00:00:48,880
will be given to you and you have to extract these bit fields and you have to store in the corresponding
8

9
00:00:49,150 --> 00:00:51,850
member elements in the structure.
9

10
00:00:51,850 --> 00:00:58,570
For example, you have to extract the CRC field and you have to store in the member element crc of this
10

11
00:00:58,720 --> 00:00:59,410
structure.
11

12
00:01:00,220 --> 00:01:06,520
So, basically you have to create a structure with member elements as packet fields as shown below.
12

13
00:01:06,520 --> 00:01:09,640
Let me show you how the output should look like.
13

14
00:01:09,820 --> 00:01:15,650
So, when you execute the program it should ask for the 32 bit a packet value.
14

15
00:01:15,790 --> 00:01:23,590
Let me enter 32 bit packet values some random 32 bit number, let's say FFFFABCD.
15

16
00:01:24,490 --> 00:01:32,320
So, you have to enter the packet value in hex. And when I hit enter, it should print the values of different
16

17
00:01:32,320 --> 00:01:36,700
fields like CRC field, status field, payload field, etc..
17

18
00:01:36,970 --> 00:01:44,740
So, you have to use bitwise operators here. And bit extraction techniques in order to extract the bits,
18

19
00:01:45,430 --> 00:01:51,860
and then you have to store them in appropriate member elements of the structure, and then you have
19

20
00:01:51,860 --> 00:01:53,620
to print the member elements.
20

21
00:01:54,460 --> 00:01:59,020
So, try this exercise and we will code this in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 11

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:06,900
So, now let's code this exercise packet field extraction program. So, first here
1

2
00:00:06,910 --> 00:00:14,290
I have created a variable of uint32, and then I am actually getting that value from the user using
2

3
00:00:14,290 --> 00:00:15,100
scanf,
3

4
00:00:15,670 --> 00:00:25,120
and after that I have created this structure. So, where CRC is actually 2 bits, but I have to use 
4

5
00:00:25,120 --> 00:00:30,570
uint8 variable type. So, that is sufficient to store two bits.
5

6
00:00:30,610 --> 00:00:35,410
So, later we can see how we can optimize the bits using the bit fields,
6

7
00:00:36,280 --> 00:00:44,060
but before that let's try this. And here the status is stored in uint8, payload is of 12 bits,
7

8
00:00:44,110 --> 00:00:53,050
so I have selected  uint16 for that, like that I have used the data types. And after that, I have created
8

9
00:00:53,080 --> 00:01:04,510
a structure variable packet. Let's start decoding. First let's calculate what is CRC. So, the CRC is the
9

10
00:01:04,510 --> 00:01:06,210
first 2 bits.
10

11
00:01:06,370 --> 00:01:08,480
So, what shall we do here?
11

12
00:01:08,500 --> 00:01:15,010
So, crc = packetValue & 0x3.
12

13
00:01:15,940 --> 00:01:19,280
So, I'm interested in only the last two bits.
13

14
00:01:20,050 --> 00:01:24,090
So, that's why, I'm zeroing out all the other bits.
14

15
00:01:24,220 --> 00:01:32,830
And after that let's type cast this to uint8, that's it. And after that,
15

16
00:01:32,830 --> 00:01:46,120
now let's try for status. Status is 1bit, but it comes at this position, actually it comes at 0 1 2
16

17
00:01:46,180 --> 00:01:47,920
second position.
17

18
00:01:47,920 --> 00:01:50,500
So, how do you extract that?
18

19
00:01:50,500 --> 00:01:57,830
So, for that what you have to do is, let's take the packet value and right shift by 2.
19

20
00:01:57,880 --> 00:02:02,800
So, now that bit comes to the least significant bit position,
20

21
00:02:03,220 --> 00:02:09,610
and after that lets & 0x1. That's it.
21

22
00:02:10,010 --> 00:02:19,170
And after that let's typecast this, so I'm using another parentheses here in order to store this calculation.
22

23
00:02:19,500 --> 00:02:25,030
For this you have to typecast. That looks good.
23

24
00:02:26,410 --> 00:02:41,010
And next is packet payload. Payload is equal to so payload starts from 3, so you have to shift by three
24

25
00:02:41,010 --> 00:02:42,230
now.
25

26
00:02:42,260 --> 00:02:50,800
Same thing you have to use here this is shift by 3, you have to extract now the first 12 bits.
26

27
00:02:51,180 --> 00:02:54,800
So, to extract 12 bits, what should be the mask value?
27

28
00:02:54,800 --> 00:02:56,180
12 once.
28

29
00:02:56,180 --> 00:02:58,280
So, what is 12 once?
29

30
00:02:58,280 --> 00:03:08,750
F is 4 once, another F 8 once, another F  12 once. So, FFF. So, here the payload is of type  
30

31
00:03:08,750 --> 00:03:12,870
uint16. So, I have to use uint16 here.
31

32
00:03:13,060 --> 00:03:15,810
The next one is BAT value.
32

33
00:03:15,910 --> 00:03:19,810
So, now you have to shift at the right hand side, and the shift amount. 
33

34
00:03:19,840 --> 00:03:23,040
You can calculate by adding these bit widths.
34

35
00:03:23,270 --> 00:03:29,640
So, 12 plus 13 plus 2, 15. So, here it is 15,
35

36
00:03:29,980 --> 00:03:31,930
here it should be 18,
36

37
00:03:31,960 --> 00:03:33,340
like that.
37

38
00:03:33,340 --> 00:03:34,240
This should be 15,
38

39
00:03:34,240 --> 00:03:35,070
this should be 18,
39

40
00:03:35,080 --> 00:03:36,610
let's do that.
40

41
00:03:36,640 --> 00:03:41,910
So, now let me calculate here, so these are uint8
41

42
00:03:42,470 --> 00:03:46,270
and here it should be 15,
42

43
00:03:47,350 --> 00:03:51,480
and you have to extract the first three bits.
43

44
00:03:51,790 --> 00:03:52,920
So, 7.
44

45
00:03:53,290 --> 00:03:58,620
So, here as well it is 7. And this is 18,
45

46
00:03:58,640 --> 00:04:02,460
so this is BAT,
46

47
00:04:02,480 --> 00:04:06,590
this is sense. After that,
47

48
00:04:06,820 --> 00:04:08,920
So, in order to extract the long address,
48

49
00:04:08,940 --> 00:04:16,920
so you have to shift this value by 21, and in order to extract the short address you have to shift by  
49

50
00:04:16,920 --> 00:04:17,700
29,
50

51
00:04:18,040 --> 00:04:24,180
and in order to address the address mode you have to shift by 31.
51

52
00:04:24,190 --> 00:04:26,270
So, let's do that.
52

53
00:04:26,300 --> 00:04:33,800
So, now the next one is 21 and the width is 8 bits.
53

54
00:04:34,130 --> 00:04:37,610
So, that's why, I'm using FF as a mask value,
54

55
00:04:37,730 --> 00:04:48,170
so this is long address. And after that, lets calculate for short address, so you have to shift by  
55

56
00:04:48,170 --> 00:04:53,030
29 and short address is of 2 bits.
56

57
00:04:53,050 --> 00:05:00,940
So, that's why, I would use three here. So, after that to get the address mode packet values should be shifted
57

58
00:05:00,960 --> 00:05:03,140
by 31.
58

59
00:05:03,350 --> 00:05:04,510
That's it.
59

60
00:05:04,620 --> 00:05:11,050
So, since we are inputting the number in hex format, for the scanf please use %X.
60

61
00:05:11,670 --> 00:05:17,640
So, now let's build this project and let's run this program.
61

62
00:05:17,640 --> 00:05:18,630
So, let me enter
62

63
00:05:18,690 --> 00:05:23,220
let's say 03FF1234.
63

64
00:05:24,060 --> 00:05:26,980
So, this is a 32 bit packet value.
64

65
00:05:27,070 --> 00:05:29,420
Let's run this.
65

66
00:05:29,490 --> 00:05:33,330
So, these are the values of different fields.
66

67
00:05:33,330 --> 00:05:36,420
So, let's print them in hex.
67

68
00:05:36,420 --> 00:05:41,730
Actually, it is getting printed in hex but the 0 cross symbol is missing.
68

69
00:05:41,730 --> 00:05:43,080
So, let's fix that.
69

70
00:05:43,830 --> 00:05:49,470
So, in between %X you can give # symbol here.
70

71
00:05:49,800 --> 00:05:55,050
That would add that to zero cross symbol for the hex value.
71

72
00:05:55,080 --> 00:05:57,540
Let's try eeee.
72

73
00:05:57,960 --> 00:06:02,850
So, these are the results, so we can verify that by calculating manually.
73

74
00:06:03,780 --> 00:06:08,850
And now let's print, what exactly the size of this structure.
74

75
00:06:08,850 --> 00:06:19,370
So, let's print the size of the structure, printf sizeof struct is % I64u\n
75

76
00:06:19,370 --> 00:06:31,930
sizeof, what is that? packet. So, if I calculate this manually it would be around 1 2 4 5 6 7 8 9
76

77
00:06:31,930 --> 00:06:34,910
or 10. I think it may be 9 or 10.
77

78
00:06:34,960 --> 00:06:35,950
Let's see.
78

79
00:06:36,040 --> 00:06:40,790
So, it is actually 10 bytes. So, it consumes 10 bytes in a memory.
79

80
00:06:41,590 --> 00:06:46,970
So, basically the packet value what you entered was, a 4 byte value,
80

81
00:06:47,080 --> 00:06:56,050
32bits. And to store that in a structured format using various member elements you actually lost
81

82
00:06:56,680 --> 00:06:58,960
6 bytes, isn't it?
82

83
00:06:58,960 --> 00:07:01,620
It is taking 10 bytes for this structure.
83

84
00:07:02,350 --> 00:07:09,550
So, now we have to find out is there any technique by which we can minimize this memory consumption. The answer
84

85
00:07:09,550 --> 00:07:12,750
for that is using bit fields with the structure,
85

86
00:07:12,880 --> 00:07:15,310
and that is our next discussion.
86

87
00:07:15,310 --> 00:07:21,100
So, in the next discussion we'll use a same structure, same member element, but we will reduce the size
87

88
00:07:21,100 --> 00:07:24,760
of the struct. And I will show you how to do that in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 12

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:07,650
So, in this lecture what we are going to do is, we are going to learn about structure bit fields. In the
1

2
00:00:07,650 --> 00:00:09,150
previous exercise
2

3
00:00:09,150 --> 00:00:11,870
we actually did one experiment where
3

4
00:00:11,910 --> 00:00:20,340
we extracted various fields of a packet and we stored the values into different member elements of
4

5
00:00:20,340 --> 00:00:21,800
a structure.
5

6
00:00:22,020 --> 00:00:29,070
So, we actually created this structure and you know this structure actually consumes around 10 bytes in the
6

7
00:00:29,100 --> 00:00:30,300
memory.
7

8
00:00:30,450 --> 00:00:33,870
So, that means we are actually losing 6 bytes.
8

9
00:00:34,740 --> 00:00:37,980
So, to store a 32 bit packet.
9

10
00:00:38,400 --> 00:00:41,160
So, we are actually taking 10 bytes.
10

11
00:00:41,400 --> 00:00:49,050
We can minimize this memory consumption using structure bit fields. Bit fields are exactly suitable
11

12
00:00:49,050 --> 00:00:51,530
for these kinds of applications
12

13
00:00:51,530 --> 00:00:58,990
where you extract some bit fields of a packet and you store that value into a variable.
13

14
00:00:59,000 --> 00:01:07,010
So, now let's understand how to use bit fields and we are going to understand this with an example. Let's consider
14

15
00:01:07,040 --> 00:01:09,580
the same example of our packet.
15

16
00:01:09,590 --> 00:01:16,740
And here we have a packet of 32bits and here the CRC is of just 2bits.
16

17
00:01:16,820 --> 00:01:27,100
So, we extracted the CRC and stored in a variable of type U8 and CRC is equal to the extracted value.
17

18
00:01:28,130 --> 00:01:32,130
So, here we are actually losing 6 bits.
18

19
00:01:32,160 --> 00:01:34,370
So because, only two bits are being utilized.
19

20
00:01:35,300 --> 00:01:38,780
So, now how to prevent this? To prevent this
20

21
00:01:38,780 --> 00:01:43,160
we have to rewrite our structure using bit fields.
21

22
00:01:43,160 --> 00:01:51,110
So, before using bit fields in a structure first you must select the appropriate data type for the member
22

23
00:01:51,110 --> 00:01:55,180
elements. So, that is a very important step.
23

24
00:01:55,180 --> 00:02:04,020
Select a proper data type, whether you are going to use U8, U16, U32 whatever.
24

25
00:02:04,960 --> 00:02:06,720
So, you have to select that first.
25

26
00:02:07,360 --> 00:02:11,560
So, that selection depends upon what you are trying to do. Here,
26

27
00:02:11,560 --> 00:02:19,420
I have a packet of 32 bits  and I just want to extract various fields into variables.
27

28
00:02:19,420 --> 00:02:28,120
So, that's why, what I do is, I start with a data type U32 and then I create a variable let's
28

29
00:02:28,120 --> 00:02:40,320
say CRC. But I will instruct the compiler to use just 2 bits of this U32, that is a 32 bit memory.
29

30
00:02:40,600 --> 00:02:43,470
So, that's why, what I do is, I write like this.
30

31
00:02:43,480 --> 00:02:47,870
So, I give a colon here and then I write 2.
31

32
00:02:48,100 --> 00:02:58,940
So, there will be a 32 bits of memory, so this is memory of 32 bits and inside this only two bits
32

33
00:02:58,940 --> 00:03:06,680
will be given to the variable CRC. And after that comes the status.
33

34
00:03:06,680 --> 00:03:14,860
Again I continue with the same data type U32 and I write create another variable and colon 1.
34

35
00:03:15,920 --> 00:03:19,890
So, the next 1 bit will given to the variable status.
35

36
00:03:20,540 --> 00:03:27,930
That means now you can reference these two bits using the variable names CRC, this one bit
36

37
00:03:27,990 --> 00:03:32,670
you can identify with the variable status now. And after that I continue.
37

38
00:03:32,700 --> 00:03:39,960
So, I again take U32 data type, don't change the data type okay, until you consume all
38

39
00:03:39,960 --> 00:03:41,750
the remaining bits.
39

40
00:03:41,760 --> 00:03:50,560
Now I take payload, I create a variable payload and colon 12, and next continue U32
40

41
00:03:51,020 --> 00:03:55,600
Bat colon 3, like that.
41

42
00:03:55,600 --> 00:04:03,610
So, like that you are going to consume all the 32 bits and you have already asked the compiler to access
42

43
00:04:03,610 --> 00:04:07,960
those bit fields using a dedicated  variable name.
43

44
00:04:08,200 --> 00:04:14,860
So, if you do this, you can see that both your requirements are fulfilled. The first requirement was to
44

45
00:04:15,070 --> 00:04:23,680
store different bit fields in a separate variables and our second requirement is to minimize the memory consumption.
45

46
00:04:23,680 --> 00:04:26,740
Let's try this using our program.
46

47
00:04:26,800 --> 00:04:33,790
So, here I have created a new project structPacketBitfield and I have the same old project
47

48
00:04:33,790 --> 00:04:40,000
the previous one. And this was the structure we created, here we have to change this data type,
48

49
00:04:40,870 --> 00:04:48,230
so everywhere I have to write uint32_t. So, let's do that first.
49

50
00:04:48,240 --> 00:04:56,760
Now you tell the compiler to use only 2 bits for the CRC, 1 bit for the status, 12 bits for the
50

51
00:04:56,760 --> 00:05:05,400
payload, 3 bits for the bat, 3 bits for the sensor, 8 bits for the long address, 2 bits
51

52
00:05:05,400 --> 00:05:13,210
for the short address, and 1 bit for the address mode. So, if you want to make this in order, you can
52

53
00:05:13,210 --> 00:05:16,910
do that. So, you can also write like this.
53

54
00:05:17,050 --> 00:05:19,330
This looks nice.
54

55
00:05:23,040 --> 00:05:27,170
So, here is a output of our old program and the new program.
55

56
00:05:28,020 --> 00:05:33,180
So, this is a program where we didn't use any bit fields. Output looks same
56

57
00:05:33,180 --> 00:05:40,350
you can see that, so but our old program actually consume 10 bytes. So, this is output with a bit field
57

58
00:05:40,410 --> 00:05:42,070
modification 
58

59
00:05:42,150 --> 00:05:50,520
and here you can see that the structure size is just 4 bytes. So, now we come to know that by using bit
59

60
00:05:50,520 --> 00:05:55,740
fields we actually reduced the memory consumption of a structure.
60

61
00:05:55,740 --> 00:06:03,360
So, whenever you find  codes which are related to networking activities, then you often find these
61

62
00:06:03,420 --> 00:06:04,750
bit field structures.
62

63
00:06:05,730 --> 00:06:12,670
So, these are heavily used when you deal with networking activities in a code. And also you should note
63

64
00:06:12,750 --> 00:06:18,830
one point that you know the order of these member elements doesn't matter actually.
64

65
00:06:18,990 --> 00:06:25,200
So, I can even cut this CRC and put at the end, I can even put this address mode at the beginning,
65

66
00:06:25,200 --> 00:06:32,610
so this actually doesn't matter. Because, each member element will anyway going to consume only that many
66

67
00:06:32,610 --> 00:06:34,370
amount of bits.
67

68
00:06:34,380 --> 00:06:38,380
So, but the order in which they appear in the memory may change.
68

69
00:06:39,180 --> 00:06:43,940
So, that's why, it is not recommended actually to change the order.
69

70
00:06:44,100 --> 00:06:51,300
So, I actually wrote this order to referring to my packet information. So, most of the time you will be
70

71
00:06:51,480 --> 00:06:58,020
implementing bit fields like that by referring to something. So, don't change the order of the member
71

72
00:06:58,020 --> 00:06:58,550
elements.
72

73
00:06:59,460 --> 00:07:02,850
So, even if you change the order of the member elements the output will come
73

74
00:07:02,850 --> 00:07:10,770
same for this application. But that will cause a problem when you send that structure value as a 32 bit
74

75
00:07:10,980 --> 00:07:15,300
value to some other function or to some other module.
75

76
00:07:15,300 --> 00:07:17,860
In that case you may face issues.
76

77
00:07:18,210 --> 00:07:21,430
So, that's about bit fields and I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 13

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:04,460
So, now let's discuss one more use case of bit fields. Let's say here
1

2
00:00:04,470 --> 00:00:06,620
there is the problem statement.
2

3
00:00:06,780 --> 00:00:13,620
Create a structure named "CarDetails" which comprises   of below information.
3

4
00:00:13,680 --> 00:00:23,040
Now the structure should include cars speed, weight, color, price. And we also know the maximum values of
4

5
00:00:23,040 --> 00:00:25,020
these information.
5

6
00:00:25,020 --> 00:00:35,180
For example, car speed maximum value could be 400, for the weight maximum value could be 500 kilograms,
6

7
00:00:35,220 --> 00:00:37,570
color is just a ASCII code,
7

8
00:00:37,860 --> 00:00:44,670
maximum price could be a 100 million dollars. To create a structure of these information
8

9
00:00:44,670 --> 00:00:50,400
you can create a structure something like this.You where you use U16 for the speed, U16
9

10
00:00:50,400 --> 00:00:56,100
for the weight, and something like this. And you select U32 for the price.
10

11
00:00:56,940 --> 00:01:00,830
So, this structure would consume 12 bytes of memory.
11

12
00:01:00,830 --> 00:01:07,410
Since you already know the maximum values of the member elements you can also use a structure with
12

13
00:01:07,410 --> 00:01:14,610
bit fields. Here to store the value 400 you know maximum you would need around 7 bits.
13

14
00:01:14,670 --> 00:01:19,020
So, to store the value 5000 maximum you would need 13 bits.
14

15
00:01:19,770 --> 00:01:25,500
So, if you know all this information, then you can go with writing a structure with bit fields where we
15

16
00:01:25,500 --> 00:01:32,480
can write something like this. Here you can see that these three variables actually consume 27 bits.
16

17
00:01:33,330 --> 00:01:42,150
So, 27 bits of 32 bit. So, 5 is remaining. So, in the remaining 5 bits you cannot accommodate
17

18
00:01:42,540 --> 00:01:45,330
the 28 bits of information.
18

19
00:01:45,330 --> 00:01:49,740
So, that's why, you can split the 28 bits of information into 23 and 5.
19

20
00:01:50,550 --> 00:01:51,730
So, this is a price
20

21
00:01:51,750 --> 00:01:52,700
lower bits
21

22
00:01:52,980 --> 00:01:55,370
and this is a price higher bits.
22

23
00:01:55,860 --> 00:02:02,770
It would take 4 + 4 = 8bytes, or you can also rewrite this in one single statement using
23

24
00:02:02,770 --> 00:02:04,250
uint8_t
24

25
00:02:04,300 --> 00:02:09,370
price. So, this will also lead to say memory consumption.
25

26
00:02:09,460 --> 00:02:15,340
So, I hope you understood about the bit fields and you should do more exercises on this in order to
26

27
00:02:15,340 --> 00:02:22,600
understand how to use this in your code, and in the next lecture onwards let's discuss about unions.
27

28
00:02:22,650 --> 00:02:23,620
I'll see you in the next lecture.


==================================================================================


section 26---unions


video 1


0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:04,070
So, in this lecture let's understand about unions.
1

2
00:00:04,170 --> 00:00:11,730
A union in 'C' is similar to a structure except that all of its members start at the same location in
2

3
00:00:11,730 --> 00:00:21,230
memory. A union variable can represent the value of only one of its members at a time.
3

4
00:00:21,250 --> 00:00:30,310
Let's understand this. Let's say you have a structure called address and you have 2 member elements.
4

5
00:00:30,380 --> 00:00:36,310
Let's say,  U16 short address and  U32 long address.
5

6
00:00:36,350 --> 00:00:40,350
So, how does this structure variable will get saved in the memory?
6

7
00:00:40,370 --> 00:00:42,950
So, this is how it is stored.
7

8
00:00:42,950 --> 00:00:48,510
Let's say these are addresses. First this member element will be stored,
8

9
00:00:49,190 --> 00:00:55,770
so it needs 2 bytes and after that there will be a padding.
9

10
00:00:55,780 --> 00:00:56,950
So, this is short,
10

11
00:00:57,070 --> 00:00:59,120
then there will be padding.
11

12
00:00:59,200 --> 00:01:00,790
So, this is padding
12

13
00:01:00,790 --> 00:01:03,720
and after that long address will be stored here.
13

14
00:01:04,030 --> 00:01:06,910
So, it totally takes 8 bytes.
14

15
00:01:06,910 --> 00:01:09,340
But look at the Union,
15

16
00:01:09,340 --> 00:01:11,200
this is how you create the union,
16

17
00:01:11,200 --> 00:01:14,280
just replace the struct by the keyword union
17

18
00:01:14,290 --> 00:01:16,120
that's all you have to do.
18

19
00:01:16,140 --> 00:01:19,830
So, now look at the memory allocation for the union.
19

20
00:01:19,890 --> 00:01:24,010
So here, just 4 bytes being used, isn't it?
20

21
00:01:24,030 --> 00:01:30,390
So, first thing what you have to note in a union is, the memory consumed by the union is equal to the
21

22
00:01:30,390 --> 00:01:34,830
size of a its biggest member element.
22

23
00:01:34,830 --> 00:01:40,860
So here, long address is the biggest member element and its size is 4.
23

24
00:01:40,950 --> 00:01:46,290
So, that is the reason this union consumes 4 bytes in memory.
24

25
00:01:46,290 --> 00:01:48,460
So then, what is the use of this?
25

26
00:01:48,780 --> 00:01:54,660
So, you have to use the union when the member element access is mutually exclusive,
26

27
00:01:54,660 --> 00:01:57,720
only in that case you have to use union.
27

28
00:01:57,720 --> 00:02:04,650
For example, let's say you are sending one packet from transmitter to receiver. So, you either send the
28

29
00:02:04,650 --> 00:02:09,090
packet using long address or short address.
29

30
00:02:09,090 --> 00:02:11,910
So, there is a mutual exclusion.
30

31
00:02:11,910 --> 00:02:14,160
You don't send the packet using both
31

32
00:02:14,220 --> 00:02:18,810
you know long address and short address. You use either one.
32

33
00:02:18,870 --> 00:02:23,070
So, in that case instead of creating a struct you can create a union.
33

34
00:02:23,070 --> 00:02:30,300
So, here during the operation either this field will be utilized or this field will be utilized.
34

35
00:02:30,300 --> 00:02:34,200
So, that's why, this consumes 8 bytes and this consumes 4 bytes.
35

36
00:02:34,620 --> 00:02:39,180
So, that is a difference between union and a structure.
36

37
00:02:39,180 --> 00:02:45,040
So, now let's understand the behavior of union by writing one simple program.
37

38
00:02:45,060 --> 00:02:49,930
So, here I have a union definition called union address.
38

39
00:02:50,010 --> 00:02:56,190
It has two member elements here, and as I said you create the union exactly the same way
39

40
00:02:56,190 --> 00:03:03,740
how you create a structure. So, only thing is you have to use the reserved keyword union here.
40

41
00:03:04,020 --> 00:03:06,640
And after that let's create union variable,
41

42
00:03:07,320 --> 00:03:15,840
let's call this as addr. And after that, what I do is, I dereference the addr variable to set the
42

43
00:03:15,840 --> 00:03:24,950
value for let's say short address. Let's say, I program this with ABCD and after that addr
43

44
00:03:25,070 --> 00:03:26,780
long address is equal to 0
44

45
00:03:26,780 --> 00:03:27,720
cross may
45

46
00:03:27,760 --> 00:03:31,620
call this as EEEECCCC.
46

47
00:03:31,640 --> 00:03:36,240
So, now let's print the values of these member elements using printf statements.
47

48
00:03:37,040 --> 00:03:45,680
So, now when you execute this program you actually find this result. Short address = CCCC and
48

49
00:03:45,680 --> 00:03:49,960
long address = 0xEEEECCCC.
49

50
00:03:50,790 --> 00:03:53,010
So, now how do you analyze this?
50

51
00:03:53,270 --> 00:03:58,290
So, when you first store the value ABCD into short variable this is what happened.
51

52
00:03:58,530 --> 00:04:06,690
So, 0xABCD. CD is stored here and AB is stored here. And after that you are going to store the
52

53
00:04:06,690 --> 00:04:07,290
value
53

54
00:04:07,290 --> 00:04:15,270
EEEECCCC into longAddr. So, this value will be overwritten now.
54

55
00:04:15,570 --> 00:04:22,050
So, here what happens is, it will be overwritten with CC, this will be overwritten with CC, so because the format
55

56
00:04:22,050 --> 00:04:23,920
is little is endian,
56

57
00:04:23,940 --> 00:04:32,110
that's why, I am writing in reverse. And this will be EE and this will be EE. So, that's why, after the second
57

58
00:04:32,110 --> 00:04:37,420
initialization this is how the memory looks like. After that in printf
58

59
00:04:37,450 --> 00:04:43,060
you are actually reading this member element. So, that's why, it is returning
59

60
00:04:43,060 --> 00:04:52,960
the first two bytes, that is CC. And after that you are again reading this longAddr, so it is returning
60

61
00:04:52,990 --> 00:04:54,880
this whole thing.
61

62
00:04:54,880 --> 00:04:56,770
So, that's why, the output looks like this.
62

63
00:04:57,580 --> 00:05:01,540
So, the key takeaway from this lecture is very simple,
63

64
00:05:01,690 --> 00:05:07,800
this initialization is overwritten in the memory by this initialization.
64

65
00:05:07,990 --> 00:05:13,570
So, that's why, you should be careful while using the union variables.
65

66
00:05:13,570 --> 00:05:19,380
So, use the union variable when the access to the member elements are mutually exclusive.
66

67
00:05:20,270 --> 00:05:25,480
So, I hope you understood something about union and in the next lecture
67

68
00:05:25,510 --> 00:05:29,530
let's understand applicability of union in embedded system code.
68

69
00:05:29,620 --> 00:05:30,820
