I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,150 --> 00:00:05,260
In this lecture, lets understand applicability of unions in Embedded system code.
1

2
00:00:05,310 --> 00:00:09,270
Unions are actually used for two main reasons.
2

3
00:00:09,540 --> 00:00:12,350
One is for bit extraction. 
3

4
00:00:12,510 --> 00:00:19,450
So, I will show you that in a moment. So, you can also do bit extraction using unions.
4

5
00:00:19,890 --> 00:00:25,950
And second one is for storing mutually exclusive data thus saving some memory.
5

6
00:00:25,950 --> 00:00:32,250
We'll understand how you can use the combination of union and structure to extract bit fields in a given
6

7
00:00:32,250 --> 00:00:34,380
data. For that
7

8
00:00:34,380 --> 00:00:37,090
let me take you to the project.
8

9
00:00:37,260 --> 00:00:44,010
So, here I have created a small project unionBitExtraction and I have the same code which we wrote
9

10
00:00:44,040 --> 00:00:44,520
earlier.
10

11
00:00:45,570 --> 00:00:52,380
So, this is a same code in which we are actually extracting various fields of a packet.
11

12
00:00:52,410 --> 00:01:01,350
So, here I have a struct with bit fields and then we are actually taking the 32bit packet value, and
12

13
00:01:01,350 --> 00:01:08,820
after that we are actually using bitwise operations in order to extract various fields of a packet,
13

14
00:01:09,750 --> 00:01:14,760
and then we are saving into various member elements of the packet.
14

15
00:01:14,760 --> 00:01:20,940
There is one method to skip all these things. So, all these bitwise operations.
15

16
00:01:20,940 --> 00:01:27,570
The technique is to use the combination of union and a struct.
16

17
00:01:27,780 --> 00:01:29,520
So, I will show you that.
17

18
00:01:29,520 --> 00:01:37,710
So, now what I'm going to do first is, first I'm converting this into a union. So, lets you remove struct
18

19
00:01:37,710 --> 00:01:39,930
and introduce union.
19

20
00:01:39,930 --> 00:01:45,810
After that what I'm going to do is, I'm going to keep all these things inside a struct.
20

21
00:01:45,810 --> 00:01:51,390
So, basically what I'm doing is, I'm creating a struct inside a union.
21

22
00:01:51,780 --> 00:01:57,320
So, this is how you create a struct and I'm going to create the variable directly,
22

23
00:01:58,170 --> 00:02:00,750
so need not to give any tag name here,
23

24
00:02:01,170 --> 00:02:02,620
I can do this.
24

25
00:02:02,910 --> 00:02:09,150
For example, let me create a variable of this structure let me call it as a packetFields.
25

26
00:02:09,150 --> 00:02:17,070
So, this method is actually discouraged when you use structure outside of something, but it is okay, if
26

27
00:02:17,070 --> 00:02:24,870
you are using inside of a structure or inside of a union. So, when you are using nested structure or nested
27

28
00:02:24,870 --> 00:02:29,090
unions you can create the variable directly like this.
28

29
00:02:29,100 --> 00:02:30,300
This is perfectly fine,
29

30
00:02:30,300 --> 00:02:32,130
you can do that.
30

31
00:02:32,190 --> 00:02:41,160
So, here I just created one member element packetFields of this struct type. And after that let me create
31

32
00:02:41,160 --> 00:02:50,080
one more member element of type U32 and I call it as packetValue.
32

33
00:02:50,130 --> 00:02:54,280
So, now just observe this union.
33

34
00:02:54,370 --> 00:02:55,130
So, what do you think?
34

35
00:02:55,130 --> 00:02:58,680
So, how much memory will be allocated to this union?
35

36
00:02:59,290 --> 00:03:02,980
So, it will be size of the biggest member element.
36

37
00:03:02,980 --> 00:03:04,550
This also consumes 4 bytes,
37

38
00:03:04,570 --> 00:03:07,280
so this also consumes 4 bytes.
38

39
00:03:07,510 --> 00:03:10,600
So, size of this union will be 4 bytes.
39

40
00:03:10,600 --> 00:03:16,740
So, if you save the packet value here, don't you think when you read these member elements automatically
40

41
00:03:16,750 --> 00:03:24,010
they return the contents of the memory according to there bit positions in the memory, right?
41

42
00:03:24,010 --> 00:03:30,340
Because in union all member elements will be referring to the same memory area,
42

43
00:03:30,340 --> 00:03:32,800
so in this case it is of 4 bytes.
43

44
00:03:32,800 --> 00:03:34,310
So, just think about it.
44

45
00:03:35,550 --> 00:03:39,750
So, what I'm going to do now is, I'm going to get rid of this variable packetValue,
45

46
00:03:39,750 --> 00:03:44,850
I'm reading the 32 bit packet value directly into this packet value.
46

47
00:03:44,850 --> 00:03:51,810
So, for that, I'm just going to create one variable of this union. Let me create one variable let me call
47

48
00:03:51,810 --> 00:04:01,320
packet, and I have to use here address of packet dot (.) operator you have to use of packet value. And now I can
48

49
00:04:01,320 --> 00:04:04,620
get rid of all these things, not required.
49

50
00:04:10,150 --> 00:04:14,260
And then just print the value of these member elements.
50

51
00:04:14,260 --> 00:04:15,910
How do you access these member elements?
51

52
00:04:15,910 --> 00:04:21,270
You have to start with a union, then go inside the structure, and then refer to these member elements.
52

53
00:04:23,410 --> 00:04:26,020
So, that's why, packet dot
53

54
00:04:26,290 --> 00:04:30,390
struct dot CRC.
54

55
00:04:30,590 --> 00:04:36,130
Let me use that to all the member elements.
55

56
00:04:36,270 --> 00:04:37,910
That looks good.
56

57
00:04:37,980 --> 00:04:44,500
So, you just get rid of all those a bit manipulations. So, by using the combination of union and struct you know
57

58
00:04:44,790 --> 00:04:50,540
you  automatically saved the bit fields into the required member elements of the structure.
58

59
00:04:50,550 --> 00:04:55,060
So, try this at your desk and please compare the output of this with the previous output,
59

60
00:04:55,200 --> 00:04:58,120
and just check whether you get the same result or not.
60

61
00:04:58,650 --> 00:04:59,940
And I'll see you in the next lecture.


==================================================================================


section 27---Usage of bit-fields in embedded code

video 1

0
1
00:00:00,120 --> 00:00:00,810
Hey, welcome back.
1

2
00:00:00,810 --> 00:00:07,190
So, in the previous couple of videos we actually explored about the structures and the bit fields.
2

3
00:00:07,230 --> 00:00:08,130
Isn't it?
3

4
00:00:08,280 --> 00:00:16,430
And we also did one exercise of packet extraction exercise using structures and bit fields, isn't it?
4

5
00:00:16,440 --> 00:00:23,820
Let's do some more examples on bit fields, and let's see how to use structures and bit fields with
5

6
00:00:23,820 --> 00:00:24,930
our embedded code.
6

7
00:00:25,770 --> 00:00:32,660
So, in the earlier videos we also wrote one program for turning on the LED, turning off the LED, LED
7

8
00:00:32,660 --> 00:00:33,280
toggle.
8

9
00:00:34,170 --> 00:00:36,110
So, now let's take one exercise.
9

10
00:00:36,150 --> 00:00:38,150
Let's take LED toggle exercise.
10

11
00:00:38,940 --> 00:00:45,620
And this exercise we are going to modify using structures and bit fields.
11

12
00:00:45,690 --> 00:00:52,170
So, now why it is required? So,what is the need to introduce structures and bit fields? Structures
12

13
00:00:52,170 --> 00:00:58,380
and bit fields are used in embedded code heavily to bring abstraction.
13

14
00:00:58,380 --> 00:01:02,310
So, for example, consider this code statement.
14

15
00:01:02,400 --> 00:01:09,390
So here, what you are trying to do? You are trying to change the bit position, that is the 3rd bit position
15

16
00:01:09,540 --> 00:01:12,650
of this peripheral register, right?
16

17
00:01:12,660 --> 00:01:19,430
So, this happens to be the address of the RCC AHB1ENR register.
17

18
00:01:19,710 --> 00:01:22,440
Now the question is, how come you know this value?
18

19
00:01:22,440 --> 00:01:29,020
That is how come you know you have to change the third bit position of that register.
19

20
00:01:29,190 --> 00:01:36,320
This information you actually derived from the datasheet or the reference manual of the microcontroller.
20

21
00:01:36,390 --> 00:01:41,520
So, that means in order to code this you should have a reference manual, isn't it?
21

22
00:01:41,580 --> 00:01:47,700
Let's say you are writing some middleware or driver for the user.
22

23
00:01:47,700 --> 00:01:54,090
So, let's say you are the developer and you have to give some code, or interfaces, or a middleware stack,
23

24
00:01:54,360 --> 00:01:57,930
something like that to your customer or to your user.
24

25
00:01:58,200 --> 00:02:04,440
And let's say, he is going to write an application for the LED  based on your driver, or middleware, or
25

26
00:02:04,440 --> 00:02:06,720
interface, whatever you call it.
26

27
00:02:06,840 --> 00:02:13,530
You cannot tell the user to go and explore the datasheet or user manual of the microcontroller to
27

28
00:02:13,530 --> 00:02:17,740
write some simple application like turning on the LED, right?
28

29
00:02:17,760 --> 00:02:23,190
So, that's why, you have to abstract the information all these information what you are seeing here. Like
29

30
00:02:23,190 --> 00:02:31,950
changing the 24th  bit position, changing the third position of this register, changing some other bit positions
30

31
00:02:32,010 --> 00:02:33,840
of some other peripheral register.
31

32
00:02:33,840 --> 00:02:42,840
So, all this information now you have to abstract from the user. So, you give a very simple interface for
32

33
00:02:42,840 --> 00:02:50,610
the user, so that he can write the applications quickly. So, that's all about abstraction, isn't it?  So,
33

34
00:02:50,610 --> 00:02:56,520
that's a reason why we use structures and bit fields to bring abstraction. So, that we are going to
34

35
00:02:56,610 --> 00:02:59,450
learn from this lecture onwards.
35

36
00:02:59,490 --> 00:03:04,890
So, very interesting lectures I don't think you should miss these lectures. In the previous exercise
36

37
00:03:04,950 --> 00:03:13,160
we actually created a structure and bit fields to extract various bit positions of this packet
37

38
00:03:13,200 --> 00:03:20,200
and later we store those different values into different member elements of the structure, isn't it?
38

39
00:03:20,250 --> 00:03:28,840
Now you have to create a similar structure with bit fields, but in this case for the peripheral register.
39

40
00:03:28,920 --> 00:03:37,560
So, can you convert this register information into a structure with bit fields? This diagram is similar
40

41
00:03:37,560 --> 00:03:39,460
to this diagram, isn't it?
41

42
00:03:39,600 --> 00:03:41,820
It also has various positions.
42

43
00:03:41,850 --> 00:03:48,410
For example, the bit position 0 is to enable the GPIOA peripheral,
43

44
00:03:48,620 --> 00:03:57,530
I mean the clock for GPIOA peripheral.The bit position 8 is for enable the clock for the GPIOE peripheral.
44

45
00:03:58,100 --> 00:04:06,640
So, the bit position let's say 22 is to enable the clock for the DMA2 peripheral and here there are some
45

46
00:04:06,640 --> 00:04:08,460
bits are reserved.
46

47
00:04:08,590 --> 00:04:17,160
So, now I want you to create a structure with bit fields to encode all these information of a peripheral
47

48
00:04:17,170 --> 00:04:22,840
register. So, this you have to do right after this video, and I will show you an example.
48

49
00:04:23,620 --> 00:04:29,680
So, you have to create a typedef structure for a peripheral register or non typedef structure,
49

50
00:04:29,680 --> 00:04:35,740
it doesn't matter actually. You can go with typedef structure if you want. Here,
50

51
00:04:35,760 --> 00:04:39,890
this is the name of the type of structure of RCC_AHB1ENR
51

52
00:04:39,900 --> 00:04:44,520
register. And please notice how I have written this.
52

53
00:04:44,700 --> 00:04:50,830
So, I've used all capital letters to write this name, we usually use capital letters to write typedef
53

54
00:04:50,830 --> 00:04:57,020
structure name, so you can use that method or you can use lowercase
54

55
00:04:57,240 --> 00:05:00,020
doesn't matter actually. Take a look here,
55

56
00:05:00,070 --> 00:05:02,240
this is terminated with underscore t
56

57
00:05:02,270 --> 00:05:03,280
which is not mandatory
57

58
00:05:03,280 --> 00:05:12,220
remember that. So, this just signifies that this is a typedef name. And after that, I have written this
58

59
00:05:12,220 --> 00:05:20,650
name a peripheral name followed by its associated register name. RCC is one of the internal peripheral of the
59

60
00:05:20,650 --> 00:05:27,010
microcontroller and AHB1ENR is it's a associated register. The same name
60

61
00:05:27,010 --> 00:05:28,810
you can also find on the reference manual.
61

62
00:05:29,740 --> 00:05:35,420
So, now you have to create a different bit field structures for different peripheral registers remember.
62

63
00:05:35,460 --> 00:05:46,390
So, like RCC_AHB1ENR, GPIOx_MODE_t, GPIOx_ODR_t, which is for
63

64
00:05:46,390 --> 00:05:50,740
output data register, mode register, and like that.
64

65
00:05:50,740 --> 00:05:57,940
So, create different bit field structures. What should be the member elements of these structures?
65

66
00:05:58,070 --> 00:06:01,820
Again you have to take a look into the peripheral register information.
66

67
00:06:02,090 --> 00:06:08,560
So, these are the member elements, isn't it? including all these reserved everything. So, the first member
67

68
00:06:08,560 --> 00:06:14,110
element name would be I would select this one.  Use the same name
68

69
00:06:14,110 --> 00:06:18,910
what you are seeing here, you need not to think about selecting the member elements
69

70
00:06:18,920 --> 00:06:25,820
name. So, don't think too much, so use whatever there in this register information.
70

71
00:06:26,400 --> 00:06:30,840
But for the member elements while writing member elements don't use capital letters
71

72
00:06:30,870 --> 00:06:31,100
Okay,
72

73
00:06:31,110 --> 00:06:32,720
you can use small letters.
73

74
00:06:32,790 --> 00:06:34,110
Let me show you one example.
74

75
00:06:34,170 --> 00:06:42,240
Okay, so from there onwards you can continue and I have just created a new project led_toggle_bitfields.
75

76
00:06:43,110 --> 00:06:47,220
So, don't modify this so just create one new project.
76

77
00:06:47,310 --> 00:06:51,690
So, what you have to do now is, you have to create one header file,
77

78
00:06:51,730 --> 00:06:58,330
because let's keep all these typedef structure definitions in a header file.
78

79
00:06:58,500 --> 00:07:04,890
Otherwise main.c will become too much difficult to read. So, we'll keep all these information in the
79

80
00:07:04,890 --> 00:07:06,030
header file.
80

81
00:07:06,390 --> 00:07:17,300
That makes sense, isn't it?  So, let's create one header file and here let me call it as main.h. And
81

82
00:07:17,300 --> 00:07:17,840
after that 
82

83
00:07:17,840 --> 00:07:21,050
now let's go to the main.h, so
83

84
00:07:21,130 --> 00:07:23,910
these are called as include guards.
84

85
00:07:23,910 --> 00:07:32,210
So, between these include guards you have to write, whatever you want to write. First let's create typedef
85

86
00:07:32,210 --> 00:07:43,380
struct for the RCC_AHB1ENR. Since we are going to access these typedef structures from the main.c
86

87
00:07:43,710 --> 00:07:51,980
it is better to include the main.h. So, just observe that so main.h is  a user defined header
87

88
00:07:51,980 --> 00:07:57,250
file. So, that's why, you have to mention that inside the double quotes.
88

89
00:07:57,260 --> 00:07:59,540
So, this is not a standard library header file.
89

90
00:07:59,750 --> 00:08:07,010
Let's start writing the member elements. For that you have to open up the reference manual and go to the
90

91
00:08:07,010 --> 00:08:13,380
respective peripheral register. So, in this case it is AHB1ENR, isn't it?
91

92
00:08:14,770 --> 00:08:15,850
which is right here.
92

93
00:08:16,720 --> 00:08:24,130
The first member element is GPIOAEN. So, you have to first select the data type, what data type you
93

94
00:08:24,130 --> 00:08:29,840
are going to use? So, that you have to fix first. What is the date type?
94

95
00:08:30,220 --> 00:08:31,410
That depends on
95

96
00:08:31,480 --> 00:08:38,560
again as I said previously what you are trying to do. So, here we are trying to encode the information
96

97
00:08:38,680 --> 00:08:48,120
of a  32 bit memory area. So, the size of this register is actually 32 bits. And on that  32 bit
97

98
00:08:48,290 --> 00:08:49,620
memory area
98

99
00:08:49,790 --> 00:08:55,000
we are going to encode all these informations using bit fields, isn't it?
99

100
00:08:55,910 --> 00:09:01,960
So, that's a reason it makes sense to use uint32_t here as a data type.
100

101
00:09:02,040 --> 00:09:09,520
What is a first member element? gpioen colon 1 and semicolon.
101

102
00:09:09,690 --> 00:09:14,640
Because, it consumes one bit position. Similarly,
102

103
00:09:14,830 --> 00:09:16,100
this is a next one.
103

104
00:09:17,420 --> 00:09:23,310
Let me copy and paste couple of times.  GPIO
104

105
00:09:23,310 --> 00:09:30,920
sorry, this is GPIOA, this is B. It is also good if you give one underscore here.
105

106
00:09:30,930 --> 00:09:32,190
So, that also looks good.
106

107
00:09:33,060 --> 00:09:37,650
I would use that. So, don't hesitate to do something which makes sense.
107

108
00:09:39,380 --> 00:09:43,080
So, I coded all the way from GPIOA to GPIOI
108

109
00:09:43,100 --> 00:09:50,730
for these  bit positions. And after that 9, 10, and 11 are reserved.
109

110
00:09:50,750 --> 00:09:52,400
So, what you do?
110

111
00:09:52,400 --> 00:09:59,610
So, for that let's create a member element called reserved.
111

112
00:09:59,700 --> 00:10:01,680
So, what is a width of the bit field?
112

113
00:10:01,690 --> 00:10:05,060
colon 3. After that proceed with the next one,
113

114
00:10:06,070 --> 00:10:07,090
CRC enable.
114

115
00:10:07,630 --> 00:10:09,020
After that again reserved,
115

116
00:10:09,130 --> 00:10:14,010
after that all these bit positions everything you have to encode.
116

117
00:10:14,210 --> 00:10:19,820
I hope you can do that and I will see you in the next lecture. So, complete this and other structures.

1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:05,560
So, I just finished creating the typedef structure for the RCC  AHB1ENR.
1

2
00:00:05,970 --> 00:00:12,190
So, this completely encodes the  AHB1ENR register. And here, in this file
2

3
00:00:12,190 --> 00:00:19,540
also don't forget to include stdint.h. And also for the reserved member elements use different
3

4
00:00:19,630 --> 00:00:20,270
names.
4

5
00:00:21,000 --> 00:00:21,300
Here
5

6
00:00:21,300 --> 00:00:28,230
I have used reserved1,  reserved2, reserved3, like that. Because, two member elements cannot have the
6

7
00:00:28,230 --> 00:00:32,170
same name. So, that's why, you can do something like this.
7

8
00:00:33,080 --> 00:00:38,630
And after creating the typedef structure always try to compile your code, that is very much important.
8

9
00:00:39,550 --> 00:00:47,930
So, it will immediately report whether there is any errors in the structures or not. So similarly, create
9

10
00:00:47,930 --> 00:00:55,220
two more typedef structures. One for GPIO mode register and another one for GPIO output data register.
10

Hey, welcome back to the lecture.

2
00:00:01,620 --> 00:00:09,430
So, I just created typedef structures for GPIO_ODR register and also for the GPIO_MODE 
2

3
00:00:09,430 --> 00:00:17,210
register.Here I'm using the name GPIOx because, the same structure I can use with any GPIO peripheral
3

4
00:00:17,220 --> 00:00:18,250
right?
4

5
00:00:18,390 --> 00:00:22,710
It need not be  GPIOD, or GPIOA, or B.
5

6
00:00:22,910 --> 00:00:31,830
So, this is a generic structure. Because, it can be used with mode to register of any GPIO peripheral. And 
6

7
00:00:31,850 --> 00:00:34,880
these are the member elements, pin_0.
7

8
00:00:34,910 --> 00:00:39,680
So, here you can even use pin mode 0, or pin mode 1, like that.
8

9
00:00:40,400 --> 00:00:45,390
I just used the name pin_0, pin_1, like that.
9

10
00:00:45,860 --> 00:00:53,780
And for the ODR, you can see that only 15 bit positions are valid and the rest are reserved.
10

11
00:00:54,920 --> 00:00:58,190
So, that's the reason 16 bit positions are reserved.
11

00:00:59,000 --> 00:01:03,610
Let's try to compile this code, compilation should be fine.

So, I hope you have completed up to here,

so you did some things similar to this. If you have not yet completed, then please complete and I will

see you in the next lecture. In the next lecture,

00:01:27,990 --> 00:01:29,340
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 4

0
1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:01,750
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,770 --> 00:00:07,760
So, in the previous lecture we created different typedef structures, right? Let's understand what to
2

3
00:00:07,760 --> 00:00:11,230
do with those typedef structures.
3

4
00:00:11,230 --> 00:00:13,060
Our goal is very clear.
4

5
00:00:13,060 --> 00:00:18,650
Our goal is to configure the peripheral registers of a peripheral.
5

6
00:00:18,820 --> 00:00:22,600
And in this case our peripheral is GPIOD.
6

7
00:00:22,630 --> 00:00:23,920
Correct?
7

8
00:00:24,040 --> 00:00:30,220
And we also know that we configure a peripheral register using its address.
8

9
00:00:30,220 --> 00:00:37,340
So, now what you have to do is, create a pointer variable of this typedef structure.
9

10
00:00:37,430 --> 00:00:47,020
For example, let's consider the typedef struct which we created for GPIO_MODE register. Create a pointer
10

11
00:00:47,030 --> 00:00:55,950
variable, and then initialize that pointer variable with the address of GPIOD_MODE register.
11

12
00:00:55,970 --> 00:00:58,370
So, this is a initialization.
12

13
00:00:58,370 --> 00:01:02,160
So, this pointer variable is now properly initialized
13

14
00:01:02,600 --> 00:01:05,240
and this is a registrar address.
14

15
00:01:05,240 --> 00:01:13,410
Now it is very simple. Just dereference the pointer variable and select the appropriate member elements.
15

16
00:01:13,580 --> 00:01:20,190
For example, let's say I want to configure the mode value for the pin_0,
16

17
00:01:20,270 --> 00:01:22,380
then I would dereference
17

18
00:01:22,400 --> 00:01:23,410
the pointer variable.
18

19
00:01:23,420 --> 00:01:29,000
Select the appropriate member element and just store mode value.
19

20
00:01:29,000 --> 00:01:37,190
So, if you do this the first two bits of this memory location will be programmed, isn't it? So, which happens
20

21
00:01:37,190 --> 00:01:45,370
to be the place of pin_0. So, pin_0 will be refereeing to the first two bit positions,
21

22
00:01:45,770 --> 00:01:54,280
pin_1 will be refereeing to the next 2 bit positions, like that. Isn't it? So, all you need to do is initialize
22

23
00:01:54,280 --> 00:01:55,720
this pointer variable,
23

24
00:01:55,720 --> 00:02:02,960
select the appropriate member element, and store the value. That's all you have to do. So, if you want to configure
24

25
00:02:02,960 --> 00:02:09,810
the mode for the pin_1, then just select the member element pin_1 and store your value. That's it.
25

26
00:02:11,030 --> 00:02:19,070
Like that, if you want to configure the 15th pin, then select the member element pin_15 and configure
26

27
00:02:19,070 --> 00:02:21,740
the value, whatever the mode value you want to configure.
27

28
00:02:22,610 --> 00:02:30,600
So, now let's analyze this a statement. Let's see how exactly this statement works. Internally
28

29
00:02:30,800 --> 00:02:38,960
compiler converts this statement to this statement. So, compiler will generate the instructions to program
29

30
00:02:38,990 --> 00:02:42,800
the appropriate bit positions in the peripheral registrar address.
30

31
00:02:43,340 --> 00:02:51,140
So, previously we actually used bitwise operators to program the appropriate bit positions of the peripheral
31

32
00:02:51,150 --> 00:02:56,430
register, right? With this method you just shifted that work to the compiler.
32

33
00:02:57,390 --> 00:03:05,460
That's a only difference. Compiler will use various bit manipulation instructions to do something like
33

34
00:03:05,460 --> 00:03:12,310
this. The bit manipulation is still required, but you just shifted that work to compiler
34

35
00:03:12,320 --> 00:03:17,020
now. Here you can see that compiler does something similar
35

36
00:03:17,170 --> 00:03:25,260
what you did before in the earlier LED_on exercise. So, that's how you actually configure various bit
36

37
00:03:25,320 --> 00:03:31,260
positions  of a peripheral register using structures and bit fields.
37

38
00:03:31,260 --> 00:03:39,350
Let's go back to our exercise led_toggle_bit fields, and now what I want you to do is, go back to your old
38

39
00:03:39,350 --> 00:03:48,660
project led_toggle. Open the main.c, and now you have to replace all these codes where you are configuring
39

40
00:03:48,660 --> 00:03:50,610
the peripheral register.
40

41
00:03:50,610 --> 00:03:56,890
You have to replace all these codes with structure pointer access which I just explained in the previous
41

42
00:03:56,890 --> 00:04:00,430
slide, this as well  and this as well.
42

43
00:04:01,410 --> 00:04:04,560
So, I will give you some hints and based on that you can do that.
43

44
00:04:05,460 --> 00:04:11,120
So, first let's follow the same method. In our earlier project what we use to do.
44

45
00:04:11,460 --> 00:04:13,420
We first use to do this, right?
45

46
00:04:13,420 --> 00:04:22,840
Enable the clock. So, let's copy this code, this is not required, let's remove that. Let's create a pointer
46

47
00:04:22,840 --> 00:04:32,200
variable for the RCC_AHB1ENR structure. So, that is there in the main.h. Let's copy this structure
47

48
00:04:32,210 --> 00:04:42,200
name, go back to main.c and create a pointer variable pClkCtrl and is equalt to
48

49
00:04:42,300 --> 00:04:46,310
so you have to initialize  this with the address.
49

50
00:04:46,680 --> 00:04:49,560
Let's go back to our earlier project.
50

51
00:04:49,560 --> 00:04:54,040
So, this was the address, let's copy all these things.
51

52
00:04:54,090 --> 00:04:59,930
Let's paste somewhere here and lets comment it out. This was the old code.
52

53
00:05:00,310 --> 00:05:03,820
So, you have to initialize with this address.
53

54
00:05:03,970 --> 00:05:07,320
You have to properly typecast.
54

55
00:05:07,530 --> 00:05:09,840
So, how do you typecast? RCC 
55

56
00:05:09,870 --> 00:05:12,170
AHB1ENR_t *.
56

57
00:05:12,230 --> 00:05:14,670
I will just change the name of this, I will
57

58
00:05:15,030 --> 00:05:16,890
use the same mode.
58

59
00:05:17,010 --> 00:05:19,020
So, now let me write reg here.
59

60
00:05:19,920 --> 00:05:22,880
So, you just initialized this pointer variable
60

61
00:05:22,910 --> 00:05:32,030
isn't it?  So, now just use this pointer and dereference that and select the appropriate member element.
61

62
00:05:32,080 --> 00:05:35,280
So, in this case it is GPIOD is equal to
62

63
00:05:35,320 --> 00:05:36,270
we have to enable it.
63

64
00:05:36,280 --> 00:05:38,850
So, make it 1, that's it.
64

65
00:05:38,950 --> 00:05:46,690
So, here you see you actually implemented some abstraction. The user need not to think which bit position
65

66
00:05:46,810 --> 00:05:52,660
he has to touch in order to enable the clock for the GPIOD peripheral in the AHB1
66

67
00:05:52,650 --> 00:05:59,740
ENR. So, that is abstracted now. So, still he has to think whether he should mention 1 or 0
67

68
00:05:59,740 --> 00:06:00,090
here.
68

69
00:06:00,850 --> 00:06:08,350
So, again you can give multiple abstraction. For this also you can give abstraction by giving enum's or
69

70
00:06:08,470 --> 00:06:12,510
by using some other technique. So, that will see later.
70

71
00:06:12,640 --> 00:06:16,210
So, in order to enable the clock, you have to make this as 1.
71

72
00:06:16,210 --> 00:06:23,880
So here, this is again a pointer variable. So, we can also safeguard this using const, i would use const
72

73
00:06:23,920 --> 00:06:32,400
here, which is optional but you can use that. And also this is a memory mapped register.
73

74
00:06:32,590 --> 00:06:39,110
So, from our volatile discussion use volatile generously while accessing the memory mapped registers.
74

75
00:06:39,880 --> 00:06:44,210
So, that's why, let's use volatile here. Volatile for the data
75

76
00:06:44,230 --> 00:06:45,120
remember.
76

77
00:06:45,640 --> 00:06:55,820
So, I would use volatile here. So, constant pointer programmer should not change the value of this pointer
77

78
00:06:55,820 --> 00:06:59,630
variable and volatile data.
78

79
00:06:59,660 --> 00:07:06,710
Let's go back to our old project and we just completed for this line, and you have to continue for other
79

80
00:07:06,710 --> 00:07:14,120
code statements, and modify all these things with structure pointer access, and I will see you in the next
80

81
00:07:14,120 --> 00:07:15,150
lecture.
81

82
00:07:15,350 --> 00:07:22,310
So, the behavior of the application should be same, you should see LED toggling. So, the behavior should
82

not be affected.

see that we are turning on the clock for the GPIOD

3
00:00:10,980 --> 00:00:13,960
and here we are configuring the mode,

you just have to write 1. And after that this is turning on the LED and turning off the LED.

5
00:00:23,400 --> 00:00:31,190
So, now this code looks very much simpler and easy to follow compare to the earlier version, isn't it?

6
00:00:32,070 --> 00:00:38,550
So, that's how you bring the abstraction. But still you can add more abstraction using enum's and other

7
00:00:38,550 --> 00:00:39,760
things.

8
00:00:39,780 --> 00:00:41,700
So, now let's debug this code.

And as I said the behavior of the application should be same,

that shouldn't be changed.

00:00:48,510 --> 00:00:57,380
So, let's observe the ODR register. Let me expand that, let's proceed

00:00:57,430 --> 00:01:03,760
and here we are turning on the LED and here you can see that the 12th bit is set.

That's correct

and LED is indeed turning on.

to post in course Q&A Forum. And in the next lecture,

==================================================================================


section 28--Keypad interfacing



video 1


0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,260
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,260 --> 00:00:09,330
So, now in this lecture let's learn about 4x4 matrix keypad interfacing. So, we are going
2

3
00:00:09,330 --> 00:00:15,990
to interface 4x4 keypad to our STM32 microcontroller and we are going to read the
3

4
00:00:15,990 --> 00:00:20,660
key pressed, and we are going to write the code entirely from scratch.
4

5
00:00:21,450 --> 00:00:28,830
If you want to reproduce this project you would need 1 4x4 matrix keypad and few jumper
5

6
00:00:28,830 --> 00:00:36,910
wires to do the connections. And you have to write a program which detects and prints the key pressed.
6

7
00:00:37,110 --> 00:00:44,010
Don't worry, if you don't have these components right now with you. So, you just learn the concept and
7

8
00:00:44,340 --> 00:00:50,090
write the application and then when you have those components you can always test it at your desk.
8

9
00:00:50,910 --> 00:00:56,010
So, even if you don't have these components I would suggest you to continue with these lectures.
9

10
00:00:56,100 --> 00:00:56,730
Let's begin.
10

11
00:00:57,650 --> 00:01:05,250
So, now this is a 4x4 matrix keypad or it is also called as a hex keypad. And it has
11

12
00:01:05,250 --> 00:01:08,700
got 8 pins in order to read the key pressed.
12

13
00:01:09,630 --> 00:01:17,670
And as I said you would need few jumper wires to connect this keypad to the STM32 microcontroller board.
13

14
00:01:18,630 --> 00:01:21,080
And out of eight pins,
14

15
00:01:21,240 --> 00:01:29,430
the first 4 pins are called as row pins. R1, R2, R3, R4, 4 rows. 
15

16
00:01:29,430 --> 00:01:31,330
So, these are row pins.
16

17
00:01:31,740 --> 00:01:39,150
And the next 4 pins are called as column pins. C1, C2, C3, C4.
17

18
00:01:39,160 --> 00:01:43,150
So, this is how keypads  internal connection looks like.
18

19
00:01:44,080 --> 00:01:46,490
So, there are four column lines,
19

20
00:01:46,600 --> 00:01:56,440
so sorry, this is C1 and there are 4 row lines. And in between the row and a column a key is implemented.
20

21
00:01:57,440 --> 00:02:05,630
And when no key is pressed on this keypad the columns and rows are completely isolated.
21

22
00:02:05,750 --> 00:02:08,450
So, there is no connection at all.
22

23
00:02:08,640 --> 00:02:12,660
Let's take an example of the first key and that is 1.
23

24
00:02:12,690 --> 00:02:15,550
So, this is how a key looks like.
24

25
00:02:15,630 --> 00:02:23,820
So, when you press the key what happens is,  row1 and column1 comes into contact, and that you should
25

26
00:02:23,820 --> 00:02:31,310
detect in your program, and then by some logic you have to decide which key is pressed.
26

27
00:02:31,380 --> 00:02:36,720
The only thing I want you to understand from this slide is, when the key is pressed there will be a contact
27

28
00:02:36,720 --> 00:02:46,140
between a row and a column. Please note that, the columns C1, C2, C3, C4 are input to the microcontroller.
28

29
00:02:47,160 --> 00:02:57,210
Microcontroller code reads C1, C2, C3, C4 to detect the key pressed. And the pins R1, 
29

30
00:02:57,210 --> 00:03:02,130
R2, R3, R4 are outputs to the microcontroller.
30

31
00:03:02,130 --> 00:03:06,600
So, columns are input, rows are output
31

32
00:03:06,600 --> 00:03:10,530
you should remember that. Now let's proceed.
32

33
00:03:10,530 --> 00:03:17,350
So, this is how all the keys are implemented inside the keypad. Let's move forward.
33

34
00:03:17,730 --> 00:03:22,490
Let's understand how can we connect this keypad to the microcontroller.
34

35
00:03:22,710 --> 00:03:28,660
So, you just need eight IO pins on the microcontroller to do this interfacing.
35

36
00:03:29,130 --> 00:03:37,790
So, you have to arrange for 8 free IOs on your board. So, you know how to get the free IO details.
36

37
00:03:37,830 --> 00:03:42,630
You have to go to the user manual of the board and you have to understand what are all the IOs are
37

38
00:03:42,630 --> 00:03:51,580
free, so that you can use them to interface with the keypad. So, in this case these are connected to PD8,
38

39
00:03:51,610 --> 00:03:59,970
PD9, PD10, PD11. So, your software should keep all these IO pins in input mode.
39

40
00:04:01,030 --> 00:04:06,610
And all the rows are actually connected to these 4 IO pins
40

41
00:04:06,670 --> 00:04:15,170
PD0, PD1, PD2, and PD3 and your software should keep all these IO pins in output mode.
41

42
00:04:15,300 --> 00:04:18,400
So, this is how you should do the connection.
42

43
00:04:18,720 --> 00:04:23,670
And we also need pull-up resistors for the columns,
43

44
00:04:23,670 --> 00:04:27,120
otherwise the interfacing will not be complete.
44

45
00:04:27,120 --> 00:04:30,630
So, don't worry you need not to connect any external resistors,
45

46
00:04:31,020 --> 00:04:37,380
we can make use of internal pull-up resistors which are available inside the microcontroller.
46

47
00:04:37,380 --> 00:04:44,040
All modern microcontroller they come with internal resistors, for the pull-up and pull-down functionalities
47

48
00:04:44,300 --> 00:04:49,910
for the IO pins. In the next lecture, let's understand why exactly the pull-up resistors are required.
48

49
00:04:50,820 --> 00:04:51,990
And I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:09,440
Let's say you have a microcontroller here MCU. Let's say there is a column,
1

2
00:00:09,500 --> 00:00:19,920
let me call it as C1, let's say there  is a row, so let me call it as R1. And let's say it is
2

3
00:00:19,920 --> 00:00:26,280
connected to the microcontroller and this is also connected to the microcontroller.
3

4
00:00:26,330 --> 00:00:31,670
So, this is output, this is input to the microcontroller.
4

5
00:00:31,730 --> 00:00:38,570
Let me call it as PD8 and let me call this as PD0.
5

6
00:00:38,600 --> 00:00:39,950
So, now here is a key
6

7
00:00:39,980 --> 00:00:42,050
so let me draw the keys.
7

8
00:00:42,200 --> 00:00:44,360
So, when the key is not pressed,
8

9
00:00:44,360 --> 00:00:46,770
so what is the state of C1?
9

10
00:00:46,850 --> 00:00:50,120
So, you cannot read the state of the C1 properly
10

11
00:00:50,120 --> 00:00:50,560
Why?
11

12
00:00:50,570 --> 00:00:55,590
Because, you can see that C1 is open circuit, isn't it?
12

13
00:00:55,700 --> 00:01:05,420
This is actually called as open circuit or the pin is floating.
So, when the pin is floating, it can 
13

14
00:01:05,450 --> 00:01:11,400
pick up random noise of the circuit and it can fluctuate between high and low.
14

15
00:01:11,870 --> 00:01:20,910
So, it can fluctuate like this. So, let's say this is low and let's say this is high. So, the voltage
15

16
00:01:20,930 --> 00:01:23,590
can fluctuate at that end.
16

17
00:01:23,840 --> 00:01:26,670
So, let's say this is voltage and this is time.
17

18
00:01:27,650 --> 00:01:33,800
So, that's a reason when you read a floating pin  sometimes it shows high and sometimes it shows low.
18

19
00:01:34,840 --> 00:01:42,120
So, that's why, whenever you are reading from a pin you should always avoid it's floating state.
19

20
00:01:42,280 --> 00:01:46,030
So, that's a reason why we should use pull-up resistors here.
20

21
00:01:46,060 --> 00:01:53,260
So, we have to avoid this initial state of a pin by giving a proper state.
21

22
00:01:53,260 --> 00:02:02,290
So, in this case we avoid the floating state of a pin by giving a pull-up resistor between VCC
22

23
00:02:02,560 --> 00:02:04,380
and the pin.
23

24
00:02:04,420 --> 00:02:07,440
So, this is called as the pull-up resistor.
24

25
00:02:07,510 --> 00:02:11,610
You can also give pull-down resistor between the pin and the ground point.
25

26
00:02:11,920 --> 00:02:18,760
But in this case I'm just connecting a resistor between a pin and let's say +VDD which is  
26

27
00:02:18,760 --> 00:02:27,200
3.3v. And this pull-up resistor value should be very high. Something like let's say 22K
27

28
00:02:27,200 --> 00:02:30,230
or something, or higher than that.
28

29
00:02:30,260 --> 00:02:38,330
So, now when the key is not pressed you can see that C1 has got the proper state, so that is +VDD.
29

30
00:02:38,360 --> 00:02:45,680
So, that's why, whenever you read this pin PD8 when the key is not pressed, you always read high, isn't
30

31
00:02:45,680 --> 00:02:45,870
it?
31

32
00:02:46,970 --> 00:02:51,380
So, that's how you avoided the floating state. When the key is pressed,
32

33
00:02:51,380 --> 00:02:55,550
What happens? So, there comes your logic.
33

34
00:02:55,600 --> 00:03:02,800
Now we understood that key not pressed is equal to high. So, when the key is not pressed,
34

35
00:03:03,040 --> 00:03:06,220
and when you read C1, it returns high.
35

36
00:03:06,400 --> 00:03:15,400
So, that means that when the key is pressed, you should make sure that C1 is grounded. So, key pressed is
36

37
00:03:15,400 --> 00:03:24,490
equal to low. And now you should make use of this output. So, your code has to make R1 grounded,
37

38
00:03:25,880 --> 00:03:28,070
because it is a output you can control R1, isn't it?
38

39
00:03:29,030 --> 00:03:36,050
So, make R1 low. So, when the key is pressed here, what happens here?
39

40
00:03:36,360 --> 00:03:43,130
C1 will be grounded and your input reads low.
40

41
00:03:43,160 --> 00:03:50,030
That's how you distinguish between key pressed and key not pressed, high and low. When the key is pressed,
41

42
00:03:50,030 --> 00:03:54,980
the equivalent circuit would be like this.
42

43
00:03:55,080 --> 00:04:03,810
This is C1 and this is grounded. So, this is 22K. So, this is a microcontroller
43

44
00:04:03,810 --> 00:04:13,290
and this actually reads 0. So, when you read C1, it returns 0. So because, this is actually shorted to
44

45
00:04:13,310 --> 00:04:22,480
ground. Isn't it? So, or the potential difference between the C1 and the ground is 0. So, this is a equivalent
45

46
00:04:22,480 --> 00:04:25,220
circuit, when the key is pressed.
46

47
00:04:25,340 --> 00:04:32,740
Let me conclude this so why we need pull-up resistor, so to avoid the floating state of an input pin.
47

48
00:04:33,670 --> 00:04:42,420
So, in the next lecture, let's understand how to write code to detect a different key press events.
48

49
00:04:42,430 --> 00:04:43,480
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:00,660
Welcome back.
1

2
00:00:00,660 --> 00:00:07,340
So, now in this lecture let's understand implementing code to detect different key press events.
2

3
00:00:07,350 --> 00:00:14,150
First of all you should remember that when no key is pressed, C1, C2, C3, C4, reads high.
3

4
00:00:14,650 --> 00:00:22,820
And after that, the key detecting logic is very simple. Scan for keys row by row one at a time.
4

5
00:00:22,920 --> 00:00:23,780
So, that's the logic.
5

6
00:00:24,630 --> 00:00:28,550
For that now you have to get the help of output pins.
6

7
00:00:28,560 --> 00:00:40,470
So, now here you can see that R2, R3, and R4 are made high and only R1 is made low. So, at this stage
7

8
00:00:40,890 --> 00:00:45,100
software can detect key pressed in R1 row only.
8

9
00:00:45,960 --> 00:00:54,060
So, for example, when key 2 is pressed, C2 will be pulled down to ground state, isn't it?
9

10
00:00:54,060 --> 00:01:04,610
So, that's why, C2 will read 0, and C1 is still 1, C3 is 1, C4 is 1, isn't it?
10

11
00:01:05,420 --> 00:01:08,810
So, that's how you detect which key is pressed.
11

12
00:01:08,990 --> 00:01:10,870
So, here C2 returns 0,
12

13
00:01:10,870 --> 00:01:18,070
hence the software should conclude that key 2 is pressed. So, when R1 is low,
13

14
00:01:18,120 --> 00:01:26,940
if C2 reads low, then conclude that key 2 is pressed. So, that's a logic very simple. And then proceed
14

15
00:01:26,970 --> 00:01:28,400
to the next row.
15

16
00:01:28,560 --> 00:01:33,870
Make this high, this high, and this high, and make R2 low.
16

17
00:01:33,930 --> 00:01:42,390
So, at this stage software can detect key pressed in R2 row only. For example, when key 6 is pressed,
17

18
00:01:42,750 --> 00:01:47,040
C3 will be pulled to low and reads low.
18

19
00:01:47,040 --> 00:01:50,790
So, when you press this key, what happens here?
19

20
00:01:50,790 --> 00:01:59,640
C3 reads 0 and rest all will read 1. And hence you should conclude that key 6 is pressed,
20

21
00:02:00,920 --> 00:02:02,210
because R2 is low.
21

22
00:02:02,880 --> 00:02:06,420
So, after that you have to proceed to R3.
22

23
00:02:06,590 --> 00:02:13,950
So, make  R3 low and all the other rows high.And after that you can scan for the keys in the row
23

24
00:02:13,970 --> 00:02:21,530
R3. Like that you have to cover all the rows and you have to do this for every iteration of an infinite
24

25
00:02:21,590 --> 00:02:26,730
loop. So, you have to scan row by row in an infinite loop in the program.
25

26
00:02:27,590 --> 00:02:29,110
So, now let's proceed.
26

27
00:02:30,020 --> 00:02:33,310
So, now let's discuss about the implementation.
27

28
00:02:33,310 --> 00:02:37,630
So, for the implementation I have attached a flowchart  with this lecture,
28

29
00:02:37,640 --> 00:02:43,040
please, download that and you can go through it. And I will explain that in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 4

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,320
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:07,110
So, now let's explore the flowchart for detecting key press event in keypad.
2

3
00:00:07,350 --> 00:00:10,160
First two steps are initialization.
3

4
00:00:10,170 --> 00:00:18,000
First you have to keep all row GPIOs in output mode and keep all column GPIOs both in input mode.
4

5
00:00:19,050 --> 00:00:20,630
So, for this exercise
5

6
00:00:20,640 --> 00:00:26,280
I'll be considering PD8, PD9, PD10 , and PD11.
6

7
00:00:26,340 --> 00:00:31,440
So, these are column GPIOs and PD0 to PD3
7

8
00:00:31,440 --> 00:00:33,300
these row GPIOs.
8

9
00:00:34,050 --> 00:00:38,150
So, if you are using STM32 discovery board, then you can definitely use this
9

10
00:00:38,210 --> 00:00:45,270
IOs, because these are free. If you are using some other board, please investigate what are the IOs
10

11
00:00:45,330 --> 00:00:47,480
you can use with this exercise.
11

12
00:00:47,700 --> 00:00:55,170
You need 8 free IOs. So, you have to investigate in your board using user manual of your board.
12

13
00:00:56,610 --> 00:01:06,760
And after that, make all row GPIOs high state and after that the infinite loop starts. In the in finite
13

14
00:01:06,760 --> 00:01:07,180
loop,
14

15
00:01:07,210 --> 00:01:14,650
so first you have to make R1 low. So, R1=0, R2, R3, R4 should be maintained
15

16
00:01:14,650 --> 00:01:21,970
at high. And after that read C1, check whether C1 is low.
16

17
00:01:22,190 --> 00:01:31,410
If it is yes, then you should conclude that that is key 1. Just use printf statement to print the letter
17

18
00:01:31,500 --> 00:01:41,180
1. So, in between detecting and printing you give a 200 milliseconds of delay or 150 milliseconds of
18

19
00:01:41,180 --> 00:01:42,440
delay.
19

20
00:01:42,440 --> 00:01:49,900
So, this is for key debouncing. So, this much amount is required, otherwise software will continuously detect
20

21
00:01:50,080 --> 00:01:57,470
the pressed event and it may execute the printf couple of times. So, to understand why that  delay is
21

22
00:01:57,470 --> 00:01:58,530
required,
22

23
00:01:58,670 --> 00:02:02,420
that is to compensate for the button debouncing.
23

24
00:02:02,480 --> 00:02:04,950
So, what happens when you press a key?
24

25
00:02:05,090 --> 00:02:12,440
So, when you press a key, there will be a metal contact and that metal contact exists for a certain amount
25

26
00:02:12,440 --> 00:02:20,400
of time. Because, when user presses a key a user takes certain amount of time to release that. So, now
26

27
00:02:20,430 --> 00:02:27,810
let's assume one number, let's assume user takes 100 to 200 milliseconds to press and release
27

28
00:02:27,810 --> 00:02:28,250
the button.
28

29
00:02:29,330 --> 00:02:36,310
So, for that amount of time there will be a metal contact. There will be a bouncing pattern between high
29

30
00:02:36,310 --> 00:02:36,710
and low.
30

31
00:02:36,730 --> 00:02:40,820
Isn't it? So, that's what we call as button debouncing.
31

32
00:02:41,080 --> 00:02:49,740
So, when user presses a key, so the software detects that button is pressed. So, after that the software
32

33
00:02:49,740 --> 00:02:59,310
has to wait until it takes the next action. Let's say 150 milliseconds. So, for the next 150 milliseconds
33

34
00:02:59,650 --> 00:03:07,670
the software has to be in busy loop. So, busy loop you can implement by using a for loop. And once
34

35
00:03:07,670 --> 00:03:12,530
that delay is elapsed the program can take the next action.
35

36
00:03:12,530 --> 00:03:21,800
So, in this case program prints the detected key. So, that delay is used to compensate for the button debouncing.
36

37
00:03:21,910 --> 00:03:29,910
So, either you can implement 200 or 150 milliseconds of delay. So, use for loop to implement this delay.
37

38
00:03:30,820 --> 00:03:35,390
So, precise delay is not required, so you can just approximate.
38

39
00:03:35,650 --> 00:03:40,560
So, I teach you later how to approximate the delay using for loop.
39

40
00:03:40,840 --> 00:03:47,820
And after that, if C1 is not zero, then proceed to next. Read C2.
40

41
00:03:48,190 --> 00:03:55,780
If C2 is low, then conclude the key which is pressed is 2, and like that you have to cover all the columns.
41

42
00:03:56,650 --> 00:04:03,480
And after that you have to make R2 low. So, R1=1, R2=0,
42

43
00:04:03,490 --> 00:04:11,590
R3=1,  R4=1. And then again you have to scan all the columns, and you have
43

44
00:04:11,590 --> 00:04:20,370
to print the appropriate key. And after that you have to repeat the same logic for R3=0
44

45
00:04:20,760 --> 00:04:24,380
and repeat the same logic for R4=0.
45

46
00:04:25,560 --> 00:04:34,280
So, all these things should be in an infinite loop and that's how you scan the keypad for the key press event.
46

47
00:04:34,440 --> 00:04:38,710
So, now you have to implement this flowchart right after this video,
47

48
00:04:38,720 --> 00:04:40,420
I will give you some more hints.
48

49
00:04:40,470 --> 00:04:47,580
So, step 1 is find out free IOs available on your target board pin headers. That should be your first
49

50
00:04:47,580 --> 00:04:54,390
task. And after that decide which are the IO pins you are going to use to handle rows and columns
50

51
00:04:54,390 --> 00:05:03,010
of the keypad. And after that, Step 2 would be the initialization phase. In the initialization phase, create
51

52
00:05:03,070 --> 00:05:09,580
required pointer variables to handle memory-mapped registers, Initialize the pointer variables with
52

53
00:05:09,580 --> 00:05:15,590
appropriate memory-mapped register addresses, Make use of type qualifiers such as 'volatile'
53

54
00:05:15,760 --> 00:05:21,690
if memory-mapped register access is involved. And the step 3 would be initialization phase.
54

55
00:05:21,700 --> 00:05:32,920
Make all row IOs as output, make all column IOs as input, activate internal pull-up resistors for all
55

56
00:05:33,100 --> 00:05:34,980
column IOs.
56

57
00:05:35,350 --> 00:05:43,030
This is very important. And for this you have to refer to the pull-up and pull-down enable register in
57

58
00:05:43,030 --> 00:05:46,630
the reference manual of the microcontroller.
58

59
00:05:46,810 --> 00:05:54,280
Let me show you that. So, in the GPIO register section you will find a register called GPIO port pull
59

60
00:05:54,280 --> 00:06:00,640
up/pull- down register. Which is a very important register which is used to control, which is used to enable
60

61
00:06:00,640 --> 00:06:04,660
or disable the internal pull-up or pull-down resistors.
61

62
00:06:05,530 --> 00:06:13,870
So here, if you go to this register you can see that there are 4 configurations possible. 00 means
62

63
00:06:13,870 --> 00:06:20,630
no pull-up, no pull-down. 01 means pull-up, 10 means pull-down, and 11 is reserved.
63

64
00:06:21,400 --> 00:06:30,490
So, you must be using this configuration. 01 which activates the internal pull-up resistor for a given
64

65
00:06:30,490 --> 00:06:32,670
GPIO pin. Again
65

66
00:06:32,680 --> 00:06:34,390
if you explore this register,
66

67
00:06:34,480 --> 00:06:36,410
so this is for pin_0,
67

68
00:06:36,430 --> 00:06:37,920
this is for pin_1,
68

69
00:06:37,930 --> 00:06:41,020
this is for pin_2, like that.
69

70
00:06:41,020 --> 00:06:42,690
I hope you can do this and
70

71
00:06:42,850 --> 00:06:50,260
after that let's proceed and the step 4 would be implement the key detect logic as per the flow
71

72
00:06:50,270 --> 00:06:59,520
chart. And after that if you have keypad, then do the interfacing and test your application. For the  
72

73
00:06:59,520 --> 00:06:59,790
printf
73

74
00:06:59,820 --> 00:07:07,320
you can use the SWO based printf technique what we discussed in the earlier part of the course. And
74

75
00:07:07,320 --> 00:07:13,260
I would suggest you to use a bit manipulation technique to code this entire exercise.
75

76
00:07:13,440 --> 00:07:19,550
Don't use structures and bit fields. So, that is not required. That you can do later.
76

77
00:07:19,980 --> 00:07:25,820
But, I wanted to code this exercise using purely the address manipulation technique using  bitwise
77

78
00:07:25,830 --> 00:07:32,810
operators. And later you can always repeat the same exercise using structures and bit fields.
78

79
00:07:32,880 --> 00:07:36,300
I hope you can do that and in the next lecture I will code this exercise.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 5

0
1
00:00:00,060 --> 00:00:02,890
Let me code for the keypad exercise.
1

2
00:00:02,970 --> 00:00:07,920
So, here I have a new project the 011keypad,
2

3
00:00:07,920 --> 00:00:11,240
and here I have all peripheral register addresses definition.
3

4
00:00:12,150 --> 00:00:16,330
I have created couple of pointer variables and the new one is this one.
4

5
00:00:16,330 --> 00:00:17,240
Pointer for
5

6
00:00:17,280 --> 00:00:25,760
Pull up/pull down register. And since these are memory mapped register access so I have made data as volatile.
6

7
00:00:25,860 --> 00:00:30,280
The first step is enable the peripheral clock for GPIOD peripheral.
7

8
00:00:30,660 --> 00:00:32,640
So, let's do that.
8

9
00:00:32,640 --> 00:00:35,710
For that we have to referred to the clock register.
9

10
00:00:36,180 --> 00:00:40,580
* of clock control reg |= 1 left
10

11
00:00:40,640 --> 00:00:41,890
shift it by 3.
11

12
00:00:41,880 --> 00:00:53,760
Isn't it? And now let's proceed to next one. And the next one is configure PD0, PD1, PD2, PD3 as output.
12

13
00:00:54,840 --> 00:00:56,220
So, these are our rows.
13

14
00:00:56,220 --> 00:00:57,130
Isn't it?
14

15
00:00:57,130 --> 00:01:00,240
Now, let's refer to the mode register once again.
15

16
00:01:00,240 --> 00:01:03,330
So, here this is PD0, PD1,
16

17
00:01:03,330 --> 00:01:04,380
this is PD2,
17

18
00:01:04,380 --> 00:01:06,240
this is for PD3, isn't it?
18

19
00:01:07,170 --> 00:01:10,550
So, we have to keep 01, right?
19

20
00:01:10,560 --> 00:01:19,800
So, 01 here, 01 here, 01 here, and 01 here. So, this value turns out to be 0 cross
20

21
00:01:19,800 --> 00:01:21,530
this is actually 5 
21

22
00:01:21,690 --> 00:01:26,910
and this is 5, isn't it? So, we have to program 0x55.
22

23
00:01:27,470 --> 00:01:29,000
Let's do that.
23

24
00:01:29,000 --> 00:01:34,100
So, let me refer to the ModeReg. * of  ModeReg
24

25
00:01:34,250 --> 00:01:43,880
|= 0x55. So, before that you can also clear those fields. So, it's better if we clear
25

26
00:01:43,880 --> 00:01:45,900
those fields first.
26

27
00:01:45,950 --> 00:01:52,540
So, that would be &= ~(0xFF)
27

28
00:01:52,740 --> 00:01:56,980
So, this is clear, this is set.
28

29
00:01:57,930 --> 00:02:07,830
So, after that configure PD8, PD9, PD10, PD11 as inputs. For that you have to clear all those bits, isn't
29

30
00:02:07,830 --> 00:02:07,950
it?
30

31
00:02:08,880 --> 00:02:15,980
So, this is 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7, so 8 starts from here.
31

32
00:02:16,060 --> 00:02:19,520
Pin 8 9 10 11.
32

33
00:02:20,460 --> 00:02:26,910
So, all these should be zeros. So, that is actually the input mode.
33

34
00:02:27,040 --> 00:02:34,090
So, that means you have to clear 8 bits from 16th bit position onwards.
34

35
00:02:34,090 --> 00:02:35,770
Let's do that.
35

36
00:02:35,770 --> 00:02:43,330
So, for that what I would do is, let's take the ModeReg once again, star of ModeReg &=
36

37
00:02:43,330 --> 00:02:43,960
beacause,
37

38
00:02:44,080 --> 00:02:51,160
so in the mode register we are going to clear 8 consegative bit positions, right?  which appear 16th bit
38

39
00:02:51,340 --> 00:02:52,540
position onwards.
39

40
00:02:53,410 --> 00:03:05,190
So, that's why, let's use  ~(0xFF << 16).  Why 16?  Because the bit position
40

41
00:03:05,200 --> 00:03:12,300
for the 8th pin is at 16th, isn't it?  This is &= negation of
41

42
00:03:12,510 --> 00:03:20,290
I have to clear 8 bit positions. So, I would use 0xFF, but left shifted by 16.
42

43
00:03:20,320 --> 00:03:29,760
That make sense, isn't it? Next, enable internal pull-up resistors for PD8, PD9, PD10, and PD11.
43

44
00:03:30,360 --> 00:03:40,020
Let's refer to the pull-up and pull-down register and this is 8, 9, 10, 11, and it has to be pull
44

45
00:03:40,020 --> 00:03:40,710
up.
45

46
00:03:41,100 --> 00:03:48,460
So, I had to keep 01 here, 01 here, 01 here, 01 here. So, this happens to be 0
46

47
00:03:48,460 --> 00:04:00,820
cross 55, but shifted by 16. Let's do that. Let's start with pull-up/pull-down register and first
47

48
00:04:00,820 --> 00:04:11,450
let's clear those bit positions before setting. So, I can reuse this code. So, everything got clear and after
48

49
00:04:11,450 --> 00:04:17,070
that you have to set it. So, let's change this to |=
49

50
00:04:17,070 --> 00:04:23,660
0x55,   so remove this negation(~). That looks good.
50

51
00:04:24,220 --> 00:04:29,650
So, that completes the initialization phase. So, let's try to compile this code, 
51

52
00:04:30,540 --> 00:04:37,990
so there are couple of errors. So, I have to use #include stdint.h.
52

53
00:04:41,000 --> 00:04:46,340
Let's compile. So, there is a warning of unused variable.
53

54
00:04:46,360 --> 00:04:47,050
That's fine.
54

55
00:04:47,780 --> 00:04:54,400
So, we completed upto here, and in the next step is make all row GPIOs high state.
55

56
00:04:55,090 --> 00:05:00,000
So, for that, I am going to use ODR (output data register) which is right here.
56

57
00:05:00,100 --> 00:05:05,880
So, output data register |= 0x0f, isn't it?
57

58
00:05:05,950 --> 00:05:07,660
That's it.
58

59
00:05:07,660 --> 00:05:12,080
After that, make R1 low. Let's do that.
59

60
00:05:12,260 --> 00:05:15,830
The next step is make R1 low. So, R1
60

61
00:05:15,830 --> 00:05:20,700
in this case is PD0. Again lets make use of output data register
61

62
00:05:21,690 --> 00:05:32,340
we have to clear, right?  Low means clear. So, &= ~( 1 << 0) or you can
62

63
00:05:32,360 --> 00:05:33,210
simply write 1,
63

64
00:05:33,420 --> 00:05:38,110
no problem. So, that 0 has no effect. But it's OK
64

65
00:05:38,130 --> 00:05:39,430
you can write like this.
65

66
00:05:39,450 --> 00:05:40,160
No problem.
66

67
00:05:40,170 --> 00:05:41,040
That looks good.
67

68
00:05:41,910 --> 00:05:42,810
So, now
68

69
00:05:42,810 --> 00:05:45,090
after that read C1.
69

70
00:05:45,120 --> 00:05:46,790
Let's do that.
70

71
00:05:47,010 --> 00:05:49,170
Let's scan the columns.
71

72
00:05:49,170 --> 00:05:53,450
You have to check C1, that is PD8 low or high.
72

73
00:05:53,670 --> 00:05:56,750
For that, we have to use the input data register.
73

74
00:05:56,760 --> 00:05:59,150
So, now let's use the if condition.
74

75
00:05:59,370 --> 00:05:59,810
Let's
75

76
00:05:59,890 --> 00:06:05,310
dereference the input data register and we have to test now.
76

77
00:06:05,660 --> 00:06:11,130
Test whether the 8th bit position is high or low, isn't it?
77

78
00:06:11,150 --> 00:06:13,950
So, that corresponds to PD8.
78

79
00:06:14,000 --> 00:06:20,470
So, for that we have to use bitwise & 1 shifted by  8.
79

80
00:06:20,500 --> 00:06:26,000
Now, let's keep everything in another parentheses. So, here there is a parentheses
80

81

mismatch,

00:06:28,010 --> 00:06:29,980
so I have to give one more parentheses.

83
00:06:30,590 --> 00:06:32,520

84

very simple.

85

86

and I kept everything in another parentheses.

88
00:06:40,700 --> 00:06:48,980

88

89
00:06:48,980 --> 00:06:50,330
That is PD8 high,
89

90
00:06:50,330 --> 00:07:01,210
that means key is not pressed.  If this expression returns 0, then you can conclude that C1 is 0.
90

91
00:07:01,370 --> 00:07:06,120

91

92
00:07:06,290 --> 00:07:11,180

92

93
00:07:11,240 --> 00:07:17,120
So, if this expression is false, then this expression will be true, isn't it?
93

94
00:07:17,150 --> 00:07:24,410
So, then we can conclude key is pressed.Let me remove all these spaces
94

95
00:07:24,620 --> 00:07:25,990
that doesn't look good.
95

96

So, now let's print 1 here, because the key press detected is 1.
96

97
00:07:34,550 --> 00:07:37,090
Let's proceed to the next one.
97

98
00:07:37,130 --> 00:07:38,630
Check for C2.
98

99
00:07:38,900 --> 00:07:39,980
That is PD9.
99

100
00:07:40,790 --> 00:07:45,390
Let's use the same code, so change this to 9.
100

101
00:07:45,400 --> 00:07:46,930
That's it.
101

102
00:07:46,930 --> 00:07:48,810
And this would be 2, isn't it?
102

103
00:07:50,060 --> 00:08:00,880
And let's repeat the same code 10, and this is 3, and after that
103

104
00:08:01,060 --> 00:08:08,820
this would be 11. So, C1, C2, C3, and this is C4,
104

105
00:08:11,880 --> 00:08:20,000
and this is A. According to the flowchart we have to give small amount of delay, isn't it?  before
105

106

printing.

107

109
00:08:37,310 --> 00:08:46,850
top, so let's keep that empty, later we can implement that. That completes the key detection logic when

R1 is low, and you have to proceed to the next row now. Let me copy this code.

00:09:02,340 --> 00:09:11,190
So, here I have to give 1. But before that, make sure that all other rows are high. So, that is important.

113
00:09:12,060 --> 00:09:22,950
For that we can reuse this code, this one. So, first make all rows high, then make R2 low. And after
113

114

that you have to repeat the same logic. So, let's copy the same scan the column's logic.

again copy whole thing, let's

00:10:07,420 --> 00:10:16,370

1 2 3 A. So, it seems to be working, let's understand how much delay actually it introduces,

28
00:02:06,290 --> 00:02:11,690
let's calculate a rough value or an approximate value.
28

29
00:02:11,960 --> 00:02:22,530
So, let me keep the debugger here and let me run. So, let's do some instruction level analysis here, this
29

30
00:02:22,540 --> 00:02:30,010
is the assembly level implementation of this for loop. So, let's debug this with instruction level debugging.
30

31
00:02:30,010 --> 00:02:39,680
So, let me step over here and here you can see that this is the loop. So, it execute one instruction, 2,
31

32
00:02:39,700 --> 00:02:49,300
3, 4, 5, 6, 7. So, it executes 7 instructions for one iteration of a for loop, isn't it?
32

33
00:02:49,300 --> 00:02:54,400
So, now we can approximately calculate the delay introduced.
33

34
00:02:54,400 --> 00:03:06,720
So, the processor actually running with 16 megahertz of internal RC oscillator. So, the processor
34

35
00:03:06,790 --> 00:03:11,720
is actually executing the instructions and the frequency of the 16 megahertz.
35

36
00:03:11,880 --> 00:03:15,730
So, that's the speed of the processor in our STM32 board.
36

37
00:03:15,910 --> 00:03:25,650
Let's assume one instruction takes 1 clock cycle. So, that mean 1 instruction is equal to 
37

38
00:03:25,770 --> 00:03:29,680
0.0625 microseconds.
38

39
00:03:29,730 --> 00:03:31,750
So, this is just an assumption.
39

40
00:03:31,890 --> 00:03:36,820
So, we know that for one iteration of a for loop it takes 7 instructions.
40

41
00:03:36,900 --> 00:03:39,410
So, 7 instruction is equal to
41

42
00:03:39,450 --> 00:03:48,710
so this is multiplied by 7. So, which comes approximately let's say 0.5 microseconds. So,  
42

43
00:03:48,710 --> 00:03:49,870
0.43
43

44
00:03:49,880 --> 00:03:51,850
I would consider it as 0.5.
44

45
00:03:51,980 --> 00:04:00,350
That means, 0.5 microseconds is for 1 iteration of the for loop.
45

46
00:04:00,400 --> 00:04:09,710
So, how about 1000 microseconds? That means 1ms (1 milliseconds). It would be 2000 iterations.
46

47
00:04:11,550 --> 00:04:16,750
So, 2000 iterations of a for loop generates 1 millisecond of delay.
47

48
00:04:17,580 --> 00:04:28,960
So, that's why, for 150 millisecond how much?  150 x 2000 = 300K.
48

49
00:04:29,930 --> 00:04:32,970
So, that's why, I used this count in the for loop.
49

50
00:04:33,110 --> 00:04:38,090
So, that means this for loop will cause approximately 150 milliseconds of delay.

section 29---Arrays


video 1

0
1
00:00:00,060 --> 00:00:00,750
Hey, welcome back.
1

2
00:00:00,780 --> 00:00:05,600
So, from this lecture onwards let's understand about Arrays in 'C'.
2

3
00:00:05,880 --> 00:00:08,710
So, now let's understand why we need arrays.
3

4
00:00:08,850 --> 00:00:12,210
So, now let's understand one problem statement.
4

5
00:00:12,210 --> 00:00:19,580
Let's say you want to find out average age of the students of a class, and there are 100 students
5

6
00:00:19,700 --> 00:00:20,220
in a class.
6

7
00:00:20,250 --> 00:00:22,190
So, what would you do?
7

8
00:00:22,220 --> 00:00:24,560
So, how do you write the program?
8

9
00:00:24,560 --> 00:00:29,170
First of all the goal is to store the age of 100 students.
9

10
00:00:29,240 --> 00:00:33,960
So, would you create hundred different variables something like this.
10

11
00:00:34,160 --> 00:00:39,480
Now, if you create a 100 different variables and initialize them to different values then this would
11

12
00:00:39,480 --> 00:00:40,570
be a tedious process.
12

13
00:00:40,580 --> 00:00:41,830
Right?
13

14
00:00:41,950 --> 00:00:43,860
I mean of course you can do something like this,
14

15
00:00:43,870 --> 00:00:45,450
No problem.
15

16
00:00:45,490 --> 00:00:51,600
So, after that you can add individual variables and then you divide by the total number of students,
16

17
00:00:51,610 --> 00:00:54,260
then you would get the result, right?
17

18
00:00:54,320 --> 00:00:58,690
So but, creating such a huge number of variables will be a tedious process, so
18

19
00:00:58,700 --> 00:01:01,990
that's where arrays comes into picture.
19

20
00:01:02,010 --> 00:01:09,800
Now, here you can see that, all these variable definitions can be merged in one single statement.
20

21
00:01:10,080 --> 00:01:14,870
So, that is by using the concept of arrays.
21

22
00:01:14,910 --> 00:01:20,460
So, here this is what we call as an array definition or a declaration.
22

23
00:01:20,820 --> 00:01:27,570
So, an array in 'C' is a collection of data of the same data type.
23

24
00:01:28,620 --> 00:01:35,580
So, here you can see that this signifies collection of 100 different data items of the same type
24

25
00:01:35,940 --> 00:01:38,540
and the type is uint8,
25

26
00:01:39,030 --> 00:01:47,340
and this is the array name or a label given to those 100 different data items, 'a reference' you can
26

27
00:01:47,340 --> 00:01:48,480
call it as a reference.
27

28
00:01:48,480 --> 00:01:49,360
Okay.
28

29
00:01:49,710 --> 00:01:54,310
So, this is the first time you are actually encountering these square brackets, isn't it?
29

30
00:01:55,230 --> 00:02:00,520
So, using a parentheses or curly braces is completely wrong,
30

31
00:02:00,690 --> 00:02:08,170
you should not use something like this. And inside the square brackets, you have to mention the number
31

32
00:02:08,170 --> 00:02:10,780
of data items contained by that
32

33
00:02:10,790 --> 00:02:11,940
array.
33

34
00:02:12,140 --> 00:02:13,830
Now, let's move forward.
34

35
00:02:14,240 --> 00:02:16,760
So, understanding array syntax.
35

36
00:02:16,940 --> 00:02:20,520
So, now let's consider this array definition.
36

37
00:02:20,600 --> 00:02:30,620
So here, please note that 'studentsAge' is a base pointer or a reference to 100 data items of type 
37

38
00:02:30,630 --> 00:02:32,200
uint8.
38

39
00:02:32,240 --> 00:02:35,400
So, this is called as a reference.
39

40
00:02:35,420 --> 00:02:35,780
Okay.
40

41
00:02:35,810 --> 00:02:41,650
It's a reference or a base pointer to 100 different data items which are present in the memory.
41

42
00:02:42,530 --> 00:02:49,560
So, that's a reason data type of 'studentsAge' is uint8_t*
42

43
00:02:49,890 --> 00:02:50,210
Okay?
43

44
00:02:50,210 --> 00:02:53,060
not just uint8_t.
44

45
00:02:53,960 --> 00:03:00,970
So, you should note that. So, the data type of the items stored is uint8.
45

46
00:03:01,820 --> 00:03:04,520
So, now let's understand size of an array.
46

47
00:03:04,760 --> 00:03:08,820
So, total memory consumed by this array in memory,
47

48
00:03:08,930 --> 00:03:10,010
how do you calculate this?
48

49
00:03:10,070 --> 00:03:16,070
Very simple. Number of data items contained by that array multiplied by size of single data item, isn't
49

50
00:03:16,070 --> 00:03:16,530
it?
50

51
00:03:17,120 --> 00:03:20,070
So,  100 * 1 = 100 bytes, isn't it?
51

52
00:03:20,990 --> 00:03:28,090
So, you can even use the size of operator on this array name and it would give you the value 100.
52

53
00:03:29,160 --> 00:03:29,850
So here,
53

54
00:03:29,850 --> 00:03:37,640
there is a array definition, and you can use this array name with the size of operator.
54

55
00:03:37,860 --> 00:03:40,160
So, this would print 100, isn't it?
55

56
00:03:40,220 --> 00:03:50,680
Let's run. So, what if I do this as uint32_t, so a data item is of type uint32, then
56

57
00:03:50,680 --> 00:03:53,930
100 * 4,  isn't it?
57

58
00:03:53,950 --> 00:03:58,020
So, this would print 400. So, let's check.
58

59
00:04:00,710 --> 00:04:08,370
So, that's about size of an array. So, that means this array is going to consume 400 bytes of memory
59

60
00:04:08,790 --> 00:04:11,540
during runtime of the program.
60

61
00:04:11,550 --> 00:04:16,960
Let's move forward. So, let's understand arrays storage in memory.
61

62
00:04:17,160 --> 00:04:21,270
So, how exactly the array is stored in a memory? Now again
62

63
00:04:21,270 --> 00:04:28,310
let's consider the same array which has got 100 data items. And please note that, data items will
63

64
00:04:28,310 --> 00:04:32,660
be stored in contiguous memory locations in the memory.
64

65
00:04:32,870 --> 00:04:34,550
So, this is a very important point.
65

66
00:04:35,590 --> 00:04:43,090
All those 100 data items of this array will be stored in contiguous memory location. So, that means
66

67
00:04:43,120 --> 00:04:50,020
if you know the base address of that array, you can manipulate address of any data item in the memory.
67

68
00:04:50,890 --> 00:04:55,020
And this array name is actually a base pointer,
68

69
00:04:55,300 --> 00:04:57,910
it holds the base address of an array.
69

70
00:04:58,810 --> 00:05:06,730
So, that's a reason you can just try printing the value of this array name with a format 'p' specifier,
70

71
00:05:07,180 --> 00:05:09,970
you will get the base address of the array.
71

72
00:05:10,060 --> 00:05:11,930
Now, let's try that.
72

73
00:05:11,930 --> 00:05:18,190
Now here, what I'm going to do is, I'm going to add one more printf, so printf base address of the array
73

74
00:05:18,370 --> 00:05:21,600
is equal to %p\n
74

75
00:05:21,880 --> 00:05:28,120
and you just print this array name. So, you will get the base address of the array.
75

76
00:05:28,120 --> 00:05:29,470
Now, let's run this.
76

77
00:05:32,730 --> 00:05:33,280
And here it is.
77

78
00:05:33,280 --> 00:05:40,470
So, this is the base address. And also please note that, this base address also happens to be the address
78

79
00:05:40,560 --> 00:05:44,940
of the very first data item of the array, isn't it?
79

80
00:05:44,940 --> 00:05:46,850
So, if you go back to our slide,
80

81
00:05:47,130 --> 00:05:49,890
so this is a first data item, isn't it?
81

82
00:05:50,190 --> 00:05:55,430
And the base address also happens to be the address of the first data item in the array.
82

83
00:05:55,620 --> 00:05:57,640
So, you should remember that.
83

84
00:05:57,720 --> 00:06:01,480
So, the takeaway from this slide is very simple,
84

85
00:06:01,590 --> 00:06:06,210
remember that data items are stored in contiguous memory locations in the memory,
85

86
00:06:06,210 --> 00:06:13,560
there is a point number1, and the point number2 is this array name is actually a pointer which
86

87
00:06:13,560 --> 00:06:20,430
holds the base address of the array. And in the next lecture, let's understand how can we read and write
87

88
00:06:20,430 --> 00:06:21,950
to an array.
88

89
00:06:21,990 --> 00:06:23,040
I will see you in the next lecture.

So, now let's get into our IDE.

First of all let me remove all these things and let's create an array of 10 data items of type

So, uint8_t

10

00:00:54,650 --> 00:00:55,810
itself.
13

14
00:00:55,910 --> 00:01:03,670
So, what you have to do is, you have to give assignment operator you just have to write is equal to, now
14

15
00:01:03,670 --> 00:01:12,120
you have to use curly braces, and here you can initialize the individual data items.
15

16
00:01:12,130 --> 00:01:22,390
Now let's say, I do 0xff, 0xff, 0xff. So, here what I did was I just initialized the
16

17
00:01:22,390 --> 00:01:31,960
first 3 data items of this array to 0xff.  This is perfectly fine. So, you can do this, if I
17

18
00:01:31,960 --> 00:01:38,350
just build this project there are no errors. So, you can definitely partially initialize the array, no
18

19
00:01:38,350 --> 00:01:43,120
problem. But, what happens to remaining data items?
19

20
00:01:43,570 --> 00:01:50,210
Now, please note that the remaining data items will be automatically initialized to value zeros.
20

21
00:01:50,650 --> 00:01:53,000
So, that's a very important point.
21

22
00:01:53,020 --> 00:01:56,550
Now this is equivalent to this one.
22

23
00:01:56,620 --> 00:02:03,330
So, if you just partially initialize the array, then all remaining data items will be automatically initialize
23

24
00:02:03,400 --> 00:02:05,010
to zeros.
24

25
00:02:05,020 --> 00:02:10,960
So now, what happens if I remove this number of data items information?
25

26
00:02:10,960 --> 00:02:20,420
So, do you think this is a legal 'C' statement? So, of course it is. By using this initializer or initialization information,
26

27
00:02:20,510 --> 00:02:26,090
so you are giving enough information for the compiler to calculate the size of this array, isn't it?
27

28
00:02:26,930 --> 00:02:27,510
So, here
28

29
00:02:27,530 --> 00:02:33,710
during the compilation compiler will automatically calculates the size of this array by looking at the
29

30
00:02:33,710 --> 00:02:36,570
information of the initialization.
30

31
00:02:36,590 --> 00:02:40,450
So, here you are looking to initialize three data items.
31

32
00:02:40,580 --> 00:02:48,120
So, compiler will consider that this array is of three data items. So, that's perfectly fair, isn't it?
32

33
00:02:49,070 --> 00:02:56,570
So, that's why, when you do array definition and initialization in one go, then you can drop this a number
33

34
00:02:56,570 --> 00:03:01,690
of data items information, that will be automatically tuned.
34

35
00:03:01,870 --> 00:03:04,010
So, but this is wrong.
35

36
00:03:04,010 --> 00:03:13,710
For example, if I do something like this let's say someOtherData, and if I leave just like this without
36

37
00:03:13,740 --> 00:03:17,800
initializing that array then this would be an error.
37

38
00:03:17,880 --> 00:03:24,960
Let's compile this. Here we can see that array size missing in someOtherData.
38

39
00:03:25,890 --> 00:03:32,940
So, because the compiler couldn't able to calculate the array size during compile time. Let's understand
39

40
00:03:32,940 --> 00:03:37,550
one more type of array declaration or array definition.
40

41
00:03:37,770 --> 00:03:40,470
Let's say you have the array definition here.
41

42
00:03:40,530 --> 00:03:48,740
Now let's create a variable let's say, int len = 10. So, this information need not be a constant,
42

43
00:03:48,930 --> 00:03:50,270
it can be a variable.
43

44
00:03:51,330 --> 00:03:59,970
So, that's why, I can remove this 10 and in the place of 10 I can mention the variable name, so this type
44

45
00:03:59,970 --> 00:04:02,880
of array is called as variable length
45

46
00:04:02,910 --> 00:04:03,800
array.
46

47
00:04:04,170 --> 00:04:13,140
VLA (variable length array).So, this syntax is actually introduced a C99 standard onwards.
47

48
00:04:13,160 --> 00:04:17,950
So, this wasn't there in a C90 or NCC standard.
48

49
00:04:18,260 --> 00:04:25,630
For example, if I try to compile this code, you can see that there are no errors, isn't it? But, if I change
49

50
00:04:25,630 --> 00:04:34,420
the standard, so let's say I change the standard by going into the settings, and here go to the dialect
50

51
00:04:34,690 --> 00:04:46,600
and select let's say C90, and go to warnings and enable pedantic, enable this, and apply, apply and close,
51

52
00:04:47,590 --> 00:04:50,980
and now try to compile this. Here it is.
52

53
00:04:51,000 --> 00:04:53,230
So, you face a warning.
53

54
00:04:53,290 --> 00:04:58,080
ISO C90 forbids variable length array 'someData'.
54

55
00:04:58,710 --> 00:05:05,170
So, this is what we call as variable length array, where we used a variable to decide the size of an array.
55

56
00:05:06,070 --> 00:05:12,080
So, but this is perfectly fine if you are using the standard C99 onwards.
56

57
00:05:12,100 --> 00:05:15,130
Now let's revert back to the default one.
57

58
00:05:15,940 --> 00:05:23,530
So, let me uncheck this and let's select the default one, apply and close.
58

59
00:05:23,560 --> 00:05:28,640
So, this is fine. So, we just learnt how to initialize an array.

00:05:28,690 --> 00:05:32,190
Now let's look at how to read and write to an individual
60

61
00:05:32,190 --> 00:05:33,860
array elements.

Let's say, you want to write a new value to the array element 2.

00:05:39,460 --> 00:05:42,200

64
00:05:42,220 --> 00:05:43,680
in this case?

65
00:05:43,840 --> 00:05:46,960
So, these are the you know array elements.

66
00:05:47,110 --> 00:05:52,120
So, these are the a contiguous memory location addresses,
66

67
00:05:52,120 --> 00:05:56,020
and this is the base pointer or a reference.
67

68
00:05:56,020 --> 00:06:03,580
So now, how do you identify the array element 2? So, if you think normally, then this is the array element
68

69
00:06:03,610 --> 00:06:13,610
2, isn't it? So, now how do you write a new value to the array element 2?  that is this one. So, simple
69

70
00:06:13,640 --> 00:06:21,330
you just increment this pointer. So, let's say someData plus one.
70

71
00:06:21,360 --> 00:06:22,370
Right?
71

72
00:06:22,440 --> 00:06:27,770
So, if you do that it will start pointing here, and then dereference
72

73
00:06:27,930 --> 00:06:39,020
this whole value, and then write a new value, let's say 0x9. So, if we do this much, then a new value
73

74
00:06:39,020 --> 00:06:41,270
will be stored here, isn't it?
74

75
00:06:41,270 --> 00:06:50,090
That is 0x09, so that's a procedure, isn't it? So, by holding this base pointer so you can access
75

76
00:06:50,210 --> 00:06:53,170
any location of the array, isn't it?
76

77
00:06:53,180 --> 00:06:55,010
Now, let's try this.

78

So, let's say let me remove all these things and let me initialize this array to ff's, and after that

00:07:04,660 --> 00:07:14,240
let me take this base pointer plus one, and after that let me reference that, I can do this, right? and

80

let me write the new value.

So, now let's see the change, let's use printf to print the value of the second data item. Second data

83

item is equal to let's say %x\n.

you can just copy and paste here.

86
00:07:41,500 --> 00:07:45,340
So, this gives us the value of the second data item, isn't it?

So, this is before and this is after.

89

92
00:08:02,560 --> 00:08:04,540
and after that you modified
92

00:08:04,540 --> 00:08:08,440
the second data item using the reference.

94

So, you just incremented the reference to the next position, and you just dereferenced that and you stored
94

95
00:08:14,440 --> 00:08:22,870
new value, that's fine. But accessing array elements like this would be a tedious process, isn't
95

96
00:08:22,870 --> 00:08:23,050
it?

97
