Now instead of writing all this story so there is one shorthand method.Now the Shorthand method is, just to use
98

99
00:08:33,130 --> 00:08:40,160
the array name someData and after that open the square brackets, and after that mention the offset.
99

100
00:08:40,990 --> 00:08:48,450
So, here one is a offset to access the second element, isn't it? So, we just mentioned the offset and
100

101
00:08:48,660 --> 00:08:57,360
mention the value. So, this statement and this statement they are identical, one and the same. 
101

102
00:08:57,410 --> 00:09:01,760
This is just for your convenience during programming.
102

103
00:09:01,760 --> 00:09:08,860
So, when you write like this it will be internally converted to something like this by the compiler. So,
103

104
00:09:08,860 --> 00:09:15,700
here you can see that, by doing this you are actually modifying the second data element of the array,
104

105
00:09:15,730 --> 00:09:17,080
isn't it?
105

106
00:09:17,080 --> 00:09:22,600
So, in order to modify the first data element you should be doing something like this.
106

107
00:09:22,840 --> 00:09:25,390
SomeData [0]
107

108
00:09:25,810 --> 00:09:30,590
So, that's a  reason why we say array indexing starts from zero.
108

109
00:09:30,610 --> 00:09:38,370
The real reason lies in the pointer manipulation method. So, let's say you are writing some value to the
109

110
00:09:38,400 --> 00:09:39,650
first data element.
110

111
00:09:39,660 --> 00:09:45,590
So, compiler sees this something like this address of some data plus zero, isn't it?
111

112
00:09:46,380 --> 00:09:55,270
So, that's why, we always index the array elements from 0. So, the first data item we call it as the 0
112

113
00:09:55,510 --> 00:09:57,970
element of the array.
113

114
00:09:57,970 --> 00:10:03,490
So, this what we call as the first data element of the array.
114

115
00:10:04,420 --> 00:10:13,980
So, that's why, we can use this notation someData[ i ], where I ranges from 0 to 9. So, by doing this we
115

116
00:10:13,980 --> 00:10:19,580
can access different data elements or different data items of the array.
116

\n.

array.

printf contents of this

array.

and after that i++.

line.

and after that someData[i], that's it.

you got the output.

first data element, and second data element.

once again.

oh! sorry, I have to give one printf with \n.

0x33.

values. So, that's about array indexing, and why array index starts from zero, and accessing different data
162

00:14:02,010 --> 00:14:04,790
items of the array using pointer manipulation,

and this is the important takeaway from this lecture.

you just send the reference.

So, that's why, here

is sizeof uint8_t.

it?

00:02:44,680 --> 00:02:49,600

%x let me use \t, so you can use pArray, so you have to deference the pArray,

Right?

+i.

section 30---Strings


video 1


0
1
00:00:00,120 --> 00:00:00,930
Hey, welcome back.

2
00:00:00,930 --> 00:00:08,610
So, in this lecture let's understand about strings. A string is nothing but collection of characters.
2

3
00:00:08,610 --> 00:00:13,180
Please note that, a string must be terminated by a null character.
3

4
00:00:14,020 --> 00:00:21,970
So, when collection of characters are terminated by a null  character then that is called as a 'string'.
4

5
00:00:21,970 --> 00:00:30,690
So, a null character is used to indicate end of a string. Example, your name is collection of characters
5

6
00:00:31,170 --> 00:00:35,490
and can be represented as a string in a program.
6

7
00:00:35,790 --> 00:00:40,380
So, we can use an array to store Strings.
7

8
00:00:40,380 --> 00:00:49,440
So, unlike C++, Java, and Python ,'C' does not have any dedicated data type to store a string data.
8

9
00:00:50,220 --> 00:00:55,800
So, in 'C', we take the help of an array to store and manipulate string data.
9

10
00:00:56,550 --> 00:01:02,010
So, 'string' manipulations are realized through arrays in 'C'.
10

11
00:01:02,010 --> 00:01:06,370
Let's learn how to store a string. For instance
11

12
00:01:06,450 --> 00:01:09,260
this is an example of a string.
12

13
00:01:09,450 --> 00:01:10,670
So, this is a message
13

14
00:01:10,680 --> 00:01:14,400
"hello", which is written inside the double quotes.
14

15
00:01:14,460 --> 00:01:16,310
So, this is a string.
15

16
00:01:16,680 --> 00:01:20,660
So, this is how a string is written in the program.
16

17
00:01:20,660 --> 00:01:22,280
Now how do you store this in a program?
17

18
00:01:22,290 --> 00:01:22,890
Very simple.
18

19
00:01:22,890 --> 00:01:27,560
Create an array. So, array of type char.
19

20
00:01:27,570 --> 00:01:27,970
Why?
20

21
00:01:28,000 --> 00:01:31,930
Because, a string is collection of characters.
21

22
00:01:31,930 --> 00:01:34,850
So, now let's create an array.  
22

23
00:01:34,870 --> 00:01:38,580
char Msg[ ]= "hello".
23

24
00:01:38,670 --> 00:01:44,060
So, this is how you store a string in the program using an array.
24

25
00:01:44,100 --> 00:01:48,510
So, now here you can see that this is a array variable, right?
25

26
00:01:48,930 --> 00:01:54,820
or you can also call it as a pointer, or a reference, or in this case
26

27
00:01:54,870 --> 00:01:59,030
you can also call it as a string variable.
27

28
00:01:59,040 --> 00:02:00,770
This also can be called as string variable
28

29
00:02:00,780 --> 00:02:08,820
for this context. So, when you do this compiler will reserve some bytes of memory and it will store
29

30
00:02:08,820 --> 00:02:10,430
the string.
30

31
00:02:10,680 --> 00:02:12,350
So, how this will be stored in the memory?
31

32
00:02:12,360 --> 00:02:12,960
Very simple.
32

33
00:02:14,510 --> 00:02:24,840
So, let's say there are some memory location addresses let's say E00, E01, E02, E03, E04, E05
33

34
00:02:24,950 --> 00:02:26,560
and 'h' is stored here,
34

35
00:02:26,570 --> 00:02:30,680
that is the ASCII code of h, 'e' will be stored here,
35

36
00:02:30,820 --> 00:02:32,480
'l' will be stored here,
36

37
00:02:32,480 --> 00:02:34,850
this is a place for 'o'
37

38
00:02:34,940 --> 00:02:43,310
and after that please note that the compiler will automatically insert the null character to indicate
38

39
00:02:43,310 --> 00:02:53,720
the end of string. A null character. So, the ASCII code for the null character is 0. So, 0 means a null character.
39

40
00:02:54,530 --> 00:03:05,940
And the null character is also denoted by \0. So, you represent newline as \n, right?
40

41
00:03:05,940 --> 00:03:12,990
In the same way \0 is to denote a null character in the program.
41

42
00:03:12,990 --> 00:03:16,100
So, \0.
42

43
00:03:16,290 --> 00:03:20,280
So, this is how the string "hello" is stored in the memory
43

44
00:03:20,520 --> 00:03:22,650
and this is a reference.
44

45
00:03:22,680 --> 00:03:23,000
OK.
45

46
00:03:23,010 --> 00:03:34,950
Message. So, that means this string definition consumed 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 bytes in memory, not 5 bytes
46

47
00:03:35,010 --> 00:03:36,040
Okay?
47

48
00:03:36,300 --> 00:03:38,930
the required information is 5 bytes.
48

49
00:03:38,940 --> 00:03:40,230
Right?
49

50
00:03:40,230 --> 00:03:48,030
But this definition actually consumes 6 bytes in memory, because compiler terminates the string by a null
50

51
00:03:48,030 --> 00:03:49,170
character.
51

52
00:03:49,350 --> 00:03:51,850
You need not to insert any null character.
52

53
00:03:52,170 --> 00:03:55,090
The compiler automatically inserts that.
53

54
00:03:55,420 --> 00:04:01,720
Alright. So, now we know that how to store a string in a program using character array.
54

55
00:04:01,740 --> 00:04:04,110
There is also one more method to store a string.
55

56
00:04:04,150 --> 00:04:11,640
Okay. So, which is a little complicated method, but let's cover that also for the sake of coverage.
56

57
00:04:11,640 --> 00:04:15,540
So, you can also initialize this array with a string like this.
57

58
00:04:16,350 --> 00:04:22,200
So, let me create a variable message, so you can also initialize something like this.
58

59
00:04:22,200 --> 00:04:32,940
So, let's open the curly braces like you can initialize this array byte by byte like this. 'h', 'e',
59

60
00:04:33,240 --> 00:04:43,920
'l', 'l','o', and close this. If you do this then this is not a string.
60

61
00:04:43,990 --> 00:04:44,340
Okay
61

62
00:04:44,350 --> 00:04:45,900
remember that.
62

63
00:04:46,060 --> 00:04:46,640
Why?
63

64
00:04:46,660 --> 00:04:52,500
Because, here you are just storing some character data into a character array.
64

65
00:04:52,530 --> 00:04:53,630
Right?
65

66
00:04:53,710 --> 00:04:56,310
So, this is not terminated with a null character.
66

67
00:04:57,190 --> 00:05:02,580
So, that's why, this cannot be considered as a string definition remember.
67

68
00:05:02,580 --> 00:05:05,070
So, this is not a string definition.
68

69
00:05:05,140 --> 00:05:09,950
So, this is a perfect example for string definition.
69

70
00:05:09,970 --> 00:05:10,440
Why?
70

71
00:05:10,450 --> 00:05:14,870
Because, here a string is properly terminated with a null character.
71

72
00:05:15,910 --> 00:05:22,900
So, if you are doing string manipulation then you know you should not use this method actually, or if
72

73
00:05:22,900 --> 00:05:28,450
you want to use this method then you have to manually write the null character at the end.
73

74
00:05:28,540 --> 00:05:37,600
For example, here you should write the null character manually and then you finish the initialization.
74

75
00:05:37,630 --> 00:05:42,850
Now these two are identical. So, otherwise these two are not identical.
75

76
00:05:43,750 --> 00:05:48,700
So, that's why, if you want to do string manipulation then this is kind of little complicated method so don't
76

77
00:05:48,700 --> 00:05:49,150
use it,
77

78
00:05:49,170 --> 00:05:51,340
so always go for this.
78

79
00:05:51,340 --> 00:05:55,930
So, now what is a difference between this definition and this definition?
79

80
00:05:55,930 --> 00:06:00,720
So, here that is actually a array of 10 data items.
80

81
00:06:00,730 --> 00:06:07,300
Right? So, in this case you actually have partially initialized this array.
81

82
00:06:07,330 --> 00:06:08,310
Right?
82

83
00:06:08,380 --> 00:06:15,650
So, this array is capable of storing 10 items, but you have stored 5+1= 6 item.
83

84
00:06:15,670 --> 00:06:18,070
So, this is a partial initialization.
84

85
00:06:18,070 --> 00:06:20,160
So, rest of the bytes will be zeros, right?
85

86
00:06:20,170 --> 00:06:24,460
or null character. So, the memory looks something like this.
86

87
00:06:24,460 --> 00:06:30,520
hello, null character that is 0, null character, null character, like that.
87

88
00:06:30,580 --> 00:06:36,040
So, but in this case the compiler will dynamically you know tune the size of the array.
88

89
00:06:36,940 --> 00:06:45,360
So, here the compiler will reserve only six bytes for this array. So, that's a reason why we don't mention
89

90
00:06:45,360 --> 00:06:50,100
the number of data items, when we store a string data using a character array.
90

91
00:06:51,030 --> 00:06:55,430
So, here if you do sizeof of message1 it would give you 10 bytes
91

92
00:06:55,440 --> 00:06:56,160
remember that.
92

93
00:06:57,030 --> 00:07:01,190
But if you do sizeof message2 it would give you 6 bytes.
93

94
00:07:02,040 --> 00:07:04,180
So, what if you do string length?
94

95
00:07:04,260 --> 00:07:10,350
so there is a built in function called string length in the 'C' standard library.
95

96
00:07:10,350 --> 00:07:16,890
So, now if you use that function over let's say message1, then it would give you 5 bytes
96

97
00:07:16,890 --> 00:07:24,810
remember that. Because, string length function always counts only the required information, so it doesn't
97

98
00:07:24,810 --> 00:07:29,100
count the null character. So, that makes sense, isn't it?
98

99
00:07:29,690 --> 00:07:33,700
So, actual length of your information is 5 bytes.
99

100
00:07:33,740 --> 00:07:34,750
Right?
100

101
00:07:34,790 --> 00:07:40,610
So, that's why, string length always returns the length excluding the null character.
101

102
00:07:41,300 --> 00:07:47,660
So, at the same time if you do string length on message 2, what you get? 
102

103
00:07:47,660 --> 00:07:54,160
So, you again get 5 bytes. So, later we can use string length in our program.
103

104
00:07:54,160 --> 00:08:01,570
So, string length actually it's a built in function comes from the a string library of  'C' standard library.
104

105
00:08:01,570 --> 00:08:03,290
So, that's a introduction to strings.
105

106
00:08:03,310 --> 00:08:03,550
OK
106

107
00:08:03,550 --> 00:08:06,390
How you stores strings in your program?
107

108
00:08:06,670 --> 00:08:09,140
Let's continue our discussion on strings.
108

109
00:08:09,400 --> 00:08:12,970
Now let me write simple string definition something like this
109

110
00:08:12,970 --> 00:08:21,020
Char Msg[ ] = "A" in double quotes. And let's write message
110

111
00:08:21,040 --> 00:08:29,490
let me call it as message1 or message2 and char Msg2[ ] = 'A' in single quotes. Please note
111

112
00:08:29,490 --> 00:08:33,430
that, this is called as a string definition.
112

113
00:08:33,450 --> 00:08:36,770
So, this is a string, "A" is a string here OK
113

114
00:08:36,780 --> 00:08:42,100
it's not a character and here 'A' is a character.
114

115
00:08:42,100 --> 00:08:46,920
So, that's how we differentiate between a string and a character.
115

116
00:08:46,990 --> 00:08:54,640
So, here message2 is a character variable which you created to store an ASCII code for a character.
116

117
00:08:55,580 --> 00:08:57,010
That's it.
117

118
00:08:57,010 --> 00:09:02,900
But in this case you are storing a string in the string variable message1.
118

119
00:09:03,100 --> 00:09:09,370
So, the memory for this looks something like this. So, let's say there is a memory allocation E00
119

120
00:09:09,400 --> 00:09:17,310
where 'A' will be stored, and 0xE01 where the null character will be stored, and this is a
120

121
00:09:17,310 --> 00:09:19,050
reference message1.
121

122
00:09:19,470 --> 00:09:20,890
Whereas in this case,
122

123
00:09:20,890 --> 00:09:26,790
so there will be you know let's say one memory location and the ASCII code of  'A' will be stored
123

124
00:09:26,790 --> 00:09:35,180
and this is so which is actually the message2. And in the next lecture let's understand about one
124

125
00:09:35,180 --> 00:09:41,390
more string definition called string literal okay or string constant.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 2

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:00,750
Hey, welcome back.
1

2
00:00:00,780 --> 00:00:08,880
So, in this lecture let's understand about string literal or it is also called as string constant.Now
2

3
00:00:08,940 --> 00:00:10,770
let's get started.
3

4
00:00:10,770 --> 00:00:15,250
Let's say, I store the string in my program in this fashion.
4

5
00:00:15,240 --> 00:00:19,730
Char * msg1 = "hello".
5

6
00:00:20,590 --> 00:00:26,320
Now this is also a valid method to store a string in your program.
6

7
00:00:26,320 --> 00:00:32,670
So, in this method we have used a character pointer. So, here the LHS
7

8
00:00:32,720 --> 00:00:40,750
that is the left hand side is nothing but a char pointer. So,  msg1 is a pointer variable of type
8

9
00:00:40,750 --> 00:00:41,620
char *.
9

10
00:00:42,550 --> 00:00:48,430
And you know that pointer variables are used to store the pointer or an address.
10

11
00:00:48,430 --> 00:00:49,690
Right?
11

12
00:00:49,690 --> 00:00:55,380
So, under the hood this must be converted into a pointer.
12

13
00:00:55,570 --> 00:01:00,220
So, at the end of the day msg1 will hold a pointer.
13

14
00:01:00,280 --> 00:01:08,830
Right? Now the important question is who allocated the memory for this string or for this message?
14

15
00:01:08,830 --> 00:01:17,740
The answer for that is the compiler. Compiler allocates some address to this string literal and then
15

16
00:01:17,830 --> 00:01:25,250
compilers stores the base address of that message into the character pointer here.
16

17
00:01:25,630 --> 00:01:31,450
That is msg1. Then, what is the difference between this one and this one?
17

18
00:01:31,500 --> 00:01:34,140
So, both are valid 'C' definitions
18

19
00:01:34,170 --> 00:01:35,550
please note that.
19

20
00:01:35,850 --> 00:01:38,270
But, what's a difference between these two?
20

21
00:01:38,370 --> 00:01:43,320
So, now the difference is where this string is stored in the memory.
21

22
00:01:43,320 --> 00:01:52,080
Now in this case compiler allocates some memory for this string literal and that memory actually lies
22

23
00:01:52,290 --> 00:02:03,270
in the read only memory or ROM. So, this string "hello" actually lives in ROM. But in this case you
23

24
00:02:03,270 --> 00:02:06,780
have created a character array, isn't it?
24

25
00:02:06,780 --> 00:02:16,110
And you have stored the characters, right? So, in this case this string actually lives in RAM. So, that
25

26
00:02:16,110 --> 00:02:24,690
means in this case I can modify the contents of the string, isn't it? Because it lives in RAM. So, I can
26

27
00:02:24,690 --> 00:02:25,270
do this.
27

28
00:02:25,290 --> 00:02:32,010
Let's say I can do msg2[0] = 'S', I can do this.
28

29
00:02:32,190 --> 00:02:33,920
It is perfectly fine.
29

30
00:02:34,260 --> 00:02:39,420
A string will be modified. But I cannot do the same thing here.
30

31
00:02:39,510 --> 00:02:46,310
I cannot do msg1[0] = 'S', I cannot do this.
31

32
00:02:46,310 --> 00:02:48,280
This is wrong.
32

33
00:02:48,290 --> 00:02:48,740
Why?
33

34
00:02:48,770 --> 00:02:55,430
Because, you are trying to modify the value which is living in a ROM memory.
34

35
00:02:55,430 --> 00:02:58,040
So, that doesn't make any sense, isn't it?
35

36
00:02:58,040 --> 00:03:03,420
Let's investigate this practically by writing a program, so then it makes sense.
36

37
00:03:04,130 --> 00:03:10,460
Let's go back to our IDE and let's write some piece of code. Let's write a small program to understand
37

38
00:03:10,610 --> 00:03:15,910
a string variable and a string literal or a string constant.
38

39
00:03:15,980 --> 00:03:18,250
So, I have a project here.
39

40
00:03:18,290 --> 00:03:25,010
So, now first let me store a string in my program. So, now let me create a char array. 
40

41
00:03:25,010 --> 00:03:27,830
Let me call msg1 =
41

42
00:03:27,830 --> 00:03:28,600
"Hello 
42

43
00:03:28,670 --> 00:03:30,550
how are you?".
43

44
00:03:30,680 --> 00:03:33,650
And after that, let me print this message.
44

45
00:03:34,460 --> 00:03:36,480
So message is,
45

46
00:03:36,590 --> 00:03:45,170
So, in order to print a string you should use a format specifier%s. So, that's a format specifier
46

47
00:03:45,170 --> 00:03:49,540
to print string and \n.
47

48
00:03:49,640 --> 00:03:53,400
And then just mention the string variable that is Msg1. 
48

49
00:03:53,810 --> 00:03:55,190
That's it.
49

50
00:03:55,250 --> 00:03:59,680
Now, let's try this through our SWV viewer.
50

51
00:04:00,410 --> 00:04:04,050
Alright. Now let me just build and debug this code.
51

52
00:04:04,070 --> 00:04:11,450
Now let me just do single step and here you can see that the message is printed, right?
52

53
00:04:11,480 --> 00:04:13,360
So, now let's analyze this variable.
53

54
00:04:14,200 --> 00:04:18,020
So, now let's see where exactly this variable is stored in the memory.
54

55
00:04:18,850 --> 00:04:25,650
So, for that, let's take the help of a memory browser, but before that let's check the variable window.
55

56
00:04:26,980 --> 00:04:31,340
So, go to show view and click on variables.
56

57
00:04:31,390 --> 00:04:39,160
So, here you can get the information about that variable, this is a array variable, so you can expand that array variable
57

58
00:04:39,220 --> 00:04:46,730
and here you can see that this is how the characters are stored in this array. And the base address is
58

59
00:04:46,730 --> 00:04:49,380
this one 2001ffdc,
59

60
00:04:49,390 --> 00:04:53,180
so that's actually a RAM location, right?
60

61
00:04:53,210 --> 00:04:56,440
So, RAM starts from 0x200.
61

62
00:04:56,600 --> 00:05:01,070
That proves that our array is actually stored in RAM.
62

63
00:05:01,070 --> 00:05:01,850
You can see that.
63

64
00:05:01,880 --> 00:05:04,490
So, let's go to the memory browser
64

65
00:05:06,410 --> 00:05:12,360
and I can type 0x2001ffdc
65

66
00:05:12,410 --> 00:05:14,230
and here it is.
66

67
00:05:14,420 --> 00:05:17,120
It shows that data.
67

68
00:05:17,310 --> 00:05:21,330
Now let's do the same string definition using a char pointer.

1
00:00:00,060 --> 00:00:02,620
Now let's analyze this program.
1

2
00:00:02,850 --> 00:00:09,960
First of all to explore this program further, I'm going to add two more printf statements.

00:00:10,060 --> 00:00:15,910
Now the first printf is going to print the address of msg1 variable.

4
00:00:16,150 --> 00:00:25,810
So, that's a reason I use &msg1. And the second one is going to print value which is contained
4

5
00:00:25,810 --> 00:00:28,710
in the variable msg1.
5

6
00:00:28,720 --> 00:00:30,800
So, that's why, I'm going to print sorry
6

7
00:00:30,850 --> 00:00:33,000
just msg1 here.

8
00:00:33,010 --> 00:00:37,600
So, now let's execute this program and let's see what happens.

9
00:00:37,630 --> 00:00:40,100
I am the debug mode, so you can just click over this.

10
00:00:40,130 --> 00:00:40,450
OK?
10

11
00:00:40,460 --> 00:00:48,030
terminate and relaunch option. So, you need not to close this and compile again and debug.
11

12
00:00:48,070 --> 00:00:53,670
So, just launch this. It will compile and relaunch. All right.
12

13
00:00:53,680 --> 00:01:01,490
Now let's execute. So, here it is. The address of msg1 variable is  
13

14
00:01:01,500 --> 00:01:04,500
0x2001ffdc.
14

15
00:01:04,660 --> 00:01:07,850
So, now where do you think this address belongs to?
15

16
00:01:08,110 --> 00:01:09,070
Take a close look.
16

17
00:01:09,070 --> 00:01:11,830
This actually belongs to the RAM address.
17

18
00:01:11,830 --> 00:01:13,000
Right?
18

19
00:01:13,030 --> 00:01:19,690
So because, RAM starts from 0x200 in this microcontroller. And look at the value of message
19

20
00:01:19,690 --> 00:01:20,050
1.
20

21
00:01:21,010 --> 00:01:25,840
So, when you have an array, so the address of msg1 or the value of msg1.
21

22
00:01:25,900 --> 00:01:28,430
So, both are one and the same, isn't it?
22

23
00:01:28,450 --> 00:01:32,470
So, if you are confused let me explain once again.
23

24
00:01:32,530 --> 00:01:43,240
So, here we have an array let's say 0x2001ffdc and it is DE, DF, like that
24

25
00:01:43,240 --> 00:01:46,980
and after that E0, E1.
25

26
00:01:46,990 --> 00:01:48,940
So, this is the first element
26

27
00:01:49,000 --> 00:01:49,710
"Hello"
27

28
00:01:49,990 --> 00:01:52,970
and this is msg1.
28

29
00:01:52,990 --> 00:01:59,250
So, if you print the value of this definitely it is going to print this, isn't it?
29

30
00:01:59,260 --> 00:02:03,520
So, what if you do &msg1?
30

31
00:02:04,510 --> 00:02:08,340
So, this is nothing but  &(msg1+0)
31

32
00:02:08,350 --> 00:02:09,610
Right?
32

33
00:02:09,610 --> 00:02:11,410
So,but what is this?
33

34
00:02:11,440 --> 00:02:12,850
This is a first element.
34

35
00:02:13,360 --> 00:02:17,950
So, that's basically what you're asking is address of the first element.
35

36
00:02:18,640 --> 00:02:20,800
So, what is the address of the first element?
36

37
00:02:20,950 --> 00:02:22,180
This one.
37

38
00:02:22,180 --> 00:02:29,440
So, that's why, in an array the value of this array variable or the address of this array variable is
38

39
00:02:29,440 --> 00:02:34,010
one and the same. So, that's why, you can see that these are same values.
39

40
00:02:34,940 --> 00:02:37,700
So, now you can also go to the variable window,
40

41
00:02:37,770 --> 00:02:38,110
OK
41

42
00:02:38,120 --> 00:02:46,370
You can go to show view and variables and you can also investigate from here. You can see that, if you
42

43
00:02:46,370 --> 00:02:47,360
expand this
43

44
00:02:47,420 --> 00:02:48,320
"Hello how are you?"
44

45
00:02:48,320 --> 00:02:56,300
these characters are stored from this address onwards. But take a closer look into this statement.
45

46
00:02:56,300 --> 00:03:00,890
So basically, this is a local variable, isn't it?
46

47
00:03:00,890 --> 00:03:03,980
or you can also call it as a local array.
47

48
00:03:04,100 --> 00:03:05,140
It is not a global data,
48

49
00:03:05,210 --> 00:03:08,060
it's a local to the main function.
49

50
00:03:08,060 --> 00:03:14,020
So, from our earlier discussion on variables you know that local variables are transient variables, right?
50

51
00:03:14,060 --> 00:03:17,120
They comment go dynamically during runtime of the program.
51

52
00:03:17,900 --> 00:03:24,020
So, that's why, it doesn't make any sense to store this in the data memory permanently throughout the
52

53
00:03:24,020 --> 00:03:25,340
lifespan of a program.
53

54
00:03:26,180 --> 00:03:31,320
So, the global data will be stored in the data memory throughout the lifespan of a program.
54

55
00:03:32,000 --> 00:03:36,110
So, this data is actually a transient data.
55

56
00:03:36,290 --> 00:03:42,230
So, that's a reason it doesn't make any sense to store this permanently on the data memory.
56

57
00:03:42,230 --> 00:03:50,380
So, that's why, what happens is, this data is copied from the flash memory into this array during runtime
57

58
00:03:50,400 --> 00:03:52,210
of the program.
58

59
00:03:52,220 --> 00:03:54,470
So, now let's investigate that.
59

60
00:03:54,470 --> 00:04:00,230
So, what I'm going to do is, I'm going to open the disassembly and I'm going to enable the instruction
60

61
00:04:00,230 --> 00:04:04,070
level debugging, let me just reset it.
61

62
00:04:04,670 --> 00:04:06,300
So, here it is.
62

63
00:04:06,350 --> 00:04:13,790
So, let's just step over and take a look into the value of r3 register.
63

64
00:04:13,790 --> 00:04:20,060
So, that is actually a flash address, right? 0x8001354.
64

65
00:04:20,300 --> 00:04:26,290
So, but we know this belongs to RAM. Arrays created in the RAM. All right.
65

66
00:04:26,320 --> 00:04:31,870
Now let's open the memory browser and type 2001ffdc,
66

67
00:04:32,170 --> 00:04:34,750
so these are the memory allocations
67

68
00:04:34,900 --> 00:04:39,310
and this is where our array is created, right?
68

69
00:04:40,180 --> 00:04:44,210
So, these memory locations actually belong to our
69

70
00:04:44,510 --> 00:04:44,890
array,
70

71
00:04:44,890 --> 00:04:45,970
that is msg1.
71

72
00:04:47,040 --> 00:04:53,250
Now what happens is, these instructions will copy this string from flash into this
72

73
00:04:53,250 --> 00:04:53,570
array.
73

74
00:04:54,450 --> 00:04:56,460
So, because when you create the binary
74

75
00:04:56,480 --> 00:04:56,690
OK
75

76
00:04:56,700 --> 00:04:59,250
so this is part of the binary, isn't it?
76

77
00:04:59,250 --> 00:05:02,160
So, which will be stored in the flash memory.
77

78
00:05:02,430 --> 00:05:10,000
So, you have to note one point here that you know these data copying is not done by the startup code.
78

79
00:05:10,040 --> 00:05:18,440
So, this is done during the function execution. So, in the earlier part of the course we also saw that
79

80
00:05:18,560 --> 00:05:25,640
there is a hidden startup code in our project which copies data from flash to the RAM, Isn't it?
80

81
00:05:25,700 --> 00:05:35,830
So, that data copying belongs to global data, initialized global data. But here this is not a global data
81

82
00:05:35,920 --> 00:05:36,240
OK?
82

83
00:05:36,250 --> 00:05:39,910
So, this is a transient data or a local data.
83

84
00:05:40,780 --> 00:05:46,720
So, that's why, data copying happens when that function is called. Here the main is called.
84

85
00:05:46,720 --> 00:05:51,890
So, that's why, it is taking place here during runtime of the function.
85

86
00:05:52,120 --> 00:05:57,690
And once this function exits this data will be removed from the RAM.
86

87
00:05:58,540 --> 00:06:05,820
So, strictly speaking this variable is actually created in stack memory of the RAM.
87

88
00:06:05,860 --> 00:06:08,800
So, if you are confused let me explain with some diagrams.
88

89
00:06:08,800 --> 00:06:09,680
OK?
89

90
00:06:09,940 --> 00:06:13,060
So, we have a RAM, Isn't it?
90

91
00:06:13,060 --> 00:06:15,390
So, let's call it as a RAM
91

92
00:06:15,460 --> 00:06:24,670
and in this microcontroller we have RAM of 192 kilobytes(192KB).
92

93
00:06:24,850 --> 00:06:27,560
So, we know what is a start of the RAM.
93

94
00:06:27,970 --> 00:06:31,500
So, let's call it as start and let's call it as
94

95
00:06:31,500 --> 00:06:32,860
end of RAM.
95

96
00:06:33,240 --> 00:06:35,280
And we know that what is the start of RAM.
96

97
00:06:35,430 --> 00:06:35,760
OK.
97

98
00:06:35,760 --> 00:06:41,000
0x2000-0000.
98

99
00:06:41,040 --> 00:06:52,170
So, this is a start of the RAM and end of the RAM will be 0x2002-FFFC. And the RAM
99

100
00:06:52,170 --> 00:07:00,280
will be divided into couple of  a sections like there will be one section which is for global data.
100

101
00:07:00,600 --> 00:07:02,160
This is for global data.
101

102
00:07:03,180 --> 00:07:06,550
And there will be one section for stack data.
102

103
00:07:07,500 --> 00:07:14,700
So here, the transient variables will be created and destroyed.This is a stack. Some part of the RAM will be
103

104
00:07:14,700 --> 00:07:18,430
reserved for heap for dynamic memory allocation.
104

105
00:07:18,480 --> 00:07:25,380
So, here we don't care about heap because we are not using any dynamic memory allocation APIs. When
105

106
00:07:25,380 --> 00:07:33,040
the function is called all local variables are created in the stack, and when the program exits those
106

107
00:07:33,220 --> 00:07:35,580
memories will be reclaimed.
107

108
00:07:35,590 --> 00:07:41,710
So, a stack will be tracked by the stack pointer register of the processor.
108

109
00:07:41,710 --> 00:07:46,730
So, there is a stack pointer register and that will be used to track the stack.
109

110
00:07:46,750 --> 00:07:50,650
So, here this will be let's say start of stack.
110

111
00:07:50,650 --> 00:07:56,200
Start of stack and let's say let's call it as this as end of stack.
111

112
00:07:56,200 --> 00:08:03,670
So, whenever the variables are created in the stack, the stack actually decrements. Stack pointer actually
112

113
00:08:03,670 --> 00:08:11,860
decrements. And whenever variables are destroyed a stack pointer will reclaim those memory addresses. So,
113

114
00:08:11,860 --> 00:08:14,620
dynamically stack will shrink and expand.
114

115
00:08:15,430 --> 00:08:21,910
So, in our case the msg1 variable  or array is a local variable, right?
115

116
00:08:21,970 --> 00:08:29,590
So, that's a reason msg1 will be created here in the stack. And we know that there is also a flash
116

117
00:08:29,590 --> 00:08:32,750
memory, which is our permanent memory flash.
117

118
00:08:32,860 --> 00:08:39,100
And so when you load the binary into the flash, the "hello how are you?" that string is stored here, isn't
118

119
00:08:39,100 --> 00:08:41,130
it? in the flash.
119

120
00:08:41,170 --> 00:08:47,320
So, when the main function is called that string is actually copied into msg1
120

121
00:08:47,350 --> 00:08:48,080
array.
121

122
00:08:48,190 --> 00:08:50,300
So, this is not done by the startup code.
122

123
00:08:50,500 --> 00:08:54,740
So, this is done during runtime of the function.
123

124
00:08:54,820 --> 00:09:01,510
So, suppose if there is any array called let's say global msg1 array, so let's say you create a
124

125
00:09:01,510 --> 00:09:09,760
global variable. In that case, the startup code would have copied the string into global data into global section.
125

126
00:09:10,600 --> 00:09:11,220
That happens
126

127
00:09:11,220 --> 00:09:13,810
control comes prior to the main.
127

128
00:09:14,110 --> 00:09:20,430
In that case startup code would have copied that data from flash to the global array.
128

129
00:09:20,470 --> 00:09:23,400
So, in our program there is no global array,
129

130
00:09:23,470 --> 00:09:25,330
this is what happening behind the scene.
130

131
00:09:26,140 --> 00:09:29,610
So, now let me just step over, now
131

132
00:09:29,620 --> 00:09:36,730
let me just execute these instructions, and here you can see that the string is copied from flash into
132

133
00:09:36,910 --> 00:09:38,320
RAM into this array.
133

134
00:09:38,350 --> 00:09:39,670
Isn't it?
134

135
00:09:39,700 --> 00:09:40,410
Remember that,
135

136
00:09:40,420 --> 00:09:43,620
now you have two copies of this string.
136

137
00:09:43,880 --> 00:09:50,680
So, this is copy-1 and one more copies created in the stack or in the RAM.
137

138
00:09:50,710 --> 00:09:55,470
This is copy-2. So, 2 copies are created.
138

139
00:09:55,510 --> 00:10:03,130
So, when you do msg1[0] is equal to let's say if you assign some other value, then you are
139

140
00:10:03,190 --> 00:10:08,440
actually changing this copy not this copy remember.
140

141
00:10:08,500 --> 00:10:12,880
This copy cannot be modified, right? because that is in the flash.
141

142
00:10:13,690 --> 00:10:18,850
So, you cannot just change the contents of the flash using your pointer access.
142

143
00:10:19,270 --> 00:10:20,260
That's not possible,
143

144
00:10:20,260 --> 00:10:22,400
I'll tell you why later.
144

145
00:10:22,420 --> 00:10:30,400
So, basically when you do this you are actually changing this copy. So, that's why, this is fine no problem.
145

146
00:10:30,400 --> 00:10:31,930
It works.
146

147
00:10:31,980 --> 00:10:37,650
Now let's discuss the case of string literal or string constant.
147

148
00:10:37,910 --> 00:10:38,180
All right.
148

149
00:10:38,190 --> 00:10:41,910
Now let's discuss the case of string literal.
149

150
00:10:41,910 --> 00:10:45,980
I'm going to remove all these things or let me keep that.
150

151
00:10:46,140 --> 00:10:54,200
So, here I'm going to add one more string definition and this time I'm going to use a char pointer.
151

152
00:10:54,450 --> 00:10:59,060
So, let me call pmsg2 is equal to let me write the string
152

153
00:10:59,070 --> 00:11:07,900
let's say "fastbitlab.com". And after that I'm going to print msg1 that's OK, and I'm going
153

154
00:11:07,900 --> 00:11:10,270
to print the msg2 as well.
154

155
00:11:11,140 --> 00:11:19,030
So, you can use  %s, as I said before %s needs char *, which is terminated with
155

156
00:11:19,030 --> 00:11:20,740
a null character.
156

157
00:11:20,740 --> 00:11:22,630
So, you should remember that.
157

158
00:11:22,840 --> 00:11:25,500
Let me write pmsg2 here.
158

159
00:11:25,580 --> 00:11:32,860
I'm going to print address of pmsg2 variable, because this is a pointer variable.
159

160
00:11:32,860 --> 00:11:42,070
So, &pmsg2. And value of pmsg2 variable is equal to I am going to write pmsg2.
160

161
00:11:42,070 --> 00:11:50,790
So, before executing this do you have any guesses what could be the result especially of these two
161

162
00:11:50,800 --> 00:11:52,770
printf's? So,
162

163
00:11:52,780 --> 00:11:54,490
now let me guess.
163

164
00:11:54,490 --> 00:12:02,710
&pmsg2, that is address of this variable. So, we know that this is a local variable.
164

165
00:12:03,520 --> 00:12:07,290
So, address of this variable will be in RAM, isn't it?
165

166
00:12:07,330 --> 00:12:11,290
So, definitely it is going to print some RAM address.
166

167
00:12:11,290 --> 00:12:12,670
There is no question about that.
167

168
00:12:12,670 --> 00:12:13,670
Right?
168

169
00:12:13,750 --> 00:12:16,100
So, both are local variables.
169

170
00:12:16,170 --> 00:12:18,410
Now, what about this?
170

171
00:12:18,540 --> 00:12:23,450
Now this is a string literal or a string constant which is stored in a flash.
171

172
00:12:23,500 --> 00:12:26,680
Okay?,So, both are stored in a flash.
172

173
00:12:26,760 --> 00:12:27,250
Why?
173

174
00:12:27,290 --> 00:12:30,190
Because, it is  part of the binary, right?
174

175
00:12:30,200 --> 00:12:33,250
So, when you create a binary it is part of the flash.
175

176
00:12:33,260 --> 00:12:36,880
So, both are living in ROM or flash.
176

177
00:12:37,160 --> 00:12:40,430
In this case there is a data copy remember.
177

178
00:12:40,610 --> 00:12:47,620
But in this case there is no data copy just a pointer assignment, that's it.
178

179
00:12:47,630 --> 00:12:55,660
So, the pmsg2 is going to display what? It is going to display a flash address. 
179

180
00:12:55,810 --> 00:13:00,970
Let's check. I'm going to hit this terminate and relaunch.
180

181
00:13:01,030 --> 00:13:07,690
So, now let's start the trace and let's see what happens. Here
181

182
00:13:07,720 --> 00:13:12,200
it is. So, this is a RAM address. So, we have no doubt on that.
182

183
00:13:12,370 --> 00:13:13,000
Look at this.
183

184
00:13:13,030 --> 00:13:13,250
OK.
184

185
00:13:13,270 --> 00:13:15,560
So, this is a flash address.
185

186
00:13:15,710 --> 00:13:17,690
So, that makes clear that,
186

187
00:13:17,740 --> 00:13:22,240
so this variable living in RAM pointing to flash.
187

188
00:13:22,240 --> 00:13:23,400
Right?
188

189
00:13:23,620 --> 00:13:30,910
Let me right pmsg2 here.So, pmsg2 is created in stack.
189

190
00:13:31,510 --> 00:13:33,670
So, this is a flash let's say,
190

191
00:13:33,670 --> 00:13:36,130
so this is a string,
191

192
00:13:36,130 --> 00:13:43,770
so this is created in stack but it is pointing to flash. So, that's what's happening.
192

193
00:13:43,840 --> 00:13:50,280
So, that's a reason you can't do pmsg2[0] =
193

194
00:13:50,290 --> 00:13:52,440
you can't assign some other value.
194

195
00:13:52,720 --> 00:13:53,520
You can't do that.
195

196
00:13:53,530 --> 00:13:54,650
OK?
196

197
00:13:54,790 --> 00:13:57,780
Because, pmsg2 is pointing to flash.
197

198
00:13:57,790 --> 00:13:57,990
OK?
198

199
00:13:58,000 --> 00:14:02,140
you cannot change the flash contents like this.
199

200
00:14:02,230 --> 00:14:03,450
It's not allowed.
200

201
00:14:03,940 --> 00:14:07,900
Even if you do this it has no effect on the microcontroller.
201

202
00:14:08,260 --> 00:14:14,410
Because, we write access to the flash will be detained or it will be blocked by the flash controller
202

203
00:14:14,530 --> 00:14:20,500
unless you give some special instructions to the flash controller like you have to insert some password
203

204
00:14:20,500 --> 00:14:27,880
and all, and you have to unlock write access to the flash, then only you can do this technique to write
204

205
00:14:27,880 --> 00:14:29,370
into the flash.
205

206
00:14:29,440 --> 00:14:36,520
Otherwise, simply you cannot modify the flash content by taking its address. The flash controller will
206

207
00:14:36,520 --> 00:14:38,770
not allow you to do like that.
207

208
00:14:38,770 --> 00:14:44,980
So, this has no effect on the microcontroller. But the same code if you try in your host computer, then
208

209
00:14:45,160 --> 00:14:50,850
this may lead to unexpected result, your application may even crash.
209

210
00:14:51,190 --> 00:14:53,740
We can investigate that later.
210

211
00:14:53,740 --> 00:14:57,090
So, now here what I'm going to do is, I'm going to do
211

212
00:14:57,130 --> 00:15:06,160
msg1 of a let's say 0 is equal to I am going to replace this 'h' by let's say 'b'. And
212

213
00:15:06,250 --> 00:15:10,840
I'm going to do the same thing for the pointer also.
213

214
00:15:10,840 --> 00:15:13,160
Now let's check.
214

215
00:15:13,990 --> 00:15:21,250
So, here we can see that compiler didn't issue any error.So, compiler has no problem with this code, even
215

216
00:15:21,250 --> 00:15:23,200
though this code is wrong. 
216

217
00:15:23,210 --> 00:15:25,220
So, compiler actually ignored this,
217

218
00:15:25,220 --> 00:15:31,570
so compiler basically won't throw any warning or error on this line. 
218

219
00:15:31,570 --> 00:15:34,310
So, actually it's a wrong code, right?
219

220
00:15:34,420 --> 00:15:39,630
So, you should not be doing something like this. Because you are trying to modify a read only memory.
220

221
00:15:39,670 --> 00:15:41,020
Right?
221

222
00:15:41,050 --> 00:15:48,340
So, that's why, why you should maintain this variable definition as a constant data definition?
222

223
00:15:48,340 --> 00:15:55,190
So, the best way to write this a string definition is char const.
223

224
00:15:55,240 --> 00:16:02,310
This is a valid or more suitable string definition for a string literal or a string constant.
224

225
00:16:02,320 --> 00:16:09,080
Now, if you try to execute this there is an error.Here we can see that there is an error.
225

226
00:16:09,390 --> 00:16:12,870
So, compiler will not allow you to do something like this.
226

227
00:16:12,900 --> 00:16:19,520
So, for a time being let's remove this const and let's try once again. All right.
227

228
00:16:19,530 --> 00:16:26,840
So, now let me just run this code.
228

229
00:16:26,920 --> 00:16:36,440
You can see that 'h' replaced by 'b', but here we can see that this is not modified.So, it has no effect.
229

230
00:16:36,490 --> 00:16:43,250
That's about the difference between a string variable and a string constant Ok or a string literal.
230

231
00:16:43,260 --> 00:16:47,720
So, and don't forget to use const here, that's a good practice.
231

Now let's test this code in host, the same code I'm using and let's return 0 here.

Now let's compile and see what happens here.

236
00:17:13,910 --> 00:17:14,240
Right?

00:17:14,240 --> 00:17:15,320
So, let's run.

And here we can see that this text is modified by this change, so that is visible and it actually disabled
238

239
00:17:27,700 --> 00:17:28,180
this line.

240

241
00:17:29,710 --> 00:17:32,770
And here we can see that this is actually the RAM address.
241

242
00:17:33,430 --> 00:17:37,180
So, there is a difference between this address and this address.
242

243
00:17:37,210 --> 00:17:42,220
So, this actually belongs to the read only section or read only memory.
243

244
00:17:42,220 --> 00:17:45,790
So, this is happening on the computer.
244

245
00:17:45,790 --> 00:17:47,440
Now let me enable this line.
245

246
00:17:48,370 --> 00:17:49,240
Let's see what happens.
246

247
00:17:49,240 --> 00:17:54,940
So, here we are trying to change the content of the read only memory location.
247

248
00:17:54,970 --> 00:17:58,720
Now, let's see how this program behaves on the host.
248

249
00:17:59,140 --> 00:18:01,900
And let me run once again.
249

250
00:18:01,900 --> 00:18:08,560
And here it is the application crashes. Because, the operating system detected that you are trying to
250

251
00:18:08,560 --> 00:18:14,350
change the content of the read only section and that's a reason the operating system terminated your
251

252
00:18:14,350 --> 00:18:16,120
executable.
252

253
00:18:16,120 --> 00:18:17,470
We saw that on the microcontroller
253

254
00:18:17,470 --> 00:18:19,670
it has no effect, right?
254

255
00:18:19,680 --> 00:18:20,250
That's a reason
255

256
00:18:20,250 --> 00:18:27,480
if you have some string definition something like this, then always you should maintain the data as constant.
256

257
00:18:27,480 --> 00:18:32,960
So, if anyone tries to modify that you can see that the compiler throws an error.
257

258
00:18:33,480 --> 00:18:39,600
So, with that note I would like to end this discussion and I will continue in the next lecture.
258

259
00:18:39,600 --> 00:18:46,410
So, in the next lecture let's see how to input string, I mean how to take string as an input from the
259

260
00:18:46,410 --> 00:18:47,620
user.
260

261
00:18:47,830 --> 00:18:49,050
I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 4

0
1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:00,810
Hey, welcome back.
1

2
00:00:00,810 --> 00:00:06,570
So, in this lecture let's understand about printing and inputting a string. So, we learnt about printing
2

3
00:00:06,570 --> 00:00:09,440
a string where we used %s, right?
3

4
00:00:09,480 --> 00:00:14,280
Let's learn about how to input a string using %s format specifier.
4

5
00:00:14,670 --> 00:00:20,880
So, use the format specifier %s with printf to print a string and with scanf to input
5

6
00:00:20,880 --> 00:00:21,300
a string.
6

7
00:00:21,390 --> 00:00:29,070
So, you should remember that. And a be careful with using %s with array of characters. The array
7

8
00:00:29,070 --> 00:00:35,130
must be terminated with a NULL character; otherwise, it may result in 'segmentation fault'. For %s
8

9
00:00:35,250 --> 00:00:39,490
the string must be terminated with a NULL character.
9

10
00:00:39,750 --> 00:00:42,000
Let's learn how to input a string.
10

11
00:00:42,000 --> 00:00:44,520
Now let's just practice to input a string.
11

12
00:00:44,520 --> 00:00:47,250
Let's learn how to input your name. For that,
12

13
00:00:47,250 --> 00:00:50,610
let me write a small program printf("Enter your name").
13

14
00:00:50,610 --> 00:00:52,460
So, just observe what I'm doing.
14

15
00:00:52,470 --> 00:00:54,990
So, later you can practice I'll give you an exercise.
15

16
00:00:55,350 --> 00:00:56,390
So, enter your name 
16

17
00:00:56,430 --> 00:01:00,860
and now let's say you have to use a scanf to take the name from the user.
17

18
00:01:01,410 --> 00:01:05,090
So, now first let's reserve some space to store that string.
18

19
00:01:05,220 --> 00:01:07,820
So, we have to use the character array, isn't it?
19

20
00:01:08,070 --> 00:01:13,850
Let's use the character array. char let's say name and let's reserve some bytes to store a name.
20

21
00:01:13,860 --> 00:01:20,340
So, let's say 30 bytes. So, your name must be less than 29 characters, because 1 byte is required
21

22
00:01:20,430 --> 00:01:21,800
to store the null character, Isn't it?
22

23
00:01:21,920 --> 00:01:24,240
So, that's why, your name must be of 
23

24
00:01:24,240 --> 00:01:27,990
29 characters or less than that for this application.
24

25
00:01:27,990 --> 00:01:30,410
And after that let's use scanf.
25

26
00:01:30,420 --> 00:01:35,040
So, you have to use a format specifier %s to read a string
26

27
00:01:35,190 --> 00:01:37,200
and the argument is name.
27

28
00:01:37,200 --> 00:01:40,690
So, this is a array name of type char*, right?
28

29
00:01:40,710 --> 00:01:49,480
And after that, let's greet the user "Hi, %s". And you have to give the argument name.
29

30
00:01:49,500 --> 00:01:50,580
So, this is a message.
30

31
00:01:50,760 --> 00:01:52,440
Let's try this.
31

32
00:01:52,620 --> 00:02:01,020
Let's run this. So, now let me type the name let's say kumar and Hi, kumar. So, you can see that it printed.
32

33
00:02:01,110 --> 00:02:10,300
So, now let's run once again and this time I am going to enter ram kumar. And here we can see that it
33

34
00:02:10,300 --> 00:02:10,880
couldn't
34

35
00:02:10,930 --> 00:02:18,310
able to read the characters after the white space character. The white space character here is space, isn't
35

36
00:02:18,310 --> 00:02:18,600
it?
36

37
00:02:18,610 --> 00:02:21,100
So, it only read till here.
37

38
00:02:21,100 --> 00:02:21,520
Right?
38

39
00:02:21,520 --> 00:02:23,440
So, we'll investigate why is that.
39

40
00:02:23,440 --> 00:02:30,260
I'm going to investigate the contents of this array after inputting the string. So, I'm going to use a for loop
40

41
00:02:30,640 --> 00:02:38,540
and I'm going to iterate over 30 bytes and I'm going to print the contents of this
41

42
00:02:38,560 --> 00:02:40,960
array in the form of hex.
42

43
00:02:41,200 --> 00:02:45,480
So, "%x\n, name[i]".
43

44
00:02:45,490 --> 00:02:49,990
So, this is to read, this is to print, and to investigate this
44

45
00:02:49,990 --> 00:02:50,390
array.
45

46
00:02:50,620 --> 00:02:57,550
Let's run. Oh! sorry, for a time being let me use int. So,
46

47
00:02:57,560 --> 00:03:02,220
now let me enter the name and here it is.
47

48
00:03:02,250 --> 00:03:03,930
So, this is a first element of the
48

49
00:03:03,930 --> 00:03:04,210
array.
49

50
00:03:04,290 --> 00:03:08,930
So, this is for 'k', 'i', 'r','a','n', isn't it?
50

51
00:03:08,970 --> 00:03:13,530
So, after n which is this one there is a 0, isn't it?
51

52
00:03:13,530 --> 00:03:15,320
So, this is a NULL character.
52

53
00:03:15,420 --> 00:03:15,750
Right?
53

54
00:03:15,750 --> 00:03:19,890
So, that NULL character is added by the scanf automatically.
54

55
00:03:20,460 --> 00:03:24,850
So, you did not to insert any NULL character to make it a string.
55

56
00:03:24,870 --> 00:03:30,930
So, when you use scanf along with %s a string will be terminated by a NULL character.
56

57
00:03:31,320 --> 00:03:35,760
And after that all these are some random a values which are there in this
57

58
00:03:35,760 --> 00:03:38,940
array. Let's execute the same program in our IDE,
58

59
00:03:39,390 --> 00:03:49,680
let me go to the IDE and let's paste here, and let's compile this, and let's try to run. And here you can
59

60
00:03:49,680 --> 00:03:51,690
see that we are not getting this printf,
60

61
00:03:51,730 --> 00:03:56,020
that's because for this console you have to use fflush.
61

62
00:03:56,160 --> 00:04:02,700
So, if you want to check the output in Eclipse console you have to use a fflush(stdout) here,
62

63
00:04:02,700 --> 00:04:08,340
and after that here as well. Actually here it is not required, because there is a return.
63

64
00:04:08,370 --> 00:04:11,410
So, when it returns automatically everything will be flushed out.
64

65
00:04:11,430 --> 00:04:13,890
So, that's why, this is redundant actually.
65

66
00:04:13,890 --> 00:04:15,390
Let's run this.
66

67
00:04:18,160 --> 00:04:20,920
So, enter your name,
67

68

let's enter ashok and it prints correctly.
68

69
00:04:24,340 --> 00:04:31,270

69

70
00:04:31,270 --> 00:04:33,580
character, and I'll see you in the next lecture.

video 5

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,650
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,650 --> 00:00:07,680
So, in this lecture let's learn how to input a string with space character in between.
2

3
00:00:07,680 --> 00:00:11,550
Before that, let's revisit our scanf functionality once again.
3

4
00:00:11,550 --> 00:00:15,010
So, for that, let's ask the user to enter three numbers.
4

5
00:00:15,030 --> 00:00:17,880
How do you read three numbers from the user?
5

6
00:00:17,880 --> 00:00:19,580
Let's create three variables for that.
6

7
00:00:19,590 --> 00:00:27,970
So, let's say int a, b, c and you know that you can just use scanf with multiple format specifiers.
7

8
00:00:28,200 --> 00:00:29,370
Like %d
8

9
00:00:29,370 --> 00:00:31,060
%d %d.
9

10
00:00:31,440 --> 00:00:32,430
Right?
10

11
00:00:32,550 --> 00:00:39,900
And you can use &a, &b, &c.Let's printf
11

12
00:00:40,090 --> 00:00:43,060
numbers are %d %d
12

13
00:00:43,070 --> 00:00:46,960
%d \n, a, b, c.
13

14
00:00:46,960 --> 00:00:47,830
Let's see what happens.
14

15
00:00:48,040 --> 00:00:48,280
All right.
15

16
00:00:48,280 --> 00:00:51,880
Now let's run this. Enter three numbers.
16

17
00:00:51,920 --> 00:00:55,520
How do you enter? A user may enter like this 1
17

18
00:00:55,540 --> 00:00:58,000
space, 2 space, 3.
18

19
00:00:58,090 --> 00:00:58,920
Right?
19

20
00:00:58,930 --> 00:01:00,920
So, the input was successful.
20

21
00:01:00,940 --> 00:01:02,770
So, now 'a' charries 1, 
21

22
00:01:02,770 --> 00:01:05,590
'b' carries 2, 'c' carries 3.
22

23
00:01:05,650 --> 00:01:06,100
Right?
23

24
00:01:06,100 --> 00:01:11,480
So, if you observe this what scanf did? It actually ignored this space character.
24

25
00:01:11,490 --> 00:01:11,770
Right?
25

26
00:01:11,770 --> 00:01:13,350
So, that's a behavior of scanf.
26

27
00:01:13,570 --> 00:01:20,960
So, it actually ignores the whitespace characters like space, and form feed, new line, etc..
27

28
00:01:21,130 --> 00:01:27,730
So, now let's run once again. I'm going to give the number like this 1, there is a big gap 2, another big
28

29
00:01:27,730 --> 00:01:28,890
gap 3.
29

30
00:01:28,930 --> 00:01:31,720
Now what do you think the values of a, b, and c?
30

31
00:01:31,720 --> 00:01:36,770
Still it is correct. a is 1, b is 2, and c is 3.
31

32
00:01:36,850 --> 00:01:38,670
The scanf was successful, right?
32

33
00:01:38,680 --> 00:01:42,730
So, that means it doesn't care about these whitespace characters.
33

34
00:01:42,850 --> 00:01:43,820
Right?
34

35
00:01:43,840 --> 00:01:45,780
Let's run once again.
35

36
00:01:45,820 --> 00:01:52,960
So, this time I'm going to give 1, couple of spaces 2, couple of spaces 3.
36

37
00:01:53,230 --> 00:01:56,530
And here it is, still the read was successful, right?
37

38
00:01:56,530 --> 00:02:00,040
So, here the whitespace character was \n or
38

39
00:02:00,070 --> 00:02:01,160
new line, isn't it?
39

40
00:02:02,020 --> 00:02:03,760
So, that's a behavior of the scanf.
40

41
00:02:03,910 --> 00:02:07,300
So, it doesn't care about the in-between whitespace characters.
41

42
00:02:07,330 --> 00:02:12,100
So, that's why, you cannot read a string which has a space in between.
42

43
00:02:12,730 --> 00:02:18,500
But there are alternative ways by which we can achieve a reading of complete string.
43

44
00:02:18,520 --> 00:02:19,840
One method is,
44

45
00:02:19,840 --> 00:02:27,970
so now let's address that issue. Let's write a program to read first name and last name of a user.
45

46
00:02:28,090 --> 00:02:35,620
So, for that, let me create character arrays for first name. So, let's give 30 bytes to it,
46

47
00:02:35,770 --> 00:02:39,170
and let me create another array lname 30.
47

48
00:02:39,430 --> 00:02:47,220
So, after that, what I'm going to do is, let's ask the user to enter your full name.
48

49
00:02:47,860 --> 00:02:52,960
So, if I use scanf here "%s", fname
49

50
00:02:52,960 --> 00:03:01,470
let me read like that and if I use printf "your name is : %s\n"
50

51
00:03:01,720 --> 00:03:05,170
and let me write fname here.
51

52
00:03:05,290 --> 00:03:13,780
So, if I run this program, here we can see that if I type ram space kumar, it doesn't take this word, right?
52

53
00:03:14,500 --> 00:03:18,250
Now what you can do is, you can use another %s here.
53

54
00:03:18,280 --> 00:03:25,630
Just like the earlier example where we use %d 3 times, isn't it? So, for this %s you use
54

55
00:03:25,690 --> 00:03:30,280
lname. So, your name is %s space %s.
55

56
00:03:30,280 --> 00:03:34,790
So, you can write fname, lname. You can do something like this.
56

57
00:03:34,810 --> 00:03:37,480
So, this kind of a tedious method, right?
57

58
00:03:37,480 --> 00:03:45,690
So, now let me enter john smith. So, here you can see that, now you can process something like this. But
58

59
00:03:45,690 --> 00:03:50,000
the thing is you couldn't able to store this whole string in one array.
59

60
00:03:50,040 --> 00:03:51,030
Right?
60

61
00:03:51,120 --> 00:03:58,620
So, it is now split into two arrays, so you can use mem copy techniques or string concatenation techniques
61

62
00:03:58,620 --> 00:04:00,220
to make it as a one string.
62

63
00:04:00,360 --> 00:04:02,030
So, but there is one trick.
63

64
00:04:02,070 --> 00:04:07,680
The trick is to use the scan sets of scanf function.
64

65
00:04:07,740 --> 00:04:09,750
So, there is something called scan set.
65

66
00:04:09,750 --> 00:04:14,780
So, that's why, we have to use something called scan set of a scanf function.
66

67
00:04:14,800 --> 00:04:23,520
Scan set is actually an argument which is mentioned between this % sign and the letter 's'. 
67

68
00:04:23,550 --> 00:04:26,870
So, here you have to create a square bracket
68

69
00:04:26,970 --> 00:04:29,360
and this is what we call as scan set.
69

70
00:04:29,460 --> 00:04:30,970
Here you have to mention the argument,
70

71
00:04:30,990 --> 00:04:32,670
so these are different examples.
71

72
00:04:32,700 --> 00:04:35,440
Let's learn how to use the scan set.
72

73
00:04:35,460 --> 00:04:38,910
Let's go back to our earlier example and here
73

74
00:04:39,070 --> 00:04:42,820
let's use only one array to store the entire string.
74

75
00:04:42,840 --> 00:04:44,690
Let me call  it as name.
75

76
00:04:45,000 --> 00:04:46,550
So, enter your full name.
76

77
00:04:46,560 --> 00:04:49,890
Let's use only %s and name.
77

78
00:04:49,980 --> 00:04:52,240
So, your name is %s.
78

79
00:04:52,380 --> 00:04:54,240
So, let's maintain only one
79

80
00:04:54,300 --> 00:04:55,250
array. Here,
80

81
00:04:55,270 --> 00:04:56,860
let's give the scan set.
81

82
00:04:57,060 --> 00:04:59,790
So, open the square bracket.
82

83
00:04:59,790 --> 00:05:01,130
Let's see one example.
83

84
00:05:01,140 --> 00:05:03,150
So, now let's read all the characters
84

85
00:05:03,150 --> 00:05:08,840
what user enters until there is a character 's' in the string.
85

86
00:05:08,850 --> 00:05:15,210
So, if you want to go to give such a condition to scanf you can use a exponent symbol,
86

87
00:05:15,240 --> 00:05:17,070
so or a carat(^) symbol,
87

88
00:05:17,190 --> 00:05:20,340
So, this is a carat symbol or an exponent symbol.
88

89
00:05:20,820 --> 00:05:22,870
And let's use the letter
89

90
00:05:22,940 --> 00:05:26,600
let's say 's', or 'r', or 'a', whatever.
90

91
00:05:26,610 --> 00:05:29,980
So, now let's see how this scanf behaves.
91

92
00:05:30,000 --> 00:05:31,440
Let's run.
92

93
00:05:31,530 --> 00:05:33,670
So, I'm going to enter some string.
93

94
00:05:33,690 --> 00:05:36,980
So, let's say, hello how are you
94

95
00:05:37,020 --> 00:05:40,120
and my name is ram.
95

96
00:05:40,170 --> 00:05:41,960
So, now look at the output.
96

97
00:05:41,970 --> 00:05:48,120
The scanf actually read all these characters until it met with the letter s.
97

98
00:05:48,540 --> 00:05:49,990
So, according to the scan set.
98

99
00:05:50,100 --> 00:05:57,570
So, the meaning of this is read till there is a letter s. So, read all the input characters until there is
99

100
00:05:57,570 --> 00:06:04,610
a letter s. So, that's why, scanf read everything up to the occurrence of the first s, isn't it?
100

101
00:06:04,680 --> 00:06:05,880
that is here.
101

102
00:06:05,910 --> 00:06:07,950
After that everything is ignored.
102

103
00:06:07,950 --> 00:06:09,760
That's why you got this result.
103

104
00:06:09,780 --> 00:06:12,950
Now apply this logic to \n.
104

105
00:06:13,050 --> 00:06:18,310
So, that means read all the characters until there is a new line character.
105

106
00:06:19,320 --> 00:06:24,000
So, that's why, if you give this scan set, then scanf would read all the characters.
106

107
00:06:24,010 --> 00:06:27,600
So, doesn't matter whether it is a space character or something else,
107

108
00:06:27,630 --> 00:06:32,730
so until the scanf encounters the new line character it reads everything.
108

109
00:06:32,820 --> 00:06:33,600
Right?
109

110
00:06:33,600 --> 00:06:37,380
So, that's why, we can use this technique of scan set.
110

111
00:06:37,410 --> 00:06:39,210
Let's try.
111

112
00:06:39,240 --> 00:06:40,380
Here we can see that,
112

113
00:06:40,470 --> 00:06:41,000
hello
113

114
00:06:41,010 --> 00:06:43,260
how are you
114

115
00:06:43,260 --> 00:06:45,000
my name is kiran.
115

116
00:06:45,150 --> 00:06:49,380
So, I don't know whether it crosses this array bound or not, you should be careful.
116

117
00:06:49,860 --> 00:06:51,120
So, now this works.
117

118
00:06:51,120 --> 00:06:57,840
Now we can see that, everything is read into one single array, isn't it? using the technique of scan
118

119
00:06:57,840 --> 00:06:58,170
set.

The program must maintain records of 10 students and you should give below features to your program.
4

5
00:00:14,670 --> 00:00:21,510
So, you should be able to display all the records, you should give the feature for adding a new record,
5

6
00:00:21,870 --> 00:00:22,950
delete a record.
6

7
00:00:22,980 --> 00:00:26,160
So, all these provisions you should give to your application.
7

8
00:00:26,160 --> 00:00:33,870
The program also should avoid or alert about the below situations. Like duplication of a record, no space
8

9
00:00:33,870 --> 00:00:36,960
to add a new record, deleting an unknown record.
9

10
00:00:37,020 --> 00:00:39,300
So, these cases should be taken care.
10

11
00:00:39,300 --> 00:00:40,800
Let me show you some demo,
11

12

you will get some hints.

15
00:00:47,070 --> 00:00:48,540
you need not to worry too much.
15

00:00:48,540 --> 00:00:51,750

16

17

17

add, delete, and exit the application. Let's say user enters the menu code 1.

22

23
00:01:10,650 --> 00:01:12,770
so that is actually deleting a record.

Enter the roll number of the student,

let me add one roll number.

28

let me add 6789,

Let me add 7 and the data of

31

00:02:28,440 --> 00:02:31,710
So, it's a very simple application show you should be able to do. So,

but let me give you some hints.

51
00:02:33,360 --> 00:02:39,620
So, for this application you should be using a structure typedef structure, if you want to maintain a
51

52
00:02:39,620 --> 00:02:45,780
student record. A student records should contain the below information. Like a roll number as an integer,
52

53

a some char arrays for name branch and data of birth, and some integer to store semister information or whatever
53

54
00:02:52,260 --> 00:02:55,230
information, you are free to add more information.
54

55
00:02:55,230 --> 00:02:59,470
So, reserve space to hold 10 students records.
55

56
00:02:59,550 --> 00:03:01,140
So, how do you reserve the space?
56

57

there are various methods like using dynamic memory allocation and other things.
57

58
00:03:05,430 --> 00:03:07,620
So, but we have not yet covered those things.
58

59
00:03:07,620 --> 00:03:12,780
So, we should be using a global array of structure which is something like this. Very simple.
59

60
00:03:12,780 --> 00:03:15,380
So, you have int a[10].

61
00:03:15,390 --> 00:03:16,600
So, if you have this,
61

62
00:03:16,620 --> 00:03:18,480
so this is a integer array.

63
00:03:18,510 --> 00:03:19,050
Right?

64
00:03:19,050 --> 00:03:19,680
So, similarly
64

65

this is a structure array, that's it. Instead of integer
65

66
00:03:22,620 --> 00:03:28,680
you have your user defined data type that is this structure, isn't it? You should maintain this array
66

67
00:03:28,680 --> 00:03:30,370
in global space.
67

68
00:03:30,460 --> 00:03:33,400
So, this is how a structure array looks like.

69
00:03:33,400 --> 00:03:38,680
So, just compare this with an integer array or a char array.So, there is nothing special about this.
69

70
00:03:38,770 --> 00:03:44,380

70

71
00:03:44,410 --> 00:03:48,220
and this is able to hold another student record, like that.

72

72

73
00:03:54,880 --> 00:03:56,200
address of the array.
73

74

74

75
00:04:03,340 --> 00:04:03,870
it?
75

76
00:04:03,880 --> 00:04:06,760
So, that's why, it will be pointing here like that.

77
00:04:06,760 --> 00:04:13,450
So, here students is a variable of type STUDENT_INFO_t *.

78
00:04:13,720 --> 00:04:15,940
Let me talk about how you should add a record.
78

79
00:04:16,240 --> 00:04:18,370
So, this is just my rules,
79

80

you can implement your own rules.
80

81
00:04:20,230 --> 00:04:22,950
There is absolutely no reason why you should follow this method.
81

82
00:04:22,960 --> 00:04:26,040
You can implement the way you like. But,
82

83
00:04:26,230 --> 00:04:29,340
so let me explain how the code is implemented.
83

84
00:04:29,410 --> 00:04:31,510
So, but let me give you some hints.
84

85
00:04:31,540 --> 00:04:35,830
First find out the empty element in an array. Empty element you have to find out.
85

86
00:04:36,130 --> 00:04:43,120
So, I'll call it as an empty node. So, whether a node in an array is empty or not is decided by the roll
86

87
00:04:43,120 --> 00:04:44,310
number field.
87

88
00:04:44,380 --> 00:04:46,230
So, if the roll number is 0,
88

89
00:04:46,240 --> 00:04:51,010
consider that node is empty and a new record can be added.
89

90
00:04:51,040 --> 00:04:56,520
Also the user given roll number should not match with other existing roll numbers
90

91
00:04:56,530 --> 00:05:02,190
otherwise it will be considered as duplication and record should not be added.So, that's
91

92
00:05:02,200 --> 00:05:03,490
how you add a record.
92

93
00:05:03,490 --> 00:05:09,040
How do you delete a record? So, ask the user to enter the roll number of the student. Match the user
93

94
00:05:09,040 --> 00:05:15,880
entered roll number with the existing roll numbers of the nodes. If a match is found, then zero out all the
94

95
00:05:15,880 --> 00:05:17,480
member elements of the node.
95

96
00:05:17,500 --> 00:05:22,160
So, remember that deleting means zeroing all the member elements of a node.
96

97
00:05:22,170 --> 00:05:24,040
So, that's what delete means.
97

98
00:05:24,040 --> 00:05:25,720

00:13:17,180 --> 00:13:23,480
So, for that you have to use base address of i, and then select the member element.
227

228
00:13:23,480 --> 00:13:24,280
Right?
228

229
00:13:24,320 --> 00:13:30,060
So here, why we are using dot operator? because, this entity is not a pointer
229

230
00:13:30,080 --> 00:13:32,420
remember that. This is non pointer.
230

231
00:13:32,450 --> 00:13:35,070
So, that's a reason why we should use dot operator here.
231

232
00:13:35,450 --> 00:13:42,560
So, remember from our earlier array pointer discussion, when we had a uint8 * and the pointer we
232

233
00:13:42,560 --> 00:13:48,050
actually accessed different array elements using this notation, right?
233

234
00:13:48,050 --> 00:13:53,270
So, that same logic you can also leverage for structure pointers as well.
234

235
00:13:53,660 --> 00:13:55,340
So, what is this one actually?
235

236
00:13:55,340 --> 00:14:01,100
So, can you recall from your array discussion? This is nothing but I can rewrite something like this.
236

237
00:14:01,100 --> 00:14:10,450
So, this is actually equivalent to this code, right? * of code +i, right?
237

238
00:14:11,020 --> 00:14:14,120
So, this is actually equivalent to this one, right?
238

239
00:14:14,140 --> 00:14:18,010
Just recall this from our earlier array manipulation methods.
239

240
00:14:18,010 --> 00:14:18,850
Right?
240

241
00:14:18,850 --> 00:14:24,850
So, that's why, I can replace this here. I can do this.So,
241

242
00:14:24,890 --> 00:14:27,260
but you cannot simply do like this,
242

243
00:14:27,300 --> 00:14:31,470
you have to give one more parenthesis here, right?
243

244
00:14:31,480 --> 00:14:33,610
So, take a look into this one.
244

245
00:14:33,620 --> 00:14:36,610
So, this is equivalent to this one.
245

246
00:14:36,640 --> 00:14:38,390
So, this is just for your information,
246

247
00:14:38,410 --> 00:14:44,380
so you need not to write something like this because, this looks very confusing and even though this syntax
247

248
00:14:44,380 --> 00:14:46,870
is correct, it doesn't make people comfortable.
248

249
00:14:46,870 --> 00:14:47,140
Right?
249

250
00:14:47,140 --> 00:14:54,790
So, that's why, let's use the easiest method that is just take the base address of i,  and use this with
250

251
00:14:54,790 --> 00:14:55,880
a dot operator.
251

252
00:14:55,960 --> 00:15:01,660
That would be the very easiest method instead of doing all these things incrementing steps and all.
252

253
00:15:02,100 --> 00:15:04,760
So, this would be the best method I guess.So,
253

254
00:15:04,890 --> 00:15:08,550
but all the other cases what we discussed are equivalent codes,
254

255
00:15:08,560 --> 00:15:15,630
they are one and the same.So, that's why, we would be manipulating different a structure array elements
255

256
00:15:15,750 --> 00:15:17,610
using this method.
256

257
00:15:17,610 --> 00:15:20,340
So, when a structure pointer is given?
257

258
00:15:20,340 --> 00:15:22,200
So, now let's check this code,
258

259
00:15:22,260 --> 00:15:25,260
let's run once again, right?
259

260
00:15:25,290 --> 00:15:27,390
So, this is also same no problem.
260

261
00:15:27,390 --> 00:15:28,080
Fair enough.
261

262
00:15:28,080 --> 00:15:33,780
So, why I explain all these things? Because, you'd be doing something similar in the project which you
262

263
00:15:33,780 --> 00:15:35,160
are going to implement next.
263

264
00:15:35,160 --> 00:15:37,920
Now you can start implementing this program.
264

265
00:15:37,980 --> 00:15:43,400
So, take a look into the resource section of this lecture. I have attached 2 files for you.
265

266
00:15:43,500 --> 00:15:50,070
One  is a starter code. So, the starter file is implemented partially and I have given various to do's
266

267
00:15:50,070 --> 00:15:57,210
for you, so you can refer to the starter code only if you are not confident about a coding this program.

268

If you need some initial hints you can refer to the starter code.

Otherwise, need not to refer it,

you can state away of start coding.

And the second file is a complete solution, which you can refer and you can compare with your logic. And

the logic can be anything you need not to follow what instructor has said. You can implement your own

ideas no problem.
