section 31----Pre-Processor directives in 'C'


video 1

0
1
00:00:00,120 --> 00:00:01,590
Hey, welcome back to the course.
1

2
00:00:01,590 --> 00:00:08,450
So, in this section we're going to study about a pre-processor directives. So, in 'C' programming pre-processor
2

3
00:00:08,490 --> 00:00:15,480
directives are used to affect compile time settings of the program. So, pre-processor directives are 
3

4
00:00:15,480 --> 00:00:23,340
also used to create macros which are used as a textual replacement for numbers and other things.
4

5
00:00:23,340 --> 00:00:31,860
So, pre-processor  directives begin with the symbol #. For example, # include, #define, # if, #
5

6
00:00:31,920 --> 00:00:33,420
else if like that.
6

7
00:00:33,480 --> 00:00:35,530
So, those are different types of  pre-processor
7

8
00:00:35,560 --> 00:00:38,650
directives, supported by the 'C' programming language.
8

9
00:00:38,670 --> 00:00:45,660
So, in all your program you actually used these pre-processor directive, at least you actually used #include. 
9

10
00:00:45,930 --> 00:00:52,590
So,# include is one type of pre-processor directive, which actually instructs the compiler to do the
10

11
00:00:52,590 --> 00:00:54,130
file inclusion, isn't it?
11

12
00:00:54,330 --> 00:01:01,560
So, pre-processor directives are resolved or taken cared during the pre-processing stage of compilation.
12

13
00:01:01,560 --> 00:01:08,640
So, you have to remember that. And pre-processor directives they actually direct the compiler to take
13

14
00:01:08,640 --> 00:01:11,660
certain actions during compile time.
14

15
00:01:11,670 --> 00:01:16,620
So, now let's see what are the pre-processor directive supported by the 'C' programming language. We most
15

16
00:01:16,620 --> 00:01:24,230
commonly use pre-processor directives while we write macros and when we want to do file inclusion.
16

17
00:01:24,270 --> 00:01:29,330
So, if you want to write macros in your program, so then you have to use # define.
17

18
00:01:29,490 --> 00:01:35,490
So, macros are nothing but they are just textual replacement for numbers and other things, which you are
18

19
00:01:35,490 --> 00:01:36,240
going to see later.
19

20
00:01:36,240 --> 00:01:43,710
So, a #define is one type of pre-processor directive and it is used to write macros in 'C'. And after that,
20

21
00:01:43,810 --> 00:01:50,040
#include is another type of pre-processor directive supported by 'C', which is used for file
21

22
00:01:50,040 --> 00:01:54,270
inclusion.So, a file may be header file or any other file
22

23
00:01:54,300 --> 00:02:00,570
you can include standard library header file or user defined file etc.. And there are other pre-processor
23

24
00:02:00,720 --> 00:02:03,990
directives which are used for conditional compilation.
24

25
00:02:03,990 --> 00:02:10,550
So, examples are #ifdef, endif, if, else, if not defined (ifndef), undefined (undef) etc..
25

26
00:02:10,680 --> 00:02:16,860
So, these are used to direct the compiler about code compilation. And there are other pre-processor
26

27
00:02:16,860 --> 00:02:19,800
directive such as pragmas and error.
27

28
00:02:20,050 --> 00:02:28,200
Let's move forward. First let's explore about macros or #define.So, macros are written in 'C' using
28

29
00:02:28,200 --> 00:02:35,250
#define pre-processor directive. So, macros are used for textual replacement in the code, commonly used
29

30
00:02:35,250 --> 00:02:37,130
to define constants.
30

31
00:02:37,260 --> 00:02:39,700
So, this is a syntax of a macro.
31

32
00:02:39,840 --> 00:02:43,650
So, it should start with a pre-processor directive #define,
32

33
00:02:43,710 --> 00:02:46,680
this is a identifier and this is a value,
33

34
00:02:46,680 --> 00:02:53,880
and please note that there is a space between this pre-processor directive and the identifier and there is a space
34

35
00:02:53,880 --> 00:02:59,560
between identifier and a value. For example, #define MAX_RECORD 10.
35

36
00:02:59,580 --> 00:03:04,050
So, here this is actually a textual replacement for this number
36

37
00:03:04,050 --> 00:03:07,260
in the program. That's it. Now let's consider one simple use case.
37

38
00:03:07,260 --> 00:03:08,900
So, here is a program,
38

39
00:03:09,000 --> 00:03:13,340
so here there is a variable which is compared with a constant, isn't it?
39

40
00:03:13,470 --> 00:03:21,500
You can see that, this expression is written here using a macro, and this macro is defined here
40

41
00:03:21,590 --> 00:03:24,230
#define MIN_AGE 18.
41

42
00:03:24,230 --> 00:03:27,650
So, during the pre-processing stage of the compilation,
42

43
00:03:27,710 --> 00:03:34,500
so this identifier will be replaced by this number by the pre-processing engine of the compiler.
43

44
00:03:34,970 --> 00:03:38,370
So, but what is the advantage of using such macros?
44

45
00:03:38,450 --> 00:03:46,040
Now the one important advantage is, a let's say you used this a constant at several places in the program.
45

46
00:03:46,330 --> 00:03:51,980
And let's say tomorrow the minimum age changes. So, let's say it becomes 20.
46

47
00:03:52,010 --> 00:03:54,970
So then, if you don't use these macros,
47

48
00:03:55,010 --> 00:03:56,480
so then what you'd be doing?
48

49
00:03:56,480 --> 00:04:00,160
So, you'd be changing program at various places, isn't it?
49

50
00:04:00,200 --> 00:04:03,730
Suppose, if this constant is used at several places.
50

51
00:04:03,770 --> 00:04:04,110
Right?
51

52
00:04:04,760 --> 00:04:07,050
So, but in this case what happens is,
52

53
00:04:07,070 --> 00:04:11,230
so you have used a textual replacement, right? by using a macro.
53

54
00:04:11,360 --> 00:04:15,080
So, you have to change in only one place that is here.
54

55
00:04:15,140 --> 00:04:19,630
So, if tomorrow minimum age becomes 20, then change this to 20 that's it.
55

56
00:04:19,640 --> 00:04:21,350
So, everywhere it will take effect.
56

57
00:04:21,380 --> 00:04:22,070
Right?
57

58
00:04:22,100 --> 00:04:28,850
So, that's one of the important use case of #define macros. So, note that during pre-processing stage of
58

59
00:04:28,850 --> 00:04:34,880
the compilation process, macro names or identifiers are replaced by the associated values inside the
59

60
00:04:34,880 --> 00:04:38,700
program. And you should also note that this is not a variable,
60

61
00:04:38,720 --> 00:04:41,710
this is an identifier or a macro name.
61

62
00:04:41,750 --> 00:04:45,700
So, this is just a textual replacement for a number. That's it.
62

63
00:04:45,770 --> 00:04:47,300
Let's proceed.
63

64
00:04:47,300 --> 00:04:54,470
So, in Embedded system programming, we use lots of 'C' macros to define pin numbers, pin values, crystal speed,
64

65
00:04:54,560 --> 00:05:00,160
peripheral register addresses, memory addresses and for various other configuration values.
65

66
00:05:00,170 --> 00:05:01,840
So, these are some of the examples.
66

67
00:05:01,880 --> 00:05:08,210
And here you can see that, a macro names are actually written using uppercase letters, right?
67

68
00:05:08,210 --> 00:05:10,390
So, that is actually a convention.
68

69
00:05:10,550 --> 00:05:14,990
So, you can either use a capital letters or lowercase letters,
69

70
00:05:14,990 --> 00:05:19,300
it doesn't matter actually. But we usually use a capital letters
70

71
00:05:19,440 --> 00:05:25,040
so distinguish between a variable name and a macro. Because, variable names we don't write in capital
71

72
00:05:25,040 --> 00:05:25,670
letters, right?
72

73
00:05:25,790 --> 00:05:31,040
So, we always use a combination of capital and small letters or something like that.
73

74
00:05:31,070 --> 00:05:35,720
So, if you use all capital letters for the macros, then you can easily distinguish between a variable
74

75
00:05:35,780 --> 00:05:42,630
and a macro name. And you should also note that, that is no semicolon to terminate this statement.
75

76
00:05:42,710 --> 00:05:46,570
You should not give any semicolons. And take this example here.
76

77
00:05:46,580 --> 00:05:54,590
So here, crystal speed is 8 mega, right? and it is terminated with a letter UL. So, UL means unsigned
77

78
00:05:54,590 --> 00:05:55,840
long, so that is allowed.
78

79
00:05:55,850 --> 00:05:57,470
So, you can use those characters.
79

80
00:05:57,590 --> 00:06:03,710
So, with the number to tell the compiler that this number is of type
80

81
00:06:03,710 --> 00:06:04,760
Unsigned long.
81

82
00:06:04,820 --> 00:06:11,020
So, compiler considers this macro as a unsigned long value. Let's move forward.
82

83
00:06:11,030 --> 00:06:17,010
So, now let's learn about function like macros. To define a function like macro,
83

84
00:06:17,030 --> 00:06:23,930
use the same '#define' directive, but put a pair of parentheses immediately after the macro name.
84

85
00:06:24,050 --> 00:06:25,550
So, now let's see an example.
85

86
00:06:25,550 --> 00:06:28,610
So, here this is a normal macro definition
86

87
00:06:28,610 --> 00:06:32,180
and now this is actually a function like macro.
87

88
00:06:32,180 --> 00:06:34,110
So, this is a macro name
88

89
00:06:34,250 --> 00:06:35,920
and this is a value.
89

90
00:06:35,960 --> 00:06:37,460
And here we can see that,
90

91
00:06:37,460 --> 00:06:45,800
So this is a function like macro, because, so we have used a parentheses and that is actually one argument
91

92
00:06:45,890 --> 00:06:51,890
'r', and that 'r' is used for the expression of the value.
92

93
00:06:51,920 --> 00:06:58,100
So, this is a value here. And the value contains one more macro here, that is PI_VALUE which has to be
93

94
00:06:58,100 --> 00:07:07,090
defined before this. So, lets say you want to calculate the area of a circle where the radius is given.
94

95
00:07:07,110 --> 00:07:08,640
So, area of circle
95

96
00:07:08,650 --> 00:07:10,590
so this is a variable =
96

97
00:07:10,980 --> 00:07:17,930
So, now you can make use of this macro now, AREA_OF_CIRCLE and you can pass any argument here. Like  
97

98
00:07:17,930 --> 00:07:20,530
another variable or any number.
98

99
00:07:20,550 --> 00:07:22,600
Now this is a 'C' statement, right?
99

100
00:07:22,620 --> 00:07:28,590
So, now this original 'C' statement will be converted into this 'C' statement by the pre-processor engine
100

101
00:07:28,620 --> 00:07:29,660
of the compilation.
101

102
00:07:29,850 --> 00:07:32,020
So, it happens in 2 stages actually.
102

103
00:07:32,070 --> 00:07:36,200
First what happens is this one. So, area of circle is equal to
103

104
00:07:36,210 --> 00:07:39,060
so this is replaced by the value.
104

105
00:07:39,120 --> 00:07:41,940
So, that's why, PI_VALUE multiplied by
105

106
00:07:41,940 --> 00:07:45,210
so here you are passing radius to this, right?
106

107
00:07:45,240 --> 00:07:47,790
So, r will be replaced by the radius.
107

108
00:07:47,820 --> 00:07:51,220
So, PI_VALUE multiplied by radius, multiplied by radius.
108

109
00:07:51,240 --> 00:07:58,010
And after that, this statement will be converted into this statement where this PI_VALUE macro will resolved.
109

110
00:07:58,020 --> 00:08:01,410
This identifier will be replaced by its value,
110

111
00:08:01,500 --> 00:08:02,930
that is this one, right?
111

112
00:08:02,940 --> 00:08:08,550
So, finally this expression actually converted into this expression by the pre-processing stage of the
112

113
00:08:08,550 --> 00:08:09,790
compilation.
113

114
00:08:09,840 --> 00:08:17,020
So, whenever you are evaluating a macros you should always think in terms of replacement.
114

115
00:08:17,130 --> 00:08:26,940
For example, in this case, this macro name is replaced by its associated value, that is this one. Exactly
115

116
00:08:26,940 --> 00:08:29,130
the way you have written the value.
116

117
00:08:29,130 --> 00:08:37,080
So, during pre-processing this disappears and this appears. And after that, this disappears and it will
117

118
00:08:37,080 --> 00:08:40,130
be replaced by its associated value.
118

119
00:08:40,260 --> 00:08:43,760
So, that's why, always you should think in terms of replacement.
119

120
00:08:43,950 --> 00:08:45,380
Let's move forward.
120

121
00:08:45,420 --> 00:08:46,350
This is very important.
121

122
00:08:46,380 --> 00:08:51,190
So, this macro is poorly written and it is dangerous.
122

123
00:08:51,410 --> 00:08:54,850
You should not write a function like macro like this.
123

124
00:08:55,040 --> 00:08:56,470
Why is that? So,
124

125
00:08:56,690 --> 00:09:01,330
please note that, you have to be careful with macro 'values'.
125

126
00:09:01,360 --> 00:09:07,700
So, this is a macro value, when you are doing some "operations" using multiple "operands".
126

127
00:09:07,700 --> 00:09:09,270
Consider this value.
127

128
00:09:09,320 --> 00:09:14,700
So, this value is a combination of operators and operands, right?
128

129
00:09:14,720 --> 00:09:20,980
So, when the value is combination of operands and operators you have to be careful.
129

130
00:09:21,020 --> 00:09:23,010
You should not write something like this.
130

131
00:09:23,090 --> 00:09:24,230
What is the solution then?
131

132
00:09:24,530 --> 00:09:28,550
So, before I talk about solution now let's see why it is bad.
132

133
00:09:28,760 --> 00:09:35,680
So,we will write one small piece of code to understand why exactly this is poorly written and how it is dangerous.
133

134
00:09:35,900 --> 00:09:42,380
So, let's implement a small code. So, now let's write a program to calculate area of a circle.
134

135
00:09:42,830 --> 00:09:51,490
So, now let me create a variable, let's say int  area_circle. And I'm going to use function like macro.
135

136
00:09:51,580 --> 00:09:53,530
First let me write the macro.
136

137
00:09:53,530 --> 00:09:54,830
So, #define.
137

138
00:09:54,830 --> 00:09:59,670
So, let me use PI_VALUE as 3.1415
138

139
00:10:00,040 --> 00:10:07,540
and let's consider this as a float. So, you can give 'f' or small 'f' here. To tell the compiler that this
139

140
00:10:07,540 --> 00:10:08,770
is actually float,
140

141
00:10:08,770 --> 00:10:09,790
otherwise compiler will
141

142
00:10:09,790 --> 00:10:13,240
treat this as a double. What you can do is,
142

143
00:10:13,240 --> 00:10:17,500
so, now let's write one more macro to calculate area of a circle.
143

144
00:10:18,060 --> 00:10:26,270
So, let me call it as AREA_OF_CIRCLE() and this macro takes an argument, let's call it as x.
144

145
00:10:26,350 --> 00:10:32,560
Let's write the value now, PI_VALUE multiplied by x, multiplied by x, isn't it?
145

146
00:10:32,560 --> 00:10:36,340
So, now let's call this macro.
146

147
00:10:36,400 --> 00:10:44,830
So, now let's make use of this macro and let me pass a number let's say 2. And after that, let me print
147

148
00:10:44,830 --> 00:10:45,530
the result.
148

149
00:10:45,550 --> 00:10:49,120
So, I would consider this as float. Very simple.
149

150
00:10:49,120 --> 00:10:50,380
Let's compile and run.
150

151
00:10:50,410 --> 00:10:53,220
So, you got the answer 12.566.
151

152
00:10:53,230 --> 00:10:54,490
That's correct.
152

153
00:10:54,490 --> 00:11:00,530
So, now what I do is, instead of passing 2 here I pass something like this 1 + 1.
153

154
00:11:00,530 --> 00:11:03,670
So, people may use your macro in various ways, isn't it?
154

155
00:11:03,760 --> 00:11:06,760
So, you never know how user is going to use your macro.
155

156
00:11:06,910 --> 00:11:09,400
So, let's say I use something like this 1 + 1.
156

157
00:11:09,600 --> 00:11:10,940
So, answer must be same, isn't it?
157

158
00:11:11,260 --> 00:11:13,450
So, the answer changes, right?
158

159
00:11:13,480 --> 00:11:15,560
So, that means something is wrong with the macro.
159

160
00:11:15,580 --> 00:11:16,350
Right?
160

161
00:11:16,360 --> 00:11:18,620
So, what happens when we do this?
161

162
00:11:18,760 --> 00:11:20,050
There is a replacement, right?
162

163
00:11:20,050 --> 00:11:22,390
So, this is replaced by this one, right?
163

164
00:11:22,510 --> 00:11:24,070
So, let's do that.
164

165
00:11:24,070 --> 00:11:28,260
So, and x is replaced by 1+1, right? right.
165

166
00:11:28,290 --> 00:11:30,960
So, the final code looks something like this
166

167
00:11:30,960 --> 00:11:33,530
and this is replaced by this one.
167

168
00:11:33,550 --> 00:11:38,530
So, now here this expression will be evaluated according to the precedents rule.
168

169
00:11:38,730 --> 00:11:43,050
So, that's why, the result what you get here is actually an error.
169

170
00:11:43,050 --> 00:11:43,900
Right?
170

171
00:11:43,920 --> 00:11:45,240
So, this is a wrong result.
171

172
00:11:45,810 --> 00:11:47,570
So, that's what I wanted to say
172

173
00:11:47,590 --> 00:11:48,660
"Never write
173

174
00:11:48,660 --> 00:11:50,880
value like this for a macro".
174

175
00:11:50,880 --> 00:11:54,440
So, you have to write something like this.
175

176
00:11:54,570 --> 00:12:01,730
So, you have to use parentheses generously. So, use parentheses for every operand. For example,
176

177
00:12:01,740 --> 00:12:06,930
this is one operand of this operator. So, use parentheses here, use parentheses for this,
177

178
00:12:06,930 --> 00:12:14,250
use parentheses for this, and then use one more parentheses to hold all these other components of the
178

179
00:12:14,250 --> 00:12:15,140
value.
179

180
00:12:15,150 --> 00:12:16,620
So, this is a correct method
180

181
00:12:16,770 --> 00:12:18,700
and it's a safest method. 
181

182
00:12:18,770 --> 00:12:20,040
Let's do that.
182

183
00:12:20,040 --> 00:12:26,050
So, now let me get back to my original code. So, here
183

184
00:12:26,090 --> 00:12:33,320
let's use first the outer parentheses and after that you should guard this individual component using
184

185
00:12:33,530 --> 00:12:36,120
there own parentheses like this.
185

186
00:12:36,320 --> 00:12:37,540
Right?
186

187
00:12:37,550 --> 00:12:41,730
And here also this must be guarded by it's own parentheses.
187

188
00:12:41,780 --> 00:12:42,050
Right?
188

189
00:12:42,050 --> 00:12:44,420
So, this is a best way to write a macro.
189

190
00:12:44,840 --> 00:12:48,530
Let's check this. Right?
190

191
00:12:48,530 --> 00:12:50,320
You get the correct answer.
191

192
00:12:50,660 --> 00:12:56,660
So, that's why, best practices while writing macros in 'C' use meaningful macro names.
192

193
00:12:56,690 --> 00:13:02,730
It's recommended that you use uppercase letters for macro names to distinguish them from variables.
193

194
00:13:02,750 --> 00:13:06,090
Remember, macro names are not variables.
194

195
00:13:06,140 --> 00:13:12,800
They are labels or identifiers, and they don't consume any code space or ram space during compile time
195

196
00:13:12,920 --> 00:13:14,390
or runtime of the program.
196

197
00:13:14,420 --> 00:13:18,150
So, those are just labels or identifiers.
197

198
00:13:18,170 --> 00:13:25,220
So, make sure that parentheses surround the macro value. So, while using function-like macros or when you
198

199
00:13:25,220 --> 00:13:31,910
are using macros along with any operators, always surround the operands with parentheses. In the next
199

200
00:13:31,910 --> 00:13:37,970
lecture, I'm going to talk about conditional compilation using pre-processor directives. And I will see
200

201
00:13:37,970 --> 00:13:38,980
you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


video 2

0
1
00:00:00,150 --> 00:00:06,010
In this lecture, let's understand about conditional compilation using pre-processor directives.
1

2
00:00:06,020 --> 00:00:07,530
Now let's begin.
2

3
00:00:07,530 --> 00:00:10,520
So, there are various conditional compilation directives
3

4
00:00:10,520 --> 00:00:13,990
available in 'C' programming language.
4

5
00:00:14,040 --> 00:00:18,300
So, these are some of the conditional compilation directives.
5

6
00:00:18,300 --> 00:00:26,280
Now, these directives help you to include or exclude individual code blocks based on various conditions
6

7
00:00:26,280 --> 00:00:27,400
set in the program.
7

8
00:00:28,270 --> 00:00:33,660
So, now let's explore how to use these conditional compilation directives.
8

9
00:00:33,670 --> 00:00:38,650
Now, let's first start with #if and #endif directives.
9

10
00:00:38,650 --> 00:00:43,750
This is a syntax how you should use a #if and #endif directives.
10

11
00:00:44,530 --> 00:00:48,650
So, you have to write #if followed by a constant expression.
11

12
00:00:48,670 --> 00:00:56,110
So, this must be a number or an integer. So, like 0 or non-zero value. Here is an example.
12

13
00:00:57,120 --> 00:00:58,970
So, #if 0,
13

14
00:00:58,980 --> 00:01:06,720
so here you can write your code block, and scope of this if is marked or decided by the following
14

15
00:01:06,730 --> 00:01:10,350
endif. That's why an endif always should follow a
15

16
00:01:10,350 --> 00:01:18,000
if. So, please note that. So, this directive checks whether the constant expression is zero or non-zero
16

17
00:01:18,000 --> 00:01:19,030
value.
17

18
00:01:19,260 --> 00:01:25,500
So, if constant is 0, then the code block that is this code block will not be included for the code
18

19
00:01:25,500 --> 00:01:26,370
compilation
19

20
00:01:26,370 --> 00:01:30,860
remember that. So, it will be excluded from the compilation itself.
20

21
00:01:31,980 --> 00:01:38,940
So, that means these pre-processor directives will be handled by the pre-processor stage of the compilation.
21

22
00:01:38,940 --> 00:01:42,750
So, pre-processor will remove this code in this case.
22

23
00:01:42,750 --> 00:01:43,070
Why?
23

24
00:01:43,080 --> 00:01:44,930
Because, this is 0, isn't it?
24

25
00:01:45,720 --> 00:01:51,190
So, if constant is non-zero the code block will be included for the code compilation.
25

26
00:01:51,300 --> 00:01:57,190
And remember that, endif directive marks the end of scope of #if or
26

27
00:01:57,210 --> 00:02:01,590
#ifdef, or if not def (ifndef), else, elif directives.
27

28
00:02:01,950 --> 00:02:03,480
So, more on that will see later.
28

29
00:02:04,380 --> 00:02:11,910
So, please don't confuse this if with your decision making if statement, both are entirely different.
29

30
00:02:12,180 --> 00:02:16,010
So, this is conditional compilation directive.
30

31
00:02:16,290 --> 00:02:24,090
So, that is a 'C' statement. So, that if what we used in decision making is a if statement. So, that is checked
31

32
00:02:24,210 --> 00:02:31,080
during runtime of the program. But this is checked during pre-processor stage of the compilation, whether
32

33
00:02:31,080 --> 00:02:36,110
to include a code block or not in the final executable of the program.
33

34
00:02:36,930 --> 00:02:42,740
So, in this case, this code block will not be the part of final executable of the program.
34

35
00:02:42,750 --> 00:02:44,580
Let's see how to use this in a program.
35

36
00:02:45,360 --> 00:02:49,920
So, now let's see how to use  #if and #endif in a program.
36

37
00:02:50,760 --> 00:02:53,850
So, now here in this program there are 2 code blocks.
37

38
00:02:54,330 --> 00:03:02,970
So, this code block actually computes the area of a circle and this code blog computes area of a triangle.
38

39
00:03:02,970 --> 00:03:10,260
Now, lets say, I want to exclude this code block from the compilation itself. So, I can comment it out. Right?
39

40
00:03:11,070 --> 00:03:15,790
So, I can just use multiline commenting and I can remove that step, isn't it? 
40

41
00:03:16,530 --> 00:03:23,600
But comments are generally used to give some description or to provide some information, right?
41

42
00:03:23,610 --> 00:03:27,370
So, using that to remove a code block is actually not good.
42

43
00:03:27,570 --> 00:03:32,250
And also what would you do, if you want to re-enable this code block.
43

44
00:03:32,250 --> 00:03:34,450
Again you have to remove this comments.
44

45
00:03:34,500 --> 00:03:35,560
Right?
45

46
00:03:35,580 --> 00:03:38,490
That is actually a bad idea.
46

47
00:03:38,490 --> 00:03:46,500
Instead of that you can use pre-processor directive #if. So, I can use #if  here.#if 0.
47

48
00:03:46,500 --> 00:03:54,440
A #if must be terminated by its associated endif. endif actually decides the scope of this.
48

49
00:03:54,450 --> 00:03:57,860
Now, lets say I want to disable code up to here.
49

50
00:03:57,960 --> 00:03:59,960
OK? So, that's why, I would give 
50

51
00:04:00,040 --> 00:04:01,640
endif here.
51

52
00:04:01,640 --> 00:04:04,430
So, that's how I remove a code block.
52

53
00:04:04,470 --> 00:04:09,610
So, now this code block is actually excluded from the build itself.
53

54
00:04:09,630 --> 00:04:15,870
For example, I can type some random things here. So, this would have given error before, but now you see
54

55
00:04:16,050 --> 00:04:23,540
there are no errors. So, it builds fine. Because, this is excluded from the build, right?
55

56
00:04:23,610 --> 00:04:29,490
So, now if you want to enable this code block again, very simple make this as non-zero and you are done.
56

57
00:04:30,420 --> 00:04:33,660
So, that's how you use #if and #endif.
57

58
00:04:34,410 --> 00:04:36,780
Please note that, you should remember 2 points
58

59
00:04:36,780 --> 00:04:44,280
1 is it must be a constant expression here, and this #if must be terminated by its associated endif,
59

60
00:04:44,550 --> 00:04:49,370
otherwise you will face problems. Let's say inside this code block,
60

61
00:04:49,380 --> 00:04:53,250
I want to disable this line. Again
61

62
00:04:53,260 --> 00:05:02,260
you can use nested #if's.#if 0 and endif. So, this endif belongs to this one and this endif
62

63
00:05:02,260 --> 00:05:08,340
belongs to this one. So, here this code block is removed from the build.
63

64
00:05:08,350 --> 00:05:15,680
So, now let me make it 0 and let's see how the output behaves. So, when I run this code, you can see
64

65
00:05:15,680 --> 00:05:18,340
that it directly goes here.
65

66
00:05:18,380 --> 00:05:20,820
It is not executing this one.
66

67
00:05:20,880 --> 00:05:29,610
Now let's understand #if, #else, and #endif. So, you can also use #else in between #if and
67

68
00:05:29,760 --> 00:05:33,710
#endif. This is an example here.
68

69
00:05:33,730 --> 00:05:41,650
So here, a  #if constant expression is 1. So, code block-1 will be included for the build and
69

70
00:05:41,650 --> 00:05:45,310
this code block will not be considered for the build.
70

71
00:05:45,310 --> 00:05:51,150
Suppose, if this is 0, then code block-2 will be considered for the build.
71

72
00:05:51,360 --> 00:05:58,060
Now here, if I want to use #if, #else, and #endif, so what I would do is, very simple.
72

73
00:05:58,120 --> 00:06:09,710
So, here I would remove this. if 0, #else, endif. So, here we want to execute either one of these code
73

74
00:06:09,710 --> 00:06:10,460
blocks.
74

75
00:06:10,460 --> 00:06:18,560
In that case, you can use #else directive along with #if and #endif. So here,
75

76
00:06:18,560 --> 00:06:19,510
this is false.
76

77
00:06:20,500 --> 00:06:23,790
So, that's a reason this is included for the build.
77

78
00:06:23,840 --> 00:06:27,870
Now, if this is true, so let me make this true,
78

79
00:06:27,910 --> 00:06:31,660
so now this is included and this is excluded.
79

80
00:06:31,660 --> 00:06:37,200
So, that's why, use  #else when you want to execute either one of code blocks.
80

81
00:06:37,290 --> 00:06:42,370
Now, let's explore about  #ifdef. As it name indicates
81

82
00:06:42,370 --> 00:06:44,930
so this is slightly different from #if.
82

83
00:06:45,590 --> 00:06:47,320
So, here as it name indicates
83

84
00:06:47,410 --> 00:06:54,030
so the evaluation of this pre-processor directive depends upon definition of a macro.
84

85
00:06:54,050 --> 00:07:01,840
So, that's why, it is called as  #ifdef, that is if defined. So, that's a reason we use an identifier
85

86
00:07:01,870 --> 00:07:05,640
for the evaluation, so not a constant expression.
86

87
00:07:05,680 --> 00:07:14,160
So, #ifdef, so there is a space and you should use an identifier. And as usual you have to use an endif
87

88
00:07:14,160 --> 00:07:16,480
to terminate the scope.
88

89
00:07:16,500 --> 00:07:16,740
All right.
89

90
00:07:16,770 --> 00:07:18,790
So, here is an example.
90

91
00:07:18,810 --> 00:07:27,000
So, what this tells to the pre-processor stage of the compilation is that. If this new feature macro is
91

92
00:07:27,000 --> 00:07:34,710
defined in the program, then only include this code block otherwise exclude this code block. So, that's
92

93
00:07:34,710 --> 00:07:37,150
a meaning of these statements.
93

94
00:07:37,150 --> 00:07:45,850
Now, let's see how to use this in the program. Execute triangle area calculation code block only if  
94

95
00:07:45,880 --> 00:07:49,030
AREA_TRI macro is defined in the program.
95

96
00:07:49,880 --> 00:07:53,050
Now let's see how to meet this objective.
96

97
00:07:53,050 --> 00:08:00,460
So, you have to execute the triangle area of block only if a macro AREA_TRI is defined in the
97

98
00:08:00,460 --> 00:08:01,970
program.
98

99
00:08:01,970 --> 00:08:04,130
Now let's get into the code.
99

100
00:08:04,330 --> 00:08:11,960
So, since the decision must be taken on the definition of a macro we cannot use #if, we have to use
100

101
00:08:11,960 --> 00:08:14,210
#ifdef.
101

102
00:08:14,240 --> 00:08:14,450
All right.
102

103
00:08:14,450 --> 00:08:16,610
Now let me remove this first.
103

104
00:08:16,940 --> 00:08:18,860
So, #ifdef
104

105
00:08:18,890 --> 00:08:29,350
AREA_TRI, if this is defined then only include this code block. So, endif. So, we can achieve this objective
105

106
00:08:29,430 --> 00:08:31,750
making these changes.
106

107
00:08:31,860 --> 00:08:36,780
So, here in this program this macro is not defined, right?
107

108
00:08:36,870 --> 00:08:40,360
So, that's a reason this code block is disabled.
108

109
00:08:40,470 --> 00:08:47,430
Now, if I defined that macro in the program using  #define, then you can see that, that code block
109

110
00:08:47,460 --> 00:08:54,750
is enabled. So, if you can't able to edit your source file to include this statement, you can also send
110

111
00:08:54,750 --> 00:08:58,390
this macro name as a compiler argument.
111

112
00:08:58,410 --> 00:09:08,540
So, now let's see how to do that.For that, copy this macro name, go to settings, go to properties, settings
112

113
00:09:10,140 --> 00:09:20,390
and here go to GCC compiler, pre processor, and add your symbol. OK? or macro name.
113

114
00:09:20,390 --> 00:09:21,750
All right.
114

115
00:09:21,870 --> 00:09:32,220
And then apply, yes and close. Here you can see that, this code block is automatically enabled. When you
115

116
00:09:32,220 --> 00:09:35,290
compile this code,
116

117
00:09:35,340 --> 00:09:43,940
you can see that, that macro name is passed as a compiler argument by using the option -D. So, I
117

118
00:09:43,940 --> 00:09:51,350
hope you are getting this. If you don't want to give this as a compiler argument, then you can also mention
118

119
00:09:51,350 --> 00:09:55,440
in the program itself by using  #define.
119

120
00:09:56,270 --> 00:09:58,190
Now let's define that.
120

121
00:09:58,250 --> 00:10:06,700
Now, let's see what is our next objective. Execute circle area calculation code block only if  
121

122
00:10:06,700 --> 00:10:11,390
AREA_CIR macro is defined and execute
122

123
00:10:11,460 --> 00:10:19,470
triangle area calculation code block only if so AREA_TRI macro is defined in the program.
123

124
00:10:19,680 --> 00:10:20,490
So, how do you do this?
124

125
00:10:20,490 --> 00:10:25,660
So, you have to put one conditional compilation macros here.
125

126
00:10:25,680 --> 00:10:32,160
Now here you have to make this as conditional compilation code block by using  
126

127
00:10:32,190 --> 00:10:39,690
#ifdef AREA_CIR and endif. Here you can see that this is defined, right?
127

128
00:10:39,720 --> 00:10:42,440
So, that's why, only this is enabled
128

129
00:10:42,630 --> 00:10:46,750
and since this is not defined, this is not enabled.
129

130
00:10:47,130 --> 00:10:53,010
Whenever you have a macro which is already defined in the program, but if you want to undefined that,
130

131
00:10:53,520 --> 00:11:02,070
there is one trick. You need not to disable this. What you do is, use #undef.
131

132
00:11:02,120 --> 00:11:05,020
So, now we can see that both are disabled.
132

133
00:11:05,120 --> 00:11:10,080
Let's imagine this is defined in some other file or by someone else.
133

134
00:11:10,220 --> 00:11:15,050
Still we can overwrite that using  #undef in your program.
134

135
00:11:15,080 --> 00:11:16,150
So, here this is defined,
135

136
00:11:16,160 --> 00:11:18,190
this is undefined.
136

137
00:11:18,270 --> 00:11:19,800
That's great.
137

138
00:11:19,800 --> 00:11:28,340
Now let's see one more example, the example number 3 execute circle area code block only if  
138

139
00:11:28,740 --> 00:11:32,990
AREA_TRI macro is not defined.
139

140
00:11:33,050 --> 00:11:34,740
How do you do this?
140

141
00:11:34,790 --> 00:11:45,130
So, first let me remove all these things. So, execute this code block only if AREA_TRI is not defined.
141

142
00:11:45,240 --> 00:11:52,790
So, that means if AREA_TRI is defined, then you should execute this and you should not execute this.
142

143
00:11:52,820 --> 00:11:58,610
So, in this case we can make use of #ifndef.
143

144
00:11:58,640 --> 00:12:00,750
So, here is an example.
144

145
00:12:00,800 --> 00:12:08,940
So, the meaning of this code block is, if new feature is not defined then execute this code block.
145

146
00:12:09,140 --> 00:12:19,680
So, that's why, for this use case we have to use if not def(ifndef). So, we have to use  #ifndef not def.  
146

147
00:12:19,730 --> 00:12:25,700
AREA_TRI endif. This we can guard using #ifdef.
147

148
00:12:25,900 --> 00:12:32,120
Now here we can see that, since this is not defined so this code block is enabled.
148

149
00:12:32,120 --> 00:12:39,980
So, the moment I defined this macro and this is enabled. Now you can also use #else along with  
149

150
00:12:40,010 --> 00:12:40,580
#ifdef.
150

151
00:12:41,390 --> 00:12:42,740
So, here is an example.
151

152
00:12:44,160 --> 00:12:51,260
If the NEW_FEATURE macro is really defined, then code block for new feature will be included.
152

153
00:12:51,330 --> 00:12:58,680
So, if NEW_FEATURE macro is not defined, then it is assumed that program should include the code block
153

154
00:12:58,680 --> 00:13:00,420
for old feature.
154

155
00:13:00,690 --> 00:13:07,270
So, that's why, if this is not defined then this code block will be included for the build.
155

156
00:13:07,330 --> 00:13:12,810
Alright. So, in the next lecture let's learn about #if and 'defined' operator.
156

157
00:13:12,900 --> 00:13:14,450
Okay. I'll see you in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

video 3

0
1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:01,350
Hey, welcome back to the lecture.
1

2
00:00:01,380 --> 00:00:08,210
So, now in this lecture let's understand about the 'defined' operator. So, this 'defined' operator is used
2

3
00:00:08,300 --> 00:00:14,900
more frequently with the #if pre-processor directive. The defined operator is used when you want to
3

4
00:00:14,900 --> 00:00:25,840
check definitions of multiple macros using single #if, or  #ifdef, or #ifndef directives.
4

5
00:00:26,060 --> 00:00:32,440
So, you can also use 'C' logical operators such as AND, NOT, OR with 'defined' operator.
5

6
00:00:32,450 --> 00:00:35,710
Now let's see how to use the 'defined' operator.
6

7
00:00:35,740 --> 00:00:40,130
Now, let's say in the program I have a printf statement here.
7

8
00:00:40,280 --> 00:00:46,220
So, the printf says this is the area calculation program for both circle and triangle.
8

9
00:00:47,030 --> 00:00:53,180
But, I want to include this printf statement only if both the macros are defined,
9

10
00:00:53,180 --> 00:00:55,670
that is AREA_CIR and AREA_TRI.
10

11
00:00:56,630 --> 00:00:59,000
Now, how do you meet this objective? 
11

12
00:00:59,030 --> 00:01:06,860
Now let's assume that both macros are defined #define AREA_CIR and #define AREA_TRI.
12

13
00:01:07,690 --> 00:01:13,540
So, only in this case, I should print this. So, I can quickly write something like this.  
13

14
00:01:13,670 --> 00:01:23,360
#ifdef AREA_CIR and use one more #ifdef AREA_TRI, endif and another endif. So, you can also indent
14

15
00:01:23,360 --> 00:01:24,340
the code here.
15

16
00:01:24,480 --> 00:01:24,770
Alright.
16

17
00:01:24,770 --> 00:01:26,320
Like this.
17

18
00:01:26,390 --> 00:01:33,530
So, here since both these macros are defined, a this will be included.  If I disable this one,
18

19
00:01:33,530 --> 00:01:36,960
now here you can see that, this will not be included for the build. 
19

20
00:01:37,160 --> 00:01:45,680
So, instead of doing a multiple nested #ifdef's you can use defined operator with logical operator.
20

21
00:01:46,550 --> 00:01:48,800
So, what I would do is, in this case,
21

22
00:01:48,920 --> 00:01:51,210
so just watch this.
22

23
00:01:51,280 --> 00:01:59,610
So, this is how you should use the defined operator  #if defined and open the parentheses,
23

24
00:01:59,860 --> 00:02:04,790
give the macro name, && defined another macro name, and give
24

25
00:02:04,800 --> 00:02:08,570
endif. So, this looks nice, right?
25

26
00:02:08,650 --> 00:02:13,530
So, here you can evaluate multiple macros in one single line,
26

27
00:02:13,660 --> 00:02:16,610
otherwise you would have to use multiple lines, isn't it?
27

28
00:02:17,320 --> 00:02:19,900
So, observe this logical operator here.
28

29
00:02:20,320 --> 00:02:24,220
So, you can use that when you use defined operator.
29

30
00:02:24,370 --> 00:02:26,830
So, I hope you can understand this.
30

31
00:02:26,890 --> 00:02:32,960
So, most of the time defined operator is used along with  #if pre-processor directive.
31

32
00:02:32,980 --> 00:02:35,020
Now let's take our fourth example.
32

33
00:02:35,020 --> 00:02:41,840
Make sure that compilation fails if none of the macros are defined. So, you have to code in such a way
33

34
00:02:41,840 --> 00:02:45,860
that user atleast must mention either one of those macros.
34

35
00:02:46,670 --> 00:02:52,940
So, if none of the macros are mentioned, then the compilation should terminate with an error, you should
35

36
00:02:52,940 --> 00:02:55,140
send some message to the user.
36

37
00:02:55,280 --> 00:02:57,330
Let's see how to do this.
37

38
00:02:57,410 --> 00:03:04,070
So, our objective is if none of the macros are defined, then the compilation should not continue. The compilation
38

39
00:03:04,070 --> 00:03:09,490
should terminate with an error message. To terminate the compilation with an error message,
39

40
00:03:09,500 --> 00:03:16,130
we can use #error pre-processor directive. Now this is how you can meet that objective.
40

41
00:03:17,000 --> 00:03:26,370
So here, if !defined this macro && not defined this macro, then terminate the compilation with
41

42
00:03:26,370 --> 00:03:26,950
error message.
42

43
00:03:26,980 --> 00:03:35,060
So, for that, you can use #error. So, give the message "no macros defined" like that or any message,
43

44
00:03:35,450 --> 00:03:38,140
and then end this if block.
44

45
00:03:38,960 --> 00:03:44,660
So, now here we can see that since these are defined compilation will be fine, no problem.
45

46
00:03:44,690 --> 00:03:49,320
So, suppose if I undefined both these macros now let me remove that,
46

47
00:03:50,240 --> 00:03:57,080
so now here we can see that, these code blocks are disabled and the compilation will terminate saying
47

48
00:03:57,140 --> 00:04:03,950
no macros defined. And please note that there is no semicolon here, because this is a pre processor
48

49
00:04:03,970 --> 00:04:08,490
directive. So, that's about conditional compilations
49

50
00:04:08,730 --> 00:04:15,100
and you can use AND,NOT, and OR operators a with defined operator.
50

51
00:04:15,960 --> 00:04:17,760
And this is how you should use it.
51

52
00:04:18,300 --> 00:04:20,930
So, here there is no executable 'C' statement, right?
52

53
00:04:20,940 --> 00:04:26,970
So, that's why, you can also cut this code and keep it outside the function like this.
53

54
00:04:26,970 --> 00:04:28,860
This is also fine.
54

55
00:04:29,010 --> 00:04:31,310
Now let me try to build this.
55

56
00:04:31,430 --> 00:04:31,630
Yes,
56

57
00:04:31,640 --> 00:04:33,250
this also works.
57

58
00:04:33,380 --> 00:04:37,560
So, it would be better if you keep that outside the function here.
58

59
00:04:37,580 --> 00:04:40,010
So, now in this case a compilation terminates.
59

60
00:04:40,040 --> 00:04:41,270
Right?
60

61
00:04:41,300 --> 00:04:47,810
So, what if you don't want to terminate the compilation, but you just want to give a warning. In that case,
61

62
00:04:47,930 --> 00:04:55,030
instead of error you can use warning. So, this would continue with a compilation, but just with a warning,
62

63
00:04:55,030 --> 00:04:55,650
that's it.
63

64
00:04:56,470 --> 00:04:58,360
Let's compile this.
64

65
00:04:58,480 --> 00:05:03,240
Now we can see that, the exe is created but with a warning.
65

66
00:05:03,340 --> 00:05:11,080
So, with that note I would like to end this lectures on 'C' pre-processing directives and let me know if
66

67
00:05:11,080 --> 00:05:14,700
you have any questions, and I'll see you in the next lecture.

58
00:03:57,680 --> 00:04:03,270
Let's tell the compiler to consider this number as a unsigned long integer. For that,
58

59
00:04:03,270 --> 00:04:06,920
otherwise by default compiler will consider this as an integer.
59

60
00:04:06,920 --> 00:04:07,520

61

So, you can use a characters 'u' and 'l' along with a number.
61

62
00:04:12,150 --> 00:04:18,980
Now this actually tells the compiler to treat this number as unsigned long, a 32 bit value.
62

63
00:04:19,020 --> 00:04:25,470
So, either you can mention it as ul or either you can mention this in lower case letters or you can
63

64
00:04:25,470 --> 00:04:27,090
use uppercase letters.
64

65
00:04:27,090 --> 00:04:35,720
And please note that, according to our macro writing guidelines all these things should be in parentheses.
65
